> 4. **Confusing TIT (Turbine Inlet Temperature) with $T_3$.** TIT is the temperature of gases entering the turbine. Always state it as $T_3$ in your solution.
> 5. **Not sketching P-V and T-S diagrams** in efficiency derivation questions. KTU examiners award **1 mark** separately for the diagrams.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 1 — Classification: Rapid Revision Master Sheet**

- [ ] **Four primary classification criteria:** Cycle type, Shaft arrangement, Flow direction, Application.
- [ ] **Open cycle** = air in, exhaust out. Light & simple → aviation.
- [ ] **Closed cycle** = same working fluid recirculated. Heavy & clean → nuclear/industrial.
- [ ] **Semi-closed cycle** = hybrid, partial exhaust recirculation.
- [ ] **Single-shaft** = rigid coupling, constant speed, power generation.
- [ ] **Twin-shaft** = free turbine, variable speed, helicopter/marine.
- [ ] **Three-shaft** = LP + IP + HP spools, modern high-bypass turbofans.
- [ ] **Axial flow** = parallel to shaft, multi-stage, high flow, high pressure ratio.
- [ ] **Radial flow** = perpendicular to shaft, compact, small-scale.
- [ ] **Master equation:** $\eta_{th} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{(r_p)^{(\gamma - 1)/\gamma}}$.
- [ ] **Higher $r_p$ → higher efficiency**, but optimum $r_p \approx 8$–$12$.
- [ ] **Compressor work:** $W_C = c_p (T_2 - T_1)$.
- [ ] **Turbine work:** $W_T = c_p (T_3 - T_4)$.
- [ ] **Net work:** $W_{net} = W_T - W_C$.
- [ ] **Heat input:** $Q_{in} = c_p (T_3 - T_2)$.
- [ ] **Constants to memorize:** $\gamma = 1.4$, $c_p = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K, $R = 0.287$ kJ/kg·K.
- [ ] **SFC formula:** $SFC = \dot{m}_f / \dot{W}_{net}$ in **kg/kWh**.
- [ ] **Always include P-V and T-S diagrams** in derivation questions (1 free mark).
- [ ] **Always convert TIT ($T_3$) and $T_1$ in Kelvin**, not Celsius, before calculation.
- [ ] **Modified cycles** (regenerative, intercooled, reheated) increase either efficiency or net work — remember the trade-off.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Energy Equation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMET741 - GAS TURBINE AND JET PROPULSION > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Energy Equation # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) The **Energy Equation**, in the context of Gas Turbine and Jet Propulsion engineering, refers to the **Steady Flow Energy Equation (SFEE)** — the first law of thermodynamics applied to an **open system (control volume)** through which the working fluid (air, combustion gases) flows steadily. For a steady-flow control volume exchanging heat $q$ and work $w$ with the surroundings, the SFEE is mathematically stated as: $$h_1 + \frac{V_1^{2}}{2} + gZ_1 + q \;=\; h_2 + \frac{V_2^{2}}{2} + gZ_2 + w$$ where every term is expressed **per unit mass (kJ/kg)** of the working fluid. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The Energy Equation is the *single most important governing equation* in the entire Gas Turbine subject. It is the backbone used to analyze the **compressor, combustion chamber, turbine, nozzle, and diffuser** of every jet engine. Mastery of SFEE is a prerequisite for solving all cycle problems (Brayton cycle with reheat, regeneration, and intercooling) covered in Modules 2 and 3. > [!NOTE] > **Reference State:** Enthalpy $h$ and internal energy $u$ are thermodynamic properties whose absolute values depend on the chosen datum. KTU convention uses $0\,^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ or $25\,^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ as the reference for property tables of air and combustion products. Only **changes** in $h$ (i.e., $c_p\,\Delta T$) carry physical meaning in cycle analysis. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Water Wheel Pipeline Imagine water flowing steadily through a pipe fitted with a pump, a heater coil, and a small water turbine (a Pelton wheel). At the inlet (Station 1) the water has some pressure, velocity, and height above the ground; at the outlet (Station 2) it leaves with different values. Between the two stations, the pump *adds* energy, the heater *adds* heat, and the turbine *extracts* work. **The Steady Flow Energy Equation simply states that the total energy carried in by the water plus all the energy added from outside equals the total energy carried out plus all the energy rejected.** No energy is created or destroyed — it is merely reshuffled. Now replace "water" with **air**, "pump" with **compressor**, "heater" with **combustion chamber**, and "Pelton wheel" with **gas generator turbine**. The mathematics remains identical — and that is precisely why SFEE is universal in gas-turbine analysis. --- ## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics | Quantity | Symbol | Standard Unit (SI) | Engineering Unit Used in KTU Problems | |---|---|---|---| | Specific enthalpy | $h$ | $\mathrm{J/kg}$ | $\mathrm{kJ/kg}$ | | Velocity | $V$ | $\mathrm{m/s}$ | $\mathrm{m/s}$ | | Elevation | $Z$ | $\mathrm{m}$ | $\mathrm{m}$ | | Acceleration of gravity | $g$ | $9.81\,\mathrm{m/s^{2}}$ | $9.81\,\mathrm{m/s^{2}}$ | | Specific heat at constant pressure | $c_p$ | $\mathrm{J/(kg\cdot K)}$ | $\mathrm{1.005\;kJ/(kg\cdot K)}$ for air, $\mathrm{1.148\;kJ/(kg\cdot K)}$ for products | | Heat added per unit mass | $q$ | $\mathrm{J/kg}$ | $\mathrm{kJ/kg}$ | | Work done per unit mass | $w$ | $\mathrm{J/kg}$ | $\mathrm{kJ/kg}$ | > [!NOTE] > **Why $\mathbf{c_p = 1.005\;kJ/(kg\cdot K)}$ matters:** In the gas-turbine syllabus, the vast majority of problems assume air behaves as a *perfect gas with constant specific heats*. Therefore $h_2 - h_1 = c_p(T_2 - T_1)$, a linear function of temperature only — which makes the energy equation algebraic rather than requiring tabulated property lookups. --- # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Origin — Why Does the Energy Equation Exist? The Energy Equation is a direct mathematical consequence of the **First Law of Thermodynamics** (conservation of energy) when applied to a **control volume** rather than a closed system. In a gas-turbine engine, mass continuously *crosses* the system boundary (air enters the inlet, exhaust gases leave the nozzle). Hence a **closed-system** formulation of the First Law is insufficient — we must adopt the **open-system (control-volume)** form. ## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic Behind the Equation The derivation proceeds along five logical steps: - **Step 1 — Choose a control volume** that encloses the gas-turbine component of interest (e.g., the compressor rotor, the combustion chamber, a single turbine stage). Mark the inlet as **Station 1** and the outlet as **Station 2**. - **Step 2 — Write the general First Law for an open system** in rate form: $$\frac{dE_{cv}}{dt} \;=\; \dot{Q}_{in} - \dot{W}_{out} + \sum \dot{m}_{in}\!\left(h + \tfrac{V^{2}}{2} + gZ\right)_{in} - \sum \dot{m}_{out}\!\left(h + \tfrac{V^{2}}{2} + gZ\right)_{out}$$ - **Step 3 — Apply the steady-flow assumption**: all properties at any point inside the control volume are *time-invariant*. Therefore: $$\frac{dE_{cv}}{dt} \;=\; 0$$ Mass balance reduces to $\dot{m}_{in} = \dot{m}_{out} = \dot{m}$. - **Step 4 — Divide the entire energy balance by $\dot{m}$** to obtain the *per-unit-mass* form (the equation KTU students will use 95% of the time). - **Step 5 — Identify the work term**: the only work term that can cross a steady control-volume boundary is the **shaft work** $w_s$ (compressor/turbine) plus any **flow work** already embedded inside the enthalpy $h = u + Pv$. The flow work is *not* written separately — it is "absorbed" into the enthalpy. ## 2.3 Term-by-Term Physical Meaning | Term | Name | Physical Meaning | |---|---|---| | $h$ | Specific enthalpy | Internal energy + flow work already carried by the moving fluid | | $\dfrac{V^{2}}{2}$ | Specific kinetic energy | Energy due to bulk motion of the fluid | | $gZ$ | Specific potential energy | Energy due to elevation above a datum (gravity) | | $q$ | Heat transferred per unit mass | Energy added by combustion or rejected to surroundings | | $w$ | Work done per unit mass | Shaft work extracted (+) or supplied (−) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Sign Convention (as used in KTU textbooks):** > - $q$ is **positive** when heat is *supplied* to the system (e.g., in the combustor). > - $q$ is **negative** when heat is *rejected* (e.g., intercooler, aftercooler). > - $w$ is **positive** when work is *done by* the system (turbine producing shaft power). > - $w$ is **negative** when work is *done on* the system (compressor consuming shaft power). > - Students who invert these signs routinely lose 2–3 marks in KTU valuation — examiners are *very* particular about sign discipline. --- ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Simplified Form of SFEE (per unit mass) | Typical Component Where Used | Assumption | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $h_1 + \dfrac{V_1^{2}}{2} = h_2 + \dfrac{V_2^{2}}{2}$ | **Nozzle, Diffuser** | Adiabatic ($q = 0$), no shaft work ($w = 0$), $\Delta Z \approx 0$ | | 2 | $h_1 = h_2 + w$ | **Turbine** | Adiabatic, $\Delta KE \approx 0$, $\Delta Z \approx 0$ → $w = h_1 - h_2 = c_p(T_1 - T_2)$ | | 3 | $h_1 - w = h_2$ (equivalently $w = h_1 - h_2$) | **Compressor** | Adiabatic, $\Delta KE \approx 0$ → work *input* $= c_p(T_2 - T_1)$ | | 4 | $h_1 + q = h_2 + \dfrac{V_2^{2}}{2}$ | **Combustion Chamber (Ramjet, Afterburner)** | No shaft work; KE at inlet negligible; $q = c_p(T_2 - T_1) + \dfrac{V_2^{2}}{2}$ | | 5 | $h_1 + \dfrac{V_1^{2}}{2} + q = h_2 + \dfrac{V_2^{2}}{2} + w$ | **General open system with all effects** | Full form, no simplification | > [!NOTE] > **Universal Companion Equation:** When working with the SFEE in gas turbines, the **perfect-gas enthalpy relation** $h_2 - h_1 = c_p(T_2 - T_1)$ is *always* used together with SFEE. Memorize this pair as a single inseparable unit — it appears in roughly **9 out of every 10** KTU gas-turbine numerical problems. ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility The SFEE is not a textbook abstraction — it is the **design equation** behind: - Turbofan and turbojet engine performance (specific thrust, thrust-specific fuel consumption). - Industrial gas-turbine power plants (Brayton cycle efficiency and specific work output). - Rocket-engine nozzle design (expansion ratio, exit Mach number, specific impulse). - Aircraft environmental control systems (ECS) and auxiliary power units (APU). Without the SFEE, no quantitative analysis of any of these systems would be possible. --- # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Steady Flow Energy Equation ### Starting Point — The General First Law for an Open System For a control volume $CV$ that exchanges both heat and work with the surroundings and through which mass flows, the **First Law of Thermodynamics in rate form** states that the rate of change of total stored energy inside the CV equals the net rate of energy influx: $$\frac{dE_{cv}}{dt} \;=\; \dot{Q}_{cv} - \dot{W}_{cv} + \dot{m}_{in}\!\left(h + \tfrac{V^{2}}{2} + gZ\right)_{in} - \dot{m}_{out}\!\left(h + \tfrac{V^{2}}{2} + gZ\right)_{out}$$ **Conversion logic — text row:** The term $E_{cv}$ represents *all* forms of energy stored inside the control volume (internal, kinetic, potential). $\dot{Q}_{cv}$ is the net heat transfer rate *into* the CV. $\dot{W}_{cv}$ is the net work transfer rate *out* of the CV (note the minus sign convention). The last two terms carry the *energy convected* by the inflowing and outflowing fluid streams; each stream carries enthalpy (which itself contains internal energy + flow work $Pv$), plus any bulk kinetic and potential energy. --- ### Step 1 — Expand $E_{cv}$ $$E_{cv} \;=\; U_{cv} + \tfrac{1}{2}\,m_{cv}\,V_{cv}^{2} + m_{cv}\,g\,Z_{cv}$$ **Text row:** Inside the control volume, fluid possesses internal energy $U_{cv}$, kinetic energy of bulk motion $\tfrac{1}{2} m_{cv} V_{cv}^{2}$, and potential energy $m_{cv} g Z_{cv}$. For most engineering applications the kinetic and potential energies of the fluid *trapped inside* the CV are neglected compared with the internal energy. --- ### Step 2 — Impose the Steady-Flow Condition A *steady* flow means that, at every point inside the CV, properties do **not** change with time. Therefore the *total* energy stored inside the CV cannot change either: $$\frac{dE_{cv}}{dt} \;=\; 0$$ **Text row:** Because the system has been operating long enough that the transient startup phase is over (the engine is at design speed and stable operating point), no further energy is accumulating or depleting inside the CV. --- ### Step 3 — Apply Conservation of Mass Under steady conditions, mass can neither accumulate nor deplete inside the CV: $$\sum \dot{m}_{in} \;=\; \sum \dot{m}_{out}$$ For a single-stream device (which all single-spool gas turbines and nozzles effectively are): $$\dot{m}_{1} \;=\; \dot{m}_{2} \;=\; \dot{m}$$ **Text row:** Whatever mass enters the CV per second must leave per second. Otherwise mass would pile up inside the engine — clearly impossible in steady cruise or design operation. --- ### Step 4 — Substitute Back Into the First Law Substituting $\dot{m}_1 = \dot{m}_2 = \dot{m}$ and $\tfrac{dE_{cv}}{dt} = 0$ into the original rate equation, and dividing *every term* by $\dot{m}$, we obtain the **per-unit-mass form** of the SFEE: $$\begin{aligned} 0 \;=\; \frac{\dot{Q}_{cv}}{\dot{m}} - \frac{\dot{W}_{cv}}{\dot{m}} \; &+\; \left(h + \frac{V^{2}}{2} + gZ\right)_{1} - \left(h + \frac{V^{2}}{2} + gZ\right)_{2} \end{aligned}$$ Rearranging all inlet terms to the **left** side and all outlet terms to the **right** side (the conventional textbook presentation): $$h_{1} + \frac{V_{1}^{2}}{2} + gZ_{1} + q \;=\; h_{2} + \frac{V_{2}^{2}}{2} + gZ_{2} + w$$ **Text row:** Here $q = \dot{Q}_{cv}/\dot{m}$ and $w = \dot{W}_{cv}/\dot{m}$ are the heat added and work done *per unit mass* of fluid passing through. This is the canonical SFEE form that every KTU student must memorize verbatim. --- ### Step 5 — Simplification for Gas-Turbine Applications For gas-turbine engines the change in elevation between inlet and outlet is negligible (the engine dimensions are $\sim$ 2–5 m, while velocities are $\sim$ 100–600 m/s). Therefore $g(Z_2 - Z_1) \approx 0$ and the equation reduces to: $$h_{1} + \frac{V_{1}^{2}}{2} + q \;=\; h_{2} + \frac{V_{2}^{2}}{2} + w$$ **Text row:** Dropping $gZ$ is justified because $g \cdot \Delta Z$ is typically three orders of magnitude smaller than the kinetic-energy term $V^{2}/2$. Examiners at KTU explicitly state "neglect changes in potential energy" in the majority of numerical problems — this sentence is the examiner's blessing to drop the $gZ$ terms. --- ## 3.2 Component-Specific Derivations from the General SFEE ### 3.2.1 Compressor (Adiabatic, $\Delta KE \approx 0$) Starting from the full equation: $$h_1 + q = h_2 + w$$ With $q = 0$ (no heat transfer; compressors are highly insulated and process times are too short for significant heat loss) and $\Delta KE \approx 0$: $$\begin{aligned} h_1 \;&=\; h_2 + w_{c} \\[4pt] w_{c} \;&=\; h_1 - h_2 \;=\; c_p(T_1 - T_2) \end{aligned}$$ **Text row:** Because work is *done on* the fluid by the compressor, the temperature rises ($T_2 > T_1$) and the work $w_c$ itself is **negative** under the KTU sign convention. In KTU textbooks the *magnitude* of compressor work is reported as a positive quantity $W_c = c_p(T_2 - T_1) > 0$, with the wording "compressor work input". --- ### 3.2.2 Turbine (Adiabatic, $\Delta KE \approx 0$) $$h_1 + q = h_2 + w$$ With $q = 0$: $$\begin{aligned} h_1 \;&=\; h_2 + w_{T} \\[4pt] w_{T} \;&=\; h_1 - h_2 \;=\; c_p(T_1 - T_2) \end{aligned}$$ **Text row:** Now the temperature falls ($T_2 < T_1$) because the fluid *gives up* its enthalpy to do work on the turbine blades. The work $w_T$ is **positive** — the turbine produces shaft power. --- ### 3.2.3 Nozzle (Adiabatic, No Shaft Work, $\Delta Z \approx 0$) $$h_1 + \frac{V_1^{2}}{2} = h_2 + \frac{V_2^{2}}{2}$$ Rearranging: $$\begin{aligned} \frac{V_2^{2} - V_1^{2}}{2} \;&=\; h_1 - h_2 \;=\; c_p(T_1 - T_2) \end{aligned}$$ **Text row:** A nozzle converts the *thermal* energy of the gas into *kinetic* energy — temperature drops while velocity rises. This equation is the basis of the jet-propulsion thrust equation (Module 4 of the syllabus). --- ### 3.2.4 Diffuser (Adiabatic, No Shaft Work, $\Delta Z \approx 0$) Identical equation to the nozzle: $$\frac{V_1^{2} - V_2^{2}}{2} \;=\; h_2 - h_1 \;=\; c_p(T_2 - T_1)$$ **Text row:** A diffuser (used at the inlet of turbojet and turbofan engines) *decelerates* the oncoming airstream and converts its kinetic energy into a pressure/temperature rise — preparing the air for efficient compression. --- ### 3.2.5 Combustion Chamber (No Shaft Work, $\Delta KE \approx 0$) $$h_1 + q \;=\; h_2$$ Therefore: $$q \;=\; h_2 - h_1 \;=\; c_p(T_2 - T_1)$$ **Text row:** In a combustor, chemical energy of fuel is released as heat $q$ and added to the air. The mass flow rate of fuel is typically only 1–2% of the air mass flow, so we ignore the fuel's own kinetic and potential energies — a small but standard simplification in KTU cycle analysis. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Quick Decision Tree for Component Identification | Given a device with… | The dominating SFEE term is… | Simplified equation | |---|---|---| | One inlet, one outlet, no shaft, no heat | KE ↔ enthalpy | $h_1 + V_1^{2}/2 = h_2 + V_2^{2}/2$ | | One inlet, one outlet, shaft work present, adiabatic | Enthalpy ↔ work | $h_1 - h_2 = w$ | | One inlet, one outlet, heat addition only, no shaft | Enthalpy ↔ heat | $h_2 - h_1 = q$ | | Multiple inlets/outlets, all effects | Full SFEE on each stream | Sum after applying mass balance | --- # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of SFEE Application in a Turbojet Engine The Mermaid diagram below maps *which* simplified form of the SFEE is applied at *each* station of a single-spool turbojet engine — from inlet to propulsive nozzle. This is the analytical roadmap students should sketch in the margin of every KTU numerical answer. ```mermaid flowchart LR A0["Ambient Air
Station 0"] --> A1["Diffuser Inlet
Station 1"] A1 --> A2["Compressor Inlet
Station 2"] A2 --> A3["Combustion Chamber
Station 3"] A3 --> A4["Turbine Inlet
Station 4"] A4 --> A5["Turbine Exit
Station 5"] A5 --> A6["Jet Nozzle
Station 6"] A6 --> A7["Exhaust to Atmosphere
Station 7"] A1 -- SFEE: KE to Enthalpy
h1 + V1s2 div 2 = h2 + V2s2 div 2 --> A2 A2 -- SFEE: Shaft Work Input
wc = cp times T3 minus T2 --> A3 A3 -- SFEE: Heat Addition
qin = cp times T4 minus T3 --> A4 A4 -- SFEE: Shaft Work Output
wt = cp times T4 minus T5 --> A5 A5 -- SFEE: Enthalpy to KE
h5 + V5s2 div 2 = h6 + V6s2 div 2 --> A6 ``` **Reading the diagram:** Each labelled arrow corresponds to *one* component and *one* simplified SFEE form (Table in §2.4). At the diffuser, KE converts to enthalpy; at the compressor, shaft work enters the fluid; at the combustion chamber, fuel energy is added; at the turbine, enthalpy is converted back to shaft work; at the nozzle, the remaining enthalpy becomes the propulsive jet KE. --- ## 4.2 Control-Volume Schematic — Generic Application of SFEE ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph cv["CONTROL VOLUME - CV"] direction LR IN1["Inlet Stream
dot-m, h1, V1, Z1"] --> DEV["Device
Compressor OR Turbine
OR Nozzle OR Combustor"] --> OUT1["Outlet Stream
dot-m, h2, V2, Z2"] QAR["Heat q
per kg"] -. In OR Out .-> DEV WAR["Shaft Work w
per kg"] -. In OR Out .-> DEV end DEV -- SFEE --> EQN["h1 + Vsq1 div 2 + gZ1 + q = h2 + Vsq2 div 2 + gZ2 + w"] ``` **Reading the diagram:** A generic control volume has one inlet stream and one outlet stream, both carrying the same mass flow rate $\dot{m}$. Heat $q$ and shaft work $w$ may cross the boundary in either direction. The SFEE relates *all* these streams at the two stations. This is the **universal** picture a student should keep in mind before specializing to any particular gas-turbine component. --- ## 4.3 Mermaid Compilation Safeguard Note > [!IMPORTANT] > **Implementation note:** Inside the node labels above the symbols $V^{2}$, $c_p$, etc. have been replaced with their *spelled-out* ASCII forms ("Vsq1", "cp", "div") because Mermaid's parser strips LaTeX markup inside double-quoted node labels. The mathematical meaning is unchanged; the original LaTeX equation is presented separately in §2 and §3 for clarity and examination use. --- # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PEMET741]` **State the Steady Flow Energy Equation (SFEE) and explain the significance of each term.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The SFEE for a steady-flow control volume, per unit mass of working fluid, is: $$h_{1} + \frac{V_{1}^{2}}{2} + gZ_{1} + q \;=\; h_{2} + \frac{V_{2}^{2}}{2} + gZ_{2} + w$$ Term-by-term meaning (1 mark each, last mark for correct equation form): - $h$ — *specific enthalpy* (internal energy + flow work) of the fluid. - $V^{2}/2$ — *specific kinetic energy* associated with bulk motion of the fluid. - $gZ$ — *specific potential energy* due to elevation above a datum. - $q$ — *heat transferred per unit mass* into the control volume. - $w$ — *shaft work done per unit mass* by the fluid (positive for turbine, negative for compressor). It is the **First Law of Thermodynamics** applied to an open system and is the *governing equation* for analysing every component of a gas-turbine engine (compressor, combustor, turbine, nozzle, diffuser). --- ### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, PEMET741]` **For a nozzle, show how the Steady Flow Energy Equation reduces to a relation between stagnation temperature and static temperature.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** For an adiabatic ($q = 0$) nozzle with no shaft work ($w = 0$) and negligible change in elevation ($\Delta Z \approx 0$), the SFEE reduces to: $$h_{1} + \frac{V_{1}^{2}}{2} \;=\; h_{2} + \frac{V_{2}^{2}}{2}$$ For a perfect gas with constant $c_p$: $$c_{p}T_{1} + \frac{V_{1}^{2}}{2} \;=\; c_{p}T_{2} + \frac{V_{2}^{2}}{2}$$ The **stagnation (or total) temperature** at any station is defined as $T_{0} = T + \dfrac{V^{2}}{2c_p}$. Therefore: $$c_{p}T_{01} \;=\; c_{p}T_{02} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; T_{01} \;=\; T_{02}$$ Hence in an *ideal adiabatic nozzle*, the **stagnation temperature remains constant** from inlet to outlet. This is the key insight behind the use of stagnation properties in gas-turbine cycle analysis. **(1 mark for stating SFEE reduction, 1 mark for defining $T_0$, 1 mark for the conclusion $T_{01} = T_{02}$.)** --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) --- ### QUESTION A `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PEMET741]` — **Module 1 Choice** > **(a)** Derive the Steady Flow Energy Equation (SFEE) for a steady-flow control volume. State clearly all assumptions made during the derivation. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** Air enters a compressor of a gas-turbine engine at $100\,\mathrm{kPa}$ and $300\,\mathrm{K}$ with a velocity of $50\,\mathrm{m/s}$. The air leaves the compressor at $500\,\mathrm{kPa}$ and $580\,\mathrm{K}$ with a velocity of $150\,\mathrm{m/s}$. The compressor absorbs $160\,\mathrm{kW}$ of shaft power from the engine shaft. Assuming air behaves as a perfect gas with $c_p = 1.005\,\mathrm{kJ/(kg\cdot K)}$ and neglecting heat transfer and changes in potential energy, determine the mass flow rate of air through the compressor. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks) The complete derivation is shown in §3.1 of these notes. The candidate is expected to present the following six logical steps: 1. Write the general First Law for an open system (control volume) in rate form — **1 mark**. 2. Expand the total stored energy $E_{cv} = U_{cv} + \tfrac{1}{2}m_{cv}V_{cv}^{2} + m_{cv}gZ_{cv}$ — **1 mark**. 3. Impose the steady-flow assumption $\tfrac{dE_{cv}}{dt} = 0$ — **1 mark**. 4. Impose conservation of mass $\dot{m}_{in} = \dot{m}_{out} = \dot{m}$ — **1 mark**. 5. Divide the resulting equation by $\dot{m}$ to obtain the per-unit-mass form and rearrange terms — **2 marks**. 6. List the simplifying assumptions explicitly: steady flow, single inlet/outlet stream, negligible $\Delta KE$ and $\Delta PE$ of CV contents, perfect-gas with constant $c_p$ — **1 mark**. Final form expected: $$h_1 + \frac{V_1^{2}}{2} + gZ_1 + q \;=\; h_2 + \frac{V_2^{2}}{2} + gZ_2 + w$$ #### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks) **Given data:** $$P_1 = 100\,\mathrm{kPa},\quad T_1 = 300\,\mathrm{K},\quad V_1 = 50\,\mathrm{m/s}$$ $$P_2 = 500\,\mathrm{kPa},\quad T_2 = 580\,\mathrm{K},\quad V_2 = 150\,\mathrm{m/s}$$ $$\dot{W}_c = 160\,\mathrm{kW},\quad c_p = 1.005\,\mathrm{kJ/(kg\cdot K)}$$ Assumptions: adiabatic ($q = 0$), $\Delta PE = 0$. **Step 1 — Write SFEE for the compressor.** With $q = 0$ and $\Delta PE \approx 0$: $$h_1 + \frac{V_1^{2}}{2} = h_2 + \frac{V_2^{2}}{2} + w_c$$ **Step 2 — Compute the enthalpy change** using the perfect-gas relation $h_2 - h_1 = c_p(T_2 - T_1)$: $$\begin{aligned} h_2 - h_1 \;&=\; 1.005 \times (580 - 300) \\ \;&=\; 1.005 \times 280 \\ \;&=\; 281.4\,\mathrm{kJ/kg} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3 — Compute the kinetic-energy change:** $$\begin{aligned} \frac{V_2^{2} - V_1^{2}}{2} \;&=\; \frac{(150)^{2} - (50)^{2}}{2} \;\mathrm{J/kg} \\ \;&=\; \frac{22500 - 2500}{2} \\ \;&=\; 10000\,\mathrm{J/kg} \\ \;&=\; 10\,\mathrm{kJ/kg} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 4 — Solve SFEE for the specific compressor work $w_c$ (input positive):** $$\begin{aligned} w_c \;&=\; (h_2 - h_1) - \frac{V_1^{2} - V_2^{2}}{2} \\ \;&=\; (h_2 - h_1) + \frac{V_2^{2} - V_1^{2}}{2} \\ \;&=\; 281.4 + 10 \\ \;&=\; 291.4\,\mathrm{kJ/kg} \end{aligned}$$ **Text row:** Compressor work *input* per kg of air is $291.4\,\mathrm{kJ/kg}$. The kinetic-energy contribution $10\,\mathrm{kJ/kg}$ is small ($\sim 3\%$) compared with the enthalpy rise, which is why many textbook problems state "neglect changes in KE" — but here the problem *includes* velocities, so we must retain the term. **Step 5 — Compute the mass flow rate** using $\dot{W}_c = \dot{m}\, w_c$: $$\begin{aligned} \dot{m} \;&=\; \frac{\dot{W}_c}{w_c} \\ \;&=\; \frac{160}{291.4} \\ \;&=\; 0.549\,\mathrm{kg/s} \end{aligned}$$ **Final Answer:** $$\boxed{\dot{m} \;\approx\; 0.549\;\mathrm{kg/s} \;\;\text{(or}\; 32.94\;\mathrm{kg/min})}$$ --- ### QUESTION B `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, PEMET741]` — **Module 1 Choice** > **(a)** For each of the following gas-turbine components, write the *reduced form* of the Steady Flow Energy Equation and clearly state the assumptions used: **(i)** diffuser, **(ii)** compressor, **(iii)** combustion chamber, **(iv)** gas-turbine, **(v)** propulsive nozzle. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** Combustion gases leave the combustion chamber of a turbojet engine at $850\,\mathrm{K}$ and $50\,\mathrm{m/s}$. The gases expand through a nozzle and leave at $650\,\mathrm{K}$ and $450\,\mathrm{m/s}$. Assuming the nozzle is adiabatic and the gases behave as a perfect gas with $c_p = 1.148\,\mathrm{kJ/(kg\cdot K)}$, determine whether the process is physically possible. Also calculate the heat loss (if any) per kg of gas if the nozzle is *not* perfectly adiabatic. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks) Common assumptions in *every* component below: steady flow, single-stream inlet and outlet, perfect gas with constant $c_p$, $\Delta Z \approx 0$. Each correctly derived form carries **1.4 marks** (= 7 ÷ 5). **(i) Diffuser** — adiabatic ($q = 0$), no shaft work ($w = 0$): $$h_1 + \frac{V_1^{2}}{2} \;=\; h_2 + \frac{V_2^{2}}{2}$$ **(ii) Compressor** — adiabatic, $\Delta KE \approx 0$: $$w_c \;=\; h_1 - h_2 \;=\; c_p(T_1 - T_2)\quad (\text{magnitude } = c_p(T_2 - T_1))$$ **(iii) Combustion chamber** — no shaft work, $\Delta KE \approx 0$: $$q_{in} \;=\; h_2 - h_1 \;=\; c_p(T_2 - T_1)$$ **(iv) Gas turbine** — adiabatic, $\Delta KE \approx 0$: $$w_T \;=\; h_1 - h_2 \;=\; c_p(T_1 - T_2) \;>\; 0$$ **(v) Propulsive nozzle** — adiabatic, no shaft work: $$h_1 + \frac{V_1^{2}}{2} \;=\; h_2 + \frac{V_2^{2}}{2}$$ #### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks) **Given data:** $$T_1 = 850\,\mathrm{K},\;\; V_1 = 50\,\mathrm{m/s},\;\; T_2 = 650\,\mathrm{K},\;\; V_2 = 450\,\mathrm{m/s}$$ $$c_p = 1.148\,\mathrm{kJ/(kg\cdot K)}$$ **Step 1 — Compute the change in kinetic energy** per kg: $$\begin{aligned} \Delta KE \;&=\; \frac{V_2^{2} - V_1^{2}}{2} \\ \;&=\; \frac{(450)^{2} - (50)^{2}}{2} \\ \;&=\; \frac{202500 - 2500}{2} \\ \;&=\; 100000\,\mathrm{J/kg} \\ \;&=\; 100\,\mathrm{kJ/kg} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 2 — Compute the change in enthalpy** per kg: $$\begin{aligned} \Delta h \;&=\; c_p(T_2 - T_1) \\ \;&=\; 1.148 \times (650 - 850) \\ \;&=\; 1.148 \times (-200) \\ \;&=\; -229.6\,\mathrm{kJ/kg} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3 — Test physical possibility** under the adiabatic assumption. The SFEE for an adiabatic nozzle with no shaft work says energy conservation requires: $$\Delta h + \Delta KE \;=\; 0 \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad \Delta KE \;=\; -\Delta h$$ Plug in the numbers: $$\begin{aligned} -\Delta h \;&=\; 229.6\,\mathrm{kJ/kg} \\[4pt] \Delta KE \;&=\; 100\,\mathrm{kJ/kg} \end{aligned}$$ Since $\Delta KE = 100\,\mathrm{kJ/kg} \neq 229.6\,\mathrm{kJ/kg} = -\Delta h$, the process **violates the First Law** if assumed adiabatic. The enthalpy drop *available* ($229.6\,\mathrm{kJ/kg}$) is much larger than the kinetic-energy *gained* ($100\,\mathrm{kJ/kg}$). Physically this *is* possible — it simply means there is **energy unaccounted for**, which must be heat loss. **Step 4 — Compute the heat loss** by applying the *general* (non-adiabatic) SFEE with $w = 0$: $$\begin{aligned} q \;&=\; (h_2 - h_1) + \frac{V_2^{2} - V_1^{2}}{2} \\ \;&=\; \Delta h + \Delta KE \\ \;&=\; -229.6 + 100 \\ \;&=\; -129.6\,\mathrm{kJ/kg} \end{aligned}$$ The **negative sign** indicates that heat is *lost by* the gas to the surroundings. Therefore the nozzle is *not* adiabatic and approximately $129.6\,\mathrm{kJ/kg}$ of energy is being rejected as heat. **Final Answer:** $$\boxed{q \;\approx\; -129.6\,\mathrm{kJ/kg}\;\;(\text{heat rejected by the gas})}$$ **Conclusion:** The process *is* physically possible, but only because $129.6\,\mathrm{kJ/kg}$ of heat is being lost from the gas to the surroundings; it is **not** a perfectly adiabatic nozzle. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Pitfalls where KTU students routinely lose marks on SFEE questions:** > > 1. **Sign confusion on $w$.** A common error is to write $w = h_2 - h_1$ for a compressor (instead of the correct $w_c = h_1 - h_2$ which is *negative*, or equivalently report the magnitude as $h_2 - h_1 > 0$ and explicitly call it "compressor work *input*"). Examiners *do* deduct 1–2 marks for this. > 2. **Forgetting the kinetic-energy term** when velocities are explicitly given in the data. If the problem provides $V_1$ and $V_2$, you *must* include $V^{2}/2$ terms in the SFEE — examiners test this trap frequently. > 3. **Unit mismatch between $c_p T$ (kJ/kg) and $V^{2}/2$ (J/kg).** Always convert $V^{2}/2$ to kJ/kg by dividing by 1000. Mixing J and kJ in the same equation is the single most common arithmetic error in KTU scripts — usually a **2-mark penalty**. > 4. **Using $c_p = 1.005$ for combustion products** instead of $1.148\,\mathrm{kJ/(kg\cdot K)}$. The two values are *not* interchangeable — products of combustion have different specific heat from air because of the extra $\mathrm{CO_2}$ and $\mathrm{H_2O}$ molecules. > 5. **Dropping the absolute value signs in $|T_2 - T_1|$ carelessly** in compressor/turbine work. Always state the direction explicitly ("compressor work input" vs "turbine work output"). > 6. **Writing SFEE for a closed system** ($Q = \Delta U + W$) when the question is about an open system. If mass is crossing the boundary, *always* use the SFEE form — closed-system form is **wrong** and gets **zero** credit. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - ☐ **SFEE per unit mass:** $h_1 + V_1^{2}/2 + gZ_1 + q = h_2 + V_2^{2}/2 + gZ_2 + w$. Memorize verbatim. - ☐ **Origin:** First Law of Thermodynamics applied to a *control volume* under *steady* conditions. - ☐ **Sign convention (KTU standard):** $q > 0$ = heat supplied to system; $w > 0$ = work done *by* system. - ☐ **Perfect-gas companion:** $h_2 - h_1 = c_p(T_2 - T_1)$. Use $c_p = 1.005\,\mathrm{kJ/(kg\cdot K)}$ for air and $c_p = 1.148\,\mathrm{kJ/(kg\cdot K)}$ for combustion products. - ☐ **Diffuser / Nozzle (adiabatic, no shaft work):** $h + V^{2}/2 = $ constant; equivalently, stagnation temperature $T_0 = T + V^{2}/(2 c_p)$ is *constant*. - ☐ **Compressor (adiabatic):** $w_c = h_2 - h_1 = c_p(T_2 - T_1) > 0$ — work *input*, fluid temperature *rises*. - ☐ **Turbine (adiabatic):** $w_T = h_1 - h_2 = c_p(T_1 - T_2) > 0$ — work *output*, fluid temperature *falls*. - ☐ **Combustion chamber:** $q_{in} = h_2 - h_1 = c_p(T_2 - T_1)$; no shaft work; KE changes small. - ☐ **Neglect $gZ$ always** in gas-turbine problems unless specifically asked. - ☐ **Unit conversion rule:** $V^{2}/2$ in $\mathrm{J/kg}$ ÷ 1000 = $\mathrm{kJ/kg}$ before adding to $c_p T$. - ☐ **Mass conservation:** $\dot{m}_{in} = \dot{m}_{out} = \dot{m}$ under steady flow — use this *before* SFEE for any multi-stream device. - ☐ **SFEE applies per unit mass;** multiply by $\dot{m}$ at the end to obtain power ($\dot{Q}$ or $\dot{W}$ in kW).

topics.title: Thermodynamic Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET741 - GAS TURBINE AND JET PROPULSION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Thermodynamic Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Thermodynamic Analysis of a Gas Turbine** refers to the systematic application of the First and Second Laws of Thermodynamics to the working fluid (typically air) as it undergoes a sequence of processes through the principal components of the gas turbine plant — namely the **compressor**, the **combustion chamber**, and the **turbine** — to determine the cycle's net work output, thermal efficiency, specific fuel consumption, and the effect of operating parameters such as **pressure ratio (rp)** and **turbine inlet temperature (TIT)**.

The fundamental ideal cycle upon which the thermodynamic analysis is based is the **Brayton Cycle** (also called the **Joule Cycle**). The Brayton cycle consists of four internally reversible processes:

| Process | Operation | Thermodynamic Nature |
|---------|-----------|----------------------|
| 1 → 2 | Isentropic compression | Compressor |
| 2 → 3 | Constant pressure heat addition | Combustion Chamber |
| 3 → 4 | Isentropic expansion | Turbine |
| 4 → 1 | Constant pressure heat rejection | Atmosphere (open cycle) |

For the open-cycle gas turbine (the dominant configuration used in aviation and industrial power generation), the heat rejection process (4 → 1) occurs in the atmosphere when the exhaust gases leave the turbine and mix with the surrounding air at atmospheric pressure. Hence, the cycle operates on an **open loop**, although thermodynamically it can still be analyzed using the closed Brayton cycle idealization.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Imagine a "controlled continuous explosion engine":** Think of the gas turbine like a high-speed industrial hairdryer that breathes air, squeezes it really hard, heats it up using fuel, and then allows the expanding hot air to spin a shaft that does useful work — all in a matter of milliseconds and continuously, thousands of times per minute.

**A simple real-world analogy:**

Picture a bicycle pump attached to a sealed steel can. When you push the pump handle:
- **Compression** (1→2): You push air into the can — this is like the compressor doing work *on* the gas.
- **Heating** (2→3): If you now light a small burner under the can at constant pressure (open a relief valve), heat flows into the gas without changing its pressure significantly.
- **Expansion** (3→4): The hot, high-pressure gas rushes out through a small turbine wheel, spinning it — this is the turbine extracting work *from* the gas.
- **Cooling** (4→1): The remaining low-energy exhaust gases leave to the atmosphere.

The **net work** is the difference between what the turbine produces and what the compressor consumes — this is the *useful* shaft work available to drive a propeller, generator, or compressor of a jet engine.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in Thermodynamic Analysis

The following physical constants and standard metrics are essential:

- **γ (gamma)** = **specific heat ratio = Cp / Cv ≈ 1.4** for air at room temperature
- **R** = **Universal Gas Constant = 8.314 kJ/kg·mol·K**
- **R_air** = **Specific gas constant for air = 0.287 kJ/kg·K**
- **Standard atmospheric pressure** = **101.325 kPa**
- **Standard atmospheric temperature** = **288.15 K**
- **Lower Heating Value (LHV) of kerosene-type jet fuel ≈ 43,100 kJ/kg**
- **Lower Heating Value (LHV) of methane ≈ 50,000 kJ/kg**

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Examiner's Insight:** In Module 1 questions, the standard reference state for cycle analysis is almost always **T₁ = 300 K** and **P₁ = 1 bar (100 kPa)**, with **γ = 1.4** for air (treated as an ideal gas with constant specific heats). Always state these assumptions explicitly before solving.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Logical Analysis of the Ideal Brayton Cycle

### Stage 1: Isentropic Compression (Process 1 → 2)

The ambient air enters the compressor at state **1** (T₁, P₁). The compressor is treated as an **adiabatic, internally reversible** device, hence isentropic.

- The compressor consumes work.
- Temperature rises; pressure rises significantly.
- The exit pressure is fixed by the chosen **pressure ratio**.

**Key relation:** The compressor work is positive when considered as work input to the fluid (negative on the system):

$$W_C = h_2 - h_1 = C_p (T_2 - T_1)$$

**Isentropic temperature-pressure relation:**

$$\frac{T_2}{T_1} = \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma}} = r_p^{\frac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma}}$$

### Stage 2: Constant Pressure Heat Addition (Process 2 → 3)

Fuel is burned in the combustion chamber using compressed air from the compressor. To maintain stable combustion, only a fraction of compressed air is used for combustion; the rest is mixed later to control TIT. Thermodynamically, this stage is idealized as:

- **Pressure remains constant:** P₃ = P₂
- Heat is added from an external source (chemical energy of fuel).

$$Q_{in} = h_3 - h_2 = C_p (T_3 - T_2)$$

The maximum temperature **T₃** is called the **Turbine Inlet Temperature (TIT)** or **T₃** — this is metallurgically limited (typically 1500–1700 K for modern turbines with cooling).

### Stage 3: Isentropic Expansion (Process 3 → 4)

Hot gases expand through the turbine back to atmospheric pressure (open cycle) or to the cycle's lowest pressure. The turbine produces work.

$$W_T = h_3 - h_4 = C_p (T_3 - T_4)$$

**Isentropic relation:**

$$\frac{T_4}{T_3} = \left(\frac{P_4}{P_3}\right)^{\frac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma}} = \left(\frac{1}{r_p}\right)^{\frac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma}}$$

### Stage 4: Constant Pressure Heat Rejection (Process 4 → 1)

In the **open cycle**, the exhaust gases leave to the atmosphere at P₁. Heat rejection is therefore notional — it represents the loss of thermal energy to the surroundings when exhaust mixes with ambient air.

$$Q_{out} = h_4 - h_1 = C_p (T_4 - T_1)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why "Why" and "How" matter:** The Brayton cycle's efficiency depends *only* on the pressure ratio for the *ideal* cycle, *not* on the turbine inlet temperature. This counterintuitive result is one of the most-tested concepts in KTU Module 1.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formulas — Memorize These for the KTU Exam**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $r_p = P_2 / P_1 = P_3 / P_4$ | Pressure ratio of compressor | dimensionless |
| 2 | $T_2 / T_1 = r_p^{(\gamma - 1)/\gamma}$ | Isentropic compression temperature relation | K |
| 3 | $T_4 / T_3 = (1 / r_p)^{(\gamma - 1)/\gamma}$ | Isentropic expansion temperature relation | K |
| 4 | $W_C = C_p (T_2 - T_1)$ | Compressor work (per kg of air) | kJ/kg |
| 5 | $W_T = C_p (T_3 - T_4)$ | Turbine work (per kg of air) | kJ/kg |
| 6 | $W_{net} = W_T - W_C = C_p [(T_3 - T_4) - (T_2 - T_1)]$ | Net cycle work output | kJ/kg |
| 7 | $Q_{in} = C_p (T_3 - T_2)$ | Heat added (per kg of air) | kJ/kg |
| 8 | $\eta_{th} = W_{net} / Q_{in} = 1 - (1 / r_p^{(\gamma - 1)/\gamma})$ | Ideal Brayton thermal efficiency | dimensionless |
| 9 | $\eta_{th} = 1 - (T_1 / T_2)$ | Alternate form for ideal Brayton | dimensionless |
| 10 | $r_{p,opt} = (T_3 / T_1)^{[\gamma / (2(\gamma - 1))]}$ | Pressure ratio for max net work | dimensionless |
| 11 | $\eta_{th,opt} = 1 - \sqrt{(T_1 / T_3)}$ | Max efficiency at $r_{p,opt}$ | dimensionless |
| 12 | $SFC = (m_f / W_{net}) \times 3600$ | Specific Fuel Consumption | kg/kWh |
| 13 | $\eta_{propulsive} = K.E. \ propulsive / W_{net}$ | Propulsive efficiency (jet engines) | dimensionless |
| 14 | $\eta_{overall} = \eta_{th} \times \eta_{propulsive}$ | Overall / propulsive thermal efficiency | dimensionless |
| 15 | $\eta_{cycle} = \eta_{th} \times \eta_{mech} \times \eta_{gen}$ | Plant thermal efficiency | dimensionless |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The thermodynamic analysis of gas turbines has profound real-world applications:

- **Aviation Jet Engines:** Turbojet, turbofan, turboshaft — all are Brayton-cycle devices with varying degrees of cycle sophistication (reheat, regeneration, intercooling).
- **Industrial Power Generation:** Simple-cycle and combined-cycle gas turbine power plants dominate modern electricity markets.
- **Marine Propulsion:** Gas turbine-powered naval vessels (e.g., U.S. Navy destroyers) leverage high power-to-weight ratios.
- **Gas Pipeline Compressors:** Stationary gas turbines drive centrifugal compressors for natural gas pipelines.
- **Combined Heat and Power (CHP):** Industrial cogeneration systems use gas turbine exhaust heat for process steam.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineer's Takeaway:** The Brayton cycle analysis is the *foundation* on which all advanced gas turbine cycles (regenerative, reheat, intercooled) are built. Mastering this module is essential for understanding more complex cycles in Module 2 and 3.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Ideal Brayton Cycle Thermal Efficiency

The thermal efficiency of any heat engine cycle is defined as:

$$\eta_{th} = \frac{Net \ Work \ Output}{Heat \ Supplied} = \frac{W_{net}}{Q_{in}}$$

**Step 1:** Express net work output.

The net work output equals the turbine work minus the compressor work:

$$W_{net} = W_T - W_C$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the expressions in terms of specific enthalpies.

$$W_T = h_3 - h_4 = C_p (T_3 - T_4)$$

$$W_C = h_2 - h_1 = C_p (T_2 - T_1)$$

**Step 3:** Substitute back into the net work expression.

$$\begin{aligned}
W_{net} &= C_p (T_3 - T_4) - C_p (T_2 - T_1) \\
W_{net} &= C_p [(T_3 - T_4) - (T_2 - T_1)] \\
W_{net} &= C_p [(T_3 - T_2) - (T_4 - T_1)]
\end{aligned}$$

This last form is insightful: the net work equals the heat supplied (T₃ - T₂) minus the heat rejected (T₄ - T₁).

**Step 4:** Express the heat input.

$$Q_{in} = h_3 - h_2 = C_p (T_3 - T_2)$$

**Step 5:** Form the efficiency ratio.

$$\eta_{th} = \frac{W_{net}}{Q_{in}} = \frac{C_p [(T_3 - T_2) - (T_4 - T_1)]}{C_p (T_3 - T_2)}$$

**Step 6:** Simplify by canceling Cₚ.

$$\begin{aligned}
\eta_{th} &= \frac{(T_3 - T_2) - (T_4 - T_1)}{T_3 - T_2} \\
\eta_{th} &= 1 - \frac{T_4 - T_1}{T_3 - T_2}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 7:** Apply the isentropic relations to express T₄ and T₂ in terms of T₃ and T₁.

From the isentropic compression relation (process 1→2):

$$T_2 = T_1 \cdot r_p^{(\gamma - 1)/\gamma}$$

From the isentropic expansion relation (process 3→4):

$$T_4 = T_3 \cdot \left(\frac{1}{r_p}\right)^{(\gamma - 1)/\gamma} = \frac{T_3}{r_p^{(\gamma - 1)/\gamma}}$$

Let us denote the isentropic exponent for convenience:

$$\alpha = \frac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma}$$

So $T_2 = T_1 \cdot r_p^{\alpha}$ and $T_4 = T_3 / r_p^{\alpha}$.

**Step 8:** Substitute these relations into the efficiency expression.

$$\begin{aligned}
\eta_{th} &= 1 - \frac{(T_3 / r_p^{\alpha}) - T_1}{T_3 - (T_1 \cdot r_p^{\alpha})} \\
\eta_{th} &= 1 - \frac{T_1 [(T_3 / (T_1 \cdot r_p^{\alpha})) - 1]}{T_1 [(T_3 / T_1) - r_p^{\alpha}]} \\
\eta_{th} &= 1 - \frac{(T_3 / T_1) / r_p^{\alpha} - 1}{(T_3 / T_1) - r_p^{\alpha}}
\end{aligned}$$

To proceed cleanly, multiply numerator and denominator of the fraction by $r_p^{\alpha}$:

$$\eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{T_3 - T_1 \cdot r_p^{\alpha}}{(T_3 - T_1 \cdot r_p^{\alpha}) \cdot r_p^{\alpha}} \cdot \frac{r_p^{\alpha}}{1}$$

After careful algebraic manipulation (factoring out $T_1$ from numerator and denominator):

$$\begin{aligned}
\eta_{th} &= 1 - \frac{1}{r_p^{\alpha}} \\
\eta_{th} &= 1 - \frac{1}{r_p^{(\gamma - 1)/\gamma}}
\end{aligned}$$

**Final result:** For an ideal Brayton cycle operating with cold air-standard assumptions, the thermal efficiency is:

$$\boxed{\eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{1}{r_p^{(\gamma - 1)/\gamma}}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The key insight:** The ideal Brayton cycle efficiency depends **only on the pressure ratio** and **γ**, NOT on the turbine inlet temperature T₃. This is fundamentally different from the Carnot cycle and the Rankine cycle.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Optimum Pressure Ratio for Maximum Net Work

To find the pressure ratio that maximizes net work output, we differentiate $W_{net}$ with respect to $r_p$ and set the derivative to zero.

**Step 1:** Express the net work in terms of $r_p$.

$$W_{net} = C_p [(T_3 - T_4) - (T_2 - T_1)]$$

Using $T_2 = T_1 \cdot r_p^{\alpha}$ and $T_4 = T_3 / r_p^{\alpha}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
W_{net} &= C_p \left[ \left(T_3 - \frac{T_3}{r_p^{\alpha}}\right) - (T_1 \cdot r_p^{\alpha} - T_1) \right] \\
W_{net} &= C_p \left[ T_3 \left(1 - \frac{1}{r_p^{\alpha}}\right) - T_1 (r_p^{\alpha} - 1) \right]
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2:** Differentiate with respect to $r_p$.

$$\frac{dW_{net}}{dr_p} = C_p \left[ T_3 \cdot \frac{\alpha}{r_p^{\alpha + 1}} - T_1 \cdot \alpha \cdot r_p^{\alpha - 1} \right]$$

**Step 3:** Set the derivative to zero.

$$C_p \left[ \frac{T_3 \cdot \alpha}{r_p^{\alpha + 1}} - T_1 \cdot \alpha \cdot r_p^{\alpha - 1} \right] = 0$$

$$\frac{T_3}{r_p^{\alpha + 1}} = T_1 \cdot r_p^{\alpha - 1}$$

$$T_3 = T_1 \cdot r_p^{2\alpha}$$

$$r_p^{2\alpha} = \frac{T_3}{T_1}$$

**Step 4:** Solve for $r_{p,opt}$.

$$\begin{aligned}
r_p &= \left(\frac{T_3}{T_1}\right)^{1 / (2\alpha)} \\
r_p &= \left(\frac{T_3}{T_1}\right)^{\gamma / [2(\gamma - 1)]}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{r_{p,opt} = \left(\frac{T_3}{T_1}\right)^{\gamma / [2(\gamma - 1)]}}$$

**Step 5:** Substitute this optimum pressure ratio into the thermal efficiency formula.

Substituting $r_{p,opt}^{2\alpha} = T_3/T_1$ means $r_{p,opt}^{\alpha} = \sqrt{T_3/T_1}$, hence:

$$\eta_{th,opt} = 1 - \frac{1}{r_{p,opt}^{\alpha}} = 1 - \sqrt{\frac{T_1}{T_3}}$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{th,opt} = 1 - \sqrt{\frac{T_1}{T_3}}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering interpretation:** At low pressure ratios, increasing $r_p$ dramatically increases both work and efficiency. Beyond $r_{p,opt}$, additional increases in pressure ratio still raise efficiency but **reduce net work output**. This is why real engines operate at moderate pressure ratios (10–25 for aviation, 5–15 for industrial) to maximize work per unit mass flow.

## 3.3 Effect of Component Inefficiencies on the Cycle

For real (non-ideal) gas turbines, **isentropic efficiencies** are introduced:

**Compressor isentropic efficiency:**

$$\eta_C = \frac{h_{2s} - h_1}{h_{2a} - h_1} = \frac{T_{2s} - T_1}{T_{2a} - T_1}$$

Where $T_{2s}$ = ideal isentropic exit temperature, $T_{2a}$ = actual exit temperature.

**Turbine isentropic efficiency:**

$$\eta_T = \frac{h_3 - h_{4a}}{h_3 - h_{4s}} = \frac{T_3 - T_{4a}}{T_3 - T_{4s}}$$

**Pressure loss in combustion chamber:**

$$\Delta P_{cc} = P_2 - P_3 \quad \text{or} \quad \text{often expressed as \% loss} = \frac{P_2 - P_3}{P_2} \times 100\%$$

Typical values: $\eta_C \approx 0.85 - 0.92$, $\eta_T \approx 0.88 - 0.93$, $\Delta P_{cc} \approx 2 - 5\%$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key insight:** Compressor isentropic efficiency has the **largest effect** on net work output because any inefficiency in the compressor directly increases the work that the turbine must supply to drive it.

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example

**Problem:** Air enters the compressor of a gas turbine at 100 kPa and 300 K. The pressure ratio is 8, and the turbine inlet temperature is 1300 K. Assuming an ideal Brayton cycle with cold air-standard assumptions (γ = 1.4, Cp = 1.005 kJ/kg·K), determine:
(a) The temperatures at all four state points.
(b) The net work output per kg of air.
(c) The thermal efficiency.
(d) The specific fuel consumption, assuming a calorific value of 43,000 kJ/kg.

**Solution:**

**Given:** $P_1 = 100$ kPa, $T_1 = 300$ K, $r_p = 8$, $T_3 = 1300$ K, $\gamma = 1.4$, $C_p = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K.

**Step 1:** Calculate the isentropic exponent.

$$\alpha = \frac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma} = \frac{0.4}{1.4} = 0.2857$$

**Step 2:** Find $T_2$.

$$T_2 = T_1 \cdot r_p^{\alpha} = 300 \cdot 8^{0.2857}$$

Calculating $8^{0.2857}$:

$$8^{0.2857} = e^{0.2857 \cdot \ln 8} = e^{0.2857 \cdot 2.0794} = e^{0.5941} = 1.8114$$

$$T_2 = 300 \times 1.8114 = 543.4 \text{ K}$$

**Step 3:** Find $T_4$.

$$T_4 = \frac{T_3}{r_p^{\alpha}} = \frac{1300}{1.8114} = 717.7 \text{ K}$$

**Step 4:** Calculate the compressor work.

$$W_C = C_p (T_2 - T_1) = 1.005 \times (543.4 - 300) = 1.005 \times 243.4 = 244.6 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Step 5:** Calculate the turbine work.

$$W_T = C_p (T_3 - T_4) = 1.005 \times (1300 - 717.7) = 1.005 \times 582.3 = 585.2 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Step 6:** Calculate net work.

$$W_{net} = W_T - W_C = 585.2 - 244.6 = 340.6 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Step 7:** Calculate heat input.

$$Q_{in} = C_p (T_3 - T_2) = 1.005 \times (1300 - 543.4) = 1.005 \times 756.6 = 760.4 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Step 8:** Calculate thermal efficiency.

$$\eta_{th} = \frac{W_{net}}{Q_{in}} = \frac{340.6}{760.4} = 0.4479 = 44.79\%$$

**Verification using formula:**

$$\eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{1}{r_p^{\alpha}} = 1 - \frac{1}{1.8114} = 1 - 0.5521 = 0.4479 = 44.79\%$$

✓ Matches.

**Step 9:** Calculate specific fuel consumption.

The fuel-air ratio $f = Q_{in} / \text{CV} = 760.4 / 43000 = 0.01768$ kg fuel / kg air.

$$SFC = \frac{f \times 3600}{W_{net}} = \frac{0.01768 \times 3600}{340.6} = \frac{63.65}{340.6} = 0.1869 \text{ kg/kWh}$$

**Final Answers:**
- $T_1 = 300$ K, $T_2 = 543.4$ K, $T_3 = 1300$ K, $T_4 = 717.7$ K
- $W_{net} = 340.6$ kJ/kg
- $\eta_{th} = 44.79\%$
- $SFC = 0.187$ kg/kWh

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram of an Open-Cycle Gas Turbine Plant

The following Mermaid block-level functional architecture illustrates the thermodynamic flow through the major components:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Ambient Air Inlet T1 P1] --> B[Air Filter and Inlet Duct]
    B --> C[Compressor Wc input]
    C --> D[Compressed Air T2 P2]
    D --> E[Combustion Chamber]
    F[Fuel Supply] --> E
    E --> G[Hot Combustion Gases T3 P3]
    G --> H[Turbine Wt output]
    H --> I[Exhaust Gases T4 P1]
    I --> J[Atmosphere]
    H --> K[Shaft Output Wnet]
    C -.->|Drives| L[Gearbox]
    K --> L
    L --> M[Generator or Load]
```

## 4.2 Thermodynamic State Point Diagram (T-s Diagram)

The ideal Brayton cycle on a Temperature-Entropy (T-s) diagram:

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ST1["State 1: Inlet"]
        S1["T1 LOW, P1 LOW, S1 LOW"]
    end
    subgraph ST2["State 2: Compressor Exit"]
        S2["T2 HIGH, P2 HIGH, S2 EQUALS S1"]
    end
    subgraph ST3["State 3: Turbine Inlet"]
        S3["T3 HIGHEST, P3 EQUALS P2, S3 HIGH"]
    end
    subgraph ST4["State 4: Turbine Exit"]
        S4["T4 MODERATE, P4 EQUALS P1, S4 EQUALS S3"]
    end
    S1 -->|Isentropic Compression 1 to 2| S2
    S2 -->|Constant Pressure Heat Addition 2 to 3| S3
    S3 -->|Isentropic Expansion 3 to 4| S4
    S4 -->|Constant Pressure Heat Rejection 4 to 1| S1
```

## 4.3 P-v Diagram of the Ideal Brayton Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1["State 1 LOW P HIGH V"] -->|Isentropic Compression| A2["State 2 HIGH P LOW V"]
    A2 -->|Constant P Heat Add| A3["State 3 HIGH P VERY HIGH V"]
    A3 -->|Isentropic Expansion| A4["State 4 LOW P VERY HIGH V"]
    A4 -->|Constant P Heat Reject| A1
```

## 4.4 Work and Heat Interaction Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph INPUT["Energy Inputs"]
        Q1["Qin Combustion Energy Cv x mf"]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUTS["Energy Outputs"]
        Wnet["Wnet Useful Shaft Work"]
        Qout["Qout Exhaust Heat Loss"]
    end
    subgraph LOSSES["Performance Penalties"]
        L1["Compressor Isentropic Loss 1 minus eta_c"]
        L2["Turbine Isentropic Loss 1 minus eta_t"]
        L3["Combustion Chamber Pressure Drop delta P"]
        L4["Mechanical and Generator Losses"]
    end
    Q1 --> CYCL["Brayton Cycle Core"]
    CYCL --> Wnet
    CYCL --> Qout
    L1 --> CYCL
    L2 --> CYCL
    L3 --> CYCL
    L4 --> CYCL
```

## 4.5 Performance Trade-off Between Work and Efficiency

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PR["Pressure Ratio rp increases"]
        PR1["Low rp below optimum"]
        PR2["Optimum rp maximum net work"]
        PR3["High rp above optimum"]
    end
    subgraph W["Net Work Output"]
        W1["Increasing then Decreasing"]
    end
    subgraph EFF["Thermal Efficiency"]
        E1["Monotonically Increasing toward Carnot limit"]
    end
    PR1 --> W1
    PR2 --> W1
    PR3 --> W1
    PR1 --> E1
    PR2 --> E1
    PR3 --> E1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The trade-off curve shows the central design dilemma in gas turbine engineering — the pressure ratio that gives the highest *efficiency* is not the same as the pressure ratio that gives the highest *net work output*. Modern aviation engines are typically designed closer to the optimum efficiency ratio, while industrial simple-cycle plants are designed closer to the optimum work ratio for maximum power output per unit mass flow.
