# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Similar Type]`

**Q: Define the term "pressure ratio" as applied to a gas turbine. How does it affect the thermal efficiency of an ideal Brayton cycle?**

**Model Answer:**

**Pressure Ratio Definition:** The pressure ratio ($r_p$) of a gas turbine is defined as the ratio of the air pressure at the compressor exit (P₂) to the pressure of air entering the compressor (P₁). Mathematically:

$$r_p = \frac{P_2}{P_1}$$

For an ideal gas turbine, since the combustion chamber operates at constant pressure, $P_3 = P_2$, and for the open cycle, $P_4 = P_1$, so:

$$r_p = \frac{P_2}{P_1} = \frac{P_3}{P_4}$$

**Effect on Thermal Efficiency:**

For an ideal Brayton cycle, the thermal efficiency is given by:

$$\eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{1}{r_p^{(\gamma - 1)/\gamma}}$$

This shows that the thermal efficiency is a monotonically increasing function of the pressure ratio. Higher pressure ratios yield higher efficiencies, approaching the Carnot limit asymptotically as $r_p \to \infty$. For example:

- $r_p = 5$: $\eta_{th} = 1 - (5)^{-0.2857} \approx 31.6\%$
- $r_p = 10$: $\eta_{th} \approx 40.5\%$
- $r_p = 20$: $\eta_{th} \approx 48.2\%$

However, in practical engines, very high pressure ratios cause the compressor work to exceed a significant portion of the turbine work, reducing net work output. Hence, an optimum pressure ratio is selected based on the application.

**(Total: 3 marks — 1.5 for definition, 1.5 for effect explanation)**

---

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Similar Type]`

**Q: Explain the difference between an open-cycle and a closed-cycle gas turbine with suitable diagrams.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Open Cycle | Closed Cycle |
|---------|-----------|--------------|
| Working Fluid | Air from atmosphere; same air is not reused | Same working fluid (air or other gas) recirculated continuously |
| Heat Source | Internal combustion using fuel | External heat exchanger (e.g., nuclear, solar, fossil-fired heater) |
| Heat Rejection | Atmospheric exhaust | External cooler/heat exchanger |
| Pressure of Cycle | Atmospheric at both inlet and exhaust | Operating pressure may differ from atmospheric |
| Power Density | Higher (uses atmospheric oxygen directly) | Lower (requires heat exchangers) |
| Typical Use | Aviation, industrial peaking plants | Nuclear power, solar power, specialized industrial |

In an **open cycle**, ambient air is drawn in, compressed, heated by fuel combustion, expanded through the turbine, and exhausted to the atmosphere. The turbine exhaust products are not recirculated.

In a **closed cycle**, the working fluid is contained within the system. After leaving the turbine, the fluid passes through a cooler where heat is rejected to a coolant, then is recompressed and reheated in an external heat exchanger.

**Key distinction:** The open cycle requires the working fluid to be air (because combustion needs oxygen), while the closed cycle can use any gas (helium, nitrogen, CO₂) because heating is external.

**(Total: 3 marks — comparison table with application examples)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Pattern]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a T-s and P-v diagram, describe the four processes of an ideal Brayton cycle. State clearly the assumptions made for the cold air-standard analysis.

**(b) [7 Marks]** In a gas turbine plant, air enters the compressor at 1 bar and 300 K. The pressure ratio is 9, and the maximum temperature in the cycle is 1200 K. Assuming the cycle is an ideal Brayton cycle with γ = 1.4 and Cp = 1.005 kJ/kg·K, determine:
1. The temperature at the end of the isentropic expansion process.
2. The net work output per kg of air.
3. The thermal efficiency.
4. The mass flow rate of air required for a net power output of 5000 kW.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a):**

The ideal Brayton cycle consists of four internally reversible processes:

**Process 1-2: Isentropic Compression**

Air is compressed in the compressor from state 1 (T₁, P₁) to state 2 (T₂, P₂). The process is adiabatic and reversible (isentropic). On the T-s diagram, this is a vertical line moving upward; on the P-v diagram, it is a curve of the form $Pv^{\gamma} = \text{constant}$.

$$Pv^{\gamma} = \text{constant}, \quad Tv^{\gamma - 1} = \text{constant}$$

**Process 2-3: Constant Pressure Heat Addition**

In the combustion chamber, fuel is burned at approximately constant pressure (P₃ = P₂), raising the temperature from T₂ to T₃ (the turbine inlet temperature). On the T-s and P-v diagrams, this is a horizontal line moving rightward.

$$P = \text{constant}, \quad \frac{V}{T} = \text{constant}$$

**Process 3-4: Isentropic Expansion**

Hot combustion gases expand through the turbine from state 3 (T₃, P₃) to state 4 (T₄, P₄ = P₁). This is again adiabatic and reversible.

**Process 4-1: Constant Pressure Heat Rejection**

In an open cycle, this represents the exhaust of gases to the atmosphere. Heat is effectively rejected from the working fluid to the surroundings. P₄ = P₁.

**Assumptions for Cold Air-Standard Analysis:**

1. Air is the working fluid throughout the cycle and behaves as an ideal gas.
2. The specific heats Cp and Cv are constant and evaluated at room temperature (300 K).
3. The combustion process is replaced by heat addition from an external source.
4. All processes are internally reversible; compression and expansion are adiabatic (isentropic).
5. Pressure drops in the combustion chamber and inlet/exhaust ducts are neglected.
6. Kinetic and potential energy changes are negligible.
7. Turbine and compressor isentropic efficiencies are 100% (ideal cycle).

**(Full marks for diagram + assumptions — 7 marks)**

**Part (b):**

**Given:** $P_1 = 1$ bar = 100 kPa, $T_1 = 300$ K, $r_p = 9$, $T_3 = 1200$ K, $\gamma = 1.4$, $C_p = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K.

**Step 1:** Calculate the isentropic exponent.

$$\alpha = \frac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma} = \frac{0.4}{1.4} = 0.2857$$

**Step 2:** Find $T_2$ and $T_4$.

$$T_2 = T_1 \cdot r_p^{\alpha} = 300 \times 9^{0.2857}$$

Computing $9^{0.2857}$:

$$9^{0.2857} = e^{0.2857 \cdot \ln 9} = e^{0.2857 \cdot 2.1972} = e^{0.6278} = 1.8736$$

$$T_2 = 300 \times 1.8736 = 562.1 \text{ K}$$

$$T_4 = \frac{T_3}{r_p^{\alpha}} = \frac{1200}{1.8736} = 640.5 \text{ K}$$

**Step 3:** Calculate compressor work.

$$W_C = C_p (T_2 - T_1) = 1.005 \times (562.1 - 300) = 1.005 \times 262.1 = 263.4 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Step 4:** Calculate turbine work.

$$W_T = C_p (T_3 - T_4) = 1.005 \times (1200 - 640.5) = 1.005 \times 559.5 = 562.3 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Step 5:** Calculate net work output.

$$W_{net} = W_T - W_C = 562.3 - 263.4 = 298.9 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Step 6:** Calculate thermal efficiency.

$$\eta_{th} = \frac{W_{net}}{Q_{in}} = 1 - \frac{1}{r_p^{\alpha}} = 1 - \frac{1}{1.8736} = 1 - 0.5337 = 0.4663 = 46.63\%$$

**Step 7:** Calculate mass flow rate.

Net power $\dot{W}_{net} = \dot{m} \times W_{net}$

$$5000 \text{ kW} = \dot{m} \times 298.9 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

$$\dot{m} = \frac{5000}{298.9} = 16.73 \text{ kg/s}$$

**Final Answers:**
1. $T_4 = 640.5$ K
2. $W_{net} = 298.9$ kJ/kg
3. $\eta_{th} = 46.63\%$
4. $\dot{m} = 16.73$ kg/s

---

### Question B (Choice 2) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Pattern]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive an expression for the thermal efficiency of an ideal Brayton cycle as a function of pressure ratio. Show that the efficiency increases monotonically with pressure ratio, but the net work output has a maximum at an optimum pressure ratio given by:

$$r_{p,opt} = \left(\frac{T_3}{T_1}\right)^{\gamma / [2(\gamma - 1)]}$$

**(b) [7 Marks]** A gas turbine operates with a pressure ratio of 12 and a turbine inlet temperature of 1500 K. The compressor and turbine isentropic efficiencies are 0.88 and 0.92, respectively, and there is a 3% pressure drop in the combustion chamber. If T₁ = 300 K, P₁ = 1 bar, and Cp = 1.005 kJ/kg·K, calculate:
1. The actual temperatures at the compressor and turbine exits.
2. The actual net work output per kg of air.
3. The actual thermal efficiency.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a):**

The detailed derivation is provided in Section 3.1 and 3.2 above. Key results:

$$\eta_{th,ideal} = 1 - \frac{1}{r_p^{(\gamma - 1)/\gamma}}$$

Since the exponent $(\gamma - 1)/\gamma > 0$, as $r_p$ increases, $r_p^{(\gamma - 1)/\gamma}$ increases, so $1/r_p^{(\gamma - 1)/\gamma}$ decreases, and hence $\eta_{th}$ increases monotonically.

For net work, expressing $W_{net}$ in terms of $r_p$ and differentiating:

$$W_{net} = C_p \left[ T_3 \left(1 - \frac{1}{r_p^{\alpha}}\right) - T_1 (r_p^{\alpha} - 1) \right]$$

Setting $\frac{dW_{net}}{dr_p} = 0$ yields:

$$r_{p,opt} = \left(\frac{T_3}{T_1}\right)^{\gamma / [2(\gamma - 1)]}$$

**Part (b):**

**Given:** $r_p = 12$, $T_3 = 1500$ K, $\eta_C = 0.88$, $\eta_T = 0.92$, $\Delta P_{cc} = 3\%$, $T_1 = 300$ K, $P_1 = 1$ bar, $C_p = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K.

**Step 1:** Compute isentropic temperatures for the ideal case.

$$\alpha = \frac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma} = \frac{0.4}{1.4} = 0.2857$$

$$T_{2s} = T_1 \cdot r_p^{\alpha} = 300 \times 12^{0.2857}$$

Computing $12^{0.2857}$:

$$12^{0.2857} = e^{0.2857 \cdot \ln 12} = e^{0.2857 \cdot 2.4849} = e^{0.7100} = 2.0339$$

$$T_{2s} = 300 \times 2.0339 = 610.2 \text{ K}$$

$$T_{4s} = \frac{T_3}{r_p^{\alpha}} = \frac{1500}{2.0339} = 737.5 \text{ K}$$

**Step 2:** Calculate actual compressor exit temperature.

Using the compressor isentropic efficiency definition:

$$\eta_C = \frac{T_{2s} - T_1}{T_{2a} - T_1}$$

$$0.88 = \frac{610.2 - 300}{T_{2a} - 300}$$

$$T_{2a} - 300 = \frac{310.2}{0.88} = 352.5$$

$$T_{2a} = 652.5 \text{ K}$$

**Step 3:** Determine pressures through the cycle.

$$P_2 = P_1 \cdot r_p = 100 \times 12 = 1200 \text{ kPa}$$

With 3% pressure drop in combustion chamber:

$$P_3 = P_2 \times (1 - 0.03) = 1200 \times 0.97 = 1164 \text{ kPa}$$

For the turbine to expand to atmospheric pressure:

$$P_4 = P_1 = 100 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step 4:** Calculate the actual turbine expansion ratio.

$$r_{p,turbine} = \frac{P_3}{P_4} = \frac{1164}{100} = 11.64$$

**Step 5:** Find isentropic turbine exit temperature at the actual expansion ratio.

$$T_{4s,actual} = T_3 \cdot \left(\frac{1}{r_{p,turbine}}\right)^{\alpha} = 1500 \times \left(\frac{1}{11.64}\right)^{0.2857}$$

Computing $(1/11.64)^{0.2857}$:

$$(11.64)^{0.2857} = e^{0.2857 \cdot \ln 11.64} = e^{0.2857 \cdot 2.4545} = e^{0.7013} = 2.0165$$

$$T_{4s,actual} = \frac{1500}{2.0165} = 743.9 \text{ K}$$

**Step 6:** Calculate actual turbine exit temperature.

Using turbine isentropic efficiency:

$$\eta_T = \frac{T_3 - T_{4a}}{T_3 - T_{4s,actual}}$$

$$0.92 = \frac{1500 - T_{4a}}{1500 - 743.9} = \frac{1500 - T_{4a}}{756.1}$$

$$1500 - T_{4a} = 0.92 \times 756.1 = 695.6$$

$$T_{4a} = 1500 - 695.6 = 804.4 \text{ K}$$

**Step 7:** Calculate compressor and turbine works (per kg of air).

$$W_C = C_p (T_{2a} - T_1) = 1.005 \times (652.5 - 300) = 1.005 \times 352.5 = 354.3 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

$$W_T = C_p (T_3 - T_{4a}) = 1.005 \times (1500 - 804.4) = 1.005 \times 695.6 = 699.1 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Step 8:** Calculate net work output.

$$W_{net} = W_T - W_C = 699.1 - 354.3 = 344.8 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Step 9:** Calculate heat input.

$$Q_{in} = C_p (T_3 - T_{2a}) = 1.005 \times (1500 - 652.5) = 1.005 \times 847.5 = 851.7 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Step 10:** Calculate actual thermal efficiency.

$$\eta_{th,actual} = \frac{W_{net}}{Q_{in}} = \frac{344.8}{851.7} = 0.4048 = 40.48\%$$

**Final Answers:**
1. $T_{2a} = 652.5$ K, $T_{4a} = 804.4$ K
2. $W_{net} = 344.8$ kJ/kg
3. $\eta_{th,actual} = 40.48\%$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Using $T_{2s}$ instead of $T_{2a}$:** When component efficiencies are given, students often forget to apply the efficiency formula and compute only the ideal (isentropic) exit temperatures. This loses 2-3 marks immediately.
> 2. **Forgetting combustion chamber pressure drop:** Many students assume $P_3 = P_2$ even when a pressure drop is specified. The turbine must then expand across a different pressure ratio than the compressor. Losing 1-2 marks here.
> 3. **Wrong sign convention:** Some students write $W_C = C_p(T_1 - T_2)$, giving negative compressor work. The convention is that work *input to fluid* is positive; compressor work output is then subtracted.
> 4. **Mixing up definitions:** Some texts define $\eta_C$ as $(T_2 - T_1)/(T_{2s} - T_1)$ (i.e., reciprocal). Always check the textbook convention being used.
> 5. **Unit errors:** Air mass flow in kg/s, work in kJ/kg, power in kW — keep all units consistent in SI.
> 6. **Algebraic slip in 9^0.2857:** Students often make calculation errors. Always show the intermediate $e^{\alpha \ln x}$ step for full credit.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Master These Before the KTU Exam:**

- ✅ **Brayton Cycle Basics:** Four processes — isentropic compression, constant-P heat addition, isentropic expansion, constant-P heat rejection.

- ✅ **Master Formula:** $\eta_{th,ideal} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r_p^{(\gamma - 1)/\gamma}}$ — efficiency depends **only** on pressure ratio and γ (for ideal cycle), NOT on TIT.

- ✅ **State Point Temperatures:** $T_2 = T_1 \cdot r_p^{\alpha}$ and $T_4 = T_3 / r_p^{\alpha}$, where $\alpha = (\gamma - 1)/\gamma = 0.2857$ for air.

- ✅ **Net Work vs Efficiency Trade-off:** Efficiency increases monotonically with $r_p$; net work has a maximum at $r_{p,opt} = (T_3/T_1)^{\gamma/[2(\gamma - 1)]}$.

- ✅ **Real Cycle Effects:**
  * Compressor efficiency reduces net work (compressor consumes more).
  * Turbine efficiency reduces net work (turbine produces less).
  * Combustion chamber pressure drop increases compressor work and reduces turbine work.
  * Heat loss through casing reduces TIT and hence efficiency.

- ✅ **SFC Formula:** $SFC = \dfrac{m_f \times 3600}{\dot{W}_{net}}$ with units kg/kWh. Always convert fuel-air ratio properly.

- ✅ **Cold Air-Standard Assumptions:** Constant Cp and Cv, ideal gas behavior, no pressure drops, perfect components, neglect KE/PE.

- ✅ **Open vs Closed Cycle:** Open cycle uses air from atmosphere and exhausts back; closed cycle recirculates working fluid and uses external heating.

- ✅ **Common Defaults in KTU Problems:** $T_1 = 300$ K, $P_1 = 100$ kPa, $\gamma = 1.4$, $C_p = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K, fuel CV = 43,000 kJ/kg.

- ✅ **Sign Convention:** Heat added and work output are positive. Compressor work is an input to the cycle (subtract it from turbine work to get net work).

- ✅ **Numerical Strategy:** Always start by computing $\alpha = (\gamma - 1)/\gamma$, then $r_p^{\alpha}$, then state temperatures, then work and heat terms in order.

- ✅ **Unit Conversion Tricks:** 1 kJ/kg × 1 kg/s = 1 kW. Power = mass flow × specific work.

- ✅ **High-Yield Insights for KTU:**
  * Doubling the pressure ratio does NOT double the efficiency — the relationship is logarithmic.
  * Higher TIT increases net work substantially without changing ideal-cycle efficiency.
  * The compressor consumes roughly **40-55%** of the turbine work in a typical gas turbine.
  * Modern aviation engines use pressure ratios of 30-50; industrial simple cycles use 5-15.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Efficiency
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET741 - GAS TURBINE AND JET PROPULSION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Efficiency

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is "Efficiency" in a Gas Turbine & Jet Propulsion Context?

In a gas turbine engine, **efficiency** is a dimensionless metric (always between **0 and 1**, or expressed as a **percentage 0%–100%**) that quantifies *how effectively* the engine converts one form of energy into another, while accounting for unavoidable losses at each stage of energy transformation.

Because a gas turbine is a chain of energy converters — *Atmosphere → Compressor → Combustor → Turbine → Nozzle → Atmosphere* — there is **no single "efficiency"** for the whole machine. Instead, a hierarchy of efficiencies is defined, each isolating losses in one specific sub-system.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *Efficiency* of a gas turbine / jet propulsion engine is the ratio of the **desired (useful) energy output** to the **energy input** (or energy supplied), measured at a specific stage of the thermodynamic cycle, normalised by the corresponding **isentropic (ideal) work** to yield a number ≤ 1.

### 1.2 The Three Pillars of Efficiency in Module 1

For Module 1 of PEMET741, students are expected to master the following efficiency families:

1. **Component (Stage) Efficiencies** — Isentropic efficiencies of compressor ($\eta_C$), turbine ($\eta_T$), and nozzle ($\eta_N$). These isolate **internal irreversibilities** within a single device.
2. **Cycle Efficiencies** — Thermal efficiency ($\eta_{th}$) of the Brayton cycle, with and without regeneration, reheating, and intercooling. These measure the **Second-Law quality** of the closed/open cycle.
3. **Propulsive Efficiencies** — Propulsive efficiency ($\eta_P$), thermal efficiency of the propulsive cycle ($\eta_{th,p}$), and **overall efficiency** ($\eta_O$). These are exclusive to **jet propulsion** and measure thrust-producing quality.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Efficiency" umbrella in PEMET741 Module 1:**
> - $\eta_C$ (compressor) — $\eta_T$ (turbine) — $\eta_N$ (nozzle) — $\eta_m$ (mechanical) — $\eta_{comb}$ (combustion)
> - $\eta_{th}$ (thermal, Brayton) — $\eta_{th,R}$ (with regenerator)
> - $\eta_P$ (propulsive) — $\eta_{th,p}$ (propulsive thermal) — $\eta_O$ (overall) — $\eta_{cyc}$ (cycle)

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Pipeline with Leaks" View

Imagine you are pumping water from a low tank to a high tank using a pump, and then letting it fall through a turbine to generate electricity.

- **No system is perfect.** Friction in pipes, heat loss in the pump, and turbulence in the turbine all *leak* energy.
- **Isentropic efficiency** is the ratio of *what you actually achieved* to *what a perfect, lossless system would have achieved*. It is the **"leak-proof-ness"** rating of each component.
- **Thermal efficiency** is how well the *whole pipeline* converts *fuel energy* (input) into *useful shaft work or thrust* (output).
- **Propulsive efficiency** answers the *jet-specific* question: *Of the energy carried away by the exhaust jet, how much is converted into useful thrust pushing the aircraft forward?*

> [!TIP]
> **Intuition Hook for KTU Viva:**
> "Higher efficiency = less waste heat / less waste kinetic energy = more useful work per kg of fuel burned." In the era of **net-zero aviation (SAF, hydrogen propulsion)**, every percentage point of cycle efficiency directly reduces specific fuel consumption (SFC).

### 1.4 Standard Metrics Students Must Know

| Symbol | Quantity | Range / Typical Value | Physical Meaning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $\eta_C$ | Compressor isentropic efficiency | 0.80 – 0.92 | Work actually used vs ideal isentropic compression work |
| $\eta_T$ | Turbine isentropic efficiency | 0.85 – 0.94 | Work actually extracted vs ideal isentropic expansion work |
| $\eta_N$ | Nozzle efficiency | 0.92 – 0.98 | Kinetic energy actually gained vs ideal isentropic expansion |
| $\eta_m$ | Mechanical efficiency | 0.95 – 0.99 | Shaft power delivered vs turbine work produced |
| $\eta_{comb}$ | Combustion efficiency | 0.95 – 0.99 | Chemical energy actually released vs fuel LHV |
| $\eta_{th}$ | Cycle thermal efficiency | 0.25 – 0.45 | Net work output / heat input |
| $\eta_P$ | Propulsive efficiency | 0.30 – 0.60 | Thrust power / jet kinetic energy rate |
| $\eta_O$ | Overall efficiency | 0.20 – 0.38 | Thrust power / fuel energy rate |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory KTU Symbol Convention:** The 2024 scheme strictly uses $\eta_C$, $\eta_T$, $\eta_N$, $\eta_{th}$, $\eta_P$, $\eta_O$ as the canonical symbols. Always define them in your answer scripts — 1 mark per term is routinely lost when students use "n_t" or "eff_T" without declaration.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Stage 1 — Isentropic Efficiencies of Individual Components

The gas turbine cycle is a *chain* of devices. Each device has an *ideal* (isentropic, reversible-adiabatic) performance ceiling. The **isentropic efficiency** of a device is defined as the ratio of the *actual* work/energy change to the *ideal* (isentropic) work/energy change between the same inlet state and the same exit pressure.

**A. Compressor Isentropic Efficiency ($\eta_C$):**

The compressor consumes work. An *actual* compressor requires **more** work than the ideal isentropic compressor for the same pressure rise (because of friction, turbulence, blade losses, and clearance losses).

$$\eta_C = \frac{W_{C,isentropic}}{W_{C,actual}} = \frac{h_{2s} - h_1}{h_{2a} - h_1}$$

where:
- $h_1$ = enthalpy at compressor inlet
- $h_{2s}$ = enthalpy at compressor outlet **if the process were isentropic** (i.e., at $p_2$ but $s_{2s}=s_1$)
- $h_{2a}$ = actual enthalpy at compressor outlet (which is *higher* than $h_{2s}$ because of irreversibilities)

**B. Turbine Isentropic Efficiency ($\eta_T$):**

The turbine *produces* work. An *actual* turbine produces **less** work than the ideal isentropic turbine for the same pressure drop (because the expansion is not perfectly reversible).

$$\eta_T = \frac{W_{T,actual}}{W_{T,isentropic}} = \frac{h_3 - h_{4a}}{h_3 - h_{4s}}$$

where:
- $h_3$ = enthalpy at turbine inlet
- $h_{4a}$ = actual enthalpy at turbine outlet (which is *higher* than $h_{4s}$)
- $h_{4s}$ = enthalpy at turbine outlet **if the expansion were isentropic** (at $p_4$ but $s_{4s}=s_3$)

**C. Nozzle Efficiency ($\eta_N$):**

Used primarily for the propelling nozzle in a jet engine. The nozzle converts the *enthalpy drop* of the gas into *kinetic energy* of the jet.

$$\eta_N = \frac{V_{a}^{2}/2}{h_5 - h_{6s}} = \frac{\text{Actual KE gained}}{\text{Isentropic enthalpy drop}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sign Convention Trap (Very Common KTU Mistake):**
> For *work-consuming* devices (compressor, pump) — efficiency = (ideal) / (actual). For *work-producing* devices (turbine, nozzle) — efficiency = (actual) / (ideal). Mixing this up flips the ratio and is the **#1 cause of wrong numerical answers** in KTU scripts.

**D. Mechanical Efficiency ($\eta_m$):**

Some shaft work is lost to bearing friction, windage, and to driving accessories (fuel pump, oil pump, generator). The remaining net shaft work is what propels the aircraft (turbo-prop) or drives the compressor in a turbojet/turbofan.

$$\eta_m = \frac{W_{shaft,net}}{W_{shaft,gross}}$$

### 2.2 Stage 2 — Cycle Thermal Efficiency ($\eta_{th}$)

The **thermal efficiency** of a Brayton cycle is defined as:

$$\eta_{th} = \frac{W_{net}}{Q_{in}} = \frac{W_{T} - W_{C}}{Q_{in}}$$

For the **ideal Brayton cycle** (cold-air-standard, constant specific heats):

$$\boxed{\eta_{th,ideal} = 1 - \frac{1}{r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}} = 1 - \frac{T_1}{T_2}}$$

where $r_p = p_2 / p_1$ is the **pressure ratio**, and $\gamma = c_p / c_v$ is the ratio of specific heats.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight (asked almost every year):** The thermal efficiency of the *ideal* Brayton cycle depends **only on the pressure ratio** — not on the turbine inlet temperature! Increasing $T_3$ (TIT) does **not** improve $\eta_{th}$ for an ideal cycle; it only increases the *net work per cycle* (specific work output). This is the *opposite* of the Rankine cycle, where $T_3$ matters.

For the **actual Brayton cycle with component inefficiencies**, the thermal efficiency is **less** than the ideal value:

$$\eta_{th,actual} = \frac{W_{T,actual} \cdot \eta_m - W_{C,actual}}{Q_{in}}$$

with $Q_{in} = c_p (T_{3a} - T_{2a})$ using the actual $T_{3a}$ (limited by metallurgical TIT).

### 2.3 Stage 3 — Propulsive Efficiencies (Jet Engines Only)

For an aircraft moving at flight velocity $V_a$ and producing thrust $F$, the following are defined:

**A. Propulsive Power ($P_P$):** Rate at which *useful* work is done on the aircraft.

$$P_P = F \cdot V_a$$

**B. Thrust Power (Propulsive Power) vs Jet Kinetic Energy Rate:**

The jet leaves the nozzle with absolute velocity $V_j$ relative to the ground (or $V_{rel} = V_j - V_a$ relative to the aircraft). The kinetic energy *carried away* (wasted) by the jet per second is:

$$\dot{KE}_{jet} = \frac{\dot{m} \cdot V_{rel}^{2}}{2}$$

**C. Propulsive Efficiency ($\eta_P$):**

$$\eta_P = \frac{\text{Thrust Power}}{\text{Thrust Power} + \text{KE rate of jet}} = \frac{F \cdot V_a}{F \cdot V_a + \frac{\dot{m}(V_j - V_a)^{2}}{2}}$$

A more compact and famous form, derived from the thrust equation $F = \dot{m}(V_j - V_a)$:

$$\boxed{\eta_P = \frac{2 V_a / V_{rel}}{1 + (V_a / V_{rel})} = \frac{2}{1 + (V_j/V_a)} \quad \text{[Common KTU form]}}$$

**D. Propulsive Thermal Efficiency ($\eta_{th,p}$):**

Energy released by fuel per second = $\dot{m}_f \cdot CV = Q_R$ (rate of heat release). The fraction of fuel energy that becomes *propulsive power*:

$$\eta_{th,p} = \frac{F \cdot V_a}{\dot{Q}_R} = \frac{P_P}{\dot{Q}_R}$$

**E. Overall Efficiency ($\eta_O$):**

The single most important performance metric of a jet engine — the fraction of *fuel energy* that ends up as *useful propulsive work*:

$$\eta_O = \eta_{th,p} \cdot \eta_P = \eta_{th} \cdot \eta_P$$

For a turbojet, this product is the **figure of merit**.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Module 1 — Efficiency)

> [!TIP]
> Print or memorise this table. Every numerical problem in the KTU exam is solved using one of these expressions. **No pipe `|` symbols used in table cells — use `\vert` instead.**

| # | Efficiency | Formula | Variables / Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Compressor isentropic | $\eta_C = (h_{2s} - h_1) / (h_{2a} - h_1)$ | $h_{2a} > h_{2s}$ always |
| 2 | Turbine isentropic | $\eta_T = (h_3 - h_{4a}) / (h_3 - h_{4s})$ | $h_{4a} > h_{4s}$ always |
| 3 | Nozzle efficiency | $\eta_N = (V_a^{2}/2) / (h_5 - h_{6s})$ | Or $(V_{a}^{2}/2) / (V_{s}^{2}/2)$ |
| 4 | Mechanical efficiency | $\eta_m = W_{shaft,net} / W_{shaft,gross}$ | $0.95 \le \eta_m \le 0.99$ |
| 5 | Ideal Brayton thermal | $\eta_{th,ideal} = 1 - r_p^{-(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$ | **Independent of $T_3$** |
| 6 | Actual cycle thermal | $\eta_{th,actual} = (W_T \eta_m - W_C) / Q_{in}$ | Always $< \eta_{th,ideal}$ |
| 7 | Propulsive efficiency | $\eta_P = 2 V_a / (V_j + V_a) = 2 / (1 + V_j/V_a)$ | $\eta_P \to 1$ as $V_j \to V_a$ |
| 8 | Propulsive thermal eff. | $\eta_{th,p} = (F V_a) / (\dot{m}_f \cdot CV)$ | $CV \approx 43\,\text{MJ/kg}$ for Jet-A |
| 9 | Overall efficiency | $\eta_O = \eta_{th,p} \cdot \eta_P = \eta_{th} \cdot \eta_P$ | Figure of merit of a jet engine |
| 10 | Specific thrust | $F/\dot{m} = V_j - V_a$ | Useful for cycle optimisation |
| 11 | Specific fuel consumption | $SFC = \dot{m}_f / F \; [\text{kg/(N·s)}]$ | Inverse of efficiency merit |

### 2.5 Real-World Utility of These Efficiencies

- **Aircraft Engine Design:** Airlines pay for **fuel**, not thrust. A 1% improvement in $\eta_O$ of a turbofan saves **tens of millions of dollars per year per aircraft**. The GE9X, Trent XWB, and Pratt & Whitney GTF all target $\eta_O$ improvements through higher **bypass ratios** and **TIT**.
- **Industrial Gas Turbines (IGT):** Used in power plants (e.g., GE 7HA, Siemens SGT-9000HL) and gas-pipeline compression. The relevant metric is **thermal efficiency** $\eta_{th}$ alone — there is no jet. Combined-cycle plants exceed **64%** thermal efficiency (H-class turbines + bottoming Rankine cycle).
- **Marine & Locomotive GTs:** Use **propulsive efficiency** for propellers, but the *propeller* $\eta_P$ behaves differently from a *jet* $\eta_P$ (peaks around $V_a / V_{tip} \approx 0.7$).
- **Propulsion Revolution:** Hydrogen-fueled turbofans and **rotating detonation engines (RDEs)** are evaluated by the *same* $\eta_O$ metric — which is why this topic is *immortal* in the KTU syllabus.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation A — Ideal Brayton Cycle Thermal Efficiency (with cold-air-standard assumption)

The Brayton cycle consists of four internally reversible processes:
- **1→2:** Isentropic compression in the compressor
- **2→3:** Constant-pressure heat addition in the combustion chamber
- **3→4:** Isentropic expansion in the turbine
- **4→1:** Constant-pressure heat rejection (in an *open* cycle, this is replaced by exhausting to the atmosphere)

**Step 1 — Net Work of the Cycle:**

The net work per unit mass of working fluid is the algebraic sum of turbine work and compressor work:

$$W_{net} = W_T - W_C$$

**Step 2 — Apply Steady-Flow Energy Equation (SFEE) to Each Component (adiabatic, no KE/PE change):**

For the compressor (1→2): $W_C = h_2 - h_1 = c_p (T_2 - T_1)$

For the turbine (3→4): $W_T = h_3 - h_4 = c_p (T_3 - T_4)$

Therefore:

$$W_{net} = c_p (T_3 - T_4) - c_p (T_2 - T_1) = c_p \left[ (T_3 - T_4) - (T_2 - T_1) \right]$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Isentropic Relations to find $T_2$ and $T_4$ in terms of $T_1$, $T_3$, and the pressure ratio $r_p$:**

For isentropic compression $1 \to 2s$ with $p_2/p_1 = r_p$:

$$T_{2s} = T_1 \cdot r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$$

For isentropic expansion $3 \to 4s$ with $p_3/p_4 = r_p$ (since $p_3 = p_2$ and $p_4 = p_1$ in a Brayton cycle):

$$T_{4s} = T_3 \cdot r_p^{-(\gamma-1)/\gamma} = \frac{T_3}{r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}}$$

**Step 4 — Heat Input:**

The only heat addition is in the constant-pressure process 2→3:

$$Q_{in} = c_p (T_3 - T_2) = c_p \left( T_3 - T_1 \cdot r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} \right)$$

**Step 5 — Form the Ratio $\eta_{th} = W_{net} / Q_{in}$:**

$$\eta_{th} = \frac{c_p \left[ T_3 - T_3 / r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - T_1 r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} + T_1 \right]}{c_p \left[ T_3 - T_1 r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} \right]}$$

After algebraic simplification (factor and cancel $T_3 - T_1 r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$ from numerator and denominator):

$$\eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{T_1 \left( r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - 1 \right)}{T_3 - T_1 r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}}$$

**Step 6 — Introduce the limiting temperature ratio $\tau = T_3 / T_1$ and the function $x = r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$:**

After more algebra, the expression collapses to the elegant form:

$$\boxed{\eta_{th,Brayton,ideal} = 1 - \frac{1}{r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}} = 1 - \frac{T_1}{T_2} = 1 - \frac{T_4}{T_3}}$$

**Step 7 — Physical Interpretation:**

- As $r_p \to \infty$, $\eta_{th} \to 1$ (theoretically). In practice, $r_p$ is limited by compressor discharge temperature, which would melt the blades.
- $\eta_{th}$ is **independent of $T_3$** in the ideal cycle — this is a uniquely *Brayton* result.

### 3.2 Derivation B — Actual Thermal Efficiency with Component Losses

When $\eta_C < 1$ and $\eta_T < 1$, the actual exit temperatures are:

$$T_{2a} = T_1 + \frac{T_{2s} - T_1}{\eta_C}$$

$$T_{4a} = T_3 - \eta_T (T_3 - T_{4s})$$

**Actual net work (with mechanical efficiency $\eta_m$ on the turbine side):**

$$W_{net,actual} = \eta_m \, c_p (T_3 - T_{4a}) - c_p (T_{2a} - T_1)$$

**Actual heat input (using the *actual* TIT, $T_{3a}$):**

$$Q_{in,actual} = c_p (T_{3a} - T_{2a})$$

**Final actual thermal efficiency:**

$$\eta_{th,actual} = \frac{\eta_m \, c_p (T_3 - T_{4a}) - c_p (T_{2a} - T_1)}{c_p (T_{3a} - T_{2a})}$$

This expression is what KTU expects you to evaluate numerically in Part B problems (with given $T_1, T_3, r_p, \gamma, \eta_C, \eta_T, \eta_m$).

### 3.3 Derivation C — Propulsive Efficiency $\eta_P$ in the Standard KTU Form

**Step 1 — Define Thrust:**

For a turbojet with exhaust velocity $V_j$ relative to the engine (or to the ground for stationary observer), the thrust from momentum theory is:

$$F = \dot{m} (V_j - V_a)$$

where $\dot{m}$ is the mass flow rate of air through the engine and $V_a$ is the aircraft forward velocity.

**Step 2 — Propulsive (Thrust) Power:**

The rate at which useful work is done on the aircraft:

$$P_P = F \cdot V_a = \dot{m} V_a (V_j - V_a)$$

**Step 3 — Kinetic Energy Carried Away by the Jet per Second:**

The jet leaves with velocity $V_j$ relative to the ground. Relative to the *aircraft*, the jet velocity is $V_{rel} = V_j - V_a$. The wasted KE rate:

$$\dot{KE}_{waste} = \frac{1}{2} \dot{m} (V_j - V_a)^{2}$$

**Step 4 — Energy Available in the Jet (per unit time):**

This is the *total* kinetic-energy-like energy supplied to the jet:

$$\dot{E}_{jet,total} = P_P + \dot{KE}_{waste} = \dot{m} V_a (V_j - V_a) + \frac{1}{2} \dot{m} (V_j - V_a)^{2}$$

**Step 5 — Simplify:**

Factor out $\dot{m} (V_j - V_a)$:

$$\dot{E}_{jet,total} = \dot{m} (V_j - V_a) \left[ V_a + \frac{V_j - V_a}{2} \right] = \dot{m} (V_j - V_a) \cdot \frac{V_j + V_a}{2}$$

**Step 6 — Form the Ratio $\eta_P = P_P / \dot{E}_{jet,total}$:**

$$\eta_P = \frac{\dot{m} V_a (V_j - V_a)}{\dot{m} (V_j - V_a) \cdot (V_j + V_a)/2} = \frac{2 V_a}{V_j + V_a}$$

Dividing numerator and denominator by $V_a$:

$$\boxed{\eta_P = \frac{2}{1 + (V_j / V_a)} = \frac{2 (V_j - V_a)}{(V_j - V_a)(1 + V_j/V_a)}}$$

**Step 7 — Limiting Cases (Test for Physical Sanity):**

- $V_j = V_a$ (no relative exhaust velocity — pure propeller, ideal): $\eta_P = 2/(1+1) = 1$ (100%) — **ideal propeller limit**.
- $V_j = 2 V_a$: $\eta_P = 2/(1+2) = 2/3 \approx 66.7\%$
- $V_j \to \infty$ (rocket-like jet): $\eta_P \to 0$ — all fuel energy goes to waste kinetic energy in the jet.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Trick Question Warning:** The maximum propulsive efficiency of a turbojet is **2/3 = 66.7%** (when $V_j = 2 V_a$). For a turbofan with bypass ratio $\beta$, the equivalent $\eta_P$ is higher because part of the thrust comes from a slower, larger fan stream.

### 3.4 Derivation D — Overall Efficiency $\eta_O = \eta_P \cdot \eta_{th}$

**Step 1 — Energy Balance on the Engine (per unit time):**

$$\dot{Q}_R = P_P + \dot{KE}_{waste} + \dot{Q}_{lost} + \dot{Q}_{exhaust}$$

where $\dot{Q}_R$ is the chemical energy released by fuel per second.

**Step 2 — Define $\eta_{th,p}$ and $\eta_P$:**

$$\eta_{th,p} = \frac{P_P}{\dot{Q}_R} = \frac{F \cdot V_a}{\dot{m}_f \cdot CV}$$

$$\eta_P = \frac{P_P}{P_P + \dot{KE}_{waste}}$$

**Step 3 — Multiply:**

$$\eta_{th,p} \cdot \eta_P = \frac{P_P}{\dot{Q}_R} \cdot \frac{P_P}{P_P + \dot{KE}_{waste}} = \frac{P_P^{2}}{\dot{Q}_R \cdot (P_P + \dot{KE}_{waste})}$$

**Step 4 — Show that the *useful output* is $P_P$ and the *input* is $\dot{Q}_R$, so overall efficiency is:**

$$\boxed{\eta_O = \frac{\text{Useful Propulsive Power Output}}{\text{Fuel Energy Input Rate}} = \frac{P_P}{\dot{Q}_R} = \eta_{th,p} = \eta_{th} \cdot \eta_P}$$

(Note: $\eta_{th,p}$ *is* the overall efficiency — the terms are interchangeable in the KTU convention.)

**Step 5 — Express $\eta_O$ in terms of fundamental ratios:**

Using $F = \dot{m}(V_j - V_a)$ and $P_P = F V_a$:

$$\eta_O = \frac{\dot{m} V_a (V_j - V_a)}{\dot{m}_f \cdot CV}$$

For $\dot{m}_f \ll \dot{m}$ (typical for jet engines, fuel-to-air ratio $f \approx 0.02$), $\dot{m} \approx \dot{m}_a$ and we can write the result in terms of $f$:

$$\eta_O = \frac{V_a (V_j - V_a)}{f \cdot CV}$$

This compact form is what KTU numerical problems require.

### 3.5 Numerical Illustration (KTU-Style Worked Example)

> **Example Problem (Model Solution):** A turbojet engine operates with: $T_1 = 290\,\text{K}$, $T_3 = 1500\,\text{K}$, $r_p = 12$, $\gamma = 1.4$, $c_p = 1.005\,\text{kJ/kg·K}$, $V_a = 250\,\text{m/s}$, $V_j = 600\,\text{m/s}$, fuel-air ratio $f = 0.018$, $CV = 43\,\text{MJ/kg}$, $\eta_C = \eta_T = 0.88$, $\eta_m = 0.98$.
>
> **Compute:** (a) Ideal Brayton thermal efficiency, (b) Propulsive efficiency, (c) Overall efficiency, (d) Net specific thrust and SFC.

**Solution:**

**(a) Ideal Brayton $\eta_{th}$:**

Compute the exponent $(\gamma-1)/\gamma = 0.4/1.4 \approx 0.2857$:

$$r_p^{0.2857} = 12^{0.2857} \approx 2.034$$

$$\eta_{th,ideal} = 1 - 1/2.034 = 1 - 0.4916 = 0.5084 \approx 50.8\%$$

**(b) Propulsive Efficiency:**

$$\eta_P = \frac{2 \cdot 250}{600 + 250} = \frac{500}{850} = 0.5882 \approx 58.8\%$$

**(c) Overall Efficiency:**

$$\eta_O = \eta_{th} \cdot \eta_P = 0.5084 \cdot 0.5882 = 0.299 \approx 29.9\%$$

(If component losses are considered, $\eta_O$ drops to about **22–24%** — typical for an early-generation turbojet.)

**(d) Specific Thrust and SFC:**

$$F/\dot{m} = V_j - V_a = 600 - 250 = 350\,\text{N·s/kg}$$

$$SFC = \frac{f}{F/\dot{m}} = \frac{0.018}{350} = 5.14 \times 10^{-5}\,\text{kg/(N·s)} = 0.185\,\text{kg/(kN·s)}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Acceptable KTU Answer Format:** Always box the final result, state units explicitly, and present to **3 significant figures** unless otherwise specified.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Energy-Flow Block Diagram of a Turbojet (showing where each efficiency acts)

The block diagram below maps the energy transformations and the *exact* location where each efficiency is computed. Every block is a physical sub-system of the engine; every arrow is an energy flux.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Atmospheric Air<br/>Inlet: T1, p1, Va] --> B[Diffuser<br/>ram recovery]
    B --> C[Compressor<br/>eta_C = W_C,s / W_C,a]
    C --> D[Combustion Chamber<br/>eta_comb: Q_in supplied]
    D --> E[Turbine<br/>eta_T = W_T,a / W_T,s]
    E --> F[Nozzle<br/>eta_N = KE_actual / KE_isentropic]
    F --> G[Exhaust Jet<br/>V_j, p_atm]

    H[Fuel Tank<br/>CV = 43 MJ/kg] --> D

    E -.drives.-> C

    I[Fuel Energy Input<br/>dot Q_R = m_dot_f * CV] --> J[Overall Efficiency<br/>eta_O = F * Va / dot Q_R]

    G --> K[KE Wasted in Jet<br/>0.5 * m_dot * V_rel^2] --> J
    A --> L[Thrust Power<br/>F * Va] --> J

    style A fill:#e6f3ff,stroke:#0066cc
    style D fill:#ffe6e6,stroke:#cc0000
    style E fill:#e6ffe6,stroke:#009900
    style J fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#cc9900
    style C fill:#f0e6ff,stroke:#6600cc
    style F fill:#f0e6ff,stroke:#6600cc
```

### 4.2 Hierarchy of Efficiencies — "Efficiency Tree"

This diagram shows the *logical* decomposition of overall efficiency into its sub-efficiencies, in the exact order KTU expects students to reproduce in the exam.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Overall Efficiency<br/>eta_O = F * Va / dot Q_R] --> B[Propulsive Efficiency<br/>eta_P = 2 Va / V_j + Va]
    A --> C[Propulsive Thermal Efficiency<br/>eta_th_p = F * Va / m_dot_f * CV]

    B --> D[Thrust Power F * Va]
    B --> E[Jet Wasted KE 0.5 m_dot V_rel^2]

    C --> F[Fuel Energy dot Q_R]
    C --> G[Combustion Efficiency eta_comb]

    H[Cycle Thermal Efficiency eta_th] --> I[Compressor Efficiency eta_C]
    H --> J[Turbine Efficiency eta_T]
    H --> K[Mechanical Efficiency eta_m]
    H --> L[Nozzle Efficiency eta_N]

    H --> C
    I --> M[Actual Work W_C,a]
    J --> N[Actual Work W_T,a]
    K --> O[Net Shaft Work W_shaft,net]
    L --> P[Jet KE V_j^2 / 2]

    style A fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#990000
    style H fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366
    style I fill:#e6f3ff,stroke:#0066cc
    style J fill:#e6f3ff,stroke:#0066cc
    style K fill:#e6f3ff,stroke:#0066cc
    style L fill:#e6f3ff,stroke:#0066cc
```

### 4.3 T–s Diagram of the Actual vs Ideal Brayton Cycle (Schematic)

The T–s (Temperature–Entropy) diagram is the single most important pictorial aid in Module 1. Below is a Mermaid adaptation showing the four states of the cycle and how component inefficiencies *spread* the constant-pressure lines and the isentropic lines.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph T_s_Diagram["T-s Diagram: Actual vs Ideal Brayton Cycle"]
        direction TB
        P1[State 1: T1, p1<br/>Ambient inlet] --> P2[State 2s: T2s, p2<br/>Isentropic compression]
        P2 --> P2a[State 2a: T2a, p2<br/>ACTUAL compression T2a greater T2s]
        P2a --> P3[State 3: T3, p3 = p2<br/>Combustion heat addition]
        P3 --> P4s[State 4s: T4s, p4 = p1<br/>Isentropic expansion]
        P4s --> P4a[State 4a: T4a, p4<br/>ACTUAL expansion T4a greater T4s]
        P4a --> P1b[State 1 cycle closes back to ambient]
        P1b --> P1
    end

    style P1 fill:#cce5ff
    style P2s fill:#e6f3ff
    style P2a fill:#ffe6e6
    style P3 fill:#ffcccc
    style P4s fill:#e6ffe6
    style P4a fill:#ffe6cc
```

**Key observations from the T–s diagram (write these in your KTU answer):**

- The ideal isentropic processes 1→2s and 3→4s are **vertical lines** (constant entropy).
- The *actual* processes 1→2a and 3→4a slope **to the right** (entropy increases due to irreversibilities).
- $T_{2a} > T_{2s}$ (more work needed in real compressor).
- $T_{4a} > T_{4s}$ (less work extracted in real turbine).
- The area enclosed by **1→2s→3→4s→1** represents the *net ideal work*.
- The area enclosed by **1→2a→3→4a→1** represents the *net actual work* (smaller area).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Mermaid here?** A real T–s diagram needs curved isobars and isentropes. Mermaid cannot draw curves with mathematical fidelity, so the schematic above is the *block-level* representation that is KTU-examinable as a labelled schematic. In your answer script, draw the actual T–s plot with axes ($T$ vertical, $s$ horizontal) and label states 1, 2s, 2a, 3, 4s, 4a.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 1, Q1(a)]**

**Q1. (3 Marks)** Define the terms *compressor isentropic efficiency* and *turbine isentropic efficiency*. State their typical numerical ranges for a modern aero-derivative gas turbine.

**Model Answer:**

- **Compressor isentropic efficiency** is the ratio of the *ideal (isentropic) compression work* to the *actual compression work*, for the same inlet state and same exit pressure: $\eta_C = (h_{2s} - h_1)/(h_{2a} - h_1)$.
- **Turbine isentropic efficiency** is the ratio of the *actual expansion work* to the *ideal (isentropic) expansion work*, for the same inlet state and same exit pressure: $\eta_T = (h_3 - h_{4a})/(h_3 - h_{4s})$.
- Typical ranges: $\eta_C \approx 0.80$–$0.92$ and $\eta_T \approx 0.85$–$0.94$ for modern aero-derivative units (e.g., GE LM2500, Rolls-Royce Trent).

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 1, Q1(b)]**

**Q2. (3 Marks)** What is *propulsive efficiency* of a jet engine? Why does it approach unity when the jet velocity equals the aircraft velocity, and approach zero when the jet velocity is very large?

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Propulsive efficiency is the ratio of *thrust power* (useful work done on the aircraft per second) to the *rate of kinetic energy supplied to the exhaust jet*:

$$\eta_P = \frac{F \cdot V_a}{F \cdot V_a + \frac{1}{2} \dot{m} (V_j - V_a)^{2}} = \frac{2 V_a}{V_j + V_a}$$

- **Case 1 — $V_j = V_a$ (ideal propeller):** The relative exhaust velocity $(V_j - V_a) = 0$, so the jet carries *no* wasted KE. All energy in the jet becomes propulsive power, so $\eta_P = 1$ (100%).
- **Case 2 — $V_j \gg V_a$ (rocket-like regime):** The denominator grows as $V_j^{2}$ (through the $(V_j - V_a)^{2}$ term) while the numerator grows only as $V_j$ (through the $F \cdot V_a$ term). Hence $\eta_P \to 0$, because most of the fuel's energy is dumped into the *wasted* KE of a high-speed jet.

---

### Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

> **Internal Choice Convention (KTU 2024):** Attempt **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. Each is split into (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Model Paper Set 2]

**(a) (7 Marks)** A gas turbine cycle operates with compressor pressure ratio $r_p = 8$, turbine inlet temperature $T_3 = 1400\,\text{K}$, ambient temperature $T_1 = 300\,\text{K}$, $\gamma = 1.4$, $c_p = 1.005\,\text{kJ/kg·K}$. Assuming **ideal (isentropic) compression and expansion**, calculate:
- (i) The net specific work output.
- (ii) The thermal efficiency of the cycle.
- (iii) The work ratio (turbine work / compressor work).

**(b) (7 Marks)** If the *actual* compressor and turbine isentropic efficiencies are both $\eta_C = \eta_T = 0.85$, and the mechanical efficiency is $\eta_m = 0.98$, recalculate the net specific work output, the cycle thermal efficiency, and the work ratio. Comment on the effect of component inefficiencies.

---

**Model Solution to Question A:**

**(a)(i) Net Specific Work Output (Ideal):**

Isentropic exit temperature of compressor:

$$T_{2s} = T_1 \cdot r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} = 300 \cdot 8^{0.2857} = 300 \cdot 1.8114 = 543.4\,\text{K}$$

Compressor work: $W_C = c_p (T_{2s} - T_1) = 1.005 \cdot (543.4 - 300) = 244.4\,\text{kJ/kg}$

Isentropic turbine exit temperature:

$$T_{4s} = T_3 / r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} = 1400 / 1.8114 = 772.9\,\text{K}$$

Turbine work: $W_T = c_p (T_3 - T_{4s}) = 1.005 \cdot (1400 - 772.9) = 630.4\,\text{kJ/kg}$

$$\boxed{W_{net} = W_T - W_C = 630.4 - 244.4 = 386.0\,\text{kJ/kg}}$$

**(a)(ii) Thermal Efficiency (Ideal):**

$$\eta_{th} = 1 - 1/r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} = 1 - 1/1.8114 = 1 - 0.5520 = \boxed{0.448 \text{ or } 44.8\%}$$

**(a)(iii) Work Ratio (Ideal):**

$$\text{Work Ratio} = \frac{W_T}{W_C} = \frac{630.4}{244.4} = \boxed{2.579}$$

**(b) Effect of Component Inefficiencies:**

Actual compressor exit temperature:

$$T_{2a} = T_1 + \frac{T_{2s} - T_1}{\eta_C} = 300 + \frac{543.4 - 300}{0.85} = 300 + 286.4 = 586.4\,\text{K}$$

Actual turbine exit temperature:

$$T_{4a} = T_3 - \eta_T (T_3 - T_{4s}) = 1400 - 0.85 \cdot (1400 - 772.9) = 1400 - 533.1 = 866.9\,\text{K}$$

Actual turbine work (gross): $W_{T,actual} = c_p (T_3 - T_{4a}) = 1.005 \cdot (1400 - 866.9) = 535.7\,\text{kJ/kg}$

Shaft work (net, after mechanical losses): $W_{shaft} = \eta_m \cdot W_{T,actual} = 0.98 \cdot 535.7 = 525.0\,\text{kJ/kg}$

Actual compressor work: $W_{C,actual} = c_p (T_{2a} - T_1) = 1.005 \cdot (586.4 - 300) = 287.8\,\text{kJ/kg}$

**(b)(i) Net Specific Work (Actual):**

$$\boxed{W_{net,actual} = W_{shaft} - W_{C,actual} = 525.0 - 287.8 = 237.2\,\text{kJ/kg}}$$

**(b)(ii) Thermal Efficiency (Actual):**

$$Q_{in,actual} = c_p (T_3 - T_{2a}) = 1.005 \cdot (1400 - 586.4) = 817.7\,\text{kJ/kg}$$

$$\eta_{th,actual} = \frac{W_{net,actual}}{Q_{in,actual}} = \frac{237.2}{817.7} = \boxed{0.290 \text{ or } 29.0\%}$$

**(b)(iii) Work Ratio (Actual):**

$$\boxed{\text{Work Ratio} = \frac{W_{T,actual}}{W_{C,actual}} = \frac{535.7}{287.8} = 1.861}$$

**Comment:** The component inefficiencies cause:
- Net work drops from $386.0$ to $237.2\,\text{kJ/kg}$ — a **38.6% reduction**.
- Thermal efficiency drops from $44.8\%$ to $29.0\%$ — a **35.3% reduction**.
- Work ratio drops from $2.58$ to $1.86$ — meaning more of the turbine's gross work is consumed *internally* to drive the compressor, leaving less for the load.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — May 2023, Module 1]

**(a) (7 Marks)** A turbojet engine is in level flight at $V_a = 240\,\text{m/s}$. The exhaust jet velocity is $V_j = 580\,\text{m/s}$, the fuel-air ratio is $f = 0.020$, and the calorific value of the fuel is $CV = 43\,\text{MJ/kg}$. Calculate:
- (i) The propulsive efficiency of the engine.
- (ii) The thrust power per unit mass flow rate.
- (iii) The propulsive thermal efficiency.
- (iv) The overall efficiency of the engine.

**(b) (7 Marks)** A modern high-bypass turbofan engine operates with $V_a = 270\,\text{m/s}$, $V_j = 380\,\text{m/s}$, $f = 0.012$, $CV = 43\,\text{MJ/kg}$. Compare its propulsive efficiency and overall efficiency with the turbojet in part (a). Discuss, with the help of equations, why high-bypass turbofans are more fuel-efficient for subsonic commercial aircraft.

---

**Model Solution to Question B:**

**(a)(i) Propulsive Efficiency:**

$$\eta_P = \frac{2 V_a}{V_j + V_a} = \frac{2 \cdot 240}{580 + 240} = \frac{480}{820} = \boxed{0.585 \text{ or } 58.5\%}$$

**(a)(ii) Thrust Power per Unit Mass Flow Rate:**

Specific thrust: $F/\dot{m} = V_j - V_a = 580 - 240 = 340\,\text{N·s/kg}$

Thrust power per unit mass flow: $(F \cdot V_a)/\dot{m} = F/\dot{m} \cdot V_a = 340 \cdot 240 = \boxed{81{,}600\,\text{W per kg/s} = 81.6\,\text{kW per kg/s}}$

**(a)(iii) Propulsive Thermal Efficiency:**

$$\eta_{th,p} = \frac{F \cdot V_a}{\dot{m}_f \cdot CV} = \frac{V_a (V_j - V_a)}{f \cdot CV} = \frac{240 \cdot 340}{0.020 \cdot 43{,}000{,}000}$$

$$= \frac{81{,}600}{860{,}000} = \boxed{0.0949 \text{ or } 9.49\%}$$

**(a)(iv) Overall Efficiency:**

$$\eta_O = \eta_P \cdot \eta_{th,p} = 0.585 \cdot 0.0949 = \boxed{0.0555 \text{ or } 5.55\%}$$

(Equivalently, $\eta_O = F V_a / (\dot{m} f CV)$ using the same numbers.)

**(b) Comparison for the High-Bypass Turbofan:**

Propulsive efficiency of the turbofan:

$$\eta_P = \frac{2 \cdot 270}{380 + 270} = \frac{540}{650} = 0.8308 \approx \boxed{83.1\%}$$

Propulsive thermal efficiency:

$$\eta_{th,p} = \frac{V_a (V_j - V_a)}{f \cdot CV} = \frac{270 \cdot (380 - 270)}{0.012 \cdot 43{,}000{,}000} = \frac{29{,}700}{516{,}000} = 0.0576 \approx 5.76\%$$

Overall efficiency:

$$\eta_O = 0.8308 \cdot 0.0576 = \boxed{0.0479 \text{ or } 4.79\%}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign-of-velocity confusion:** Students often use $V_j + V_a$ in the numerator of $\eta_P$. It must be $V_a \cdot (V_j - V_a)$ in the *thrust power* term because thrust is $\dot{m}(V_j - V_a)$.
> 2. **Forgetting the $f \cdot CV$ factor:** $\eta_{th,p}$ uses *fuel energy* ($\dot{m}_f \cdot CV$), not *air* energy. Conflating $\dot{m}$ with $\dot{m}_f$ produces a $1/f$ error (~50× wrong answer).
> 3. **Mixing $\eta_O = \eta_{th} \cdot \eta_P$ with $\eta_O = \eta_{th,p} \cdot \eta_P$:** Both are correct **only** if the propulsive thermal efficiency $\eta_{th,p}$ is defined as $F V_a / \dot{Q}_R$. KTU convention: $\eta_O = \eta_P \cdot \eta_{th,p}$. Always state your definitions.
> 4. **Not unit-converting $CV$:** $CV = 43\,\text{MJ/kg} = 43{,}000\,\text{kJ/kg} = 43 \times 10^{6}\,\text{J/kg}$. Students forget the $10^{6}$ factor and get $\eta_{th,p}$ three orders of magnitude too small.
> 5. **Writing $\eta_P$ in % without units in the formula sheet:** Penalty: 0.5 marks.
> 6. **Skipping the "comment" part of a 7-mark sub-question:** Even if your numbers are correct, the *commentary* on physical meaning carries **1–2 marks**. Always end with a one-sentence physical insight.
> 7. **Using $V_j$ relative to engine vs $V_j$ relative to ground:** KTU convention is $V_j$ = absolute exhaust velocity *relative to the engine* (i.e., relative to the aircraft). Be consistent.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> **Last 24 hours before the KTU exam? Read this list twice. Three times if you are not confident.**

- [ ] **Efficiency is dimensionless**, between 0 and 1 (or 0%–100%). Always state units explicitly.
- [ ] **Isentropic efficiency of a work-consuming device** (compressor) = (ideal work) / (actual work). **Isentropic efficiency of a work-producing device** (turbine, nozzle) = (actual work) / (ideal work). **Reversing the ratio is the #1 mistake.**
- [ ] **Ideal Brayton $\eta_{th} = 1 - r_p^{-(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$** depends *only* on pressure ratio, not on TIT. To prove it, derive it.
- [ ] **$T_{2a} > T_{2s}$** (compressor inefficiency) and **$T_{4a} > T_{4s}$** (turbine inefficiency). On a T–s diagram, the *actual* curves slope *to the right* of the isentropic curves.
- [ ] **Mechanical efficiency $\eta_m$** is applied to the *turbine* work (it represents the fraction of gross turbine work that becomes useful shaft work).
- [ ] **Combustion efficiency $\eta_{comb}$** relates actual heat release to fuel LHV. In well-designed chambers, $\eta_{comb} > 0.98$, so KTU often assumes $\eta_{comb} = 1$.
- [ ] **Propulsive efficiency** is for **jet engines only** — irrelevant for power-generation GTs. $\eta_P = 2 V_a / (V_j + V_a) = 2/(1 + V_j/V_a)$.
- [ ] **Maximum $\eta_P$ for a turbojet is 2/3 ≈ 66.7%** (at $V_j = 2 V_a$). Turbofans beat this by having a *bypass stream* with much smaller $(V_j - V_a)$.
- [ ] **Overall efficiency $\eta_O$** is the figure of merit for a jet engine: $\eta_O = \eta_P \cdot \eta_{th} = F V_a / (\dot{m}_f \cdot CV)$.
- [ ] **SFC** (specific fuel consumption) = $\dot{m}_f / F$ [kg/(N·s)]. Low SFC = good engine. Modern turbofans target $SFC \approx 0.3$–$0.5\,\text{kg/(kN·s)}$ at cruise.
- [ ] **The general efficiency chain:** Fuel Energy → $\eta_{comb}$ → Heat to Gas → $\eta_{th}$ (cycle) → Shaft Work → $\eta_m$ → Net Shaft Work → $\eta_P$ (for jets) → Thrust Power.
- [ ] **Typical magnitudes** to memorise: $\eta_C \approx 0.88$, $\eta_T \approx 0.90$, $\eta_m \approx 0.98$, $\eta_{th} \approx 0.35$, $\eta_P \approx 0.6$ (turbojet) / $0.8$ (turbofan), $\eta_O \approx 0.30$ (turbojet) / $0.40$ (turbofan).
- [ ] **KTU-specific marks-padding tips:** Always (a) draw a labelled T–s or h–s diagram, (b) define all symbols before use, (c) box the final answer, (d) state units, and (e) end with a one-line physical insight/comment.
- [ ] **Bonus tip:** When asked "compare turbojet and turbofan" — always mention that a turbofan replaces *one* high-speed jet with *two* streams: a *core jet* (high $V_j$) and a *fan/bypass jet* (low $V_j$). The low-velocity bypass stream dramatically raises $\eta_P$ without a large penalty in $\eta_{th}$.
- [ ] **Past-year KTU frequency:** *Efficiency* appears in **every single ESE paper** of PEMET741 — typically 8–10 marks of a 14-mark question, plus 3–6 marks in Part A. This is a *high-weightage* topic.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basics of Airfoil Theory
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET741 - GAS TURBINE AND JET PROPULSION
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Basics of Airfoil Theory

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is an Airfoil?

An **airfoil** (also spelled *aerofoil*) is a cross-sectional shape of a body, typically a wing, blade, or sail, designed to generate a useful aerodynamic reaction — most commonly **lift** — when moving through a fluid (air). In the context of gas turbines and jet propulsion, airfoils form the cross-sections of **compressor blades, turbine blades, and propeller blades**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Syllabus Standard):** An airfoil is the two-dimensional cross-section of a wing or blade bounded by an upper surface (suction side), a lower surface (pressure side), a rounded leading edge, and a sharp trailing edge.

---

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Hand in Water" Concept

Imagine holding your right hand flat, palm down, and moving it horizontally through a swimming pool with your palm angled slightly upward (tilted). You will feel a force pushing your hand **upward** while it tries to move forward. That upward push is exactly what happens to an airfoil.

- Your **palm** = the lower (pressure) surface of the airfoil.
- The **tilt angle** = the **angle of attack**.
- The **upward push** = the **lift force**.
- The **resistance** you feel pulling your hand back = the **drag force**.

A real aircraft wing simply does this with air instead of water, but using carefully sculpted curved surfaces instead of a flat palm — which is dramatically more efficient.

---

### 1.3 Key Geometric Terminology

The following terms form the foundation of every airfoil problem in KTU exams:

| Term | Symbol | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **Leading Edge** | LE | The front-most rounded point of the airfoil where flow first meets it. |
| **Trailing Edge** | TE | The rear-most sharp point of the airfoil where flow leaves. |
| **Chord Line** | $c$ | The straight imaginary line joining LE and TE. |
| **Chord Length** | $c$ | The length of the chord line (the reference dimension of the airfoil). |
| **Mean Camber Line** | — | The locus of points midway between upper and lower surfaces. |
| **Camber** | — | The maximum distance between the mean camber line and the chord line. |
| **Thickness** | $t$ | The maximum distance between upper and lower surfaces (measured perpendicular to the chord). |
| **Thickness Ratio** | $t/c$ | The ratio of maximum thickness to chord length (typically **0.10 to 0.15** for subsonic airfoils). |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Favourite:** Students often confuse **camber** with **thickness**. Remember: *Thickness* is how fat the airfoil is; *camber* is how curved its backbone is. A perfectly **symmetric airfoil** has **zero camber**.

---

### 1.4 Classification of Airfoils

1. **Symmetric Airfoil** — Mean camber line coincides with chord line. Zero camber. Used in turbomachinery (compressor/turbine blades when set at zero incidence).
2. **Cambered Airfoil** — Mean camber line is curved. Generates lift even at zero geometric angle of attack. Used in aircraft wings.
3. **Concave (S-shaped) Airfoil** — Used in supersonic applications to reduce wave drag.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters for Gas Turbines:** Gas turbine compressor and turbine blades use **highly cambered airfoil profiles** because flow enters at large angles (often $30^{\circ}$–$60^{\circ}$), and camber helps recover pressure efficiently.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Forces and Moments on an Airfoil

When a uniform free-stream flow of velocity $V_\infty$ meets an airfoil at an **angle of attack $\alpha$**, three fundamental aerodynamic quantities arise:

1. **Lift ($L$)** — The aerodynamic force component **perpendicular** to the free-stream velocity.
2. **Drag ($D$)** — The aerodynamic force component **parallel** to the free-stream velocity (opposing motion).
3. **Aerodynamic Moment ($M$)** — The tendency of the airfoil to **rotate** (pitch) about a reference point, usually the leading edge or the quarter-chord point.

These forces are conventionally defined through **dimensionless coefficients** to make results independent of the size of the test object or the flow speed.

---

### 2.2 The Angle of Attack

The **angle of attack** $\alpha$ is the angle between the **chord line** of the airfoil and the **direction of the free-stream velocity** $V_\infty$.

$$\alpha = \angle(\text{chord line}, V_\infty \text{ direction})$$

A **positive angle of attack** tilts the leading edge upward relative to the flow, increasing lift (up to the stall angle).

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> All equations below are **mandatory** for KTU University Exam questions from this module.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Lift Coefficient | $C_L = \dfrac{L}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_\infty^{2}\, c}$ | $L$ per unit span. |
| 2 | Drag Coefficient | $C_D = \dfrac{D}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_\infty^{2}\, c}$ | $D$ per unit span. |
| 3 | Moment Coefficient | $C_M = \dfrac{M}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_\infty^{2}\, c^{2}}$ | $M$ about leading edge (per span). |
| 4 | Dynamic Pressure | $q_\infty = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_\infty^{2}$ | In $\text{N/m}^{2}$ (Pascal). |
| 5 | Lift Force | $L = C_L \cdot q_\infty \cdot c$ | Per unit span. |
| 6 | Drag Force | $D = C_D \cdot q_\infty \cdot c$ | Per unit span. |
| 7 | Lift–Drag Relation | $\dfrac{L}{D} = \dfrac{C_L}{C_D}$ | Aerodynamic efficiency ratio. |
| 8 | Pressure Coefficient | $C_p = \dfrac{p - p_\infty}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_\infty^{2}}$ | Local pressure differential. |
| 9 | Resultant Aerodynamic Force | $R = \sqrt{L^{2} + D^{2}}$ | Magnitude of total force. |
| 10 | Airfoil Efficiency | $E = \dfrac{C_L}{C_D}$ | Higher $E$ = more efficient airfoil. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Unit Consistency Rule for KTU:** Always use **SI units** — density $\rho$ in $\text{kg/m}^{3}$, velocity $V_\infty$ in $\text{m/s}$, chord $c$ in $\text{m}$, forces in $\text{N}$, and moment in $\text{N·m}$. The coefficients $C_L$, $C_D$, $C_M$ are **dimensionless**.

---

### 2.4 Physical Interpretation of Coefficients

- **$C_L$ (Lift Coefficient):** Grows almost **linearly** with $\alpha$ for small angles, peaks at the **stall angle** ($\alpha_{stall} \approx 15^{\circ}$ to $20^{\circ}$ for typical airfoils), then drops.
- **$C_D$ (Drag Coefficient):** Has a minimum value $C_{D,\min}$ near the *zero-lift* angle of attack, and increases sharply after stall.
- **$C_M$ (Moment Coefficient):** For a *cambered* airfoil, the moment about the **leading edge** is **negative (nose-down)**. About the **aerodynamic center** (≈ quarter-chord point for thin airfoils), the moment is approximately **constant** with respect to $\alpha$.

---

### 2.5 Why This Matters in Gas Turbine Engineering

In jet engines, the **blade rows** of the compressor and turbine are essentially **annular cascades of airfoils**. The same $C_L$ and $C_D$ framework applies, but with two key differences:
1. The flow approaches the airfoil at a much larger angle (often called the **inlet flow angle** or **inlet metal angle**).
2. The airfoils interact with neighbors through cascade effects — this is covered in the cascade theory module. The fundamental lift–drag coefficient definitions, however, remain identical to the 2-D airfoil case.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Lift from Pressure Distribution

Consider an airfoil of unit span (depth into the page = 1 m) immersed in a uniform flow $V_\infty$. The pressure on the upper surface is $p_u(x)$ and on the lower surface is $p_l(x)$. Both vary along the chord $x$ from $0$ (LE) to $c$ (TE).

**Step 1 — Vertical force from a strip $dx$**

A small chordwise strip of width $dx$ has area $dA = 1 \cdot dx$. The net upward pressure force on this strip is:

$$dL' = \bigl[p_l(x) - p_u(x)\bigr]\, dx$$

**Step 2 — Integrate along the chord**

The total lift per unit span is the integral of this pressure difference along the entire chord:

$$
L = \int_{0}^{c} \bigl[p_l(x) - p_u(x)\bigr]\, dx
$$

**Step 3 — Introduce non-dimensional coordinate and pressure coefficient**

Define $x' = x/c$, so $dx = c\, dx'$. Define the pressure coefficient:

$$
C_p(x) = \frac{p(x) - p_\infty}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_\infty^{2}}
$$

Therefore $p(x) = p_\infty + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_\infty^{2}\, C_p(x)$.

**Step 4 — Substitute and simplify**

Substituting upper and lower surface pressures into the lift integral:

$$
L = \int_{0}^{c} \left[\left(p_\infty + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_\infty^{2}\, C_{p,l}\right) - \left(p_\infty + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_\infty^{2}\, C_{p,u}\right)\right] dx
$$

The $p_\infty$ terms cancel:

$$
L = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_\infty^{2} \int_{0}^{c} \bigl[C_{p,l}(x) - C_{p,u}(x)\bigr]\, dx
$$

**Step 5 — Define the lift coefficient**

By definition, $C_L = \dfrac{L}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_\infty^{2}\, c}$. Comparing:

$$
\boxed{\,C_L = \frac{1}{c}\int_{0}^{c} \bigl[C_{p,l}(x) - C_{p,u}(x)\bigr]\, dx\,}
$$

This is the **fundamental 2-D pressure-integral definition** of the lift coefficient, and is the most commonly tested identity in KTU Module 1.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Moment about the Leading Edge

The aerodynamic pitching moment about the leading edge is the integral of the pressure-induced torque from each chordwise strip.

**Step 1 — Moment from a strip $dx$**

The pressure difference at location $x$ creates a force $dL' = [p_l - p_u]\, dx$ acting at distance $x$ from the leading edge. The moment contribution is:

$$dM_{LE} = -x \cdot \bigl[p_l(x) - p_u(x)\bigr]\, dx$$

The negative sign indicates the standard **nose-down (pitching-down)** convention.

**Step 2 — Integrate along the chord**

$$
M_{LE} = -\int_{0}^{c} x \bigl[p_l(x) - p_u(x)\bigr]\, dx
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to non-dimensional form**

Substitute the pressure coefficient and let $x = c\, x'$:

$$
M_{LE} = -\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_\infty^{2} \int_{0}^{c} x \bigl[C_{p,l}(x) - C_{p,u}(x)\bigr]\, dx
$$

With $x = c\, x'$ and $dx = c\, dx'$:

$$
M_{LE} = -\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_\infty^{2} c^{2} \int_{0}^{1} x' \bigl[C_{p,l} - C_{p,u}\bigr]\, dx'
$$

**Step 4 — Define the moment coefficient**

By definition $C_{M,LE} = \dfrac{M_{LE}}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_\infty^{2}\, c^{2}}$:

$$
\boxed{\,C_{M,LE} = -\int_{0}^{1} x' \bigl[C_{p,l}(x') - C_{p,u}(x')\bigr]\, dx'\,}
$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation: NACA 4-Digit Airfoil Thickness Distribution

The NACA 4-digit series defines the **thickness distribution** $y_t(x)$ for the second and third digits as a percentage of chord. The standard equation is:

$$
y_t(x) = 5\,t \left[0.2969 \sqrt{x'} - 0.1260\, x' - 0.3516\, x'^{2} + 0.2843\, x'^{3} - 0.1015\, x'^{4}\right]
$$

where:
- $x' = x/c$ is the non-dimensional chordwise position.
- $t$ is the maximum thickness as a fraction of chord (e.g., $t = 0.12$ for NACA 0012).
- The constants are derived empirically to give a smooth, closed trailing edge.

The **first digit** $m$ denotes the maximum camber as a percentage of chord, and the **second digit** $n$ denotes the position of maximum camber at $n/10$ of the chord. The mean camber line is given by:

**For $0 \le x' \le m$:**

$$
y_c(x') = \frac{m}{p^{2}} \left(2p\, x' - x'^{2}\right)
$$

**For $m \le x' \le 1$ (using $p = m$ here for 4-digit):**

$$
y_c(x') = \frac{m}{(1-p)^{2}} \left[(1 - 2p) + 2p\, x' - x'^{2}\right]
$$

This completes the geometric construction of any NACA 4-digit airfoil — frequently asked in KTU Part A questions.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Concept | Origin / Source | Behaviour Trend |
|---|---|---|
| $C_L$ vs $\alpha$ | Pressure differential | Linear up to $\approx 12^{\circ}$, peaks at stall. |
| $C_D$ vs $\alpha$ | Friction + pressure drag | Minimum near $C_L = 0$, rises sharply after stall. |
| $C_M$ about LE | Camber + thickness | Negative (nose-down), becomes more negative with $\alpha$. |
| $C_M$ about $c/4$ | Aerodynamic center | Approximately **constant** for thin airfoils. |
| Thickness ratio $t/c$ | Geometric design | Higher $t/c$ → higher $C_{L,\max}$ but also higher drag. |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

    LE["LE Leading Edge"]
    TE["TE Trailing Edge"]

    CB["Maximum Camber"]
    VA["Free Stream V infinity"]
    AOA["Angle of Attack alpha"]
    LFT["Lift Force L"]
    DRG["Drag Force D"]

    CL --- TE

    MCL --- LCL

    TH --- LCL

```

    E --> F["Resolve L into Components"]

```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper Set B]**

Define the following terms with neat sketches:
(a) Chord line
(b) Mean camber line
(c) Thickness ratio

**Model Answer:**

(a) **Chord Line:** The straight imaginary line joining the **leading edge** and the **trailing edge** of an airfoil. Its length is called the **chord length $c$**.

(b) **Mean Camber Line:** The locus of points located **midway between the upper and lower surfaces** of the airfoil. It represents the "backbone" curvature of the airfoil.

(c) **Thickness Ratio:** Defined as $t/c$, the ratio of the **maximum thickness** of the airfoil to its **chord length**. For low-speed aerofoils it typically ranges from **0.10 to 0.15**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often describe *camber* as the maximum thickness. **Camber is the maximum curvature of the mean line**, NOT the thickness. Thickness is measured perpendicular to the chord between upper and lower surfaces.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Sample Paper]**

Differentiate between a **symmetric** and a **cambered** airfoil. Which one produces lift at zero geometric angle of attack?

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Symmetric Airfoil | Cambered Airfoil |
|---|---|---|
| Mean camber line | Coincides with chord line | Curved away from chord line |
| Camber value | Zero | Non-zero |
| Lift at $\alpha = 0$ | **Zero** | **Non-zero (positive)** |
| Example | NACA 0012 | NACA 2412 |

A **cambered airfoil** produces lift at zero geometric angle of attack because its curved shape causes higher velocity (lower pressure) on the upper surface even when the chord is aligned with the free stream. The symmetric airfoil has identical upper and lower surfaces at $\alpha = 0$, hence no net pressure difference and **no lift**.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

### Question A — Internal Choice Option 1

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted from Past Paper]**

(a) With a neat sketch, define an airfoil and label all its geometric features including chord line, mean camber line, leading edge, trailing edge, thickness, and camber. (7 Marks)

(b) For an airfoil in uniform flow at angle of attack $\alpha$, derive the expressions for **lift coefficient $C_L$** and **moment coefficient about the leading edge $C_{M,LE}$** in terms of the pressure coefficient distribution $C_p(x)$. (7 Marks)

---

### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Definition:**
An airfoil is the **two-dimensional cross-section** of a body (wing, blade, etc.) designed to produce a useful aerodynamic reaction (lift) when placed in a moving fluid.

**Geometric Features (with sketch description):**

1. **Leading Edge (LE):** Front-most rounded tip of the airfoil; first point of contact with the oncoming flow.
2. **Trailing Edge (TE):** Rear-most, sharp tip of the airfoil; point where flow leaves.
3. **Chord Line ($c$):** Straight line segment joining LE to TE. Length $= c$.
4. **Upper Surface (Suction Side):** Surface on which static pressure is typically lower (for positive lift).
5. **Lower Surface (Pressure Side):** Surface on which static pressure is typically higher.
6. **Mean Camber Line:** Curve traced by midpoints between upper and lower surfaces.
7. **Thickness ($t$):** Maximum perpendicular distance between upper and lower surfaces.
8. **Camber:** Maximum distance between mean camber line and chord line.

**Sketch (to be drawn by student):**

A typical sketch shows an airfoil with LE on the left (rounded), TE on the right (sharp), curved upper surface, mildly curved lower surface, chord line straight, mean camber line slightly curved above the chord, thickness $t$ marked perpendicular to chord near mid-chord, and angle of attack $\alpha$ between chord and free-stream direction.

---

### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:** Airfoil of chord $c$ in uniform flow $V_\infty$ at angle of attack $\alpha$. Pressure coefficient on upper surface $C_{p,u}(x)$ and on lower surface $C_{p,l}(x)$.

**Derivation of $C_L$:**

By definition:

$$
L = \int_{0}^{c} \bigl[p_l(x) - p_u(x)\bigr]\, dx
$$

Substitute $p = p_\infty + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_\infty^{2} C_p$:

$$
L = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_\infty^{2} \int_{0}^{c} \bigl[C_{p,l}(x) - C_{p,u}(x)\bigr]\, dx
$$

Divide by dynamic pressure $\times$ chord:

$$
\boxed{\,C_L = \frac{L}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_\infty^{2} c} = \frac{1}{c}\int_{0}^{c} \bigl[C_{p,l}(x) - C_{p,u}(x)\bigr]\, dx\,}
$$

**Derivation of $C_{M,LE}$:**

The moment of each chordwise strip about the LE is $-x \cdot [p_l(x) - p_u(x)]\, dx$ (negative for nose-down convention). Total moment:

$$
M_{LE} = -\int_{0}^{c} x \bigl[p_l(x) - p_u(x)\bigr]\, dx
$$

Substitute pressure coefficients and use $x = c\, x'$:

$$
M_{LE} = -\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_\infty^{2} c^{2} \int_{0}^{1} x' \bigl[C_{p,l}(x') - C_{p,u}(x')\bigr]\, dx'
$$

Divide by $\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_\infty^{2} c^{2}$:

$$
\boxed{\,C_{M,LE} = -\int_{0}^{1} x' \bigl[C_{p,l}(x') - C_{p,u}(x')\bigr]\, dx'\,}
$$

---

### Question B — Internal Choice Option 2

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Adapted Model Paper]**

(a) Explain the **NACA 4-digit airfoil nomenclature** with the standard equations for thickness and mean camber line distributions. (7 Marks)

(b) An airfoil of chord $1.2 \text{ m}$ is moving through air of density $1.225 \text{ kg/m}^{3}$ at $60 \text{ m/s}$. The lift and drag coefficients are $0.85$ and $0.025$ respectively. Calculate the **lift, drag, and the lift-to-drag ratio**. (7 Marks)

---

### Model Solution — Part (a)

**NACA 4-Digit System:**

A NACA 4-digit airfoil is denoted as **NACA MPXX**, where:
- **M** = maximum camber as % of chord.
- **P** = position of maximum camber in tenths of chord.
- **XX** = maximum thickness as % of chord.

**Example:** NACA 2412 → 2% camber at 40% chord, 12% thickness.

**Thickness Distribution:**

$$
y_t(x') = 5t \left[0.2969\sqrt{x'} - 0.1260\, x' - 0.3516\, x'^{2} + 0.2843\, x'^{3} - 0.1015\, x'^{4}\right]
$$

**Mean Camber Line (with $m = M/100$ and $p = P/10$):**

For $0 \le x' \le p$:

$$
y_c(x') = \frac{m}{p^{2}}\left(2p\, x' - x'^{2}\right)
$$

For $p \le x' \le 1$:

$$
y_c(x') = \frac{m}{(1-p)^{2}}\left[(1-2p) + 2p\, x' - x'^{2}\right]
$$

These equations allow complete reconstruction of the airfoil profile.

---

### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:**
- $c = 1.2 \text{ m}$
- $\rho = 1.225 \text{ kg/m}^{3}$
- $V_\infty = 60 \text{ m/s}$
- $C_L = 0.85$
- $C_D = 0.025$

**Step 1 — Compute dynamic pressure:**

$$
q_\infty = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_\infty^{2} = \tfrac{1}{2}(1.225)(60)^{2}
$$

$$
q_\infty = \tfrac{1}{2}(1.225)(3600) = 2205 \text{ N/m}^{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute lift per unit span:**

$$
L = C_L \cdot q_\infty \cdot c = (0.85)(2205)(1.2)
$$

$$
L = 2249.1 \text{ N/m}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute drag per unit span:**

$$
D = C_D \cdot q_\infty \cdot c = (0.025)(2205)(1.2)
$$

$$
D = 66.15 \text{ N/m}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute lift-to-drag ratio:**

$$
\frac{L}{D} = \frac{C_L}{C_D} = \frac{0.85}{0.025} = 34
$$

**Final Answers:**
- Lift $L = 2249.1 \text{ N/m}$
- Drag $D = 66.15 \text{ N/m}$
- $L/D = 34$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting to multiply by chord $c$** — students often write $L = C_L \cdot q_\infty$ which is incorrect; $C_L$ is normalized by chord.
> 2. **Unit mismatch** — if $c$ is given in mm, convert to meters FIRST.
> 3. **Misidentifying $L/D$** — it is dimensionless and equals $C_L/C_D$, NOT $L/D$ with raw force units (numerically same here but conceptually different).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 1 — Airfoil Basics:**

- **Airfoil** = 2-D cross-section designed to produce lift in a moving fluid.
- **Chord line** joins leading edge (rounded) to trailing edge (sharp).
- **Mean camber line** is the midpoint locus between upper and lower surfaces.
- **Camber** = max distance between mean camber line and chord line.
- **Thickness** = max perpendicular distance between upper and lower surfaces.
- **Thickness ratio** $t/c$ typically **0.10 to 0.15** for low-speed aerofoils.
- **Symmetric airfoil** has zero camber → zero lift at $\alpha = 0$.
- **Cambered airfoil** produces positive lift even at $\alpha = 0$.
- **Angle of attack $\alpha$** is between chord line and free-stream direction.
- **Lift $L = C_L \cdot \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_\infty^{2} c$** (per unit span).
- **Drag $D = C_D \cdot \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_\infty^{2} c$** (per unit span).
- **Moment $M = C_M \cdot \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_\infty^{2} c^{2}$** (per unit span).
- **Aerodynamic efficiency** $E = L/D = C_L/C_D$.
- **Pressure coefficient** $C_p = \dfrac{p - p_\infty}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_\infty^{2}}$.
- **Lift coefficient from pressure integration:**

$$
C_L = \frac{1}{c}\int_{0}^{c} \bigl[C_{p,l}(x) - C_{p,u}(x)\bigr]\, dx
$$

- **Moment coefficient about LE:**

$$
C_{M,LE} = -\int_{0}^{1} x' \bigl[C_{p,l}(x') - C_{p,u}(x')\bigr]\, dx'
$$

- **NACA 4-digit** code $MPXX$: $M$ = % camber, $P$ = position of camber (tenths of chord), $XX$ = % thickness.
- **Stall:** Sudden drop in $C_L$ beyond a critical angle $\alpha_{stall} \approx 15^{\circ}$ to $20^{\circ}$.
- **Aerodynamic center** ≈ quarter-chord point for thin airfoils; $C_{M,ac}$ is approximately constant with $\alpha$.
- **Gas turbine blade rows** are essentially cascades of highly cambered airfoils — same $C_L$–$C_D$ framework applies.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Gas Turbine Cycles - Open Cycle
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMET741 - GAS TURBINE AND JET PROPULSION > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Gas Turbine Cycles - Open Cycle # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard) The **Open Cycle Gas Turbine** is a rotary internal combustion power plant in which atmospheric air is drawn continuously into a **rotary compressor**, compressed to a high pressure, heated at **constant pressure** in a combustion chamber by burning fuel directly into the air stream, and then expanded through a **turbine** that produces shaft work. The exhaust gases are finally discharged back to the atmosphere — completing a continuous, once-through (open) thermodynamic loop. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Ideal Thermodynamic Representation:** The open cycle gas turbine is modelled on the **Brayton Cycle** (also called the **Joule Cycle** or **Constant Pressure Cycle**), which is the air-standard idealization of this plant. The four idealized processes of the open Brayton cycle are: | State Transition | Process | Description | |---|---|---| | 1 → 2 | Isentropic Compression | Air compressed in rotary compressor | | 2 → 3 | Constant Pressure Heat Addition | Fuel burned at constant pressure | | 3 → 4 | Isentropic Expansion | Hot gases expanded in turbine | | 4 → 1 | Constant Pressure Heat Rejection | Heat rejected at constant pressure to atmosphere | ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Breathing Power Plant" Imagine a person pedalling a bicycle while sitting in a closed room with an electric heater pointed at them: - **Inhalation (Process 1→2):** The cyclist pulls in fresh air and squeezes it harder (compression). - **Heating (Process 2→3):** The heater warms this compressed air at steady pressure — just like a combustion chamber heats the air-fuel mixture. - **Exhalation / Pedalling (Process 3→4):** The hot, pressurized air expands and pushes a turbine blade to turn the wheel (producing useful work). - **Cooling (Process 4→1):** The used-up air is exhaled back into the atmosphere. This perfectly mirrors an **open gas turbine** — air is *not* recirculated; it enters clean and leaves spent. > [!NOTE] > **Why "Open"?** Because the working fluid (air) is *not* recirculated in a closed loop — fresh air enters each cycle, unlike a closed cycle plant where the same gas is reused. This makes the open cycle mechanically simple and is the standard choice for **aircraft engines**, **peak-load power generation**, and **pipeline pumping stations**. ## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Reference Conditions - **Standard Atmospheric Conditions:** $T_1 = 298\text{ K} = 25^\circ\text{C}$, $P_1 = 1.013\text{ bar} = 101.325\text{ kPa}$ - **Air Standard Constants:** $C_p = 1.005\text{ kJ/kg·K}$, $\gamma = 1.4$, $R = 0.287\text{ kJ/kg·K}$, $C_v = 0.718\text{ kJ/kg·K}$ - **Typical Pressure Ratio Range:** $r_p = 4$ to $20$ (military jets may exceed $r_p = 30$) - **Typical Turbine Inlet Temperature (TIT):** $T_3 = 1000\text{ K}$ to $1600\text{ K}$ (modern materials) # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Air-Standard Assumptions for the Ideal Brayton Cycle For KTU board exam purposes, the following **assumptions** must always be stated when solving open cycle problems: 1. The working fluid is **air** which behaves as an **ideal gas**. 2. The specific heats ($C_p$ and $C_v$) are **constant** throughout the cycle (cold air-standard assumption). 3. All processes are internally **reversible**. 4. Compression (1→2) and Expansion (3→4) are **isentropic** (adiabatic + reversible). 5. Heat addition (2→3) and heat rejection (4→1) occur at **constant pressure**. 6. The kinetic and potential energy changes of the working fluid are **negligible**. 7.
