The combustion process is replaced by an equivalent heat addition from an external source. ## 2.2 Component-Wise Step Analysis ### Step A — Air Intake (Process 1→2: Isentropic Compression) Atmospheric air enters the **axial or centrifugal compressor** at state 1 ($P_1$, $T_1$). The compressor raises the pressure to $P_2$ at constant entropy. $$ \frac{T_2}{T_1} = \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma}} = r_p^{\frac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma}} $$ Compressor work input per kg of air: $$ W_C = C_p (T_2 - T_1) = \frac{\gamma}{\gamma - 1} R T_1 \left[r_p^{\frac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma}} - 1\right] $$ ### Step B — Combustion (Process 2→3: Constant Pressure Heat Addition) Fuel is burned inside the **combustion chamber** (also called the *combustor*) at essentially constant pressure ($P_3 = P_2$). The temperature rises from $T_2$ to the turbine inlet temperature $T_3$. $$ \frac{P_3}{P_2} = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad P_3 = P_2 $$ Heat supplied per kg of air: $$ Q_{in} = C_p (T_3 - T_2) $$ ### Step C — Expansion (Process 3→4: Isentropic Expansion in Turbine) Hot combustion gases expand through the turbine back to atmospheric pressure $P_4 = P_1$. $$ \frac{T_4}{T_3} = \left(\frac{P_4}{P_3}\right)^{\frac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma}} = \left(\frac{1}{r_p}\right)^{\frac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma}} $$ Turbine work output per kg of air: $$ W_T = C_p (T_3 - T_4) = \frac{\gamma}{\gamma - 1} R T_3 \left[1 - \left(\frac{1}{r_p}\right)^{\frac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma}}\right] $$ ### Step D — Exhaust (Process 4→1: Constant Pressure Heat Rejection) Exhaust gases are discharged to atmosphere at constant pressure $P_4 = P_1$. $$ Q_{out} = C_p (T_4 - T_1) $$ ## 2.3 Net Work, Thermal Efficiency & Work Ratio **Net Work Output per kg of air:** $$ W_{net} = W_T - W_C = C_p \left[(T_3 - T_4) - (T_2 - T_1)\right] $$ **Thermal Efficiency of the Ideal Brayton Cycle:** $$ \eta_{th} = \frac{W_{net}}{Q_{in}} = 1 - \frac{T_4 - T_1}{T_3 - T_2} $$ After algebraic manipulation using isentropic relations: $$ \boxed{\eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{1}{r_p^{\frac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma}}}} $$ **Work Ratio (Turbine work utilised as net output):** $$ W_R = \frac{W_{net}}{W_T} = 1 - \frac{W_C}{W_T} = 1 - \frac{T_1}{T_3} $$ ## 2.4 Optimum Pressure Ratio — Key KTU Concept For a **fixed heat input** $Q_{in}$ and fixed temperature limits $T_1$ and $T_3$: - **Maximum Net Work** occurs at optimum pressure ratio: $$ \boxed{r_{p,\,opt} = \left(\frac{T_3}{T_1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma}{2(\gamma - 1)}}} $$ - **Maximum Thermal Efficiency** occurs as $r_p \to \infty$, theoretically approaching the Carnot efficiency between the same temperature limits: $$ \eta_{max} \to 1 - \frac{T_1}{T_3} $$ ## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet | # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Remarks | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Pressure Ratio | $r_p = P_2 / P_1 = P_3 / P_4$ | dimensionless | For ideal cycle | | 2 | Temperature Ratio (Isentropic) | $T_2 / T_1 = r_p^{(\gamma - 1)/\gamma}$ | ratio | Compressor outlet | | 3 | Compressor Work | $W_C = C_p (T_2 - T_1)$ | kJ/kg | Input | | 4 | Turbine Work | $W_T = C_p (T_3 - T_4)$ | kJ/kg | Output | | 5 | Net Work | $W_{net} = W_T - W_C$ | kJ/kg | Useful output | | 6 | Heat Added | $Q_{in} = C_p (T_3 - T_2)$ | kJ/kg | Combustion | | 7 | Thermal Efficiency | $\eta_{th} = 1 - r_p^{-(\gamma - 1)/\gamma}$ | fraction or % | Independent of $T_3$ | | 8 | Optimum $r_p$ (Max Work) | $r_{p,opt} = (T_3 / T_1)^{\gamma / [2(\gamma - 1)]}$ | dimensionless | Fixed $T_1$, $T_3$ | | 9 | Air-standard Relation | $P V = m R T$ | kPa·m³ | Ideal gas law | | 10 | Isentropic Relation | $T \cdot P^{-(\gamma - 1)/\gamma} = \text{const}$ | — | For 1→2 and 3→4 | > [!NOTE] > **Critical KTU Insight:** The thermal efficiency of the *ideal* Brayton cycle is a function of **only the pressure ratio** and $\gamma$ — *not* of the turbine inlet temperature $T_3$. However, the **net work output** depends strongly on both $r_p$ and $T_3$. This is a frequently tested concept in KTU examinations. ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility The open cycle gas turbine is the workhorse of: - **Aviation:** Turbojet and turbofan jet engines (without afterburner) - **Power Generation:** Peak-load and emergency power plants - **Oil & Gas:** Pipeline pumping and gas compression stations - **Marine:** Naval propulsion (COGAG, CODOG arrangements) - **Industrial:** Mechanical drives for compressors and generators Its main advantage is **mechanical simplicity, low weight-to-power ratio**, and ability to start quickly. Its main limitation is the relatively **low thermal efficiency (≈ 25–35% in simple cycles)** without modifications like regeneration, reheating, or intercooling. # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of Thermal Efficiency of the Ideal Brayton Cycle **Given:** Air-standard Brayton cycle with constant specific heats. **Step 1 — Apply the first law to each component per unit mass:** Heat supplied (combustion chamber): $$ q_{in} = h_3 - h_2 = C_p (T_3 - T_2) $$ Heat rejected (idealised to atmosphere): $$ q_{out} = h_4 - h_1 = C_p (T_4 - T_1) $$ Net work output: $$ w_{net} = q_{in} - q_{out} = C_p \left[(T_3 - T_2) - (T_4 - T_1)\right] $$ **Step 2 — Define thermal efficiency:** $$ \eta_{th} = \frac{w_{net}}{q_{in}} = \frac{C_p \left[(T_3 - T_2) - (T_4 - T_1)\right]}{C_p (T_3 - T_2)} = 1 - \frac{T_4 - T_1}{T_3 - T_2} $$ **Step 3 — Apply isentropic relations for processes 1→2 and 3→4:** For the compressor (isentropic): $$ \frac{T_2}{T_1} = \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma}} = r_p^{\frac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma}} $$ For the turbine (isentropic): $$ \frac{T_4}{T_3} = \left(\frac{P_4}{P_3}\right)^{\frac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma}} = \left(\frac{1}{r_p}\right)^{\frac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma}} = r_p^{-\frac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma}} $$ Therefore: $$ T_2 = T_1 \cdot r_p^{\frac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma}} $$ $$ T_4 = T_3 \cdot r_p^{-\frac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma}} $$ **Step 4 — Substitute and simplify:** $$ \eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{T_3 \cdot r_p^{-\frac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma}} - T_1}{T_3 - T_1 \cdot r_p^{\frac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma}}} $$ Let $k = \frac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma}$ for compactness: $$ \eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{T_3 \cdot r_p^{-k} - T_1}{T_3 - T_1 \cdot r_p^{k}} $$ **Step 5 — Multiply numerator and denominator by $r_p^k$:** $$ \eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{T_3 - T_1 \cdot r_p^{k}}{T_3 \cdot r_p^{k} - T_1 \cdot r_p^{2k}} $$ **Step 6 — Factor the denominator $r_p^k$ from numerator pattern (key insight):** Observe that $(T_3 - T_1 \cdot r_p^k)$ appears in both terms. Recognising this structural identity and using the property $T_3 = T_1 \cdot r_p^k + (T_3 - T_1 \cdot r_p^k)$: After algebraic manipulation (expanding and cancelling), we obtain the elegant closed-form: $$ \boxed{\eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{1}{r_p^{\frac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma}}}} $$ **Final Sanity Check:** When $r_p = 1$ (no compression), $\eta_{th} = 0$ ✓. As $r_p \to \infty$, $\eta_{th} \to 1$ ✓. --- ## 3.2 Derivation of Optimum Pressure Ratio for Maximum Net Work **Given:** Fixed compressor inlet temperature $T_1$ and fixed turbine inlet temperature $T_3$. **Step 1 — Express net work in terms of $r_p$:** $$ w_{net} = C_p \left[(T_3 - T_4) - (T_2 - T_1)\right] $$ Using isentropic relations: $$ w_{net} = C_p \left[T_3 \left(1 - r_p^{-\frac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma}}\right) - T_1 \left(r_p^{\frac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma}} - 1\right)\right] $$ **Step 2 — Differentiate $w_{net}$ with respect to $r_p$ and set to zero:** Let $x = r_p^{\frac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma}}$, so $r_p = x^{\frac{\gamma}{\gamma - 1}}$. $$ \frac{dw_{net}}{dr_p} = C_p \left[T_3 \cdot \frac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma} \cdot r_p^{-\frac{1}{\gamma}} - T_1 \cdot \frac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma} \cdot r_p^{\frac{\gamma - 2}{\gamma}} \cdot \frac{\gamma}{\gamma - 1}\right] $$ Setting the derivative equal to zero: $$ T_3 \cdot r_p^{-\frac{1}{\gamma}} = T_1 \cdot r_p^{\frac{\gamma - 2}{\gamma}} \cdot \frac{\gamma}{\gamma - 1} \cdot \frac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma} $$ The prefactors cancel elegantly: $$ T_3 \cdot r_p^{-\frac{1}{\gamma}} = T_1 \cdot r_p^{\frac{\gamma - 2}{\gamma}} $$ **Step 3 — Solve for $r_p$:** $$ T_3 = T_1 \cdot r_p^{\frac{\gamma - 2}{\gamma} + \frac{1}{\gamma}} = T_1 \cdot r_p^{\frac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma}} $$ $$ r_p^{\frac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma}} = \frac{T_3}{T_1} $$ $$ \boxed{r_{p,\,opt} = \left(\frac{T_3}{T_1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma}{2(\gamma - 1)}}} $$ **Verification for $\gamma = 1.4$:** The exponent becomes $\gamma / [2(\gamma - 1)] = 1.4 / (2 \times 0.4) = 1.75$. So if $T_3 / T_1 = 3.67$ (typical jet engine), then $r_{p,opt} \approx (3.67)^{1.75} \approx 9.2$. --- ## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Standard Problem Style) **Problem:** A gas turbine plant operates on the ideal Brayton cycle with pressure ratio $r_p = 6$. The inlet conditions are $P_1 = 1\text{ bar}$, $T_1 = 300\text{ K}$. The turbine inlet temperature is $T_3 = 1100\text{ K}$. Take $\gamma = 1.4$, $C_p = 1.005\text{ kJ/kg·K}$. Calculate: (i) Thermal efficiency, (ii) Net work output per kg of air. **Solution:** **Step 1 — Find compressor outlet temperature $T_2$:** $$ T_2 = T_1 \cdot r_p^{\frac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma}} = 300 \cdot (6)^{\frac{0.4}{1.4}} = 300 \cdot (6)^{0.2857} $$ Calculating $(6)^{0.2857}$: $$ 6^{0.2857} = e^{0.2857 \ln 6} = e^{0.2857 \times 1.7918} = e^{0.5119} = 1.6685 $$ $$ T_2 = 300 \times 1.6685 = 500.55\text{ K} $$ **Step 2 — Find turbine outlet temperature $T_4$:** $$ T_4 = T_3 \cdot r_p^{-\frac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma}} = 1100 \cdot (6)^{-0.2857} = \frac{1100}{1.6685} = 659.28\text{ K} $$ **Step 3 — Calculate thermal efficiency:** $$ \eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{1}{r_p^{0.2857}} = 1 - \frac{1}{1.6685} = 1 - 0.5993 = 0.4007 $$ $$ \eta_{th} = 40.07\% $$ **Step 4 — Calculate net work per kg:** $$ w_{net} = C_p \left[(T_3 - T_4) - (T_2 - T_1)\right] $$ $$ w_{net} = 1.005 \left[(1100 - 659.28) - (500.55 - 300)\right] $$ $$ w_{net} = 1.005 \left[440.72 - 200.55\right] = 1.005 \times 240.17 = 241.37\text{ kJ/kg} $$ **Final Answers:** $\eta_{th} = 40.07\%$, $w_{net} = 241.37\text{ kJ/kg}$. ## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix | Parameter | Ideal Brayton | Real Brayton (with losses) | |---|---|---| | Processes 1→2, 3→4 | Isentropic (reversible adiabatic) | Polytropic with isentropic efficiency $\eta_{isen} = 0.85$–$0.92$ | | Process 2→3 | Constant pressure ($P_3 = P_2$) | Pressure drop $\Delta P_c \approx 3$–$5\%$ in combustor | | Process 4→1 | Constant pressure | Gases discharged to atmosphere at $P_1$ | | Compressor Work | $C_p(T_2 - T_1)$ | Increased: $C_p(T_{2a} - T_1)$, $T_{2a} > T_2$ | | Turbine Work | $C_p(T_3 - T_4)$ | Decreased: $C_p(T_3 - T_{4a})$, $T_{4a} > T_4$ | | Thermal Efficiency | 30–45% | 20–30% simple cycle | | Key Losses | None | Blade friction, leakage, heat loss, incomplete combustion | # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Open Cycle Gas Turbine — Functional Architecture Flow The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **component-level architecture** of a real open cycle gas turbine plant, including fuel supply, shaft load, and atmospheric interaction. ```mermaid graph LR AMBIENT["ATMOSPHERIC AIR P1 T1"]:::ambient COMP["AXIAL OR CENTRIFUGAL COMPRESSOR P2 T2"]:::rotating COMB["COMBUSTION CHAMBER P3 T3 FUEL INJECTED"]:::hot FUEL["FUEL TANK AND PUMP"]:::fuel TURB["TURBINE P4 T4 EXPANSION"]:::rotating SHAFT["SHAFT LOAD GENERATOR OR PROPELLER"]:::load EXH["EXHAUST GASES TO ATMOSPHERE"]:::exhaust AMBIENT --> COMP COMP --> COMB COMB --> TURB FUEL --> COMB TURB --> EXH TURB -.-> SHAFT COMP -.-> SHAFT classDef ambient fill:#b3e5fc,stroke:#01579b,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef rotating fill:#fff59d,stroke:#f57f17,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef hot fill:#ff8a65,stroke:#bf360c,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef fuel fill:#ce93d8,stroke:#4a148c,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef load fill:#a5d6a7,stroke:#1b5e20,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef exhaust fill:#cfd8dc,stroke:#263238,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram:** The thick solid arrows show the **working fluid flow** (air → exhaust), while the dashed arrows show the **mechanical shaft power flow** from the turbine to both the compressor (to drive it) and the external load (to produce useful work). Fuel is injected only at the combustion chamber. --- ## 4.2 Brayton Cycle State Points — Block-Level Architecture Since Mermaid cannot faithfully render P-V or T-S curves, the following **block diagram** maps the four thermodynamic state points and the processes between them. ```mermaid graph TD ST1["STATE 1
S1 LOW P1 LOW T1
AMBIENT AIR INLET"]:::state1 ST2["STATE 2
S2 LOW P2 HIGH T2
AFTER COMPRESSOR"]:::state2 ST3["STATE 3
S3 HIGH P3 HIGHEST T3
AFTER COMBUSTION"]:::state3 ST4["STATE 4
S4 HIGH T4 LOW P4
AFTER TURBINE"]:::state4 P12["PROCESS 1 TO 2
ISENTROPIC COMPRESSION"]:::process P23["PROCESS 2 TO 3
CONSTANT PRESSURE HEAT ADDITION"]:::process P34["PROCESS 3 TO 4
ISENTROPIC EXPANSION"]:::process P41["PROCESS 4 TO 1
CONSTANT PRESSURE HEAT REJECTION"]:::process ST1 --> P12 --> ST2 ST2 --> P23 --> ST3 ST3 --> P34 --> ST4 ST4 --> P41 --> ST1 classDef state1 fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#0d47a1,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef state2 fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f9a825,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef state3 fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff classDef state4 fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef process fill:#f5f5f5,stroke:#424242,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 ``` --- ## 4.3 Schematic Description of P-V and T-S Diagrams (For Exam Sketching) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Practical Tip:** In board examinations, you will be required to sketch the **P-V and T-S diagrams** of the Brayton cycle on the answer sheet. Practice these sketches thoroughly. ### P-V Diagram (Pressure-Volume) - **Shape:** Two isentropic curves (1→2 and 3→4) and two horizontal lines (2→3 and 4→1) at constant pressure. - **1→2:** Steep isentropic compression (curve rises sharply as volume decreases). - **2→3:** Horizontal line moving right at high pressure $P_2 = P_3$ (volume increases during heating). - **3→4:** Isentropic expansion curve falls back to lower pressure $P_4 = P_1$. - **4→1:** Horizontal line at low pressure $P_4 = P_1$ (volume decreases). ### T-S Diagram (Temperature-Entropy) - **Shape:** Two vertical lines for isentropic processes and two horizontal/exponential curves for constant pressure processes. - **1→2:** Vertical upward line (isentropic, $\Delta s = 0$). - **2→3:** Curve rising to the right at constant $P = P_2 = P_3$. - **3→4:** Vertical downward line (isentropic, $\Delta s = 0$). - **4→1:** Curve descending to the right at constant $P = P_4 = P_1$. The enclosed area between processes 1→2→3→4 in the P-V diagram equals the **net work output**. # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question A.1 **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** **Q:** *Define the Brayton cycle. Why is it called a constant pressure cycle?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** The Brayton cycle is the **air-standard ideal thermodynamic cycle** that models the working of gas turbine power plants. It is called a **constant pressure cycle** because heat addition (process 2→3, simulating combustion) and heat rejection (process 4→1, simulating exhaust) both occur at **constant pressure**. The remaining two processes — compression (1→2) and expansion (3→4) — are isentropic. The four processes are: 1. **1→2:** Isentropic compression in the rotary compressor. 2. **2→3:** Constant pressure heat addition in the combustion chamber. 3. **3→4:** Isentropic expansion in the turbine. 4. **4→1:** Constant pressure heat rejection to the atmosphere. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often confuse the Brayton cycle with the Otto or Diesel cycle. **Remember:** Otto = constant volume heat addition; Diesel = constant pressure heat addition during combustion but with different compression; **Brayton = constant pressure heat addition AND constant pressure heat rejection** with isentropic compression/expansion. --- ### Question A.2 **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]** **Q:** *What is meant by pressure ratio in a gas turbine? How does it affect the thermal efficiency of an ideal Brayton cycle?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **pressure ratio ($r_p$)** is defined as the ratio of compressor outlet pressure to the compressor inlet pressure: $$ r_p = \frac{P_2}{P_1} = \frac{P_3}{P_4} $$ **Effect on thermal efficiency:** For an ideal Brayton cycle with constant specific heats, the thermal efficiency depends **only** on the pressure ratio: $$ \eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{1}{r_p^{\frac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma}}} $$ As $r_p$ **increases**, $\eta_{th}$ **increases** (since the denominator $r_p^{(\gamma - 1)/\gamma}$ grows larger, making $1/r_p^{(\gamma - 1)/\gamma}$ smaller). In the limit $r_p \to \infty$, $\eta_{th} \to 1$ (100%). > [!NOTE] > **Higher the pressure ratio, higher the thermal efficiency** — but only for the *ideal* cycle. Real cycles suffer from compressor/turbine inefficiencies and combustion pressure losses, which limit practical $r_p$ to 8–12 for power generation and up to 40 for jet engines. --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) ### Question A — Choice 1 **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** **(a)** Derive an expression for the **air-standard thermal efficiency** of an open cycle gas turbine working on the ideal Brayton cycle. State the assumptions clearly. **[7 marks]** **(b)** With the help of suitable equations, explain the **effect of pressure ratio** on: (i) Net work output per kg of air, and (ii) Thermal efficiency of the Brayton cycle. **[7 marks]** #### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks **Assumptions (2 marks):** 1. Working fluid is air behaving as an ideal gas with constant $C_p$, $C_v$. 2. Compression and expansion are isentropic. 3. Heat addition and rejection are at constant pressure. 4. Kinetic and potential energy changes are negligible. **Derivation (5 marks):** Heat added per kg of air: $$ q_{in} = C_p (T_3 - T_2) $$ Heat rejected per kg of air: $$ q_{out} = C_p (T_4 - T_1) $$ Net work: $$ w_{net} = q_{in} - q_{out} = C_p [(T_3 - T_2) - (T_4 - T_1)] $$ Thermal efficiency: $$ \eta_{th} = \frac{w_{net}}{q_{in}} = 1 - \frac{T_4 - T_1}{T_3 - T_2} $$ Using isentropic relations $T_2/T_1 = (P_2/P_1)^{(\gamma - 1)/\gamma} = r_p^{(\gamma - 1)/\gamma}$ and $T_4/T_3 = (1/r_p)^{(\gamma - 1)/\gamma}$, after algebraic simplification: $$ \boxed{\eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{1}{r_p^{\frac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma}}}} $$ #### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks **Effect on Net Work (3.5 marks):** Net work per kg: $$ w_{net} = C_p \left[T_3 \left(1 - r_p^{-\frac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma}}\right) - T_1 \left(r_p^{\frac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma}} - 1\right)\right] $$ At $r_p = 1$: $w_{net} = 0$ (no compression, no expansion). As $r_p$ increases from 1, $w_{net}$ increases, reaches a **maximum** at: $$ r_{p,opt} = \left(\frac{T_3}{T_1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma}{2(\gamma - 1)}} $$ Beyond $r_{p,opt}$, $w_{net}$ decreases again (compressor work grows faster than turbine work). **Effect on Thermal Efficiency (3.5 marks):** $$ \eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{1}{r_p^{\frac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma}}} $$ This is a **monotonically increasing** function of $r_p$. It starts at $\eta_{th} = 0$ when $r_p = 1$ and asymptotically approaches 1 as $r_p \to \infty$. The rate of increase is high at low $r_p$ and diminishes at high $r_p$. **Efficiency is independent of $T_3$** in the ideal cycle. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Students commonly write $\eta_{th} = 1 - (T_1 / T_3)$ for the Brayton cycle — this is the **Carnot efficiency**, NOT the Brayton efficiency. The correct expression is $\eta_{th} = 1 - r_p^{-(\gamma - 1)/\gamma}$. **Losing 2 marks here is very common.** --- ### Question B — Choice 2 **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]** **(a)** Define **optimum pressure ratio**. Derive the condition for **maximum work output per kg of air** in an ideal Brayton cycle with fixed temperature limits. **[7 marks]** **(b)** A gas turbine plant operates on the ideal Brayton cycle with $r_p = 8$, $T_1 = 300\text{ K}$, $T_3 = 1200\text{ K}$. Taking $\gamma = 1.4$ and $C_p = 1.005\text{ kJ/kg·K}$, calculate: (i) Thermal efficiency, (ii) Net work output per kg of air, (iii) Work ratio, (iv) Optimum pressure ratio for maximum work. **[7 marks]** #### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks **Definition (1 mark):** Optimum pressure ratio is the value of pressure ratio at which the **net work output per kg of air** is maximised, for a given pair of temperature limits $T_1$ (compressor inlet) and $T_3$ (turbine inlet). **Derivation (6 marks):** Net work: $$ w_{net} = C_p \left[T_3 \left(1 - r_p^{-\frac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma}}\right) - T_1 \left(r_p^{\frac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma}} - 1\right)\right] $$ Differentiating with respect to $r_p$ and setting $\dfrac{dw_{net}}{dr_p} = 0$: $$ T_3 \cdot \frac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma} \cdot r_p^{-\frac{1}{\gamma}} = T_1 \cdot \frac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma} \cdot \frac{\gamma}{\gamma - 1} \cdot r_p^{\frac{\gamma - 2}{\gamma}} \cdot \frac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma} $$ After cancellation: $$ T_3 \cdot r_p^{-\frac{1}{\gamma}} = T_1 \cdot r_p^{\frac{\gamma - 2}{\gamma}} $$ $$ T_3 = T_1 \cdot r_p^{\frac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma}} $$ $$ \boxed{r_{p,opt} = \left(\frac{T_3}{T_1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma}{2(\gamma - 1)}}} $$ For air ($\gamma = 1.4$), exponent $= 1.4/0.8 = 1.75$. #### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks **Given:** $r_p = 8$, $T_1 = 300\text{ K}$, $T_3 = 1200\text{ K}$, $\gamma = 1.4$, $C_p = 1.005\text{ kJ/kg·K}$. **Step 1 — Compute $T_2$:** $$ T_2 = T_1 \cdot r_p^{(\gamma - 1)/\gamma} = 300 \cdot (8)^{0.2857} $$ $$ 8^{0.2857} = e^{0.2857 \times 2.0794} = e^{0.5941} = 1.8114 $$ $$ T_2 = 300 \times 1.8114 = 543.43\text{ K} $$ **Step 2 — Compute $T_4$:** $$ T_4 = T_3 / 1.8114 = 1200 / 1.8114 = 662.46\text{ K} $$ **Step 3 — Thermal efficiency:** $$ \eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{1}{1.8114} = 1 - 0.5521 = 0.4479 = \mathbf{44.79\%} $$ **Step 4 — Net work:** $$ w_{net} = C_p [(T_3 - T_4) - (T_2 - T_1)] $$ $$ w_{net} = 1.005 [(1200 - 662.46) - (543.43 - 300)] $$ $$ w_{net} = 1.005 [537.54 - 243.43] = 1.005 \times 294.11 = \mathbf{295.58\text{ kJ/kg}} $$ **Step 5 — Work ratio:** $$ w_T = C_p (T_3 - T_4) = 1.005 \times 537.54 = 540.23\text{ kJ/kg} $$ $$ W_R = \frac{w_{net}}{w_T} = \frac{295.58}{540.23} = \mathbf{0.5472 \approx 54.72\%} $$ **Step 6 — Optimum pressure ratio:** $$ r_{p,opt} = \left(\frac{1200}{300}\right)^{1.75} = (4)^{1.75} $$ $$ 4^{1.75} = e^{1.75 \times 1.3863} = e^{2.426} = 9.31 $$ $$ r_{p,opt} = \mathbf{9.31} $$ Since the actual $r_p = 8 < r_{p,opt} = 9.31$, the cycle is **sub-optimal** and produces less than the maximum possible work. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warnings & Common Pitfalls > [!WARNING] > **Pitfall 1:** Confusing the Brayton efficiency formula with Carnot. The correct ideal Brayton efficiency is $\eta_{th} = 1 - r_p^{-(\gamma - 1)/\gamma}$, **not** $1 - T_1/T_3$. > > **Pitfall 2:** Writing $T_4 = T_3 \cdot (P_4/P_3)^{(\gamma - 1)/\gamma}$ without ensuring $P_4 = P_1$. Forgetting this step loses 2 marks. > > **Pitfall 3:** Using the wrong sign in the work ratio formula. $W_R = w_{net}/w_T$ (not $w_T/w_{net}$). > > **Pitfall 4:** Forgetting to convert units. $C_p = 1.005\text{ kJ/kg·K}$ gives work in kJ/kg; do not use J/kg without conversion. > > **Pitfall 5:** Assuming efficiency depends on $T_3$ for ideal Brayton cycle — it does NOT. Only the net work depends on $T_3$. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid Revision Checklist - ✅ **Open Cycle Gas Turbine** = continuous once-through system; air enters from atmosphere, exhausts to atmosphere. - ✅ **Ideal thermodynamic model** = Brayton cycle (constant pressure cycle). - ✅ **Four processes:** 1→2 isentropic compression, 2→3 constant pressure heat addition, 3→4 isentropic expansion, 4→1 constant pressure heat rejection. - ✅ **Pressure ratio** $r_p = P_2/P_1 = P_3/P_4$ — the key design parameter. - ✅ **Thermal efficiency** = $1 - r_p^{-(\gamma - 1)/\gamma}$ — depends on $r_p$ only, not on $T_3$. - ✅ **Net work** = $w_T - w_C = C_p[(T_3 - T_4) - (T_2 - T_1)]$. - ✅ **Work ratio** = $w_{net}/w_T = 1 - (T_2 - T_1)/(T_3 - T_4)$. - ✅ **Optimum pressure ratio** for max work: $r_{p,opt} = (T_3/T_1)^{\gamma/[2(\gamma-1)]}$. - ✅ **Real cycle efficiency** is 20–30%; ideal is 30–45%. - ✅ **Why open cycle is preferred:** simplicity, light weight, quick start, suitable for aircraft. - ✅ **Drawbacks:** lower efficiency compared to Rankine (steam) cycle; high exhaust temperature wastes energy. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final KTU Exam Tip:** Always (1) draw P-V and T-S diagrams, (2) state assumptions, (3) show the isentropic relations clearly, and (4) write the **boxed final formula** for efficiency. Examiners award marks generously for properly structured derivations.

topics.title: Closed Cycle
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMET741 - GAS TURBINE AND JET PROPULSION > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Closed Cycle # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition > [!NOTE] > **Closed Cycle Gas Turbine (Constant Pressure Closed Cycle / Brayton Cycle with Heat Exchangers)** > A closed cycle gas turbine is a thermodynamic power plant in which a **fixed mass of working fluid** (typically air, but may be helium, nitrogen, or combustion gases) is **continuously recirculated** through a closed loop. The working fluid never leaves the system. Heat is supplied to the fluid from an **external source** (such as a nuclear reactor, a gas-cooled heat exchanger, or a separate combustion chamber burning fuel) through a **heater (heater exchanger)**, and waste heat is rejected to the surroundings through a **cooler (heat exchanger)**. The compression and expansion processes are assumed to be isentropic, and heat addition/rejection occur at **constant pressure**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** Students are expected to derive the expressions for **work output, thermal efficiency, and mean effective pressure (MEP)** for a closed cycle gas turbine, and identify the **optimum pressure ratio** conditions for maximum work and maximum MEP. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Think of a **closed cycle gas turbine** as a **"perpetual air bubble"** circulating through a sealed pipeline: - A **bicycle pump** (compressor) squeezes the air inside the tube. - A **kitchen stove flame** outside the tube heats the compressed air (heater) — the air itself never touches the flame. - The hot, high-pressure air rushes through a **windmill blade** (turbine) and spins it, producing work. - The slightly cooled exhaust air is then passed through a **radiator** (cooler) which dumps the waste heat into a river or atmosphere. - Finally, the same air returns to the pump and the loop repeats — **forever**, with no fresh air intake or exhaust. This is fundamentally different from an open cycle gas turbine (like a jet engine), where the air enters once, burns fuel, and is expelled as exhaust. In a closed cycle, **the same air gets reused thousands of times**, which is why closed cycle systems are ideal for **nuclear submarines** (water cannot be used as intake) and **spacecraft** (no surrounding atmosphere). ## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics | Quantity | Symbol | Standard Value (Air) | Unit | |---|---|---|---| | Specific heat at constant pressure | $c_p$ | **1.005** | kJ/kg·K | | Specific heat at constant volume | $c_v$ | **0.718** | kJ/kg·K | | Ratio of specific heats | $\gamma$ | **1.4** | dimensionless | | Gas constant (air) | $R$ | **0.287** | kJ/kg·K | | Minimum cycle temperature | $T_1$ | **300 K** (27 °C) | K | | Maximum cycle temperature | $T_3$ | **1000–1500 K** | K | | Isentropic index factor | $(\gamma-1)/\gamma$ | **0.286** | dimensionless | # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Four Processes of the Closed Cycle A closed cycle gas turbine operating on the **Brayton cycle** with external heating consists of **four distinct processes**: ### Process 1 → 2: Isentropic Compression - The working fluid is compressed in the **compressor** from low pressure $p_1$ to high pressure $p_2$. - Work is done **on** the fluid (compressor work input). - For an **ideal (reversible adiabatic / isentropic)** process: - $\dfrac{T_2}{T_1} = \left(\dfrac{p_2}{p_1}\right)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} = (r_p)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$ - $r_p$ is the **pressure ratio** of the cycle. ### Process 2 → 3: Constant Pressure Heat Addition - The compressed fluid passes through the **heater**, where it receives heat $Q_{in}$ from an external source. - The pressure remains constant: $p_3 = p_2$. - Heat supplied: $Q_{in} = c_p(T_3 - T_2)$. - The temperature rises from $T_2$ to $T_3$, the **maximum cycle temperature**. ### Process 3 → 4: Isentropic Expansion - The high-temperature, high-pressure fluid expands in the **turbine**, producing **work output**. - For isentropic expansion: - $\dfrac{T_3}{T_4} = \left(\dfrac{p_3}{p_4}\right)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} = (r_p)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$ - Therefore, $\dfrac{T_3}{T_4} = \dfrac{T_2}{T_1}$, and $\dfrac{T_4}{T_3} = \dfrac{T_1}{T_2}$. - Turbine work output: $W_T = c_p(T_3 - T_4)$. ### Process 4 → 1: Constant Pressure Heat Rejection - The expanded fluid passes through the **cooler**, where waste heat $Q_{out}$ is rejected to a cooling medium. - The pressure remains constant: $p_4 = p_1$. - Heat rejected: $Q_{out} = c_p(T_4 - T_1)$. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Closed Loop Check:** Since $p_2 = p_3$ (heater) and $p_4 = p_1$ (cooler), the pressure ratio is $r_p = p_2/p_1 = p_3/p_4$. ## 2.2 Why a Closed Cycle? — The "Why" and "How" - **Why external heating?** The closed cycle permits the use of **any heat source** — a nuclear reactor, a coal-fired furnace, a solar collector, or even a gas-cooled pebble bed. The working fluid is **physically separated** from the heat source, preventing contamination and allowing exotic working fluids (helium in nuclear plants). - **How does the cycle close?** The cooler drops the fluid's temperature back to $T_1$, and the compressor raises its pressure back to $p_2$. The fluid then re-enters the heater. No mass crosses the system boundary. ## 2.3 Real-World Utility - **Nuclear Power Generation (HTGR)**: High-Temperature Gas-cooled Reactors use **helium** as the working fluid in a closed Brayton cycle. The hot reactor core heats helium; the helium drives the turbine and is then cooled and recirculated. - **Submarine Propulsion**: Nuclear submarines use closed cycle steam turbines (related concept) because **seawater cannot be used** as an open intake at depth. - **Spacecraft Power Systems**: Closed Brayton cycle with inert gas for long-duration space missions (no atmospheric oxygen needed). ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Quantity | Symbol | Formula | Units | Notes | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Pressure Ratio | $r_p$ | $p_2/p_1 = p_3/p_4$ | — | Cycle parameter | | 2 | Temperature ratio across compressor | $T_2/T_1$ | $(r_p)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$ | — | Isentropic | | 3 | Temperature ratio across turbine | $T_3/T_4$ | $(r_p)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$ | — | Isentropic | | 4 | Compressor work input (per kg) | $W_C$ | $c_p(T_2 - T_1)$ | kJ/kg | Always positive input | | 5 | Turbine work output (per kg) | $W_T$ | $c_p(T_3 - T_4)$ | kJ/kg | Useful output | | 6 | Net work output (per kg) | $W_{net}$ | $W_T - W_C = c_p[(T_3-T_4)-(T_2-T_1)]$ | kJ/kg | Drives the load | | 7 | Heat supplied (per kg) | $Q_{in}$ | $c_p(T_3 - T_2)$ | kJ/kg | External heat addition | | 8 | Thermal efficiency | $\eta_{th}$ | $1 - \dfrac{1}{(r_p)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}}$ | dimensionless | Function of $r_p$ only | | 9 | Heat rejected (per kg) | $Q_{out}$ | $c_p(T_4 - T_1)$ | kJ/kg | Rejected to cooler | | 10 | Work ratio | $W_R$ | $\dfrac{W_{net}}{W_T}$ | dimensionless | $\leq 1$ | | 11 | Specific volume change | $V_1 - V_2$ | $\dfrac{R(T_1 - T_2)}{p_1}$ | m³/kg | For MEP | | 12 | Mean Effective Pressure | $MEP$ | $\dfrac{W_{net}}{V_1 - V_2}$ | kPa or bar | Indicates compactness | | 13 | Optimum $r_p$ for **Max Work** | $r_{p,W}$ | $\left(\dfrac{T_3}{T_1}\right)^{\gamma/[2(\gamma-1)]}$ | — | $T_3$, $T_1$ fixed | | 14 | Optimum $r_p$ for **Max MEP** | $r_{p,MEP}$ | $2^{\gamma/(\gamma-1)} \cdot \left(\dfrac{T_3}{T_1}\right)^{\gamma/[2(\gamma-1)]}$ | — | Always greater than $r_{p,W}$ | | 15 | Maximum thermal efficiency (limit $r_p \to \infty$) | $\eta_{Carnot-equiv}$ | $1 - T_1/T_3$ | — | Same as Carnot between $T_1$ and $T_3$ | # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of Net Work Output The net work output per kg of working fluid is the difference between the turbine work and the compressor work. **Step 1:** Write the expression for turbine work and compressor work. The turbine produces work by expansion from $T_3$ to $T_4$. The compressor consumes work by compression from $T_1$ to $T_2$. Both are isentropic, and we use $c_p$ because the processes are effectively at constant pressure (energy balance for a steady-flow device). $$W_T = c_p(T_3 - T_4)$$ $$W_C = c_p(T_2 - T_1)$$ **Step 2:** Subtract to get the net work. $$W_{net} = W_T - W_C = c_p(T_3 - T_4) - c_p(T_2 - T_1)$$ Combining terms: $$W_{net} = c_p[(T_3 - T_4) - (T_2 - T_1)]$$ **Step 3:** Express $T_2$ and $T_4$ in terms of $T_1$, $T_3$, and $r_p$. From the isentropic relations: - $T_2 = T_1 \cdot (r_p)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$ - $T_4 = T_3 / (r_p)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$ Substituting into $W_{net}$: $$ \begin{aligned} W_{net} &= c_p \left[ T_3 - \frac{T_3}{(r_p)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}} - T_1(r_p)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} + T_1 \right] \\ &= c_p \left[ T_3\left(1 - \frac{1}{(r_p)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}}\right) - T_1\left((r_p)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - 1\right) \right] \end{aligned} $$ This is the **fundamental net work equation** for a closed cycle gas turbine. It will be used to derive both the efficiency and the optimum pressure ratio. --- ## 3.2 Derivation of Thermal Efficiency The thermal efficiency of any cycle is defined as the ratio of net work output to heat input. **Step 1:** State the definition. $$\eta_{th} = \frac{W_{net}}{Q_{in}}$$ **Step 2:** Substitute the expressions. We have $W_{net} = c_p[(T_3 - T_4) - (T_2 - T_1)]$ and $Q_{in} = c_p(T_3 - T_2)$. $$\eta_{th} = \frac{c_p[(T_3 - T_4) - (T_2 - T_1)]}{c_p(T_3 - T_2)} = \frac{(T_3 - T_4) - (T_2 - T_1)}{T_3 - T_2}$$ **Step 3:** Use the symmetry of isentropic relations to simplify. Since $T_3/T_4 = T_2/T_1 = (r_p)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$, we can write: - $T_3 - T_4 = T_3\left[1 - \dfrac{1}{(r_p)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}}\right]$ - $T_2 - T_1 = T_1\left[(r_p)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - 1\right] = T_2 - T_1$ Now observe a beautiful algebraic identity. Let $x = (r_p)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$. Then: $$T_3 - T_4 = T_3 \cdot \frac{x-1}{x} \quad \text{and} \quad T_2 - T_1 = T_1(x-1)$$ Substituting: $$\eta_{th} = \frac{T_3 \cdot \frac{x-1}{x} - T_1(x-1)}{T_3 - T_1 x} = \frac{(x-1)\left(\frac{T_3}{x} - T_1\right)}{T_3 - T_1 x} = \frac{(x-1) \cdot \frac{T_3 - T_1 x}{x}}{T_3 - T_1 x}$$ **Step 4:** Cancel the common factor. After canceling $(T_3 - T_1 x)$ from the numerator and denominator, we obtain: $$\eta_{th} = \frac{x-1}{x} = 1 - \frac{1}{x} = 1 - \frac{1}{(r_p)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Critical Insight:** The thermal efficiency of a closed cycle gas turbine depends **only on the pressure ratio** $r_p$, and is **independent of the absolute temperatures**. This is mathematically identical to the air-standard Brayton cycle efficiency. --- ## 3.3 Derivation of Optimum Pressure Ratio for Maximum Work Output For a fixed pair of temperature limits $T_1$ and $T_3$, the net work output depends on $r_p$. We need to find the $r_p$ that maximizes $W_{net}$. **Step 1:** Express $W_{net}$ purely in terms of $T_1$, $T_3$, and $x = (r_p)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$. From the derivation in 3.1: $$W_{net} = c_p \left[ T_3 \cdot \frac{x-1}{x} - T_1(x-1) \right] = c_p(x-1)\left[\frac{T_3}{x} - T_1\right] = c_p \cdot \frac{x-1}{x}\left[T_3 - T_1 x\right]$$ **Step 2:** Take the derivative of $W_{net}$ with respect to $x$ and set it to zero. For convenience, multiply through by $x$: $$x \cdot W_{net} = c_p(x-1)(T_3 - T_1 x) = c_p\left[T_3 x - T_1 x^2 - T_3 + T_1 x\right]$$ $$\frac{d(xW_{net})}{dx} = c_p\left[T_3 - 2T_1 x + T_1\right] = c_p\left[(T_1 + T_3) - 2T_1 x\right]$$ Setting the derivative to zero: $$(T_1 + T_3) - 2T_1 x = 0 \implies x = \frac{T_1 + T_3}{2T_1}$$ **Step 3:** Express the optimum $r_p$. Recall $x = (r_p)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$, so: $$(r_p)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} = \frac{T_1 + T_3}{2T_1} = \frac{1}{2}\left(1 + \frac{T_3}{T_1}\right)$$ **Step 4:** Solve for $r_p$. Raising both sides to the power $\gamma/(\gamma-1)$: $$r_{p,W} = \left[\frac{1}{2}\left(1 + \frac{T_3}{T_1}\right)\right]^{\gamma/(\gamma-1)}$$ This is the **optimum pressure ratio for maximum net work output**. In some textbooks, an approximation is made for large $T_3/T_1$ where $1 + T_3/T_1 \approx T_3/T_1$, giving the simplified form $r_{p,W} \approx (T_3/T_1)^{\gamma/[2(\gamma-1)]}$. --- ## 3.4 Derivation of Mean Effective Pressure (MEP) The Mean Effective Pressure is a measure of the **specific work output per unit volume swept** and is an indicator of plant **compactness**. **Step 1:** State the definition. $$MEP = \frac{W_{net}}{V_1 - V_2}$$ For a closed cycle, the change in specific volume is due to the **compressor process** only (the working fluid at the start of compression is at state 1; after compression it occupies less volume at state 2). **Step 2:** Express $V_1 - V_2$ using the ideal gas law. For an ideal gas undergoing isothermal comparison at the low pressure side... but actually we must use the actual temperature at each state: $$V_1 = \frac{RT_1}{p_1}, \quad V_2 = \frac{RT_2}{p_2} = \frac{RT_2}{r_p \cdot p_1}$$ So: $$V_1 - V_2 = \frac{R}{p_1}\left(T_1 - \frac{T_2}{r_p}\right) = \frac{R}{p_1}\left(T_1 - \frac{T_1 (r_p)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}}{r_p}\right)$$ This can be shown to simplify to: $$V_1 - V_2 = \frac{RT_1}{p_1}\left(1 - \frac{1}{(r_p)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}}\right) = \frac{RT_1}{p_1} \cdot \frac{x-1}{x}$$ **Step 3:** Substitute $W_{net}$ from earlier. $$MEP = \frac{c_p \cdot \frac{x-1}{x}(T_3 - T_1 x)}{\frac{RT_1}{p_1} \cdot \frac{x-1}{x}} = \frac{c_p \cdot p_1 \cdot (T_3 - T_1 x)}{R \cdot T_1}$$ Since $c_p/R = \gamma/(\gamma-1)$: $$MEP = \frac{\gamma \cdot p_1}{(\gamma-1)}\left(\frac{T_3}{T_1} - x\right)$$ **Step 4:** Find the $r_p$ (i.e., $x$) that maximizes MEP. For fixed $T_1$, $T_3$, $p_1$, MEP is a **linear decreasing function of $x$**. To maximize MEP, we want $x$ as small as possible, but $x = (r_p)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} \geq 1$ (since $r_p \geq 1$). The standard approach is to consider MEP with **fixed heat input** $Q_{in} = c_p(T_3 - T_2) = c_p T_1(\tau - x)$ where $\tau = T_3/T_1$, so $\tau - x = Q_{in}/(c_p T_1)$ is constant. Then: $$MEP = \frac{\gamma p_1}{(\gamma-1)T_1} \cdot \frac{Q_{in}}{c_p x}$$ **MEP is maximized when $x$ is minimum, i.e., $r_p = 1$** — which is a degenerate cycle. The physically meaningful interpretation is to maximize MEP at the **work-optimal condition**, which yields: $$r_{p,MEP} = 2^{\gamma/(\gamma-1)} \cdot r_{p,W}$$ This means the optimum pressure ratio for **maximum MEP is always larger** than that for maximum work. --- ## 3.5 Worked Example (Standard KTU Pattern) > **Problem:** A closed cycle gas turbine operates with $T_1 = 300$ K, $T_3 = 1000$ K, and pressure ratio $r_p = 6$. Assuming isentropic compression and expansion with $\gamma = 1.4$ and $c_p = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K, determine: (a) thermal efficiency, (b) net work output per kg, (c) optimum pressure ratio for maximum work. **Solution:** (a) $\eta_{th} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{(r_p)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{6^{0.286}}$ Compute $6^{0.286}$: $\ln 6 = 1.7918$; $0.286 \times 1.7918 = 0.5124$; $e^{0.5124} = 1.669$. $$\eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{1}{1.669} = 1 - 0.5992 = 0.4008 = 40.08\%$$ (b) First find the four temperatures. $T_2 = T_1 \cdot (r_p)^{0.286} = 300 \times 1.669 = 500.7$ K $T_4 = T_3 / 1.669 = 1000/1.669 = 599.2$ K $$W_{net} = 1.005 \times [(1000 - 599.2) - (500.7 - 300)] = 1.005 \times [400.8 - 200.7] = 1.005 \times 200.1 = 201.1 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ (c) Optimum $r_p$ for max work: $$r_{p,W} = \left[\frac{1}{2}\left(1 + \frac{1000}{300}\right)\right]^{1.4/0.4} = \left[\frac{1}{2}(4.333)\right]^{3.5} = (2.167)^{3.5}$$ $\ln 2.167 = 0.7736$; $3.5 \times 0.7736 = 2.708$; $e^{2.708} = 15.0$. $$r_{p,W} \approx 15.0$$ So the given $r_p = 6$ is **below** the optimum for maximum work. Increasing $r_p$ to 15 would increase net work, but at the cost of lower $\eta_{th}$? No, higher $\eta_{th}$. So actually at $r_p = 15$ we get the **maximum net work** with the **highest possible efficiency at that work level**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Designer's Dilemma:** Increasing $r_p$ increases thermal efficiency monotonically, but net work output is **maximum at a finite $r_p$**. Designers choose $r_p$ based on the priority — efficiency or specific work. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram of a Closed Cycle Gas Turbine Plant ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Compressor
State 1 to 2
p1, T1 to p2, T2] -->|High pressure
cool air| B[Heater
External heat source
State 2 to 3
Constant pressure] B -->|High pressure
hot gas| C[Turbine
State 3 to 4
Isentropic expansion] C -->|Low pressure
warm gas| D[Cooler
Heat rejected to
cooling water/air
State 4 to 1] D -->|Low pressure
cool gas| A C -->|Shaft work output| E[External Load
Generator or
Propeller] A -.->|Work input from turbine| C B -.->|Heat input Qin| H[Heat Source
Nuclear / Solar /
Fossil furnace] D -.->|Heat rejected Qout| J[Cooling Medium
River water /
Atmosphere] ``` ## 4.2 T-S Diagram (Temperature-Entropy) of the Ideal Closed Cycle ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph TS["T-S Diagram: Ideal Closed Cycle (Brayton)"] direction TB T1["State 1: p1, T1
LOW PRESSURE COOL GAS
Start of compression"] -->|Process 1-2
Isentropic compression
Vertical line on T-S| T2 T2["State 2: p2, T2
HIGH PRESSURE WARM GAS
End of compression"] -->|Process 2-3
Constant pressure heat addition
Curved line sloping upward| T3 T3["State 3: p3 = p2, T3
HIGH PRESSURE HOT GAS
Maximum temperature"] -->|Process 3-4
Isentropic expansion
Vertical line on T-S| T4 T4["State 4: p4 = p1, T4
LOW PRESSURE WARM GAS
End of expansion"] -->|Process 4-1
Constant pressure heat rejection
Curved line sloping downward| T1 end ``` ## 4.3 P-V Diagram (Pressure-Volume) of the Ideal Closed Cycle ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph PV["P-V Diagram: Closed Cycle (Process Direction)"] direction TB P1["State 1: p1, V1, T1
LARGE VOLUME LOW PRESSURE"] -->|Process 1-2
Isentropic compression
Curve PV^n = constant
n = gamma| P2 P2["State 2: p2, V2, T2
SMALL VOLUME HIGH PRESSURE"] -->|Process 2-3
Constant pressure heat addition
Horizontal line rightward| P3 P3["State 3: p3 = p2, V3, T3
LARGER VOLUME HIGH PRESSURE"] -->|Process 3-4
Isentropic expansion
Curve PV^n = constant
n = gamma| P4 P4["State 4: p4 = p1, V4, T4
EVEN LARGER VOLUME LOW PRESSURE"] -->|Process 4-1
Constant pressure heat rejection
Horizontal line leftward| P1 end ``` ## 4.4 Closed Cycle vs Open Cycle — Comparative Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph OC["Open Cycle Gas Turbine"] O1[Intake
Fresh air] --> O2[Compressor] --> O3[Combustion Chamber
Fuel burned in air] --> O4[Turbine] --> O5[Exhaust to atmosphere] end subgraph CC["Closed Cycle Gas Turbine"] C1[Compressor] --> C2[Heater
External heat] --> C3[Turbine] --> C4[Cooler
Heat rejected] --> C1 end ``` ## 4.5 Functional Architecture — Energy Flow Mapping ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph INPUT["External Inputs"] Q1[Heat Input Qin] WF1[Fuel or Nuclear Heat] end subgraph CYCLE["Closed Brayton Cycle"] COMP[Compressor
Consumes Wc] HTR[Heater
Adds Qin at constant p] TURB[Turbine
Produces Wt] COL[Cooler
Removes Qout at constant p] end subgraph OUTPUT["Outputs"] Wnet[Net Work Wnet = Wt minus Wc] Q2[Heat Rejected Qout] W2[Useful Shaft Work] end WF1 --> Q1 --> HTR COMP --> HTR HTR --> TURB TURB --> COL COL --> COMP TURB --> Wnet Q1 --> HTR COL --> Q2 Wnet --> W2 ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Model Paper] **List the four processes of an ideal closed cycle gas turbine and state which component performs each process.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** 1. **Isentropic compression** (1 → 2) — performed by the **compressor**; the working fluid is compressed from low pressure $p_1$ to high pressure $p_2$ with temperature rising from $T_1$ to $T_2$. (0.75 mark) 2. **Constant pressure heat addition** (2 → 3) — performed by the **heater (heat exchanger)**; external heat $Q_{in}$ is added at constant pressure $p_2 = p_3$, raising temperature from $T_2$ to $T_3$. (0.75 mark) 3. **Isentropic expansion** (3 → 4) — performed by the **turbine**; the high-temperature gas expands from $p_3$ to $p_4$, producing work and dropping temperature from $T_3$ to $T_4$. (0.75 mark) 4. **Constant pressure heat rejection** (4 → 1) — performed by the **cooler (heat exchanger)**; waste heat $Q_{out}$ is rejected to the surroundings at constant pressure $p_4 = p_1$, lowering temperature from $T_4$ back to $T_1$. (0.75 mark) --- ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022] **Why is the thermal efficiency of an ideal closed cycle gas turbine a function of pressure ratio alone?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** For an ideal closed cycle with isentropic compression and expansion, the temperature ratios across the compressor and turbine are equal: $T_2/T_1 = T_3/T_4 = (r_p)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$. (1 mark) Substituting into the efficiency definition: $$\eta_{th} = \frac{(T_3 - T_4) - (T_2 - T_1)}{T_3 - T_2}$$ algebraic simplification (using the equal temperature ratio property) yields: (1 mark) $$\eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{1}{(r_p)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}}$$ Hence $\eta_{th}$ depends **only on $r_p$** and the working fluid's $\gamma$, not on the absolute temperatures $T_1$ and $T_3$. (1 mark) --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) ### ⭐ Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam – June 2024] **A closed cycle gas turbine operates between $T_1 = 310$ K and $T_3 = 1100$ K with a pressure ratio of 8. The working fluid is air with $c_p = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K and $\gamma = 1.4$.** **(a)** Draw the P-V and T-S diagrams of the cycle. Label all four states. (7 marks) **(b)** Calculate the net work output per kg of air, the heat supplied per kg, the thermal efficiency, and the optimum pressure ratio for maximum work. (7 marks) --- #### Model Solution for Question A: **(a) P-V and T-S Diagrams (7 marks)** A complete schematic showing the closed cycle in both diagrams. (Diagrams carry full marks — see Section 4 above for reference.) - **T-S Diagram**: States 1 (low $T$, low $p$) → 2 (medium $T$, high $p$, isentropic compression, vertical line) → 3 (high $T$, high $p$, constant pressure heating, curved line) → 4 (medium $T$, low $p$, isentropic expansion, vertical line) → 1 (heat rejection, curved line). (3.5 marks) - **P-V Diagram**: 1 (large $V$, low $p$) → 2 (small $V$, high $p$, isentropic curve) → 3 (slightly larger $V$ at same $p$, constant pressure line) → 4 (large $V$, low $p$, isentropic curve) → 1 (constant pressure line closing loop). (3.5 marks) **(b) Calculations (7 marks)** **Step 1: Compute the temperature ratio across the compressor and turbine.** $$(r_p)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} = 8^{0.286}$$ $\ln 8 = 2.0794$; $0.286 \times 2.0794 = 0.5947$; $e^{0.5947} = 1.812$. $$(r_p)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} = 1.812$$ **Step 2: Find the four temperatures.** $T_2 = T_1 \times 1.812 = 310 \times 1.812 = 561.7$ K $T_4 = T_3 / 1.812 = 1100 / 1.812 = 607.1$ K $T_1 = 310$ K (given), $T_3 = 1100$ K (given). **Step 3: Calculate turbine work, compressor work, and net work.** $$W_T = c_p(T_3 - T_4) = 1.005 \times (1100 - 607.1) = 1.005 \times 492.9 = 495.4 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ $$W_C = c_p(T_2 - T_1) = 1.005 \times (561.7 - 310) = 1.005 \times 251.7 = 252.9 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ $$W_{net} = W_T - W_C = 495.4 - 252.9 = 242.5 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ **Step 4: Calculate heat supplied.** $$Q_{in} = c_p(T_3 - T_2) = 1.005 \times (1100 - 561.7) = 1.005 \times 538.3 = 540.9 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ **Step 5: Calculate thermal efficiency.** $$\eta_{th} = \frac{W_{net}}{Q_{in}} = \frac{242.5}{540.9} = 0.4484 = 44.84\%$$ **Verification using direct formula:** $$\eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{1}{(r_p)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}} = 1 - \frac{1}{1.812} = 1 - 0.5520 = 0.4480 = 44.80\%$$ (The two values match to within rounding error. ✓) **Step 6: Optimum pressure ratio for maximum work.** $$r_{p,W} = \left[\frac{1}{2}\left(1 + \frac{T_3}{T_1}\right)\right]^{\gamma/(\gamma-1)} = \left[\frac{1}{2}\left(1 + \frac{1100}{310}\right)\right]^{3.5} = \left[\frac{1}{2}(4.548)\right]^{3.5} = (2.274)^{3.5}$$ $\ln 2.274 = 0.8213$; $3.5 \times 0.8213 = 2.8746$; $e^{2.8746} = 17.73$. $$\boxed{r_{p,W} \approx 17.7}$$ > **Conclusion:** The given $r_p = 8$ is well below the optimum $r_{p,W} = 17.7$. Increasing $r_p$ to 17.7 would yield **maximum net work output**, but would also require a heavier compressor and more robust construction. --- ### ⭐ Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam – June 2023, Supplementary] **(a)** Starting from fundamental principles, derive an expression for the thermal efficiency of an ideal closed cycle gas turbine. Show that the efficiency depends only on the pressure ratio. (7 marks) **(b)** For a closed cycle gas turbine with $T_1 = 300$ K, $T_3 = 900$ K, $r_p = 5$, and air as the working fluid, compute (i) net work output, (ii) mean effective pressure, and (iii) the optimum pressure ratio for maximum MEP. (7 marks) --- #### Model Solution for Question B: **(a) Derivation of Thermal Efficiency (7 marks)** **Step 1: Define the four processes of the ideal closed cycle.** (1 mark) - 1-2: Isentropic compression in the compressor - 2-3: Constant pressure heat addition in the heater - 3-4: Isentropic expansion in the turbine - 4-1: Constant pressure heat rejection in the cooler **Step 2: Write the isentropic relations for compressor and turbine.** (1 mark) $$\frac{T_2}{T_1} = (r_p)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}, \quad \frac{T_3}{T_4} = (r_p)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$$ **Step 3: Define work and heat terms.** (1 mark) $W_T = c_p(T_3 - T_4)$ (turbine work) $W_C = c_p(T_2 - T_1)$ (compressor work) $Q_{in} = c_p(T_3 - T_2)$ (heat input in heater) $Q_{out} = c_p(T_4 - T_1)$ (heat rejected in cooler) **Step 4: State efficiency definition and substitute.** (1 mark) $$\eta_{th} = \frac{W_{net}}{Q_{in}} = \frac{c_p[(T_3 - T_4) - (T_2 - T_1)]}{c_p(T_3 - T_2)}$$ **Step 5: Substitute $T_2 = T_1 (r_p)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$ and $T_4 = T_3/(r_p)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$.** (1.5 marks) After algebraic simplification (as shown in Section 3.2), the $T_3$ and $T_1$ terms cancel, leaving: $$\eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{1}{(r_p)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}}$$ **Step 6: Conclude.** (0.5 mark) This expression shows that $\eta_{th}$ depends only on $r_p$ and the working fluid property $\gamma$ (typically 1.4 for air). It is **independent of the absolute temperatures** $T_1$ and $T_3$. As $r_p \to \infty$, $\eta_{th} \to 1$, and as $r_p \to 1$, $\eta_{th} \to 0$. **(b) Numerical Calculations (7 marks)** Given: $T_1 = 300$ K, $T_3 = 900$ K, $r_p = 5$, $c_p = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K, $\gamma = 1.4$. **Step 1: Temperature ratio across compressor and turbine.** $5^{0.286}$: $\ln 5 = 1.6094$; $0.286 \times 1.6094 = 0.4603$; $e^{0.4603} = 1.5846$. So $(r_p)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} = 1.585$. **Step 2: Find the four temperatures.** $T_2 = 300 \times 1.585 = 475.5$ K $T_4 = 900 / 1.585 = 567.8$ K $T_1 = 300$ K, $T_3 = 900$ K. **(i) Net work output per kg (1.5 marks):** $$W_{net} = 1.005 \times [(900 - 567.8) - (475.5 - 300)] = 1.005 \times [332.2 - 175.5] = 1.005 \times 156.7 = 157.5 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ **(ii) Mean effective pressure (3.5 marks):** For MEP, we need the specific volume change $V_1 - V_2$. $$V_1 - V_2 = \frac{R(T_1 - T_2)}{p_1} = \frac{0.287 \times (300 - 475.5)}{p_1} = \frac{-50.3}{p_1}$$ Wait, $T_2 > T_1$, so we should use the magnitude or rewrite. The proper formulation is: $$V_1 - V_2 = \frac{RT_1}{p_1}\left(1 - \frac{1}{(r_p)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}}\right) = \frac{0.287 \times 300}{p_1}\left(1 - \frac{1}{1.585}\right) = \frac{86.1}{p_1} \times 0.369 = \frac{31.78}{p_1}$$ The MEP is: $$MEP = \frac{W_{net}}{V_1 - V_2} = \frac{157.5}{31.78/p_1} = 4.956 \cdot p_1 \text{ kPa (with } p_1 \text{ in kPa)}$$ If $p_1 = 100$ kPa (atmospheric), then $MEP = 495.6$ kPa $\approx$ **4.96 bar**. **(iii) Optimum pressure ratio for maximum MEP (2 marks):** $$r_{p,MEP} = 2^{\gamma/(\gamma-1)} \cdot r_{p,W}$$ where $r_{p,W} = (T_3/T_1)^{\gamma/[2(\gamma-1)]} = (900/300)^{3.5/2} = 3^{1.75}$. $\ln 3 = 1.0986$; $1.75 \times 1.0986 = 1.9226$; $e^{1.9226} = 6.838$. $r_{p,W} \approx 6.84$. $2^{\gamma/(\gamma-1)} = 2^{3.5} = 11.31$. $$r_{p,MEP} = 11.31 \times 6.84 = 77.4$$ $$\boxed{r_{p,MEP} \approx 77.4}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls** > 1. **Forgetting to subtract compressor work.** A very common error is to write $W_{net} = c_p(T_3 - T_4)$ instead of $W_{net} = c_p[(T_3 - T_4) - (T_2 - T_1)]$. The compressor consumes a significant portion of the turbine work (often 40–60%) and **must always be subtracted** to obtain the net work. (-2 to -3 marks) > 2. **Mixing up the optimum pressure ratios.** Students frequently confuse $r_{p,W}$ and $r_{p,MEP}$. Remember: **$r_{p,MEP} = 2^{\gamma/(\gamma-1)} \cdot r_{p,W} \approx 11.3 \cdot r_{p,W}$ for air**. The MEP optimum is always higher. (-1 to -2 marks) > 3. **Incorrect $V_1 - V_2$ formula.** The MEP denominator uses the change in specific volume from state 1 to state 2 of the compressor (not the entire cycle). Using $V_3 - V_4$ or the wrong pair loses marks. (-1 mark) > 4. **Pressure ratio in $r_p$ vs $r_{p}^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$ confusion.** When computing temperatures, students often write $T_2 = T_1 \cdot r_p$ instead of $T_2 = T_1 \cdot (r_p)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$. The exponent **0.286 for air** is critical. (-1 mark) > 5. **Not labeling P-V and T-S diagrams properly.** Always label the four states, the process names, and the curves. Skipping the labels loses 1–2 marks even if the diagram is correctly drawn. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Closed Cycle Gas Turbine** - **Cycle Composition:** Compressor (1→2) + Heater (2→3) + Turbine (3→4) + Cooler (4→1). All four processes form a closed loop; the working fluid is **recycled continuously**. - **Working Fluid:** Usually **air**, but can be **helium** (nuclear), **nitrogen**, or **argon**. The fluid never contacts the heat source directly. - **Key Assumption:** Isentropic compression and expansion, ideal gas behavior, constant pressure heat addition/rejection. This is the **air-standard Brayton cycle**. - **Net Work Formula:** $W_{net} = c_p[(T_3 - T_4) - (T_2 - T_1)]$ — **always subtract** compressor work. - **Thermal Efficiency:** $\eta_{th} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{(r_p)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}}$ — **depends only on $r_p$**, not on absolute temperatures. - **Optimum $r_p$ for Maximum Work:** $r_{p,W} = \left[\dfrac{1}{2}\left(1 + \dfrac{T_3}{T_1}\right)\right]^{\gamma/(\gamma-1)}$ - **Optimum $r_p$ for Maximum MEP:** $r_{p,MEP} = 2^{\gamma/(\gamma-1)} \cdot r_{p,W}$ — always **larger** than $r_{p,W}$. - **MEP Definition:** $MEP = \dfrac{W_{net}}{V_1 - V_2}$ — measures **compactness** of the cycle (work per unit volume change in compressor). - **Symmetry Property:** $\dfrac{T_2}{T_1} = \dfrac{T_3}{T_4} = (r_p)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$ — this is the root cause of efficiency's dependence on $r_p$ alone. - **Heat Terms:** $Q_{in} = c_p(T_3 - T_2)$; $Q_{out} = c_p(T_4 - T_1)$; by energy balance $Q_{in} = W_{net} + Q_{out}$. - **Diagrams to Master:** P-V and T-S diagrams with all four states clearly labeled. **Vertical lines** on T-S represent isentropic processes; **horizontal lines** on P-V represent constant pressure processes. - **Comparison with Open Cycle:** Closed cycle permits any heat source, allows variable $T_1$ and $T_3$, suits nuclear and space applications. Open cycle is simpler, lighter, and preferred for aviation. - **Efficiency Limit:** As $r_p \to \infty$, $\eta_{th} \to 1$ (theoretically), but practical limits on $r_p$ (typically 5–20 for aviation, 30+ for industrial) prevent this. Also, real cycle inefficiencies (component losses) reduce actual efficiency by 20–40% below the ideal. - **Numerical Tricks:** Always compute $(r_p)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$ **once** and use it for all four temperatures. For air, $\gamma = 1.4$, so $(\gamma-1)/\gamma = 0.286$ and $\gamma/(\gamma-1) = 3.5$ — memorize these! - **Maximum Work vs. Maximum Efficiency Trade-off:** $r_{p,W} < r_{p,\text{no work limit}}$. Designers pick $r_p$ based on priority. - **Limiting Cases:** At $r_p = 1$, the cycle produces **zero net work** and **zero efficiency** — no compression means no expansion, hence no work.

topics.title: Ideal Cycle
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMET741 - GAS TURBINE AND JET PROPULSION > - **Module:** Module Module 1 > - **Topic:** Ideal Cycle ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition The **Ideal Cycle** of a Gas Turbine is the theoretical thermodynamic model upon which all real-world gas turbine performance is benchmarked. It is universally known as the **Brayton Cycle** (or the **Joule Cycle** in European nomenclature). It models the gas turbine as a closed, steady-flow, cyclic system in which a fixed mass of working fluid (air, behaving as an **Ideal Gas**) undergoes four internally reversible processes: > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PEMET741 - Module 1)** > The ideal cycle of a Gas Turbine consists of two **isentropic** (reversible adiabatic) processes and two **isobaric** (constant pressure) processes. It is the foundational reference cycle for analyzing aircraft jet engines, industrial power turbines, and standalone turbo-generators. ### 1.2 The Four Cardinal Processes | State Point | Process | Thermodynamic Nature | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **1 → 2** | **Compression** | Isentropic Compression in the Compressor | | **2 → 3** | **Heat Addition** | Isobaric (Constant Pressure) Heat Addition in the Combustion Chamber | | **3 → 4** | **Expansion** | Isentropic Expansion in the Turbine | | **4 → 1** | **Heat Rejection** | Isobaric (Constant Pressure) Heat Rejection (Closed Cycle only) | ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Breath of the Turbine" > [!NOTE] > **Intuitive Analogy: The Bellows and the Fire** > Imagine a blacksmith's **bellows** (compressor) squeezing air into a sealed chamber, a **furnace fire** (combustion chamber) that instantly heats that compressed air to a brilliant glow, and a **steam release valve** (turbine) that lets that scorching, high-pressure air blast out through vanes to spin a wheel. > The bellows squish = **Isentropic Compression**. > The furnace heat = **Isobaric Heat Addition**. > The expanding blast through the vanes = **Isentropic Expansion**. > The cooling of the spent exhaust before it goes back into the bellows = **Isobaric Heat Rejection**. > In a *closed* cycle (the textbook ideal), this exhaust is cooled and routed back into the compressor. In an *open* cycle (real aircraft engines), the exhaust is simply dumped into the atmosphere and fresh air is sucked in. ### 1.4 Standard Physical Constants & Assumptions > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard Air-Fuel Cycle Assumptions (Working Fluid: Air)** > - Working fluid = **Air**, treated as an **Ideal Gas** with constant specific heats. > - Specific heat ratio: **γ = 1.4** for cold air-standard analysis. > - Specific heat at constant pressure: **cp = 1.005 kJ/kg·K**. > - Gas constant: **R = 0.287 kJ/kg·K**. > - All four processes are **internally reversible**. > - Compression and Expansion are **Adiabatic** (no heat transfer to surroundings during these steps). > - Kinetic and Potential energy changes of the working fluid across components are negligible. > - No pressure drop across the combustion chamber. > - In an *Open Cycle*, atmospheric air is the source and exhaust is discharged to the atmosphere. ### 1.5 Open Cycle vs Closed Cycle — The Real-World Distinction > [!NOTE] > **Open Cycle (Aerospace / Most Industrial):** Air is drawn from the atmosphere, compressed, heated, expanded through the turbine, and exhausted to the atmosphere. State 4 is *not* routed back to State 1 by a heat exchanger; instead, State 1 = ambient inlet conditions, and the cycle restarts by ingesting a fresh charge of air. > > **Closed Cycle (Nuclear / Specialized Industrial):** The same working fluid is sealed in a loop. A dedicated cooler between the turbine exit and compressor inlet rejects heat to maintain State 1 conditions. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operational Logic — The "Why" Behind Each Step The Brayton Cycle is engineered around one central engineering requirement: **maximizing the temperature difference that produces useful work while minimizing the compression work consumed.** **Step 1 — Isentropic Compression (1 → 2):** The compressor draws in ambient air and pressurizes it. Because the process is idealized as **adiabatic and reversible**, the entropy remains constant. This raises both the pressure *and* the temperature of the fluid. Energy required = $W_C = c_p(T_2 - T_1)$. **Step 2 — Isobaric Heat Addition (2 → 3):** Fuel is burned (in an open cycle) or heat is supplied externally (closed cycle) at **constant pressure**. The combustion chamber is modeled as a constant-pressure device. The temperature spikes from $T_2$ to a maximum $T_3$ (turbine inlet temperature, TIT). Heat supplied = $q_{in} = c_p(T_3 - T_2)$. **Step 3 — Isentropic Expansion (3 → 4):** The high-temperature, high-pressure gas expands through the turbine, dropping in pressure and temperature while delivering shaft work. Process is again **adiabatic and reversible**. Work extracted = $W_T = c_p(T_3 - T_4)$. **Step 4 — Isobaric Heat Rejection (4 → 1):** In a closed cycle, the exhaust gas passes through a cooler to return to the initial state $T_1$. Heat rejected = $q_{out} = c_p(T_4 - T_1)$. ### 2.2 The Governing Equation — Air-Standard Efficiency The **thermal efficiency** of the cycle is derived purely from the First Law applied to a cyclic, steady-flow device. Because the cycle is closed (or quasi-closed), the net heat transfer equals the net work output: $$\eta_{th} = \frac{W_{net}}{Q_{in}} = 1 - \frac{Q_{out}}{Q_{in}}$$ For the ideal Brayton Cycle, this elegantly simplifies to a function of only the **pressure ratio** $r_p$ and the specific heat ratio $\gamma$: $$\boxed{\eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{1}{r_p^{\,(\gamma - 1)/\gamma}}}$$ ### 2.3 The Critical Engineering Insight — The "Back-Work Ratio" > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why Efficiency Depends Only on Pressure Ratio (and NOT on Temperature Ratio):** > This is the single most counter-intuitive yet vital insight of the Brayton Cycle. The thermal efficiency is governed **exclusively** by the compressor pressure ratio $r_p$ and the specific heat ratio $\gamma$. It is **independent** of the turbine inlet temperature $T_3$. > In practical engineering, this means increasing $T_3$ does NOT directly raise thermal efficiency — it raises **specific work output and specific power**, but the *efficiency* itself is locked by $r_p$. This is why modern engines push $T_3$ higher (for power density) while simultaneously using higher $r_p$ (for fuel economy). ### 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet > [!TIP] > **Master these seven formulas — they solve 95% of all KTU numerical questions on the Ideal Cycle.** | # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | **Pressure Ratio** | $r_p = P_2 / P_1 = P_3 / P_4$ | Dimensionless ratio | | 2 | **Compressor Exit Temperature** | $T_2 / T_1 = r_p^{(\gamma - 1)/\gamma}$ | Isentropic relation | | 3 | **Turbine Exit Temperature** | $T_4 / T_3 = \left(1 / r_p\right)^{(\gamma - 1)/\gamma}$ | Inverse isentropic relation | | 4 | **Heat Supplied** | $q_{in} = c_p \,(T_3 - T_2)$ | kJ/kg, constant pressure | | 5 | **Heat Rejected** | $q_{out} = c_p \,(T_4 - T_1)$ | kJ/kg, constant pressure | | 6 | **Net Work Output** | $W_{net} = c_p [(T_3 - T_2) - (T_4 - T_1)]$ | kJ/kg per unit mass | | 7 | **Thermal Efficiency** | $\eta_{th} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r_p^{(\gamma - 1)/\gamma}}$ | Air-standard; $\gamma = 1.4$ | | 8 | **Back-Work Ratio** | $BWR = \dfrac{W_C}{W_T} = \dfrac{T_2 - T_1}{T_3 - T_4}$ | Fraction of turbine work to drive compressor | | 9 | **Specific Work (Alt)** | $W_{net} = c_p \, T_1 \left(r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - 1\right) \left(\dfrac{T_3}{T_1} - \dfrac{T_4}{T_1}\right) \cdot \text{...}$ | Composite form for given $T_3, T_1, r_p$ | ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility > [!NOTE] > **Where the Ideal Brayton Cycle is Applied:** > - **Aviation:** Turbojet, turbofan, turboprop, and turboshaft engines (with modifications for propulsion efficiency). > - **Power Generation:** Industrial gas turbine power plants (simple-cycle and combined-cycle with Rankine bottoming). > - **Marine Propulsion:** COGAS (Combined Gas turbine And Steam) naval propulsion. > - **Pipeline Compression:** Gas turbine-driven centrifugal compressors for natural gas pipelines. > - **Reference Benchmark:** All real-engine irreversibilities (component efficiencies, pressure drops, turbine cooling) are measured as **deviations** from this ideal benchmark. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of the Isentropic Process Relations (Process 1-2 and 3-4) For an **isentropic process** applied to an ideal gas with constant specific heats, the governing relation between temperature and pressure is derived from the combined gas laws: $$P v^\gamma = \text{constant} \quad \text{and} \quad T v^{\gamma - 1} = \text{constant} \quad \text{and} \quad \frac{T}{P^{(\gamma - 1)/\gamma}} = \text{constant}$$ Eliminating the specific volume $v$ between the first two relations, we obtain the canonical **Isentropic T-P Relation**: $$\frac{T_2}{T_1} = \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(\gamma - 1)/\gamma}$$ **Applied to Process 1-2 (Compressor):** Since the compressor raises pressure from $P_1$ to $P_2$, with the pressure ratio defined as $r_p = P_2 / P_1$: $$\boxed{T_2 = T_1 \cdot r_p^{(\gamma - 1)/\gamma}}$$ **Applied to Process 3-4 (Turbine):** The turbine expands from $P_3$ to $P_4$. In an ideal cycle, $P_3 = P_2$ (no combustion pressure drop) and $P_4 = P_1$ (exhaust to ambient in open cycle). Therefore: $$\frac{T_4}{T_3} = \left(\frac{P_4}{P_3}\right)^{(\gamma - 1)/\gamma} = \left(\frac{P_1}{P_2}\right)^{(\gamma - 1)/\gamma} = \left(\frac{1}{r_p}\right)^{(\gamma - 1)/\gamma}$$ $$\boxed{T_4 = T_3 \cdot \left(\frac{1}{r_p}\right)^{(\gamma - 1)/\gamma} = \frac{T_3}{r_p^{(\gamma - 1)/\gamma}}}$$ ### 3.2 Derivation of Thermal Efficiency (The Crown Jewel) **Step 1 — Apply the First Law to a Cyclic Steady-Flow System:** For any device operating in a cycle, the net work output equals the net heat input: $$W_{net} = Q_{net} = Q_{in} - Q_{out}$$ **Step 2 — Express Heat Transfer in Terms of Temperatures:** Heat is supplied at constant pressure from State 2 to State 3, and rejected at constant pressure from State 4 to State 1. Using $q = c_p \Delta T$: $$q_{in} = c_p (T_3 - T_2) \qquad q_{out} = c_p (T_4 - T_1)$$ **Step 3 — Substitute into the Definition of Thermal Efficiency:** $$\eta_{th} = \frac{W_{net}}{Q_{in}} = 1 - \frac{Q_{out}}{Q_{in}}$$ $$\eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{c_p (T_4 - T_1)}{c_p (T_3 - T_2)} = 1 - \frac{T_4 - T_1}{T_3 - T_2}$$ **Step 4 — Substitute the Isentropic Relations:** From the compressor relation: $T_2 = T_1 \cdot r_p^{(\gamma - 1)/\gamma}$, hence: $$T_3 - T_2 = T_3 - T_1 \cdot r_p^{(\gamma - 1)/\gamma}$$ From the turbine relation: $T_4 = T_3 \cdot \left(1/r_p\right)^{(\gamma - 1)/\gamma}$, hence: $$T_4 - T_1 = \frac{T_3}{r_p^{(\gamma - 1)/\gamma}} - T_1 = \frac{T_3 - T_1 \cdot r_p^{(\gamma - 1)/\gamma}}{r_p^{(\gamma - 1)/\gamma}}$$ **Step 5 — Recognize the Identical Numerator:** Both numerator and denominator contain the same expression: $(T_3 - T_1 \cdot r_p^{(\gamma - 1)/\gamma})$. The terms cancel, yielding: $$\frac{T_4 - T_1}{T_3 - T_2} = \frac{1}{r_p^{(\gamma - 1)/\gamma}}$$ **Step 6 — Final Canonical Result:** $$\boxed{\eta_{th,\,Brayton} = 1 - \frac{1}{r_p^{(\gamma - 1)/\gamma}}}$$ ### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Standard KTU-Style Problem > [!IMPORTANT] > **Worked Example (Model Answer Style for KTU Valuation)** > > **Problem:** Air enters the compressor of an ideal Brayton cycle at 100 kPa, 300 K with a pressure ratio of 8. The turbine inlet temperature is 1300 K. Determine: > (i) Compressor exit temperature > (ii) Turbine exit temperature > (iii) Net work output per kg of air > (iv) Thermal efficiency > Take $\gamma = 1.4$ and $c_p = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K. **Given Data:** $T_1 = 300$ K, $P_1 = 100$ kPa, $r_p = 8$, $T_3 = 1300$ K, $\gamma = 1.4$, $c_p = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K **Solution:** **Part (i) — Compressor Exit Temperature $T_2$:** The isentropic exponent is calculated as: $$\frac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma} = \frac{1.4 - 1}{1.4} = \frac{0.4}{1.4} = 0.2857$$ The pressure ratio raised to this exponent: $$r_p^{(\gamma - 1)/\gamma} = 8^{0.2857} = 1.8114$$ Applying the isentropic relation: $$T_2 = T_1 \cdot r_p^{(\gamma - 1)/\gamma} = 300 \times 1.8114$$ $$\boxed{T_2 = 543.43 \text{ K}}$$ **Part (ii) — Turbine Exit Temperature $T_4$:** $$T_4 = \frac{T_3}{r_p^{(\gamma - 1)/\gamma}} = \frac{1300}{1.8114}$$ $$\boxed{T_4 = 717.67 \text{ K}}$$ **Part (iii) — Net Work Output:** $$W_{net} = c_p [(T_3 - T_2) - (T_4 - T_1)]$$ $$W_{net} = 1.005 \times [(1300 - 543.43) - (717.67 - 300)]$$ $$W_{net} = 1.005 \times [756.57 - 417.67]$$ $$W_{net} = 1.005 \times 338.90$$ $$\boxed{W_{net} = 340.59 \text{ kJ/kg}}$$ **Part (iv) — Thermal Efficiency:** Method 1 — Direct from pressure ratio: $$\eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{1}{r_p^{(\gamma - 1)/\gamma}} = 1 - \frac{1}{1.8114} = 1 - 0.5520$$ $$\boxed{\eta_{th} = 0.4480 = 44.80\%}$$ Method 2 — Verification via heat transfer: $$\eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{T_4 - T_1}{T_3 - T_2} = 1 - \frac{717.67 - 300}{1300 - 543.43} = 1 - \frac{417.67}{756.57} = 1 - 0.5520 = 44.80\% \checkmark$$ > [!NOTE] > **Method 1 and Method 2 must agree.** If they don't, a calculation error exists in either $T_2$ or $T_4$. This is the KTU examiner's first cross-check during valuation. ### 3.4 The Mean Effective Pressure (MEP) Analogy For completeness with compression-ignition engine comparisons, the MEP of the Brayton cycle can be expressed as a function of temperatures. However, in KTU examinations, MEP is rarely asked for the Brayton cycle — focus on thermal efficiency and net work output. --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 P-V and T-S Schematic Representation of the Ideal Brayton Cycle > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Reading Guide** > The Mermaid schematic below illustrates the four thermodynamic states and processes on the **Pressure-Specific Volume (P-V)** and **Temperature-Entropy (T-S)** planes. The cycle traces 1 → 2 → 3 → 4 → 1. ```mermaid graph LR A["STATE 1: Compressor Inlet
Low P, Low T, Low v
Ambient conditions"] B["STATE 2: Compressor Outlet
High P, Med T, Low v
End of Isentropic Compression"] C["STATE 3: Turbine Inlet
High P, Max T, High v
End of Isobaric Heat Addition"] D["STATE 4: Turbine Outlet
Low P, Med T, High v
End of Isentropic Expansion"] E["Process 1-2
Isentropic Compression
Pv^gamma = const"] F["Process 2-3
Isobaric Heat Addition
P = const, T rises"] G["Process 3-4
Isentropic Expansion
Pv^gamma = const"] H["Process 4-1
Isobaric Heat Rejection
P = const, T falls"] I["PV Diagram
Upper curve 1-2
Lower curve 3-4
Area = Net Work"] J["TS Diagram
Two isentropics vertical
Two isobars horizontal
Area under 2-3 minus 4-1"] A -->|Process 1-2| B B -->|Process 2-3| C C -->|Process 3-4| D D -->|Process 4-1| A E -.describes.-> A F -.describes.-> B G -.describes.-> C H -.describes.-> D I -.represents on PV plane.-> A J -.represents on TS plane.-> A style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#01579b,stroke-width:2px style B fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,stroke-width:2px style C fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c,stroke-width:2px style D fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,stroke-width:2px style E fill:#f5f5f5,stroke:#424242,stroke-width:1px style F fill:#f5f5f5,stroke:#424242,stroke-width:1px style G fill:#f5f5f5,stroke:#424242,stroke-width:1px style H fill:#f5f5f5,stroke:#424242,stroke-width:1px ``` ### 4.2 Real-Component-to-Ideal-State Mapping (Block Architecture) ```mermaid graph TD A["AMBIENT AIR INLET
State 1: P1, T1"] B["COMPRESSOR
Isentropic Compression
Wc = cp x T2 minus T1
State 2: P2, T2"] C["COMBUSTION CHAMBER
Isobaric Heat Addition
qin = cp x T3 minus T2
State 3: P3 equals P2, T3"] D["TURBINE
Isentropic Expansion
Wt = cp x T3 minus T4
State 4: P4 equals P1, T4"] E["EXHAUST TO ATMOSPHERE
or HEAT EXCHANGER
qout = cp x T4 minus T1"] F["SHAFT LOAD
Generator or Propeller
Wnet = Wt minus Wc"] A -->|Ingestion| B B -->|Hot Compressed Air| C C -->|High Energy Gas| D D -->|Spent Gas| E D -->|Shaft Power| F E -.in closed cycle.-> A style A fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1,stroke-width:2px style B fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,stroke-width:2px style C fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c,stroke-width:2px style D fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,stroke-width:2px style E fill:#e1bee7,stroke:#4a148c,stroke-width:2px style F fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100,stroke-width:2px ``` ### 4.3 Energy-Flow Sankey-Like Description > [!NOTE] > **Energy Flow in the Ideal Cycle (per kg of air):** > - **Energy In (Fuel):** $q_{in} = c_p (T_3 - T_2)$ — added at constant pressure in the combustion chamber. > - **Energy Out (Shaft Work, useful):** $W_{net} = W_T - W_C$ — net of turbine and compressor. > - **Energy Out (Exhaust Heat):** $q_{out} = c_p (T_4 - T_1)$ — rejected to the atmosphere or to a cooler. > - **Energy Balance:** $q_{in} = W_{net} + q_{out}$ (First Law verification). --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Question A1:** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **State the four processes of the ideal Brayton cycle on P-V and T-S diagrams, and write the expression for its air-standard efficiency.** **Model Answer:** The four processes are: 1. **Process 1-2:** Isentropic compression in the compressor. 2. **Process 2-3:** Isobaric (constant pressure) heat addition in the combustion chamber. 3. **Process 3-4:** Isentropic expansion in the turbine. 4. **Process 4-1:** Isobaric (constant pressure) heat rejection in the cooler (closed cycle). On the **P-V diagram**, the cycle appears as two curved isentropes (concave to the origin) joined by two horizontal isobaric lines. On the **T-S diagram**, the two isentropes are vertical lines and the two isobars are curves diverging to the right. Air-standard efficiency expression: $$\eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{1}{r_p^{(\gamma - 1)/\gamma}}$$ where $r_p$ is the pressure ratio and $\gamma$ is the specific heat ratio of air. --- **Question A2:** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Why is the thermal efficiency of the ideal Brayton cycle a function of pressure ratio only, and independent of the turbine inlet temperature?** **Model Answer:** The thermal efficiency of the ideal Brayton cycle is derived from the First Law applied to a cyclic steady-flow system: $$\eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{q_{out}}{q_{in}} = 1 - \frac{T_4 - T_1}{T_3 - T_2}$$ Substituting the isentropic relations $T_2 = T_1 \cdot r_p^{(\gamma - 1)/\gamma}$ and $T_4 = T_3 / r_p^{(\gamma - 1)/\gamma}$, the temperature terms $T_1$ and $T_3$ cancel algebraically (numerator and denominator share the same composite expression). This leaves: $$\eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{1}{r_p^{(\gamma - 1)/\gamma}}$$ Hence, efficiency depends **only** on the pressure ratio $r_p$ and $\gamma$. Increasing $T_3$ raises the specific power output and net work per kg but does **not** change the thermal efficiency. In practice, raising $T_3$ is limited by metallurgical constraints of turbine blades. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice) --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]` **(a)** Derive the expression for the air-standard efficiency of an ideal Brayton cycle. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A gas turbine operating on an ideal Brayton cycle has a pressure ratio of 9 and operates between temperature limits of 310 K and 1500 K. Calculate the compressor work, turbine work, net work output, and cycle efficiency. Take $c_p = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K and $\gamma = 1.4$. **(7 Marks)** --- **Solution to Part (a) — Derivation (7 Marks):** Refer to **Section 3.2** above for the complete six-step derivation. Key checkpoints for valuation: > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Part (a)** > - **2 Marks:** Students who do not explicitly state the First Law assumption $W_{net} = Q_{in} - Q_{out}$ for a cyclic device lose marks. Always begin with this. > - **2 Marks:** Forgetting to write $q = c_p \Delta T$ for **both** heat addition and rejection (constant pressure processes) loses marks. > - **2 Marks:** Algebraic cancellation of $(T_3 - T_1 \cdot r_p^{(\gamma - 1)/\gamma})$ from numerator and denominator — if skipped, no credit for the final expression. > - **1 Mark:** Final answer boxed with units and proper notation. **Solution to Part (b) — Numerical (7 Marks):** **Given:** $r_p = 9$, $T_1 = 310$ K, $T_3 = 1500$ K, $c_p = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K, $\gamma = 1.4$. **Step 1 — Compute the isentropic exponent:** $$(\gamma - 1)/\gamma = (1.4 - 1)/1.4 = 0.2857$$ $$r_p^{(\gamma - 1)/\gamma} = 9^{0.2857} = 1.9325$$ **Step 2 — Compressor Exit Temperature $T_2$:** $$T_2 = T_1 \cdot r_p^{(\gamma - 1)/\gamma} = 310 \times 1.9325 = 599.08 \text{ K}$$ **Step 3 — Turbine Exit Temperature $T_4$:** $$T_4 = T_3 / r_p^{(\gamma - 1)/\gamma} = 1500 / 1.9325 = 776.19 \text{ K}$$ **Step 4 — Compressor Work per kg:** $$W_C = c_p (T_2 - T_1) = 1.005 \times (599.08 - 310) = 1.005 \times 289.08$$ $$\boxed{W_C = 290.53 \text{ kJ/kg}}$$ **Step 5 — Turbine Work per kg:** $$W_T = c_p (T_3 - T_4) = 1.005 \times (1500 - 776.19) = 1.005 \times 723.81$$ $$\boxed{W_T = 727.43 \text{ kJ/kg}}$$ **Step 6 — Net Work Output per kg:** $$W_{net} = W_T - W_C = 727.43 - 290.53$$ $$\boxed{W_{net} = 436.90 \text{ kJ/kg}}$$ **Step 7 — Cycle Thermal Efficiency:** $$\eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{1}{r_p^{(\gamma - 1)/\gamma}} = 1 - \frac{1}{1.9325} = 1 - 0.5175$$ $$\boxed{\eta_{th} = 0.4825 = 48.25\%}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Part (b)** > - **Common Error:** Using $r_p = 9$ instead of $r_p = 9$ in the isentropic exponent — students sometimes confuse $r_p$ with $r_v$. Always re-read the question: *"pressure ratio"* means $P_2/P_1$. > - **Common Error:** Computing $W_C$ but forgetting the sign convention (compressor work is *consumed*, turbine work is *produced*). $W_{net} = W_T - W_C$, never $W_C - W_T$. > - **Common Error:** Reporting efficiency in fraction (0.4825) without stating **"48.25%"** in the final answer. Examiners will deduct 0.5 marks. > - **Verification Trick:** Always cross-check $\eta_{th}$ via two methods (pressure ratio formula AND $1 - (T_4-T_1)/(T_3-T_2)$). They must match. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` **(a)** Explain the difference between the open cycle and closed cycle gas turbines with neat sketches. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** An ideal gas turbine receives air at 1 bar, 300 K and compresses it to 6 bar with a turbine inlet temperature of 1100 K. Determine the cycle efficiency, work ratio, and specific fuel consumption if the calorific value of fuel is 43 MJ/kg. Take $c_p = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K and $\gamma = 1.4$. **(7 Marks)** --- **Solution to Part (a) — Theory (7 Marks):** > **Open Cycle Gas Turbine:** > - Working fluid (air) is drawn from the atmosphere, used once, and discharged back to the atmosphere. > -
