No heat exchanger; the turbine exhaust is dumped to the surroundings. > - The combustion chamber directly burns fuel with the compressed air. > - State 1 = ambient inlet conditions. > - Simpler construction; used in **aviation** and most **industrial** gas turbines. > **Closed Cycle Gas Turbine:** > - The same working fluid (air or helium) is continuously recirculated in a sealed loop. > - A heat exchanger (heater) replaces the combustion chamber; heat is supplied externally (e.g., from a nuclear reactor). > - A dedicated cooler rejects heat from the turbine exhaust before the fluid re-enters the compressor. > - Allows independent control of pressure (using a surge tank) and prevents contamination of the working fluid. > - Used in **nuclear power plants** and specialized applications. > [!NOTE] > **Marks Distribution:** 2 Marks definition of each, 2 Marks neat sketch description (compressor, combustor/heater, turbine, cooler/exhaust), 1 Mark advantages/disadvantages, 1 Mark for examples/applications. **Solution to Part (b) — Numerical (7 Marks):** **Given:** $P_1 = 1$ bar, $T_1 = 300$ K, $P_2 = 6$ bar, $T_3 = 1100$ K, $c_p = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K, $\gamma = 1.4$, $\text{CV} = 43$ MJ/kg. **Step 1 — Pressure Ratio and Isentropic Exponent:** $$r_p = P_2 / P_1 = 6/1 = 6$$ $$r_p^{(\gamma - 1)/\gamma} = 6^{0.2857} = 1.6685$$ **Step 2 — Compressor Exit Temperature:** $$T_2 = 300 \times 1.6685 = 500.55 \text{ K}$$ **Step 3 — Turbine Exit Temperature:** $$T_4 = 1100 / 1.6685 = 659.27 \text{ K}$$ **Step 4 — Cycle Thermal Efficiency:** $$\eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{1}{1.6685} = 1 - 0.5994$$ $$\boxed{\eta_{th} = 0.4006 = 40.06\%}$$ **Step 5 — Compressor and Turbine Works:** $$W_C = 1.005 \times (500.55 - 300) = 1.005 \times 200.55 = 201.55 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ $$W_T = 1.005 \times (1100 - 659.27) = 1.005 \times 440.73 = 442.93 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ **Step 6 — Work Ratio:** The work ratio is defined as net work output divided by turbine work: $$\text{Work Ratio} = \frac{W_{net}}{W_T} = \frac{W_T - W_C}{W_T} = \frac{442.93 - 201.55}{442.93}$$ $$\boxed{\text{Work Ratio} = 0.5450}$$ **Step 7 — Specific Fuel Consumption (SFC):** First, find heat supplied per kg of air: $$q_{in} = c_p (T_3 - T_2) = 1.005 \times (1100 - 500.55) = 1.005 \times 599.45 = 602.45 \text{ kJ/kg of air}$$ The SFC is the mass of fuel required per unit net work output: $$\text{SFC} = \frac{3600}{\eta_{th} \times \text{CV}} = \frac{3600}{0.4006 \times 43000} \text{ kg/kW·h}$$ $$\text{SFC} = \frac{3600}{17225.8}$$ $$\boxed{\text{SFC} = 0.2090 \text{ kg/kW·h}}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Question B** > - **Work Ratio vs Back-Work Ratio:** Students often confuse these. **Work Ratio = $W_{net}/W_T$**, **Back-Work Ratio = $W_C/W_T$**. They sum to 1: $\text{WR} + \text{BWR} = 1$. > - **SFC Units:** The factor **3600** converts kJ to kW·h. Forgetting this gives an answer in wrong units (e.g., kg/kJ instead of kg/kW·h). > - **Calorific Value Units:** CV is given as 43 MJ/kg = 43000 kJ/kg. Mixing units (using 43 directly) is a guaranteed loss of 1 mark. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **Rapid Revision Checklist for the Ideal Brayton Cycle** > > ✅ The four processes: **1-2 Isentropic Compression**, **2-3 Isobaric Heat Addition**, **3-4 Isentropic Expansion**, **4-1 Isobaric Heat Rejection**. > > ✅ Pressure ratio: $r_p = P_2/P_1 = P_3/P_4$ (combustion chamber has no pressure drop in the ideal cycle). > > ✅ Isentropic exponent for air: $(\gamma - 1)/\gamma = 0.4/1.4 = 0.2857$ (memorize this exact value). > > ✅ Compressor relation: $T_2 = T_1 \cdot r_p^{0.2857}$. > > ✅ Turbine relation: $T_4 = T_3 / r_p^{0.2857}$. > > ✅ **Thermal efficiency depends ONLY on pressure ratio** — NOT on turbine inlet temperature. > > ✅ Heat supplied: $q_{in} = c_p (T_3 - T_2)$. Heat rejected: $q_{out} = c_p (T_4 - T_1)$. > > ✅ Net work: $W_{net} = c_p [(T_3 - T_2) - (T_4 - T_1)]$. > > ✅ **Open cycle** = air from atmosphere, exhausted to atmosphere (no cooler). **Closed cycle** = working fluid in a sealed loop with a heater and cooler. > > ✅ **Work Ratio** = $W_{net}/W_T$. **Back-Work Ratio** = $W_C/W_T$. They sum to 1. > > ✅ **SFC formula**: $\text{SFC} = 3600 / (\eta_{th} \times \text{CV})$ in **kg/kW·h**. > > ✅ Always cross-verify thermal efficiency using **two methods**: the pressure-ratio formula AND the $1 - (T_4-T_1)/(T_3-T_2)$ formulation. > > ✅ For air-standard analysis: $\gamma = 1.4$, $c_p = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K, $R = 0.287$ kJ/kg·K.

topics.title: Simple Cycle
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMET741 - GAS TURBINE AND JET PROPULSION > - **Module:** Module Module 1 > - **Topic:** Simple Cycle # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus) The **Simple Cycle** (also called the **Brayton Cycle** or **Joule Cycle**) is the ideal thermodynamic air-standard cycle that governs the operation of a basic gas turbine plant. It comprises four internally reversible processes acting on a fixed mass of working fluid (air behaving as an ideal gas): | Process No. | Process Name | Thermodynamic Nature | |:-----------:|:-------------|:---------------------| | 1 → 2 | Isentropic (Adiabatic) Compression | Reversible adiabatic | | 2 → 3 | Constant Pressure Heat Addition | Isobaric | | 3 → 4 | Isentropic (Adiabatic) Expansion | Reversible adiabatic | | 4 → 1 | Constant Pressure Heat Rejection | Isobaric | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Module 1 Highlight:** In an *open cycle* gas turbine, the exhaust at state 4 is discharged to atmosphere and fresh air is inducted at state 1; in a *closed cycle*, the same working fluid is continuously recirculated through a heat exchanger. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Wind Bag & The Bellows" Imagine you have a **bicycle pump (compressor)**, a **pressure cooker on low flame (combustion chamber)**, and a **pinwheel (turbine)** all connected by pipes. 1. You push the pump handle hard → air gets squeezed and hot internally → this is **process 1–2** (compression, no heat exchange because it happens fast). 2. The cooker heats the squeezed air at constant pressure → temperature soars → this is **process 2–3** (heat addition). 3. The hot air rushes out and spins the pinwheel, losing pressure and temperature → this is **process 3–4** (expansion in turbine). 4. Finally, the cooler air escapes out (open) or is cooled (closed) → this is **process 4–1** (heat rejection). **Net useful work** comes out because the turbine expands more than it took to compress, just like releasing a stretched spring does more work than you put in to stretch it. ## 1.3 Standard Reference Constants - Specific heat of air (cp) ≈ **1.005 kJ/kg·K** - Ratio of specific heats (γ) ≈ **1.4** - Gas constant R = **0.287 kJ/kg·K** - Standard atmospheric pressure = **101.325 kPa** - Standard ambient temperature = **288.15 K (15°C)** - Adiabatic index for combustion products (assumed) ≈ **1.333** > [!NOTE] > Throughout KTU valuation, the working fluid is taken as **air** with **variable specific heat neglected** unless the question explicitly states otherwise. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Logic of the Simple Cycle — Stepwise Breakdown **Step 1 — Ambient Induction (State 1)** Fresh atmospheric air enters the axial/centrifugal compressor at ambient conditions (T₁, P₁). In an *open cycle*, this is simply the surrounding air. **Step 2 — Isentropic Compression (1 → 2)** The compressor consumes **compressor work (Wc)** to raise pressure from P₁ to P₂. Since the process is rapid and well-insulated, it is modeled as **isentropic**. Temperature rises from T₁ to T₂. **Step 3 — Isobaric Heat Addition (2 → 3)** Fuel is burned in the combustion chamber at approximately constant pressure (P₂ = P₃). A large quantity of heat **Qs** is added, raising the temperature from T₂ to T₃ (the **Turbine Inlet Temperature, TIT**). **Step 4 — Isentropic Expansion (3 → 4)** Hot gases expand through the turbine, which extracts **turbine work (Wt)**. Expansion is modeled as isentropic, dropping both pressure (P₄ ≈ P₁ for open cycle) and temperature (T₃ → T₄). **Step 5 — Heat Rejection (4 → 1)** In a closed cycle, exhaust gases pass through a cooler, rejecting heat **Qr** at constant pressure to return to state 1. In an open cycle, gases are simply vented. ## 2.2 Why and How the Cycle Produces Net Work - **Compressor work** is spent to "load" the working fluid with pressure energy. - **Turbine work** recovers part of that energy plus the energy injected by combustion. - **Net Work = Turbine Work − Compressor Work** - The cycle is only feasible when **Turbine Work > Compressor Work**, which requires a minimum pressure ratio called the **threshold pressure ratio**. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Use $\vert$ or $\mid$ instead of \vert symbol in math to prevent markdown table breakage.** | # | Quantity | Formula | Units | KTU Remarks | |:-:|:---------|:--------|:------|:------------| | 1 | Pressure Ratio | $r_p = \dfrac{P_2}{P_1} = \dfrac{P_3}{P_4}$ | dimensionless | Key design parameter | | 2 | Temperature after compression | $T_2 = T_1 \cdot r_p^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}}$ | K | Isentropic relation | | 3 | Temperature after expansion | $T_4 = T_3 \cdot \left(\dfrac{1}{r_p}\right)^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} = \dfrac{T_3}{r_p^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}}}$ | K | Isentropic relation | | 4 | Compressor Work | $W_c = c_p (T_2 - T_1)$ | kJ/kg | Negative by sign convention | | 5 | Turbine Work | $W_t = c_p (T_3 - T_4)$ | kJ/kg | Positive work output | | 6 | Net Work Output | $W_{net} = W_t - W_c = c_p[(T_3 - T_4) - (T_2 - T_1)]$ | kJ/kg | Per kg of air | | 7 | Heat Supplied | $Q_s = c_p (T_3 - T_2)$ | kJ/kg | In combustion chamber | | 8 | Heat Rejected | $Q_r = c_p (T_4 - T_1)$ | kJ/kg | In heat exchanger (closed) | | 9 | Air-Standard Thermal Efficiency | $\eta_{th} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r_p^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}}}$ | dimensionless | Depends ONLY on r_p | | 10 | Work Ratio | $R_w = \dfrac{W_{net}}{W_t} = 1 - \dfrac{W_c}{W_t}$ | dimensionless | Must be > 0 for operation | | 11 | Compressor Work Ratio | $\dfrac{W_c}{W_t} = \dfrac{T_2 - T_1}{T_3 - T_4}$ | dimensionless | | | 12 | Mean Effective Pressure | $MEP = \dfrac{W_{net}}{v_1 - v_2} = \dfrac{W_{net} \cdot r_p}{v_1 (r_p - 1)}$ | kPa | Performance index | | 13 | Optimum Pressure Ratio | $r_{p,opt} = \left(\dfrac{\gamma \cdot T_3}{T_1}\right)^{\dfrac{\gamma}{2(\gamma-1)}}$ | dimensionless | For max net work | | 14 | Max Net Work per kg | $W_{net,max} = c_p \left[T_3 - T_1 \sqrt{(\gamma \cdot T_3 / T_1)^{\tfrac{2\gamma-2}{\gamma}}}\right]$ …(derived form) | kJ/kg | KTU frequently asks this | ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility The simple Brayton cycle is the heart of: - **Aircraft jet engines** (turbojets, turbofans) - **Industrial gas turbines** for power generation (combined with steam Rankine in CCGT) - **Gas turbine propulsion systems** in naval vessels - **Auxiliary Power Units (APUs)** in aircraft Engineers use the efficiency formula $\eta_{th} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}}$ to select optimum pressure ratios, balancing efficiency against mechanical complexity and component stresses. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of Air-Standard Thermal Efficiency **Given:** Ideal Brayton cycle with isentropic compression and expansion, isobaric heat addition and rejection. **Step 1 — Write Heat Added and Heat Rejected** Heat is supplied at constant pressure from state 2 to 3: $$ Q_s = c_p (T_3 - T_2) $$ Heat is rejected at constant pressure from state 4 to 1: $$ Q_r = c_p (T_4 - T_1) $$ **Step 2 — Apply Efficiency Definition** The thermal efficiency is defined as the ratio of net work to heat supplied, and by energy balance equals 1 minus the ratio of heat rejected to heat added: $$ \eta_{th} = \dfrac{W_{net}}{Q_s} = 1 - \dfrac{Q_r}{Q_s} $$ **Step 3 — Substitute the Heat Expressions** $$ \eta_{th} = 1 - \dfrac{c_p(T_4 - T_1)}{c_p(T_3 - T_2)} = 1 - \dfrac{T_4 - T_1}{T_3 - T_2} $$ **Step 4 — Apply Isentropic Relations for T₂ and T₄** For an isentropic process in an ideal gas: $$ \dfrac{T_2}{T_1} = \left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} = r_p^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} $$ $$ \dfrac{T_3}{T_4} = \left(\dfrac{P_3}{P_4}\right)^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} = r_p^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} $$ This yields: $$ T_2 = T_1 \cdot r_p^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} \qquad T_4 = \dfrac{T_3}{r_p^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}}} $$ **Step 5 — Algebraic Manipulation (the KTU magic step)** Multiply and divide the efficiency expression strategically: $$ \eta_{th} = 1 - \dfrac{T_4 - T_1}{T_3 - T_2} $$ Divide numerator and denominator by $T_1$: $$ \eta_{th} = 1 - \dfrac{(T_4/T_1) - 1}{(T_3/T_1) - (T_2/T_1)} $$ Let $\tau = r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$, so $T_2/T_1 = \tau$ and $T_4/T_1 = T_3/(T_1 \tau)$. $$ \eta_{th} = 1 - \dfrac{T_3/T_1\tau - 1}{T_3/T_1 - \tau} $$ Multiply numerator and denominator by $\tau$: $$ \eta_{th} = 1 - \dfrac{T_3/T_1 - \tau}{(T_3/T_1)\tau - \tau^2} = 1 - \dfrac{T_3 - T_1\tau}{T_1\tau(T_3/T_1 - \tau)} $$ $$ \eta_{th} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{\tau} \cdot \dfrac{T_3 - T_1 \tau}{T_3 - T_1 \tau} $$ $$ \eta_{th} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{\tau} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Result:** $\boxed{\eta_{th} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}}}$ — This is the **most tested derivation** in KTU Module 1. ## 3.2 Derivation of Optimum Pressure Ratio (for Maximum Net Work) **Given:** T₃ (TIT) and T₁ (ambient) are fixed. Find $r_p$ that maximizes $W_{net}$. **Step 1 — Express Net Work in Terms of $r_p$** $$ W_{net} = c_p(T_3 - T_4) - c_p(T_2 - T_1) $$ Substitute isentropic relations: $$ W_{net} = c_p T_3 \left[1 - \dfrac{1}{r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}}\right] - c_p T_1 \left[r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - 1\right] $$ **Step 2 — Differentiate With Respect to $r_p$ and Set to Zero** Let $x = r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$ to simplify. Then: $$ W_{net} = c_p T_3(1 - x^{-1}) - c_p T_1(x - 1) $$ Wait — careful substitution gives: $$ W_{net} = c_p\left[T_3 - \dfrac{T_3}{x} - T_1 x + T_1\right] $$ Differentiating with respect to $x$: $$ \dfrac{dW_{net}}{dx} = c_p\left[\dfrac{T_3}{x^2} - T_1\right] = 0 $$ **Step 3 — Solve for Optimum x** $$ \dfrac{T_3}{x^2} = T_1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad x^2 = \dfrac{T_3}{T_1} $$ $$ x = \sqrt{\dfrac{T_3}{T_1}} $$ **Step 4 — Convert Back to Pressure Ratio** Since $x = r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$: $$ r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} = \sqrt{\dfrac{T_3}{T_1}} $$ Raise both sides to power $\gamma/(\gamma-1)$: $$ \boxed{r_{p,opt} = \left(\dfrac{T_3}{T_1}\right)^{\dfrac{\gamma}{2(\gamma-1)}}} $$ ## 3.3 Derivation of Mean Effective Pressure (MEP) MEP is the equivalent constant pressure that, acting over the volume change $(v_1 - v_2)$, would produce the same net work. **Step 1 — Write MEP Definition** $$ MEP = \dfrac{W_{net}}{v_1 - v_2} $$ **Step 2 — Use Ideal Gas Equation to Find Volumes** For an ideal gas at states 1 and 2: $$ P_1 v_1 = R T_1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad v_1 = \dfrac{R T_1}{P_1} $$ $$ P_2 v_2 = R T_2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad v_2 = \dfrac{R T_2}{P_2} = \dfrac{R T_2}{P_1 r_p} $$ **Step 3 — Compute $v_1 - v_2$** $$ v_1 - v_2 = \dfrac{R T_1}{P_1} - \dfrac{R T_2}{P_1 r_p} = \dfrac{R}{P_1}\left[T_1 - \dfrac{T_2}{r_p}\right] $$ Since $T_2 = T_1 r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} = T_1 r_p^{0.4/1.4}$ for $\gamma = 1.4$: $$ v_1 - v_2 = \dfrac{R T_1}{P_1}\left[1 - r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma - 1}\right] $$ **Step 4 — Final Form (KTU Standard Result)** $$ \boxed{MEP = \dfrac{P_1 \cdot W_{net}}{R T_1 \left[1 - r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma - 1}\right]}} $$ # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram — Simple Open Cycle Gas Turbine Plant ```mermaid flowchart LR A[AMBIENT AIR
State 1: T1, P1] --> B[COMPRESSOR
Wc absorbed
State 2: T2, P2] B --> C[COMBUSTION CHAMBER
Heat Qs added
State 3: T3, P3] C --> D[TURBINE
Wt produced
State 4: T4, P4] D --> E[EXHAUST TO ATMOSPHERE
State 1 conditions] D --> F[LOAD / GENERATOR / PROPELLER
Wt - Wc = Wnet] B --> F F --> G[NET POWER OUTPUT] ``` ## 4.2 Process Flow — P-V and T-S Representation ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph PV["P-V DIAGRAM LOGIC"] direction LR P1["1 to 2: Isentropic Compression
Slope increases sharply"] P2["2 to 3: Constant Pressure Heat Addition
Horizontal line at P2"] P3["3 to 4: Isentropic Expansion
Curve descending"] P4["4 to 1: Constant Pressure Heat Rejection
Horizontal line at P1"] P1 --> P2 --> P3 --> P4 end subgraph TS["T-S DIAGRAM LOGIC"] direction LR T1["1 to 2: Vertical line up
Entropy constant"] T2["2 to 3: Curve rising right
Heat input at constant P"] T3["3 to 4: Vertical line down
Entropy constant"] T4["4 to 1: Curve falling left
Heat rejected at constant P"] T1 --> T2 --> T3 --> T4 end ``` ## 4.3 Energy Flow Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB Qs["HEAT INPUT Qs
from fuel combustion"] Qs --> Wnet["NET WORK OUTPUT
Wnet = Wt minus Wc"] Wt["TURBINE WORK Wt"] Wc["COMPRESSOR WORK Wc
internal consumption"] Wt --> Wnet Wc --> Wnet Qr["HEAT REJECTION Qr
to atmosphere or cooler"] Qs --> Qr Wc -.->|drives via shaft| CompressorBlock[Mechanical Coupling] Wt -.-> CompressorBlock ``` ## 4.4 State Variable Progression Table | State | Pressure | Temperature | Description | Process Entering | |:-----:|:--------:|:-----------:|:------------|:----------------| | 1 | P₁ (low) | T₁ (ambient) | Air entry | Heat rejection end | | 2 | P₂ = rₚP₁ | T₂ > T₁ | Compressor exit | Isentropic compression | | 3 | P₃ = P₂ | T₃ (max, TIT) | Turbine inlet | Isobaric heat addition | | 4 | P₄ = P₁ | T₄ > T₁ | Turbine exit | Isentropic expansion | # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]** **Define the Brayton cycle. Why is it called an air-standard cycle?** **Model Answer:** The Brayton cycle is the ideal thermodynamic cycle on which gas turbine plants operate. It consists of two isentropic processes (compression 1-2 and expansion 3-4) and two isobaric processes (heat addition 2-3 and heat rejection 4-1). It is called an *air-standard cycle* because: 1. The working fluid is assumed to be **air** (a fixed composition ideal gas) throughout the cycle. 2. Combustion is replaced by an equivalent **heat addition from an external source** at constant pressure, so the chemical reaction of fuel burning is not modeled. 3. The exhaust process is replaced by an equivalent **heat rejection** to return the working fluid to its initial state. 4. All processes are assumed internally reversible (idealisations). This simplifies analysis drastically, allowing the use of ideal gas tables and fixed specific heats. --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **State the assumptions made in the air-standard analysis of the simple gas turbine cycle.** **Model Answer:** The principal assumptions are: 1. The working fluid is **air** which behaves as an **ideal gas** throughout. 2. Specific heats ($c_p$, $c_v$) are **constant** and evaluated at room temperature. 3. The compression and expansion processes are **internally reversible and adiabatic** (isentropic). 4. Heat addition (replacing combustion) and heat rejection occur at **constant pressure**. 5. **Kinetic and potential energy changes** are negligible. 6. The mass of fuel is **negligible** compared to the mass of air (so air mass flow ≈ working fluid mass flow). 7. The cycle is a **closed system** with a fixed mass of working fluid (even for open cycle, energy analysis is per unit mass). --- ## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!NOTE] > **Module 1 Internal Choice Pattern:** KTU provides a choice between two questions, each carrying 7 + 7 marks. Model solutions are given below. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024]** **(a)** Derive an expression for the air-standard efficiency of the Brayton cycle in terms of pressure ratio. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A gas turbine plant operates on the simple Brayton cycle with a pressure ratio of 8. Air enters the compressor at 300 K. The turbine inlet temperature is 1200 K. Assuming $\gamma = 1.4$ and $c_p = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K, calculate: - (i) Thermal efficiency - (ii) Net work output per kg of air - (iii) Work ratio - (iv) Heat supplied per kg of air - **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution — Part (a) Refer to the full derivation in **Section 3.1** above. The key steps are: - Heat supplied: $Q_s = c_p(T_3 - T_2)$ - Heat rejected: $Q_r = c_p(T_4 - T_1)$ - $\eta_{th} = 1 - \dfrac{Q_r}{Q_s} = 1 - \dfrac{T_4 - T_1}{T_3 - T_2}$ - Apply isentropic relations: $T_2 = T_1 r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$ and $T_4 = T_3/r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$ - After substitution and simplification: $\boxed{\eta_{th} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}}}$ #### Model Solution — Part (b) **Given:** $r_p = 8$, $T_1 = 300$ K, $T_3 = 1200$ K, $\gamma = 1.4$, $c_p = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K. **Step 1 — Compute the exponent term** $$ \dfrac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma} = \dfrac{0.4}{1.4} = 0.2857 $$ $$ r_p^{0.2857} = 8^{0.2857} = 1.8114 $$ **Step 2 — (i) Thermal Efficiency** $$ \eta_{th} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{1.8114} = 1 - 0.5520 = 0.4480 = \mathbf{44.80\%} $$ **Step 3 — Find T₂ and T₄** $$ T_2 = T_1 \cdot r_p^{0.2857} = 300 \times 1.8114 = \mathbf{543.4 \text{ K}} $$ $$ T_4 = \dfrac{T_3}{r_p^{0.2857}} = \dfrac{1200}{1.8114} = \mathbf{662.5 \text{ K}} $$ **Step 4 — (ii) Net Work Output** $$ W_c = c_p(T_2 - T_1) = 1.005 \times (543.4 - 300) = 1.005 \times 243.4 = \mathbf{244.6 \text{ kJ/kg}} $$ $$ W_t = c_p(T_3 - T_4) = 1.005 \times (1200 - 662.5) = 1.005 \times 537.5 = \mathbf{540.2 \text{ kJ/kg}} $$ $$ W_{net} = W_t - W_c = 540.2 - 244.6 = \mathbf{295.6 \text{ kJ/kg}} $$ **Step 5 — (iii) Work Ratio** $$ R_w = \dfrac{W_{net}}{W_t} = \dfrac{295.6}{540.2} = \mathbf{0.5472 \text{ or } 54.72\%} $$ **Step 6 — (iv) Heat Supplied** $$ Q_s = c_p(T_3 - T_2) = 1.005 \times (1200 - 543.4) = 1.005 \times 656.6 = \mathbf{659.9 \text{ kJ/kg}} $$ --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **(a)** Derive the expression for the optimum pressure ratio of a simple gas turbine cycle for maximum net work output. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** For a simple Brayton cycle, the turbine inlet temperature is 1500 K and the compressor inlet temperature is 300 K. Take $\gamma = 1.4$. Calculate: - (i) The optimum pressure ratio - (ii) The maximum net work per kg of air - (iii) The thermal efficiency at this optimum pressure ratio - **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution — Part (a) Refer to the full derivation in **Section 3.2** above. The key result is: $$ \boxed{r_{p,opt} = \left(\dfrac{T_3}{T_1}\right)^{\dfrac{\gamma}{2(\gamma-1)}}} $$ **Key concepts tested:** - The net work expression is differentiated with respect to $r_p$ - Maximum occurs when $\dfrac{dW_{net}}{dr_p} = 0$ - The student must clearly state that $T_3$ (TIT) and $T_1$ (ambient) are treated as **fixed constraints** #### Model Solution — Part (b) **Given:** $T_3 = 1500$ K, $T_1 = 300$ K, $\gamma = 1.4$, $c_p = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K. **Step 1 — (i) Optimum Pressure Ratio** $$ \dfrac{\gamma}{2(\gamma - 1)} = \dfrac{1.4}{2 \times 0.4} = \dfrac{1.4}{0.8} = 1.75 $$ $$ \dfrac{T_3}{T_1} = \dfrac{1500}{300} = 5 $$ $$ r_{p,opt} = 5^{1.75} = \mathbf{15.43} $$ **Step 2 — Compute $x = r_p^{0.2857}$ at optimum** $$ x_{opt} = \sqrt{\dfrac{T_3}{T_1}} = \sqrt{5} = 2.2361 $$ **Step 3 — (ii) Maximum Net Work** $$ W_{net,max} = c_p \left[T_3 + T_1 - 2\sqrt{T_1 \cdot T_3}\right] \cdot \dfrac{1}{...} $$ Using the clean form (derived from Section 3.2 step): $$ W_{net,max} = c_p \left[T_3 + T_1 - 2T_1 \sqrt{\dfrac{T_3}{T_1}}\right] $$ Wait — using the proper form derived: $$ W_{net} = c_p\left[T_3 - \dfrac{T_3}{x} - T_1 x + T_1\right] $$ Substituting $x = \sqrt{T_3/T_1}$: $$ W_{net,max} = c_p\left[T_3 - \dfrac{T_3}{\sqrt{T_3/T_1}} - T_1\sqrt{T_3/T_1} + T_1\right] $$ $$ = c_p\left[T_3 - T_3 \sqrt{T_1/T_3} - T_1 \sqrt{T_3/T_1} + T_1\right] $$ $$ = c_p\left[T_3 - \sqrt{T_1 T_3} - \sqrt{T_1 T_3} + T_1\right] $$ $$ = c_p\left[T_3 + T_1 - 2\sqrt{T_1 T_3}\right] $$ $$ = 1.005 \times \left[1500 + 300 - 2\sqrt{300 \times 1500}\right] $$ $$ = 1.005 \times \left[1800 - 2\sqrt{450000}\right] $$ $$ = 1.005 \times \left[1800 - 2 \times 670.82\right] $$ $$ = 1.005 \times \left[1800 - 1341.64\right] $$ $$ = 1.005 \times 458.36 = \mathbf{460.65 \text{ kJ/kg}} $$ **Step 4 — (iii) Thermal Efficiency at Optimum Pressure Ratio** $$ x_{opt} = 2.2361 $$ $$ \eta_{th,opt} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{x_{opt}} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{2.2361} = 1 - 0.4472 = \mathbf{0.5528 \text{ or } 55.28\%} $$ --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Sign convention confusion:** Compressor work is work input (negative), turbine work is positive. Mixing up signs destroys net work calculations. > 2. **Forgetting the assumption:** When deriving efficiency, students often forget to state that $c_p$ is constant. Without this, the $c_p$ terms cannot be cancelled. > 3. **Algebraic errors in optimum pressure ratio derivation:** Skipping the $\dfrac{dW_{net}}{dx} = 0$ step loses full marks (7 marks). > 4. **Unit inconsistency:** Mixing temperatures in °C and K. **Always use Kelvin** for gas cycle calculations. > 5. **Confusing optimum pressure ratio for work vs efficiency:** Thermal efficiency increases monotonically with $r_p$, so it has no "optimum." Optimum $r_p$ refers to **maximum net work**, not maximum efficiency. > 6. **Open vs closed cycle confusion:** In an open cycle, P₄ = P₁ (atmospheric), but in a closed cycle, P₄ ≠ P₁ necessarily — depends on cooler pressure drop. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - [x] The **simple cycle** = **Brayton cycle** = **Joule cycle** = 2 isentropic + 2 isobaric processes. - [x] Air-standard analysis assumes: ideal gas, constant $c_p$, reversible adiabatic processes, combustion replaced by heat addition at constant P. - [x] The cycle consists of: Compressor (1→2), Combustion Chamber (2→3), Turbine (3→4), Heat Rejection/Exhaust (4→1). - [x] **Key efficiency formula:** $\eta_{th} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}}$ — depends ONLY on pressure ratio for ideal cycle. - [x] **Net work** = Turbine work − Compressor work. - [x] **Work ratio** $R_w = W_{net}/W_t$ must be positive for feasible operation. - [x] **Optimum pressure ratio** for maximum net work: $r_{p,opt} = (T_3/T_1)^{\gamma/[2(\gamma-1)]}$. - [x] At optimum pressure ratio, the isentropic temperature ratio $T_2/T_1 = T_3/T_4 = \sqrt{T_3/T_1}$. - [x] **Mean Effective Pressure (MEP)** is a performance indicator — higher MEP means more compact engine for given power. - [x] Open cycle: P₄ = P₁ = atmospheric. Closed cycle: requires heat exchanger, separate cooler. - [x] Standard values: $c_p = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K, $\gamma = 1.4$ for air; $\gamma = 1.333$ for combustion products (if asked). - [x] Always express temperatures in **Kelvin**. - [x] Threshold pressure ratio exists below which $W_t < W_c$ and cycle produces no net work. - [x] Thermal efficiency of Brayton cycle is **lower than Otto and Diesel** at the same compression ratio, but increases with $r_p$.

topics.title: Heat Exchange Cycle and Reheat Cycle
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMET741 - GAS TURBINE AND JET PROPULSION > - **Module:** Module Module 1 > - **Topic:** Heat Exchange Cycle and Reheat Cycle # SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW ## 1.1 The Heat Exchange Cycle (Regenerative / Recuperative Gas Turbine Cycle) ### Formal Academic Definition The **Heat Exchange Cycle** (also known as the **Regenerative Cycle** or **Recuperative Cycle**) is a thermodynamic modification of the basic Brayton (Joule) cycle in which a **recuperative heat exchanger** (called a *regenerator* or *recuperator*) is incorporated between the gas turbine exhaust and the compressor discharge. The hot turbine exhaust gases transfer a portion of their residual thermal energy to the cooler compressed air leaving the compressor *before* the air enters the combustion chamber, thereby **recovering waste heat** and reducing the fuel input required to reach the turbine inlet temperature (TIT). The fundamental prerequisite for regeneration to be thermodynamically feasible is: $$T_4 > T_2$$ i.e., the turbine exhaust temperature must be greater than the compressor delivery temperature. If this condition is violated, the regenerator is bypassed or becomes a net heat loss. ### Conceptual Analogy — "The Pre-Heated Shower" Imagine a household geyser that is fed with cold water directly from the tank. To get a hot shower, the geyser must heat the entire volume of cold water from room temperature (say, 25 °C) to 50 °C — a large fuel/electricity demand. Now imagine placing a small copper heat exchanger coil on the hot wastewater drain pipe, which pre-warms the incoming cold water to, say, 40 °C before it enters the geyser. The geyser now only has to raise the temperature by another 10 °C. **Fuel consumption drops dramatically.** The gas turbine regenerator performs exactly this trick — it steals heat from the turbine's "exhaust drain" and pre-warms the compressor's "fresh intake." ### Standard Metrics & Constants - **Specific heat of air (cₚ)** = **1.005 kJ/kg·K** (at standard conditions, used for all KTU problems) - **Ratio of specific heats (γ)** = **1.4** (for air as a cold-standard working fluid) - **Adiabatic index (γ - 1)/γ** = **0.2857** (this is the exponent used in isentropic relations) - **Turbine Inlet Temperature (TIT)** = T₃ (also called T₅ in some textbook notations) - **Regenerator Effectiveness (ε)** — dimensionless parameter between 0 and 1 - **Isentropic efficiency of compressor (η_C)** and **turbine (η_T)** are typically assumed = 1.0 unless otherwise stated (since Module 1 deals with the *ideal cycle*). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** The Heat Exchange Cycle and Reheat Cycle are covered as two of the *four major cycle improvements* to the basic gas turbine cycle. Students must master the T-s and P-v diagrams, the efficiency formulas, and the regenerator effectiveness equation — these appear as 14-mark ESE questions every academic year. > [!NOTE] > **Naming Convention Used Throughout These Notes:** > - Station **1**: Compressor inlet (ambient air) > - Station **2**: Compressor outlet > - Station **2'**: Regenerator air-side outlet (pre-heated compressed air) > - Station **3**: Turbine inlet (after combustion) > - Station **4**: Turbine exhaust > - Station **5**: Regenerator gas-side outlet (cooled exhaust) > - Stations **3'** and **4'** are used in reheat cycle to denote HP and LP turbine stations. --- ## 1.2 The Reheat Cycle ### Formal Academic Definition The **Reheat Cycle** is a thermodynamic modification of the basic Brayton cycle in which the working fluid is **expanded in two (or more) turbine stages** with **intermediate reheat** of the partially expanded gas between the stages. After partial expansion in the **High-Pressure (HP) turbine**, the gas is sent back to a **reheater** (a second combustion chamber) where additional fuel is burned to raise its temperature back to the maximum permissible TIT, and then the gas is expanded further in the **Low-Pressure (LP) turbine** down to the exhaust pressure. The reheat cycle does not require an *increase* in mean effective pressure, but it does **increase the net specific work output** and **raise the thermal efficiency** of the cycle, while also reducing the turbine exhaust temperature. ### Conceptual Analogy — "Two Small Sips vs One Big Gulp" Imagine a person extremely thirsty on a hot day. Drinking an entire litre of water in one big gulp (a single-stage expansion) causes discomfort and some of the "coolness benefit" is wasted as the stomach cannot absorb it all at once. Drinking the same litre in two sips — first 500 ml, then a small break (the "reheat" — perhaps a brief walk in shade to let the body re-equilibrate), then another 500 ml — is more *efficient* and the body utilizes the cooling effect better. The reheat cycle works analogously: it extracts work in **stages**, and adds heat **in between stages** so that each stage operates closer to its optimum temperature, yielding greater overall work and efficiency. ### Standard Metrics & Constants - **Maximum cycle temperature (TIT)** = T₃ = T₅ (reheat brings the temperature back up to TIT before the second stage) - **Pressure ratio** = r_p (same for both turbines) - **Total work output** = W_HP + W_LP - **Total heat input** = Q_combustion + Q_reheat - **Net work** = W_total - W_compressor - The **mean effective pressure (MEP)** is higher in the reheat cycle than the simple cycle for the same pressure ratio. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why Reheat? — The Engineering "Why"** > 1. Increases net specific work output per kg of air. > 2. Improves thermal efficiency when combined with regeneration. > 3. Allows the use of a higher pressure ratio (which alone reduces efficiency) without sacrificing work output. > 4. Lowers the turbine exhaust gas temperature — useful when this heat is to be used downstream in a regenerator. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Examiner's Convention:** In reheat cycle problems, students must clearly label the *intermediate pressure* between the HP and LP turbines and the *reheat temperature*. These are the most commonly missed annotations in board exam answer sheets. # SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET ## 2.1 Heat Exchange Cycle — Operational Logic (Step-by-Step) **Step 1 — Air Intake & Compression (Process 1→2):** Ambient air at state 1 (T₁, P₁) is drawn into the **axial-flow compressor** and compressed isentropically to a higher pressure P₂ = r_p × P₁. The temperature rises from T₁ to T₂. Work is *consumed* (negative work done on the system). **Step 2 — Pre-heating in the Regenerator (Process 2→2'):** The hot compressed air passes through the *cold side* of the **regenerator (heat exchanger)**. It receives heat from the *hot* turbine exhaust (which is at temperature T₄ > T₂). The compressed air temperature rises from T₂ to T₂' (where T₂ < T₂' < T₄). **No work** is done; this is a constant-pressure (approximately) heat addition process. **Step 3 — Combustion / Heat Addition (Process 2'→3):** The pre-heated air enters the **combustion chamber**, where fuel is burned at (approximately) constant pressure. The temperature rises sharply to the **Turbine Inlet Temperature T₃**. Heat is *added* (positive Q). **Step 4 — Expansion in the Turbine (Process 3→4):** The hot, high-pressure gas expands isentropically in the **turbine**, doing work and dropping in temperature from T₃ to T₄. The work is *produced* (positive work). **Step 5 — Heat Rejection in the Regenerator (Process 4→5):** The hot exhaust gas passes through the *hot side* of the regenerator, giving up part of its heat to the cooler incoming compressed air. Its temperature drops from T₄ to T₅. It is then discharged to the atmosphere. **Step 6 — Closed Cycle Return (Process 5→1):** (only for closed cycle; for open cycle, fresh air is taken in at 1) ### KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — Heat Exchange Cycle | # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks | |---|----------|---------|-------|---------| | 1 | Isentropic compression relation | $T_2 = T_1 \cdot (r_p)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$ | K | r_p = P₂/P₁ | | 2 | Isentropic expansion relation | $T_4 = T_3 \cdot (1/r_p)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$ | K | Inverse of compression | | 3 | Compressor work (specific) | $W_C = c_p (T_2 - T_1)$ | kJ/kg | Negative for the cycle | | 4 | Turbine work (specific) | $W_T = c_p (T_3 - T_4)$ | kJ/kg | Positive for the cycle | | 5 | Net work (specific) | $W_{net} = W_T - W_C = c_p[(T_3 - T_4) - (T_2 - T_1)]$ | kJ/kg | Output | | 6 | Heat supplied (specific) | $Q_1 = c_p (T_3 - T_2')$ | kJ/kg | Reduced by regeneration | | 7 | Thermal efficiency | $\eta_{th} = \dfrac{W_{net}}{Q_1} = \dfrac{(T_3 - T_4) - (T_2 - T_1)}{T_3 - T_2'}$ | — | Dimensionless | | 8 | Regenerator effectiveness | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{T_{2'} - T_2}{T_4 - T_2}$ | — | 0 ≤ ε ≤ 1; ideal = 1 | | 9 | Maximum allowable T₂' (ε=1) | $T_{2',max} = T_4$ | K | Air leaves at T₄ | | 10 | Mean effective pressure | $MEP = \dfrac{W_{net}}{v_1 - v_5}$ (approx.) | kPa | For displacement-type | ### Regenerator Effectiveness — The Core Concept The **effectiveness ε** measures *how close* the regenerator comes to the ideal limit of heating the compressed air to the turbine exhaust temperature. $$\varepsilon = \frac{\text{Actual heat gained by air}}{\text{Maximum possible heat gain}} = \frac{T_{2'} - T_2}{T_4 - T_2}$$ - **ε = 0**: No regenerator (basic Brayton cycle) - **ε = 1**: Perfect regenerator (air exits at T₄) — physically impossible (would require infinite area and zero losses) - **Typical practical value**: ε = 0.70 to 0.85 in industrial gas turbines ### The "T₄ vs T₂" Check Regeneration is *only* useful when **T₄ > T₂**: - If T₄ < T₂ → regeneration would *cool* the air; it is bypassed. - This condition places a **lower bound** on the pressure ratio for any given TIT. Above a critical r_p, regeneration becomes useless. --- ## 2.2 Reheat Cycle — Operational Logic (Step-by-Step) **Step 1 — Air Intake & Compression (Process 1→2):** Identical to the basic cycle. Ambient air compressed isentropically. **Step 2 — Combustion (Process 2→3):** Fuel is added in the main combustion chamber, raising temperature from T₂ to TIT (= T₃). The pressure remains (approximately) constant. **Step 3 — High-Pressure Turbine Expansion (Process 3→4):** Gas expands in the HP turbine from pressure P₃ to the **intermediate pressure P₄**. Temperature drops from T₃ to T₄. **Step 4 — Reheating (Process 4→5):** In the **reheater**, additional fuel is burned at constant pressure (P₄), raising the temperature back up to the TIT (i.e., T₅ = T₃). **Step 5 — Low-Pressure Turbine Expansion (Process 5→6):** Gas expands in the LP turbine from P₅ = P₄ back down to atmospheric pressure P₁. Temperature drops from T₅ to T₆. **Step 6 — Exhaust (Process 6→1):** Gases are discharged. ### KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — Reheat Cycle | # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks | |---|----------|---------|-------|---------| | 1 | Compressor work | $W_C = c_p(T_2 - T_1)$ | kJ/kg | Same as before | | 2 | HP Turbine work | $W_{T,HP} = c_p(T_3 - T_4)$ | kJ/kg | Partial expansion | | 3 | LP Turbine work | $W_{T,LP} = c_p(T_5 - T_6)$ | kJ/kg | Second-stage expansion | | 4 | Total turbine work | $W_T = W_{T,HP} + W_{T,LP}$ | kJ/kg | Sum | | 5 | Net work | $W_{net} = W_T - W_C$ | kJ/kg | Output | | 6 | Main combustor heat | $Q_1 = c_p(T_3 - T_2)$ | kJ/kg | First heat addition | | 7 | Reheat heat | $Q_R = c_p(T_5 - T_4)$ | kJ/kg | Second heat addition | | 8 | Total heat input | $Q_{in} = Q_1 + Q_R$ | kJ/kg | Sum | | 9 | Thermal efficiency | $\eta_{th} = \dfrac{W_{net}}{Q_{in}}$ | — | Output / Input | | 10 | Optimal intermediate P | $P_4 = \sqrt{P_2 \cdot P_3} = P_2 \cdot \sqrt{r_p}$ | kPa | For maximum work | > [!NOTE] > **Optimal Intermediate Pressure (P_int):** For *maximum net work output* with reheat, the intermediate pressure should be the **geometric mean** of the compressor discharge pressure (P₂) and the main combustor pressure (P₃ ≈ P₂). This is a derivation that appears in KTU Module 1 problems and is examined as a 7-mark sub-question. ### Real-World Engineering Utility - **Heat Exchange (Regenerative) Cycle:** Used in land-based industrial gas turbines (e.g., electric power generation), where the exhaust heat is recovered to preheat compressor discharge air. Improves fuel economy by 20–30%. - **Reheat Cycle:** Used in large utility gas turbines (GE 7HA, Siemens SGT-9000HL) and jet engines, where the gas is reheated in multiple turbine stages. In jet engines, the reheater is implicit — it is the afterburner. - **Combined Heat Exchange + Reheat:** Modern high-efficiency gas turbines use **both** improvements together, achieving thermal efficiencies exceeding 42–45% (compared to ~28% for the simple Brayton cycle). # SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX ## 3.1 Derivation: Optimal Intermediate Pressure for Maximum Work in Reheat Cycle **Given:** - Compressor inlet: T₁, P₁ - Compressor outlet: T₂, P₂ = r_p · P₁ - Reheat temperature: T₃ = T₅ (TIT) - Intermediate pressure: P₄ - LP turbine exhaust: T₆, P₆ = P₁ **Step 1 — Express T₄ in terms of P₄:** The HP turbine expands from P₃ to P₄. Assuming P₃ ≈ P₂: $$T_4 = T_3 \cdot \left(\frac{P_4}{P_3}\right)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} = T_3 \cdot \left(\frac{P_4}{P_2}\right)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$$ **Step 2 — Express T₆ in terms of P₄:** The LP turbine expands from P₅ = P₄ down to P₆ = P₁: $$T_6 = T_5 \cdot \left(\frac{P_6}{P_5}\right)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} = T_3 \cdot \left(\frac{P_1}{P_4}\right)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$$ **Step 3 — Net work expression:** $$W_{net} = c_p \left[(T_3 - T_4) + (T_3 - T_6) - (T_2 - T_1)\right]$$ **Step 4 — Substitute T₄ and T₆:** $$W_{net} = c_p \left[2T_3 - T_3\left(\frac{P_4}{P_2}\right)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - T_3\left(\frac{P_1}{P_4}\right)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - (T_2 - T_1)\right]$$ **Step 5 — Differentiate W_net with respect to P₄ and set to zero:** Let $a = (\gamma - 1)/\gamma$. Then: $$\frac{dW_{net}}{dP_4} = c_p \left[-T_3 \cdot a \cdot \left(\frac{P_4}{P_2}\right)^{a-1} \cdot \frac{1}{P_2} - T_3 \cdot a \cdot \left(\frac{P_1}{P_4}\right)^{a-1} \cdot \left(-\frac{P_1}{P_4^2}\right)\right] = 0$$ **Step 6 — Simplify (note that $\left(\frac{P_1}{P_4}\right)^{a-1} \cdot \frac{P_1}{P_4^2} = \frac{P_1^a}{P_4^{a+1}}$):** $$-\frac{T_3 \cdot a \cdot P_4^{a-1}}{P_2^a} + \frac{T_3 \cdot a \cdot P_1^a}{P_4^{a+1}} = 0$$ $$\frac{P_1^a}{P_4^{a+1}} = \frac{P_4^{a-1}}{P_2^a}$$ $$P_1^a \cdot P_2^a = P_4^{a-1} \cdot P_4^{a+1} = P_4^{2a}$$ **Step 7 — Solve for P₄:** $$P_4^{2a} = (P_1 \cdot P_2)^a$$ $$P_4 = \sqrt{P_1 \cdot P_2}$$ $$\boxed{P_4 = \sqrt{P_1 \cdot P_2}}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Insight:** This is the classic result — the optimal intermediate pressure is the **geometric mean** of the inlet and exit pressures for *maximum net work*. Note: this is for maximum *work*, not maximum *efficiency*. The condition for maximum efficiency gives a different (lower) intermediate pressure. --- ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Heat Exchange Cycle **Problem Statement:** A gas turbine with a regenerator operates on the Brayton cycle with the following data: - Compressor inlet: T₁ = 300 K, P₁ = 1 bar - Pressure ratio: r_p = 6 - Turbine inlet temperature: T₃ = 1200 K - Regenerator effectiveness: ε = 0.80 - cₚ = 1.005 kJ/kg·K, γ = 1.4, (γ-1)/γ = 0.2857 **Find:** (a) Net work output, (b) Heat supplied, (c) Thermal efficiency, (d) Compare with simple Brayton cycle. **Solution — Step 1: Find T₂ using isentropic compression** $$T_2 = T_1 \cdot r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} = 300 \cdot 6^{0.2857}$$ $$6^{0.2857} = e^{0.2857 \cdot \ln 6} = e^{0.2857 \cdot 1.7918} = e^{0.5118} = 1.6684$$ $$T_2 = 300 \cdot 1.6684 = 500.5 \text{ K}$$ **Solution — Step 2: Find T₄ using isentropic expansion** $$T_4 = T_3 / r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} = 1200 / 1.6684 = 719.2 \text{ K}$$ **Solution — Step 3: Find T₂' using regenerator effectiveness** $$T_{2'} = T_2 + \varepsilon(T_4 - T_2) = 500.5 + 0.80 \cdot (719.2 - 500.5)$$ $$T_{2'} = 500.5 + 0.80 \cdot 218.7 = 500.5 + 175.0 = 675.5 \text{ K}$$ **Solution — Step 4: Compressor work** $$W_C = c_p(T_2 - T_1) = 1.005 \cdot (500.5 - 300) = 1.005 \cdot 200.5 = 201.5 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ **Solution — Step 5: Turbine work** $$W_T = c_p(T_3 - T_4) = 1.005 \cdot (1200 - 719.2) = 1.005 \cdot 480.8 = 483.2 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ **Solution — Step 6: Net work** $$W_{net} = W_T - W_C = 483.2 - 201.5 = 281.7 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ **Solution — Step 7: Heat supplied (with regenerator)** $$Q_1 = c_p(T_3 - T_{2'}) = 1.005 \cdot (1200 - 675.5) = 1.005 \cdot 524.5 = 527.1 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ **Solution — Step 8: Thermal efficiency (with regenerator)** $$\eta_{th,regen} = \frac{W_{net}}{Q_1} = \frac{281.7}{527.1} = 0.5344 = 53.44\%$$ **Solution — Step 9: Compare with simple Brayton cycle (no regenerator)** For simple Brayton: $$Q_{1,simple} = c_p(T_3 - T_2) = 1.005 \cdot (1200 - 500.5) = 703.0 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ $$\eta_{th,simple} = \frac{W_{net}}{Q_{1,simple}} = \frac{281.7}{703.0} = 0.4007 = 40.07\%$$ **Efficiency gain due to regeneration: 53.44% − 40.07% = 13.37 percentage points (a 33.4% relative improvement).** --- ## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Reheat Cycle **Problem Statement:** A gas turbine with reheat operates with: - T₁ = 300 K, P₁ = 1 bar - Pressure ratio: r_p = 8 - Maximum (TIT) temperature: T₃ = T₅ = 1200 K - Intermediate pressure: P₄ = 2.83 bar (geometric mean √8 ≈ 2.83) - cₚ = 1.005 kJ/kg·K, γ = 1.4, (γ-1)/γ = 0.2857 **Find:** (a) Net work output, (b) Total heat input, (c) Thermal efficiency. **Solution — Step 1: Find T₂** $$8^{0.2857} = e^{0.2857 \cdot 2.0794} = e^{0.5941} = 1.8114$$ $$T_2 = 300 \cdot 1.8114 = 543.4 \text{ K}$$ **Solution — Step 2: Find T₄ (HP turbine exit)** Pressure ratio of HP turbine: P₃/P₄ = 8/2.83 = 2.828 (= √8) $$T_4 = T_3 \cdot (1/2.828)^{0.2857} = 1200 / (2.828)^{0.2857}$$ $$(2.828)^{0.2857} = e^{0.2857 \cdot \ln 2.828} = e^{0.2857 \cdot 1.0397} = e^{0.2971} = 1.3459$$ $$T_4 = 1200 / 1.3459 = 891.6 \text{ K}$$ **Solution — Step 3: Find T₆ (LP turbine exit)** Pressure ratio of LP turbine: P₅/P₆ = 2.83/1 = 2.828 (= √8) $$T_6 = T_5 \cdot (1/2.828)^{0.2857} = 1200 / 1.3459 = 891.6 \text{ K}$$ (Note: T₄ = T₆ by symmetry because the intermediate pressure is the geometric mean — a special property of the optimal case.) **Solution — Step 4: Compressor work** $$W_C = 1.005 \cdot (543.4 - 300) = 1.005 \cdot 243.4 = 244.6 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ **Solution — Step 5: HP Turbine work** $$W_{T,HP} = 1.005 \cdot (1200 - 891.6) = 1.005 \cdot 308.4 = 309.9 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ **Solution — Step 6: LP Turbine work** $$W_{T,LP} = 1.005 \cdot (1200 - 891.6) = 309.9 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ **Solution — Step 7: Total turbine work & net work** $$W_T = 309.9 + 309.9 = 619.8 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ $$W_{net} = 619.8 - 244.6 = 375.2 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ **Solution — Step 8: Total heat input** $$Q_1 = 1.005 \cdot (1200 - 543.4) = 1.005 \cdot 656.6 = 659.9 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ $$Q_R = 1.005 \cdot (1200 - 891.6) = 309.9 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ $$Q_{in} = 659.9 + 309.9 = 969.8 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ **Solution — Step 9: Thermal efficiency** $$\eta_{th,reheat} = \frac{375.2}{969.8} = 0.3869 = 38.69\%$$ **Note:** The efficiency is *lower* than the simple cycle at this pressure ratio, but the **specific work output** is *higher* (375.2 vs ~280 kJ/kg). Reheat's primary value is work augmentation, not efficiency — unless combined with regeneration, where both improve. # SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS ## 4.1 Heat Exchange (Regenerative) Cycle — T-s Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TD A[State 1: Compressor Inlet
T1 = 300 K, P1 = 1 bar] --> B[Process 1 to 2
Isentropic Compression in Axial Compressor] B --> C[State 2: Compressor Outlet
T2 approx 500 K, P2 = 6 bar] C --> D[Process 2 to 2 dash
Constant-Pressure Heat Gain in Regenerator Cold Side] D --> E[State 2 dash: Regenerator Air-Side Outlet
T2 dash approx 675 K] E --> F[Process 2 dash to 3
Constant-Pressure Combustion in Main Combustion Chamber] F --> G[State 3: Turbine Inlet
T3 = 1200 K, P3 = P2] G --> H[Process 3 to 4
Isentropic Expansion in Gas Turbine] H --> I[State 4: Turbine Exhaust
T4 approx 719 K, P4 = P1] I --> J[Process 4 to 5
Constant-Pressure Heat Loss in Regenerator Hot Side] J --> K[State 5: Regenerator Gas-Side Outlet
T5 less than T4, P5 = P1] K --> L[Exhaust to Atmosphere
Process 5 to 1 for Closed Cycle] ``` ### P-v Representation of Heat Exchange Cycle ```mermaid flowchart LR P1[Point 1: Low P, Low V
Compressor Inlet] -->|Isentropic Compression| P2[Point 2: High P, Low V
Compressor Outlet] P2 -->|Regen Heating Const P| P2d[Point 2 dash: Same P, Higher V
Pre-heated Air] P2d -->|Combustion Const P| P3[Point 3: Same P, Much Higher V
Turbine Inlet] P3 -->|Isentropic Expansion| P4[Point 4: Low P, High V
Turbine Exhaust] P4 -->|Regen Cooling Const P| P5[Point 5: Same Low P, Lower V
Cooled Exhaust] ``` --- ## 4.2 Reheat Cycle — T-s Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TD A[State 1: Compressor Inlet
T1 = 300 K, P1 = 1 bar] --> B[Process 1 to 2
Isentropic Compression] B --> C[State 2: Compressor Outlet
T2 approx 543 K, P2 = 8 bar] C --> D[Process 2 to 3
Main Combustion Chamber Constant P
Q1 added] D --> E[State 3: HP Turbine Inlet
T3 = TIT = 1200 K] E --> F[Process 3 to 4
HP Turbine Isentropic Expansion to Intermediate P] F --> G[State 4: HP Turbine Exit
T4 approx 892 K, P4 = sqrt P1 P2 approx 2.83 bar] G --> H[Process 4 to 5
Reheater Constant P
QR added, T rises back to TIT] H --> I[State 5: LP Turbine Inlet
T5 = TIT = 1200 K, P5 = P4] I --> J[Process 5 to 6
LP Turbine Isentropic Expansion to P1] J --> K[State 6: LP Turbine Exit
T6 approx 892 K, P6 = 1 bar] K --> L[Exhaust to Atmosphere] ``` ### Block-Level Functional Architecture — Combined Reheat + Regeneration ```mermaid flowchart LR AIR[Ambient Air Intake] --> COMP[Axial Compressor
W consumed] COMP --> REG1[Regenerator Cold Side
Pre-heats Air] REG1 --> CC1[Main Combustion Chamber
Q1 added] CC1 --> HPT[High Pressure Turbine
W produced] HPT --> RH[Reheater
QR added] RH --> LPT[Low Pressure Turbine
W produced] LPT --> REG2[Regenerator Hot Side
Exhaust Heat Recovered] REG2 --> EXH[Exhaust to Atmosphere] HPT -.Drives.-> COMP LPT -.Drives Generator.-> GEN[Power Output Shaft] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Block Diagram:** The single-shaft arrangement shown is common in industrial gas turbines. In aviation engines, the LP turbine drives the fan/bypass (different shaft) and only the HP turbine drives the compressor. The regenerator is **not** used in jet engines (weight and pressure-loss penalty) but **is** used in land-based power turbines. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP ## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- ### **Question 1: Define regenerator effectiveness for a gas turbine cycle. What is its typical practical range?** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 | Module 1 | CO1]** **Model Answer:** The **regenerator effectiveness (ε)** is defined as the ratio of the actual heat transferred to the compressed air in the regenerator to the maximum possible heat transfer (which would occur if the compressed air were heated to the turbine exhaust temperature T₄). $$\varepsilon = \frac{T_{2'} - T_2}{T_4 - T_2}$$ where: - T₂' = temperature of air leaving the regenerator (cold side outlet) - T₂ = temperature of compressed air entering the regenerator - T₄ = turbine exhaust temperature (hot side inlet) **Practical range:** In industrial gas turbines, ε typically lies between **0.70 and 0.85** (70% to 85%). Values above 0.90 are rarely achieved due to finite heat exchanger area, pressure drop penalties, and material constraints. --- ### **Question 2: State the conditions under which regeneration is beneficial in a gas turbine.** **[KTU University Exam – June 2023 | Module 1 | CO1]** **Model Answer:** Regeneration is thermodynamically beneficial **only when the turbine exhaust temperature T₄ is greater than the compressor delivery temperature T₂**: $$T_4 > T_2$$ If T₄ < T₂, the regenerator would actually *cool* the compressed air (worsening performance), and it is bypassed. This condition places an **upper limit on the pressure ratio** for a given Turbine Inlet Temperature (TIT). As pressure ratio increases, T₂ rises while T₄ falls, eventually crossing over — beyond this critical pressure ratio, regeneration becomes useless or detrimental. This is why regeneration is paired with **low-to-moderate pressure ratios (4 to 8)** in industrial gas turbines. --- ## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice --- ### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Heat Exchange Cycle Analysis** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 | Module 1 | CO1, CO2 | Bloom: Apply/Analyze]** A gas turbine with a regenerator operates on the ideal Brayton cycle. Compressor inlet conditions are **T₁ = 300 K** and **P₁ = 1 bar**. The pressure ratio is **rₚ = 7**, and the maximum cycle temperature is **T₃ = 1300 K**. The regenerator effectiveness is **ε = 0.75**. Use cₚ = 1.005 kJ/kg·K and γ = 1.4. **Solve the following:** **(a) [7 Marks]** Calculate the net work output, heat supplied, and thermal efficiency of the cycle **with** the regenerator. Sketch the T-s diagram. **(b) [7 Marks]** Repeat the calculations **without** the regenerator (i.e., for the simple Brayton cycle) and comment on the percentage improvement in thermal efficiency due to regeneration. --- #### **Model Solution — Part (a): With Regenerator** **Step 1: Isentropic Relations** $$\frac{T_2}{T_1} = r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} = 7^{0.2857}$$ $$7^{0.2857} = e^{0.2857 \times \ln 7} = e^{0.2857 \times 1.9459} = e^{0.5558} = 1.7430$$ $$T_2 = 300 \times 1.7430 = 522.9 \text{ K}$$ $$T_4 = T_3 / r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} = 1300 / 1.7430 = 745.8 \text{ K}$$ **Step 2: Regenerator Outlet Temperature** $$T_{2'} = T_2 + \varepsilon(T_4 - T_2) = 522.9 + 0.75 \times (745.8 - 522.9)$$ $$T_{2'} = 522.9 + 0.75 \times 222.9 = 522.9 + 167.2 = 690.1 \text{ K}$$ **Step 3: Work and Heat Calculations** $$W_C = c_p(T_2 - T_1) = 1.005 \times (522.9 - 300) = 1.005 \times 222.9 = 224.0 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ $$W_T = c_p(T_3 - T_4) = 1.005 \times (1300 - 745.8) = 1.005 \times 554.2 = 557.0 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ $$W_{net} = W_T - W_C = 557.0 - 224.0 = 333.0 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ $$Q_1 = c_p(T_3 - T_{2'}) = 1.005 \times (1300 - 690.1) = 1.005 \times 609.9 = 612.9 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ **Step 4: Thermal Efficiency** $$\eta_{th,regen} = \frac{W_{net}}{Q_1} = \frac{333.0}{612.9} = 0.5433 = 54.33\%$$ **T-s Diagram (text representation):** - 1→2: Isentropic compression (vertical line in T-s) - 2→2': Constant-pressure heating in regenerator - 2'→3: Constant-pressure combustion - 3→4: Isentropic expansion - 4→5: Constant-pressure cooling in regenerator (gas side) - 5→1: Constant-pressure heat rejection (in closed cycle) --- #### **Model Solution — Part (b): Without Regenerator (Simple Brayton)** **Step 1: Same Compressor and Turbine Temperatures (T₂ and T₄ unchanged)** $$T_2 = 522.9 \text{ K}, \quad T_4 = 745.8 \text{ K}$$ **Step 2: Heat Supplied (no regenerator, so air enters combustor at T₂)** $$Q_{1,simple} = c_p(T_3 - T_2) = 1.005 \times (1300 - 522.9) = 1.005 \times 777.1 = 780.9 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ **Step 3: Thermal Efficiency (Simple Cycle)** $$\eta_{th,simple} = \frac{W_{net}}{Q_{1,simple}} = \frac{333.0}{780.9} = 0.4265 = 42.65\%$$ **Step 4: Percentage Improvement** $$\% \text{ Improvement} = \frac{\eta_{th,regen} - \eta_{th,simple}}{\eta_{th,simple}} \times 100\%$$ $$= \frac{54.33 - 42.65}{42.65} \times 100\% = \frac{11.68}{42.65} \times 100\% = 27.39\%$$ **Comment:** The regenerator reduces the fuel input from 780.9 to 612.9 kJ/kg (a saving of **21.5%** in fuel energy), thereby raising the thermal efficiency from 42.65% to 54.33% — a **27.4% relative improvement**. The net work output is unchanged, but the *fuel bill* is dramatically reduced. --- ### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Reheat Cycle Analysis** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Alternative) | Module 1 | CO1, CO2 | Bloom: Apply/Analyze]** A gas turbine with reheat operates between **P₁ = 1 bar (T₁ = 300 K)** and **P₂ = 9 bar**. The maximum cycle temperature is **T₃ = T₅ = 1400 K**. The intermediate pressure is **P₄ = 3 bar**. Use cₚ = 1.005 kJ/kg·K, γ = 1.4. **Solve the following:** **(a) [7 Marks]** Calculate the net work output, total heat input, and thermal efficiency of the reheat cycle. Compare the work output with that of a simple Brayton cycle operating between the same pressures. **(b) [7 Marks]** Show that for maximum net work, the optimal intermediate pressure is the geometric mean of the inlet and exit pressures. Find the optimal intermediate pressure for this cycle and the corresponding maximum work. --- #### **Model Solution — Part (a): Reheat Cycle Work and Efficiency** **Step 1: Compressor Outlet Temperature** $$r_p = 9, \quad 9^{0.2857} = e^{0.2857 \times \ln 9} = e^{0.2857 \times 2.1972} = e^{0.6278} = 1.8733$$ $$T_2 = 300 \times 1.8733 = 562.0 \text{ K}$$ **Step 2: HP Turbine Exit Temperature (P₃ = P₂ = 9 bar, P₄ = 3 bar)** $$\text{HP pressure ratio} = 9/3 = 3$$ $$T_4 = T_3 \times (1/3)^{0.2857} = 1400 \times 3^{-0.2857}$$ $$3^{0.2857} = e^{0.2857 \times 1.0986} = e^{0.3139} = 1.3687$$ $$T_4 = 1400 / 1.3687 = 1023.0 \text{ K}$$ **Step 3: LP Turbine Exit Temperature (P₅ = 3 bar, P₆ = 1 bar)** $$\text{LP pressure ratio} = 3/1 = 3$$ $$T_6 = T_5 \times (1/3)^{0.2857} = 1400 / 1.3687 = 1023.0 \text{ K}$$ **Step 4: Work and Heat Calculations** $$W_C = 1.005 \times (562.0 - 300) = 1.005 \times 262.0 = 263.3 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ $$W_{T,HP} = 1.005 \times (1400 - 1023.0) = 1.005 \times 377.0 = 378.9 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ $$W_{T,LP} = 1.005 \times (1400 - 1023.0) = 378.9 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ $$W_T = 378.9 + 378.9 = 757.8 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ $$W_{net} = 757.8 - 263.3 = 494.5 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ $$Q_1 = 1.005 \times (1400 - 562.0) = 1.005 \times 838.0 = 842.2 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ $$Q_R = 1.005 \times (1400 - 1023.0) = 378.9 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ $$Q_{in} = 842.2 + 378.9 = 1221.1 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ **Step 5: Thermal Efficiency** $$\eta_{th,reheat} = \frac{494.5}{1221.1} = 0.4050 = 40.50\%$$ **Step 6: Comparison with Simple Brayton Cycle** For the simple Brayton cycle (no reheat) at r_p = 9: $$T_4 = 1400 / 1.8733 = 747.3 \text{ K}$$ $$W_{T,simple} = 1.005 \times (1400 - 747.3) = 1.005 \times 652.7 = 656.0 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ $$W_{net,simple} = 656.0 - 263.3 = 392.7 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ **Net work increase due to reheat: 494.5 − 392.7 = 101.8 kJ/kg (a 25.9% increase).** --- #### **Model Solution — Part (b): Optimal Intermediate Pressure Proof** The proof was derived earlier in **Section 3.1**. The key result is: $$\boxed{P_{4,opt} = \sqrt{P_1 \cdot P_2}}$$ **For this cycle:** $$P_{4,opt} = \sqrt{1 \times 9} = 3 \text{ bar}$$ This **coincides with the given intermediate pressure** — so the given cycle is already operating at the *optimal* intermediate pressure for maximum work. **Maximum net work at optimal P₄:** With P₄ = 3 bar (optimal), the HP and LP turbines have equal pressure ratios (= √rₚ = 3), and therefore: $$T_4 = T_6 = 1023.0 \text{ K}$$ The net work has been calculated above as **W_net = 494.5 kJ/kg**, which is the **maximum** for this configuration. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Proof Summary (for board exam):** To show P₄,opt = √(P₁·P₂), take the derivative of W_net with respect to P₄, set to zero, and solve. The two terms involving T₄ (HP exit) and T₆ (LP exit) become equal in magnitude, leading to the geometric mean condition. --- ## KTU EXAMINER'S PITFALL CALLOUT > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks in KTU Board Exams:** > > 1. **Forgetting to check the regeneration feasibility condition (T₄ > T₂).** If a problem states a high pressure ratio, students often blindly compute efficiencies without verifying that regeneration is even possible. Always check first. > > 2. **Mixing up T₂' and T₂ in the heat-supplied formula.** The heat supplied Q₁ is cₚ(T₃ − T₂'), **not** cₚ(T₃ − T₂). The regenerator raises the compressed air temperature to T₂' *before* it enters the combustor. > > 3. **Forgetting to add Q_R separately in the reheat cycle.** The total heat input is Q₁ + Q_R, not just Q₁. Many students calculate η = W_net / Q₁ and get a *fictional* efficiency above 100% — a dead giveaway of the error. > > 4. **Wrong intermediate pressure identification.** In reheat cycle problems, P₄ is the pressure *between* the two turbines, not the compressor discharge. Mislabeling P₄ = P₂ loses 2–3 marks easily. > > 5. **Units inconsistency.** Pressure ratios are dimensionless; pressures in bar; temperatures in Kelvin; works in kJ/kg. Mixing units (e.g., using °C in isentropic relations) is a 1-mark penalty. > > 6. **Skipping the T-s diagram.** KTU examiners allocate **2 marks** specifically for a *correct, labeled* T-s diagram in any 14-mark cycle question. A diagram with no labels (no T₁, T₂, etc.) gets 0 marks. --- ## TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST > [!NOTE] > **The High-Density Summary You Should Memorize Before the Exam:** - ✅ **Heat Exchange Cycle** = Basic Brayton + a counter-flow heat exchanger (regenerator) that uses turbine exhaust to preheat compressor discharge air. - ✅ **Key feasibility condition:** Regeneration works **only** if **T₄ > T₂** (turbine exhaust hotter than compressor outlet). - ✅ **Regenerator effectiveness (ε)** = (T₂' − T₂) / (T₄ − T₂). Typical value: 0.70 to 0.85. - ✅ **Heat supplied (with regenerator):** Q₁ = cₚ(T₃ − T₂'). **Heat supplied (without):** Q₁ = cₚ(T₃ − T₂). - ✅ **Thermal efficiency (regen):** η = [(T₃ − T₄) − (T₂ − T₁)] / (T₃ − T₂'). - ✅ **Isentropic relations:** T₂/T₁ = rₚ^((γ−1)/γ) and T₄/T₃ = 1/rₚ^((γ−1)/γ). - ✅ **Reheat Cycle** = Basic Brayton + expansion split into HP and LP turbines with intermediate reheating back to TIT. - ✅ **Reheat temperature** = TIT (the gas is reheated to the *same* maximum temperature as the main combustor exit). - ✅ **Total heat input (reheat):** Q_in = cₚ(T₃ − T₂) + cₚ(T₅ − T₄). - ✅ **Net work (reheat):** W_net = W_T,HP + W_T,LP − W_C. - ✅ **Optimal intermediate pressure:** P₄,opt = √(P₁ · P₂) → for *maximum* work output (not maximum efficiency). - ✅ **Combined cycle benefit:** Heat exchange + reheat together raise efficiency from ~28% (simple) to 40–45% (industrial). - ✅ **Mean effective pressure** is higher in reheat cycle → smaller engine displacement for the same work. - ✅ **KTU Mark Allocation Trend:** Heat Exchange & Reheat cycles together account for ~12–14 marks (one full 14-mark ESE question) in every KTU PEMET741 exam since 2020. - ✅ **Always draw the T-s diagram** — KTU awards 2 marks for a properly labeled diagram, even if the numerical answers are partially wrong. - ✅ **Standard constants to memorize:** cₚ = 1.005 kJ/kg·K, γ = 1.4, (γ−1)/γ = 0.2857, R = 0.287 kJ/kg·K. - ✅ **Common pressure ratios in textbook problems:** rₚ = 4, 6, 7, 8, 9 — practice each one to memorize 7^0.2857, 8^0.2857, 9^0.2857 values. > **End of Module 1 — Heat Exchange Cycle and Reheat Cycle Notes | KTU 2024 Scheme | PEMET741**

topics.title: Intercooled Cycle
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMET741 - GAS TURBINE AND JET PROPULSION > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Intercooled Cycle # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Aligned) The **Intercooled Cycle** is a modified gas turbine cycle in which the working fluid (air) is compressed in **multiple stages with intermediate cooling** (called *intercooling*) between successive compressor stages, with the aim of reducing the **net compressive work** required. In a two-stage intercooled cycle, the air is cooled back to its initial compressor inlet temperature (T₁) before entering the second compression stage. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Module 1):** > An intercooled gas turbine cycle is a regenerative modification where the compression process is split into two or more stages, and the compressed air between stages is cooled to a temperature lower than the discharge temperature of the previous stage, thereby reducing the specific volume of air entering the next stage and hence reducing compressor work. ## Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are climbing a steep staircase carrying a heavy backpack. It is exhausting if you try to climb all the stairs in one go. However, if you are allowed to **stop at a landing after a few stairs, rest, and put the backpack down briefly** before continuing, you can climb more easily and use less total energy. **Intercooling in a gas turbine is exactly this idea:** - Instead of compressing air from low pressure to high pressure in **one big step** (single-stage compression), we compress it in **smaller steps**. - Between each step, we **cool the air** using an external cooler (intercooler), so the next stage doesn't have to work as hard. - The total work of compression is **less** than if we had compressed in a single shot. ## Why Intercool? (The "Why" Behind the Concept) > [!NOTE] > **Core Physical Insight:** > Compression work = ∫ v·dP. By cooling air between stages, its **specific volume (v)** decreases. So for the same pressure rise, the area under the v–P curve (which is the work done) is **smaller**. **Engineering Benefits:** - **Reduced compressor work** → increased net work output - **Increased mass flow handling** capability - **Reduced discharge temperature** from the compressor → safer operation - The heat rejected in the intercooler represents a **loss** of heat input, so there's a trade-off with cycle efficiency. ## Standard Metrics and Constants - **γ (gamma) for air** = **1.4** (ratio of specific heats, Cₚ/Cᵥ) - **Cₚ for air** = **1.005 kJ/kg·K** - **R for air** = **0.287 kJ/kg·K** - **η_c (compressor isentropic efficiency)** typically **0.80 – 0.90** - **η_t (turbine isentropic efficiency)** typically **0.85 – 0.90** - **T₁ (ambient/compressor inlet temperature)** ≈ **300 K (27°C)** - **T₃ (turbine inlet temperature, TIT)** typically **1200 K – 1500 K** ## Schematic Block View of the Intercooled Cycle The cycle consists of the following sequence of components in a **closed-loop airflow path**: 1. **Compressor Stage 1** → raises pressure from P₁ to √r_p 2. **Intercooler** → cools air back to T₁ at intermediate pressure 3. **Compressor Stage 2** → raises pressure from √r_p to P₂ = r_p · P₁ 4. **Combustion Chamber (Combustor)** → adds heat at constant pressure 5. **Turbine** → expands hot gases back to atmospheric pressure 6. **Exhaust** → gases released to atmosphere (open cycle) # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Thermodynamic Process Breakdown (Two-Stage Intercooled Cycle) The complete cycle consists of **two P–V and one T–S paths**. Let us analyze each process rigorously: ### **Process 1–2: First Stage of Compression** - Pressure rises from **P₁** to **P_x = √(r_p · P₁)** = √(r_p) · P₁ - For an isentropic process: T₂s / T₁ = (P_x/P₁)^((γ-1)/γ) = (r_p)^((γ-1)/(2γ)) - Actual temperature after stage 1: $$T_{2} = T_1 \cdot \frac{\left[r_p\right]^{\frac{\gamma-1}{2\gamma}} - 1}{\eta_c} + T_1$$ - **Compressed work (per stage, per kg of air):** $$W_{C1} = \frac{C_p \cdot T_1}{\eta_c} \cdot \left[\left(r_p\right)^{\frac{\gamma-1}{2\gamma}} - 1\right]$$ ### **Process 2–3: Intercooling (Process 2a → 3 in T–S diagram)** - Heat is rejected at **constant intermediate pressure** (P_x) - Air is cooled from T₂ back to **T₁** (ideal/perfect intercooling case) - **Heat rejected in intercooler per kg of air:** $$Q_{intercooler} = C_p \cdot (T_2 - T_1)$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Perfect Intercooling Assumption:** In KTU problems, intercooling is always assumed to be **perfect**, meaning the air is cooled back to the **initial compressor inlet temperature T₁**. This is the KTU standard convention. ### **Process 3–4: Second Stage of Compression** - Pressure rises from **P_x = √r_p · P₁** to **P₂ = r_p · P₁** - For isentropic process: T₄s / T₁ = (P₂/P_x)^((γ-1)/γ) = (r_p)^((γ-1)/(2γ)) - Actual temperature after stage 2: $$T_4 = T_1 \cdot \left[\frac{\left(r_p\right)^{\frac{\gamma-1}{2\gamma}} - 1}{\eta_c} + 1\right]$$ - **Compressed work in stage 2:** $$W_{C2} = \frac{C_p \cdot T_1}{\eta_c} \cdot \left[\left(r_p\right)^{\frac{\gamma-1}{2\gamma}} - 1\right]$$ > [!NOTE] > Since intercooling returns air to T₁, **W_C1 = W_C2** in a two-stage intercooled cycle with perfect intercooling. The total compressor work is simply **twice** the work of one stage. ### **Process 4–5: Combustion (Heat Addition)** - Constant pressure process from P₂ to P₂ - Heat added: Q_in = C_p · (T₅ – T₄) = C_p · (T₃ – T₁) *(since T₄ = T₁ after intercooling)* ### **Process 5–6: Expansion in Turbine** - Pressure drops from **P₂** to **P₁** (full pressure ratio r_p) - For isentropic process: T₆s / T₅ = (P₁/P₂)^((γ-1)/γ) = (1/r_p)^((γ-1)/γ) - **Turbine work output:** $$W_T = C_p \cdot T_3 \cdot \eta_t \cdot \left[1 - \left(\frac{1}{r_p}\right)^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}}\right]$$ *(where T₃ is the turbine inlet temperature TIT)* ### **Process 6–1: Exhaust (Heat Rejection)** - Constant pressure heat rejection to atmosphere. ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **HIGH-YIELD FORMULAS – MEMORIZE THESE FOR KTU EXAM:** | **#** | **Parameter** | **Formula** | **Units** | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Optimum intermediate pressure (P_x) | $P_x = \sqrt{P_1 \cdot P_2}$ | bar or kPa | | 2 | Stage pressure ratio | $r_{stage} = \sqrt{r_p}$ | dimensionless | | 3 | Compressor work (1 stage) | $W_C = \frac{C_p T_1}{\eta_c} \left[ (r_p)^{\frac{\gamma-1}{2\gamma}} - 1 \right]$ | kJ/kg | | 4 | Total compressor work (2 stages with intercooling) | $W_{C,total} = 2 \cdot W_C$ | kJ/kg | | 5 | Turbine work | $W_T = C_p T_3 \eta_t \left[ 1 - \left(\frac{1}{r_p}\right)^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} \right]$ | kJ/kg | | 6 | Net work output | $W_{net} = W_T - W_{C,total}$ | kJ/kg | | 7 | Heat supplied (combustor) | $Q_{in} = C_p (T_3 - T_1)$ | kJ/kg | | 8 | Heat rejected (intercooler) | $Q_{IC} = C_p (T_2 - T_1)$ | kJ/kg | | 9 | Thermal efficiency | $\eta_{th} = \frac{W_{net}}{Q_{in}}$ | dimensionless | | 10 | Optimum pressure ratio (for max W_net) | $r_p^{opt} = \left(\frac{T_3 \eta_c \eta_t}{T_1}\right)^{\frac{2\gamma}{3(\gamma-1)}}$ | dimensionless | ## 2.3 Comparison: Simple Cycle vs. Intercooled Cycle | **Parameter** | **Simple Brayton Cycle** | **Intercooled Cycle (2-stage)** | |---|---|---| | Compression work | $W_C = \frac{C_p T_1}{\eta_c} \left[ (r_p)^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} - 1 \right]$ | $W_C = \frac{2 C_p T_1}{\eta_c} \left[ (r_p)^{\frac{\gamma-1}{2\gamma}} - 1 \right]$ | | Heat input | $Q_{in} = C_p(T_3 - T_2)$ (T₂ > T₁) | $Q_{in} = C_p(T_3 - T_1)$ (lower, since T₄ = T₁) | | Net work | Higher compressor work, less net | Lower compressor work, more net | | Optimum r_p | $r_p = \left(\frac{T_3 \eta_c}{T_1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma}{2(\gamma-1)}}$ | $r_p = \left(\frac{T_3 \eta_c \eta_t}{T_1}\right)^{\frac{2\gamma}{3(\gamma-1)}}$ | ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications > [!NOTE] > **Where Intercooled Gas Turbines are Used in Industry:** > - **Heavy-duty power generation gas turbines** (e.g., GE Frame 7, Siemens V94.3) where efficiency is paramount. > - **Marine propulsion** gas turbines (LM2500, MT30) – compact, high power density. > - **Aero-derivative industrial turbines** for peaking power plants. > - **Mechanical drive applications** in oil & gas (pipeline compressors). > - **Locomotive engines** (e.g., GTEL of Union Pacific). > - **Integrated Gasification Combined Cycle (IGCC)** power plants. The **trade-off** in real life: intercoolers add **weight, volume, and pressure drop losses**, so they are mostly used in stationary and marine applications, not in aviation jet engines. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Compressor Work with Perfect Intercooling **Starting Point:** Air enters compressor at T₁, P₁. After two-stage compression with intercooling back to T₁, the final pressure is P₂. **Step 1:** Define overall pressure ratio $$r_p = \frac{P_2}{P_1}$$ **Step 2:** For minimum compression work, intermediate pressure P_x is the **geometric mean** of P₁ and P₂ (this minimizes the sum of two-stage compression work, proven by calculus). $$P_x = \sqrt{P_1 \cdot P_2} = P_1 \cdot \sqrt{r_p}$$ **Step 3:** Pressure ratio across each stage is equal to √r_p (so that work is shared equally between stages). $$r_{stage} = \frac{P_x}{P_1} = \frac{P_2}{P_x} = \sqrt{r_p}$$ **Step 4:** For isentropic compression in one stage, exit temperature is $$T_{2s} = T_1 \cdot \left(\frac{P_x}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} = T_1 \cdot \left(\sqrt{r_p}\right)^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} = T_1 \cdot \left(r_p\right)^{\frac{\gamma-1}{2\gamma}}$$ **Step 5:** Account for compressor isentropic efficiency η_c. The actual work per kg is $$W_C = C_p \cdot (T_{2a} - T_1) = \frac{C_p (T_{2s} - T_1)}{\eta_c}$$ **Step 6:** Substituting T_{2s}: $$W_C = \frac{C_p T_1}{\eta_c} \cdot \left[\left(r_p\right)^{\frac{\gamma-1}{2\gamma}} - 1\right]$$ **Step 7:** Total compressor work for two stages (since intercooling returns to T₁, the second stage has identical work): $$W_{C,total} = 2 \cdot W_C = \frac{2 C_p T_1}{\eta_c} \cdot \left[\left(r_p\right)^{\frac{\gamma-1}{2\gamma}} - 1\right]$$ ## 3.2 Derivation: Optimum Pressure Ratio for Maximum Net Work **Step 1:** Write net work expression. $$W_{net} = W_T - W_{C,total}$$ Substitute the formulas: $$W_{net} = C_p T_3 \eta_t \left[1 - \left(\frac{1}{r_p}\right)^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}}\right] - \frac{2 C_p T_1}{\eta_c} \left[\left(r_p\right)^{\frac{\gamma-1}{2\gamma}} - 1\right]$$ **Step 2:** Substitute $x = r_p^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}}$ to simplify. $$W_{net} = C_p T_3 \eta_t \left[1 - \frac{1}{x}\right] - \frac{2 C_p T_1}{\eta_c} \left[\sqrt{x} - 1\right]$$ **Step 3:** Differentiate W_net with respect to x and set to zero for maximum. $$\frac{dW_{net}}{dx} = C_p T_3 \eta_t \cdot \frac{1}{x^2} - \frac{2 C_p T_1}{\eta_c} \cdot \frac{1}{2\sqrt{x}} = 0$$ **Step 4:** Simplify the equation. $$C_p T_3 \eta_t \cdot \frac{1}{x^2} = \frac{C_p T_1}{\eta_c \sqrt{x}}$$ $$\frac{T_3 \eta_t}{x^2} = \frac{T_1}{\eta_c \sqrt{x}}$$ **Step 5:** Cross-multiply. $$T_3 \eta_t \eta_c \sqrt{x} = T_1 \cdot x^2$$ $$x^{3/2} = \frac{T_3 \eta_c \eta_t}{T_1}$$ **Step 6:** Solve for x. $$x = \left(\frac{T_3 \eta_c \eta_t}{T_1}\right)^{2/3}$$ **Step 7:** Substitute back $x = r_p^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}}$: $$r_p^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} = \left(\frac{T_3 \eta_c \eta_t}{T_1}\right)^{2/3}$$ **Step 8:** Raise both sides to the power $\frac{\gamma}{\gamma-1}$: $$\boxed{r_p^{opt} = \left(\frac{T_3 \eta_c \eta_t}{T_1}\right)^{\frac{2\gamma}{3(\gamma-1)}}}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > This is the **optimum pressure ratio** that maximizes net work output for a two-stage intercooled gas turbine cycle. The exponent $\frac{2\gamma}{3(\gamma-1)}$ is **diagnostic** for an intercooled cycle question on KTU. ## 3.3 Numerical Example (KTU-Style Problem) **Problem Statement:** A gas turbine plant operates on an intercooled cycle. Air enters the compressor at T₁ = 300 K, P₁ = 1 bar. The pressure ratio is r_p = 9. Maximum temperature in the cycle T₃ = 1200 K. Compressor efficiency η_c = 0.85, turbine efficiency η_t = 0.90. γ = 1.4, Cₚ = 1.005 kJ/kg·K. Determine: **(a)** Optimum pressure ratio, **(b)** Net work output per kg of air, **(c)** Thermal efficiency. ### **Solution:** **Part (a): Optimum Pressure Ratio** Apply the derived formula: $$r_p^{opt} = \left(\frac{T_3 \eta_c \eta_t}{T_1}\right)^{\frac{2\gamma}{3(\gamma-1)}}$$ Calculate the exponent: $$\frac{2\gamma}{3(\gamma-1)} = \frac{2(1.4)}{3(0.4)} = \frac{2.8}{1.2} = 2.333$$ Calculate the temperature ratio: $$\frac{T_3 \eta_c \eta_t}{T_1} = \frac{1200 \times 0.85 \times 0.90}{300} = \frac{918}{300} = 3.06$$ Now compute r_p^opt: $$r_p^{opt} = (3.06)^{2.333} = 12.86$$ So the optimum pressure ratio is **≈ 12.86**. **Part (b): Net Work Output** Since the given r_p = 9, we use this value (not the optimum) to find the actual net work. Step 1: Compute the exponent for temperature relation. $$\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma} = \frac{0.4}{1.4} = 0.2857$$ Step 2: Compute single-stage temperature ratio. $$\left(r_p\right)^{\frac{\gamma-1}{2\gamma}} = (9)^{0.1429} = 1.366$$ Step 3: Compressor work per stage: $$W_C = \frac{C_p T_1}{\eta_c} \left[ (r_p)^{\frac{\gamma-1}{2\gamma}} - 1 \right] = \frac{1.005 \times 300}{0.85} \times (1.366 - 1)$$ $$W_C = \frac{301.5}{0.85} \times 0.366 = 354.71 \times 0.366 = 129.82 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ Step 4: Total compressor work for two stages: $$W_{C,total} = 2 \times 129.82 = 259.64 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ Step 5: Turbine work: $$W_T = C_p T_3 \eta_t \left[ 1 - \left(\frac{1}{r_p}\right)^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} \right] = 1.005 \times 1200 \times 0.90 \times \left[ 1 - (1/9)^{0.2857} \right]$$ Compute (1/9)^0.2857: $$(1/9)^{0.2857} = 0.732$$ So: $$W_T = 1.005 \times 1200 \times 0.90 \times (1 - 0.732) = 1085.4 \times 0.268 = 290.89 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ Step 6: Net work output: $$W_{net} = W_T - W_{C,total} = 290.89 - 259.64 = 31.25 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ **Part (c): Thermal Efficiency** Heat input per kg of air (with perfect intercooling, T₄ = T₁ = 300 K): $$Q_{in} = C_p (T_3 - T_1) = 1.005 \times (1200 - 300) = 1.005 \times 900 = 904.5 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ Thermal efficiency: $$\eta_{th} = \frac{W_{net}}{Q_{in}} = \frac{31.25}{904.5} = 0.0346 = 3.46\%$$ > [!NOTE] > **Important Observation:** The thermal efficiency appears low because the given pressure ratio (r_p = 9) is **less than the optimum (12.86)**. At lower pressure ratios, turbine work drops faster than compressor work, reducing net output. For a more realistic KTU exam, the given r_p is often **close to the optimum**. ## 3.4 Summary of Key Derivations | **Derivation** | **Final Result** | **Application** | |---|---|---| | Compressor work (2-stage intercooled) | $W_C = \frac{2 C_p T_1}{\eta_c} \left[ (r_p)^{\frac{\gamma-1}{2\gamma}} - 1 \right]$ | Energy balance | | Turbine work | $W_T = C_p T_3 \eta_t \left[ 1 - r_p^{-\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} \right]$ | Power output | | Optimum r_p | $r_p^{opt} = \left(\frac{T_3 \eta_c \eta_t}{T_1}\right)^{\frac{2\gamma}{3(\gamma-1)}}$ | Design optimization | | Heat input | $Q_{in} = C_p(T_3 - T_1)$ | Efficiency calc | | Cycle efficiency | $\eta_{th} = \frac{W_T - W_C}{Q_{in}}$ | Performance metric | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow The following Mermaid diagram maps the **functional architecture** of an intercooled gas turbine plant, showing the air-flow path, energy transfers, and state points. ```mermaid flowchart LR A1[AMBIENT AIR
State 1: T1, P1] --> B1[COMPRESSOR
Stage 1] B1 --> C1[STATE 2a
Hot Compressed Air
T2a, Px] C1 --> D1[INTERCOOLER
Heat Rejection QIC] D1 --> E1[STATE 3
Cooled Air
T1, Px] E1 --> F1[COMPRESSOR
Stage 2] F1 --> G1[STATE 4
High Pressure Air
T4, P2] G1 --> H1[COMBUSTION
CHAMBER] H1 --> I1[FUEL INJECTION
QFuel] I1 --> J1[STATE 5
Hot Gases
T3, P2] J1 --> K1[TURBINE
Expansion] K1 --> L1[STATE 6
Exhaust Gases
T6, P1] L1 --> M1[EXHAUST TO
ATMOSPHERE] K1 --> N1[NET POWER
OUTPUT Wnet] B1 --> N1 F1 --> N1 H1 --> O1[Qin = Cp T3 - T1] D1 --> P1[QIC = Cp T2a - T1] ``` ## 4.2 Detailed P–V and T–S Diagram Representation The thermodynamic cycle is best represented on a **Pressure–Volume (P–V) diagram** and a **Temperature–Entropy (T–S) diagram**. The following block diagram shows the **state transitions** and the processes connecting them: ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph PV["P-V DIAGRAM (Process Path)"] direction TB P1A["State 1
P1, V1"] -->|1 to 2a Isentropic Compression Stage 1| P2A["State 2a
Px, V2a"] P2A -->|2a to 3 Constant P Cooling in Intercooler| P3A["State 3
Px, V3 V3 greater than V2a"] P3A -->|3 to 4 Isentropic Compression Stage 2| P4A["State 4
P2, V4"] P4A -->|4 to 5 Constant P Heat Addition in Combustor| P5A["State 5
P2, V5"] P5A -->|5 to 6 Isentropic Expansion in Turbine| P6A["State 6
P1, V6"] end subgraph TS["T-S DIAGRAM (Temperature Entropy Path)"] direction TB T1A["State 1
T1"] -->|1 to 2a Vertical Line Isentropic| T2A["State 2a
T2a higher than T1"] T2A -->|2a to 3 Horizontal Line Cooling| T3A["State 3
T1 (back to inlet)"] T3A -->|3 to 4 Vertical Line Isentropic| T4A["State 4
T4 equals T2a"] T4A -->|4 to 5 Curve Heat Addition| T5A["State 5
T3 maximum"] T5A -->|5 to 6 Vertical Line Isentropic| T6A["State 6
