Missing units = 0.5 to 1 mark deduction. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Pre-Exam Revision — Top 12 Must-Know Points for "Pressure Losses"** - [x] **Pressure losses are defined using stagnation (total) pressure**, never static pressure. - [x] **Loss coefficient:** $\xi_p = (P_{t1} - P_{t2})/P_{t1}$ — dimensionless, always positive. - [x] **Pressure recovery factor:** $R_p = P_{t2}/P_{t1} = 1 - \xi_p$ — measures how well a component preserves pressure. - [x] **Entropy generation:** $\Delta s_{gen} = R \ln(P_{t1}/P_{t2}) \geq 0$ — direct consequence of pressure loss. - [x] **Stagnation temperature is conserved** across an adiabatic pressure-loss process (no work, no heat transfer). - [x] **Equivalent enthalpy loss:** $\Delta h_{loss} = c_p T_{t1} \left[ 1 - (P_{t2}/P_{t1})^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} \right]$. - [x] **Typical combustor loss:** 3% to 5% (largest single loss in a GT cycle). - [x] **Typical diffuser loss:** 1% to 3% (external/inlet). - [x] **Typical nozzle/turbine loss:** 2% to 4% per row. - [x] **Net work penalty:** A pressure loss of $\xi_p$ after the compressor reduces net work by approximately $\xi_p \times (\gamma-1)/\gamma \times (W_T/W_{net})$. - [x] **Major physical causes:** viscous friction, flow separation, shock waves, heat addition, mixing of different-temperature streams. - [x] **Stagnation pressure cannot be recovered by a downstream turbine** — it is permanently lost to entropy. > **End of Module 1 — Topic: Pressure Losses | PEMET741 | KTU 2024 Scheme**

topics.title: Cycle Efficiency
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMET741 - GAS TURBINE AND JET PROPULSION > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Cycle Efficiency ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Cycle Efficiency (η_cycle)**, specifically the **Air-Standard Thermal Efficiency** of a Gas Turbine, is defined as the ratio of the **net work output** of the cycle to the **heat supplied** (heat input) in the cycle. For a gas turbine operating on the **Brayton Cycle**, it represents how effectively the chemical energy of the fuel is converted into useful mechanical work across the compression, combustion, and expansion processes. Mathematically, it is expressed as: $$\eta_{cycle} = \frac{\text{Net Work Output}}{\text{Heat Supplied}} = \frac{W_{net}}{Q_{in}}$$ In an ideal Brayton cycle, this reduces to a function of the **pressure ratio (r_p)** and the **specific heat ratio (γ)** of the working fluid (air). > [!NOTE] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU PEMET741 - Module 1):** > The Brayton cycle forms the foundation of all gas turbine analysis. Students are expected to derive the thermal efficiency expression, plot the **η vs Pressure Ratio** characteristic curve, and identify the **Optimum Pressure Ratio** for maximum work and maximum efficiency. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Think of a gas turbine cycle like a **bicycle pump attached to a balloon inside a sealed box**: - **Compression (Pump):** You push down hard on the pump (compressor), squeezing air into a smaller space. This requires *work input* and raises the air's temperature. - **Heat Addition (Flame):** You pass the compressed, hot air over a burner (combustion chamber), adding a lot of energy. The air temperature spikes even higher, like heating the balloon until it almost bursts. - **Expansion (Balloon Releasing):** The super-hot, high-pressure air rushes out through a turbine blade, spinning it powerfully. This is *work output*. - **Heat Rejection:** The leftover hot air is exhausted to the atmosphere (or cooled, in a closed cycle). **Cycle Efficiency** is simply the answer to the question: *"Out of all the heat energy we poured into the burner, how much came back to us as spinning shaft work?"* Any heat that just blows out the exhaust pipe without spinning the turbine is *wasted*. A high-efficiency cycle captures more of that heat as work. ### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics - **Specific Heat Ratio (γ) for Air:** **1.4** (at room temperature) - **Specific Heat at Constant Pressure (C_p) for Air:** **1.005 kJ/kg·K** - **Specific Heat at Constant Volume (C_v) for Air:** **0.718 kJ/kg·K** - **Gas Constant (R) for Air:** **0.287 kJ/kg·K** - **Standard Atmospheric Pressure:** **101.325 kPa** - **Typical Pressure Ratio (r_p) range for gas turbines:** **3:1 to 30:1** > [!TIP] > For the KTU exam, the value **γ = 1.4** for air is a *non-negotiable constant* in most numerical problems unless explicitly stated otherwise. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Brayton Cycle — The Backbone of Gas Turbine Analysis The **Brayton Cycle** is the ideal air-standard cycle that models the thermodynamic operation of a gas turbine. It consists of **four reversible processes**: 1. **Process 1→2: Isentropic Compression** - Ambient air is drawn into the compressor and compressed to a higher pressure. - Work is *consumed* (compressor work, W_C). - Process: **Isentropic** (Reversible Adiabatic, $dS = 0$). 2. **Process 2→3: Constant Pressure Heat Addition** - High-pressure air enters the combustion chamber, where fuel is burned at (approximately) **constant pressure**. - Heat is *added* to the cycle (Q_in). - Process: **Isobaric** ($P = \text{constant}$). 3. **Process 3→4: Isentropic Expansion** - The high-temperature, high-pressure gases expand through the turbine, producing work. - Work is *produced* (turbine work, W_T). - Process: **Isentropic**. 4. **Process 4→1: Constant Pressure Heat Rejection** - The exhaust gases release heat to the surroundings at constant pressure (in the ideal cycle). - Heat is *rejected* (Q_out). - Process: **Isobaric**. ### 2.2 The "Why" and "How" of Each Step - **Why Isentropic Compression/Expansion?** In an *ideal* (frictionless, perfectly insulated) cycle, the compression and expansion processes are modeled as isentropic to represent the *best possible* thermodynamic performance. Real-world deviations are accounted for using **Isentropic Efficiency** of the compressor and turbine (covered in later modules). - **Why Constant Pressure Heat Addition?** Gas turbines operate with a **continuous flow**, open-to-atmosphere design. The combustion chamber is designed to maintain steady pressure so that the air-fuel mixture burns stably and flows smoothly into the turbine. - **How Efficiency Depends on Pressure Ratio:** Higher pressure ratios mean the compressor squeezes the air into a smaller volume, increasing its temperature more dramatically. The turbine then has a *larger temperature drop* (and therefore more work) to work with, but the compressor also *consumes* more work. There is an **optimum pressure ratio** for maximizing net work. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Parameter | Formula | Conditions | Units | |---|-----------|---------|------------|-------| | 1 | **Thermal Efficiency (η_th)** | $\eta_{th} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{(r_p)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}}$ | Ideal Brayton Cycle | Dimensionless | | 2 | **Pressure Ratio** | $r_p = \dfrac{P_2}{P_1} = \dfrac{P_3}{P_4}$ | Isobaric Heat Processes | Dimensionless | | 3 | **Compressor Work (W_C)** | $W_C = C_p (T_2 - T_1)$ | Isentropic Compression | kJ/kg | | 4 | **Turbine Work (W_T)** | $W_T = C_p (T_3 - T_4)$ | Isentropic Expansion | kJ/kg | | 5 | **Net Work Output (W_net)** | $W_{net} = W_T - W_C = C_p [(T_3 - T_4) - (T_2 - T_1)]$ | Brayton Cycle | kJ/kg | | 6 | **Heat Supplied (Q_in)** | $Q_{in} = C_p (T_3 - T_2)$ | Isobaric Heat Addition | kJ/kg | | 7 | **Heat Rejected (Q_out)** | $Q_{out} = C_p (T_4 - T_1)$ | Isobaric Heat Rejection | kJ/kg | | 8 | **Work Ratio (WR)** | $WR = \dfrac{W_{net}}{W_T} = 1 - \dfrac{W_C}{W_T}$ | Cycle Performance Metric | Dimensionless | | 9 | **Optimum Pressure Ratio (Max Work)** | $r_{p,opt} = \left( \dfrac{\gamma \cdot T_3}{\gamma \cdot T_1} \right)^{\gamma / [2(\gamma-1)]}$ | When $T_2 = T_4$ | Dimensionless | | 10 | **Temperature Relation (Comp.)** | $T_2 = T_1 (r_p)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$ | Isentropic Process | K | | 11 | **Temperature Relation (Turb.)** | $T_4 = T_3 / (r_p)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$ | Isentropic Process | K | | 12 | **Specific Heat Ratio** | $\gamma = C_p / C_v$ | Air ≈ **1.4** | Dimensionless | > [!CAUTION] > When writing $|x|$ or absolute value expressions in tables, always use `\vert` (e.g., $T_2 = \vert T_1 \cdot r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} \vert$) to avoid breaking the markdown table structure. ### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering Cycle efficiency analysis is **the most critical performance metric** in: - **Aircraft Jet Engines** (Turbojet, Turbofan) — Higher efficiency = longer flight range. - **Industrial Power Generation** (Simple & Combined Cycle Gas Turbine plants) — Higher efficiency = lower fuel costs. - **Marine Propulsion** (COGAS, CODOG systems) — Higher efficiency = greater cruising range. - **Land-Based Gas Turbine Power Plants** — Direct drive for compressors, pumps, and generators. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of Thermal Efficiency of the Ideal Brayton Cycle **Step 1: Define the First Law for a Steady-Flow Cycle** For a steady-flow control volume (gas turbine), the energy balance gives: $$Q_{net} = W_{net}$$ **Step 2: Express Net Work Output** Net work is the difference between turbine work and compressor work: $$W_{net} = W_T - W_C$$ Substituting the constant-pressure heat relations: $$W_{net} = C_p (T_3 - T_4) - C_p (T_2 - T_1)$$ $$W_{net} = C_p [(T_3 - T_2) - (T_4 - T_1)]$$ **Step 3: Express Heat Supplied** Heat is added only in the constant-pressure combustion process (2→3): $$Q_{in} = C_p (T_3 - T_2)$$ **Step 4: Apply the Definition of Thermal Efficiency** $$\eta_{th} = \frac{W_{net}}{Q_{in}}$$ $$\eta_{th} = \frac{C_p [(T_3 - T_2) - (T_4 - T_1)]}{C_p (T_3 - T_2)}$$ The $C_p$ cancels out: $$\eta_{th} = \frac{(T_3 - T_2) - (T_4 - T_1)}{T_3 - T_2}$$ $$\eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{T_4 - T_1}{T_3 - T_2}$$ **Step 5: Introduce Isentropic Relations** For the isentropic compression (1→2): $$\frac{T_2}{T_1} = \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} = (r_p)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$$ For the isentropic expansion (3→4): $$\frac{T_3}{T_4} = \left(\frac{P_3}{P_4}\right)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} = (r_p)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$$ Therefore: $$T_2 = T_1 (r_p)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} \quad \text{and} \quad T_4 = \frac{T_3}{(r_p)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}}$$ **Step 6: Substitute Into the Efficiency Expression** $$\eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{\dfrac{T_3}{(r_p)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}} - T_1}{T_3 - T_1 (r_p)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}}$$ Factor out $T_1$ from numerator and denominator: $$\eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{T_1 \left[ \dfrac{T_3/T_1}{(r_p)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}} - 1 \right]}{T_1 \left[ \dfrac{T_3}{T_1} - (r_p)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} \right]}$$ The $T_1$ cancels: $$\eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{\dfrac{T_3/T_1}{(r_p)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}} - 1}{\dfrac{T_3}{T_1} - (r_p)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}}$$ **Step 7: Introduce the Temperature Ratio $τ = T_3/T_1$** Let $\tau = T_3 / T_1$ and $x = (r_p)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$ for compactness: $$\eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{\tau/x - 1}{\tau - x}$$ Multiply numerator and denominator by $x$: $$\eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{\tau - x}{x(\tau - x)}$$ $$\eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{1}{x}$$ **Step 8: Back-Substitute to Obtain the Famous Brayton Efficiency Formula** Since $x = (r_p)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$: $$\boxed{\eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{1}{(r_p)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}}}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > This is the **single most important equation** in Module 1 of PEMET741. Notice that the thermal efficiency of the ideal Brayton cycle depends **only on the pressure ratio** and **only on the specific heat ratio (γ)** — *not* on the absolute temperatures! This is a direct consequence of the air-standard assumption. ### 3.2 Derivation of Optimum Pressure Ratio for Maximum Net Work The net work per unit mass is: $$W_{net} = C_p (T_3 - T_4) - C_p (T_2 - T_1)$$ Using isentropic relations: $$W_{net} = C_p \left[ T_3 - \frac{T_3}{(r_p)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}} - T_1 (r_p)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} + T_1 \right]$$ $$W_{net} = C_p \left[ T_3 + T_1 - \frac{T_3}{(r_p)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}} - T_1 (r_p)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} \right]$$ To find the maximum, take the derivative with respect to $r_p$ and set it to zero: Let $y = (r_p)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$. Then $\dfrac{dy}{dr_p} = \dfrac{(\gamma-1)}{\gamma} \cdot \dfrac{y}{r_p}$. Differentiating $W_{net}$ with respect to $r_p$ and setting equal to zero: $$\frac{dW_{net}}{dr_p} = 0$$ After applying the chain rule and simplifying: $$-T_1 (r_p)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} \cdot \frac{(\gamma-1)}{\gamma \cdot r_p} \cdot \frac{1}{?} + T_3 \cdot \frac{1}{[r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}]^2} \cdot \frac{(\gamma-1)}{\gamma \cdot r_p} \cdot (r_p)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} = 0$$ The result, after canceling the common term, is: $$T_1 (r_p)^{2(\gamma-1)/\gamma} = T_3$$ Solving for $r_p$: $$\boxed{r_{p,opt} = \left( \frac{T_3}{T_1} \right)^{\gamma / [2(\gamma-1)]}}$$ > [!NOTE] > At this **optimum pressure ratio**, the **work ratio (WR)** equals **0.5** and the turbine exhaust temperature ($T_4$) equals the compressor exit temperature ($T_2$). This is a very high-yield exam point! ### 3.3 Numerical Worked Example **Problem:** A gas turbine operates on the Brayton cycle with a pressure ratio of 8. Air enters the compressor at 300 K and the turbine at 1300 K. Determine: (a) Thermal efficiency, (b) Net work output per kg of air, (c) Work ratio. Take $\gamma = 1.4$ and $C_p = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K. **Given:** - $r_p = 8$ - $T_1 = 300$ K - $T_3 = 1300$ K - $\gamma = 1.4$, $C_p = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K **Step 1: Calculate the exponent** $$(\gamma - 1)/\gamma = 0.4/1.4 = 0.2857$$ **Step 2: Calculate $T_2$ (Compressor Exit)** $$T_2 = T_1 (r_p)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} = 300 \times (8)^{0.2857}$$ $$8^{0.2857} = 1.8114$$ $$T_2 = 300 \times 1.8114 = 543.43 \text{ K}$$ **Step 3: Calculate $T_4$ (Turbine Exit)** $$T_4 = T_3 / (r_p)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} = 1300 / 1.8114$$ $$T_4 = 717.66 \text{ K}$$ **Step 4: Calculate Thermal Efficiency** $$\eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{1}{1.8114} = 1 - 0.5520 = 0.4480$$ $$\eta_{th} = 44.80\%$$ **Step 5: Calculate Compressor Work** $$W_C = C_p (T_2 - T_1) = 1.005 \times (543.43 - 300) = 1.005 \times 243.43$$ $$W_C = 244.65 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ **Step 6: Calculate Turbine Work** $$W_T = C_p (T_3 - T_4) = 1.005 \times (1300 - 717.66) = 1.005 \times 582.34$$ $$W_T = 585.25 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ **Step 7: Calculate Net Work Output** $$W_{net} = W_T - W_C = 585.25 - 244.65 = 340.60 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ **Step 8: Calculate Work Ratio** $$WR = \frac{W_{net}}{W_T} = \frac{340.60}{585.25} = 0.5820$$ > [!TIP] > The work ratio (0.582) being **less than 1** confirms net work is positive. For KTU exams, *always* state the work ratio when net work is computed — it shows full understanding of cycle performance. ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Effect of Pressure Ratio on Cycle Parameters | Pressure Ratio (r_p) | Thermal Efficiency (η_th) | Compressor Work | Turbine Work | Net Work | Work Ratio | |:--------------------:|:-------------------------:|:---------------:|:------------:|:--------:|:----------:| | 2 | 0.165 (16.5%) | Low | Low | Low | High | | 4 | 0.281 (28.1%) | Medium | Medium | Medium | Medium | | 8 | 0.448 (44.8%) | Higher | Higher | **Max** | Medium | | 12 | 0.530 (53.0%) | High | High | Decreasing | Lower | | 20 | 0.602 (60.2%) | Very High | High | Lower | Low | | 30 | 0.651 (65.1%) | Very High | Slightly Higher | Even Lower | Very Low | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Insight:** Efficiency **always increases** with pressure ratio, but **net work** first increases, reaches a maximum, and then *decreases* because the compressor work grows faster than the turbine work at high $r_p$. This is why **aircraft engines** use moderate pressure ratios (10–15) for thrust, while **power plant turbines** use higher pressure ratios (20–30) for efficiency. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 T-S Diagram of the Ideal Brayton Cycle ```mermaid flowchart TD P1["State 1: Compressor Inlet
Low P, Low T
P1 = 101.325 kPa, T1 = 300 K"] P2["State 2: Compressor Outlet
High P, Medium T
P2 = P1 x rp, T2 = T1 x rp to power 0.2857"] P3["State 3: Turbine Inlet
High P, Highest T
P3 = P2, T3 = 1300 K to 1500 K"] P4["State 4: Turbine Outlet
Low P, Medium-High T
P4 = P1, T4 = T3 / rp to power 0.2857"] C1["Process 1 to 2
Isentropic Compression
dS = 0"] C2["Process 2 to 3
Constant Pressure Heat Addition
P = constant, Q in"] C3["Process 3 to 4
Isentropic Expansion
dS = 0"] C4["Process 4 to 1
Constant Pressure Heat Rejection
P = constant, Q out"] P1 --> C1 --> P2 P2 --> C2 --> P3 P3 --> C3 --> P4 P4 --> C4 --> P1 ``` ### 4.2 P-V Diagram of the Ideal Brayton Cycle ```mermaid flowchart LR A1["State 1
V1 large, P1 low"] A2["State 2
V2 small, P2 high"] A3["State 3
V3 larger than V2, P3 high"] A4["State 4
V4 large, P4 low"] A1 -->|"Isentropic
Compression
1 to 2"| A2 A2 -->|"Isobaric
Heat Addition
2 to 3"| A3 A3 -->|"Isentropic
Expansion
3 to 4"| A4 A4 -->|"Isobaric
Heat Rejection
4 to 1"| A1 ``` ### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Gas Turbine (Open Cycle) ```mermaid flowchart LR IN["Atmospheric Air Inlet
T1, P1"] --> COMP["Compressor
W C consumed
Isentropic Compression"] COMP --> CC["Combustion Chamber
Fuel Injection
Constant Pressure Heat Addition"] CC --> TURB["Turbine
W T produced
Isentropic Expansion"] TURB --> OUT["Exhaust Gases to Atmosphere
Constant Pressure Heat Rejection"] TURB -.->|Shaft Power| LOAD["Output Shaft
Net Work Output
W net = W T minus W C"] CC -.->|Q in| HEATIN["Heat Input from Fuel"] OUT -.->|Q out| HEATOUT["Heat Rejected to Atmosphere"] ``` ### 4.4 Plot: Efficiency vs Pressure Ratio (Characteristic Curve) ```mermaid graph LR AX["Pressure Ratio rp on X axis"] --> CURVE["Thermal Efficiency increases logarithmically with rp"] CURVE --> A1["rp = 2, eta = 16.5%"] CURVE --> A2["rp = 4, eta = 28.1%"] CURVE --> A3["rp = 8, eta = 44.8%"] CURVE --> A4["rp = 16, eta = 57.5%"] CURVE --> A5["rp = 30, eta = 65.1%"] ``` > [!NOTE] > The thermal efficiency vs pressure ratio curve is **monotonically increasing but asymptotic** — efficiency rises rapidly at low $r_p$ and flattens out at high $r_p$. This is why engineers don't blindly increase $r_p$ — diminishing returns meet rising mechanical and material costs. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) #### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** **Define Air-Standard Brayton Cycle. Mention its four processes.** **Model Answer:** The **Air-Standard Brayton Cycle** is the ideal thermodynamic cycle used to model the operation of a gas turbine, assuming air as the working fluid with constant specific heats and ideal-gas behavior. Its four processes are: 1. **Process 1→2:** Isentropic (Reversible Adiabatic) Compression in the compressor. 2. **Process 2→3:** Constant Pressure (Isobaric) Heat Addition in the combustion chamber. 3. **Process 3→4:** Isentropic (Reversible Adiabatic) Expansion in the turbine. 4. **Process 4→1:** Constant Pressure (Isobaric) Heat Rejection to the atmosphere. > [!TIP] > For 3-mark definition questions, list the four processes *with their process names* and *the corresponding component*. Avoid writing more than 4–5 lines. #### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam - June 2023]** **What is meant by "Work Ratio" in a gas turbine cycle? Why is it always less than 1?** **Model Answer:** **Work Ratio (WR)** is defined as the ratio of net work output to the gross turbine work output: $$WR = \frac{W_{net}}{W_T} = \frac{W_T - W_C}{W_T} = 1 - \frac{W_C}{W_T}$$ It is **always less than 1** because the compressor always consumes a portion of the turbine's work output. Since $W_C > 0$, we have $W_{net} = W_T - W_C < W_T$, so the ratio is strictly less than unity. A higher work ratio indicates a more efficient utilization of turbine work for useful output. ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022 — Modified for 2024 Scheme]** **(a)** Derive the expression for the **thermal efficiency of an ideal Brayton cycle** in terms of pressure ratio and specific heat ratio. State clearly the assumptions made. **[7 Marks]** **(b)** A gas turbine cycle operates with a pressure ratio of 6 and inlet temperatures of 300 K and 1100 K to the compressor and turbine respectively. Calculate the **(i) thermal efficiency, (ii) net work output per kg/s of air, and (iii) work ratio**. Take $\gamma = 1.4$ and $C_p = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K. **[7 Marks]** --- **Model Solution for (a):** **Assumptions:** 1. Air is the working fluid and behaves as an ideal gas. 2. Specific heats ($C_p$, $C_v$) are constant throughout the cycle. 3. All processes are internally reversible; compression and expansion are isentropic. 4. Heat addition and rejection occur at constant pressure. **Derivation:** **Step 1:** Net work output is the difference between turbine and compressor work: $$W_{net} = W_T - W_C$$ **Step 2:** Substituting the steady-flow energy equations: $$W_{net} = C_p (T_3 - T_4) - C_p (T_2 - T_1) = C_p [(T_3 - T_2) - (T_4 - T_1)]$$ **Step 3:** Heat supplied in the constant-pressure combustion process: $$Q_{in} = C_p (T_3 - T_2)$$ **Step 4:** Defining thermal efficiency: $$\eta_{th} = \frac{W_{net}}{Q_{in}} = 1 - \frac{T_4 - T_1}{T_3 - T_2}$$ **Step 5:** Applying isentropic relations: $$T_2 = T_1 (r_p)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} \quad \text{and} \quad T_4 = \frac{T_3}{(r_p)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}}$$ **Step 6:** After substitution and simplification (as shown in Section 3.1): $$\boxed{\eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{1}{(r_p)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}}}$$ This shows that thermal efficiency depends **only** on the pressure ratio and the specific heat ratio. --- **Model Solution for (b):** **Given:** - $r_p = 6$, $T_1 = 300$ K, $T_3 = 1100$ K - $\gamma = 1.4$, $C_p = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K - $(\gamma-1)/\gamma = 0.4/1.4 = 0.2857$ **Step 1:** Compute $6^{0.2857}$: $$6^{0.2857} = 1.6685$$ **Step 2:** Compressor exit temperature: $$T_2 = 300 \times 1.6685 = 500.55 \text{ K}$$ **Step 3:** Turbine exit temperature: $$T_4 = 1100 / 1.6685 = 659.27 \text{ K}$$ **Step 4:** Thermal efficiency: $$\eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{1}{1.6685} = 1 - 0.5993 = 0.4007$$ $$\eta_{th} = 40.07\%$$ **Step 5:** Compressor work: $$W_C = 1.005 \times (500.55 - 300) = 1.005 \times 200.55 = 201.55 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ **Step 6:** Turbine work: $$W_T = 1.005 \times (1100 - 659.27) = 1.005 \times 440.73 = 442.93 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ **Step 7:** Net work output: $$W_{net} = 442.93 - 201.55 = 241.38 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ **Step 8:** Work ratio: $$WR = \frac{241.38}{442.93} = 0.5450$$ **Final Answers:** - **(i) Thermal Efficiency = 40.07%** - **(ii) Net Work Output = 241.38 kJ/kg** - **(iii) Work Ratio = 0.545** --- #### **Question B: [KTU University Exam - May 2024 — Sample Pattern]** **(a)** With the help of a T-S diagram, explain the **working of an ideal Brayton cycle**. List the four processes and state the temperature relations for the isentropic processes. **[7 Marks]** **(b)** A gas turbine operates between temperature limits of 300 K and 1200 K. Determine the **(i) optimum pressure ratio for maximum net work output, (ii) net work output at this pressure ratio, and (iii) thermal efficiency at this optimum pressure ratio**. Take $\gamma = 1.4$ and $C_p = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K. **[7 Marks]** --- **Model Solution for (a):** **Working of Ideal Brayton Cycle (with T-S diagram description):** On a **T-S (Temperature-Entropy) diagram**, the ideal Brayton cycle appears as a *curvilinear quadrilateral* with four distinct processes: - **Process 1→2 (Isentropic Compression):** A *vertical* line going **upward** (entropy remains constant) on the T-S diagram. Air temperature rises from $T_1$ to $T_2$ as pressure increases from $P_1$ to $P_2$. Work is consumed by the compressor. - **Process 2→3 (Constant Pressure Heat Addition):** An *exponentially rising curve* moving to the right (entropy increases) and upward. Heat $Q_{in}$ is added at constant pressure $P_2 = P_3$. Temperature rises from $T_2$ to $T_3$. - **Process 3→4 (Isentropic Expansion):** A *vertical* line going **downward** (entropy constant) on the T-S diagram. Temperature drops from $T_3$ to $T_4$ as pressure decreases from $P_3$ to $P_4$. Work is produced by the turbine. - **Process 4→1 (Constant Pressure Heat Rejection):** An *exponentially falling curve* moving to the left (entropy decreases) and downward. Heat $Q_{out}$ is rejected at constant pressure $P_4 = P_1$. Temperature drops from $T_4$ to $T_1$. **Temperature Relations for Isentropic Processes:** For the isentropic compression (1→2): $$\frac{T_2}{T_1} = \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$$ For the isentropic expansion (3→4): $$\frac{T_3}{T_4} = \left(\frac{P_3}{P_4}\right)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$$ --- **Model Solution for (b):** **Given:** - $T_1 = 300$ K, $T_3 = 1200$ K - $\gamma = 1.4$, $C_p = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K - $(\gamma-1)/\gamma = 0.2857$, $\gamma / [2(\gamma-1)] = 1.4/0.8 = 1.75$ **Step 1:** Optimum pressure ratio for maximum net work: $$r_{p,opt} = \left( \frac{T_3}{T_1} \right)^{\gamma / [2(\gamma-1)]} = \left( \frac{1200}{300} \right)^{1.75}$$ $$r_{p,opt} = (4)^{1.75} = 4^{1.75}$$ Computing $4^{1.75} = 4^1 \times 4^{0.75} = 4 \times 2.828 = 11.31$ $$\boxed{r_{p,opt} = 11.31}$$ **Step 2:** Net work output at this pressure ratio: At optimum $r_p$, $T_2 = T_4$. Using isentropic relations: $$T_2 = T_1 (r_p)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} = 300 \times (11.31)^{0.2857}$$ Computing $11.31^{0.2857} = 2.0$ (since $4^{1.75 \times 0.2857} = 4^{0.5} = 2$): $$T_2 = 300 \times 2 = 600 \text{ K}$$ At optimum, $T_4 = T_2 = 600$ K. **Step 3:** Net work output: $$W_{net} = C_p [(T_3 - T_4) - (T_2 - T_1)]$$ $$W_{net} = 1.005 \times [(1200 - 600) - (600 - 300)]$$ $$W_{net} = 1.005 \times [600 - 300] = 1.005 \times 300$$ $$W_{net} = 301.5 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ **Step 4:** Thermal efficiency at optimum pressure ratio: Using the Brayton efficiency formula: $$\eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{1}{(11.31)^{0.2857}} = 1 - \frac{1}{2.0} = 1 - 0.5 = 0.5$$ $$\eta_{th} = 50\%$$ **Final Answers:** - **(i) Optimum Pressure Ratio = 11.31** - **(ii) Net Work Output = 301.5 kJ/kg** - **(iii) Thermal Efficiency = 50%** --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Forgetting to apply the air-standard assumption** — Most students lose 1–2 marks by not explicitly stating assumptions in derivation questions. *Always* list the 4 key assumptions. > 2. **Sign error in $W_{net}$** — A common mistake is computing $W_{net} = W_C - W_T$. Remember: *Net work is always turbine work minus compressor work*. > 3. **Using $\gamma$ instead of $(\gamma-1)/\gamma$** — When computing $T_2$ and $T_4$, the exponent is $(\gamma-1)/\gamma = 0.2857$, **NOT** $\gamma = 1.4$. This is the **most common computational error** in KTU papers. > 4. **Confusing optimum pressure ratio for max work vs max efficiency** — They are *different*. Max efficiency occurs at $r_p \to \infty$ (asymptotic), but max net work occurs at the finite value derived above. > 5. **Not simplifying units** — Always express work in **kJ/kg** and temperatures in **K** (not °C) for full marks. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Cycle Efficiency (Module 1)** - ✅ The **Brayton Cycle** is the air-standard cycle for gas turbines — four processes: **Isentropic Compression → Isobaric Heat Addition → Isentropic Expansion → Isobaric Heat Rejection**. - ✅ Thermal efficiency formula: $\eta_{th} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{(r_p)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}}$ — depends **only on $r_p$ and $\gamma$**. - ✅ **$\gamma$ for air = 1.4** (always unless otherwise stated); **$C_p$ for air = 1.005 kJ/kg·K**. - ✅ **Efficiency increases with pressure ratio** — monotonically and asymptotically. - ✅ **Net work output is maximum** at the **optimum pressure ratio** $r_{p,opt} = (T_3/T_1)^{\gamma/[2(\gamma-1)]}$. - ✅ At optimum $r_p$: **$T_2 = T_4$** and **Work Ratio = 0.5**. - ✅ **Work Ratio (WR) = $W_{net}/W_T = 1 - W_C/W_T$** — always **less than 1**. - ✅ Net work output = Turbine work − Compressor work = $C_p[(T_3 - T_4) - (T_2 - T_1)]$. - ✅ Heat supplied = $C_p(T_3 - T_2)$; Heat rejected = $C_p(T_4 - T_1)$. - ✅ Isentropic relations: $T_2 = T_1 (r_p)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$ and $T_4 = T_3 / (r_p)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$. - ✅ On a **T-S diagram**, isentropic processes are **vertical lines**; isobaric processes are **exponential curves**. - ✅ On a **P-V diagram**, isentropic processes are **steep curves**; isobaric processes are **horizontal lines**. - ✅ **Higher $r_p$ → higher efficiency, but lower net work beyond the optimum point** — trade-off in real design. - ✅ **Typical $r_p$**: 3–6 for micro-turbines, 8–12 for aircraft, 15–30 for power plants.

topics.title: Axial Flow Compressor - Working Principle
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMET741 - GAS TURBINE AND JET PROPULSION > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Axial Flow Compressor - Working Principle # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Axial Flow Compressor (AFC):** A *dynamic, rotodynamic* turbomachine that continuously raises the static pressure of a compressible working fluid (air or combustion gases) by converting the **kinetic energy** imparted by a rotating bladed rotor into **pressure energy** through a process of diffusion, while the bulk fluid motion remains essentially **parallel to the axis of rotation (longitudinal/axial direction).** In strict KTU examination language, an axial flow compressor is classified as a **continuous-flow, steady-flow, axial-displacement turbomachine** belonging to the family of *kinetic energy → pressure energy conversion devices*. It is the work-absorbing counterpart of the axial flow turbine within a gas turbine engine. The fundamental compression unit is termed a **STAGE**. A single stage consists of: 1. **Rotor (Rotor Blades / Moving Blades / Impeller Blades)** – mounted on a rotating disc attached to the shaft. 2. **Stator (Stator Vanes / Stator Blades / IGVs / Diffuser Vanes)** – stationary guide vanes fixed to the casing. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The pair (Rotor + Stator) = **One Stage** of an axial flow compressor. A multi-stage compressor stacks *N* such stages in series to achieve the desired overall pressure ratio (typically 4:1 to 40:1 in modern aero-engines). --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Engineering Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — "The Escalator of Air":** > Imagine a long escalator in a shopping mall, but instead of carrying people, it carries air parcels. The escalator (the compressor) consists of alternating *moving steps* (the **rotor blades**, spinning at high rpm) and *stationary landings* (the **stator vanes**). As each parcel of air hops onto a moving step, it gets a forward *push* and a slight *squeeze* — the rotor adds kinetic energy. The instant the parcel steps off onto a landing, the stationary vane slows it down (diffusion) and the loss of speed translates into a rise in pressure. Repeating this hop–squeeze cycle across many stages is exactly how an axial flow compressor builds up high pressure gradually, with each stage adding only a small pressure increment. **Another intuition — "Bicycle Pump on Steroids":** A bicycle pump (reciprocating) compresses air in *discrete pulses*. An axial flow compressor does the same job, but in a *continuous, smooth, jet-engine-grade* manner — like squeezing air through a narrowing straw while a fan keeps blowing it along. ### Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (KTU Board-Exam Standard) | Metric | Typical Range / Value | |---|---| | **Aerodynamic Stage Pressure Ratio (βstage)** | **1.2 – 1.4** (modern highly-loaded) | | **Overall Pressure Ratio (Po/Pi)** | **4 : 1 to 40 : 1** (civil/military aero) | | **Polytropic Efficiency (ηpoly)** | **0.88 – 0.92** | | **Isentropic Efficiency (ηisen)** | **0.80 – 0.88** | | **Rotor Tip Speed (U)** | **300 – 450 m/s** (subsonic blading) | | **Rotor Inlet Temperature (T01)** | **288 K – 300 K** (standard day) | | **Degree of Reaction (R)** | **0.5 (50 %)** for symmetric blading | | **Number of Stages** | **8 to 20** (typical modern turbofan HPC) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why is it called "AXIAL"?** The Greek-Latin root *axis* tells us the **bulk direction of fluid flow is along the axis of rotation**, not radial. Compare with a centrifugal compressor, where air is thrown outward radially by centrifugal force. --- ## 1.3 Classification of Axial Flow Compressors (for KTU 2024 context) 1. **Based on Degree of Reaction:** - **50 % Reaction Compressor (Symmetric / Normal blading)** — most common; α₁ = β₂ and α₂ = β₁. - **Less than 50 % Reaction** — rotor does less work than stator. - **More than 50 % Reaction** — rotor does more work than stator (rare in compressors, common in turbines). 2. **Based on Flow Path Geometry:** - **Cylindrical (Constant outer/inner diameter) Flow Path** — most common. - **Conical (Tapered) Flow Path** — used when tip-speed must be limited. 3. **Based on Number of Stages:** - Single-stage (used in small turbochargers). - Multi-stage (used in all large gas turbine engines). # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Working Principle — Step-by-Step Operational Logic > [!IMPORTANT] > **The working principle of an axial flow compressor can be broken down into FOUR sequential energy-conversion stages, repeated at every row of blades.** ### Step 1 — Air Entry & Pre-Swirl (Inlet Guide Vanes – IGV) Atmospheric air enters the **eye of the compressor** through the intake duct. The first set of stationary vanes, called the **Inlet Guide Vanes (IGVs)**, impart a controlled **pre-whirl** (often *negative* swirl, i.e., opposite to rotor rotation) to the incoming air. - **Why?** Pre-whirl allows the designer to optimize the velocity triangle at the rotor inlet for maximum pressure rise with minimum losses. ### Step 2 — Energy Addition in the ROTOR (Moving Blades) The rotor blades, mounted on a rotating disc, act as **tiny airfoils in a relative-velocity frame** (just like aircraft wings, but with airflow coming from below due to relative motion). - As the rotor spins, each blade row performs **work on the fluid**. - The fluid is **deflected** (changes direction) and **accelerated** in the *relative* frame. - The *absolute* velocity of the fluid increases; the **total enthalpy (h₀) rises sharply** — this is the work input from the shaft. - A small portion of this kinetic energy is already converted to pressure through diffusion in the blade passages. ### Step 3 — Diffusion in the STATOR (Stationary Blades) The high-velocity, swirling air leaving the rotor enters the **stator (stator vanes)**, which is fixed to the casing. - The stator vanes act as **stationary diffusers** — they are shaped to *gradually expand* the flow area. - As the cross-sectional area expands, the absolute velocity **decreases** (per continuity: A·V = constant). - By **Bernoulli's principle**, this kinetic energy is converted into a **rise in static pressure**. - The swirl component of velocity is *removed* in the stator, restoring near-axial flow for the next stage. ### Step 4 — Repetition Across N Stages The fluid, now at a higher pressure and near-axial velocity, enters the **next rotor** of the next stage. The cycle repeats until the desired overall pressure ratio is achieved at the **compressor exit**. > [!NOTE] > **Net Effect per Stage:** Static pressure rises by a factor of 1.2–1.4 × per stage; total (stagnation) temperature rises by 20–40 K per stage. Across 15 stages, a modern HPC can multiply air pressure by ~12×. --- ## 2.2 Velocity Triangles — The Heart of the Axial Compressor The KTU 2024 board examiner will expect you to draw and label **velocity triangles** at the **rotor inlet (suffix 1)** and the **rotor outlet (suffix 2)**. ### Three velocity vectors at every station: | Symbol | Name | Magnitude Type | Direction | |---|---|---|---| | **C** | Absolute velocity | Magnitude $C = \sqrt{C_a^{\,2} + C_\theta^{\,2}}$ | At angle **α** to axial direction | | **W** | Relative velocity (w.r.t. rotating blade) | $W = \sqrt{W_a^{\,2} + W_\theta^{\,2}}$ | At angle **β** to axial direction | | **U** | Blade (peripheral / tangential) velocity | $U = \pi D N / 60$ | Purely tangential, direction of rotation | ### Vector Relationship (Fundamental Triangle Identity): $$ \vec{C} \;=\; \vec{W} \;+\; \vec{U} $$ This is the **single most important equation** in axial turbomachine analysis. From this, the KTU board examiner will derive everything else. ### Decomposition into Axial (a) and Tangential (θ) Components: $$ C_a \;=\; W_a $$ $$ C_\theta \;=\; U \;+\; W_\theta $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Tip:** In an axial flow compressor, the **axial component of velocity remains constant** through a stage (because of mass conservation in an annular passage of approximately constant area). This makes $C_a = W_a$ a powerful simplifying assumption. --- ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Examination Cheat Sheet) > [!TIP] > **Master these 12 equations — they constitute ~70% of all numerical questions in KTU 2024 ESE for this module.** | # | Formula | Description | Units / Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $U = \dfrac{\pi D N}{60}$ | Blade tip velocity | $D$ = mean diameter [m], $N$ = rpm | | 2 | $\vec{C} = \vec{W} + \vec{U}$ | Velocity triangle vector sum | m/s | | 3 | $C_a = W_a$ | Axial velocity constant through stage | m/s | | 4 | $W = U \,(C_{\theta 2} - C_{\theta 1})$ | Euler's work equation (specific work) | J/kg (per kg of air/s) | | 5 | $W = U \cdot \Delta C_\theta$ | Shorthand for Euler's equation | J/kg | | 6 | $\eta_{\text{stage}} = \dfrac{\Delta h_0 \,(\text{ideal})}{\Delta h_0 \,(\text{actual})}$ | Isentropic efficiency of stage | dimensionless | | 7 | $R = \dfrac{\Delta h_{\text{stator}}}{\Delta h_{\text{stage}}}$ | Degree of Reaction (0 ≤ R ≤ 1) | dimensionless | | 8 | $R = 0.5 \Rightarrow \Delta T_{\text{rotor}} = \Delta T_{\text{stator}}$ | 50 % reaction condition | equality of enthalpy drops | | 9 | $\alpha_1 = \beta_2$ and $\alpha_2 = \beta_1$ | Symmetric blading at R = 0.5 | angle identity | | 10 | $\dfrac{T_{02}}{T_{01}} = 1 + \dfrac{\Delta C_\theta \cdot U}{C_p T_{01}}$ | Stagnation temperature rise per stage | $C_p = 1005$ J/(kg·K) for air | | 11 | $\dfrac{P_{02}}{P_{01}} = \left(1 + \dfrac{\eta \Delta T_0}{T_{01}}\right)^{\frac{\gamma}{\gamma-1}}$ | Stage pressure ratio | $\gamma = 1.4$ for air | | 12 | $C_\theta = U \,-\, W_\theta = U \tan\beta - C_a \tan\alpha$ (for $\beta > 90°$ cases) | Tangential component formula | careful with sign convention | > [!WARNING] > **Sign Convention Pitfall:** At the **rotor outlet of a compressor**, $C_{\theta 2}$ is **positive** (swirl in direction of rotation). At the **rotor inlet**, $C_{\theta 1}$ can be **positive, zero, or negative** depending on IGV setting. Always draw the triangles and use the convention shown by the examiner's diagram. --- ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility | Application | Why AFC is used | |---|---| | **Aero-engine HPC (High-Pressure Compressor)** | Highest pressure ratio per unit mass flow; smallest frontal area | | **Industrial gas turbines (Frame 5, Frame 9, GE LM series)** | Continuous high-mass-flow compression for power generation | | **Turbochargers (Diesel & petrol engines)** | Single-stage AFC for rapid boost response | | **Wind-tunnel drive systems** | Large-volume, steady compressed air supply | | **Supersonic/hypersonic inlet compressors (with shock pre-compression)** | Front stage of SCRAMJET support systems | > [!NOTE] > **Industry fact:** The GE9X (Boeing 777X engine) uses an **11-stage axial HPC** with a pressure ratio of **27:1**, isentropic efficiency **~91 %**, and produces **134,000 lbf of thrust** — a testament to the engineering maturity of axial flow compressors. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Euler's Work Equation for an Axial Flow Compressor ### Starting Premise (Newton's Second Law in Tangential Direction): Consider a fluid element of mass *m* entering a rotor blade row at station 1 and leaving at station 2. The tangential force exerted by the blade on the fluid is equal to the rate of change of tangential momentum: $$ F_\theta \;=\; \dot{m} \,(C_{\theta 2} - C_{\theta 1}) $$ ### Step 1 — Express Force in terms of Torque: The tangential force, acting at a mean radius *r*, produces a torque *T* on the rotor shaft: $$ T \;=\; F_\theta \cdot r \;=\; \dot{m} \cdot r \cdot (C_{\theta 2} - C_{\theta 1}) $$ ### Step 2 — Express Work Done per Second (Power): The shaft rotates at angular velocity $\omega$ rad/s, so shaft power input is: $$ P_{\text{shaft}} \;=\; T \cdot \omega \;=\; \dot{m} \cdot r \cdot \omega \cdot (C_{\theta 2} - C_{\theta 1}) $$ ### Step 3 — Replace $r \cdot \omega$ with Blade Velocity $U$: Since the mean blade speed $U = r \cdot \omega = \pi D N / 60$: $$ P_{\text{shaft}} \;=\; \dot{m} \cdot U \cdot (C_{\theta 2} - C_{\theta 1}) $$ ### Step 4 — Specific Work (per unit mass of fluid, W = P / ṁ): $$ \boxed{\,W \;=\; U \,(C_{\theta 2} - C_{\theta 1}) \;=\; U \cdot \Delta C_\theta \,} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **This is Euler's Turbomachinery Equation** — universally applicable to pumps, compressors, turbines, and fans. In a **compressor**, W is **input** (positive); in a **turbine**, the sign convention reverses. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Degree of Reaction (R) for an Axial Compressor ### Step 1 — Define R: By definition (KTU 2024 syllabus): $$ R \;=\; \dfrac{\text{Stagnation enthalpy rise in the rotor}}{\text{Total stagnation enthalpy rise in the stage}} $$ $$ R \;=\; \dfrac{C_p (T_{02} - T_{01})}{C_p (T_{03} - T_{01})} \;=\; \dfrac{\Delta T_{0,\text{rotor}}}{\Delta T_{0,\text{stage}}} $$ ### Step 2 — Use the Steady Flow Energy Equation (SFEE): - Across the **rotor** (with shaft work input $\Delta W_{\text{rotor}}$): $$ \Delta W_{\text{rotor}} \;=\; C_p (T_{02} - T_{01}) $$ - Across the **stator** (no shaft work, but diffusion → pressure rise): $$ \Delta W_{\text{stator}} \;=\; C_p (T_{03} - T_{02}) \;=\; 0 \;\;(\text{no work, but enthalpy same since stator is adiabatic and no work}) $$ Wait — let me re-do this more carefully. ### Correct Step 2 (Revision): For a stator (no shaft work, adiabatic, no $\Delta KE$ at inlet/outlet same level): - $h_{02,\text{stator in}} = h_{03,\text{stator out}} + \Delta h_{\text{static rise in stator}}$ - More precisely, the **static enthalpy drops** in the stator as kinetic energy converts to pressure: $$ \Delta h_{0,\text{stator}} \;=\; 0 \quad \text{(no work, no heat transfer — total enthalpy constant)} $$ So total stage work done on fluid: $$ W_{\text{stage}} \;=\; W_{\text{rotor}} \;=\; U (C_{\theta 2} - C_{\theta 1}) $$ ### Step 3 — Express R in terms of Velocity Components: For a 50 % reaction compressor with symmetric blading ($\alpha_1 = \beta_2$ and $\alpha_2 = \beta_1$), a classical derivation yields: $$ R \;=\; 0.5 \;+\; \dfrac{C_a}{2U} \,(\tan\beta_1 - \tan\beta_2) $$ ### Step 4 — For Purely Axial Inlet (no pre-swirl, $C_{\theta 1} = 0$): Let $C_{\theta 1} = 0$, so $W_1 \sin\beta_1 = -U$ (the relative velocity has tangential component opposite to U at inlet when α₁ = 90°). Using the Euler equation with $C_{\theta 1} = 0$: $$ W_{\text{stage}} \;=\; U \cdot C_{\theta 2} $$ Solving the velocity triangle: $$ C_{\theta 2} \;=\; U \;-\; C_a \tan\beta_2 $$ So: $$ R \;=\; 0.5 \;-\; \dfrac{C_a}{2U} \tan\beta_2 $$ > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Board Examination Result:** For a 50 % reaction compressor with axial inlet ($C_{\theta 1} = 0$), the relationship reduces to $\tan\beta_2 = 0$, which means $\beta_2 = 0°$ — the relative velocity at rotor exit is purely axial. This is the **optimum design condition** for symmetric blading. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Stage Pressure Rise (Pressure Coefficient) ### Starting Definition: The **isentropic stage temperature rise** equals actual work input divided by $C_p$: $$ \Delta T_{0,\text{ideal}} \;=\; \dfrac{W}{C_p} \;=\; \dfrac{U (C_{\theta 2} - C_{\theta 1})}{C_p} $$ ### Step 1 — Apply Isentropic Relation for Pressure Ratio: For an isentropic compression of a perfect gas: $$ \dfrac{P_{02}}{P_{01}} \;=\; \left(\dfrac{T_{02}}{T_{01}}\right)^{\!\frac{\gamma}{\gamma - 1}} $$ ### Step 2 — Substitute the Temperature Ratio: $$ \dfrac{P_{02}}{P_{01}} \;=\; \left(1 + \dfrac{\eta \cdot U (C_{\theta 2} - C_{\theta 1})}{C_p \, T_{01}}\right)^{\!\frac{\gamma}{\gamma - 1}} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Note:** This expression gives the **stage pressure ratio**, not the overall compressor pressure ratio. For an *N*-stage compressor, the overall ratio is: $$ \left(\dfrac{P_{0N}}{P_{01}}\right)_{\text{overall}} \;=\; \prod_{k=1}^{N} \left(\dfrac{P_{0,k+1}}{P_{0,k}}\right)_{\text{stage}} $$ --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Rotor vs Stator Functions | Property | **ROTOR (Moving Blades)** | **STATOR (Stator Vanes)** | |---|---|---| | Frame of reference | Rotating | Stationary | | Work transfer | **Yes — shaft work input** | None | | Energy added | Kinetic + Pressure (small) | Pressure only (diffusion) | | Velocity change | $C$ increases; $W$ may change | $C$ decreases (diffuser action) | | Stagnation enthalpy ($h_0$) | **Increases** | Stays constant (adiabatic, no work) | | Static enthalpy ($h$) | Increases | **Decreases** (KE → PE conversion) | | Static pressure ($P$) | Rises slightly | **Rises substantially** | | Static temperature ($T$) | Rises | **Drops** (adiabatic diffusion) | | Function | Accelerates the fluid | Diffuses / decelerates the fluid | | Mounted on | Rotor disc (rotates with shaft) | Casing (stationary) | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Air Path Through a Multi-Stage Axial Flow Compressor > [!NOTE] > The following Mermaid block diagram traces the **functional path of air** through a typical 14-stage axial flow compressor, identifying every key thermodynamic and aerodynamic transformation. This is the KTU 2024-recommended schematic style. ```mermaid flowchart TD Intake[ATMOSPHERIC AIR INTAKE
T01 = 288 K, P01 = 101.325 kPa] IGV[INLET GUIDE VANES IGV
Pre-swirl control, alpha 1 set] R1[ROTOR STAGE 1
Shaft work input, KE added] S1[STATOR STAGE 1
Diffusion, KE to pressure] R2[ROTOR STAGE 2
Further work input] S2[STATOR STAGE 2
Further diffusion] Rmid[... ROTOR STAGES 3 TO N-1
Repeated work addition] Smid[... STATOR STAGES 3 TO N-1
Repeated diffusion] RN[ROTOR STAGE N
Final work input] SN[STATOR STAGE N
Final diffusion] OUT[COMPRESSOR EXIT
High pressure, high T0 air to combustor] Intake --> IGV IGV --> R1 R1 --> S1 S1 --> R2 R2 --> S2 S2 --> Rmid Rmid --> Smid Smid --> RN RN --> SN SN --> OUT IGV -. Pre-swirl alpha1 .-> R1 R1 -. C theta 2 .-> S1 S1 -. C theta 3 .-> R2 ``` ## 4.2 Stage-Internal Process Block Diagram — Single Stage Mechanics ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Rotor Inlet Station 1
C1, W1, U1, alpha1, beta1] B[Rotor Outlet Station 2
C2, W2, U2, alpha2, beta2] C[Stator Outlet Station 3
C3, alpha3, P3 greater than P2] A -->|Rotor Work Input W| B B -->|Diffusion Pressure Rise| C A -. C theta 1 .-> B B -. C theta 2 .-> C ``` ## 4.3 Velocity Triangle Schematic (Conceptual — to be drawn on exam paper) > [!IMPORTANT] > **On your KTU 2024 exam answer sheet, the examiner will expect a hand-drawn velocity triangle with these labelled components:** ### Rotor Inlet Triangle (Station 1): - **Vector C₁** at angle **α₁** above axial direction. - **Vector U** along the tangential direction (rotor spin). - **Vector W₁** at angle **β₁** below axial direction (relative velocity, opposite tangential component of U). - The vector closure: $\vec{C_1} = \vec{W_1} + \vec{U}$. ### Rotor Outlet Triangle (Station 2): - **Vector C₂** at angle **α₂** above axial direction (typically α₂ < α₁ for compressor). - **Vector U** along tangential direction. - **Vector W₂** at angle **β₂** below axial direction. - Closure: $\vec{C_2} = \vec{W_2} + \vec{U}$. ### KTU Drawing Tip: > [!TIP] > Draw the rotor inlet triangle on the **left** and rotor outlet triangle on the **right**, both sharing the same axial direction (horizontal). Mark U on the same horizontal level in both triangles. The change in $C_\theta$ ($\Delta C_\theta = C_{\theta 2} - C_{\theta 1}$) is the **tangential work component** — this is what the Euler equation directly uses. ## 4.4 Cross-Sectional View of an Axial Flow Compressor Stage ```mermaid flowchart TB CASING[Compressor Casing
Stationary outer shell] ROTOR[Rotor Disc
Rotates with shaft] RB[Rotor Blades
Airfoil-shaped, twisted] SV[Stator Vanes
Fixed to casing] SHAFT[Main Shaft
Driven by turbine] AIR[Axial Airflow Path
Through annular passages] CASING -.- SHAFT CASING --- SV ROTOR --- RB ROTOR === SHAFT AIR -.Passes through.- RB AIR -.Passes through.- SV ``` > [!NOTE] > **Physical Note:** Rotor blades are **highly twisted** from root to tip — because the blade velocity U varies with radius ($U = \omega r$), but the axial velocity $C_a$ is nearly constant. The blade angle $\beta$ is therefore adjusted along the span to maintain the desired relative flow angle, ensuring shock-free (or low-shock) entry at every radial station. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap > [!IMPORTANT] > **All questions below are modeled on the KTU 2024 Scheme End-Semester Examination (ESE) pattern for PEMET741. Mark distribution strictly follows the official Part A (2 × 3 = 6 marks) and Part B (1 × 14 = 14 marks) format with module-internal choice.** --- ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question A1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **"With a neat sketch, explain the working principle of an axial flow compressor. How does it differ from a centrifugal compressor in terms of flow direction?"** **Model Answer (3 marks):** An axial flow compressor compresses air by **continuously accelerating and decelerating** it through alternating rows of **rotor blades** (mounted on a rotating disc) and **stator vanes** (fixed to the casing). The bulk flow direction remains **parallel to the axis of rotation**, hence the name *axial*. Air enters the inlet guide vanes (IGVs) at near-ambient conditions, picks up kinetic energy in the rotor, and is then diffused to higher pressure in the stator. The cycle repeats across multiple stages. **Key difference from centrifugal compressor:** | Feature | Axial | Centrifugal | |---|---|---| | Flow direction | **Axial** (along axis) | **Radial** (outward from centre) | | Pressure rise per stage | Low (1.2–1.4 ×) | Higher (up to 4 ×) | | Frontal area | Small | Large | | Efficiency | High (88–92 %) | Moderate (78–82 %) | *(Sketch: simple diagram showing air entering IGV → rotor → stator → next stage, with axis of rotation marked. – 1 mark for sketch, 2 marks for explanation.)* --- ### Question A2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **"Define the term 'Degree of Reaction' (R) for an axial flow compressor. What is the significance of R = 0.5?"** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Definition:** Degree of reaction *R* is defined as the ratio of the **stagnation enthalpy rise in the rotor** to the **total stagnation enthalpy rise in the stage**: $$ R \;=\; \dfrac{\Delta h_{0,\text{rotor}}}{\Delta h_{0,\text{stage}}} \;=\; \dfrac{\Delta T_{0,\text{rotor}}}{\Delta T_{0,\text{stage}}} $$ **Significance of R = 0.5 (Symmetric / Normal Blading):** 1. **Equal enthalpy distribution** — the rotor and stator do equal thermodynamic work, simplifying thermal design. 2. **Symmetric velocity triangles** — $\alpha_1 = \beta_2$ and $\alpha_2 = \beta_1$, making the blading geometry identical for rotor and stator (modulo twist). 3. **Minimum loss condition** — at R = 0.5, the fluid enters and exits each blade row at the **same relative flow angle**, minimizing profile losses and improving stage efficiency. 4. **For axial inlet** ($C_{\theta 1} = 0$), R = 0.5 implies the relative velocity at rotor exit is **purely axial** ($W_{\theta 2} = 0$). --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice) --- ### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model Paper] #### **"With the help of velocity triangles at rotor inlet and outlet, derive the expression for the work done per unit mass of air in an axial flow compressor stage using Euler's equation. State the assumptions."** #### Part (a) — [7 Marks] Derivation of Euler's Work Equation and Assumptions **Model Solution:** **Assumptions:** 1. Steady, one-dimensional flow through the stage. 2. Working fluid is a perfect gas with constant specific heats ($C_p$, $\gamma$). 3. Rotor and stator blade rows are infinite in number (continuous-annulus approximation). 4. Axial velocity $C_a$ is constant through the stage (mass conservation in constant-area annular passage). 5. No heat transfer to surroundings (adiabatic process). 6. Blade-row losses are neglected in the ideal analysis (then incorporated via isentropic efficiency). **Derivation:** Consider a control volume enclosing one rotor blade row. Applying the **angular momentum equation** between rotor inlet (station 1) and rotor outlet (station 2): $$ T_{\text{shaft}} \;=\; \dot{m} \,(r_2 C_{\theta 2} - r_1 C_{\theta 1}) $$ The shaft torque $T_{\text{shaft}}$ rotates at angular velocity $\omega$, so power transferred to fluid: $$ P \;=\; T_{\text{shaft}} \cdot \omega \;=\; \dot{m} \cdot \omega \cdot (r_2 C_{\theta 2} - r_1 C_{\theta 1}) $$ For an axial flow compressor, the mean diameter is approximately constant: $r_1 \approx r_2 = r_{\text{mean}}$, so $r_1 \omega = r_2 \omega = U$ (mean blade velocity). Therefore: $$ P \;=\; \dot{m} \cdot U \cdot (C_{\theta 2} - C_{\theta 1}) $$ Specific work (per unit mass) is: $$ \boxed{\,w \;=\; \dfrac{P}{\dot{m}} \;=\; U (C_{\theta 2} - C_{\theta 1}) \;=\; U \cdot \Delta C_\theta \,} $$ This is **Euler's Turbomachinery Equation** for an axial flow compressor. ✓ **Numerical example (illustrative, not part of marks):** If $U = 250$ m/s, $C_{\theta 1} = 0$ (axial inlet), $C_{\theta 2} = 150$ m/s, then $w = 250 \times 150 = 37{,}500$ J/kg = 37.5 kJ/kg per stage. #### Part (b) — [7 Marks] Velocity Triangle Analysis & Degree of Reaction **Model Solution:** At the **rotor inlet (Station 1)**, three velocity vectors form a closed triangle: - $\vec{C_1}$ at angle $\alpha_1$ to the axial direction, - $\vec{U}$ tangential to rotor motion, - $\vec{W_1}$ at angle $\beta_1$ to the axial direction (the velocity of fluid *relative to the blade*). **Vector closure:** $\vec{C_1} = \vec{W_1} + \vec{U}$ Resolving into axial and tangential components: $$ C_{a1} \;=\; W_{a1} $$ $$ C_{\theta 1} \;=\; U \;-\; W_{\theta 1} \;=\; U \;-\; W_1 \sin\beta_1 $$ At the **rotor outlet (Station 2)**: $$ C_{a2} \;=\; W_{a2} $$ $$ C_{\theta 2} \;=\; U \;-\; W_{\theta 2} \;=\; U \;-\; W_2 \sin\beta_2 $$ **Degree of Reaction derivation:** The work done in the **rotor** is $w_{\text{rotor}} = U (C_{\theta 2} - C_{\theta 1})$, and the work done in the **stator** is zero (no shaft work, only diffusion). However, the *stagnation* enthalpy rise in the rotor vs the stage can be partitioned as: $$ R \;=\; \dfrac{w_{\text{rotor}}}{w_{\text{stage}}} \;=\; \dfrac{C_{\theta 2} - C_{\theta 1}}{C_{\theta 3} - C_{\theta 1}} \quad \text{(in compressor, with stator fully recovering swirl)} $$ For a 50 % reaction compressor (with $C_{\theta 3} = -C_{\theta 1}$ at stator exit for symmetric blading): $$ \boxed{\,R \;=\; 0.5 \,} $$ The corresponding velocity triangle symmetry condition: $$ \boxed{\,\alpha_1 \;=\; \beta_2 \quad \text{and} \quad \alpha_2 \;=\; \beta_1 \,} $$ This identity produces **mirror-symmetric triangles** and is the design optimum for minimum-loss axial compressor blading. ✓ --- ### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE [KTU University Exam – May 2024] #### **"An axial flow compressor operates at a rotational speed of 9000 rpm. The mean blade ring diameter is 0.5 m. The absolute velocity of air at rotor inlet is 180 m/s at an angle of 20° to the axial direction. At rotor exit, the absolute velocity is 200 m/s at an angle of 30° to the axial direction. Calculate: (a) the work done per kg of air per stage, and (b) the degree of reaction."** #### Part (a) — [7 Marks] Work Done per kg of Air **Given Data:** - $N = 9000$ rpm - $D = 0.5$ m - $C_1 = 180$ m/s, $\alpha_1 = 20°$ - $C_2 = 200$ m/s, $\alpha_2 = 30°$ **Step 1: Calculate the mean blade velocity $U$:** $$ U \;=\; \dfrac{\pi D N}{60} \;=\; \dfrac{\pi \times 0.5 \times 9000}{60} $$ $$ U \;=\; \dfrac{14137.17}{60} \;\approx\; 235.62 \text{ m/s} $$ **Step 2: Calculate the tangential components of absolute velocity:** $$ C_{\theta 1} \;=\; C_1 \sin\alpha_1 \;=\; 180 \times \sin 20° $$ $$ C_{\theta 1} \;=\; 180 \times 0.3420 \;\approx\; 61.56 \text{ m/s} $$ $$ C_{\theta 2} \;=\; C_2 \sin\alpha_2 \;=\; 200 \times \sin 30° $$ $$ C_{\theta 2} \;=\; 200 \times 0.5 \;=\; 100.0 \text{ m/s} $$ **Step 3: Apply Euler's Equation for specific work:** $$ w \;=\; U \cdot (C_{\theta 2} - C_{\theta 1}) $$ $$ w \;=\; 235.62 \times (100.0 - 61.56) $$ $$ w \;=\; 235.62 \times 38.44 \;\approx\; 9057.2 \text{ J/kg} $$ $$ \boxed{\,w \;\approx\; 9.06 \text{ kJ/kg per stage}\,} $$ **Step 4 (Verification):** Calculate axial components: $C_{a1} = 180 \cos 20° = 169.14$ m/s $C_{a2} = 200 \cos 30° = 173.21$ m/s *(The small difference is acceptable given the constant-area assumption is approximate.)* #### Part (b) — [7 Marks] Degree of Reaction **Step 1: Apply the degree of reaction formula:** For an axial flow compressor with non-zero $C_{\theta 1}$: $$ R \;=\; \dfrac{w_{\text{rotor}}}{w_{\text{stage}}} $$ We need the swirl at stator exit $C_{\theta 3}$. Assuming the stator is symmetric and the flow leaves the next rotor inlet with $C_{\theta 3} = -C_{\theta 1}$ (standard 50 % reaction blading assumption for this problem), then: $$ w_{\text{stage}} \;=\; U (C_{\theta 3} - C_{\theta 1}) \quad \text{? — re-check convention} $$ **Better approach:** Use the direct definition of *R* in terms of velocity triangle angles: $$ R \;=\; 0.5 \;+\; \dfrac{C_a}{2U} \,(\tan\beta_1 - \tan\beta_2) $$ **Step 2: Calculate relative velocity angles $\beta_1$ and $\beta_2$:** At rotor inlet: $W_{\theta 1} = U - C_{\theta 1} = 235.62 - 61.56 = 174.06$ m/s $W_{a1} = C_{a1} = 169.14$ m/s $W_1 = \sqrt{169.14^2 + 174.06^2} = \sqrt{28608 + 30297} = \sqrt{58905} \approx 242.7$ m/s $$ \tan\beta_1 \;=\; \dfrac{W_{\theta 1}}{W_{a1}} \;=\; \dfrac{174.06}{169.14} \;\approx\; 1.029 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \beta_1 \;\approx\; 45.81° $$ At rotor outlet: $W_{\theta 2} = U - C_{\theta 2} = 235.62 - 100.0 = 135.62$ m/s $W_{a2} = C_{a2} = 173.21$ m/s $$ \tan\beta_2 \;=\; \dfrac{135.62}{173.21} \;\approx\; 0.783 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \beta_2 \;\approx\; 38.07° $$ **Step 3: Plug into R formula:** Assuming $C_a \approx (C_{a1} + C_{a2})/2 = 171.18$ m/s (mean axial velocity): $$ R \;=\; 0.5 \;+\; \dfrac{171.18}{2 \times 235.62} \times (\tan 45.81° - \tan 38.07°) $$ $$ R \;=\; 0.5 \;+\; \dfrac{171.18}{471.24} \times (1.029 - 0.783) $$ $$ R \;=\; 0.5 \;+\; 0.3633 \times 0.246 $$ $$ R \;=\; 0.5 \;+\; 0.0894 $$ $$ \boxed{\,R \;\approx\; 0.589 \;\;\text{or}\;\; 58.9\%\,} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation:** The reaction is slightly above 50 %, meaning the rotor does a *bit more* thermodynamic work than the stator. This is acceptable and within the typical design range of 45 %–65 % for modern axial compressor stages. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls > [!WARNING] > **Where KTU 2024 students LOSE MARKS in axial flow compressor problems:** > > 1. **Sign convention error on $C_\theta$** — A compressor's $C_{\theta 2}$ must be **positive** (swirl in direction of rotation). If your velocity triangle has $C_{\theta 2}$ in the wrong direction, you'll lose 2–3 marks immediately. > > 2. **Confusing absolute (C) and relative (W) angles** — $\alpha$ is the *absolute* flow angle (from axial); $\beta$ is the *relative* flow angle. Mixing them in Euler's equation is the most common mistake. > > 3. **Forgetting units in $U = \pi DN/60$** — $D$ in **metres**, $N$ in **rpm**. If you use $N$ in rad/s, the formula changes to $U = \omega r$ with $\omega$ in rad/s. > > 4. **Not drawing the velocity triangle** — Even for purely numerical problems, KTU examiners award **1–2 marks** for a correct, labelled velocity triangle. **Always draw it.** > > 5. **Treating the stage pressure ratio as the overall pressure ratio** — A 10-stage compressor with stage ratio 1.25 has an *overall* ratio of $1.25^{10} \approx 9.31$, not 1.25. > > 6. **Incorrect application of $C_a = W_a$** — This holds only for an *ideal* constant-area annulus. For a tapered (conical) flow path, $C_a$ varies, and the problem must be solved without this simplification. > > 7. **Mixing up "Degree of Reaction" with "Pressure Ratio"** — These are completely different quantities. *R* is dimensionless and between 0 and 1; pressure ratio is a multiplicative factor > 1. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!TIP] > **Last-minute KTU 2024 exam revision — memorize and master these 18 high-yield points:** - [ ] **AFC = Rotodynamic, axial-flow, kinetic → pressure energy conversion device.** - [ ] **Stage = Rotor + Stator.** - [ ] **Air flows along the axis** — that's why it's called "axial." - [ ] **Rotor does shaft work → adds kinetic energy** (h₀ increases). - [ ] **Stator is a diffuser → converts KE to pressure** (h stays same, h_static drops, P rises). - [ ] **Velocity triangle identity: $\vec{C} = \vec{W} + \vec{U}$** — golden rule. - [ ] **$C_a = W_a$ (axial velocity constant through stage)** — simplification. - [ ] **Euler's work equation: $w = U (C_{\theta 2} - C_{\theta 1})$** — most used equation. - [ ] **$U = \pi DN / 60$** — blade tip speed. - [ ] **Degree of reaction $R$** = enthalpy rise in rotor ÷ enthalpy rise in stage. - [ ] **50 % reaction** → symmetric blading: $\alpha_1 = \beta_2$ and $\alpha_2 = \beta_1$. - [ ] **50 % reaction** → $\Delta T_{\text{rotor}} = \Delta T_{\text{stator}}$. - [ ] **Stage pressure ratio** typically 1.2–1.4; **overall** can be 4:1 to 40:1. - [ ] **For axial inlet** ($C_{\theta 1} = 0$): $w = U \cdot C_{\theta 2}$. - [ ] **Number of stages** is multiplied for high overall pressure ratios. - [ ] **Polytropic efficiency** of well-designed AFC: 88 %–92 %. - [ ] **Drawing velocity triangles correctly** = 1–2 bonus marks on every problem. - [ ] **Always use sign convention** for $C_\theta$ — positive in direction of rotation at rotor exit. --- *End of Notes — PEMET741 Module 2, Topic: Axial Flow Compressor — Working Principle.* *Prepared in accordance with KTU 2024 Scheme assessment guidelines.*

topics.title: Velocity Triangle
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET741 - GAS TURBINE AND JET PROPULSION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Velocity Triangle

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of axial-flow turbomachinery (compressors, turbines, and jet engines), a **Velocity Triangle** is a geometric vector diagram that resolves the fluid velocity into its fundamental components at the inlet and outlet of a rotor blade row. It is the cornerstone tool used to analyze energy transfer between the working fluid and the rotating bladed disk.

The velocity triangle is constructed using three primary vectors at any blade station:

- **Absolute Velocity ($C$):** The vector velocity of the fluid particle as observed by a stationary observer (fixed to the casing). It represents the true fluid velocity in the stationary frame of reference.
- **Relative Velocity ($W$):** The vector velocity of the fluid particle as observed by an observer moving with the rotating blade. It is the velocity of the fluid relative to the rotating reference frame.
- **Blade Velocity ($U$):** The linear velocity of the blade tip due to the rotational motion of the shaft. It is purely tangential and equal to $\omega \times r$, where $\omega$ is angular velocity in rad/s and $r$ is the mean blade radius in meters.

The fundamental vector relationship at any point in the rotor is given by **Euler's Vector Triangle Law**:

$$C = U + W$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The velocity triangle is the analytical foundation for deriving Euler's turbomachinery equation, calculating stage work, and determining the **Degree of Reaction (R)** of axial machines. Mastery of this topic is mandatory for Module 2 derivations and numerical problems.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**Real-World Analogy — The "Boat in a River" Model:**

Imagine you are standing on the bank of a river watching a motorboat cross the stream diagonally. To a stationary observer on the bank, the boat's velocity is the **Absolute Velocity ($C$)** — this is the true speed and direction of the boat over ground. However, to a person *sitting inside the boat*, the water appears to rush past them at an angle; this apparent velocity is the **Relative Velocity ($W$)**. The boat is also being carried downstream by the river's current, which represents the **Blade Velocity ($U$)** of our turbomachine analogy.

The relationship is simple:
- The water flowing past the boat = the **relative velocity** the blade *feels*.
- The boat's true path over ground = the **absolute velocity** of the gas in the engine frame.
- The river's current = the **rotational blade velocity** dragging the reference frame.

When the boat aims upstream, the relative and absolute velocities diverge sharply. Similarly, in a gas turbine rotor, the angle between $C$ and $W$ is dictated by the blade's rotational speed and the gas's mass flow rate.

### 1.3 Standard Physical Metrics & Conventions

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard KTU Notation Conventions (Use these in all answers):**
>
> | Symbol | Meaning | Typical Unit |
> |---|---|---|
> | $C_1, C_2$ | Absolute velocity at rotor inlet/outlet | m/s |
> | $W_1, W_2$ | Relative velocity at rotor inlet/outlet | m/s |
> | $U$ | Mean blade (mean line) velocity | m/s |
> | $\alpha_1, \alpha_2$ | Absolute flow angle (between $C$ and $U$) | degrees (°) |
> | $\beta_1, \beta_2$ | Relative flow angle (between $W$ and $U$) | degrees (°) |
> | $C_w$ | Whirl component = $C \cos(\alpha)$ | m/s |
> | $C_f$ | Flow (axial) component = $C \sin(\alpha)$ | m/s |
>
> The subscripts **1** and **2** denote the **inlet** and **outlet** of the rotor blade row, respectively.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fundamental Geometric Fact:** In a velocity triangle, the **flow component ($C_f$) is conserved** between relative and absolute frames because axial velocity is invariant under pure rotation. Therefore:
> $C_f = C_1 \sin(\alpha_1) = W_1 \sin(\beta_1) = C_2 \sin(\alpha_2) = W_2 \sin(\beta_2)$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Anatomical Breakdown of the Velocity Triangle

A single velocity triangle (say, the **inlet triangle**) is constructed as follows:

**Step 1 — Establish the Base:**
Draw a horizontal vector representing the **blade velocity $U$**. This vector points in the direction of rotation of the blade.

**Step 2 — Add the Relative Velocity:**
From the tip of vector $U$, draw the **relative velocity $W_1$** at an angle $\beta_1$ measured from the direction of $U$. The relative velocity tail is at the tip of $U$.

**Step 3 — Complete with the Absolute Velocity:**
The **absolute velocity $C_1$** is drawn from the **tail of $U$ to the tip of $W_1$**. By the triangle rule of vector addition, this automatically satisfies $C_1 = U + W_1$.

**Step 4 — Resolve Components:**
Drop a perpendicular from the tip of $C_1$ onto the $U$ line. The horizontal projection is the **whirl component $C_{w1} = C_1 \cos(\alpha_1)$**. The vertical projection is the **flow component $C_f = C_1 \sin(\alpha_1)$**.

### 2.2 The Two Triangles: Inlet vs. Outlet

A complete stage analysis requires **two velocity triangles** — one at rotor entry and one at rotor exit. For a turbine or compressor stage, the key parameters are:

- **Inlet triangle:** Uses $C_1$, $W_1$, $\alpha_1$, $\beta_1$.
- **Outlet triangle:** Uses $C_2$, $W_2$, $\alpha_2$, $\beta_2$.
- The **base length $U$** is the same for both (mean blade speed is constant along a stage).

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Closing Triangle" Trick:** When inlet and outlet velocity triangles are drawn sharing the common base $U$, the absolute velocity vectors $C_1$ and $C_2$ form two sides, and the **change in absolute velocity** between inlet and outlet is a third vector closing the figure. The horizontal projection of this closing vector is the **change in whirl velocity ($\Delta C_w$)**, which is the key parameter in Euler's work equation.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Description / Application | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $C = U + W$ | Euler's vector triangle law (absolute frame) | m/s |
| 2 | $W^2 = C^2 + U^2 - 2CU\cos(\alpha)$ | Law of cosines for relative velocity magnitude | (m/s)² |
| 3 | $\tan(\beta) = \dfrac{C \sin(\alpha)}{C \cos(\alpha) - U}$ | Relative flow angle from absolute quantities | dimensionless |
| 4 | $C_f = C \sin(\alpha) = W \sin(\beta)$ | Conservation of axial/flow component | m/s |
| 5 | $C_w = C \cos(\alpha)$ | Whirl (tangential) component of absolute velocity | m/s |
| 6 | $W = U \cdot \Delta C_w = U(C_1\cos\alpha_1 - C_2\cos\alpha_2)$ | **Euler's Turbomachinery Equation** — Work per unit mass | J/kg |
| 7 | $R = \dfrac{C_f}{2U}(\tan\beta_2 - \tan\beta_1)$ | Degree of reaction (axial turbine) | dimensionless |
| 8 | $R = 0.5$ (or 50%) | Symmetric blade condition: $\alpha_1 = \alpha_2$, $\beta_1 = \beta_2$ | dimensionless |
| 9 | $U = \pi D_m N / 60$ | Mean blade velocity from shaft speed | m/s |
| 10 | $\Delta C_w = U \cdot (\tan\beta_1 - \tan\beta_2) / 2$ for $R=0.5$ stage | Useful shortcut for 50% reaction turbines | m/s |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Sign Convention (KTU Board Pattern):** Work done is **positive** when the absolute whirl velocity **decreases** in the direction of blade motion (typical for turbines). For compressors, work is **negative** (work done *on* the fluid) because whirl velocity *increases*. Always state the sign explicitly in your answer.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Velocity triangles are not merely academic exercises — they are the **design language of the gas turbine industry**. Every modern turbofan engine (Rolls-Royce Trent, GE GE9X, Pratt & Whitney PW1000G) is designed by first sketching velocity triangles at multiple spanwise stations (hub, mean, tip) for each blade row. The triangles dictate:
- Blade camber line geometry (the metal shape of the airfoil)
- Number of stages required for a given pressure ratio
- Optimum specific speed and specific diameter for the machine
- Shock and loss prediction in transonic blading

In jet propulsion, the velocity triangle at the **turbine rotor** determines how much shaft power is extracted to drive the compressor and accessories — a balance that defines the engine's thermodynamic cycle efficiency.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Relative Velocity Magnitude from Law of Cosines

**Starting Premise:** From the vector triangle, $C = U + W$. We seek the magnitude of $W$ in terms of $C$, $U$, and the absolute flow angle $\alpha$.

**Step 1 — State the vector relationship:**
The relative velocity vector is the difference between absolute and blade velocity:
$$W = C - U$$

**Step 2 — Take the squared magnitude of both sides:**
The scalar magnitude squared of any vector difference $|A - B|^2$ is given by the law of cosines. We apply it:
$$W^2 = C^2 + U^2 - 2 \cdot C \cdot U \cdot \cos(\theta)$$

where $\theta$ is the angle between vectors $C$ and $U$ at the tail of $U$. By construction of the velocity triangle, this angle is precisely $\alpha$ (the absolute flow angle).

**Step 3 — Substitute the angle:**
Replacing $\theta$ with $\alpha$, the law of cosines for the velocity triangle becomes:
$$W^2 = C^2 + U^2 - 2CU\cos(\alpha)$$

**Step 4 — Derive the relative flow angle $\beta$:**
The flow component $C_f$ must be conserved across the rotation transformation. We split the absolute velocity $C$ into its components parallel (whirl) and perpendicular (flow) to $U$:
$$C_f = C \sin(\alpha)$$
$$C_w = C \cos(\alpha)$$

The relative velocity $W$ also has a flow component (equal to $C_f$) and a relative whirl component. The relative whirl component is $(C \cos(\alpha) - U)$ — note the subtraction because the blade is moving in the same direction as the whirl.

**Step 5 — Use the tangent of the relative angle:**
By definition, $\tan(\beta)$ is the ratio of the flow component to the relative whirl component:
$$\tan(\beta) = \frac{C \sin(\alpha)}{C \cos(\alpha) - U}$$

This compact equation is the **master formula** for converting absolute flow conditions to relative (blade-fixed) flow conditions in any axial turbomachine.

### 3.2 Derivation: Euler's Turbomachinery Equation

**Starting Premise:** The work done per unit mass by the fluid on the rotor is the rate of change of the moment of momentum (angular momentum) of the fluid.

**Step 1 — State the angular momentum principle:**
The work done per unit mass ($W_s$) by a fluid on a rotating blade is given by:
$$W_s = U_1 \cdot C_{w1} - U_2 \cdot C_{w2}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the constant mean radius assumption:**
For a simple axial-flow stage, the inlet and outlet radii are approximately equal ($D_1 \approx D_2 \approx D_m$), so the mean blade velocity is constant: $U_1 = U_2 = U$.

**Step 3 — Substitute and simplify:**
$$W_s = U(C_{w1} - C_{w2}) = U \cdot \Delta C_w$$

**Step 4 — Express whirl components using absolute flow angles:**
Since $C_{w1} = C_1 \cos(\alpha_1)$ and $C_{w2} = C_2 \cos(\alpha_2)$:
$$W_s = U(C_1 \cos(\alpha_1) - C_2 \cos(\alpha_2))$$

This is the **canonical form of Euler's turbomachinery equation**, written in terms of absolute velocities and flow angles. It is the single most important equation in Module 2.

### 3.3 Derivation: Degree of Reaction for an Axial Turbine Stage

**Starting Premise:** Degree of reaction $R$ is defined as the ratio of the static enthalpy (or temperature) drop in the rotor to the total static enthalpy drop in the stage.

**Step 1 — Express static enthalpy drops in terms of velocities:**
For an incompressible approximation or using the kinetic energy analogy:
$$\Delta h_{\text{rotor}} \propto (W_1^2 - W_2^2)/2$$
$$\Delta h_{\text{stage}} \propto (C_1^2 - C_2^2)/2 + (W_1^2 - W_2^2)/2$$

**Step 2 — Expand the squared relative velocities using the cosine law:**
Apply $W^2 = C^2 + U^2 - 2CU\cos(\alpha)$ at both inlet and outlet:
$$W_1^2 - W_2^2 = (C_1^2 - C_2^2) - 2U(C_1 \cos\alpha_1 - C_2 \cos\alpha_2)$$

**Step 3 — Form the ratio:**
$$R = \frac{W_1^2 - W_2^2}{(C_1^2 - C_2^2) + (W_1^2 - W_2^2)}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the cosine-law expression and simplify (using $C_f = C\sin\alpha = W\sin\beta$):**
After algebraic simplification (substituting $C \cos\alpha = U \pm C_f \tan\beta$ from the geometry), the result reduces to:
$$R = \frac{C_f}{2U}(\tan\beta_2 - \tan\beta_1)$$

**Step 5 — Special case: 50% Reaction:**
Setting $R = 0.5$ and solving, we find $\tan\beta_1 = -\tan\beta_2$, which gives $\beta_1 = \beta_2$ in magnitude (with opposite signs due to mirror symmetry). For 50% reaction axial stages, the velocity triangles are **symmetric** about the axial direction.

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Pattern)

**Problem:** Air enters a turbine rotor blade with $C_1 = 200$ m/s at an angle $\alpha_1 = 20°$. The mean blade speed is $U = 150$ m/s. Calculate the relative velocity $W_1$ and the relative flow angle $\beta_1$. (Assume no change in axial direction.)

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute the flow component:**
$$C_f = C_1 \sin(\alpha_1) = 200 \cdot \sin(20°)$$
$$C_f = 200 \cdot 0.3420 = 68.4 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the whirl component:**
$$C_{w1} = C_1 \cos(\alpha_1) = 200 \cdot \cos(20°)$$
$$C_{w1} = 200 \cdot 0.9397 = 187.94 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the relative whirl component (this is the projection of $W_1$ along $-U$):**
Since $C_{w1} > U$, the relative whirl is *negative* (relative flow has a component *opposite* to $U$):
$$W_{1,\text{whirl}} = C_{w1} - U = 187.94 - 150 = 37.94 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the Pythagorean relation in the velocity triangle:**
$$W_1 = \sqrt{C_f^2 + (C_{w1} - U)^2}$$
$$W_1 = \sqrt{68.4^2 + 37.94^2}$$
$$W_1 = \sqrt{4678.6 + 1439.3}$$
$$W_1 = \sqrt{6117.9}$$
$$W_1 \approx 78.2 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 5 — Compute the relative flow angle:**
$$\tan(\beta_1) = \frac{C_f}{C_{w1} - U} = \frac{68.4}{37.94} = 1.803$$
$$\beta_1 = \arctan(1.803) \approx 61°$$

**Step 6 — Verify with the law of cosines:**
$$W_1^2 = C_1^2 + U^2 - 2 C_1 U \cos(\alpha_1)$$
$$W_1^2 = 200^2 + 150^2 - 2(200)(150)\cos(20°)$$
$$W_1^2 = 40000 + 22500 - 60000(0.9397)$$
$$W_1^2 = 62500 - 56382 = 6118$$
$$W_1 = \sqrt{6118} \approx 78.2 \text{ m/s} \checkmark$$

The result is consistent. **Final Answer:** $W_1 \approx 78.2$ m/s and $\beta_1 \approx 61°$.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Velocity Triangle Analysis

The following Mermaid diagram maps the analytical workflow for solving a typical KTU velocity triangle problem, showing how the known inputs feed into the geometric relationships and ultimately yield the desired output parameters.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Stage 1: Input Parameters] --> B[Mean Blade Velocity U]
    A --> C[Absolute Velocity C1]
    A --> D[Absolute Flow Angle alpha1]
    A --> E[Absolute Velocity C2]
    A --> F[Absolute Flow Angle alpha2]
    
    B --> G[Resolve Components]
    C --> G
    D --> G
    E --> G
    F --> G
    
    G --> H[Compute Flow Component Cf = C sin alpha]
    G --> I[Compute Whirl Component Cw = C cos alpha]
    G --> J[Apply Law of Cosines]
    
    H --> K[Step 2: Relative Velocity W]
    I --> K
    J --> K
    
    K --> L[Relative Velocity W1 and W2]
    K --> M[Relative Flow Angle beta1 and beta2]
    
    L --> N[Step 3: Stage Performance]
    M --> N
    I --> N
    
    N --> O[Euler Work: Ws = U DeltaCw]
    N --> P[Degree of Reaction: R]
    N --> Q[Blade Angles: symmetric or asymmetric]
    
    P --> R{Check R = 0.5?}
    R -- Yes --> S[Symmetric Triangle]
    R -- No --> T[Asymmetric Triangle]
    
    style A fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    style G fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    style K fill:#5b9bd5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    style N fill:#9dc3e6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    style S fill:#70ad47,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    style T fill:#ed7d31,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
```

### 4.2 Vector Relationship Map (Conceptual Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    V1[Absolute Velocity C] --> V2[Vector Sum Identity C = U + W]
    V3[Blade Velocity U] --> V2
    V4[Relative Velocity W] --> V2
    
    V1 --> V5[Whirl Component Cw]
    V1 --> V6[Flow Component Cf]
    
    V4 --> V7[Relative Whirl Component]
    V4 --> V6
    
    V5 --> V8[Absolute Flow Angle alpha]
    V6 --> V8
    
    V7 --> V9[Relative Flow Angle beta]
    V6 --> V9
    
    V8 --> V10[Euler Work Equation Ws]
    V9 --> V10
    V3 --> V10
    
    V8 --> V11[Degree of Reaction R]
    V9 --> V11
    V3 --> V11
    
    style V2 fill:#c00000,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    style V8 fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    style V9 fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    style V10 fill:#70ad47,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
```
