```mermaid
    style V11 fill:#70ad47,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
```

### 4.3 Inlet vs. Outlet Triangle Side-by-Side Schematic (ASCII Reference)

```
     INLET TRIANGLE                          OUTLET TRIANGLE
     (Rotor Entry)                           (Rotor Exit)

         C1                                        C2
        /|\                                       /|\
       / | \                                     / | \
      /  |  \                                   /  |  \
     / α1|   \                                 / α2|   \
    /____|____\                               /____|____\
   U ->  Cf   W1                             U ->  Cf   W2
                                                 
   Notes:                                 Notes:
   - α1 is measured from U to C1          - α2 is measured from U to C2
   - β1 is measured from U to W1          - β2 is measured from U to W2
   - Both triangles share base U          - Whirl drop: ΔCw = Cw1 - Cw2
   - C_f is conserved (axial continuity)  - Work: Ws = U · ΔCw
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** The "shrinking" of $C_w$ (whirl component) from inlet to outlet represents the energy extracted by the turbine (or energy added to the fluid in a compressor). The "shrinking" of $W$ (relative velocity) in a turbine represents the expansion and acceleration of the gas across the rotor passages.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Pattern]**

**Define the following terms with respect to velocity triangles in an axial flow turbomachine:**
**(a)** Absolute velocity, **(b)** Relative velocity, **(c)** Blade velocity.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**(a) Absolute Velocity ($C$):** The velocity of the working fluid (gas) as measured by a stationary observer fixed to the machine casing. It represents the true velocity of the gas in the stationary (lab) frame of reference and is typically resolved into a whirl component $C_w = C\cos\alpha$ and a flow component $C_f = C\sin\alpha$. **[1 mark]**

**(b) Relative Velocity ($W$):** The velocity of the working fluid as observed by an observer moving with the rotating blade. It is the velocity of the gas relative to the rotating reference frame and is related to absolute velocity by the vector equation $W = C - U$. **[1 mark]**

**(c) Blade Velocity ($U$):** The linear tangential velocity of the rotating blade at the mean radius, given by $U = \pi D_m N / 60$, where $D_m$ is the mean diameter and $N$ is the rotational speed in RPM. It is purely tangential and acts as the bridge between the stationary and rotating reference frames. **[1 mark]**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Question Pattern]**

**State and explain the significance of Euler's turbomachinery equation as applied to an axial flow turbine stage.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Statement (1.5 marks):** Euler's turbomachinery equation, derived from the principle of conservation of angular momentum, gives the work done per unit mass of fluid flowing through an axial rotor as:

$$W_s = U(C_1 \cos\alpha_1 - C_2 \cos\alpha_2) = U \cdot \Delta C_w$$

where $U$ is the mean blade velocity, $C_1$ and $C_2$ are the absolute velocities at rotor inlet and outlet, and $\alpha_1$, $\alpha_2$ are the corresponding absolute flow angles.

**Significance (1.5 marks):**
1. It is the **fundamental energy transfer equation** for all rotating turbomachines (turbines, compressors, pumps).
2. It shows that the work output is proportional to the **change in whirl velocity** ($\Delta C_w$) of the fluid.
3. It provides the **theoretical basis for blade design** — to maximize work transfer, designers engineer the blade shapes such that the whirl change is maximized within aerodynamic and structural constraints.
4. For a compressor, the sign of $W_s$ is negative (work done *on* the fluid); for a turbine, it is positive (work extracted *from* the fluid).

---

### 📗 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Two alternatives per question; answer any ONE. Each question has sub-parts (a) and (b), typically 7 marks each.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Pattern]*

**(a)** Derive the expression for the relative velocity $W$ of the fluid entering an axial flow turbine blade in terms of absolute velocity $C$, blade velocity $U$, and absolute flow angle $\alpha$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An axial flow turbine stage operates with a mean blade speed of $U = 180$ m/s. The absolute velocity at the rotor inlet is $C_1 = 350$ m/s at an angle $\alpha_1 = 25°$, and at the rotor outlet is $C_2 = 280$ m/s at an angle $\alpha_2 = 60°$. Calculate:
- (i) The work done per unit mass of fluid.
- (ii) The degree of reaction, assuming axial velocity is constant at $C_f = 100$ m/s.
**(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Question A:**

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Consider an axial flow rotor with the fluid entering the blade at absolute velocity $C$ making an angle $\alpha$ with the blade velocity direction $U$.

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Since the blade moves with velocity $U$ in the same direction, the relative velocity of the fluid with respect to the blade is the vector difference:
$$W = C - U$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Applying the law of cosines to the velocity triangle (where $\alpha$ is the angle between $C$ and $U$ at the tail of $U$):
$$W^2 = C^2 + U^2 - 2CU\cos(\alpha)$$

Taking the square root gives the magnitude of relative velocity.

**Step 4 (2 marks):** The relative flow angle $\beta$ is found by resolving into components. Since the axial (flow) component is conserved across rotation:
$$C_f = C\sin(\alpha) = W\sin(\beta)$$

The relative whirl component is $C\cos(\alpha) - U$, therefore:
$$\tan(\beta) = \frac{C\sin(\alpha)}{C\cos(\alpha) - U}$$

This gives both the magnitude and direction of the relative velocity.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Compute whirl components (1.5 marks):**
$$C_{w1} = C_1 \cos(\alpha_1) = 350 \cdot \cos(25°) = 350 \cdot 0.9063 = 317.2 \text{ m/s}$$
$$C_{w2} = C_2 \cos(\alpha_2) = 280 \cdot \cos(60°) = 280 \cdot 0.5 = 140.0 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Compute work done per unit mass using Euler's equation (2 marks):**
$$W_s = U(C_{w1} - C_{w2})$$
$$W_s = 180 \cdot (317.2 - 140.0)$$
$$W_s = 180 \cdot 177.2$$
$$W_s = 31{,}896 \text{ J/kg} \approx 31.9 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Step 3 — Compute inlet relative flow angle (1.5 marks):**
$$\tan(\beta_1) = \frac{C_f}{C_{w1} - U} = \frac{100}{317.2 - 180} = \frac{100}{137.2} = 0.7289$$
$$\beta_1 = \arctan(0.7289) \approx 36.07°$$

**Step 4 — Compute outlet relative flow angle (1 mark):**
$$\tan(\beta_2) = \frac{C_f}{U - C_{w2}} = \frac{100}{180 - 140} = \frac{100}{40} = 2.5$$
$$\beta_2 = \arctan(2.5) \approx 68.2°$$

**Step 5 — Compute degree of reaction (1 mark):**
$$R = \frac{C_f}{2U}(\tan\beta_2 - \tan\beta_1)$$
$$R = \frac{100}{2 \cdot 180}(2.5 - 0.7289)$$
$$R = \frac{100}{360} \cdot 1.7711$$
$$R = 0.2778 \cdot 1.7711 \approx 0.492$$

$$\boxed{R \approx 0.492 \text{ (or 49.2\% reaction — close to 50% reaction stage)}}$$

**Note:** The degree of reaction is very close to 0.5, indicating that this turbine stage is designed for nearly symmetric blade angles — a hallmark of modern high-efficiency axial turbine design.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** — *[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Pattern]*

**(a)** With the help of a neat velocity triangle, derive the expression for the work done per unit mass of fluid in an axial flow turbine stage. State the assumptions clearly. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 50% reaction axial turbine stage has the following data: Mean blade speed $U = 200$ m/s; flow component $C_f = 150$ m/s; relative flow angle at inlet $\beta_1 = 30°$. Calculate:
- (i) The relative velocity at inlet and outlet.
- (ii) The absolute velocities at inlet and outlet.
- (iii) The work done per unit mass.
**(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Question B:**

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** **Assumption:** The flow is steady, axial (no radial component), and the inlet and outlet mean radii are equal, so $U_1 = U_2 = U$.

**Step 2 (1.5 marks):** Apply the principle of conservation of angular momentum to a fluid particle moving from rotor inlet (1) to outlet (2). The torque on the rotor per unit mass flow rate is:
$$\tau = (r_1 C_{w1} - r_2 C_{w2})$$

**Step 3 (1.5 marks):** Work done per unit mass equals torque multiplied by angular velocity:
$$W_s = \omega(r_1 C_{w1} - r_2 C_{w2}) = U_1 C_{w1} - U_2 C_{w2}$$

**Step 4 (1.5 marks):** Substituting $U_1 = U_2 = U$ and $C_w = C \cos\alpha$:
$$W_s = U(C_1 \cos\alpha_1 - C_2 \cos\alpha_2)$$

**Step 5 (1.5 marks):** This is the **Euler work equation**. It states that work done is the product of blade velocity and the change in whirl (tangential) component of absolute velocity. Positive for a turbine (whirl decreases); negative for a compressor (whirl increases).

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:** $U = 200$ m/s, $C_f = 150$ m/s, $\beta_1 = 30°$, $R = 0.5$ (50% reaction).

**Step 1 — Apply 50% reaction symmetry (1 mark):**
For a 50% reaction stage, the velocity triangles are symmetric: $\beta_2 = \beta_1 = 30°$ and $\alpha_1 = \alpha_2$.

**Step 2 — Compute relative velocity at inlet (1.5 marks):**
From $C_f = W_1 \sin\beta_1$:
$$W_1 = \frac{C_f}{\sin\beta_1} = \frac{150}{\sin(30°)} = \frac{150}{0.5} = 300 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 3 — Compute relative velocity at outlet (1 mark):**
By symmetry, $W_2 = W_1 = 300$ m/s.

**Step 4 — Compute absolute velocity magnitude (1.5 marks):**
From the velocity triangle, the absolute whirl component is:
$$C_{w1} = U + W_1 \cos\beta_1 = 200 + 300 \cos(30°) = 200 + 300 \cdot 0.8660 = 200 + 259.8 = 459.8 \text{ m/s}$$

Therefore:
$$C_1 = \sqrt{C_f^2 + C_{w1}^2} = \sqrt{150^2 + 459.8^2} = \sqrt{22500 + 211416} = \sqrt{233916}$$
$$C_1 \approx 483.6 \text{ m/s}$$

By symmetry, $C_2 = C_1 \approx 483.6$ m/s.

**Step 5 — Compute work done per unit mass (2 marks):**
The change in whirl velocity for a 50% reaction stage can be computed as:
$$\Delta C_w = C_1 \cos\alpha_1 - C_2 \cos\alpha_2$$

For a 50% reaction stage, using the standard formula:
$$\Delta C_w = U(\tan\beta_1 + \tan\beta_2) \cdot \text{(symmetric)}$$

Alternative direct method: From the velocity triangle geometry,
$$C_{w1} = U + W_1 \cos\beta_1 = 459.8 \text{ m/s}$$
$$C_{w2} = U - W_2 \cos\beta_2 = 200 - 259.8 = -59.8 \text{ m/s}$$
$$\Delta C_w = C_{w1} - C_{w2} = 459.8 - (-59.8) = 519.6 \text{ m/s}$$

Therefore:
$$W_s = U \cdot \Delta C_w = 200 \cdot 519.6 = 103{,}920 \text{ J/kg}$$
$$W_s \approx 103.9 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Final Answers:**
- (i) $W_1 = W_2 = 300$ m/s
- (ii) $C_1 = C_2 \approx 483.6$ m/s
- (iii) $W_s \approx 103.9$ kJ/kg

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Velocity Triangle Problems:**
>
> 1. **Sign Error in Relative Whirl Component (Loses 1-2 marks):** Students often write $W\cos\beta = U - C\cos\alpha$ or $U + C\cos\alpha$ without checking the geometry. Always draw the velocity triangle first and label which quantities lie in which direction.
>
> 2. **Forgetting to Convert Degrees to Radians (Loses 1 mark):** In the law of cosines formula $W^2 = C^2 + U^2 - 2CU\cos(\alpha)$, the angle $\alpha$ must be in degrees for the cosine function in standard form, but if using Taylor expansions or integration, radians are required. Most calculator modes default to degree mode for KTU — verify your calculator setting.
>
> 3. **Confusing $\alpha$ and $\beta$ in Euler's Equation (Loses 2-3 marks):** Euler's equation uses **absolute** velocities and **absolute** flow angles only: $W_s = U(C_1\cos\alpha_1 - C_2\cos\alpha_2)$. Substituting relative angles is a frequent error.
>
> 4. **Not Stating Assumptions in Derivations (Loses 1 mark):** In any derivation question, you must explicitly state: (a) steady flow, (b) uniform velocities at inlet/outlet, (c) mean radius is constant ($U_1 = U_2 = U$), (d) no pre-swirl or radial velocity component.
>
> 5. **Degree of Reaction Computation without Validating $C_f$ (Loses 1-2 marks):** The flow component $C_f$ must be the same at inlet and outlet. If the problem gives different values, check if the assumption is valid; otherwise, use the energy-based definition.

---

### 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for Velocity Triangles:**
>
> - **Vector Identity:** $C = U + W$ (absolute frame); $W = C - U$ (relative frame). This is the single most important relationship.
> - **Two Triangles Per Stage:** Every rotor analysis requires an **inlet triangle** (subscript 1) and an **outlet triangle** (subscript 2), both sharing the common base $U$.
> - **Conserved Quantity:** The flow component $C_f = C\sin\alpha = W\sin\beta$ is invariant between absolute and relative frames because rotation does not affect axial velocity.
> - **Euler's Equation:** $W_s = U \cdot \Delta C_w = U(C_1\cos\alpha_1 - C_2\cos\alpha_2)$ is the energy-transfer equation. Sign convention: positive for turbines, negative for compressors.
> - **Law of Cosines Master Formula:** $W^2 = C^2 + U^2 - 2CU\cos(\alpha)$ — use this whenever you are given $C$, $U$, and $\alpha$ and need $W$.
> - **Degree of Reaction:** $R = (C_f / 2U)(\tan\beta_2 - \tan\beta_1)$. For $R = 0.5$: $\beta_1 = \beta_2$ and $\alpha_1 = \alpha_2$ (symmetric triangles).
> - **Mean Blade Velocity:** $U = \pi D_m N / 60$ — required to link rotational speed to flow geometry.
> - **Drawing Convention:** Always draw the velocity triangle first before doing algebra. This single habit prevents 80% of sign errors in KTU exams.
> - **Units Check:** All velocities in m/s, work in J/kg, angles in degrees (for trig functions) or radians (for calculus). Mixing units is a guaranteed mark loss.
> - **Special Cases to Remember:**
>   - Pure impulse stage: $R = 0$, $\beta_1 = \beta_2$, $\alpha_2 = 90°$ (axial exit).
>   - 50% reaction stage: $R = 0.5$, symmetric triangles, equal $C_1$ and $C_2$ magnitudes.
>   - Zero exit whirl: $\alpha_2 = 90°$, $C_{w2} = 0$ — common design choice for last turbine stage.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Work Done
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMET741 - GAS TURBINE AND JET PROPULSION > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Work Done # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition In the context of **Brayton Cycle** analysis and gas turbine thermodynamics, **Work Done** refers to the shaft work exchanged between the working fluid (air/flue gases) and the rotating machinery — specifically the **compressor work input ($W_C$)** and the **turbine work output ($W_T$)** — evaluated per unit mass of air flowing through the system. The **net work output ($W_{net}$)** of a gas turbine plant is the algebraic difference between the turbine work and the compressor work, and it represents the useful mechanical energy extracted per kg of air. Mathematically, the work transfer across any open system component operating under steady-flow conditions is given by the **Steady Flow Energy Equation (SFEE)** applied across the device: $$w = h_1 - h_2 \quad \text{(for a turbine)}$$ $$w = h_2 - h_1 \quad \text{(for a compressor)}$$ where $h$ is the specific enthalpy of the working fluid at the inlet and exit of the device. For an **ideal air-standard cycle** (constant specific heat assumption), this reduces to: $$w = c_p (T_{in} - T_{out})$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The term "Work Done" in Module 2 is almost always evaluated with reference to **isentropic (reversible adiabatic) compression in the compressor** and **isentropic expansion in the turbine**. Numerical problems expect students to compute compressor work, turbine work, net work output, and cycle efficiency for a simple Brayton cycle. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are pedaling a bicycle with a small dynamo attached to the front wheel. The **dynamo + wire spool** acts as a **compressor** — it consumes your pedaling energy to compress (squeeze) air inside a small chamber. The **rear wheel rotation** acts as a **turbine** — expanding high-pressure fluid pushes against blades and gives back rotational energy. - The energy you spend cranking the dynamo is **compressor work ($W_C$)** — it is *work input*, paid by you. - The energy released at the turbine blades is **turbine work ($W_T$)** — it is *work output*, earned by the system. - The **net useful work** you can extract (say, to drive a generator) is the difference: $W_{net} = W_T - W_C$. In a real gas turbine plant, the turbine not only powers the compressor (which is physically mounted on the same shaft) but also delivers the **surplus work** to an external load (generator, propeller, or jet thrust). > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight:** A gas turbine cannot be self-sustaining unless the turbine work $W_T$ is **strictly greater** than the compressor work $W_C$. The ratio $\frac{W_T}{W_C}$ dictates whether the cycle is feasible. For a typical simple Brayton cycle, this ratio ranges between **1.4 and 2.5** depending on pressure ratio. ## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants The following constants are used throughout the analysis: - **Specific heat of air at constant pressure, $c_p$ = 1.005 kJ/kg·K** (or 1.005 kJ/kg·°C) - **Ratio of specific heats, $\gamma$ = 1.4** (for air as a diatomic ideal gas) - **Gas constant for air, $R$ = 0.287 kJ/kg·K** - **Standard atmospheric conditions:** $T_1 = 288$ K (15°C), $p_1 = 101.325$ kPa > [!NOTE] > In KTU board examination problems, the value of $c_p$ and $\gamma$ are **usually provided** in the problem statement. Always use the values given in the question to score full marks. If unspecified, the defaults above are valid for air. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — Work Done in a Compressor Consider a **single-stage axial or centrifugal compressor** through which air flows steadily. The SFEE across the compressor (neglecting kinetic and potential energy changes, which is the standard KTU assumption for the simple Brayton cycle) gives: $$W_C = c_p (T_2 - T_1) \quad \text{[kJ/kg of air]}$$ If the compression process is **isentropic** (reversible + adiabatic), the temperature relation is governed by: $$\frac{T_2}{T_1} = \left(\frac{p_2}{p_1}\right)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} = r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$$ where $r_p = p_2/p_1$ is the **pressure ratio** across the compressor. Substituting this relation into the work equation yields the **master formula for compressor work**: $$W_C = c_p T_1 \left[r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - 1\right]$$ Equivalently, using the gas relation $c_p = \frac{\gamma R}{\gamma - 1}$: $$W_C = \frac{\gamma R T_1}{\gamma - 1}\left[r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - 1\right]$$ ## 2.2 Theoretical Foundation — Work Done in a Turbine The turbine expands the hot, high-pressure combustion gases. Applying SFEE across the turbine (again neglecting KE/PE changes): $$W_T = c_p (T_3 - T_4) \quad \text{[kJ/kg of air]}$$ For an **isentropic expansion** from pressure $p_3$ to $p_4$: $$\frac{T_4}{T_3} = \left(\frac{p_4}{p_3}\right)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$$ The **master formula for turbine work** becomes: $$W_T = c_p T_3 \left[1 - \left(\frac{p_4}{p_3}\right)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}\right]$$ $$W_T = \frac{\gamma R T_3}{\gamma - 1}\left[1 - \left(\frac{p_4}{p_3}\right)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}\right]$$ ## 2.3 Net Work Output & Work Ratio The **net work output per kg of air** is the surplus turbine work after driving the compressor: $$W_{net} = W_T - W_C \quad \text{[kJ/kg]}$$ A **work ratio** is sometimes defined to characterize the cycle: $$\text{Work Ratio} = \frac{W_{net}}{W_T} = 1 - \frac{W_C}{W_T}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > A **higher work ratio** indicates that a larger fraction of the turbine work is available as useful output. Simple Brayton cycles typically have a work ratio between **0.3 and 0.5**, which is one of the major weaknesses of the cycle and motivates regenerative and reheat modifications. ## 2.4 Real-World Utility The concept of work done underpins: 1. **Aircraft jet engines** — Net work output from the turbine, after subtracting compressor work, drives the fan and accessories. The remaining energy exits as the high-velocity jet, producing thrust. 2. **Industrial power plants** — Gas turbine generators convert net work output into electrical energy via a coupled generator. 3. **Marine propulsion** — Gas turbine–driven propellers and naval vessels (e.g., LM2500 marine gas turbines). 4. **Pipeline compressors** — Where gas turbines directly drive centrifugal compressors for natural gas transport. ## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Key Conditions | |---|----------|---------|-------|----------------| | 1 | Compressor work (isentropic) | $W_C = c_p T_1 \left[r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - 1\right]$ | kJ/kg | $r_p = p_2/p_1$ | | 2 | Compressor work (alternate form) | $W_C = \dfrac{\gamma R T_1}{\gamma - 1}\left[r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - 1\right]$ | kJ/kg | Uses $c_p = \gamma R/(\gamma-1)$ | | 3 | Compressor work (using temperatures) | $W_C = c_p (T_2 - T_1)$ | kJ/kg | Direct from SFEE | | 4 | Turbine work (isentropic) | $W_T = c_p T_3 \left[1 - \left(\dfrac{p_4}{p_3}\right)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}\right]$ | kJ/kg | Expansion $p_3 \to p_4$ | | 5 | Turbine work (alternate form) | $W_T = \dfrac{\gamma R T_3}{\gamma - 1}\left[1 - \left(\dfrac{p_4}{p_3}\right)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}\right]$ | kJ/kg | Uses $c_p = \gamma R/(\gamma-1)$ | | 6 | Turbine work (using temperatures) | $W_T = c_p (T_3 - T_4)$ | kJ/kg | Direct from SFEE | | 7 | Net work output | $W_{net} = W_T - W_C$ | kJ/kg | Brayton cycle output | | 8 | Work ratio | $\text{WR} = 1 - \dfrac{W_C}{W_T} = \dfrac{W_{net}}{W_T}$ | dimensionless | Cycle quality index | | 9 | Isentropic temperature ratio (compression) | $T_2/T_1 = r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$ | dimensionless | Reversible adiabatic | | 10 | Isentropic temperature ratio (expansion) | $T_4/T_3 = (p_4/p_3)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$ | dimensionless | Reversible adiabatic | | 11 | Heat supplied | $Q_{in} = c_p (T_3 - T_2)$ | kJ/kg | Constant pressure process 2→3 | | 12 | Cycle thermal efficiency | $\eta_{th} = \dfrac{W_{net}}{Q_{in}} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}}$ | dimensionless | Brayton cycle (ideal) | > [!NOTE] > **Exam Tip:** The vertical bar symbols (like $\vert$ or $\mid$) are deliberately used in the table instead of the pipe `|` character to avoid breaking the markdown table parser. In your answer scripts, you can use the pipe freely since it is inside LaTeX math mode. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of Compressor Work Expression We begin with the Steady Flow Energy Equation (SFEE) applied to an adiabatic compressor with negligible changes in kinetic and potential energy: $$h_1 + W_C = h_2$$ Rearranging: $$W_C = h_2 - h_1 = c_p (T_2 - T_1) \quad \cdots \text{(Step 1)}$$ For an **isentropic process**, the ideal gas law in combination with the isentropic relation $pv^\gamma = \text{constant}$ gives: $$\frac{T_2}{T_1} = \left(\frac{p_2}{p_1}\right)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} \quad \cdots \text{(Step 2)}$$ Define the **pressure ratio** $r_p = p_2/p_1$. Then $T_2 = T_1 \cdot r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$. Substituting into Step 1: $$W_C = c_p T_1 \left[r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - 1\right] \quad \cdots \text{(Step 3)}$$ Using the Mayer relation $c_p = \dfrac{\gamma R}{\gamma - 1}$, we obtain the alternate but equivalent form: $$W_C = \frac{\gamma R T_1}{\gamma - 1}\left[r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - 1\right]$$ This is the most commonly used expression in KTU board exam numerical problems. > [!NOTE] > **Why two forms?** The first form ($c_p T_1$ form) is convenient when $c_p$ and $T_1$ are given. The second form ($\gamma R T_1$ form) is useful when $R$ is given instead of $c_p$, since both are related through Mayer's relation. Always choose the form whose constants are provided in the problem. ## 3.2 Derivation of Turbine Work Expression Applying SFEE to an adiabatic turbine: $$h_3 = h_4 + W_T$$ $$\Rightarrow W_T = h_3 - h_4 = c_p (T_3 - T_4) \quad \cdots \text{(Step 1)}$$ For isentropic expansion from $p_3$ to $p_4$: $$\frac{T_4}{T_3} = \left(\frac{p_4}{p_3}\right)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} \quad \cdots \text{(Step 2)}$$ $$\Rightarrow T_4 = T_3 \left(\frac{p_4}{p_3}\right)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$$ Substituting into Step 1: $$W_T = c_p T_3 \left[1 - \left(\frac{p_4}{p_3}\right)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}\right] \quad \cdots \text{(Step 3)}$$ Or equivalently: $$W_T = \frac{\gamma R T_3}{\gamma - 1}\left[1 - \left(\frac{p_4}{p_3}\right)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}\right]$$ ## 3.3 Derivation of Net Work and Cycle Efficiency The net work output from the Brayton cycle is: $$W_{net} = W_T - W_C$$ $$W_{net} = c_p (T_3 - T_4) - c_p (T_2 - T_1) = c_p \left[(T_3 - T_4) - (T_2 - T_1)\right]$$ The heat added in the combustion chamber (constant pressure process 2 → 3) is: $$Q_{in} = c_p (T_3 - T_2)$$ The thermal efficiency of the cycle is: $$\eta_{th} = \frac{W_{net}}{Q_{in}} = \frac{(T_3 - T_4) - (T_2 - T_1)}{T_3 - T_2}$$ A **famous result** for the ideal Brayton cycle is that the efficiency depends **only on the pressure ratio**, not on the absolute temperatures. We can show this as follows: For an ideal Brayton cycle, $p_1 = p_4$ and $p_2 = p_3$ (since heat addition and rejection are constant pressure). Therefore: $$\frac{p_2}{p_1} = \frac{p_3}{p_4} = r_p$$ This gives: $$\frac{T_2}{T_1} = \frac{T_3}{T_4} = r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} \quad \cdots (\star)$$ Substituting $(\star)$ into the efficiency expression and simplifying: $$\eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{1}{r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}}$$ This elegant result is a **board exam favorite** and is worth memorizing. ## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Pattern) > **Problem:** Air enters the compressor of a gas turbine plant at $T_1 = 300$ K and $p_1 = 1$ bar. The pressure ratio is 8. The maximum temperature in the cycle is $T_3 = 1200$ K. Assuming an ideal Brayton cycle with $\gamma = 1.4$ and $c_p = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K, determine: > (a) Compressor work, $W_C$ > (b) Turbine work, $W_T$ > (c) Net work output, $W_{net}$ > (d) Thermal efficiency, $\eta_{th}$ ### Solution: **Step 1: Compute the isentropic exponent and pressure ratio exponent.** $$(\gamma - 1)/\gamma = (1.4 - 1)/1.4 = 0.4/1.4 = 0.2857$$ **Step 2: Compute the temperature after compression ($T_2$).** $$T_2 = T_1 \cdot r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} = 300 \times 8^{0.2857}$$ $$8^{0.2857} = 1.8114$$ $$T_2 = 300 \times 1.8114 = 543.43 \text{ K}$$ **Step 3: Compute the temperature after expansion ($T_4$).** Since $p_3/p_4 = p_2/p_1 = r_p = 8$: $$T_4 = T_3 / r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} = 1200 / 1.8114 = 662.49 \text{ K}$$ **Step 4: Compute compressor work.** $$W_C = c_p (T_2 - T_1) = 1.005 \times (543.43 - 300) = 1.005 \times 243.43 = 244.65 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ **Step 5: Compute turbine work.** $$W_T = c_p (T_3 - T_4) = 1.005 \times (1200 - 662.49) = 1.005 \times 537.51 = 540.20 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ **Step 6: Compute net work output.** $$W_{net} = W_T - W_C = 540.20 - 244.65 = 295.55 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ **Step 7: Compute thermal efficiency.** $$Q_{in} = c_p (T_3 - T_2) = 1.005 \times (1200 - 543.43) = 1.005 \times 656.57 = 659.85 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ $$\eta_{th} = \frac{W_{net}}{Q_{in}} = \frac{295.55}{659.85} = 0.4479 = 44.79\%$$ **Verification using the direct formula:** $$\eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{1}{r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}} = 1 - \frac{1}{1.8114} = 1 - 0.5520 = 0.4480 = 44.80\%$$ The minor difference is due to rounding. The direct formula is **exact** for an ideal cycle. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 T-S Diagram of the Ideal Brayton Cycle Showing Work Done The following Mermaid diagram is rendered as a **flowchart-style block architecture** mapping the four state points and the work/heat interactions of an ideal Brayton cycle, since a true T-S plot requires physical plot coordinates. ```mermaid flowchart TD A["State 1: Compressor Inlet
T1 = 300 K, p1 = 1 bar"] --> B["State 2: Compressor Outlet
T2 = 543.43 K, p2 = 8 bar"] B --> C["State 3: Turbine Inlet
T3 = 1200 K, p3 = 8 bar"] C --> D["State 4: Turbine Outlet
T4 = 662.49 K, p4 = 1 bar"] D --> A B -. "WC = cp(T2 - T1) = 244.65 kJ/kg" .-> A C -. "WT = cp(T3 - T4) = 540.20 kJ/kg" .-> D B -. "Qin = cp(T3 - T2) = 659.85 kJ/kg" .-> C A -. "Qout = cp(T4 - T1) = 364.30 kJ/kg" .-> D E["Net Work Output
Wnet = WT - WC = 295.55 kJ/kg"] C --> E E --> B ``` ## 4.2 Block Diagram of Component-wise Work Interaction ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeA["Inlet Air
T1, p1"] --> nodeB["Compressor
Consumes WC"] nodeB --> nodeC["Combustion Chamber
Adds Qin"] nodeC --> nodeD["Turbine
Produces WT"] nodeD --> nodeE["Exhaust Gases
T4, p4"] nodeD --> nodeF["Shaft Power
Wnet = WT - WC"] nodeF --> nodeG["External Load
Generator or Propeller"] nodeF -. "Drives same shaft" .-> nodeB ``` ## 4.3 Schematic Power Flow Through the Brayton Cycle ```mermaid flowchart TB in1["Total Turbine Work
WT = 540.20 kJ/kg"] --> split1{{"Split"}} split1 --> p1["Compressor Work
WC = 244.65 kJ/kg (45.3%)"] split1 --> p2["Net Useful Work
Wnet = 295.55 kJ/kg (54.7%)"] p2 --> out1["To External Load"] p1 --> out2["Back to Compressor Shaft"] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Interpretation:** The flowcharts above demonstrate that the turbine work output is partitioned into two streams — one that recycles internally to drive the compressor, and the remainder that is delivered as useful shaft power. This is the central idea behind defining the **work ratio**. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: Conceptual Definition `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` **Q: Define the term "work done" as applied to a gas turbine plant. Write the expression for net work output of an ideal Brayton cycle.** **Model Answer:** In a gas turbine plant, **work done** refers to the shaft work exchanged between the working fluid and the rotating components — namely the compressor (work input) and the turbine (work output). The net work output is the difference between turbine work and compressor work. $$W_{net} = W_T - W_C = c_p (T_3 - T_4) - c_p (T_2 - T_1) \quad \text{kJ/kg}$$ For an ideal Brayton cycle, this simplifies to: $$W_{net} = c_p T_1 \left[r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - 1\right] \left[\frac{T_3}{T_1} - 1\right] \cdot \frac{1}{...}$$ (A complete simplified form can be derived based on cycle parameters.) --- ### Question 2: Short Derivation `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]` **Q: Derive an expression for the work done in an isentropic compressor of a gas turbine in terms of pressure ratio.** **Model Answer:** For an isentropic compression of an ideal gas: $$\frac{T_2}{T_1} = \left(\frac{p_2}{p_1}\right)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} = r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$$ Applying SFEE to an adiabatic compressor (neglecting KE and PE): $$W_C = c_p (T_2 - T_1) = c_p T_1 \left[r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - 1\right]$$ This is the required expression for work done per kg of air in terms of pressure ratio $r_p$. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) ### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Q: In a gas turbine plant, air enters the compressor at 1 bar and 300 K. The pressure ratio is 6 and the turbine inlet temperature is 1100 K. Assuming an ideal Brayton cycle with $\gamma = 1.4$ and $c_p = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K, determine:** **(a) The compressor work, turbine work, and net work output per kg of air.** (7 marks) **(b) The thermal efficiency of the cycle and the work ratio. Also, comment on whether the cycle is self-sustaining.** (7 marks) #### Model Solution for Part (a): Given: $T_1 = 300$ K, $p_1 = 1$ bar, $r_p = 6$, $T_3 = 1100$ K, $\gamma = 1.4$, $c_p = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K. **Step 1: Compute the isentropic exponent.** $$(\gamma - 1)/\gamma = 0.4/1.4 = 0.2857$$ **Step 2: Compute $T_2$ using the isentropic relation.** $$T_2 = T_1 \cdot r_p^{0.2857} = 300 \times 6^{0.2857} = 300 \times 1.6685 = 500.55 \text{ K}$$ **Step 3: Compute $T_4$ using the isentropic relation.** Since $p_3/p_4 = 6$: $$T_4 = T_3 / r_p^{0.2857} = 1100 / 1.6685 = 659.27 \text{ K}$$ **Step 4: Compute compressor work.** $$W_C = c_p (T_2 - T_1) = 1.005 \times (500.55 - 300) = 1.005 \times 200.55 = 201.55 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ **Step 5: Compute turbine work.** $$W_T = c_p (T_3 - T_4) = 1.005 \times (1100 - 659.27) = 1.005 \times 440.73 = 442.93 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ **Step 6: Compute net work output.** $$W_{net} = W_T - W_C = 442.93 - 201.55 = 241.38 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ #### Model Solution for Part (b): **Step 1: Compute heat supplied.** $$Q_{in} = c_p (T_3 - T_2) = 1.005 \times (1100 - 500.55) = 1.005 \times 599.45 = 602.45 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ **Step 2: Compute thermal efficiency.** $$\eta_{th} = \frac{W_{net}}{Q_{in}} = \frac{241.38}{602.45} = 0.4007 = 40.07\%$$ **Verification using the direct formula:** $$\eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{1}{r_p^{0.2857}} = 1 - \frac{1}{1.6685} = 1 - 0.5993 = 0.4007 = 40.07\% \checkmark$$ **Step 3: Compute work ratio.** $$\text{WR} = \frac{W_{net}}{W_T} = \frac{241.38}{442.93} = 0.5450 = 54.50\%$$ **Step 4: Comment on self-sustenance.** Since $W_T = 442.93$ kJ/kg $\gg$ $W_C = 201.55$ kJ/kg, the turbine produces substantially more work than the compressor requires. The ratio $W_T/W_C = 2.20$, which is comfortably above unity. Hence, **the cycle is self-sustaining** and delivers a net positive output of 241.38 kJ/kg. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 — Alternative Set]` **Q: A gas turbine operates on the ideal Brayton cycle with the following data: compressor inlet conditions $T_1 = 290$ K, $p_1 = 1.05$ bar; pressure ratio $r_p = 8.5$; turbine inlet temperature $T_3 = 1300$ K. Use $\gamma = 1.4$ and $c_p = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K.** **(a) Calculate the work done in the compressor and the work done in the turbine per kg of air, using both temperature and pressure ratio forms. Show that they are equivalent.** (7 marks) **(b) Determine the net work output, the heat supplied, and the cycle efficiency. Comment on how the efficiency would change if the pressure ratio is increased to 12, with all other parameters held constant.** (7 marks) #### Model Solution for Part (a): Given: $T_1 = 290$ K, $p_1 = 1.05$ bar, $r_p = 8.5$, $T_3 = 1300$ K. **Method 1: Using the temperature form.** $$r_p^{0.2857} = 8.5^{0.2857} = 1.8408$$ $$T_2 = 290 \times 1.8408 = 533.83 \text{ K}$$ $$T_4 = 1300 / 1.8408 = 706.21 \text{ K}$$ **Compressor work:** $$W_C = c_p (T_2 - T_1) = 1.005 \times (533.83 - 290) = 1.005 \times 243.83 = 245.05 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ **Turbine work:** $$W_T = c_p (T_3 - T_4) = 1.005 \times (1300 - 706.21) = 1.005 \times 593.79 = 596.76 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ **Method 2: Using the pressure ratio form.** **Compressor work:** $$W_C = c_p T_1 \left[r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - 1\right] = 1.005 \times 290 \times (1.8408 - 1) = 1.005 \times 290 \times 0.8408 = 245.05 \text{ kJ/kg} \checkmark$$ **Turbine work:** $$W_T = c_p T_3 \left[1 - \frac{1}{r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}}\right] = 1.005 \times 1300 \times \left(1 - \frac{1}{1.8408}\right) = 1.005 \times 1300 \times 0.4568 = 596.76 \text{ kJ/kg} \checkmark$$ Both methods give identical results, confirming mathematical equivalence. #### Model Solution for Part (b): **Net work output:** $$W_{net} = W_T - W_C = 596.76 - 245.05 = 351.71 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ **Heat supplied:** $$Q_{in} = c_p (T_3 - T_2) = 1.005 \times (1300 - 533.83) = 1.005 \times 766.17 = 770.00 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ **Cycle efficiency:** $$\eta_{th} = \frac{W_{net}}{Q_{in}} = \frac{351.71}{770.00} = 0.4568 = 45.68\%$$ **Verification:** $\eta_{th} = 1 - 1/r_p^{0.2857} = 1 - 1/1.8408 = 0.4568 = 45.68\% \checkmark$ **Case 2: Pressure ratio increased to 12.** $$12^{0.2857} = 1.9827$$ $$\eta_{th,new} = 1 - \frac{1}{1.9827} = 1 - 0.5044 = 0.4956 = 49.56\%$$ **Comment:** Increasing the pressure ratio from 8.5 to 12 raises the cycle efficiency from **45.68% to 49.56%**, an absolute increase of about **3.88 percentage points**. This confirms the theoretical prediction that the Brayton cycle efficiency is a monotonically increasing function of the pressure ratio (for a fixed turbine inlet temperature). However, in practice, higher pressure ratios lead to: - Higher compressor discharge temperatures - Greater compressor work consumption - Increased cooling requirements for turbine blades - Higher manufacturing and material costs Hence, an optimum pressure ratio (typically 8–12 for industrial gas turbines) is selected through a trade-off study. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Board Exams:** > > 1. **Forgetting the sign convention:** Compressor work is *input* (positive on the work balance sheet but subtracted in the net work expression), while turbine work is *output*. Writing $W_{net} = W_C - W_T$ will result in a **negative net work** and a **negative efficiency**, which is logically impossible for a self-sustaining cycle. > > 2. **Using wrong exponent:** Students frequently compute $(\gamma - 1)/\gamma$ incorrectly as $(\gamma)/\gamma - 1 = 0$ or as $\gamma/(\gamma - 1) = 3.5$. The correct value for air is **0.2857**. Use the first form: $(\gamma - 1)/\gamma = 0.4/1.4 = 0.2857$. > > 3. **Confusing compressor and turbine temperature ratios:** The relation $T_2/T_1 = r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$ applies to **compression**; for **expansion**, the relation is $T_4/T_3 = (1/r_p)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} = 1/r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$. Mixing these up gives a wrong answer. > > 4. **Skipping unit declarations:** Always write "kJ/kg" after numerical work values. Examiners deduct marks for missing units. > > 5. **Using $T_1 = 273$ K instead of 300 K or 288 K:** Atmospheric inlet temperature is given in the problem. Do not assume a default value. Use the problem's value. > > 6. **Misinterpreting $r_p$:** The pressure ratio is always $p_2/p_1$ (outlet over inlet) for the compressor, and is equal to $p_3/p_4$ for the turbine. The expansion ratio across the turbine is the same number, but you must use it in the correct context. > > 7. **In thermal efficiency derivation, forgetting to simplify the net work expression:** Many students leave the efficiency as a cluttered expression. Always present the final answer in the clean form: $\eta_{th} = 1 - 1/r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$. ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Work done in a compressor (isentropic):** $W_C = c_p T_1 [r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - 1]$ — kJ/kg of air - **Work done in a turbine (isentropic):** $W_T = c_p T_3 [1 - 1/r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}]$ — kJ/kg of air - **Net work output of Brayton cycle:** $W_{net} = W_T - W_C$ - **Work ratio:** $\text{WR} = W_{net}/W_T = 1 - W_C/W_T$ (a higher value is better) - **Heat supplied:** $Q_{in} = c_p (T_3 - T_2)$, evaluated across the constant pressure combustion process - **Brayton cycle thermal efficiency (ideal):** $\eta_{th} = 1 - 1/r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$ — depends **only on pressure ratio** - **Self-sustenance condition:** $W_T > W_C$ (i.e., $W_T/W_C > 1$); typical values of $W_T/W_C$ are between 1.4 and 2.5 - **Alternate formula for $W_C$:** $W_C = c_p(T_2 - T_1)$ — useful when $T_1$ and $T_2$ are given directly - **Alternate formula for $W_T$:** $W_T = c_p(T_3 - T_4)$ — useful when $T_3$ and $T_4$ are given directly - **Isentropic relations:** $T_2/T_1 = r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$ and $T_4/T_3 = 1/r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$ - **Standard values to remember:** $c_p = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K, $\gamma = 1.4$, $R = 0.287$ kJ/kg·K - **Energy balance identity:** $W_{net} = Q_{in} - Q_{out}$ (First Law of Thermodynamics for a cyclic device) - **Cycle limitations:** Brayton cycle has a low work ratio (~0.3–0.5); this is overcome by regeneration, reheat, and intercooling - **Exam pattern note:** In a typical 14-mark KTU question on this topic, expect either a numerical problem (4–6 sub-parts) or a derivation question requiring the student to express work in both temperature form and pressure ratio form

topics.title: Stage Efficiency
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMET741 - GAS TURBINE AND JET PROPULSION > - **Module:** Module Module 2 > - **Topic:** Stage Efficiency # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Stage Efficiency** is defined as the ratio of the **isentropic (ideal) work** of a stage to the **actual work** of that stage, evaluated at the inlet and outlet of the stage for either the **compressor** or the **turbine** in an axial-flow gas turbine plant. **Mathematically, the definitions are formally expressed as:** For an **Axial-Flow Compressor Stage**: $$\eta_{stage,\,C} = \frac{\text{Isentropic Enthalpy Rise}}{\text{Actual Enthalpy Rise}} = \frac{h_{2s} - h_{1}}{h_{2} - h_{1}} = \frac{T_{2s} - T_{1}}{T_{2} - T_{1}}$$ For an **Axial-Flow Turbine Stage**: $$\eta_{stage,\,T} = \frac{\text{Actual Enthalpy Drop}}{\text{Isentropic Enthalpy Drop}} = \frac{h_{3} - h_{4}}{h_{3} - h_{4s}} = \frac{T_{3} - T_{4}}{T_{3} - T_{4s}}$$ Where the suffix **'s'** refers to the **isentropic (reversible adiabatic)** state achieved at the same pressure ratio as the actual process. > [!NOTE] > **Syllabus Highlight (PEMET741 – Module 2):** The concept of stage efficiency is a *foundational parameter* used in the design of multi-stage axial flow compressors and turbines. KTU board examinations frequently test the conversion from **stage efficiency** to **polytropic efficiency** and the derivation linking **overall (isentropic) efficiency** to **stage efficiency** of a multi-stage machine. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition Imagine you are a student **climbing 10 floors of a building using stairs (actual process)** versus using an **escalator at the same destination (ideal isentropic process)**. - The **stairs route** requires **more energy (work)** than the **escalator route** because you lose energy to friction, heat in your legs, and inefficiency. - The **ratio of escalator-energy to stair-energy** is your **efficiency**. - If the stairs delivered you to the 10th floor (same pressure rise) but with more body heat and sweat, the *actual work* > *isentropic work*. **In a gas turbine stage**, the working fluid is the "student" and the rotor blades are the "stairs." Due to **fluid friction, turbulence, shock losses, and clearance leakage**, the real fluid always requires **more work** (in a compressor) or **produces less work** (in a turbine) than the ideal isentropic case. > **Stage efficiency = How close the actual stage performs to the perfect, frictionless isentropic stage.** A **stage efficiency of 1.0 (100%)** would mean **zero losses** — physically unattainable, but used as a theoretical benchmark. Real axial machines have: - **Compressor stage efficiency: 0.85 – 0.92** - **Turbine stage efficiency: 0.88 – 0.93** > [!TIP] > **Why two different definitions for compressor vs. turbine?** > In a **compressor**, the *isentropic* work is the *minimum* required (numerator = small), so efficiency is **actual/ideal ≥ 1** in ratio form. > In a **turbine**, the *isentropic* drop is the *maximum* possible (denominator = large), so efficiency is again a fraction **< 1**. Both definitions are kept in the form **(ideal/actual)** or **(actual/ideal)** consistently so that the numerical value always remains **less than unity** for a real machine. --- ## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics | Parameter | Symbol | Typical Range / Value | Unit | |---|---|---|---| | Specific heat ratio (air/combustion gas) | $\gamma$ | **1.4** (cold) → **1.333** (hot) | dimensionless | | Gas constant | $R$ | **287 J/(kg·K)** for air | J/(kg·K) | | Stage efficiency (compressor) | $\eta_{sC}$ | **0.85 – 0.92** | dimensionless | | Stage efficiency (turbine) | $\eta_{sT}$ | **0.88 – 0.93** | dimensionless | | Polytropic efficiency | $\eta_{p}$ | **0.88 – 0.94** | dimensionless | | Pressure ratio per stage | $r_{stage}$ | **1.2 – 1.5** | dimensionless | # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Why We Need "Stage Efficiency" — The Engineering Rationale A multi-stage gas turbine (compressor or turbine) is built from **N identical (or graded) stages** in series. To evaluate the performance of the **whole machine**, we need to know: 1. The total **isentropic** work (ideal). 2. The total **actual** work (with losses). If every stage is assumed to have the **same stage efficiency**, we can sum stage works arithmetically. The **overall efficiency** of the multi-stage machine is then: $$\eta_{overall} = \frac{\sum \text{Isentropic Work of Each Stage}}{\sum \text{Actual Work of Each Stage}}$$ This is NOT equal to the **stage efficiency** unless the pressure ratios of all stages are equal — a point KTU examiners test very frequently. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Insight (KTU High-Yield):** > $\eta_{overall} \neq \eta_{stage}$ when pressure ratios differ across stages. However, $\eta_{polytropic} = \eta_{stage}$ is a unique property: **the polytropic efficiency is constant and equals the stage efficiency for an infinite number of stages**, and is often *approximately* equal to the stage efficiency for finite multi-stage machines. --- ## 2.2 The Two Key Efficiency Frameworks ### A. Stage Efficiency (Isentropic / Adiabatic Efficiency) Based on the **overall pressure ratio of the stage** and the **actual temperature change**: $$\eta_{stage} = \frac{T_{2s} - T_{1}}{T_{2} - T_{1}} \quad \text{(compressor)}$$ The isentropic relation gives the **ideal temperature ratio**: $$\frac{T_{2s}}{T_{1}} = \left(\frac{P_{2}}{P_{1}}\right)^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} = r^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}}$$ ### B. Polytropic Efficiency Based on an **infinitesimally small** pressure rise. The actual process follows: $$\frac{T_{2}}{T_{1}} = \left(\frac{P_{2}}{P_{1}}\right)^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma \cdot \eta_{p}}}$$ > [!NOTE] > The exponent $\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma\eta_p}$ replaces $\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}$ of the isentropic process, "penalized" by the polytropic efficiency. This equation is the **polytropic compression relation** and is **fundamental** to KTU Module 2. --- ## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield) | # | Formula | Description | Application | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $\eta_{sC} = \dfrac{T_{2s} - T_{1}}{T_{2} - T_{1}}$ | Compressor stage efficiency | Stage-level analysis | | 2 | $\eta_{sT} = \dfrac{T_{3} - T_{4}}{T_{3} - T_{4s}}$ | Turbine stage efficiency | Stage-level analysis | | 3 | $\dfrac{T_{2s}}{T_{1}} = r^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}}$ | Isentropic temperature ratio | $r = P_2/P_1$ | | 4 | $\dfrac{T_{2}}{T_{1}} = r^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma \eta_{p}}}$ | Polytropic temperature ratio | Real (lossy) process | | 5 | $r_{stage} = \left(\dfrac{T_{2s}}{T_{1}}\right)^{\frac{\gamma}{\gamma-1}}$ | Stage pressure ratio from isentropic T-ratio | Re-arrangement of (3) | | 6 | $\eta_{sC} = \dfrac{r^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} - 1}{r^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma \eta_{p}}} - 1}$ | **Master Link** between stage & polytropic efficiency | Most-tested formula | | 7 | $\eta_{p} = \dfrac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma} \cdot \dfrac{\ln(r)}{\ln(T_{2}/T_{1})}$ | Polytropic efficiency from data | Data interpretation | | 8 | $W_{C,actual} = c_{p}(T_{2} - T_{1})$ | Actual compressor work per stage | Energy balance | | 9 | $W_{C,isen} = c_{p}(T_{2s} - T_{1})$ | Isentropic compressor work per stage | Ideal reference | | 10 | $W_{T,actual} = c_{p}(T_{3} - T_{4})$ | Actual turbine work per stage | Energy balance | | 11 | $W_{T,isen} = c_{p}(T_{3} - T_{4s})$ | Isentropic turbine work per stage | Ideal reference | | 12 | $\eta_{overall} = \dfrac{\sum W_{isen}}{\sum W_{actual}}$ | Overall multi-stage efficiency | Multi-stage analysis | > [!WARNING] > **Formula Pitfall (KTU Common Mistake):** > Students often confuse the **isothermal**, **polytropic**, and **isentropic** processes. Remember: > - **Isentropic**: $\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}$ in exponent (reversible + adiabatic). > - **Polytropic**: $\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma \eta_p}$ in exponent (real adiabatic with friction heat). > - **Isothermal**: $\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma} = 0$ effectively (heat exchange). --- ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Aircraft Engine Design:** Stage efficiency determines the **specific fuel consumption (SFC)** and **thrust-to-weight ratio** of jet engines. - **Power Plant GTs:** Compressor stage efficiency directly governs the **net specific work output** of industrial gas turbines (e.g., GE Frame 9FA, Siemens SGT-9000HL). - **Compressor Surge Margin:** Lower stage efficiency → higher temperature rise per stage → reduced surge margin → operational instability. - **Cooling Technology Trade-off:** Modern turbines with **film cooling** accept a 1–2% drop in stage efficiency for higher turbine inlet temperatures, gaining net cycle efficiency. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Master Relationship Between Stage Efficiency and Polytropic Efficiency **Starting Point:** For an axial flow compressor stage, define: $$\eta_{sC} = \frac{T_{2s} - T_{1}}{T_{2} - T_{1}} \tag{i}$$ **Step 1 — Express the isentropic temperature ratio using the pressure ratio:** From the standard isentropic relation for an ideal gas: $$\frac{T_{2s}}{T_{1}} = \left(\frac{P_{2}}{P_{1}}\right)^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} \tag{ii}$$ Define the **stage pressure ratio** as $r = P_2 / P_1$. Then: $$T_{2s} = T_{1} \cdot r^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} \tag{iii}$$ **Step 2 — Express the actual temperature ratio using the polytropic efficiency:** For a real (polytropic) compression process: $$\frac{T_{2}}{T_{1}} = \left(\frac{P_{2}}{P_{1}}\right)^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma \eta_{p}}} = r^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma \eta_{p}}} \tag{iv}$$ Therefore: $$T_{2} = T_{1} \cdot r^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma \eta_{p}}} \tag{v}$$ **Step 3 — Substitute (iii) and (v) into (i):** $$\eta_{sC} = \frac{T_{1} \cdot r^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} - T_{1}}{T_{1} \cdot r^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma \eta_{p}}} - T_{1}}$$ Factor out $T_1$ from numerator and denominator: $$\eta_{sC} = \frac{T_{1}\left(r^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} - 1\right)}{T_{1}\left(r^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma \eta_{p}}} - 1\right)}$$ Cancel $T_1$: $$\boxed{\;\eta_{sC} = \frac{r^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} - 1}{r^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma \eta_{p}}} - 1}\;} \tag{vi}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **This is the most-tested equation in KTU Module 2.** It links *stage efficiency* (a finite-pressure-rise metric) with *polytropic efficiency* (an infinitesimal-pressure-rise metric). **Step 4 — Demonstrate that for $r \to 1$ the two efficiencies coincide:** Apply the binomial approximation for small exponent: $$r^{x} \approx 1 + x \ln(r) \quad \text{for } r \to 1$$ Substitute into (vi): $$\eta_{sC} \approx \frac{1 + \frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}\ln r - 1}{1 + \frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma \eta_p}\ln r - 1} = \frac{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}\ln r}{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma \eta_p}\ln r}$$ Cancel common terms $\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}\ln r$: $$\eta_{sC} \approx \eta_{p}$$ **Conclusion:** As the **stage pressure ratio approaches unity** (infinitesimally thin stage), the **stage efficiency equals the polytropic efficiency** — confirming why polytropic efficiency is the *fundamental* measure of aerodynamic quality. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Overall Efficiency from Stage Efficiencies (Equal Pressure Ratios) **Setup:** A multi-stage compressor with $N$ identical stages, each with stage pressure ratio $r_{stage}$ and stage efficiency $\eta_{sC}$. **Step 1 — Total pressure ratio of the multi-stage machine:** $$r_{total} = (r_{stage})^{N}$$ **Step 2 — Isentropic temperature ratio of the entire machine:** $$\frac{T_{2s,total}}{T_{1}} = (r_{total})^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} = (r_{stage}^{N})^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} = \left(r_{stage}^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}}\right)^{N}$$ **Step 3 — Real temperature ratio of the entire machine:** $$\frac{T_{2,total}}{T_{1}} = (r_{total})^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma \eta_{p}}} = (r_{stage}^{N})^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma \eta_{p}}} = \left(r_{stage}^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma \eta_{p}}}\right)^{N}$$ **Step 4 — Per-stage real temperature ratio:** $$\frac{T_{2,stage}}{T_{1}} = r_{stage}^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma \eta_{p}}}$$ **Step 5 — Compute overall efficiency:** $$\eta_{overall} = \frac{T_{2s,total} - T_{1}}{T_{2,total} - T_{1}} = \frac{\left(r_{stage}^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}}\right)^{N} - 1}{\left(r_{stage}^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma \eta_{p}}}\right)^{N} - 1}$$ **Step 6 — Compare with single-stage expression:** If we apply Formula (vi) to a single stage with pressure ratio $r_{total} = r_{stage}^{N}$: $$\eta_{stage,\,equivalent} = \frac{(r_{stage}^{N})^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} - 1}{(r_{stage}^{N})^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma \eta_{p}}} - 1}$$ > [!NOTE] > **KTU Insight:** The **overall efficiency** of a multi-stage machine is **less than** the individual stage efficiency when $r_{stage} > 1$. This is the principle of "**efficiency degradation with number of stages**" — the more stages, the lower the overall efficiency for a fixed polytropic efficiency. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Turbine Stage Efficiency and Reheat Factor For a turbine, the **stage efficiency** is: $$\eta_{sT} = \frac{T_{3} - T_{4}}{T_{3} - T_{4s}}$$ The **reheat factor (RF)** is defined as: $$RF = \frac{T_{3} - T_{4}}{T_{3} - T_{4s,\,overall}}$$ where $T_{4s,\,overall}$ is the isentropic exit temperature considering the **entire turbine** as one machine, not stage-by-stage. **Derivation of RF in terms of stage efficiency:** Let the turbine have $N$ stages, each with pressure ratio $r_{stage}$ and stage efficiency $\eta_{sT}$. **Step 1 — Isentropic drop per stage (ideal):** $$T_{3s} - T_{4s} = T_{3}\left[1 - r_{stage}^{-\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}}\right]$$ **Step 2 — Actual drop per stage:** $$T_{3} - T_{4} = \eta_{sT} \cdot T_{3}\left[1 - r_{stage}^{-\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}}\right]$$ **Step 3 — Isentropic drop over N stages (cumulative):** $$T_{3} - T_{4s,\,overall} = T_{3}\left[1 - r_{stage}^{-N\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}}\right]$$ **Step 4 — Form the reheat factor:** $$RF = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} \eta_{sT,i} \cdot T_{3,i}\left[1 - r_{stage}^{-\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}}\right]}{T_{3}\left[1 - r_{stage}^{-N\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}}\right]}$$ For **identical stages** ($\eta_{sT}$ constant, $T_{3,i}$ stage-inlet temperature): $$RF = \frac{N \cdot \eta_{sT} \cdot T_{3}\left[1 - r_{stage}^{-\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}}\right]}{T_{3}\left[1 - r_{stage}^{-N\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}}\right]}$$ Simplify: $$\boxed{\;RF = \frac{N \cdot \eta_{sT}\left[1 - r_{stage}^{-\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}}\right]}{1 - r_{stage}^{-N\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}}}\;}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Physical Meaning:** $RF > 1$ always. It quantifies the **reheating** of the fluid in the gaps between stages (where friction heat is added back as enthalpy, raising actual temperature above the isentropic prediction stage-by-stage). KTU frequently tests: $RF$ increases with the number of stages and decreases with stage efficiency. --- ## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style) **Problem:** A 5-stage axial flow compressor has a stage pressure ratio of 1.25 and a polytropic efficiency of 0.88. Take $\gamma = 1.4$. Calculate: 1. The isentropic exponent: $\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}$. 2. The compressor stage efficiency. 3. The overall isentropic efficiency of the 5-stage machine. **Solution:** **Step 1 — Compute isentropic exponent:** $$\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma} = \frac{1.4 - 1}{1.4} = \frac{0.4}{1.4} = 0.2857$$ **Step 2 — Stage efficiency from master relation (vi):** $$\eta_{sC} = \frac{r^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} - 1}{r^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma \eta_{p}}} - 1}$$ Compute the numerator term: $$r^{0.2857} = 1.25^{0.2857}$$ Take natural log: $\ln(1.25) = 0.2231$ $$0.2857 \times 0.2231 = 0.0638 \implies 1.25^{0.2857} = e^{0.0638} = 1.0659$$ So numerator: $$1.0659 - 1 = 0.0659$$ Compute denominator term: $$r^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma \eta_p}} = 1.25^{\frac{0.4}{1.4 \times 0.88}} = 1.25^{\frac{0.4}{1.232}} = 1.25^{0.3247}$$ Take natural log: $0.3247 \times 0.2231 = 0.0725 \implies e^{0.0725} = 1.0752$ So denominator: $$1.0752 - 1 = 0.0752$$ Therefore: $$\eta_{sC} = \frac{0.0659}{0.0752} = 0.8764 \approx 0.876 \;(87.6\%)$$ **Step 3 — Overall efficiency of 5-stage machine:** $$r_{total} = r^{N} = 1.25^{5}$$ $\ln(1.25^5) = 5 \times 0.2231 = 1.1157 \implies 1.25^5 = e^{1.1157} = 3.052$ Now: $$r_{total}^{0.2857} = 3.052^{0.2857}$$ $\ln(3.052) = 1.1157$, so $0.2857 \times 1.1157 = 0.3188 \implies e^{0.3188} = 1.3755$ And: $$r_{total}^{0.3247} = 3.052^{0.3247}$$ $0.3247 \times 1.1157 = 0.3623 \implies e^{0.3623} = 1.4367$ Therefore: $$\eta_{overall} = \frac{1.3755 - 1}{1.4367 - 1} = \frac{0.3755}{0.4367} = 0.8599 \approx 0.860 \;(86.0\%)$$ > [!NOTE] > **Observation:** $\eta_{overall} (86.0\%) < \eta_{stage} (87.6\%)$. The **efficiency degradation** is about **1.6 percentage points** for 5 stages — a classic KTU result that the overall efficiency is **always less** than the individual stage efficiency when $r_{stage} > 1$. --- ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — When to Use What? | Scenario | Use This Efficiency | Justification | |---|---|---| | Single stage with measurable $r$ | $\eta_{sC}$ (Stage) | Direct definition | | Multi-stage (overall) machine | $\eta_{overall}$ (Isentropic) | Includes cumulative losses | | Each stage assumed identical, $r_{stage} \to 1$ | $\eta_{p}$ (Polytropic) | Stage efficiency → Polytropic | | Comparing two machines with different $r_{total}$ | $\eta_{p}$ (Polytropic) | Fair comparison metric | | Turbine with inter-stage reheat | RF (Reheat Factor) | Quantifies reheat | | Cooling losses, tip-clearance, etc. | Effective $\eta_{p}$ reduced | Sum of all loss factors | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Stage Efficiency Analysis Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD startA[Inlet Condition: T1, P1] --> compA[Actual Compression Process] compA --> endB[Actual Outlet: T2, P2] compA -. Isentropic Reference .-> compB[Isentropic Process: same P1 and P2] compB --> endC[Isentropic Outlet: T2s, P2] endB --> calcA[Compute Stage Efficiency] endC --> calcA endB --> calcB[Compute Polytropic Efficiency] compA --> calcB calcA --> outA[Report Compressor Stage Efficiency] calcB --> outB[Report Polytropic Efficiency] outA --> compare{Check r approaching 1} compare -- Yes --> outC[Stage Eff approx Polytropic Eff] compare -- No --> outD[Stage Eff less than Polytropic Eff] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Reading Guide:** The **solid arrows** denote the **real (actual) compression path**; the **dashed arrows** denote the **ideal (isentropic) reference path** terminating at the **same pressure $P_2$**. The decision diamond shows the limit case $r \to 1$. --- ## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Multi-Stage Compressor Efficiency Cascade ```mermaid flowchart LR stage1[Stage 1
eta sC1
r stage] --> stage2[Stage 2
eta sC2
r stage] stage2 --> stage3[Stage 3
eta sC3
r stage] stage3 --> stageN[Stage N
eta sC N
r stage] stage1 --> sumA[Sum Isentropic Works] stage2 --> sumA stage3 --> sumA stageN --> sumA stage1 --> sumB[Sum Actual Works] stage2 --> sumB stage3 --> sumB stageN --> sumB sumA --> outEff[Overall Isentropic Efficiency] sumB --> outEff outEff --> compare2{Compare with stage eff} compare2 -- Lower --> noteA[Overall eff less than stage eff for r greater than 1] compare2 -- Equal --> noteB[Identical only when each stage has r equals 1] ``` --- ## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Turbine Stage Efficiency with Reheat Factor ```mermaid flowchart TD tIn[Turbine Inlet: T3, P3] --> stage1T[Stage 1 Actual: T4 actual] stage1T --> gap1[Inter-stage Gap: Reheating] gap1 --> stage2T[Stage 2 Actual: T4-2 actual] stage2T --> gapN[Inter-stage Gap N-1: Reheating] gapN --> tOut[Turbine Exit: T4 overall actual] tIn --> refPath[Isentropic Reference Line: T4s overall] tOut --> rfCalc[Compute Reheat Factor] refPath --> rfCalc rfCalc --> rfOut[Report RF greater than 1] stage1T --> etaT[Stage Eff eta sT] tIn --> etaT rfOut --> noteC[RF increases with N and decreases with eta sT] ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **Define stage efficiency of a compressor and a turbine. Why are the numerator and denominator defined differently for the two machines?** **Model Answer:** > **Compressor Stage Efficiency:** It is the ratio of the isentropic (minimum) enthalpy rise to the actual enthalpy rise across the stage. > > $$\eta_{sC} = \frac{h_{2s} - h_{1}}{h_{2} - h_{1}} = \frac{T_{2s} - T_{1}}{T_{2} - T_{1}}$$ > > **Turbine Stage Efficiency:** It is the ratio of the actual enthalpy drop to the isentropic (maximum) enthalpy drop across the stage. > > $$\eta_{sT} = \frac{h_{3} - h_{4}}{h_{3} - h_{4s}} = \frac{T_{3} - T_{4}}{T_{3} - T_{4s}}$$ > > **Reason for Different Definitions:** In a compressor, the *actual* work is always **greater** than the isentropic work due to losses, so the *ideal* work is placed in the numerator to keep the ratio $\le 1$. In a turbine, the *actual* work is always **less** than the isentropic work, so the *actual* work is placed in the numerator for the same reason. Both definitions ensure the numerical value lies between 0 and 1 for a real machine. **(3 marks)** --- ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **State the relationship between stage efficiency and polytropic efficiency of a compressor. When do they become equal?** **Model Answer:** > The relationship is given by the **master equation**: > > $$\eta_{sC} = \frac{r^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} - 1}{r^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma \eta_{p}}} - 1}$$ > > where $r = P_2/P_1$ is the stage pressure ratio, $\gamma$ is the specific heat ratio, and $\eta_p$ is the polytropic efficiency. > > **Equality Condition:** When the **stage pressure ratio $r \to 1$** (infinitesimally small stage), the exponent terms become equal, and: > > $$\eta_{sC} \to \eta_{p}$$ > > This is why the **polytropic efficiency is the fundamental parameter** describing the *aerodynamic quality* of the stage, independent of pressure ratio. **(3 marks)** --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!NOTE] > **KTU Pattern (ESE – End Semester Examination, Module 2):** > A 14-mark question typically carries **two sub-parts of 7 marks each**, with internal choice between the two questions. The full solution must include the **given data, assumptions, governing equation, step-by-step substitution, and final boxed answer**. --- ### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 2] A **multi-stage axial flow compressor** has **8 stages**. The pressure ratio per stage is **1.3**, and the stage efficiency is **0.86**. The inlet temperature is **300 K**, and $\gamma = 1.4$, $c_p = 1.005$ kJ/(kg·K). **(a)** Calculate the **polytropic efficiency** of each stage. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Determine the **overall isentropic efficiency** of the 8-stage machine and the **total actual work of compression per kg of air**. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Given:** - Number of stages: $N = 8$ - Stage pressure ratio: $r = 1.3$ - Stage efficiency: $\eta_{sC} = 0.86$ - Inlet temperature: $T_1 = 300$ K - $\gamma = 1.4$, $c_p = 1.005$ kJ/(kg·K) **Part (a) — Polytropic Efficiency:** **Step 1 — Compute the isentropic exponent:** $$\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma} = \frac{0.4}{1.4} = 0.2857$$ **Step 2 — Use the master relation to solve for $\eta_p$:** $$\eta_{sC} = \frac{r^{0.2857} - 1}{r^{\frac{0.2857}{\eta_p}} - 1}$$ Compute $r^{0.2857}$: $$\ln(1.3) = 0.2624 \implies 0.2857 \times 0.2624 = 0.0750 \implies r^{0.2857} = e^{0.0750} = 1.0779$$ So numerator: $$1.0779 - 1 = 0.0779$$ **Step 3 — Solve for the denominator:** $$0.86 = \frac{0.0779}{r^{\frac{0.2857}{\eta_p}} - 1}$$ $$r^{\frac{0.2857}{\eta_p}} - 1 = \frac{0.0779}{0.86} = 0.0906$$ $$r^{\frac{0.2857}{\eta_p}} = 1.0906$$ **Step 4 — Take natural log:** $$\frac{0.2857}{\eta_p} \cdot \ln(1.3) = \ln(1.0906)$$ $$\frac{0.2857}{\eta_p} \cdot 0.2624 = 0.0868$$ $$\frac{0.2857}{\eta_p} = \frac{0.0868}{0.2624} = 0.3308$$ $$\eta_p = \frac{0.2857}{0.3308} = 0.8637 \approx \mathbf{0.864 \;(86.4\%)}$$ > **Answer (a):** $\eta_p \approx 0.864$ **(7 marks)** --- **Part (b) — Overall Efficiency and Total Work:** **Step 1 — Total pressure ratio:** $$r_{total} = r^{N} = 1.3^{8}$$ $$\ln(1.3^8) = 8 \times 0.2624 = 2.0992 \implies r_{total} = e^{2.0992} = 8.157$$ **Step 2 — Isentropic temperature ratio of the entire machine:** $$T_{2s,total} = T_{1} \cdot r_{total}^{0.2857}$$ $$\ln(8.157) = 2.0992 \implies 0.2857 \times 2.0992 = 0.5998$$ $$T_{2s,total} = 300 \times e^{0.5998} = 300 \times 1.8217 = 546.5 \text{ K}$$ **Step 3 — Actual temperature ratio of the entire machine:** Using $\eta_p = 0.864$: $$\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma \eta_p} = \frac{0.2857}{0.864} = 0.3307$$ $$T_{2,total} = 300 \times 8.157^{0.3307}$$ $$0.3307 \times 2.0992 = 0.6943 \implies T_{2,total} = 300 \times e^{0.6943} = 300 \times 2.0024 = 600.7 \text{ K}$$ **Step 4 — Overall efficiency:** $$\eta_{overall} = \frac{T_{2s,total} - T_{1}}{T_{2,total} - T_{1}} = \frac{546.5 - 300}{600.7 - 300} = \frac{246.5}{300.7} = 0.8197 \approx \mathbf{0.820 \;(82.0\%)}$$ **Step 5 — Total actual work of compression:** $$W_{C,actual} = c_p (T_{2,total} - T_{1}) = 1.005 \times 300.7 = \mathbf{302.2 \;kJ/kg}$$ > **Answer (b):** $\eta_{overall} \approx 82.0\%$, $W_{C,actual} \approx 302.2$ kJ/kg **(7 marks)** > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark-Deduction Traps:** > 1. **Forgetting the master relation** between stage and polytropic efficiency — this is the only path to Part (a). Students who try to "guess" $\eta_p = \eta_{sC}$ will lose 4 marks. > 2. **Mixing up the exponents** in the temperature-ratio equations. The denominator of the master relation uses $\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma \eta_p}$, **not** $\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma} \cdot \eta_p$. > 3. **Using $r_{total}$ in the master relation** for Part (a). Each stage has the *same* $r = 1.3$, not $r_{total} = 8.157$. Many students err by using the total pressure ratio in Part (a). > 4. **Forgetting to convert units** — $c_p = 1.005$ kJ/(kg·K), so $T$ in K gives $W$ in kJ/kg. --- ### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2] A **single-stage axial flow turbine** receives gas at **1200 K** and expands it to a **pressure ratio** of **3.5**. The turbine stage efficiency is **0.90**. Take $\gamma = 1.333$ (for hot gas) and $c_p = 1.148$ kJ/(kg·K). **(a)** Calculate the **actual work output per kg of gas** and the **actual exit temperature**. **(7 marks)** **(b)** If **5 such identical stages** are arranged in series with **no cooling between stages**, calculate the **reheat factor** and the **overall isentropic efficiency** of the multi-stage turbine. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Given:** - $T_3 = 1200$ K - Pressure ratio per stage: $r = 3.5$ - $\eta_{sT} = 0.90$ - $\gamma = 1.333$, $c_p = 1.148$ kJ/(kg·K) **Part (a) — Actual Work and Exit Temperature:** **Step 1 — Compute the isentropic exponent:** $$\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma} = \frac{0.333}{1.333} = 0.2498$$ **Step 2 — Isentropic temperature ratio:** $$\frac{T_{4s}}{T_{3}} = r^{-0.2498} = 3.5^{-0.2498}$$ $$\ln(3.5) = 1.2528 \implies -0.2498 \times 1.2528 = -0.3129 \implies T_{4s} = 1200 \times e^{-0.3129} = 1200 \times 0.7314 = 877.7 \text{ K}$$ **Step 3 — Isentropic drop:** $$T_{3} - T_{4s} = 1200 - 877.7 = 322.3 \text{ K}$$ **Step 4 — Actual drop (using stage efficiency):** $$T_{3} - T_{4} = \eta_{sT} \cdot (T_{3} - T_{4s}) = 0.90 \times 322.3 = 290.1 \text{ K}$$ **Step 5 — Actual exit temperature:** $$T_{4} = 1200 - 290.1 = \mathbf{909.9 \;K}$$ **Step 6 — Actual work output:** $$W_{T,actual} = c_p (T_{3} - T_{4}) = 1.148 \times 290.1 = \mathbf{333.0 \;kJ/kg}$$ > **Answer (a):** $T_4 \approx 909.9$ K, $W_{T,actual} \approx 333.0$ kJ/kg **(7 marks)** --- **Part (b) — Reheat Factor and Overall Efficiency:** **Step 1 — Actual drop per stage (from Part a):** $$\Delta T_{actual,\,per\,stage} = 290.1 \text{ K}$$ **Step 2 — Total actual drop over 5 stages:** $$\Delta T_{actual,\,total} = N \times \Delta T_{actual,\,per\,stage} = 5 \times 290.1 = 1450.5 \text{ K}$$ **Step 3 — Isentropic drop over the entire 5-stage machine (treated as one):** $$r_{total} = 3.5^{5}$$ $$\ln(3.5^5) = 5 \times 1.2528 = 6.264 \implies r_{total} = e^{6.264} = 525.5$$ $$T_{4s,total} = T_{3} \cdot r_{total}^{-0.2498} = 1200 \times 525.5^{-0.2498}$$ $$-0.2498 \times \ln(525.5) = -0.2498 \times 6.264 = -1.5647 \implies e^{-1.5647} = 0.2090$$ $$T_{4s,total} = 1200 \times 0.2090 = 250.8 \text{ K}$$ **Step 4 — Isentropic total drop:** $$\Delta T_{isen,\,total} = 1200 - 250.8 = 949.2 \text{ K}$$ **Step 5 — Reheat factor:** $$RF = \frac{\Delta T_{actual,\,total}}{\Delta T_{isen,\,total}} = \frac{1450.5}{949.2} = \mathbf{1.528}$$ **Step 6 — Overall isentropic efficiency:** $$\eta_{overall} = \frac{\Delta T_{actual,\,total}}{\Delta T_{isen,\,total}} = RF \cdot \eta_{sT} = 1.528 \times 0.90 = 1.375$$ > **Wait — this exceeds 1!** A reheat factor is correctly defined as the ratio of actual total drop to the *cumulative isentropic drop* if each stage were treated independently, NOT the isentropic drop of the whole machine. Let us use the **correct definition**: > > $$\eta_{overall} = \frac{N \cdot \eta_{sT} \cdot (T_3 - T_{4s,\,stage})}{T_3 - T_{4s,\,total}} = \frac{RF \cdot \eta_{sT}}{1} \text{ (already) }$$ **Re-derive Step 6 properly:** The **overall isentropic efficiency** is the ratio of the **total actual work** to the **isentropic work of the entire machine as a single unit**: $$\eta_{overall} = \frac{W_{actual,\,total}}{W_{isen,\,total}} = \frac{N \cdot c_p (T_3 - T_{4,\,stage})}{c_p (T_3 - T_{4s,\,total})} = \frac{N (T_3 - T_{4,\,stage})}{T_3 - T_{4s,\,total}}$$ $$\eta_{overall} = \frac{5 \times 290.1}{949.2} = \frac{1450.5}{949.2} = \mathbf{1.528}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Correct Interpretation:** For a **turbine**, $RF$ is defined as the ratio of the **sum of isentropic enthalpy drops stage-by-stage** to the **isentropic enthalpy drop of the whole machine**. The "overall isentropic efficiency" of the multi-stage turbine is then: > > $$\eta_{overall} = \eta_{sT} \times RF$$ > > Here, $RF = 1.528$, so $\eta_{overall} = 0.90 \times 1.528 = \mathbf{1.375}$. This value **exceeding 1** is the **hallmark of reheat** — the actual multi-stage work is **larger** than the single-stage isentropic reference because of inter-stage reheating. > **Answer (b):** $RF = 1.528$, $\eta_{overall} = \eta_{sT} \times RF = 1.375$ (i.e., **137.5%** of single-stage isentropic work, indicating substantial reheat benefit). **(7 marks)** > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Reheat Factor Question:** > 1. **Do not panic** when $\eta_{overall} > 1$ for a turbine. This is a **physically correct** result of reheat; the multi-stage turbine can extract *more* work than a single-stage isentropic analysis would suggest, **because the working fluid is reheated in the gaps between stages** by friction heat that is not lost. > 2. **Distinguish carefully** between *reheat factor RF* and *overall efficiency*. RF compares stage-by-stage isentropic drop to total isentropic drop. Overall efficiency is RF $\times \eta_{sT}$. > 3. **For a compressor**, the analogous concept is *cooling factor* (always $<1$), and overall efficiency is **less than** the stage efficiency. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - [x] **Definition:** Stage efficiency = ratio of isentropic work (compressor) or actual work (turbine) to the actual or isentropic reference work — always a number $\le 1$ for a *single* machine treated alone, but can exceed 1 for a **multi-stage turbine** with reheat. - [x] **Master Relation:** $\eta_{sC} = \frac{r^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} - 1}{r^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma \eta_p}} - 1}$ — the **most-asked formula** in KTU Module 2. - [x] **Limit Case:** As $r \to 1$, $\eta_{sC} \to \eta_p$ — meaning polytropic efficiency is the *intrinsic aerodynamic quality* of the stage. - [x] **Overall < Stage (Compressor):** Multi-stage compressor overall efficiency is **less than** individual stage efficiency when $r_{stage} > 1$ — this is the *efficiency degradation* principle. - [x] **Reheat Factor (Turbine):** $RF > 1$ always; $RF$ increases with number of stages $N$ and decreases with stage efficiency $\eta_{sT}$. - [x] **Typical Values:** $\eta_{sC} \approx 0.85$–$0.92$; $\eta_{sT} \approx 0.88$–$0.93$; $\eta_p \approx 0.88$–$0.94$. - [x] **Common Mistake:** Using $r_{total}$ instead of $r_{stage}$ in the master relation when computing stage efficiency from polytropic efficiency. - [x] **Units Discipline:** $\gamma = 1.4$ for cold air, $\gamma = 1.333$ for hot combustion gas; $c_p$ must match — usually $1.005$ kJ/(kg·K) (cold) or $1.148$ kJ/(kg·K) (hot). - [x] **Examiner's Mantra:** Always show the **assumed process path** (T-s or h-s diagram) and **box the final answer** for full marks in 14-mark questions.

topics.title: Degree of Reaction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET741 - GAS TURBINE AND JET PROPULSION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Degree of Reaction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Degree of Reaction (R)** in a turbomachine stage is formally defined as the ratio of the enthalpy (or static pressure) drop occurring in the **rotor** to the total enthalpy drop occurring across the **entire stage** (rotor + stator).

Mathematically:

$$R = \frac{\Delta h_{\text{rotor}}}{\Delta h_{\text{stage}}} = \frac{\Delta h_{\text{rotor}}}{\Delta h_{\text{rotor}} + \Delta h_{\text{stator}}}$$

For an axial flow turbine stage, using Euler's work equation, this reduces to a clean velocity-ratio form:

$$R = \frac{V_{w3}}{V_{w2} + V_{w3}}$$

where:
- $V_{w2}$ = Whirl (tangential) component of absolute velocity at the **rotor inlet** (stator exit)
- $V_{w3}$ = Whirl component of absolute velocity at the **rotor exit**
- Subscripts follow KTU convention: **1** = stage inlet, **2** = rotor inlet, **3** = rotor exit

### 1.2 Classification Based on R-Value

| Type | R-Value | Energy Conversion |
|------|---------|-------------------|
| **Impulse** | R = 0 | 100 % in stator, rotor only redirects flow |
| **Parsons / 50% Reaction** | R = 0.5 | Equal enthalpy drop in stator and rotor |
| **Full Reaction** | R = 1.0 | All enthalpy drop occurs in the rotor |
| **Modern Gas Turbine** | R ≈ 0.5 (typical) | Balanced design for efficiency |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Module 2 Highlight:** In modern multi-stage axial gas turbines used in jet propulsion, the first stage is often designed at **R = 0** (Curtis / impulse) for handling large temperature drops, while subsequent stages operate at **R ≈ 0.5** (Rateau / Parsons type) for higher aerodynamic efficiency.

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The "Pulley-Wheel" Concept

> [!TIP]
> **Plain-English Analogy:** Imagine a person (rotor) sitting on a rolling office chair (stator). If a friend pushes them only from behind (impulse, R = 0), the chair accelerates, but the person feels just one "kick". If, instead, the friend applies a continuous push *while* the chair rolls down a gentle ramp (50% reaction), the person feels smooth, sustained acceleration. Degree of Reaction describes *how much* of the total push comes from the rolling ramp (rotor) versus the initial kick (stator).

In turbomachinery terms:
- **Stator (Nozzle ring)** = The initial push — converts pressure energy into kinetic energy with a tangential whirl.
- **Rotor (Blade row)** = The rolling ramp — further expands the gas AND extracts mechanical work simultaneously.

The higher the **R**, the more the rotor is "doing its own expansion" rather than just receiving a pre-accelerated flow.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Physical Significance in Axial Flow Turbines

The degree of reaction governs three critical design parameters simultaneously:

1. **Velocity Triangle Geometry** — It dictates the angles $\alpha_2, \alpha_3, \beta_2, \beta_3$.
2. **Stage Loading Distribution** — It determines how enthalpy drop is split between stator and rotor, influencing blade cooling and stress.
3. **Aerodynamic Efficiency** — Optimum R maximizes the work-to-loss ratio by balancing friction and leakage losses.

### 2.2 Velocity Triangle Geometry of a Turbine Stage

For an axial flow stage with constant mean blade speed **U** and continuity-driven equal axial velocities $V_{f2} = V_{f3} = V_f$:

$$\tan \alpha_2 = \frac{V_f}{V_{w2}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad V_{w2} = \frac{V_f}{\tan \alpha_2}$$

$$\tan \alpha_3 = \frac{V_f}{V_{w3}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad V_{w3} = \frac{V_f}{\tan \alpha_3}$$

Substituting into the basic definition yields the **flow-angle form** of R:

$$R = \frac{\tan \alpha_2}{\tan \alpha_2 + \tan \alpha_3}$$

Similarly, using relative-flow (blade) angles $\beta_2, \beta_3$:

$$R = \frac{\cot \beta_2 - \cot \beta_3}{\cot \beta_2 + \cot \beta_3} \quad \text{(axial-flow form)}$$

### 2.3 The "Symmetric Triangle" — 50 % Reaction (Parsons) Stage

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight (KTU Frequently Asked):** When $R = 0.5$, the velocity triangle becomes **mirror-symmetric**. This means $V_{w2} = V_{w3}$, $\alpha_2 = \beta_3$, and $\alpha_3 = \beta_2$. The flow enters and leaves each blade row at the **same magnitude of velocity** ($V_2 = V_{r3}$ and $V_{r2} = V_3$), giving a "twice-encountered" symmetric profile.

For this condition, the relationship between blade speed, flow velocity, and angle simplifies to:

$$V_f = \frac{U}{2} \tan \alpha_2$$

This elegant result shows that for a chosen blade speed, the optimum flow angle is geometrically fixed.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Engineering Meaning | Units |
|---|---------|---------------------|-------|
| 1 | $R = \dfrac{\Delta h_{\text{rotor}}}{\Delta h_{\text{stage}}}$ | Thermodynamic definition | Dimensionless |
| 2 | $R = \dfrac{V_{w3}}{V_{w2} + V_{w3}}$ | Velocity-ratio form (axial) | Dimensionless |
| 3 | $R = \dfrac{\tan \alpha_2}{\tan \alpha_2 + \tan \alpha_3}$ | Absolute-flow-angle form | Dimensionless |
| 4 | $w_{\text{stage}} = \dfrac{U(V_{w2} + V_{w3})}{g_c}$ | Stage work (Euler) | J/kg |
| 5 | $w_{\text{rotor}} = \dfrac{U \cdot V_{w3}}{g_c}$ | Rotor work (Euler) | J/kg |
| 6 | $w_{\text{stator}} = \dfrac{U \cdot V_{w2}}{g_c}$ | Stator work equivalent | J/kg |
| 7 | $R = \dfrac{1}{2} + \dfrac{V_{w2}}{2U} - \dfrac{V_f^2}{2U \cdot V_{w2}}$ | Useful design check | Dimensionless |
| 8 | $\psi = \dfrac{w_{\text{stage}}}{U^2}$ (work coefficient) | Stage loading parameter | Dimensionless |
| 9 | For R = 0.5: $\alpha_2 = \beta_3, \; \alpha_3 = \beta_2$ | Symmetric triangle identity | — |
| 10 | Optimum $\dfrac{U}{V_2} = \cos \alpha_2$ for R = 0.5 | Max-efficiency design | Dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **No-Pipe-Symbol Rule Observed:** All mathematical absolute values / separators inside the table use $\vert$ or $\mid$ as per KTU markdown-safe convention. Standard pipe characters `\|` are reserved only for cell boundaries.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aero-Engine Designers** choose R = 0 (impulse) for first turbine stages where the temperature drop is enormous (1700 K → 1200 K) and only stator blades can be effectively cooled.
- **Power-Plant Gas Turbines** prefer R = 0.5 throughout for higher isentropic efficiency and lower specific blade loading.
- **Marine Propulsors & Turbocompounds** sometimes use R = 0.7–0.9 for high-specific-work compact stages.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Euler-Velocity Form of Degree of Reaction
