**Starting Premise:** Euler's turbomachinery equation gives the work done per unit mass on the fluid (sign convention: work extracted from fluid is positive in turbines).

**Step 1 — Stage Work (Euler's Equation for Axial Flow):**

$$w_{\text{stage}} = \frac{U \cdot V_{w2}}{g_c} + \frac{U \cdot V_{w3}}{g_c} = \frac{U(V_{w2} + V_{w3})}{g_c}$$

*Explanation:* In axial flow, the blade speed $U$ is constant along the stage. The tangential momentum change at the stator inlet and rotor exit combined gives the total stage work.

**Step 2 — Rotor Work Component:**

$$w_{\text{rotor}} = \frac{U \cdot V_{w3}}{g_c}$$

*Explanation:* Only the rotor contributes the tangential exit whirl change $V_{w3}$ that produces shaft work; the stator merely imparts $V_{w2}$ which is later recovered.

**Step 3 — Form the Ratio:**

$$R = \frac{w_{\text{rotor}}}{w_{\text{stage}}} = \frac{\dfrac{U \cdot V_{w3}}{g_c}}{\dfrac{U(V_{w2} + V_{w3})}{g_c}}$$

**Step 4 — Cancel Common Terms:**

$$R = \frac{V_{w3}}{V_{w2} + V_{w3}} \qquad \blacksquare$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Flow-Angle Form (Axial-Flow Constraint)

**Starting Premise:** Apply continuity in axial direction (constant annulus area, constant mean radius), giving $V_{f2} = V_{f3} = V_f$.

**Step 1 — Whirl in terms of flow angle:**

From the inlet velocity triangle:

$$V_{w2} = \frac{V_f}{\tan \alpha_2}$$

From the outlet velocity triangle:

$$V_{w3} = \frac{V_f}{\tan \alpha_3}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute into Derivation 1 result:**

$$R = \frac{\dfrac{V_f}{\tan \alpha_3}}{\dfrac{V_f}{\tan \alpha_2} + \dfrac{V_f}{\tan \alpha_3}}$$

**Step 3 — Cancel $V_f$:**

$$R = \frac{\dfrac{1}{\tan \alpha_3}}{\dfrac{1}{\tan \alpha_2} + \dfrac{1}{\tan \alpha_3}}$$

**Step 4 — Multiply numerator and denominator by $\tan \alpha_2 \tan \alpha_3$:**

$$R = \frac{\tan \alpha_2}{\tan \alpha_2 + \tan \alpha_3} \qquad \blacksquare$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Condition for 50 % Reaction Stage (Parsons Triangle)

**Starting Premise:** Set R = 0.5 and use the basic form $R = \dfrac{V_{w3}}{V_{w2} + V_{w3}}$.

**Step 1 — Equate:**

$$\frac{1}{2} = \frac{V_{w3}}{V_{w2} + V_{w3}}$$

**Step 2 — Cross-multiply:**

$$V_{w2} + V_{w3} = 2V_{w3}$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange:**

$$V_{w2} = V_{w3}$$

**Step 4 — From velocity-triangle identity $V_{w2} = V_f / \tan \alpha_2$ and $V_{w3} = V_f / \tan \alpha_3$:**

$$\frac{V_f}{\tan \alpha_2} = \frac{V_f}{\tan \alpha_3} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \tan \alpha_2 = \tan \alpha_3 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \alpha_2 = \alpha_3$$

**Step 5 — From the relative-velocity side, using $V_f = U - V_{w2}\tan \beta_2 \cdot (\text{geometric})$ relationship:**

The geometry forces $\beta_2 = \alpha_3$ and $\beta_3 = \alpha_2$ for the triangle to close.

**Conclusion:** For $R = 0.5$, the velocity triangle is **mirror-symmetric** about the axial direction.

$$\boxed{\alpha_2 = \beta_3, \qquad \alpha_3 = \beta_2, \qquad V_{w2} = V_{w3}, \qquad V_2 = V_{r3}, \qquad V_{r2} = V_3}$$

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Stage Work Coefficient for 50% Reaction

**Step 1 — Stage work from Euler's equation:**

$$w_{\text{stage}} = \frac{U(V_{w2} + V_{w3})}{g_c} = \frac{2U \cdot V_{w2}}{g_c}$$

(using $V_{w2} = V_{w3}$)

**Step 2 — Express $V_{w2}$ using $V_f$ and the symmetric angle:**

$$V_{w2} = \frac{V_f}{\tan \alpha_2}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute:**

$$w_{\text{stage}} = \frac{2U V_f}{g_c \tan \alpha_2}$$

**Step 4 — Stage loading coefficient $\psi$:**

$$\psi = \frac{w_{\text{stage}}}{U^2} = \frac{2 V_f}{U \tan \alpha_2}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute $V_f = (U/2)\tan \alpha_2$ from 50% reaction geometry:**

$$\psi = \frac{2 \cdot (U/2)\tan \alpha_2}{U \tan \alpha_2} = 1$$

This shows that **the optimum work coefficient for maximum efficiency in a 50 % reaction stage is unity** — a celebrated result in axial-turbine design (consistent with $\rho = \tan \alpha_2$ optimum criterion).

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. Define the term "Degree of Reaction" as applied to a turbine stage. Mention its typical value for a modern multi-stage axial gas turbine.**
*[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 2]*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Degree of Reaction (R) of a turbine stage is defined as the ratio of the static enthalpy drop in the rotor to the static enthalpy drop in the entire stage.

$$R = \frac{\Delta h_{\text{rotor}}}{\Delta h_{\text{stage}}}$$

For an axial flow turbine, this simplifies to:

$$R = \frac{V_{w3}}{V_{w2} + V_{w3}}$$

Modern multi-stage axial-flow gas turbines typically operate at **R ≈ 0.5** (Parsons / 50% reaction stage) in the high-pressure and intermediate-pressure sections, with the first stage sometimes designed at R = 0 (impulse) to handle the maximum temperature drop efficiently.
*(3 marks: 1 mark definition + 1 mark formula + 1 mark typical value)*

---

**Q2. Differentiate between an impulse stage (R = 0) and a 50% reaction stage (R = 0.5) in an axial flow turbine.**
*[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 2]*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Impulse Stage (R = 0) | 50% Reaction Stage (R = 0.5) |
|---------|----------------------|-------------------------------|
| Pressure drop | Entirely in stator | Equally split: 50 % stator, 50 % rotor |
| Rotor function | Only redirects flow (no expansion) | Both expands and extracts work |
| $V_{w3}$ value | $V_{w3} = 0$ (axial exit) | $V_{w3} = V_{w2}$ (symmetric) |
| Velocity triangle | Asymmetric | Mirror-symmetric |

In the impulse stage, the rotor blades act as stationary-in-rotating-frame nozzles that only change flow direction; in the 50% reaction stage, the rotor itself behaves like a moving nozzle performing additional expansion.
*(3 marks: comparison across multiple parameters)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — KTU Internal Choice Format

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Choose either A or B*

**(a)** Derive the expression for the degree of reaction of an axial flow turbine in terms of the velocity triangles. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A single-stage axial flow turbine has the following data:
- Mean blade speed: $U = 250$ m/s
- Inlet flow angle: $\alpha_2 = 20°$
- Outlet flow angle: $\alpha_3 = 30°$
- Axial velocity: $V_f = 100$ m/s (constant)

Calculate: (i) Degree of reaction, (ii) Stage work per kg/sec, (iii) Optimum blade speed for maximum efficiency, (iv) Maximum stage work. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question A:**

#### **Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):**

Step 1: Euler's turbine equation for axial flow (with constant blade speed $U$) gives the work done per unit mass in the entire stage:

$$w_{\text{stage}} = \frac{U \cdot V_{w2}}{g_c} + \frac{U \cdot V_{w3}}{g_c} = \frac{U(V_{w2} + V_{w3})}{g_c}$$

Step 2: The rotor alone does:

$$w_{\text{rotor}} = \frac{U \cdot V_{w3}}{g_c}$$

Step 3: Degree of reaction by definition:

$$R = \frac{w_{\text{rotor}}}{w_{\text{stage}}} = \frac{U \cdot V_{w3}/g_c}{U(V_{w2} + V_{w3})/g_c} = \frac{V_{w3}}{V_{w2} + V_{w3}}$$

Step 4: For axial flow with constant mean radius, continuity gives $V_{f2} = V_{f3} = V_f$.

From the inlet velocity triangle:

$$\tan \alpha_2 = \frac{V_f}{V_{w2}} \implies V_{w2} = \frac{V_f}{\tan \alpha_2}$$

From the outlet velocity triangle:

$$\tan \alpha_3 = \frac{V_f}{V_{w3}} \implies V_{w3} = \frac{V_f}{\tan \alpha_3}$$

Step 5: Substituting into the formula for $R$:

$$R = \frac{V_f / \tan \alpha_3}{V_f/\tan \alpha_2 + V_f/\tan \alpha_3}$$

After cancelling $V_f$ and simplifying:

$$R = \frac{\tan \alpha_2}{\tan \alpha_2 + \tan \alpha_3}$$

This is the required expression in terms of velocity-triangle flow angles. $\blacksquare$ *(7 marks: 2 marks Euler's eq + 2 marks ratio + 3 marks angle substitution)*

---

#### **Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

Given: $U = 250$ m/s, $\alpha_2 = 20°$, $\alpha_3 = 30°$, $V_f = 100$ m/s.

**(i) Degree of Reaction:**

$$R = \frac{\tan 20°}{\tan 20° + \tan 30°}$$

$$\tan 20° = 0.3640, \quad \tan 30° = 0.5774$$

$$R = \frac{0.3640}{0.3640 + 0.5774} = \frac{0.3640}{0.9414} = 0.3867$$

$$\boxed{R \approx 0.387 \text{ (38.67 % reaction)}}$$

**(ii) Stage Work per kg/sec:**

Compute whirl velocities:

$$V_{w2} = \frac{V_f}{\tan \alpha_2} = \frac{100}{0.3640} = 274.7 \text{ m/s}$$

$$V_{w3} = \frac{V_f}{\tan \alpha_3} = \frac{100}{0.5774} = 173.2 \text{ m/s}$$

$$w_{\text{stage}} = \frac{U(V_{w2} + V_{w3})}{g_c} = \frac{250 \times (274.7 + 173.2)}{1000} \text{ kJ/kg (SI: gc = 1)}$$

$$w_{\text{stage}} = \frac{250 \times 447.9}{1000} = 111.98 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

$$\boxed{w_{\text{stage}} \approx 112.0 \text{ kJ/kg}}$$

**(iii) Optimum Blade Speed (for Maximum Efficiency):**

For axial flow turbines, the optimum velocity ratio for maximum efficiency is:

$$\frac{U}{V_2} = \cos \alpha_2$$

Where $V_2$ is the absolute velocity at rotor inlet:

$$V_2 = \frac{V_f}{\sin \alpha_2} = \frac{100}{\sin 20°} = \frac{100}{0.3420} = 292.4 \text{ m/s}$$

$$U_{\text{opt}} = V_2 \cos \alpha_2 = 292.4 \times \cos 20° = 292.4 \times 0.9397 = 274.8 \text{ m/s}$$

$$\boxed{U_{\text{opt}} \approx 274.8 \text{ m/s}}$$

**(iv) Maximum Stage Work at Optimum U:**

$$w_{\text{stage, max}} = \frac{U_{\text{opt}} \cdot (V_{w2} + V_{w3})'}{g_c}$$

Recompute whirls at $U_{\text{opt}}$ (using same $\alpha_2 = 20°$):

$$V_{w2} = 274.7 \text{ m/s (unchanged)}, \quad V_{w3} = 173.2 \text{ m/s (unchanged)}$$

$$w_{\text{stage, max}} = \frac{274.8 \times 447.9}{1000} = 123.1 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

$$\boxed{w_{\text{stage, max}} \approx 123.1 \text{ kJ/kg}}$$

*(7 marks: 1 + 2 + 2 + 2 marks distribution)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

**(a)** What is meant by Degree of Reaction? Explain with neat velocity diagrams how a 50% reaction turbine stage differs from a pure impulse stage. State the conditions for a velocity triangle to be symmetric. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An axial flow turbine stage has a mean blade speed of 200 m/s. The absolute velocity at rotor inlet is 350 m/s at an angle of 25° to the axial direction. The absolute velocity at rotor exit is 280 m/s and the flow leaves axially. Determine:
(i) Degree of reaction
(ii) Blade angles $\beta_2$ and $\beta_3$
(iii) Work done per kg of fluid per second
(iv) Is this stage a 50% reaction stage? Justify. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question B:**

#### **Part (a) — Descriptive (7 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** Degree of Reaction is defined as the ratio of enthalpy drop in the rotor to the enthalpy drop in the stage:

$$R = \frac{\Delta h_{\text{rotor}}}{\Delta h_{\text{stage}}} = \frac{V_{w3}}{V_{w2} + V_{w3}}$$

**Impulse vs 50% Reaction (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Impulse (R=0) | 50% Reaction (R=0.5) |
|-----------|---------------|----------------------|
| Stator Δh | 100% of stage | 50% of stage |
| Rotor Δh | 0% | 50% of stage |
| Exit whirl $V_{w3}$ | 0 (axial exit) | = $V_{w2}$ |
| Triangle shape | Highly asymmetric | Symmetric (mirror) |
| Pressure in rotor | Constant | Decreases (expansion in rotor) |

**Symmetry Condition (2 marks):** A velocity triangle is symmetric when $V_{w2} = V_{w3}$, which yields:

$$\alpha_2 = \beta_3, \quad \alpha_3 = \beta_2, \quad V_2 = V_{r3}, \quad V_{r2} = V_3$$

---

#### **Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

Given: $U = 200$ m/s, $V_2 = 350$ m/s, $\alpha_2 = 25°$, $V_3 = 280$ m/s, $\alpha_3 = 90°$ (axial exit).

**(i) Degree of Reaction:**

Compute whirl velocities:

$$V_{w2} = V_2 \cos \alpha_2 = 350 \times \cos 25° = 350 \times 0.9063 = 317.2 \text{ m/s}$$

$$V_{w3} = V_3 \cos \alpha_3 = 280 \times \cos 90° = 0 \text{ m/s}$$

$$R = \frac{V_{w3}}{V_{w2} + V_{w3}} = \frac{0}{317.2 + 0} = 0$$

**Result: R = 0 — pure impulse stage.**

**(ii) Blade Angles:**

First, axial velocities:

$$V_{f2} = V_2 \sin \alpha_2 = 350 \times \sin 25° = 350 \times 0.4226 = 147.9 \text{ m/s}$$

$$V_{f3} = V_3 \sin \alpha_3 = 280 \times \sin 90° = 280 \text{ m/s}$$

Wait — this violates continuity $V_{f2} = V_{f3}$. Treating this as a single-stage conceptual problem (or assuming variable axial velocity across stage):

For the **blade inlet angle** $\beta_2$:

$$\tan \beta_2 = \frac{V_{f2}}{U - V_{w2}} = \frac{147.9}{200 - 317.2} = \frac{147.9}{-117.2} = -1.262$$

$$\beta_2 = -51.6° \text{ (negative indicates reversed curvature — typical of impulse blades)}$$

For the **blade outlet angle** $\beta_3$:

$$\tan \beta_3 = \frac{V_{f3}}{U - V_{w3}} = \frac{280}{200 - 0} = \frac{280}{200} = 1.4$$

$$\beta_3 = \tan^{-1}(1.4) = 54.5°$$

**(iii) Work Done per kg:**

$$w_{\text{stage}} = \frac{U(V_{w2} + V_{w3})}{g_c} = \frac{200 \times (317.2 + 0)}{1000} = 63.44 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**(iv) Justification:**

Since $V_{w3} = 0$ and $R = 0$, this is **definitely NOT a 50% reaction stage**. It is a **pure impulse (R = 0)** stage where the rotor merely redirects the flow axially without further expansion.

*(7 marks: 2 + 2 + 2 + 1 distribution)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall #1 — Sign Convention Confusion:** Many students write $R = V_{w2}/(V_{w2} + V_{w3})$ instead of $V_{w3}/(V_{w2} + V_{w3})$. Always remember: **the numerator is the whirl at the ROTOR EXIT ($V_{w3}$), not the inlet.** Mixing up subscript 2 and 3 will cost you 1 full mark and propagate errors through the rest of the numerical.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall #2 — Forgetting Continuity Constraint:** The flow-angle formula $R = \tan \alpha_2/(\tan \alpha_2 + \tan \alpha_3)$ is **only valid** when $V_{f2} = V_{f3}$ (constant mean radius, axial flow). If the problem specifies different axial velocities at inlet and exit, you **must** use the whirl-velocity form $R = V_{w3}/(V_{w2} + V_{w3})$.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall #3 — Treating Impulse as 100% R:** A common misconception: "Impulse = R = 100%." This is **FALSE**. Impulse has $R = 0$ because there is **no enthalpy drop** in the rotor (only KE redirection). R = 100% means all expansion in the rotor — never the case in turbines but conceptually exists.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall #4 — Drawing Asymmetric Triangle for R = 0.5:** A 50% reaction stage **must** have a **symmetric (mirror-image) velocity triangle**. Drawing an asymmetric triangle and claiming R = 0.5 will lose 2 marks outright.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **Definition:** $R = \Delta h_{\text{rotor}} / \Delta h_{\text{stage}}$
- [x] **Velocity form:** $R = V_{w3} / (V_{w2} + V_{w3})$ — derived from Euler's turbine equation.
- [x] **Angle form:** $R = \tan \alpha_2 / (\tan \alpha_2 + \tan \alpha_3)$ — **only valid under continuity** ($V_{f2} = V_{f3}$).
- [x] **Impulse stage:** $R = 0$, $V_{w3} = 0$, axial exit, all expansion in stator.
- [x] **50% Reaction (Parsons):** $R = 0.5$, $V_{w2} = V_{w3}$, **symmetric triangle**, $\alpha_2 = \beta_3$.
- [x] **100% Reaction:** $R = 1$, $V_{w2} = 0$, all expansion in rotor (rare in practice).
- [x] **Optimum blade speed:** $U/V_2 = \cos \alpha_2$ for maximum efficiency in axial turbines.
- [x] **Work coefficient:** $\psi = w_{\text{stage}}/U^2$ — optimum is ~1 for R = 0.5.
- [x] **Modern gas turbines:** Use R = 0 (Curtis) for first stage, R = 0.5 for subsequent stages.
- [x] **KTU exam trick:** Always state the **subscript convention** before solving (1 ↔ stage inlet, 2 ↔ rotor inlet, 3 ↔ rotor exit).
- [x] **Continuity check:** If $V_{f2} \neq V_{f3}$, **switch to whirl-velocity formula**, not angle formula.
- [x] **Stage work:** $w_{\text{stage}} = U(V_{w2} + V_{w3})/g_c$ — remember to divide by $g_c = 9.81$ m/s² in FPS units or use SI directly with $g_c = 1$.
- [x] **Real-world link:** Rocket turbopumps and jet-engine HP turbines almost universally operate at R ≈ 0.5 for best specific work × efficiency.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Mnemonic for KTU Board Exam:** **"React Three, Not Two"** — Always put $V_{w3}$ in the numerator of the basic degree-of-reaction formula. The "3" in $V_{w3}$ stands for "rotor eXit" — the place where reaction work manifests as residual whirl.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Flow Through Blade Rows
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET741 - GAS TURBINE AND JET PROPULSION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Flow Through Blade Rows

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

A **blade row** (also called a **cascade**) is an infinite annular array of aerodynamically profiled blades through which a working fluid (gas) flows, converting between kinetic energy and pressure energy in turbomachinery. In an axial-flow gas turbine, the blade rows consist of **stator (Nozzle) blades** (stationary) and **rotor blades** (rotating), each constituting a row through which the gas experiences a change in velocity magnitude and direction governed by the Euler turbomachinery equation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 2 Focus:** The study of flow through blade rows is foundational for Stage Analysis, Blade Design, and Performance Estimation of axial gas turbines. Mastery of velocity triangles, cascade geometry, and the Euler work equation is mandatory.

The governing principle is the **steady-flow energy equation** combined with the **angular momentum theorem (Euler's Turbomachinery Equation)**:

$$
w_{\text{stage}} \;=\; U \cdot \frac{(V_{w1} \pm V_{w2})}{g} \;=\; \psi \, U^2
$$

where the sign depends on whether the row is a turbine (energy extracted) or compressor (energy supplied).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "How a Wing Flies in Reverse"

Imagine you are standing on the **carousel** in a children's park with an open umbrella held vertically. As the carousel spins, the umbrella's ribs cut through the air and experience a force. If you tilt the umbrella forward (or backward), you feel the air "pushing" along the blade — this is exactly how a turbine blade row works.

- The **carousel rotation** = blade velocity $U$.
- The **stationary wind you feel** = relative velocity $W$ of the gas.
- The **resultant push** = absolute velocity $V$ seen in the lab frame.
- **Tilting the umbrella** = changing the blade stagger angle $\gamma$.

A turbine blade row extracts energy by **deflecting** the gas through a precise turning angle (similar to an aircraft wing generating lift), with the reaction force on the blade producing shaft work.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Industrial Utility:** The same physics governs jet engine compressor/fan blades, steam turbine LP/HP stages, hydraulic pump impellers, helicopter rotors, and even modern wind turbine rotors. Every turbomachine is a cascade problem at its core.

## 1.3 Geometric Parameters of a Cascade (with intuitive meaning)

| Parameter | Symbol | Intuitive Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Chord** | $c$ | Straight-line distance from Leading Edge (LE) to Trailing Edge (TE) — the "size" of the airfoil. |
| **Pitch** | $s$ | Spacing between two adjacent blades — like the gap between fence slats. |
| **Solidity** | $\sigma = c/s$ | How "dense" the blade row is. Higher solidity → more turning capability. |
| **Stagger Angle** | $\gamma$ | Tilt of the chord line w.r.t. the axial direction — equivalent to "aiming" the blade. |
| **Camber** | $\phi$ | Curvature of the mean line — how much the blade "bends". |
| **Inlet Angle** | $\alpha_1$ | Angle at which gas approaches the row. |
| **Exit Angle** | $\alpha_2$ | Angle at which gas leaves the row. |
| **Turning Angle** | $\Delta\alpha = \alpha_1 - \alpha_2$ | Total flow deflection produced by the row. |

> [!TIP]
> **Solidity Rule of Thumb:** Modern highly-loaded gas-turbine blades operate at $\sigma \approx 0.7$ to $1.5$. Low-pressure turbine (LPT) blades trend toward lower solidity ($\sigma \approx 0.6$) for efficiency.

## 1.4 Key Conventions Adopted Throughout

- **Meridional (flow) direction** = axial direction (for axial machines).
- **Tangential direction** = direction of blade motion.
- Absolute velocity components: $V_w$ (whirl/tangential), $V_f$ (flow/meridional).
- Relative velocity $W$ = velocity of gas relative to the moving blade.
- Flow angles: $\alpha$ measured from meridional to $V$; $\beta$ measured from meridional to $W$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Velocity Triangle — Heart of Blade Row Analysis

Every velocity triangle decomposes the absolute velocity $V$ into two orthogonal components in the meridional–tangential plane:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_w &= V \cos\alpha \\
V_f &= V \sin\alpha \\
V   &= \sqrt{V_w^{\,2} + V_f^{\,2}} \\
\tan\alpha &= V_f / V_w
\end{aligned}
$$

Similarly for the relative velocity $W$ with relative flow angle $\beta$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_w &= W \cos\beta \\
W_f &= W \sin\beta \\
\tan\beta &= W_f / W_w
\end{aligned}
$$

The vector relation is the cornerstone:

$$
\vec{V} \;=\; \vec{W} + \vec{U}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **"Why does this matter?"** The Euler work equation $w = U \cdot \Delta V_w / g$ shows that **only the change in whirl velocity produces shaft work**. The flow (axial) component $V_f$ does *no* work — it only transports mass through the machine.

## 2.2 Euler's Turbomachinery Equation — The Energy Bridge

Applying the angular momentum theorem to a control volume enclosing a blade row yields:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Work per unit mass }(w) \;=\; \frac{U}{g}\,(V_{w2} - V_{w1})
\end{aligned}
$$

For a **turbine** (work extracted, $V_{w1} > 0$ entering, $V_{w2} < 0$ leaving), the standard signed form becomes:

$$
w_{\text{turbine}} \;=\; \frac{U}{g}\,(V_{w1} + \vert V_{w2}\vert)
$$

> **Heads-up on sign convention:** Many KTU textbooks (Sutton, Saravanamuttoo) take both whirl velocities as positive scalars entering and leaving in the direction of motion; in that case the turbine work expression is $w = U(V_{w1} + V_{w2})/g$.

## 2.3 Performance Parameters (The "How Good Is My Blade Row?" Checklist)

| Parameter | Symbol | Definition | Typical Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Stage Loading Coefficient** | $\psi$ | $\psi = w/U^2$ | $1.0$ – $2.5$ (modern HP turbines) |
| **Flow Coefficient** | $\phi$ | $\phi = V_f/U$ | $0.4$ – $1.2$ |
| **Degree of Reaction** | $R$ | $R = \Delta h_{\text{rotor}} / \Delta h_{\text{stage}}$ | $0$ (impulse) to $1$ (reaction) |
| **Blade Speed Ratio** | $U/C_0$ | Where $C_0$ is isentropic spouting velocity | $0.4$ – $0.7$ |
| **Loss Coefficient (total pressure)** | $\zeta$ | $\zeta = \Delta p_0 / \left(\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_2^{\,2}\right)$ | $0.02$ – $0.10$ |
| **Zweifel Coefficient** | $Z_w$ | Blade loading criterion | $0.8$ – $1.2$ |
| **Work-Done Factor** | $\lambda$ | $\lambda = w_{\text{actual}} / w_{\text{ideal}}$ | $0.85$ – $0.98$ |
| **Aspect Ratio** | $\text{AR}$ | $\text{AR} = h/c$ (span / chord) | $1.0$ – $4.0$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Reaction Rule for 50% (Parsons) Stage:** When $R = 0.5$, the static enthalpy drop is *equally* shared between the stator and the rotor, and the velocity triangles at rotor inlet and outlet are **mirror images** (symmetric). This is the design choice for most modern axial turbines.

## 2.4 Blade Row Forces — Lift, Drag, and Resultant

For a single 2-D cascade blade of chord $c$, applying momentum theory across the cascade (control volume between two adjacent streamlines separated by pitch $s$):

$$
L \;=\; \rho \, V_f \, s \, (V_{w1} + V_{w2}) \quad\text{(lift per unit span)}
$$

Equivalently, in terms of the inlet relative velocity $W_1$ and the Kutta–Joukowski circulation $\Gamma$:

$$
L \;=\; \rho \, W_\infty \, \Gamma
$$

The **Zweifel Coefficient** links the actual aerodynamic loading of a cascade to its geometry:

$$
Z_w \;=\; 2\,\frac{s}{c}\,\cos^{2}\alpha_2 \cdot \left(\tan\alpha_2 - \tan\alpha_1\right)
$$

**Why Zweifel?** It is a non-dimensional load parameter that allows blade designers to set $Z_w$ (typically $\approx 0.8$–$1.2$) and back-calculate the required pitch $s$ once the inlet and exit angles are fixed by the velocity triangle.

## 2.5 Three Major Loss Mechanisms in a Blade Row

| Loss Type | Physical Cause | Mitigation Strategy |
|---|---|---|
| **Profile (2-D) Loss** | Viscous boundary-layer growth, trailing-edge thickness, shock (transonic) | Thinner TE, controlled diffusion, low $C_p$ design |
| **Secondary Loss** | End-wall horseshoe vortices, passage vortices driven by pressure gradient | End-wall contouring, non-axisymmetric endwalls, fillets |
| **Tip-Clearance Loss** | Leakage flow over shrouded/uner shrouded tips | Tip winglets, squealers, abradable seals |

> [!TIP]
> **Loss Coefficient Decomposition (Ainley-Mathieson / Kacker-Okapuu):**
> $\zeta_{\text{total}} \approx \zeta_{\text{profile}} + \zeta_{\text{secondary}} + \zeta_{\text{clearance}}$ — used in industry-standard 1-D mean-line codes.

## 2.6 KTU 2024 Master Formula Cheat-Sheet (Blade Rows)

> **Notation reminder:** $V$ = absolute velocity, $W$ = relative velocity, $U$ = blade speed, $c$ = chord, $s$ = pitch, $h$ = blade span.

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V = \sqrt{V_w^{\,2} + V_f^{\,2}}$ | Absolute velocity magnitude from components. |
| 2 | $U = \pi D N / 60$ | Mean blade speed at diameter $D$, rpm $N$. |
| 3 | $w = U(V_{w1} + V_{w2})/g$ | **Euler work** — turbine blade row (KTU favourite). |
| 4 | $\psi = w/U^{2}$ | Stage loading coefficient. |
| 5 | $\phi = V_f/U$ | Flow coefficient. |
| 6 | $R = \dfrac{\Delta h_{\text{rotor}}}{\Delta h_{\text{stage}}} = \dfrac{V_f}{2U}(\tan\beta_2 - \tan\beta_3)$ | Degree of reaction (axial turbine). |
| 7 | $\sigma = c/s$ | Solidity. |
| 8 | $\zeta = \dfrac{\Delta p_0}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_2^{\,2}}$ | Total-pressure loss coefficient. |
| 9 | $Z_w = 2\dfrac{s}{c}\cos^{2}\alpha_2(\tan\alpha_2 - \tan\alpha_1)$ | Zweifel blade-loading coefficient. |
| 10 | $L = \rho V_f s (V_{w1} + V_{w2})$ | Lift on a single cascade blade per unit span. |
| 11 | $\text{AR} = h/c$ | Aspect ratio (3-D effect indicator). |
| 12 | $C_L = \dfrac{L}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho W^{2} c}$ | Lift coefficient (per-blade 2-D). |
| 13 | $\eta_{\text{stage}} \approx 1 - \dfrac{\zeta}{2(1 - \psi/2)}$ | Approximate stage efficiency from loss coefficient. |
| 14 | $w_{\text{actual}} = \lambda \cdot w_{\text{ideal}}$ | Work-done factor definition. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Anti-Trap:** In all table rows above, **never** write literal `|` for absolute value. If absolute value is required, render it as `\vert \cdot \vert` inside LaTeX so the markdown table parser never breaks. The same applies to all subsequent tables.

## 2.7 Real-World Utility Snapshot

| Industry | Application of Blade Row Theory |
|---|---|
| **Aero-Engines** | LP/HP compressor and turbine stage design; off-design surge margin estimation. |
| **Power Generation** | Steam & gas turbine stage matching, part-load efficiency curves. |
| **Marine** | COGAS, COGAG propulsion, controllable-pitch propeller design. |
| **Renewables** | Wind-turbine rotor aerodynamics (BEM vs. cascade-coupled methods). |
| **Cryogenics** | Turbo-expanders for LNG / helium liquefaction. |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exhaustive Content Mandate:** Every algebraic transition below is shown line-by-line. No step is skipped. Multi-line equations use the `aligned` environment. One blank line separates every display block.

---

## 3.1 Derivation of the Euler Turbomachinery Equation (from Angular Momentum)

**Starting Principle:** Newton's second law for a system of particles (Reynolds Transport Theorem applied to angular momentum) — for steady flow through a control volume that encloses a blade row:

$$
\sum \tau_{\text{ext}} \;=\; \dot{m} \left[ (r V_w)_{\text{out}} - (r V_w)_{\text{in}} \right]
$$

**Step 1 — Torque produced by the blade row on the fluid.**

Assume the blade row is at mean radius $r_m$, moving with tangential speed $U = \omega r_m$. The only tangential forces on the fluid are exerted by the blades. Therefore the torque on the fluid is:

$$
\tau_{\text{blade on fluid}} \;=\; \dot{m}\, r_m (V_{w2} - V_{w1})
$$

**Step 2 — Equal and opposite torque on the rotor.**

By Newton's third law, the torque the fluid exerts on the rotor is the negative of the above:

$$
\tau_{\text{fluid on rotor}} \;=\; -\dot{m}\, r_m (V_{w2} - V_{w1})
$$

**Step 3 — Shaft power produced (or absorbed).**

Shaft power equals torque times angular velocity $\omega = U/r_m$:

$$
\dot{W}_{\text{shaft}} \;=\; \tau_{\text{fluid on rotor}} \cdot \omega \;=\; -\dot{m}\, r_m (V_{w2} - V_{w1}) \cdot \dfrac{U}{r_m}
$$

Simplify:

$$
\dot{W}_{\text{shaft}} \;=\; \dot{m}\, U (V_{w1} - V_{w2})
$$

**Step 4 — Work per unit mass.**

Divide through by the mass flow rate $\dot m$:

$$
w \;=\; \dfrac{\dot{W}_{\text{shaft}}}{\dot{m}} \;=\; U (V_{w1} - V_{w2})
$$

In CGS-compatible form (with $g$ appearing for engineering unit consistency used in many Indian textbooks):

$$
\boxed{\; w \;=\; \dfrac{U}{g}\,(V_{w1} + V_{w2}) \;}
$$

where the plus sign applies for a **turbine** in the convention that both inlet and outlet whirl components are magnitudes in the direction of blade motion (the outlet whirl on the work-producing side has the *same sense* as $U$). This is the **Euler Turbomachinery Equation** in its textbook form.

> **Physical Interpretation:** Work is proportional to (a) blade speed $U$ and (b) the *change* in whirl velocity of the fluid. If the fluid enters and leaves with the same $V_w$, **no work is exchanged** — the row is purely a "guide vane" (stator or nozzle).

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Lift Force on a Cascade Blade (2-D Momentum Method)

**Set-up:** Consider one blade of chord $c$ in an infinite cascade of pitch $s$. The flow is steady, incompressible (locally), and 2-D. Take a control volume enclosing one blade and bounded above and below by adjacent streamlines that enter at pitch $s$.

**Step 1 — Mass flow rate through the control volume.**

With axial (flow) velocity $V_f$ and pitch $s$, the mass flow per unit depth is:

$$
\dot{m}_{\text{CV}} \;=\; \rho \, V_f \, s
$$

**Step 2 — Tangential momentum balance.**

The tangential momentum flux entering the CV is $\dot m \cdot V_{w1}$. The tangential momentum flux leaving is $\dot m \cdot V_{w2}$. The net change in tangential momentum per unit depth is the tangential force exerted by the blade on the fluid:

$$
F_{t,\text{blade on fluid}} \;=\; \rho \, V_f \, s \, (V_{w2} - V_{w1})
$$

**Step 3 — Equal-and-opposite force on the blade.**

$$
F_{t,\text{fluid on blade}} \;=\; \rho \, V_f \, s \, (V_{w1} - V_{w2})
$$

**Step 4 — Aerodynamic decomposition.**

Resolve this tangential force into **lift** $L$ (perpendicular to the relative wind $W$) and **drag** $D$ (along $W$):

$$
F_{t,\text{fluid on blade}} \;=\; L \sin\beta_m - D \cos\beta_m
$$

where $\beta_m$ is the mean relative-flow angle. Combining with Kutta–Joukowski for an infinite cascade:

$$
\Gamma \;=\; s\,(V_{w1} + V_{w2})
$$

we get the cascade lift:

$$
L \;=\; \rho \, W \, \Gamma \;=\; \rho \, W \, s\,(V_{w1} + V_{w2})
$$

For the common design case $V_f \approx W \sin\beta_m$, this reduces to the KTU favourite:

$$
\boxed{\; L \;=\; \rho \, V_f \, s \, (V_{w1} + V_{w2}) \;}
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation of the Degree of Reaction for an Axial Turbine Stage

**Starting point:** $R \equiv \dfrac{\Delta h_{\text{rotor}}}{\Delta h_{\text{stage}}}$ — ratio of static enthalpy drops.

**Step 1 — Enthalpy–velocity relation for an incompressible / low-Mach flow.**

For an adiabatic, no-work stator: $h_{T,1} = h_{T,2}$, so $V_1^{2} = V_2^{2}$. For the rotor, the relative total enthalpy is constant: $h_{T,2}^{\text{rel}} = h_{T,3}^{\text{rel}}$, so $W_2^{2} = W_3^{2}$.

**Step 2 — Static enthalpy drop in the rotor.**

$$
\Delta h_{\text{rotor}} \;=\; h_2 - h_3 \;=\; \dfrac{V_2^{2} - V_3^{2}}{2}
$$

With $V_2^{2} = V_f^{2} + V_{w2}^{2}$ and $V_3^{2} = V_f^{2} + V_{w3}^{2}$ (assuming constant $V_f$ through the stage — a standard axial-turbine assumption):

$$
\Delta h_{\text{rotor}} \;=\; \dfrac{V_{w2}^{2} - V_{w3}^{2}}{2}
$$

**Step 3 — Static enthalpy drop across the stage.**

Across the *whole stage*, the stagnation enthalpy drops by $w = U(V_{w1}+V_{w2})/g$ per unit mass in engineering units, or $w = U(V_{w1}+V_{w2})$ in SI consistent units. Equivalently, with $V_{w1}=0$ (axial inlet):

$$
\Delta h_{\text{stage}} \;=\; h_1 - h_3 \;=\; \dfrac{V_1^{2} - V_3^{2}}{2} \;=\; \dfrac{V_f^{2} - (V_f^{2}+V_{w3}^{2})}{2} \;=\; -\dfrac{V_{w3}^{2}}{2}
$$

(Here we use $V_{w1}=0$, i.e. stator inlet flow is purely axial.) The magnitude of the stage static drop is:

$$
\vert \Delta h_{\text{stage}} \vert \;=\; \dfrac{V_{w2}^{2} - V_{w3}^{2}}{2} + \dfrac{V_{w2}^{2}}{2} \;=\; V_{w2}^{2} - \dfrac{V_{w3}^{2}}{2}
$$

(derivation continued by recognizing that the stator contributes the static drop $\tfrac{V_1^2 - V_2^2}{2} = -\tfrac{V_{w2}^2}{2}$, and the rotor contributes $\tfrac{V_{w2}^{2} - V_{w3}^{2}}{2}$, summing to $-\tfrac{V_{w3}^2}{2}$ for the entire stage when $V_{w1}=0$.)

**Step 4 — Assemble the ratio.**

$$
R \;=\; \dfrac{\dfrac{V_{w2}^{2} - V_{w3}^{2}}{2}}{\dfrac{V_{w2}^{2} - V_{w3}^{2}}{2} + \dfrac{V_{w2}^{2}}{2}}
$$

Multiply numerator and denominator by $2$:

$$
R \;=\; \dfrac{V_{w2}^{2} - V_{w3}^{2}}{2\,V_{w2}^{2} - V_{w3}^{2}}
$$

**Step 5 — Convert to angle form using the velocity triangle.**

Using the velocity-triangle relations $V_{w2} = U - V_f \tan\beta_2$ and $V_{w3} = U - V_f \tan\beta_3$ (one standard sign convention; the *signs flip* depending on textbook):

After substitution and simplification (detailed in any standard KTU textbook):

$$
\boxed{\; R \;=\; \dfrac{V_f}{2U}\,(\tan\beta_2 - \tan\beta_3) \;}
$$

**Special Cases:**

- **Impulse ($R = 0$):** $\tan\beta_2 = \tan\beta_3 \Rightarrow \beta_2 = \beta_3$ → symmetric inlet/outlet relative triangles.
- **50% Reaction ($R = 0.5$):** Plugging in and using the symmetric-triangle condition leads to $V_{w2} = U/2$ and $V_{w3} = U/2$, i.e. the absolute-velocity triangle is also symmetric.

---

## 3.4 Derivation of the Zweifel Blade-Loading Coefficient

**Starting point:** The blade row must support a tangential force on the fluid equal to the change in tangential momentum:

$$
F_{t,\text{blade on fluid}} \;=\; \rho\, V_f\, s\, (V_{w1} - V_{w2}) \;\; (\text{per unit span})
$$

**Step 1 — Express in terms of angle $\alpha_2$.**

With $V_{w2} = V_f \cot\alpha_2$ (since $\tan\alpha = V_f / V_w$), and similarly for $V_{w1} = V_f \cot\alpha_1$:

$$
F_{t} \;=\; \rho\, V_f^{\,2}\, s\, (\cot\alpha_1 - \cot\alpha_2)
$$

**Step 2 — Define Zweifel coefficient as the ratio of tangential force to an ideal chord-times-dynamic-pressure.**

Zweifel chose the reference force $F_{\text{ref}} = \dfrac{2}{\cos\alpha_2}\,\cdot\,\tfrac{1}{2}\rho\, V_2^{2}\, c$, giving:

$$
Z_w \;=\; \dfrac{F_{t}}{F_{\text{ref}}} \;=\; \dfrac{\rho V_f^{2} s (\cot\alpha_1 - \cot\alpha_2)}{\rho V_2^{2} c / \cos\alpha_2}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to angle form.**

Since $V_f = V_2 \sin\alpha_2$ and $\cot\alpha_1 - \cot\alpha_2 = \dfrac{\sin(\alpha_2 - \alpha_1)}{\sin\alpha_1 \sin\alpha_2}$:

$$
Z_w \;=\; \dfrac{V_2^{2} \sin^{2}\alpha_2 \cdot s \cdot \dfrac{\sin(\alpha_2-\alpha_1)}{\sin\alpha_1 \sin\alpha_2}}{V_2^{2} c \, / \, \cos\alpha_2}
$$

After trigonometric simplification:

$$
\boxed{\; Z_w \;=\; 2\,\dfrac{s}{c}\,\cos^{2}\alpha_2\,(\tan\alpha_2 - \tan\alpha_1) \;}
$$

This is the form tabulated in Section 2.6 (entry 9). Designers typically target $Z_w \approx 0.8$–$1.2$.

---

## 3.5 Derivation: Loss Coefficient → Stage Efficiency Approximation

**Starting Point:** Total-pressure loss coefficient definition $\zeta = \Delta p_0 / (\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_2^{2})$.

**Step 1 — Kinetic-energy loss.**

The lost kinetic energy per unit mass is:

$$
\Delta \text{KE} \;=\; \dfrac{\Delta p_0}{\rho} \;=\; \zeta \cdot \dfrac{V_2^{2}}{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Stage work output.**

For a stage with loading coefficient $\psi$, the useful work output per unit mass is $w_{\text{ideal}} = \psi\, U^{2}$.

**Step 3 — Stage efficiency.**

$$
\eta_{\text{stage}} \;=\; \dfrac{w_{\text{ideal}} - \Delta\text{KE}}{w_{\text{ideal}}} \;=\; 1 - \dfrac{\zeta\, V_2^{2}/2}{\psi\, U^{2}}
$$

Express $V_2$ in terms of $U$ using the velocity triangle: $V_2^{2} = V_f^{2} + V_{w2}^{2} = (\phi U)^{2} + (\psi U + 0)^{2}$ approximately, yielding:

$$
\boxed{\; \eta_{\text{stage}} \;\approx\; 1 - \dfrac{\zeta}{2\,(1 - \psi/2)} \;}
$$

This KTU-friendly form lets students estimate efficiency directly from $\zeta$ and $\psi$ in design problems.

---

## 3.6 Summary of Derived Master Relations

| # | Master Relation | Origin / Used In |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $w = U(V_{w1}+V_{w2})/g$ | Euler's equation — turbine work |
| 2 | $L = \rho V_f s(V_{w1}+V_{w2})$ | Cascade lift — 2-D momentum |
| 3 | $R = (V_f/2U)(\tan\beta_2-\tan\beta_3)$ | Degree of reaction — axial turbine |
| 4 | $Z_w = 2(s/c)\cos^{2}\alpha_2(\tan\alpha_2-\tan\alpha_1)$ | Zweifel — blade design |
| 5 | $\eta \approx 1 - \zeta/[2(1-\psi/2)]$ | Stage efficiency from loss & loading |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> **Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Applied:**
> - All node IDs are alphanumeric (`n1`, `blade1`, etc.) — no reserved words.

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
Define the following terms with neat sketches: (i) Blade Chord, (ii) Pitch, (iii) Stagger Angle, (iv) Camber Angle.

**Model Answer:**

- **(i) Chord ($c$):** The straight-line distance between the **leading edge** (LE) and **trailing edge** (TE) of an aerofoil blade. It is the dimensional reference for blade size.

- **(ii) Pitch ($s$):** The spacing between corresponding points (LE or TE) of two adjacent blades in a cascade, measured along the circumferential direction for an axial cascade.

- **(iii) Stagger Angle ($\gamma$):** The angle between the chord line of the blade and the plane of the cascade (or the axial direction in an axial cascade). It determines the "tilt" of the airfoil.

- **(iv) Camber Angle ($\phi$):** The angle between the tangents to the mean camber line at the leading and trailing edges. It quantifies the curvature or "bending" of the blade.

> **Examiner Note (3 marks split):** 1 mark for each correctly defined term, with a neat labelled sketch earning the remaining marks cumulatively.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
Distinguish between **Impulse** and **Reaction** blade rows. Give one example each from a real gas turbine.

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Impulse Blade Row | Reaction Blade Row |
|---|---|---|
| Static enthalpy drop | Entirely in the **stator (nozzle)** | Distributed between stator and rotor |
| Static pressure across rotor | Constant | Decreases in rotor (expansion) |
| Relative velocity magnitude across rotor | Constant ($W_2 = W_3$) | $W_3 < W_2$ (accelerates) |
| Reaction $R$ | $R = 0$ | $R > 0$ (often $0.5$ for Parsons) |
| Typical application | Curtis (velocity-compounded) stages | Modern multi-stage HP/LP turbines |
| Example | First stage of some old steam turbines | Modern jet-engine HP turbine |

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU Rule:** Answer **ONE** full question (either Q-A *or* Q-B). Each question has two sub-parts of 7 marks each. Provide all derivations, diagrams, and final boxed answers.

---

### ❑ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — *Velocity Triangle + Cascade Aerodynamics*

#### (a) Derive the Euler Turbomachinery Equation from the angular momentum principle. Show all steps clearly. **[7 marks]**

**Model Answer:**

1. **State the principle:** Apply the angular momentum theorem to a steady-flow control volume enclosing a rotating blade row of mean radius $r_m$ and tangential blade speed $U = \omega r_m$.

2. **Torque equation:**

$$
\tau_{\text{fluid on rotor}} \;=\; \dot{m}\,r_m\,(V_{w2} - V_{w1})
$$

3. **Shaft power:**

$$
\dot W_{\text{shaft}} \;=\; \tau_{\text{fluid on rotor}} \cdot \omega \;=\; \dot{m}\,r_m\,(V_{w2}-V_{w1}) \cdot \dfrac{U}{r_m}
$$

4. **Simplify:**

$$
\dot W_{\text{shaft}} \;=\; \dot{m}\,U\,(V_{w2} - V_{w1})
$$

5. **Work per unit mass (engineering / CGS with $g$):**

$$
w \;=\; \dfrac{U}{g}\,(V_{w1} + V_{w2})
$$

for a turbine in the convention that $V_{w2}$ on the work-producing side is in the direction of $U$.

6. **Final boxed answer:**

$$
\boxed{\; w_{\text{turbine}} \;=\; \dfrac{U}{g}\,(V_{w1} + V_{w2}) \;}
$$

7. **Physical comment:** Work is produced only because the blade row changes the *whirl* component of the gas; the flow (axial) component carries no angular momentum change.

**[Mark Distribution Guide]:** Principle statement — 1; control-volume sketch — 1; torque — 1.5; shaft power — 1.5; per-unit-mass work — 1; boxed answer — 0.5; physical comment — 0.5.

#### (b) For an axial turbine stage with mean blade speed $U = 300$ m/s, mean radius $r_m = 0.4$ m, axial velocity $V_f = 200$ m/s, and inlet relative flow angle $\beta_2 = 30°$. Compute: (i) the relative velocity at inlet, (ii) the absolute velocity at inlet, (iii) the whirl velocity at inlet, (iv) the work done per kg if the whirl velocity drops to $V_{w3} = 50$ m/s at rotor exit. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $U = 300$ m/s, $r_m = 0.4$ m, $V_f = 200$ m/s, $\beta_2 = 30°$, $V_{w3} = 50$ m/s.

**(i) Relative velocity at inlet:**

From the velocity triangle: $W_2 = V_f / \sin\beta_2 = 200 / \sin 30°$.

$$
W_2 = \dfrac{200}{0.5} = 400 \text{ m/s}
$$

**(ii) Whirl velocity at inlet:**

Using $W_w = W \cos\beta$ and $V_{w2} = U - W \cos\beta_2$ (rotor inlet relation):

$$
W \cos\beta_2 = 400 \times \cos 30° = 400 \times 0.866 = 346.4 \text{ m/s}
$$

$$
V_{w2} = 300 - 346.4 = -46.4 \text{ m/s}
$$

The negative sign indicates the whirl is *opposite* to the direction of blade motion at inlet (this is the standard configuration for an axial turbine rotor). The **magnitude** is $\vert V_{w2}\vert = 46.4$ m/s.

**(iii) Absolute velocity at inlet:**

$$
V_2 = \sqrt{V_f^{2} + V_{w2}^{2}} = \sqrt{200^{2} + 46.4^{2}} = \sqrt{40000 + 2153} = \sqrt{42153} \approx 205.3 \text{ m/s}
$$

**(iv) Work done per kg:**

Using $w = U(V_{w1} + V_{w2})/g$. Here, in the consistent convention $V_{w2} = +46.4$ m/s (magnitude, direction of motion), $V_{w3} = 50$ m/s (magnitude). Use the formulation appropriate to the sign convention adopted in the course (KTU textbook — Sutton): work done by the fluid on the rotor per kg:

$$
w = \dfrac{U}{g}\,(V_{w2} + V_{w3}) = \dfrac{300}{9.81}\,(46.4 + 50) = 30.58 \times 96.4 \approx 2948 \text{ J/kg}
$$

(Equivalent: $\approx 3.0$ kJ/kg.)

$$
\boxed{\; w \;\approx\; 2.95 \text{ kJ/kg} \;}
$$

**[Mark split]:** (i) 1.5; (ii) 2; (iii) 1.5; (iv) 2.

---

### ❑ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — *Reaction + Blade Design Parameters*

#### (a) Define Degree of Reaction. For an axial turbine stage with $V_f = 180$ m/s, $U = 280$ m/s, $\beta_2 = 40°$, $\beta_3 = 25°$, compute the degree of reaction and the loading coefficient. **[7 marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** The **Degree of Reaction** $R$ is the ratio of the static enthalpy drop occurring in the rotor to the static enthalpy drop occurring across the entire stage:

$$
R \;=\; \dfrac{\Delta h_{\text{rotor}}}{\Delta h_{\text{stage}}}
$$

For an axial-flow turbine with constant axial velocity, this simplifies to the KTU form:

$$
R \;=\; \dfrac{V_f}{2U}\,(\tan\beta_2 - \tan\beta_3)
$$

**Calculation:**

$$
\tan 40° = 0.8391, \quad \tan 25° = 0.4663
$$

$$
\tan\beta_2 - \tan\beta_3 = 0.8391 - 0.4663 = 0.3728
$$

$$
R = \dfrac{180}{2 \times 280} \times 0.3728 = \dfrac{180}{560} \times 0.3728 = 0.3214 \times 0.3728 \approx 0.12
$$

$$
\boxed{\; R \;\approx\; 0.12 \;}
$$

**Loading Coefficient:** $\psi = w / U^2$. From the velocity triangle, $V_{w2} = U - V_f \tan\beta_2 = 280 - 180(0.8391) = 280 - 151.0 = 129.0$ m/s. $V_{w3} = U - V_f \tan\beta_3 = 280 - 180(0.4663) = 280 - 83.9 = 196.1$ m/s.

$$
\psi = \dfrac{V_{w2} - V_{w3}}{U} = \dfrac{129.0 - 196.1}{280} = \dfrac{-67.1}{280} \approx -0.24
$$

(Negative sign reflects a turbine producing work — students should quote magnitude $\vert\psi\vert \approx 0.24$.)

$$
\boxed{\; \vert\psi\vert \;\approx\; 0.24 \;}
$$

**Interpretation:** With $R = 0.12$, this stage behaves close to an impulse design; ~88% of the static enthalpy drop occurs in the stator.

**[Mark split]:** Definition — 1; formula — 1; substitution — 2; answer — 1; interpretation — 1; loading — 1.

#### (b) Explain the **Zweifel Coefficient**. For a cascade with $\alpha_1 = 30°$, $\alpha_2 = 60°$, and pitch-to-chord ratio $s/c = 1.0$, calculate the Zweifel coefficient and comment on whether the blade loading is acceptable. **[7 marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**Concept (2 marks):** The Zweifel coefficient $Z_w$ is a non-dimensional blade-loading parameter that quantifies how hard a cascade is being "worked" aerodynamically. It compares the actual tangential force on the blade to an ideal reference force (chord × dynamic pressure with normal-to-exit-flow projection). Designers typically aim for $Z_w$ between **0.8 and 1.2** — below this range, the blade is "under-loaded" (large, heavy, costly); above, it risks flow separation and high loss.

**Formula:**

$$
Z_w \;=\; 2\,(s/c)\,\cos^{2}\alpha_2\,(\tan\alpha_2 - \tan\alpha_1)
$$

**Calculation:**

- $\tan 30° = 0.5774$, $\tan 60° = 1.7321$.
- $\cos 60° = 0.5$, so $\cos^{2}\alpha_2 = 0.25$.

$$
\tan\alpha_2 - \tan\alpha_1 = 1.7321 - 0.5774 = 1.1547
$$

$$
Z_w = 2 \times 1.0 \times 0.25 \times 1.1547 = 0.5774
$$

$$
\boxed{\; Z_w \;\approx\; 0.58 \;}
$$

**Comment (2 marks):** $Z_w = 0.58$ is **below the recommended range** of 0.8–1.2. This indicates the cascade is **under-loaded** for the given turning ($\Delta\alpha = 30°$). The remedy is to either:
- Increase the pitch $s$ (reduces $s/c$ – actually this *reduces* $Z_w$; the correct direction is *decrease* $s/c$, i.e. larger chord or smaller pitch) – careful: $Z_w \propto (s/c)$, so reducing pitch reduces $Z_w$, hence we need to *increase* chord or *increase* turning to lift $Z_w$ into the recommended band.
- Re-design with a larger turning angle (higher $\alpha_2$ or lower $\alpha_1$) so that more work is extracted per blade.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Sign Convention on Whirl Velocities:** Many candidates lose 2–3 marks by blindly plugging signed values into $w = U(V_{w1}+V_{w2})/g$ without confirming whether both whirl components are *magnitudes* in the direction of $U$ or carry sign. **Always draw the velocity triangle and label directions first.**
>
> 2. **Degree of Reaction — Wrong Formula:** Confusing $R = (V_f/2U)(\tan\beta_2 - \tan\beta_3)$ with $R = 1 - (V_f/U)\tan\beta_2$. The *correct* formula depends on the sign convention; if the answer is "obviously" negative or greater than 1, **stop and re-check the velocity triangle.**
>
> 3. **Zweifel — Forgetting $\cos^{2}\alpha_2$:** A common slip is to write $Z_w = 2(s/c)(\tan\alpha_2 - \tan\alpha_1)$, omitting the $\cos^{2}\alpha_2$ factor. This gives wrong magnitudes (over-estimate by $\approx 4\times$ for $\alpha_2 = 60°$).
>
> 4. **Aspect Ratio vs. Solidity:** Students interchange AR ($h/c$) and $\sigma$ ($c/s$). Memorize: $\sigma$ is *in-plane*, AR is *out-of-plane* (3-D).
>
> 5. **Lift Force Direction:** Lift is perpendicular to the *relative* velocity $W$, not the absolute $V$. Dropping 1–2 marks for mis-stating this is routine.
>
> 6. **Units of $g$ in Euler Equation:** When $U$ is in m/s and $V_w$ in m/s, the SI form is $w = U \cdot \Delta V_w$ (J/kg). The CGS / engineering form $w = U \cdot \Delta V_w / g$ requires $w$ in kgf·m/kg. Match the form to the question's expected unit. **Showing both and stating the assumption is the safest play.**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Use this as a **last-night-before-exam checklist**. Tick each item once you've verified mastery.

- [ ] **Velocity Triangle** is the single most important tool — always draw it before plugging numbers.
- [ ] **Euler's Equation:** $w = U \cdot \Delta V_w$ (SI) or $w = U(V_{w1}+V_{w2})/g$ (engineering).
- [ ] **Only whirl-component changes produce work.** Axial velocity does not.
- [ ] **Degree of Reaction** $R$ ranges from 0 (impulse) to 1 (pure reaction). 50% reaction = Parsons stage = symmetric triangles.
- [ ] **Solidity** $\sigma = c/s$; **Aspect Ratio** $\text{AR} = h/c$ — never confuse them.
- [ ] **Zweifel Coefficient** $Z_w \in [0.8,\,1.2]$ is the design target; below = under-loaded, above = risky separation.
- [ ] **Lift on a cascade blade:** $L = \rho V_f s (V_{w1} + V_{w2})$ — derived from 2-D momentum theory.
- [ ] **Loss coefficient** $\zeta$ decomposes into profile, secondary, and tip-clearance contributions.
- [ ] **50% Reaction** has equal static enthalpy drop in stator and rotor; rotor inlet relative angle = rotor exit relative angle (in symmetric configuration).
- [ ] **Flow Coefficient** $\phi = V_f/U$ controls incidence and loading; **Loading Coefficient** $\psi = w/U^2$ sets the work per blade.
- [ ] **Stagger angle $\gamma$** is the chord-line tilt; **Camber angle $\phi$** is the mean-line curvature.
- [ ] **Work-done factor $\lambda$** accounts for real-gas and 3-D losses; $\lambda = w_{\text{actual}}/w_{\text{idealEuler}}$.
- [ ] For a 50% reaction stage with axial exit ($V_{w3} = 0$): $V_{w2} = U$, and the work simplifies to $w = U^{2}/g$ in engineering units.
- [ ] **Cascade lift** is *always* perpendicular to the *relative* wind $W$, never to $V$.

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Mantra:** *"Draw triangle → label all angles and components → apply Euler → compute R and Z_w → check units → comment on physical interpretation."* This five-step ritual covers 90% of KTU 2024 Module-2 blade-row questions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Losses
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET741 - GAS TURBINE AND JET PROPULSION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Losses

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of a **Brayton Cycle (Gas Turbine)**, **Losses** refer to the irreversible thermodynamic, mechanical, and fluid dynamic inefficiencies that cause the **actual (real) cycle** to deviate from the **ideal (air-standard) cycle**. These losses are responsible for:

- A reduction in net specific work output
- A decrease in overall thermal efficiency
- An increase in specific fuel consumption (SFC)

In the KTU 2024 syllabus (PEMET741, Module 2), losses are classified as **component-level losses** occurring across the four primary gas turbine stations:

| Station | Component | Primary Loss Type |
|---|---|---|
| 1–2 | **Intake / Diffuser** | Ram pressure loss, kinetic energy dissipation |
| 2–3 | **Compressor** | Internal fluid friction & non-isentropic compression |
| 3–4 | **Combustion Chamber** | Pressure loss, incomplete combustion, heat loss |
| 4–5 | **Turbine** | Non-isentropic expansion, blade friction |
| 5 | **Exhaust** | Unconverted kinetic energy (exit loss) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Note:** Every loss is expressed as either a **pressure drop (Δp)**, an **efficiency (η < 1)**, or a **percentage of work/heat (%).** Marks are awarded in Part B questions when students correctly identify which type of loss each component contributes to the overall cycle degradation.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are **blowing air through a long, bent, slightly leaky garden hose** to inflate a balloon.

- **No matter how hard you blow**, some energy is lost in the bends (this is like **compressor internal loss**).
- **Some air leaks out the side** before reaching the balloon (this is like **combustion chamber pressure loss**).
- **The balloon doesn't fill as much** as it should, even though you used the same energy (this is like **reduced net work output**).

In a gas turbine, **air is the working fluid** and the "hose" is the combination of the **compressor, combustor, and turbine**. Every component eats away a portion of the energy, and the leftover (after subtracting all losses) is the **useful shaft work** that drives the load.

## 1.3 Key Physical Quantities Used to Quantify Losses

- **Isentropic Efficiency of Compressor (η_C):** typically **0.85 – 0.90**
- **Isentropic Efficiency of Turbine (η_T):** typically **0.88 – 0.92**
- **Combustion Pressure Loss (Δp_3 / p_3):** typically **3 % – 5 %**
- **Mechanical Transmission Efficiency (η_m):** typically **0.97 – 0.99**
- **Specific Heat Ratio (γ):** **1.4** for air (ideal), **1.33** for combustion products
- **Gas Constant (R):** **287 J/kg·K** for air

> [!NOTE]
> The **golden rule of gas turbine design**: Every 1% loss in compressor or turbine efficiency reduces the **net specific work by approximately 2–3%**. Hence, even small losses have a magnified impact on cycle performance.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Losses — Step-by-Step Logic

The actual Brayton cycle is built by introducing loss parameters one by one. The KTU module focuses on six primary loss categories.

### 2.1.1 Compressor Losses (Process 2 → 3)

**Why they occur:**
- Air is a **real, viscous fluid**; internal friction between air layers creates entropy.
- Blade surface friction, tip-clearance leakage, and shock losses at high pressure ratios.
- The compression is **not isentropic**, and the **actual work of compression is higher** than the ideal work.

**How to model it:**
- Introduced via **isentropic efficiency of compressor (η_C)**, where $0 < \eta_C < 1$.
- The actual exit temperature T3 is **higher** than the ideal isentropic T3s.

**Mathematical representation:**

$$W_{C,\text{actual}} = \frac{W_{C,\text{isentropic}}}{\eta_C}$$

$$T_3 = T_2 + \frac{T_{3s} - T_2}{\eta_C}$$

### 2.1.2 Combustion Chamber Losses (Process 3 → 4)

**Why they occur:**
- **Incomplete combustion** of fuel due to poor mixing or insufficient residence time.
- **Heat loss to the walls** of the combustor (cooling, radiation).
- **Pressure drop** across the combustor due to flow restriction (baffles, liners).

**How to model it:**
- Modelled as a **percentage pressure loss** $\Delta p_{comb}$ (typically 3–5% of combustor inlet pressure $p_3$).
- Also modelled as a **combustion efficiency $\eta_B$** ($< 1$) representing the fraction of fuel energy actually released.

**Mathematical representation:**

$$p_4 = p_3 \left(1 - \frac{\Delta p_{comb}}{p_3}\right)$$

$$Q_{\text{in,actual}} = \eta_B \cdot Q_{\text{in,ideal}}$$

### 2.1.3 Turbine Losses (Process 4 → 5)

**Why they occur:**
- **Blade friction, tip-clearance leakage, and exit swirl** in the turbine.
- Expansion is non-isentropic, so **actual work output is less** than isentropic work.

**How to model it:**
- Modelled via **isentropic efficiency of turbine (η_T)**.
- The actual exit temperature T5 is **higher** than the ideal isentropic T5s.

**Mathematical representation:**

$$W_{T,\text{actual}} = \eta_T \cdot W_{T,\text{isentropic}}$$

$$T_5 = T_4 - \eta_T \cdot (T_4 - T_{5s})$$

### 2.1.4 Intake / Diffuser Loss (Process 1 → 2)

**Why it occurs:** Friction in the inlet duct converts some kinetic energy into heat (irreversibility).

**Modelling:** A small **ram efficiency $\eta_D$** (< 1) or an equivalent **pressure loss factor**.

$$p_2 = p_1 \cdot \eta_D$$

### 2.1.5 Exhaust / Exit Kinetic Energy Loss

**Why it occurs:** The hot gas leaving the turbine still possesses **kinetic energy** that is not converted to useful shaft work.

**Modelling:** Treated as a **residual enthalpy term** in the energy balance.

### 2.1.6 Mechanical Loss

**Why it occurs:** Friction in bearings, power consumed by oil pumps, fuel pumps, and auxiliaries.

**Modelling:** A **mechanical efficiency $\eta_m$** (< 1) applied to net work.

$$W_{\text{shaft}} = \eta_m \cdot (W_T - W_C)$$

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Loss Type | Symbol | Governing Formula | Typical Value |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Compressor isentropic efficiency | $\eta_C$ | $\eta_C = \dfrac{W_{C,\text{isen}}}{W_{C,\text{actual}}} = \dfrac{T_{3s}-T_2}{T_3-T_2}$ | 0.85 – 0.90 |
| 2 | Turbine isentropic efficiency | $\eta_T$ | $\eta_T = \dfrac{W_{T,\text{actual}}}{W_{T,\text{isen}}} = \dfrac{T_4-T_5}{T_4-T_{5s}}$ | 0.88 – 0.92 |
| 3 | Combustion pressure loss | $\Delta p_{comb}$ | $p_4 = p_3 - \Delta p_{comb}$ | 3–5 % of $p_3$ |
| 4 | Combustion efficiency | $\eta_B$ | $\eta_B = \dfrac{Q_{\text{actual}}}{Q_{\text{fuel}}}$ | 0.97 – 0.99 |
| 5 | Diffuser / Intake efficiency | $\eta_D$ | $\eta_D = \dfrac{h_{2s}-h_1}{h_2-h_1}$ | 0.92 – 0.98 |
| 6 | Mechanical efficiency | $\eta_m$ | $W_{\text{net,shaft}} = \eta_m (W_T - W_C)$ | 0.97 – 0.99 |
| 7 | Overall thermal efficiency | $\eta_{\text{th}}$ | $\eta_{\text{th}} = \dfrac{W_{\text{net}}}{Q_{\text{in}}}$ | 0.30 – 0.42 |
| 8 | Pressure ratio | $r_p$ | $r_p = \dfrac{p_2}{p_1} = \dfrac{p_3}{p_2}$ | 6 – 25 (modern) |
| 9 | Isentropic temperature relation | $T_{3s}/T_2$ | $T_{3s}/T_2 = (p_3/p_2)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$ | — |
| 10 | Net specific work (with losses) | $w_{\text{net}}$ | $w_{\text{net}} = \eta_m \eta_T w_T - \dfrac{w_{C,\text{isen}}}{\eta_C}$ | — |

> [!NOTE]
> The relation $T_{3s}/T_2 = (p_3/p_2)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$ is **non-negotiable** in KTU derivations. Memorize it as a power-law relation between temperature ratio and pressure ratio.

## 2.3 Real-World Utility

These loss models are not just academic — they directly influence:

- **Aerospace engine design** (GE, Rolls-Royce, Pratt & Whitney) where every fraction of a percent in efficiency translates to millions of dollars of fuel saved over a fleet's lifetime.
- **Industrial power generation gas turbines** (Siemens, MHPS) where combined cycle plants achieve **> 60% efficiency** by carefully minimizing combustor pressure loss and turbine exit losses.
- **Marine propulsion (COGAS, CODOG)** where gas turbines drive ships — losses directly affect fuel bunker cost and range.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Net Work Output With All Major Losses

**Goal:** Express the net specific work output of the actual Brayton cycle, accounting for compressor, turbine, and mechanical losses.

### Step 1: Ideal (Isentropic) Compressor Work

The work required to compress air from $p_2$ to $p_3$ isentropically (i.e., reversible adiabatic):

$$W_{C,\text{isen}} = c_{p,a} (T_{3s} - T_2)$$

where $c_{p,a}$ is the specific heat of air at constant pressure (**1005 J/kg·K**).

### Step 2: Isentropic Temperature Relation

For an isentropic process, the temperature–pressure relation is:

$$\frac{T_{3s}}{T_2} = \left(\frac{p_3}{p_2}\right)^{(\gamma_a-1)/\gamma_a}$$

Hence:

$$T_{3s} = T_2 \left(\frac{p_3}{p_2}\right)^{(\gamma_a-1)/\gamma_a}$$

### Step 3: Actual Compressor Work (Introducing $\eta_C$)

By definition of isentropic efficiency:

$$\eta_C = \frac{W_{C,\text{isen}}}{W_{C,\text{actual}}} = \frac{c_{p,a}(T_{3s}-T_2)}{c_{p,a}(T_3-T_2)}$$

Therefore:

$$T_3 = T_2 + \frac{T_{3s} - T_2}{\eta_C}$$

And the actual compressor work is:

$$W_{C,\text{actual}} = \frac{c_{p,a}(T_{3s}-T_2)}{\eta_C}$$

### Step 4: Ideal (Isentropic) Turbine Work

For the turbine, expansion is from $p_4$ to $p_5$:

$$W_{T,\text{isen}} = c_{p,g}(T_4 - T_{5s})$$

where $c_{p,g}$ is the specific heat of combustion gases (**1148 J/kg·K** for typical products).

The isentropic relation gives:

$$T_{5s} = T_4 \left(\frac{p_5}{p_4}\right)^{(\gamma_g-1)/\gamma_g}$$

### Step 5: Actual Turbine Work (Introducing $\eta_T$)

By definition:

$$\eta_T = \frac{W_{T,\text{actual}}}{W_{T,\text{isen}}} = \frac{T_4 - T_5}{T_4 - T_{5s}}$$

Rearranging:

$$T_5 = T_4 - \eta_T (T_4 - T_{5s})$$

So the actual turbine work is:

$$W_{T,\text{actual}} = \eta_T \cdot c_{p,g}(T_4 - T_{5s})$$

### Step 6: Net Ideal Work

$$W_{\text{net,isen}} = W_{T,\text{isen}} - W_{C,\text{isen}}$$

### Step 7: Net Actual Work (Before Mechanical Loss)

$$W_{\text{net,actual}} = \eta_T \cdot c_{p,g}(T_4 - T_{5s}) - \frac{c_{p,a}(T_{3s}-T_2)}{\eta_C}$$

### Step 8: Shaft Work (Applying Mechanical Efficiency $\eta_m$)

$$W_{\text{shaft}} = \eta_m \cdot W_{\text{net,actual}}$$

$$W_{\text{shaft}} = \eta_m \left[ \eta_T \cdot c_{p,g}(T_4 - T_{5s}) - \frac{c_{p,a}(T_{3s}-T_2)}{\eta_C} \right]$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This **single final expression** is the **key formula** for the KTU 14-mark problems. It captures **three simultaneous losses** in one elegant equation.

## 3.2 Derivation: Overall Thermal Efficiency With Losses

### Step 1: Heat Input

Heat is added in the combustion chamber (process 3 → 4):

$$Q_{\text{in}} = c_{p,g}(T_4 - T_3)$$

If combustion efficiency $\eta_B$ is considered:

$$Q_{\text{in,actual}} = \eta_B \cdot c_{p,g}(T_4 - T_3)$$

### Step 2: Thermal Efficiency

$$\eta_{\text{th}} = \frac{W_{\text{net,actual}}}{Q_{\text{in,actual}}}$$

$$\eta_{\text{th}} = \frac{\eta_m \left[\eta_T \cdot c_{p,g}(T_4 - T_{5s}) - \dfrac{c_{p,a}(T_{3s}-T_2)}{\eta_C}\right]}{c_{p,g}(T_4 - T_3)}$$

### Step 3: Practical Simplification for KTU Problems

In most KTU numerical problems, the working fluid is treated as a single gas with a constant specific heat (either $c_{p,a}$ or $c_{p,g}$ for the entire cycle, depending on the assumption). Under the **cold-air-standard assumption**:

$$c_{p,a} = c_{p,g} = c_p = 1005 \text{ J/kg·K}$$

The expression collapses to:

$$\eta_{\text{th}} = \frac{\eta_m \eta_T (T_4 - T_{5s}) - (T_{3s} - T_2)/\eta_C}{T_4 - T_3}$$

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Pattern)

**Problem Statement:**
A gas turbine operates with a pressure ratio of **8**. The inlet temperature is **300 K**, and the turbine inlet temperature (TIT) is **1300 K**. Take $\gamma = 1.4$, $c_p = 1005$ J/kg·K, $\eta_C = 0.88$, $\eta_T = 0.90$, $\eta_m = 0.98$. Calculate:
(a) Net specific work output (in kJ/kg)
(b) Thermal efficiency
(c) Comment on the effect of $\eta_C$ dropping to 0.80.

### Step 1: Compute $T_{3s}$ (Isentropic Compressor Exit)

$$T_{3s} = T_2 \cdot r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$$

$$T_{3s} = 300 \times 8^{(0.4/1.4)} = 300 \times 8^{0.2857}$$

Computing $8^{0.2857}$:

$$8^{0.2857} = e^{0.2857 \cdot \ln 8} = e^{0.2857 \cdot 2.0794} = e^{0.5941} \approx 1.8114$$

Therefore:

$$T_{3s} = 300 \times 1.8114 = 543.43 \text{ K}$$

### Step 2: Compute Actual Compressor Exit Temperature $T_3$

$$T_3 = T_2 + \frac{T_{3s} - T_2}{\eta_C}$$

$$T_3 = 300 + \frac{543.43 - 300}{0.88} = 300 + \frac{243.43}{0.88}$$

$$T_3 = 300 + 276.62 = 576.62 \text{ K}$$

### Step 3: Compute Compressor Work

$$W_C = c_p (T_3 - T_2) = 1005 \times (576.62 - 300) = 1005 \times 276.62$$

$$W_C = 278{,}003 \text{ J/kg} \approx 278.0 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

### Step 4: Compute $T_{5s}$ (Isentropic Turbine Exit)

$$T_{5s} = T_4 \cdot \left(\frac{1}{r_p}\right)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} = 1300 \times (1/8)^{0.2857}$$

$$T_{5s} = 1300 \times 1.8114^{-1} = \frac{1300}{1.8114} = 717.66 \text{ K}$$

### Step 5: Compute Actual Turbine Exit Temperature $T_5$

$$T_5 = T_4 - \eta_T (T_4 - T_{5s})$$

$$T_5 = 1300 - 0.90 \times (1300 - 717.66) = 1300 - 0.90 \times 582.34$$

$$T_5 = 1300 - 524.11 = 775.89 \text{ K}$$

### Step 6: Compute Turbine Work

$$W_T = c_p (T_4 - T_5) = 1005 \times (1300 - 775.89)$$

$$W_T = 1005 \times 524.11 = 526{,}730 \text{ J/kg} \approx 526.7 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

### Step 7: Net Work With Mechanical Loss

$$W_{\text{shaft}} = \eta_m (W_T - W_C) = 0.98 \times (526.7 - 278.0)$$

$$W_{\text{shaft}} = 0.98 \times 248.7 = 243.7 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

### Step 8: Thermal Efficiency

$$Q_{\text{in}} = c_p (T_4 - T_3) = 1005 \times (1300 - 576.62) = 1005 \times 723.38$$

$$Q_{\text{in}} = 727{,}000 \text{ J/kg} = 727.0 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

$$\eta_{\text{th}} = \frac{243.7}{727.0} = 0.3352 \approx 33.5\%$$

### Step 9: Effect of $\eta_C$ Dropping to 0.80

Recompute $T_3$ with $\eta_C = 0.80$:

$$T_3 = 300 + \frac{243.43}{0.80} = 300 + 304.29 = 604.29 \text{ K}$$

$$W_C = 1005 \times (604.29 - 300) = 1005 \times 304.29 = 305{,}811 \text{ J/kg} \approx 305.8 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

New net work:

$$W_{\text{net,shaft}} = 0.98 \times (526.7 - 305.8) = 0.98 \times 220.9 = 216.5 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

New efficiency:

$$\eta_{\text{th}} = \frac{216.5}{1005 \times (1300 - 604.29)} = \frac{216.5}{700.0} = 0.3093 \approx 30.9\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Drop in net work:** $243.7 - 216.5 = 27.2$ kJ/kg (≈ **11.2% reduction**)
> **Drop in efficiency:** $33.5\% - 30.9\% = 2.6$ percentage points.
> This proves the **golden rule**: small efficiency losses cause large work and efficiency penalties.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Loss Distribution Block Diagram (Mermaid)

The following block diagram maps how losses propagate through the gas turbine system, showing the interaction of each component's loss mechanism with the overall energy flow.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Fresh Air Inlet T1, p1] --> B[Intake Diffuser]
    B --> C{eta_D = 0.95}
    C --> D[Compressed Air T2, p2]
    D --> E[Compressor]
    E --> F{eta_C = 0.88}
    F --> G[High Pressure Air T3, p3]
    G --> H[Combustion Chamber]
    H --> I{Fuel Added Q_in}
    I --> J{Delta p_comb = 4 percent}
    J --> K[Hot Gas T4, p4 reduced]
    K --> L[Turbine]
    L --> M{eta_T = 0.90}
    M --> N[Exhaust Gas T5, p5]
    N --> O[Exhaust Nozzle]
    O --> P{Exit Kinetic Loss}
    P --> Q[Shaft Work Output]
    Q --> R{eta_m = 0.98}
    R --> S[Useful Shaft Power W_shaft]

    style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd
    style S fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style F fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
    style J fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
    style M fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
    style R fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
    style P fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c
```

## 4.2 Loss-Cycle T-s Diagram Description

In a **Temperature-Entropy (T-s) diagram**, the actual cycle appears as a **skewed quadrilateral** deviating from the ideal rectangle:

- **Process 1–2 (Isentropic compression in ideal):** Vertical line going up.
- **Process 2–3 (Actual compression with $\eta_C < 1$):** Tilts to the right (entropy increases), making T3 higher than T3s.
- **Process 3–4 (Combustion at lower $p_4$):** A nearly horizontal line, but with $p_4 < p_3$ due to combustor pressure loss.
- **Process 4–5 (Actual expansion with $\eta_T < 1$):** Tilts to the right (entropy increases), making T5 higher than T5s.
- **The enclosed area** of the **actual cycle is smaller** than the ideal cycle → **less work output**.

| Feature | Ideal Cycle | Actual Cycle (with Losses) |
|---|---|---|
| Compression line (1–2) | Vertical (isentropic) | Slanted right |
| Combustion line (3–4) | Horizontal at $p_3$ | Slightly lower at $p_4 = p_3 - \Delta p$ |
| Expansion line (4–5) | Vertical (isentropic) | Slanted right |
| Cycle area | Maximum (ideal work) | Reduced (actual work) |
| Net work | $W_{T,\text{isen}} - W_{C,\text{isen}}$ | $\eta_T W_{T,\text{isen}} - W_{C,\text{isen}}/\eta_C$ |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
Define **isentropic efficiency of a compressor** and write the mathematical expression relating actual and isentropic compression work.

**Model Answer:**
The isentropic efficiency of a compressor ($\eta_C$) is defined as the ratio of the **isentropic (ideal) work required for compression** to the **actual work required for compression**, both measured at the same inlet and exit pressures.

$$\eta_C = \frac{W_{C,\text{isentropic}}}{W_{C,\text{actual}}} = \frac{T_{3s} - T_2}{T_3 - T_2}$$

For an ideal compressor, $\eta_C = 1$; for an actual compressor, $\eta_C < 1$. Typical values lie between **0.85 and 0.90**.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
List **any four major losses** in an actual gas turbine cycle and state how each is modelled mathematically.

**Model Answer:**

| # | Loss | Mathematical Model |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Compressor internal loss | $\eta_C = (T_{3s} - T_2) / (T_3 - T_2)$ |
| 2 | Combustion pressure loss | $p_4 = p_3 (1 - \Delta p_{comb}/p_3)$ |
| 3 | Turbine internal loss | $\eta_T = (T_4 - T_5) / (T_4 - T_{5s})$ |
| 4 | Mechanical loss | $W_{\text{shaft}} = \eta_m (W_T - W_C)$ |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks)

### Question A (Internal Choice Option 1)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]**
A gas turbine has the following operating data:

- Pressure ratio, $r_p = 10$
- Compressor inlet temperature, $T_2 = 300$ K
- Turbine inlet temperature, $T_4 = 1400$ K
- $\gamma = 1.4$, $c_p = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K
- Compressor isentropic efficiency, $\eta_C = 0.86$
- Turbine isentropic efficiency, $\eta_T = 0.90$
- Mechanical efficiency, $\eta_m = 0.97$

**Part (a) [7 Marks]:** Calculate the **net specific work output** and the **thermal efficiency** of the cycle.

**Part (b) [7 Marks]:** If a **regenerator of effectiveness 0.75** is added, calculate the **new thermal efficiency** and the **percentage improvement**.

#### Solution to Part (a):

**Step 1: Compute isentropic compressor exit temperature**

$$T_{3s} = T_2 \cdot r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} = 300 \times 10^{0.4/1.4} = 300 \times 10^{0.2857}$$

Computing $10^{0.2857}$:

$$10^{0.2857} = e^{0.2857 \cdot \ln 10} = e^{0.2857 \cdot 2.3026} = e^{0.6578} \approx 1.9307$$

$$T_{3s} = 300 \times 1.9307 = 579.2 \text{ K}$$

**Step 2: Compute actual compressor exit temperature**

$$T_3 = T_2 + \frac{T_{3s} - T_2}{\eta_C} = 300 + \frac{579.2 - 300}{0.86} = 300 + 324.65 = 624.65 \text{ K}$$

**Step 3: Compute actual compressor work**

$$W_C = c_p (T_3 - T_2) = 1.005 \times (624.65 - 300) = 1.005 \times 324.65 = 326.27 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Step 4: Compute isentropic turbine exit temperature**

$$T_{5s} = \frac{T_4}{r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}} = \frac{1400}{1.9307} = 725.1 \text{ K}$$

**Step 5: Compute actual turbine exit temperature**

$$T_5 = T_4 - \eta_T (T_4 - T_{5s}) = 1400 - 0.90 \times (1400 - 725.1) = 1400 - 607.41 = 792.59 \text{ K}$$

**Step 6: Compute actual turbine work**

$$W_T = c_p (T_4 - T_5) = 1.005 \times (1400 - 792.59) = 1.005 \times 607.41 = 610.45 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Step 7: Net work with mechanical efficiency**

$$W_{\text{shaft}} = \eta_m (W_T - W_C) = 0.97 \times (610.45 - 326.27) = 0.97 \times 284.18 = 275.65 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Step 8: Heat input and thermal efficiency**

$$Q_{\text{in}} = c_p (T_4 - T_3) = 1.005 \times (1400 - 624.65) = 1.005 \times 775.35 = 779.23 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

$$\eta_{\text{th}} = \frac{W_{\text{shaft}}}{Q_{\text{in}}} = \frac{275.65}{779.23} = 0.3538 \approx 35.4\%$$

**Final Answer (a):** $W_{\text{shaft}} = 275.65$ kJ/kg, $\eta_{\text{th}} = 35.4\%$

#### Solution to Part (b):

**Step 1: Regenerator effectiveness definition**

A regenerator preheats the compressed air using waste heat from the turbine exhaust:

$$T_3' = T_3 + \varepsilon (T_5 - T_3)$$

where $\varepsilon = 0.75$ is the regenerator effectiveness.

**Step 2: Compute new compressor exit temperature (regenerator outlet)**

$$T_3' = 624.65 + 0.75 \times (792.59 - 624.65) = 624.65 + 0.75 \times 167.94$$

$$T_3' = 624.65 + 125.96 = 750.61 \text{ K}$$

**Step 3: New heat input (reduced due to regeneration)**

$$Q_{\text{in,regen}} = c_p (T_4 - T_3') = 1.005 \times (1400 - 750.61) = 1.005 \times 649.39 = 652.64 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Step 4: New thermal efficiency**

$$\eta_{\text{th,regen}} = \frac{W_{\text{shaft}}}{Q_{\text{in,regen}}} = \frac{275.65}{652.64} = 0.4224 \approx 42.2\%$$

**Step 5: Percentage improvement**

$$\% \text{ Improvement} = \frac{42.2 - 35.4}{35.4} \times 100 = \frac{6.8}{35.4} \times 100 \approx 19.2\%$$

**Final Answer (b):** $\eta_{\text{th,regen}} = 42.2\%$, **improvement = 19.2%**

---

### Question B (Internal Choice Option 2)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
**(a) [7 Marks]:** With the help of a **T-s diagram**, explain the difference between the **ideal and actual Brayton cycle** of a gas turbine. Discuss the major losses introduced and their effect on net work output.

**(b) [7 Marks]:** A simple open-cycle gas turbine has the following data:
- Pressure ratio = 6
- Air inlet temperature = 27 °C = **300 K**
- Maximum cycle temperature = **1000 K**
- $\eta_C = 0.85$, $\eta_T = 0.88$, $\eta_m = 0.98$
- $c_p = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K, $\gamma = 1.4$

Compute the **net power output per kg/s of air flow** and the **work ratio**.

#### Solution to Part (a):

**Ideal Cycle (Reference):**
- Process 1–2: Isentropic compression (vertical line in T-s diagram)
- Process 2–3: Constant pressure heat addition (horizontal line)
- Process 3–4: Isentropic expansion (vertical line)
- Process 4–1: Constant pressure heat rejection (horizontal line)

**Actual Cycle (With Losses):**

| Process | Ideal | Actual | Reason |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1–2 | Isentropic | $p_2 < \text{ideal}$ (loss) | Intake friction |
| 2–3 | Vertical in T-s | Tilts right | Compressor inefficiency, $\eta_C < 1$ |
| 3–4 | $p_4 = p_3$ | $p_4 < p_3$ | Combustion pressure loss |
| 4–5 | Vertical in T-s | Tilts right | Turbine inefficiency, $\eta_T < 1$ |

**Major Losses and Their Effect on Net Work:**

1. **Compressor Loss ($\eta_C = 0.85$):** The actual T3 > T3s, so **compressor work increases** by factor $1/\eta_C$.
2. **Turbine Loss ($\eta_T = 0.88$):** The actual T5 > T5s, so **turbine work decreases** by factor $\eta_T$.
3. **Combustion Pressure Loss:** Reduces the effective pressure ratio across the turbine.
4. **Mechanical Loss ($\eta_m = 0.98$):** Net shaft work is slightly less than net gas work.

**Net Work Penalty:**

$$W_{\text{net,actual}} = \eta_T W_{T,\text{isen}} - \frac{W_{C,\text{isen}}}{\eta_C}$$

If the ideal net work is $W_{\text{ideal}}$, then $W_{\text{actual}} \ll W_{\text{ideal}}$, often **15–25% less**.

#### Solution to Part (b):

**Step 1: Compute isentropic compressor exit temperature**

$$T_{3s} = T_2 \cdot r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} = 300 \times 6^{0.4/1.4} = 300 \times 6^{0.2857}$$

Computing $6^{0.2857}$:

$$6^{0.2857} = e^{0.2857 \cdot \ln 6} = e^{0.2857 \cdot 1.7918} = e^{0.5119} \approx 1.6685$$

$$T_{3s} = 300 \times 1.6685 = 500.55 \text{ K}$$

**Step 2: Compute actual compressor exit temperature**

$$T_3 = T_2 + \frac{T_{3s} - T_2}{\eta_C} = 300 + \frac{500.55 - 300}{0.85} = 300 + 235.94 = 535.94 \text{ K}$$

**Step 3: Actual compressor work**

$$W_C = c_p (T_3 - T_2) = 1.005 \times (535.94 - 300) = 1.005 \times 235.94 = 237.12 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Step 4: Isentropic turbine exit temperature**

$$T_{5s} = \frac{T_4}{r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}} = \frac{1000}{1.6685} = 599.35 \text{ K}$$

**Step 5: Actual turbine exit temperature**

$$T_5 = T_4 - \eta_T (T_4 - T_{5s}) = 1000 - 0.88 \times (1000 - 599.35)$$

$$T_5 = 1000 - 0.88 \times 400.65 = 1000 - 352.57 = 647.43 \text{ K}$$

**Step 6: Actual turbine work**

$$W_T = c_p (T_4 - T_5) = 1.005 \times (1000 - 647.43) = 1.005 \times 352.57 = 354.34 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Step 7: Net power per kg/s (with mechanical efficiency)**

$$W_{\text{shaft}} = \eta_m (W_T - W_C) = 0.98 \times (354.34 - 237.12) = 0.98 \times 117.22 = 114.88 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Net Power per kg/s = 114.88 kW (per kg/s of air flow)**

**Step 8: Work Ratio**

Work ratio is defined as:

$$\text{Work Ratio} = \frac{W_{\text{net}}}{W_T} = \frac{117.22}{354.34} = 0.331$$

**Final Answer (b):** $W_{\text{shaft}} = 114.88$ kJ/kg (or 114.88 kW per kg/s), **Work Ratio = 0.331**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to apply $\eta_C$ and $\eta_T$:** Many students compute $T_3$ and $T_5$ using only isentropic relations, missing 4–5 marks.
> 2. **Mixing up $\eta_C$ and $\eta_T$ definitions:** $\eta_C$ is on the *work output* (denominator), but $\eta_T$ is on the *work output* (numerator). Confusing them gives wrong sign.
> 3. **Using $c_p = 1005$ J/kg·K inconsistently:** Some textbooks use $c_{p,a} = 1005$ and $c_{p,g} = 1148$. In the **cold air standard**, both are **1005 J/kg·K**.
> 4. **Forgetting $\eta_m$:** Mechanical efficiency is a **mandatory final step** for shaft work. Skipping it loses 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Sign errors in $T_5$ calculation:** $T_5 = T_4 - \eta_T(T_4 - T_{5s})$, not $T_4 + \eta_T \cdot \ldots$

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — "Losses" (Module 2, PEMET741)**

- **Six Major Losses:** Intake, Compressor, Combustion (pressure + incomplete), Turbine, Exhaust (kinetic), Mechanical.
- **Compressor Loss:** Modelled by $\eta_C = (T_{3s}-T_2)/(T_3-T_2)$. Actual $T_3 > T_{3s}$.
- **Turbine Loss:** Modelled by $\eta_T = (T_4-T_5)/(T_4-T_{5s})$. Actual $T_5 > T_{5s}$.
- **Combustion Loss:** Modelled as pressure drop $\Delta p_{comb} = (3\text{–}5\%) \cdot p_3$ **or** combustion efficiency $\eta_B$.
- **Mechanical Loss:** $W_{\text{shaft}} = \eta_m (W_T - W_C)$.
- **Master Formula:**
$$W_{\text{net,shaft}} = \eta_m \left[ \eta_T c_{p,g} (T_4 - T_{5s}) - \frac{c_{p,a}(T_{3s}-T_2)}{\eta_C} \right]$$
- **Isentropic Relation (memorize):** $T_{3s}/T_2 = (p_3/p_2)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$.
- **Typical Values:** $\eta_C \approx 0.85\text{–}0.90$, $\eta_T \approx 0.88\text{–}0.92$, $\eta_m \approx 0.97\text{–}0.99$.
- **Golden Rule:** A 1% drop in $\eta_C$ or $\eta_T$ causes **2–3% drop in net work** — losses are magnified.
- **T-s Diagram Visual:** Actual cycle lines tilt **rightward** (entropy generation); cycle area shrinks, indicating reduced work.
- **Regenerator as a Loss-Mitigation Device:** Recovers some exhaust heat, raises $\eta_{\text{th}}$ by **15–25%**, but is **not a "loss"** itself — it is a cycle improvement.
- **In Part B problems, ALWAYS:**
  1. Compute $T_{3s}$ first using isentropic relation.
  2. Apply $\eta_C$ to get $T_3$.
  3. Compute $W_C$, $T_{5s}$, $T_5$, $W_T$.
  4. Apply $\eta_m$ to get $W_{\text{shaft}}$.
  5. Compute $Q_{\text{in}}$ and $\eta_{\text{th}}$.
- **Units Check:** All temperatures in **Kelvin (K)**; all work in **kJ/kg** (use $c_p$ in kJ/kg·K = 1.005).
- **Examiner Tip:** Always **state assumptions** at the start of a 14-mark answer (e.g., "Assuming cold air standard, constant specific heats, neglect of kinetic energy changes unless specified"). This earns **1 mark** for "approach clarity."

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Performance Characteristics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET741 - GAS TURBINE AND JET PROPULSION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Performance Characteristics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Performance Characteristics** of a gas turbine refer to the systematic study of how key output parameters — namely **specific power output (SP)**, **specific fuel consumption (SFC)**, **thermal efficiency (η_th)**, and **work ratio** — vary with operating variables such as **pressure ratio (r_p)**, **turbine inlet temperature (T_3)**, **compressor isentropic efficiency (η_c)**, and **turbine isentropic efficiency (η_t)**.

In engineering terms, performance characteristics provide the parametric "fingerprint" of a gas turbine plant, enabling the designer to predict off-design behaviour, select optimum design points, and match the compressor and turbine on a common operating line.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

Imagine a **bicycle gear system**: pedalling fast (high T_3) gives you more thrust but uses more "fuel" (your energy). Changing gear ratio (analogous to pressure ratio r_p) changes how efficiently your effort translates into motion. Some gears (low r_p) waste energy as heat; some (very high r_p) lose too much energy compressing air without enough expansion. The "sweet spot" gear — the one where you travel farthest per calorie burnt — is exactly the **optimum pressure ratio** of a gas turbine. Performance characteristics is the engineering chart that maps these trade-offs.

## 1.3 Core Performance Metrics
