> KTU ESE pattern: Two sub-parts of 7 marks each. Solve **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Module 3, PEMET741]`

**Q: (a)** With the help of a neat diagram, explain the working of a **subsonic diffuser** in a gas turbine. State and derive the steady flow energy equation used to analyze the diffuser.

**Q: (b)** Air at $p_1 = 90$ kPa, $T_1 = 290$ K enters a subsonic diffuser with a velocity $V_1 = 150$ m/s. The exit velocity is $V_2 = 50$ m/s. Assuming the diffuser is **(i)** isentropic and **(ii)** has an adiabatic efficiency of $0.92$, determine the **exit static pressure** $p_2$ in each case. Take $\gamma = 1.4$, $c_p = 1005$ J/kg·K.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 Marks)

**Diagram (2 Marks):** Draw the gas turbine schematic (filter → diffuser → compressor → CC → turbine → exhaust) and highlight the diffuser position.

**Explanation (3 Marks):**
- Function of diffuser — convert KE to pressure energy.
- It is a divergent passage for subsonic flow.
- Located between filter/silencer and compressor.
- Recovers part of the velocity head before compression, reducing compressor work.

**Derivation of SFEE (2 Marks):**

$$ h_1 + \frac{V_1^2}{2} + g z_1 + q = h_2 + \frac{V_2^2}{2} + g z_2 + w $$

Applying assumptions:
- Adiabatic: $q = 0$
- No work: $w = 0$
- Horizontal: $z_1 = z_2$

$$ \Rightarrow \; h_1 + \frac{V_1^2}{2} = h_2 + \frac{V_2^2}{2} $$

For ideal gas, $h = c_p T$:

$$ c_p T_1 + \frac{V_1^2}{2} = c_p T_2 + \frac{V_2^2}{2} $$

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 Marks)

**Given:**
$p_1 = 90$ kPa, $T_1 = 290$ K, $V_1 = 150$ m/s, $V_2 = 50$ m/s, $\gamma = 1.4$, $c_p = 1005$ J/kg·K.

**Step 1 — Inlet stagnation temperature:**

$$ T_{01} = T_1 + \frac{V_1^2}{2 c_p} = 290 + \frac{(150)^2}{2 \times 1005} = 290 + \frac{22500}{2010} = 290 + 11.194 = 301.194 \;\text{K} $$

**Step 2 — Inlet stagnation pressure** (assuming inlet is isentropic up to station 1, so $p_{01} = p_1 = 90$ kPa at the diffuser inlet):

$$ p_{01} = p_1 \left(\frac{T_{01}}{T_1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma}{\gamma-1}} = 90 \times \left(\frac{301.194}{290}\right)^{3.5} $$

$$ \left(\frac{301.194}{290}\right)^{3.5} = (1.0386)^{3.5} \approx 1.1404 $$

$$ p_{01} = 90 \times 1.1404 \approx 102.63 \;\text{kPa} $$

**Step 3 — Ideal exit temperature** (isentropic case): $T_{2s} = T_{01} - \dfrac{V_2^2}{2 c_p} = 301.194 - \dfrac{2500}{2010} = 301.194 - 1.244 = 299.95$ K.

**Step 4 — Ideal exit pressure** $p_2$ in the isentropic case:

$$ p_{2s} = \frac{p_{01}}{(T_{01}/T_{2s})^{\gamma/(\gamma-1)}} = \frac{102.63}{(301.194/299.95)^{3.5}} $$

$$ \frac{T_{01}}{T_{2s}} = \frac{301.194}{299.95} = 1.00415 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; (1.00415)^{3.5} \approx 1.01459 $$

$$ p_{2s} = \frac{102.63}{1.01459} \approx \boxed{101.15 \;\text{kPa}} $$

**Step 5 — Real exit temperature** with $\eta_D = 0.92$:

$$ T_2 = T_{01} - \eta_D (T_{01} - T_{2s}) = 301.194 - 0.92 \times 1.244 = 301.194 - 1.144 = 300.05 \;\text{K} $$

**Step 6 — Real exit pressure** $p_2$:

Assuming the stagnation pressure ratio equals the efficiency (for moderate Mach numbers):

$$ p_{02} = \eta_D \cdot p_{01} = 0.92 \times 102.63 = 94.42 \;\text{kPa} $$

Wait — this is incorrect for a **diffuser**. In a **nozzle**, losses *reduce* $p_{02}$. In a **diffuser**, the stagnation pressure is **recovered** by deceleration, so the relation is:

$$ p_{02} = p_{01} - \Delta p_{loss} = p_{01} \cdot \eta_D \text{ (when efficiency is defined as pressure recovery)} $$

But since we are using the **enthalpy-based** efficiency, we should use the temperature relation directly:

$$ p_2 = \frac{p_{01}}{(T_{01}/T_2)^{\gamma/(\gamma-1)}} \cdot \eta_D \approx \frac{102.63}{(301.194/300.05)^{3.5}} \cdot 0.92 $$

$$ \frac{301.194}{300.05} = 1.00381 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; (1.00381)^{3.5} \approx 1.01336 $$

$$ p_2 \approx \frac{102.63}{1.01336} \times 0.92 \approx 101.28 \times 0.92 \approx \boxed{93.18 \;\text{kPa}} $$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Pitfall:** Mixing up **nozzle** and **diffuser** sign conventions. In a diffuser, the entropy *increases*, so $p_{02} < p_{01}$. In a nozzle, the same holds. The static pressure at the **exit of a diffuser rises** even though stagnation pressure drops — because the velocity drop (KE loss) is *greater* than the stagnation pressure loss.

**Comparison:**

| Case | $T_2$ (K) | $p_2$ (kPa) |
|------|-----------|-------------|
| Isentropic ($\eta_D = 1.0$) | 299.95 | **101.15** |
| Real ($\eta_D = 0.92$) | 300.05 | **93.18** |

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 3, PEMET741]`

**Q: (a)** Explain with a neat diagram the working of a **supersonic diffuser**. Why does it need a convergent-divergent shape? State the area-Mach relation.

**Q: (b)** Air enters a convergent-divergent diffuser at $M_1 = 2.5$, $p_1 = 30$ kPa, $T_1 = 220$ K. The throat area is $A^* = 0.05$ m². If the exit Mach number is $M_2 = 0.30$, calculate **(i)** the inlet area $A_1$, **(ii)** the exit area $A_2$, **(iii)** the exit static pressure $p_2$ assuming isentropic flow. Take $\gamma = 1.4$.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 Marks)

**Diagram (2 Marks):** Show a C-D duct with three labelled sections: converging (supersonic deceleration), throat ($M=1$), and diverging (subsonic deceleration). Mark inlet and exit Mach numbers.

**Working Explanation (2 Marks):**
- Supersonic air enters the converging section.
- As area decreases, velocity decreases (supersonic deceleration).
- At throat, $M = 1$ (sonic condition).
- Past throat, the flow is subsonic and further deceleration occurs as area increases.
- Static pressure rises substantially through the diffuser.

**Why C-D Geometry (2 Marks):**

From $\frac{dA}{A} = (M^2 - 1)\frac{dV}{V}$:

- For $M > 1$ (supersonic), $(M^2 - 1) > 0$, so $\dfrac{dA}{A}$ and $\dfrac{dV}{V}$ have the **same sign**. To decelerate ($dV < 0$), we need $dA < 0$ → converging.
- At $M = 1$, area is at its minimum ($A = A^*$, the throat).
- For $M < 1$ (subsonic), $(M^2 - 1) < 0$, so $\dfrac{dA}{A}$ and $\dfrac{dV}{V}$ have **opposite signs**. To decelerate ($dV < 0$), we need $dA > 0$ → diverging.

**Area-Mach Relation (1 Mark):**

$$ \frac{A}{A^*} = \frac{1}{M}\left[\frac{2}{\gamma+1}\left(1 + \frac{\gamma-1}{2}M^2\right)\right]^{\frac{\gamma+1}{2(\gamma-1)}} $$

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 Marks)

**Given:** $M_1 = 2.5$, $p_1 = 30$ kPa, $T_1 = 220$ K, $A^* = 0.05$ m², $M_2 = 0.30$.

**Step 1 — Compute $A_1/A^*$ at $M_1 = 2.5$:**

$$ \frac{A_1}{A^*} = \frac{1}{2.5}\left[\frac{2}{2.4}\left(1 + \frac{0.4}{2}(2.5)^2\right)\right]^{\frac{2.4}{0.8}} $$

$$ = \frac{1}{2.5}\left[0.8333 \times (1 + 1.25)\right]^{3} $$

$$ = \frac{1}{2.5}\left[0.8333 \times 2.25\right]^{3} = \frac{1}{2.5}\left[1.875\right]^{3} = \frac{1}{2.5} \times 6.5918 = 2.6367 $$

**Step 2 — Inlet area:**

$$ A_1 = 2.6367 \times 0.05 = \boxed{0.1318 \;\text{m}^2} $$

**Step 3 — Compute $A_2/A^*$ at $M_2 = 0.30$:**

$$ \frac{A_2}{A^*} = \frac{1}{0.30}\left[0.8333 \times \left(1 + \frac{0.4}{2}(0.30)^2\right)\right]^{3} $$

$$ = \frac{1}{0.30}\left[0.8333 \times (1 + 0.018)\right]^{3} = \frac{1}{0.30}\left[0.8333 \times 1.018\right]^{3} $$

$$ = \frac{1}{0.30}\left[0.8483\right]^{3} = \frac{1}{0.30} \times 0.6104 = 2.0347 $$

**Step 4 — Exit area:**

$$ A_2 = 2.0347 \times 0.05 = \boxed{0.1017 \;\text{m}^2} $$

**Step 5 — Inlet stagnation pressure** (assuming flow up to station 1 is isentropic, so $p_{01} = p_1 \cdot \text{function}(M_1)$):

Wait — since $p_1$ is the static pressure and the flow is isentropic within the diffuser, $p_{01} = p_1 \left(1 + \dfrac{\gamma-1}{2}M_1^2\right)^{\gamma/(\gamma-1)}$.

$$ p_{01} = 30 \times \left(1 + 0.2 \times 6.25\right)^{3.5} = 30 \times (2.25)^{3.5} $$

$$ (2.25)^{3.5} = 2.25^3 \times 2.25^{0.5} = 11.3906 \times 1.5 = 17.0859 $$

$$ p_{01} = 30 \times 17.0859 = 512.58 \;\text{kPa} $$

**Step 6 — Isentropic exit static pressure:**

For isentropic flow, $p_{01} = p_{02}$.

$$ p_2 = \frac{p_{02}}{\left(1 + \dfrac{\gamma-1}{2}M_2^2\right)^{\gamma/(\gamma-1)}} = \frac{512.58}{\left(1 + 0.2 \times 0.09\right)^{3.5}} $$

$$ = \frac{512.58}{(1.018)^{3.5}} = \frac{512.58}{1.0644} \approx \boxed{481.55 \;\text{kPa}} $$

**Pressure rise in supersonic diffuser:** $p_2 - p_1 = 481.55 - 30 = 451.55$ kPa. The supersonic diffuser achieves a **massive** pressure rise, which is why it is critical in ramjet/scramjet engines.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 WHERE STUDENTS LOSE MARKS IN DIFFUSER QUESTIONS:**
>
> 1. **Sign convention confusion** — Mixing up nozzle ($p_2 < p_1$, $V_2 > V_1$) and diffuser ($p_2 > p_1$, $V_2 < V_1$). Always verify by checking if area is increasing.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the Stagnation Pressure Drop in Real Diffusers** — Even though static pressure rises, stagnation pressure $p_{02}$ falls below $p_{01}$ due to entropy increase. Stating "$p_2 > p_1$ implies $p_{02} > p_{01}$" is a fatal error.
>
> 3. **Wrong direction of C-D duct** — A supersonic **diffuser** uses the converging section first, then the diverging section. Some students draw it like a regular nozzle (diverging first), which is the *opposite* direction.
>
> 4. **Numerical errors in Area-Mach relation** — The exponent $\frac{\gamma+1}{2(\gamma-1)}$ is frequently mistyped as $3$ instead of $3$ (which is correct for $\gamma = 1.4$, but the general form is the exponent shown above).
>
> 5. **Ignoring elevation (z) and heat (q) terms in SFEE** — Always state assumptions clearly: $q=0$, $w=0$, $\Delta z \approx 0$ for a diffuser.
>
> 6. **Using $\gamma = 1.4$ for combustion gases** — Combustion products have $\gamma \approx 1.33$. Always check what gas the problem specifies.
>
> 7. **Confusing $T_0$ and $T_{stagnation}$** — These are the *same* thing. Writing them differently in consecutive steps loses marks for inconsistency.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Diffuser (Module 3, PEMET741)**

### Core Concepts
- ☐ Diffuser = device that converts **kinetic energy into pressure energy** by **decelerating** the fluid.
- ☐ Located between the **air intake** and **compressor** in a gas turbine.
- ☐ **Subsonic diffuser:** Diverging passage ($A \uparrow$).
- ☐ **Supersonic diffuser:** Convergent-Divergent (C-D) passage.
- ☐ Working principle: **SFEE** — $h_1 + V_1^2/2 = h_2 + V_2^2/2$ (adiabatic, no work).

### Key Equations
- ☐ $T_{01} = T_{02}$ (stagnation temperature conservation in adiabatic flow).
- ☐ $p_{01} > p_{02}$ (real diffuser — stagnation pressure drops due to losses).
- ☐ $\dfrac{dA}{A} = (M^2 - 1) \dfrac{dV}{V}$ — the **fundamental** area-velocity relation.
- ☐ Area-Mach relation: $\dfrac{A}{A^*} = \dfrac{1}{M}\left[\dfrac{2}{\gamma+1}\left(1+\dfrac{\gamma-1}{2}M^2\right)\right]^{\frac{\gamma+1}{2(\gamma-1)}}$.
- ☐ Diffuser efficiency: $\eta_D = (T_{01} - T_2)/(T_{01} - T_{2s})$.
- ☐ Pressure recovery: $\eta_D = p_{02}/p_{01}$ (for stagnation-pressure definition).

### Typical Numerical Values
- ☐ Typical diffuser pressure ratio: **$1.05$ – $1.9$**.
- ☐ Typical diffuser efficiency: **$0.85$ – $0.98$**.
- ☐ Typical compressor inlet velocity after diffuser: **$30$ – $60$ m/s**.
- ☐ Typical subsonic inlet Mach number: **$M_1 \approx 0.3$ – $0.6$**.

### KTU Exam Focus Areas
- ☐ SFEE derivation under adiabatic assumption.
- ☐ Area-Mach relation derivation.
- ☐ Numerical problems on subsonic diffuser with isentropic and real cases.
- ☐ Supersonic C-D diffuser area calculations.
- ☐ Justification for C-D geometry using $(M^2-1)$ sign analysis.
- ☐ Sketch of gas turbine with diffuser highlighted.

### One-Line Definition to Memorise
> **"A diffuser is an adiabatic, work-free, divergent (for subsonic) or convergent-divergent (for supersonic) passage in which the kinetic energy of a flowing fluid is converted into a rise in static pressure."**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Supersonic Inlets
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET741 - GAS TURBINE AND JET PROPULSION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Supersonic Inlets

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal KTU Definition

A **Supersonic Inlet** (also called a **Supersonic Diffuser** or **Ram Inlet**) is the front-most component of a jet propulsion engine whose primary function is to **decelerate the oncoming supersonic free-stream air from the aircraft's flight Mach number ($M_\infty$) down to a subsonic condition** at the engine face (compressor inlet), while simultaneously **maximizing the preservation of total (stagnation) pressure** and ensuring stable, steady flow across the entire operational Mach range.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight — Definition to Memorize:**
> "A supersonic inlet is a device designed to convert kinetic energy of a supersonic airstream into a pressure rise by means of one or more shock waves (oblique and/or normal), delivering air to the combustion chamber at the lowest possible Mach number with the highest possible total pressure recovery ($\eta_d$)."

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Funnel & Air Brake" Picture

Imagine you are cruising on a highway at **200 km/h** on a motorcycle. You want to slow down to **40 km/h** smoothly before entering a narrow parking garage. If you simply slap on the brakes, you waste massive kinetic energy as heat and the bike becomes unstable. Instead, an experienced rider would:

1. **Slightly tilt the bike** (creating a controlled **oblique shock** on a cone-shaped inlet spike) — losing only a small amount of energy.
2. **Then apply the main brake** (a stronger **normal shock** at the cowl lip) — taking the rest of the speed down safely.
3. **Glide gently into the garage** through a gradually widening duct (the **subsonic diffuser** section) — recovering more pressure smoothly.

A supersonic inlet does exactly this to oncoming air:

| Bike Maneuver | Inlet Process | Effect |
|---|---|---|
| Tilt / angled body | **Oblique shock** on spike | Small entropy rise, turns flow |
| Main brake | **Normal shock** at cowl lip | Strong deceleration, M → subsonic |
| Gentle glide into garage | **Subsonic diffuser** | Isentropic pressure recovery |

## 1.3 Why Supersonic Inlets Are Critical

- In a turbojet, **compressor blades cannot tolerate supersonic flow** at the inlet eye — it causes shock-induced vibration, buzz, and mechanical failure.
- The inlet must therefore act as a **"supersonic–subsonic converter."**
- Any loss of total pressure in the inlet directly **reduces thrust**, because thrust $\propto \sqrt{T_{04}/T_{02}}$ in ideal cycle analysis.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Performance Metric:** The figure of merit for an inlet is the **Total Pressure Recovery**, defined as:
> $$\eta_d = \frac{p_{02}}{p_{0\infty}}$$
> where $p_{02}$ is the stagnation pressure at the compressor face and $p_{0\infty}$ is the freestream stagnation pressure. For ideal (isentropic) deceleration, $\eta_d = 1$; in practice $\eta_d = 0.85$–$0.98$ depending on $M_\infty$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Physical Mechanism — How a Supersonic Inlet Works

The deceleration must obey the **Second Law of Thermodynamics**: you *cannot* isentropically slow a supersonic stream in a fixed-area duct (the area–Mach number relation $A/A^* = f(M)$ requires a *convergent* duct for supersonic flow and a *divergent* duct for subsonic flow). Therefore shocks are unavoidable.

**Step-by-step operational logic:**

1. **Shock System Generation:** As the freestream $M_\infty > 1$ enters the inlet, it encounters a converging cross-section (either external spike or internal ramp). An **oblique shock** forms on the spike/ramp, turning the flow inward while reducing Mach number slightly with minimal loss.

2. **Cowl Lip & Capture:** The **cowl lip** is the leading edge of the inlet's outer barrel. It must be positioned on the **last shock wave** to capture all the airflow that has passed through the shock system (no spillage).

3. **Normal Shock Termination:** A **normal shock** sits at or near the cowl lip. Across it, flow drops from $M > 1$ to $M < 1$ abruptly. Total pressure drops by an irreversible entropy increase.

4. **Subsonic Diffusion:** Downstream of the normal shock, $M < 1$. The duct is now **divergent** (subsonic diffuser), allowing **near-isentropic** further deceleration and pressure rise up to the engine face.

5. **Bleed & Bypass:** Excess boundary layer air is removed via **bleed ducts** to prevent shock–boundary-layer interaction and buzz.

## 2.2 Classification of Supersonic Inlets (KTU High-Yield)

| # | Type | Compression Mechanism | Typical Application | Starting Mach $M_s$ |
|---|------|----------------------|---------------------|---------------------|
| 1 | **Pitot / Normal-Shock Inlet** | Single normal shock at inlet | Subsonic / low supersonic | $M_s = 1$ (no starting problem) |
| 2 | **External-Cone (Spike) Inlet** | Series of oblique shocks on central cone | $M = 1.5$–$2.5$ | $M_s \approx 1/\sin\theta_c$ |
| 3 | **External-Wedge Inlet** | Oblique shocks on 2D wedge ramp | $M = 1.5$–$3.0$ | $M_s = 1/\sin\beta$ |
| 4 | **Internal-Compression Inlet** | Shocks form *inside* the duct on internal ramps | $M = 2.0$–$3.5$ | Higher $M_s$ possible |
| 5 | **Mixed-Compression Inlet** | Oblique shocks external + normal/internal shock | $M = 2.0$–$4.0+$ | Variable, requires variable geometry |
| 6 | **Conical-Shock Inlet (Busemann)** | Detached conical shock | Hypersonic research | Complex |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Concept of "Starting":** An inlet is said to be **started** when a stable shock system sits *on* the cowl lip with no flow spillage. **Unstarted** flow means the shock is pushed forward of the lip, spilling flow around the cowl — causing **spillage drag** and **loss of thrust**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are stated for **calorically perfect gas** with ratio of specific heats $\gamma = 1.4$ unless stated. Use `\vert` notation for absolute values.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|----------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | Total pressure recovery (definition) | $\eta_d = p_{02} \div p_{0\infty}$ | Dimensionless, $0 < \eta_d \le 1$ |
| 2 | Isentropic stagnation relation | $p_0/p = \left(1 + \dfrac{\gamma-1}{2}M^2\right)^{\gamma/(\gamma-1)}$ | Valid for isentropic flow |
| 3 | Area–Mach relation | $\dfrac{A}{A^*} = \dfrac{1}{M}\left[\dfrac{2}{\gamma+1}\left(1+\dfrac{\gamma-1}{2}M^2\right)\right]^{(\gamma+1)/\left[2(\gamma-1)\right]}$ | $A^*$ = throat area |
| 4 | Normal shock: pressure ratio | $\dfrac{p_2}{p_1} = 1 + \dfrac{2\gamma}{\gamma+1}\left(M_1^2 - 1\right)$ | Across shock |
| 5 | Normal shock: temperature ratio | $\dfrac{T_2}{T_1} = \dfrac{\left[1+\dfrac{2\gamma}{\gamma+1}(M_1^2-1)\right]\left[2+(\gamma-1)M_1^2\right]}{(\gamma+1)M_1^2}$ | Across shock |
| 6 | Normal shock: downstream Mach | $M_2^2 = \dfrac{(\gamma-1)M_1^2 + 2}{2\gamma M_1^2 - (\gamma-1)}$ | Across shock |
| 7 | Normal shock: total-pressure ratio | $\dfrac{p_{02}}{p_{01}} = \left[\dfrac{(\gamma+1)M_1^2}{(\gamma-1)M_1^2+2}\right]^{\gamma/(\gamma-1)} \cdot \left[\dfrac{\gamma+1}{2\gamma M_1^2-(\gamma-1)}\right]^{1/(\gamma-1)}$ | Key loss metric |
| 8 | Mass flow rate | $\dot{m} = \rho_\infty \, A_c \, V_\infty = \dfrac{p_{0\infty}}{\sqrt{T_{0\infty}}}\sqrt{\dfrac{\gamma}{R}}M_\infty \left(1+\dfrac{\gamma-1}{2}M_\infty^2\right)^{-(\gamma+1)/\left[2(\gamma-1)\right]} A_c$ | $A_c$ = capture area |
| 9 | Capture-area ratio | $A_c/A_\infty = \dfrac{\dot{m}\sqrt{T_{0\infty}/p_{0\infty}}}{A_\infty} \cdot \dfrac{1}{M_\infty}\left[\dfrac{(\gamma+1)/2}{1+(\gamma-1)M_\infty^2/2}\right]^{(\gamma+1)/\left[2(\gamma-1)\right]}$ | $A_c \le A_\infty$ |
| 10 | Starting Mach (conical inlet, half-angle $\theta_c$) | $M_s = \dfrac{1}{\sin\theta_c}$ | Approximate, geometric |
| 11 | Starting Mach (wedge inlet, shock angle $\beta$) | $M_s = \dfrac{1}{\sin\beta}$ | From $\theta$-$\beta$-$M$ relation |
| 12 | Spillage drag coefficient | $C_{D,spill} = \left(\dfrac{\dot{m}_{spilled}}{\dot{m}_{total}}\right)^2 \cdot C_{D_A}$ | Penalty for unstarted inlet |
| 13 | Additive (wave) drag | $D_{add} = \dot{m}(V_\infty - V_{exit}) + (p_{02} - p_\infty)A_{exit}$ | Pressure thrust recovery term |
| 14 | Diffuser efficiency (isentropic) | $\eta_d = \dfrac{h_{02s} - h_0}{h_{02} - h_0} = \dfrac{p_{02}/p_{0\infty} - 1}{(p_{02}/p_{0\infty})^{\text{if isentropic}} - 1}$ | Often $\approx p_{02}/p_{0\infty}$ |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Civil Aviation:** Boeing 747, Airbus A320 — operate at $M \le 0.85$; hence use simple **subsonic pitot inlets** with $\eta_d \approx 0.99$.
- **Military Fighters:** F-15, Su-30MKI at $M = 2.5$ — use **variable-geometry mixed-compression inlets** with translating centerbody (spike).
- **Concorde / Boom Overture:** $M = 2.02$ cruise — uses a **variable-ramp external-compression inlet** with $\eta_d \approx 0.96$.
- **SR-71 Blackbird:** $M = 3.2$ cruise — uses a **fully axisymmetric mixed-compression inlet** with $\eta_d \approx 0.90$ (using titanium to withstand 300 °C spike temperatures).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Total Pressure Recovery Across a Normal Shock

The total pressure recovery is the single most important performance parameter for a supersonic inlet. We will derive it from first principles.

### Setup

Consider a stationary normal shock wave. Upstream (region 1) the flow is supersonic with Mach $M_1$; downstream (region 2) the flow is subsonic with Mach $M_2$. Both regions share the same stagnation enthalpy ($h_{01} = h_{02}$), but stagnation pressures differ because the shock is irreversible.

### Step 1 — Express $\eta_d$ as a Product of Three Ratios

By definition, the total pressure recovery is the ratio of stagnation pressures across the shock:

$$
\eta_d \;\equiv\; \frac{p_{02}}{p_{01}}
$$

We insert two intermediate static pressures to break this into three physically meaningful pieces:

$$
\eta_d = \frac{p_{02}}{p_{01}} \;=\; \underbrace{\frac{p_{02}}{p_2}}_{\text{(a) subsonic side isentropic}} \cdot \underbrace{\frac{p_2}{p_1}}_{\text{(b) normal shock pressure jump}} \cdot \underbrace{\frac{p_1}{p_{01}}}_{\text{(c) supersonic side isentropic}}
$$

### Step 2 — Piece (a): Subsonic Isentropic Compression from $p_2$ to $p_{02}$

Downstream of the shock, the flow is subsonic with Mach $M_2$. Assuming the deceleration from $M_2$ to rest is isentropic (subsonic diffuser), the standard isentropic relation gives:

$$
\frac{p_{02}}{p_2} = \left(1 + \frac{\gamma-1}{2}M_2^2\right)^{\gamma/(\gamma-1)}
$$

### Step 3 — Piece (c): Supersonic Isentropic Deceleration from $M_1$ to $p_{01}$

Upstream, the flow at $M_1$ decelerates isentropically to its own stagnation state:

$$
\frac{p_{01}}{p_1} = \left(1 + \frac{\gamma-1}{2}M_1^2\right)^{\gamma/(\gamma-1)} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \frac{p_1}{p_{01}} = \left(1 + \frac{\gamma-1}{2}M_1^2\right)^{-\gamma/(\gamma-1)}
$$

### Step 4 — Piece (b): Rankine–Hugoniot Pressure Jump Across the Shock

From continuity, momentum, and energy across a normal shock we obtain the Rankine–Hugoniot pressure jump:

$$
\frac{p_2}{p_1} = 1 + \frac{2\gamma}{\gamma+1}\left(M_1^2 - 1\right)
$$

### Step 5 — Express $M_2^2$ in Terms of $M_1$

From the normal-shock continuity–momentum solution, the post-shock Mach number squared is:

$$
M_2^2 = \frac{(\gamma-1)M_1^2 + 2}{2\gamma M_1^2 - (\gamma-1)}
$$

Therefore:

$$
1 + \frac{\gamma-1}{2}M_2^2 \;=\; 1 + \frac{\gamma-1}{2}\cdot \frac{(\gamma-1)M_1^2 + 2}{2\gamma M_1^2 - (\gamma-1)}
$$

Combining over a common denominator:

$$
1 + \frac{\gamma-1}{2}M_2^2 \;=\; \frac{2\gamma M_1^2 - (\gamma-1) + \frac{\gamma-1}{2}\left[(\gamma-1)M_1^2 + 2\right]}{2\gamma M_1^2 - (\gamma-1)}
$$

Simplifying the numerator:

$$
2\gamma M_1^2 - (\gamma-1) + \frac{(\gamma-1)^2 M_1^2}{2} + (\gamma-1) \;=\; 2\gamma M_1^2 + \frac{(\gamma-1)^2 M_1^2}{2}
$$

Factor out $M_1^2/2$:

$$
= \frac{M_1^2}{2}\left[4\gamma + (\gamma-1)^2\right] \;=\; \frac{M_1^2}{2}\left[(\gamma+1)^2\right] \;=\; \frac{(\gamma+1)^2 M_1^2}{2}
$$

Hence, in compact form:

$$
1 + \frac{\gamma-1}{2}M_2^2 \;=\; \frac{(\gamma+1)^2 M_1^2}{2\left[2\gamma M_1^2 - (\gamma-1)\right]}
$$

### Step 6 — Assemble All Three Pieces

Now we write:

$$
\eta_d = \left[1 + \frac{\gamma-1}{2}M_2^2\right]^{\gamma/(\gamma-1)} \cdot \left[1 + \frac{2\gamma}{\gamma+1}(M_1^2 - 1)\right] \cdot \left[1 + \frac{\gamma-1}{2}M_1^2\right]^{-\gamma/(\gamma-1)}
$$

Substituting the result from Step 5 into the first bracket:

$$
\eta_d = \left\{\frac{(\gamma+1)^2 M_1^2}{2\left[2\gamma M_1^2 - (\gamma-1)\right]}\right\}^{\gamma/(\gamma-1)} \cdot \left\{\frac{2 + (\gamma-1)M_1^2}{\gamma+1}\right\} \cdot \left\{1 + \frac{\gamma-1}{2}M_1^2\right\}^{-\gamma/(\gamma-1)}
$$

where we have rewritten $\;1 + \frac{2\gamma}{\gamma+1}(M_1^2-1) = \frac{2 + (\gamma-1)M_1^2}{\gamma+1}$.

After algebraic rearrangement (combining exponents on the $M_1^2$ and constant terms), we obtain the standard textbook result:

$$
\boxed{\;\frac{p_{02}}{p_{01}} = \left[\frac{(\gamma+1)M_1^2}{(\gamma-1)M_1^2 + 2}\right]^{\gamma/(\gamma-1)} \cdot \left[\frac{\gamma+1}{2\gamma M_1^2 - (\gamma-1)}\right]^{1/(\gamma-1)}\;}
$$

### Step 7 — Numerical Sample to Verify

Take $\gamma = 1.4$ and $M_1 = 2.0$:

$$
\frac{p_{02}}{p_{01}} = \left[\frac{2.4 \times 4}{0.4 \times 4 + 2}\right]^{3.5} \cdot \left[\frac{2.4}{2 \times 1.4 \times 4 - 0.4}\right]^{2.5}
$$

Evaluate each factor:

$$
= \left[\frac{9.6}{3.6}\right]^{3.5} \cdot \left[\frac{2.4}{10.8}\right]^{2.5} = (2.667)^{3.5} \cdot (0.2222)^{2.5}
$$

Compute:

$$
(2.667)^{3.5} \approx 30.97 \quad\text{and}\quad (0.2222)^{2.5} \approx 0.02309
$$

So:

$$
\eta_d \approx 30.97 \times 0.02309 \approx 0.7209
$$

This matches the standard normal-shock table value $p_{02}/p_{01} = 0.7209$ at $M_1 = 2.0$. ✓

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Starting Mach Number of a Conical Inlet

A conical (spike) inlet has a central cone of half-angle $\theta_c$. The oblique shock leaves the cone tip at angle $\beta$ to the freestream. The inlet is **started** when this shock wave grazes the cowl lip.

### Geometric Starting Condition

For starting, the shock from the cone tip must pass through the cowl lip. From triangle geometry (cone tip → cowl lip → cowl tip):

$$
\sin\theta_c = \frac{r_{lip}}{L_s}
$$

where $L_s$ is the slanted length from the cone tip to the cowl lip along the shock. The Mach number behind the oblique shock on the cone surface must satisfy the post-shock streamline that turns by angle $\theta_c$. Using the **simple wave approximation** at the verge of starting:

$$
M_s \cdot \sin\theta_c \approx 1
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{\;M_s \;\approx\; \frac{1}{\sin\theta_c}\;}
$$

For a typical $\theta_c = 15°$:

$$
M_s = \frac{1}{\sin 15°} = \frac{1}{0.2588} \approx 3.86
$$

This means a $15°$ half-angle cone inlet cannot be started below $M \approx 3.86$ in pure external compression — a major design constraint.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mitigation:** Reduce $\theta_c$ (sharper spike) to lower $M_s$, OR use **variable geometry** (translating spike), OR use **mixed compression** with internal normal shock.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Mass Flow Through a Supersonic Inlet

For a choked inlet operating at design condition, the mass flow is set by the **throat** (minimum area, where $M = 1$ after the shock):

$$
\dot{m} = \rho^* A^* V^*
$$

Using the critical condition $T^*/T_0 = 2/(\gamma+1)$ and $p^*/p_0 = [2/(\gamma+1)]^{\gamma/(\gamma-1)}$:

$$
\dot{m} = \frac{p_0 A^*}{\sqrt{T_0}} \sqrt{\frac{\gamma}{R}} \left(\frac{2}{\gamma+1}\right)^{(\gamma+1)/[2(\gamma-1)]}
$$

In terms of freestream conditions and capture area $A_c$, the inlet captures the flow through area $A_c$:

$$
\dot{m} = \rho_\infty V_\infty A_c = \frac{p_\infty}{\sqrt{T_\infty}} M_\infty \sqrt{\frac{\gamma}{R}} A_c
$$

Since $p_\infty = p_{0\infty}/[1+(\gamma-1)M_\infty^2/2]^{\gamma/(\gamma-1)}$, we obtain:

$$
\dot{m} = \frac{p_{0\infty} M_\infty A_c}{\sqrt{T_{0\infty}}} \sqrt{\frac{\gamma}{R}} \left[1 + \frac{\gamma-1}{2}M_\infty^2\right]^{-(\gamma+1)/[2(\gamma-1)]}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering insight:** At higher $M_\infty$, $\dot{m}$ increases (more kinetic energy flux), but $\eta_d$ decreases (shock losses rise). The product $\dot{m}\sqrt{T_{02}/T_{0\infty}}$ — which governs thrust — has a **maximum around $M = 3$–$4$** for fixed geometry, beyond which inlet losses dominate.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of a Supersonic Inlet System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Freestream Air M Infinity greater than 1] --> B[Pre Entry Oblique Shock System on Spike or Ramp]
    B --> C[Cowl Lip Capture]
    C --> D[Normal Shock at Throat M equals 1 to subsonic]
    D --> E[Subsonic Divergent Diffuser Section]
    E --> F[Bleed Ducts for Boundary Layer Removal]
    E --> G[Compressor Face Engine Station 2]
    F --> H[Bypass Duct to Bypass Air Around Engine]
    G --> I[Performance Parameters Computed]
    I --> J[Total Pressure Recovery eta d]
    I --> K[Mass Flow Ratio A capture over A throat]
    I --> L[Starting Mach Number M start]
    I --> M[Spillage and Additive Drag]
```

## 4.2 Comparative Block Diagram — Inlet Classification by Compression Strategy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Supersonic Inlets] --> B[External Compression]
    A --> C[Internal Compression]
    A --> D[Mixed Compression]
    B --> B1[Pitot Normal Shock]
    B --> B2[Spike Conical]
    B --> B3[Wedge Oblique]
    C --> C1[Internal Ramp 2D]
    C --> C2[Conical Centerbody]
    D --> D1[External Spike plus Internal Normal]
    D --> D2[Variable Geometry Translating Spike]
    D --> D3[Variable Ramp Bypass Doors]
```

## 4.3 Process Flow Diagram — Flow Path Through a Mixed-Compression Inlet

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Station 0 Freestream M equals 2.5] --> S1[Station 1 After External Oblique Shock on Spike]
    S1 --> S2[Station 2 After Internal Oblique Shock on Ramp]
    S2 --> S3[Station 3 Just Upstream of Normal Shock]
    S3 --> S4[Station 4 Just Downstream of Normal Shock Subsonic]
    S4 --> S5[Station 5 Engine Face Compressor Inlet]
    S0 -. Freestream Stagnation p0 infinity .-> S5
    S5 -. Measured Stagnation p02 .-> PERF[Performance Block]
    PERF --> ETA[eta d equals p02 over p0 infinity]
    S0 -. Mass Flow m dot .-> S5
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this:** Each "Station $N$" corresponds to a thermodynamic state the air attains as it passes through successive shock/diffusion elements. The performance block computes $\eta_d$ and compares it to ideal isentropic deceleration.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define a supersonic inlet and explain the phenomenon of "inlet starting" with reference to the cowl lip.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A supersonic inlet is the front section of a jet engine designed to decelerate the oncoming supersonic airstream to subsonic conditions at the compressor face while maximizing total pressure recovery $\eta_d = p_{02}/p_{0\infty}$. **(1 mark)**

"Inlet starting" refers to the establishment of a stable shock system with the terminal normal shock seated on the cowl lip and **no spillage** of air around the outside of the cowl. **(1 mark)**

An inlet is said to be "started" when the freestream Mach number exceeds the starting Mach number $M_s$, below which the shock system is pushed forward of the lip, causing severe spillage drag, loss of mass flow, and unstable buzz. **(1 mark)**

---

### Question 2: **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between external-compression and internal-compression supersonic inlets.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | External Compression | Internal Compression |
|---|---|---|
| Location of shocks | On external spike/ramp *before* the cowl lip | Inside the duct on internal ramps/cones |
| Starting problem | Severe — high $M_s$ | Less severe — lower $M_s$ |
| Spike/blunt body drag | High (external protrusion) | Lower (flush installation) |
| Typical Mach range | $M = 1.5$–$3.0$ | $M = 2.5$–$4.5$ |
| Geometric complexity | Simple fixed spike | Often needs variable geometry |

(Allocate ~1.5 marks for the contrast and 1.5 marks for the implication/typical application.)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks): **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Modified]**

**Part (a) — [7 Marks]**
*Derive, starting from the Rankine–Hugoniot relations, an expression for the total pressure recovery across a normal shock in terms of the upstream Mach number $M_1$ and the ratio of specific heats $\gamma$. Clearly state all assumptions.*

**Model Solution:**

**Assumptions (1 mark):**
- Calorically perfect gas, $\gamma = \text{const}$
- Adiabatic shock (no heat transfer), normal to freestream
- One-dimensional, steady flow
- Shock treated as a discontinuity

**Derivation (6 marks):** Follow the 7-step derivation in **Section 3.1** of these notes:

1. Write $\eta_d = (p_{02}/p_2)(p_2/p_1)(p_1/p_{01})$.
2. Substitute isentropic stagnation relations for $p_{02}/p_2$ and $p_1/p_{01}$.
3. Use Rankine–Hugoniot jump for $p_2/p_1$.
4. Substitute the post-shock Mach relation $M_2^2 = [(\gamma-1)M_1^2 + 2]/[2\gamma M_1^2 - (\gamma-1)]$.
5. Simplify the bracket $1 + (\gamma-1)M_2^2/2 = (\gamma+1)^2 M_1^2/[2(2\gamma M_1^2 - (\gamma-1))]$.
6. Combine and take common powers of $(\gamma-1)$.
7. Final boxed formula:

$$
\frac{p_{02}}{p_{01}} = \left[\frac{(\gamma+1)M_1^2}{(\gamma-1)M_1^2 + 2}\right]^{\gamma/(\gamma-1)} \cdot \left[\frac{\gamma+1}{2\gamma M_1^2 - (\gamma-1)}\right]^{1/(\gamma-1)}
$$

---

**Part (b) — [7 Marks]**
*A supersonic inlet operates at $M_\infty = 2.8$ with freestream static pressure $p_\infty = 30$ kPa and freestream static temperature $T_\infty = 240$ K. Assuming a single normal shock sits at the cowl lip and $\gamma = 1.4$, calculate:*
*(i) The total pressure recovery $\eta_d$*
*(ii) The stagnation pressure at the compressor face*
*(iii) The static pressure ratio across the shock.*

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $M_1 = 2.8$, $p_\infty = 30$ kPa, $T_\infty = 240$ K, $\gamma = 1.4$.

**(i) Total pressure recovery $\eta_d$ (3 marks):**

Using the derived formula with $M_1 = 2.8$:

$$
\eta_d = \left[\frac{2.4 \times 7.84}{0.4 \times 7.84 + 2}\right]^{3.5} \cdot \left[\frac{2.4}{2 \times 1.4 \times 7.84 - 0.4}\right]^{2.5}
$$

Compute each factor:

$$
\frac{2.4 \times 7.84}{0.4 \times 7.84 + 2} = \frac{18.816}{5.136} \approx 3.664
$$

$$
\frac{2.4}{2 \times 1.4 \times 7.84 - 0.4} = \frac{2.4}{21.552} \approx 0.1114
$$

Therefore:

$$
\eta_d = (3.664)^{3.5} \cdot (0.1114)^{2.5}
$$

Evaluate:

$$
(3.664)^{3.5} \approx 91.13 \quad\text{and}\quad (0.1114)^{2.5} \approx 0.004143
$$

So:

$$
\eta_d \approx 91.13 \times 0.004143 \approx 0.3775
$$

**Answer:** $\eta_d \approx 0.378$.

**(ii) Stagnation pressure at compressor face (2 marks):**

$$
p_{0\infty} = p_\infty \left(1 + \frac{\gamma-1}{2}M_\infty^2\right)^{\gamma/(\gamma-1)}
$$

$$
p_{0\infty} = 30 \times \left(1 + 0.2 \times 7.84\right)^{3.5} = 30 \times (2.568)^{3.5}
$$

$(2.568)^{3.5} \approx 27.86$

$$
p_{0\infty} \approx 30 \times 27.86 \approx 835.7 \text{ kPa}
$$

$$
p_{02} = \eta_d \cdot p_{0\infty} = 0.3775 \times 835.7 \approx 315.5 \text{ kPa}
$$

**Answer:** $p_{02} \approx 315.5$ kPa.

**(iii) Static pressure ratio across the shock (2 marks):**

$$
\frac{p_2}{p_1} = 1 + \frac{2\gamma}{\gamma+1}(M_1^2 - 1) = 1 + \frac{2 \times 1.4}{2.4}(7.84 - 1)
$$

$$
= 1 + 1.1667 \times 6.84 = 1 + 7.98 = 8.98
$$

**Answer:** $p_2/p_1 \approx 8.98$.

---

### Question B (14 Marks): **[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Modified]**

**Part (a) — [7 Marks]**
*With the help of neat sketches, classify supersonic inlets based on the location and type of shock compression. Briefly state one advantage and one disadvantage of each type.*

**Model Solution:**

**Classification (5 marks):**

1. **Pitot (Normal-Shock) Inlet:** Single normal shock at the inlet plane. Simple, no starting problem. Disadvantage: very low $\eta_d$ at $M > 1.6$.

2. **External-Cone (Spike) Inlet:** Central conical spike generates a series of oblique shocks converging to a weak normal shock at the spike base. Higher $\eta_d$. Disadvantage: severe starting problem (high $M_s$), high external drag from the spike.

3. **External-Wedge (Ramp) Inlet:** 2D wedge creates two oblique shocks (one from each ramp surface). Good for $M = 1.5$–$3$. Disadvantage: limited compression; asymmetric flow if 2D.

4. **Internal-Compression Inlet:** Shock system forms *inside* the duct on internal ramps. Lower external drag, lower $M_s$. Disadvantage: complex duct geometry, requires careful boundary-layer control.

5. **Mixed-Compression Inlet:** Combines external oblique shocks with internal normal shock. Highest $\eta_d$ for $M > 2.5$. Disadvantage: requires **variable geometry** (translating spike, bypass doors) for off-design operation.

**Conclusion (2 marks):** In practice, mixed-compression inlets with variable geometry are preferred for supersonic cruise aircraft (Concorde, fighter jets) because they balance $\eta_d$, starting margin, and off-design performance.

---

**Part (b) — [7 Marks]**
*A conical inlet has a cone half-angle $\theta_c = 12°$. Calculate:*
*(i) The starting Mach number of the inlet.*
*(ii) Comment on whether the inlet will start at $M = 4.5$ with a fixed geometry.*
*(iii) Suggest ONE design modification to lower the starting Mach number.*

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Starting Mach number (3 marks):**

$$
M_s \approx \frac{1}{\sin\theta_c} = \frac{1}{\sin 12°} = \frac{1}{0.2079} \approx 4.81
$$

**Answer:** $M_s \approx 4.81$.

**(ii) Startability comment (2 marks):**

Since $M_\infty = 4.5 < M_s = 4.81$, the inlet will **NOT start** at $M = 4.5$ with fixed geometry. The shock will be pushed forward of the cowl lip, causing significant spillage drag and unstarted buzz.

**(iii) Design modification (2 marks):**

**Option 1:** Reduce the cone half-angle (e.g., to $\theta_c = 10°$) — this lowers $M_s = 1/\sin 10° \approx 5.76$. **Wait — this raises $M_s$, so opposite!**

**Correct Option:** Increase the cone half-angle to $\theta_c = 15°$: $M_s = 1/\sin 15° \approx 3.86$, which is below $4.5$ — the inlet will now start.

**Alternative correct modifications (any one):**
- Use **variable-geometry translating spike** that extends at low Mach (reducing effective $\theta_c$ at high Mach) — this is the SR-71 solution.
- Add **internal-compression ramps** behind the spike to share compression work.
- Use **auxiliary inlet doors/bypass** to bleed off excess air during starting transients.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Deduction Traps in Supersonic Inlet Problems:**
>
> 1. **Unit confusion:** Mixing static $p$ with stagnation $p_0$. Always check whether the question gives you $p_\infty$ or $p_{0\infty}$. Many students use $p_\infty$ directly as $p_{0\infty}$ and lose 2 marks.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the "started" condition:** For a normal-shock-only pitot inlet, students often write $M_s > 1$ — but the pitot inlet *always* starts at $M = 1$ (the shock sits exactly on the lip). Conical/wedge inlets have $M_s \gg 1$.
>
> 3. **Wrong formula for $\eta_d$:** Students sometimes use the simpler isentropic relation $p_0/p = (1+0.2M^2)^{3.5}$ for *both* $p_{02}/p_{01}$ and $p_{02}/p_2$. The shock formula is fundamentally different because the shock is irreversible.
>
> 4. **Sign error in shock relations:** $M_2^2$ formula has $(\gamma-1)$ and $2\gamma$ — these are easily swapped. Cross-verify: at $M_1 = 1$, $M_2^2$ should equal 1.
>
> 5. **Skipping the convergence of the normal shock to oblique-shock logic:** At higher $M$, a normal-shock inlet is grossly inefficient. Always compute $\eta_d$ first to justify an oblique-shock design.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Summary — Read this the night before the exam:**

- [ ] **Definition:** Supersonic inlet = supersonic-to-subsonic decelerator with shock waves; goal is maximum $\eta_d = p_{02}/p_{0\infty}$.
- [ ] **Why shocks?** Because isentropic supersonic deceleration is impossible in a fixed-area duct (area–Mach relation).
- [ ] **Five inlet types:** Pitot (normal shock), External spike (conical), External wedge (oblique), Internal compression, Mixed compression.
- [ ] **Starting concept:** Inlet "starts" when shock sits on cowl lip with no spillage. Below $M_s$, the inlet is "unstarted" — buzz, spillage drag, thrust loss.
- [ ] **Starting Mach for cone:** $M_s \approx 1/\sin\theta_c$. For $\theta_c = 15°$, $M_s \approx 3.86$.
- [ ] **Normal-shock $\eta_d$ formula (memorize):**
$$
\frac{p_{02}}{p_{01}} = \left[\frac{(\gamma+1)M_1^2}{(\gamma-1)M_1^2+2}\right]^{\gamma/(\gamma-1)}\left[\frac{\gamma+1}{2\gamma M_1^2-(\gamma-1)}\right]^{1/(\gamma-1)}
$$
- [ ] **Limiting values of $\eta_d$:** At $M_1 = 1$, $\eta_d = 1$. At $M_1 = 3$, $\eta_d \approx 0.328$. At $M_1 \to \infty$, $\eta_d \to 0$.
- [ ] **Mixed compression** is preferred for $M > 2.5$ because oblique shocks give lower entropy rise than normal shocks.
- [ ] **Mass flow rate** scales as $\dot{m} \propto p_{0\infty} A_c / \sqrt{T_{0\infty}}$ times a function of $M_\infty$ that rises with $M_\infty$.
- [ ] **Boundary-layer bleed** is essential to prevent shock/boundary-layer interaction and inlet unstart.
- [ ] **Variable geometry** (translating spike, bypass doors) is the modern solution to the starting problem on mixed-compression inlets.
- [ ] **Thrust-relevant metric:** $T \propto \dot{m}\sqrt{T_{04}/T_{0\infty}}\,(1+\eta_d\,\text{terms})$. Lower $\eta_d$ directly hurts thrust.
- [ ] **Additive drag** = drag from the external spike/ramp = penalty you pay for shock compression.
- [ ] **For KTU exams:** Always show $\gamma = 1.4$ explicitly; always write units; always state assumptions before derivation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Exhaust Nozzles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET741 - GAS TURBINE AND JET PROPULSION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Exhaust Nozzles

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (Exhaust Nozzle):**
> An **Exhaust Nozzle** is the terminal component of a gas turbine engine (or jet propulsion system) whose primary function is to **expand the high-energy, high-pressure combustion gases** leaving the turbine (or afterburner) and convert their **enthalpy (thermal energy) into directed kinetic energy**, thereby producing a high-velocity exhaust jet. This jet, when expelled rearward, generates the propulsive **thrust** governed by Newton's Third Law of Motion.

In the context of the **KTU PEMET741** course, the exhaust nozzle is analyzed as both a **thermodynamic device** (governed by compressible flow equations) and a **propulsive element** (contributing directly to specific thrust and propulsive efficiency).

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Garden Hose Nozzle**
> Imagine pressing your thumb over the open end of a running garden hose. By narrowing the exit area, you force the water to shoot out *much faster* and reach farther. The **Exhaust Nozzle in a jet engine performs exactly this trick** — but instead of using a thumb, it uses a precisely machined, aerodynamically profiled duct (often converging, or converging-then-diverging) to accelerate a **super-hot, high-pressure gas** to speeds of **500 m/s to 1500+ m/s**.
>
> The faster the gas leaves, the **more thrust** the engine produces. The nozzle is, therefore, the *throat of the beast* — the place where stored thermal energy finally transforms into the kinetic motion that pushes an aircraft through the sky.

---

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Highlighted)

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Standard Metrics Used in Exhaust Nozzle Analysis:**
> - Specific heat ratio for combustion gases: **γ ≈ 1.33** (typical for hot products of combustion)
> - Specific heat ratio for cold air: **γ ≈ 1.4**
> - Universal gas constant: **R = 8.314 kJ/(kmol·K)**
> - Gas constant for combustion products: **R ≈ 0.287 kJ/(kg·K)**
> - Critical (choking) pressure ratio for γ = 1.33: **P*/P₀ ≈ 0.5404**
> - Critical pressure ratio for γ = 1.4: **P*/P₀ ≈ 0.5283**

---

### 1.4 Functional Role of the Exhaust Nozzle in the Engine Cycle

The nozzle sits at the end of the thermodynamic cycle and performs three critical functions:

1. **Kinetic Energy Conversion:** Converts residual enthalpy of turbine exhaust gases into a high-velocity jet.
2. **Thrust Generation:** Provides the reactive momentum exchange that propels the aircraft.
3. **Pressure Recovery Management:** In turbofan engines, the nozzle also matches the fan bypass flow with core flow pressure to avoid spillage losses.

---

### 1.5 Classification of Exhaust Nozzles

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Classification of Exhaust Nozzles:**

| Nozzle Type | Description | Typical Application |
|-------------|-------------|---------------------|
| **Convergent Nozzle (C)** | Single converging passage; area decreases continuously toward exit | Subsonic exhaust, turbojet at low altitude |
| **Convergent-Divergent Nozzle (C-D / De Laval)** | Converging section followed by a diverging section (throat at minimum area) | Supersonic exhaust, high-speed jet engines, rockets |
| **Variable Area Convergent Nozzle** | Convergent exit area mechanically variable | Turboprop, APUs, afterburning engines |
| **Variable Area C-D Nozzle** | Both throat and exit areas adjustable | Supersonic military aircraft (e.g., Mirage 2000, Su-30) |
| **Plug Nozzle** | Central conical body with annular flow around it | High-altitude, altitude-compensating applications |
| **Thrust Reverser / Clamshell Nozzle** | Deflects exhaust forward to assist braking | Commercial airliners on landing |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Thermodynamic Operating Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why and How" Behind Each Stage of Nozzle Operation:**

- **Step 1 — Receipt of Turbine Exhaust Gases:** Highly energetic gases enter the nozzle at **stagnation conditions** (P₀, T₀) — i.e., the total (stagnation) pressure and temperature representing what the gas *would* reach if brought to rest isentropically.

- **Step 2 — Subsonic Acceleration (Convergent Section):** As the duct area decreases, by the **Continuity Equation** (ṁ = ρAV = constant), the gas velocity *must* increase. Pressure and temperature drop in tandem (subsonic compressible flow rule: ↓Area → ↑V, ↓P, ↓T).

- **Step 3 — Critical / Choked Condition at the Throat:** When the local Mach number reaches **M = 1** at the minimum cross-section (throat), the flow is said to be **choked**. Beyond this point, mass flow rate cannot increase further regardless of how low the downstream (back) pressure drops.

- **Step 4 — Supersonic Acceleration (Divergent Section of C-D Nozzle):** Once the flow is sonic at the throat, a *further reduction* in back pressure (P_b < P*) causes the divergent section to accelerate the flow to **supersonic speeds (M > 1)**. Here the rule reverses: ↑Area → ↑V (in supersonic regime).

- **Step 5 — Exit at Ambient Pressure (Design Condition):** For maximum propulsive efficiency in a C-D nozzle, the exit pressure P_e should equal the ambient (atmospheric) pressure P_a. This is the *ideally expanded* condition.

---

### 2.2 Choked Flow Condition — The Critical Pressure Ratio

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why choking matters:** Choking sets the **maximum mass flow rate** that any nozzle of given throat area can pass under given upstream stagnation conditions. This is a fundamental constraint in gas turbine design.

For the flow to be just sonic at the throat, the ratio of throat static pressure to upstream stagnation pressure must equal the **critical pressure ratio**:

$$\frac{P^*}{P_0} = \left(\frac{2}{\gamma + 1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma}{\gamma - 1}}$$

For γ = 1.4 (cold air): P*/P₀ ≈ **0.5283**
For γ = 1.33 (combustion gases): P*/P₀ ≈ **0.5404**

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Exhaust Nozzles)

> [!NOTE]
> **Comprehensive Formula Sheet for KTU Board Examination Use:**

| # | Formula / Equation | Meaning of Symbols | Validity / Notes |
|---|---------------------|---------------------|------------------|
| 1 | $V_e = \sqrt{2 \, C_p \, T_0 \left[1 - \left(\dfrac{P_e}{P_0}\right)^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}}\right]}$ | V_e = exit velocity, C_p = specific heat at const. pressure, T_0 = stagnation temperature, P_0 = stagnation pressure, P_e = exit static pressure | Isentropic expansion; ideal nozzle |
| 2 | $\dfrac{P^*}{P_0} = \left(\dfrac{2}{\gamma+1}\right)^{\gamma/(\gamma-1)}$ | P* = throat (critical) pressure | Choked flow condition |
| 3 | $\dot{m} = \dfrac{P_0 \, A^*}{\sqrt{T_0}} \, \sqrt{\dfrac{\gamma}{R}} \left(\dfrac{2}{\gamma+1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma+1}{2(\gamma-1)}}$ | ṁ = mass flow rate, A* = throat area, R = specific gas constant | Maximum (choked) mass flow rate |
| 4 | $\dfrac{T^*}{T_0} = \dfrac{2}{\gamma+1}$ | T* = critical (throat) static temperature | At choking (M = 1) |
| 5 | $F = \dot{m}(V_e - V_0) + (P_e - P_a) A_e$ | F = net thrust, V_0 = flight velocity, P_a = ambient pressure, A_e = exit area | **General thrust equation** |
| 6 | $F_{gross} = \dot{m} V_e$ | Gross (momentum) thrust | If P_e = P_a |
| 7 | $\eta_N = \dfrac{V_e^2}{2 \, C_p \, T_0 \left[1 - (P_e/P_0)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}\right]}$ | η_N = nozzle efficiency (also written = actual KE / isentropic KE) | Lossless = 100% |
| 8 | $\dfrac{A_e}{A^*} = \dfrac{1}{M_e}\left[\dfrac{2}{\gamma+1}\left(1+\dfrac{\gamma-1}{2}M_e^2\right)\right]^{\frac{\gamma+1}{2(\gamma-1)}}$ | A_e/A* = area ratio for isentropic flow | Used for designing C-D nozzle |
| 9 | $P_0 = P_e \left(1 + \dfrac{\gamma-1}{2} M_e^2\right)^{\gamma/(\gamma-1)}$ | Isentropic relation between stagnation & static | Standard compressible flow |
| 10 | $T_0 = T_e \left(1 + \dfrac{\gamma-1}{2} M_e^2\right)$ | Stagnation temperature relation | Standard compressible flow |

---

### 2.4 Nozzle Operating Regimes — Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three Practical Operating Regimes of a Convergent-Divergent Nozzle:**

1. **Underexpanded Flow (P_e > P_a):** Nozzle exit pressure exceeds ambient. The gas continues expanding outside the nozzle — energy is "wasted" as overexpansion waves and shock diamonds. Common at **high altitude** with a nozzle sized for sea-level operation.

2. **Ideally Expanded Flow (P_e = P_a):** The design-point condition. **Maximum propulsive efficiency.** No external expansion/compression losses.

3. **Overexpanded Flow (P_e < P_a):** Nozzle exit pressure below ambient. The flow undergoes **internal compression shocks** inside the divergent section. Excessive overexpansion can cause **flow separation** and dangerous side loads on the nozzle structure.

**Engineering Utility:** Modern variable-geometry C-D nozzles (e.g., in the **F-16, Eurofighter Typhoon**) are continuously adjusted to maintain P_e ≈ P_a across a wide flight envelope, maximizing thrust from takeoff to supersonic cruise.

---

### 2.5 Thrust Augmentation Techniques

> [!NOTE]
> **Three Major Thrust Augmentation Strategies Involving the Nozzle:**

1. **Afterburning (Reheat):** Fuel is injected and burned between turbine and nozzle exit, raising T_0 and hence V_e dramatically. Thrust can rise by **40–70%**.
2. **Variable Nozzle Area:** Adjusting A_e to optimize expansion ratio as altitude/Mach number varies.
3. **Thrust Reverser / Clamshell Nozzle:** Mechanical petals deflect the exhaust forward for landing rollout — saves brake wear.

---

### 2.6 Nozzle Efficiency Definitions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Two Definitions Used in KTU Boards:**

- **Velocity (or Kinetic Energy) Efficiency:**
$$\eta_N = \dfrac{V_e^2}{V_{e,\text{ideal}}^2}$$

- **Thrust Efficiency (with mass flow):**
$$\eta_N = \dfrac{\text{Actual Thrust}}{\text{Ideal Thrust (isentropic)}}$$

Typical values: **η_N = 0.95 to 0.99** for well-designed nozzles.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Isentropic Exit Velocity from Stagnation Conditions

**Given:** A nozzle with stagnation pressure P₀ and stagnation temperature T₀ at inlet; static exit pressure P_e at outlet. Find the ideal exit velocity V_e.

**Starting Point — Steady Flow Energy Equation (SFEE) for an adiabatic nozzle (no work, no heat transfer):**

$$h_0 = h_e + \dfrac{V_e^2}{2}$$

where h₀ = stagnation (total) enthalpy at inlet, h_e = static enthalpy at exit.

**Express enthalpy in terms of temperature (h = C_p T):**

$$C_p T_0 = C_p T_e + \dfrac{V_e^2}{2}$$

**Solve for V_e:**

$$V_e^2 = 2 C_p (T_0 - T_e)$$

$$V_e = \sqrt{2 C_p (T_0 - T_e)}$$

**Now apply the isentropic relation between stagnation and static temperature:**

$$\dfrac{T_0}{T_e} = \left(\dfrac{P_0}{P_e}\right)^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}}$$

**Rearrange to express T_e in terms of T₀ and pressure ratio:**

$$T_e = T_0 \left(\dfrac{P_e}{P_0}\right)^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}}$$

**Substitute back into the velocity expression:**

$$V_e = \sqrt{2 C_p \, T_0 \left[1 - \left(\dfrac{P_e}{P_0}\right)^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}}\right]}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Result — Standard KTU Exit Velocity Formula:**
>
> $$\boxed{\,V_e = \sqrt{2 \, C_p \, T_0 \left[1 - \left(\dfrac{P_e}{P_0}\right)^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}}\right]}\,}$$

**Engineering Insight:** This equation shows that V_e increases when T₀ increases (more thermal energy available) or when P_e/P₀ decreases (more expansion).

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Critical (Choking) Pressure Ratio

**Given:** Find the ratio P*/P₀ at which Mach number at the throat becomes unity (M = 1).

**Use the isentropic pressure-temperature relation in terms of Mach number:**

$$\dfrac{P_0}{P} = \left(1 + \dfrac{\gamma - 1}{2} M^2\right)^{\frac{\gamma}{\gamma-1}}$$

**At the throat, M = 1:**

$$\dfrac{P_0}{P^*} = \left(1 + \dfrac{\gamma - 1}{2}\right)^{\frac{\gamma}{\gamma-1}}$$

**Simplify the bracket:**

$$1 + \dfrac{\gamma - 1}{2} = \dfrac{2 + \gamma - 1}{2} = \dfrac{\gamma + 1}{2}$$

**Substitute:**

$$\dfrac{P_0}{P^*} = \left(\dfrac{\gamma + 1}{2}\right)^{\frac{\gamma}{\gamma-1}}$$

**Take the reciprocal:**

$$\dfrac{P^*}{P_0} = \left(\dfrac{\gamma + 1}{2}\right)^{-\frac{\gamma}{\gamma-1}} = \left(\dfrac{2}{\gamma + 1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma}{\gamma-1}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Result — Critical Pressure Ratio:**
>
> $$\boxed{\,\dfrac{P^*}{P_0} = \left(\dfrac{2}{\gamma + 1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma}{\gamma-1}}\,}$$

**Numerical Values:**

For γ = 1.4 (cold air):
$$\dfrac{P^*}{P_0} = \left(\dfrac{2}{2.4}\right)^{3.5} = (0.8333)^{3.5} \approx 0.5283$$

For γ = 1.33 (combustion products):
$$\dfrac{P^*}{P_0} = \left(\dfrac{2}{2.33}\right)^{4.077} \approx 0.5404$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Maximum (Choked) Mass Flow Rate Through the Nozzle

**Starting Point — Mass flow rate definition:**

$$\dot{m} = \rho^* \, A^* \, V^*$$

**Apply the ideal gas law at the throat (static density):**

$$\rho^* = \dfrac{P^*}{R \, T^*}$$

**Use choked conditions for T* and P*:**
- $T^* = T_0 \cdot \dfrac{2}{\gamma+1}$
- $P^* = P_0 \cdot \left(\dfrac{2}{\gamma+1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma}{\gamma-1}}$

**Speed of sound at the throat (since M = 1):**

$$V^* = \sqrt{\gamma R T^*}$$

**Substitute all into mass flow rate equation:**

$$\dot{m} = \dfrac{P_0 \left(\dfrac{2}{\gamma+1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma}{\gamma-1}}}{R \, T_0 \, \dfrac{2}{\gamma+1}} \cdot A^* \cdot \sqrt{\gamma \, R \, T_0 \, \dfrac{2}{\gamma+1}}$$

**Combine and simplify using $\dfrac{P_0}{\sqrt{T_0}} \sqrt{\dfrac{\gamma}{R}}$ as the leading factor:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Result — Maximum Choked Mass Flow Rate:**
>
> $$\boxed{\,\dot{m}_{max} = \dfrac{P_0 \, A^*}{\sqrt{T_0}} \sqrt{\dfrac{\gamma}{R}} \left(\dfrac{2}{\gamma+1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma+1}{2(\gamma-1)}}\,}$$

**Significance:** This is the **design equation** for sizing the throat area of a C-D nozzle.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4: General Thrust Equation

**Starting from Newton's Second Law applied to a control volume:**

$$F_{net} = \dot{m} V_e - \dot{m} V_0 + (P_e - P_a) A_e$$

**Explanation of each term:**
- $\dot{m} V_e$: momentum flux leaving the nozzle at velocity V_e
- $\dot{m} V_0$: momentum flux entering the engine intake at flight velocity V_0
- $(P_e - P_a) A_e$: **pressure thrust** — net pressure force on the exit area

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Result — General Thrust Equation (KTU Board Standard Form):**
>
> $$\boxed{\,F = \dot{m}(V_e - V_0) + (P_e - P_a) A_e\,}$$

**Special Case — Ideal Nozzle (P_e = P_a):**

$$F = \dot{m}(V_e - V_0)$$

This is the **momentum thrust** equation and represents the propulsive force when the nozzle is ideally expanded.

---

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem:**
> Combustion gases enter a nozzle of a jet engine at stagnation pressure P₀ = 350 kPa and stagnation temperature T₀ = 900 K. The exit pressure is P_e = 110 kPa. Take γ = 1.33, C_p = 1.148 kJ/(kg·K), R = 0.287 kJ/(kg·K). Calculate:
> (a) The exit velocity V_e.
> (b) The critical pressure ratio.
> (c) Comment on the nozzle expansion state.

**Solution:**

**Part (a) — Exit velocity:**

$$V_e = \sqrt{2 \times 1148 \times 900 \times \left[1 - \left(\dfrac{110}{350}\right)^{(1.33-1)/1.33}\right]}$$

$$= \sqrt{2 \times 1148 \times 900 \times \left[1 - (0.3143)^{0.2481}\right]}$$

**Compute pressure ratio exponent:**
- $\dfrac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma} = \dfrac{0.33}{1.33} = 0.2481$
- $(0.3143)^{0.2481}$: take log = $0.2481 \times \log(0.3143) = 0.2481 \times (-0.5025) = -0.1247$
- Antilog = $0.7516$

$$V_e = \sqrt{2 \times 1148 \times 900 \times (1 - 0.7516)}$$

$$= \sqrt{2 \times 1148 \times 900 \times 0.2484}$$

$$= \sqrt{513{,}138.6} \approx 716.5 \text{ m/s}$$

**Part (b) — Critical pressure ratio:**

$$\dfrac{P^*}{P_0} = \left(\dfrac{2}{1.33 + 1}\right)^{1.33/0.33} = \left(\dfrac{2}{2.33}\right)^{4.0303}$$

$$= (0.8584)^{4.0303} \approx 0.5404$$

So P* = 0.5404 × 350 = **189.1 kPa**.

**Part (c) — Expansion state:**

Since P_e = 110 kPa < P* = 189.1 kPa, the flow is **choked** and supersonic in the divergent section (assuming C-D nozzle). If P_e < P_a (ambient), it is **overexpanded**; if P_e > P_a, **underexpanded**. Without knowing P_a, we can only state the flow is choked.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Exhaust Nozzle System

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Applied:**
> - All node IDs are purely alphanumeric (stepA, stepB, comp1, comp2, etc.).
> - No reserved keywords (`end`, `subgraph`, `graph`, `style`) used as standalone node names.
> - Node labels are raw uppercase alphanumeric text with no bold/italic markdown tags.
> - Diagram represents functional flow rather than physical geometry (mermaid limitation).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    stepA["TURBINE EXIT GASES RECEIVED AT STAGNATION P0 T0"] --> stepB["ENTRY TO CONVERGENT SECTION SUB ACCELERATION"]
    stepB --> stepC{"PRESSURE RATIO LESS THAN CRITICAL"}
    stepC -- NO --> stepD["NOZZLE UNCHOKED SUBSONIC EXIT ONLY"]
    stepC -- YES --> stepE["FLOW CHOKES AT THROAT M EQUALS ONE"]
    stepE --> stepF{"CONVERGENT DIVERGENT NOZZLE PRESENT"}
    stepF -- NO --> stepG["SUBSONIC EXIT ONLY MAX M AT EXIT IS ONE"]
    stepF -- YES --> stepH["DIVERGENT SECTION SUPERSONIC ACCELERATION"]
    stepH --> stepI["DESIGN CONDITION PE EQUALS PA"]
    stepH --> stepJ["UNDEREXPANDED PE GREATER THAN PA"]
    stepH --> stepK["OVEREXPANDED PE LESS THAN PA WITH INTERNAL SHOCKS"]
    stepI --> stepL["THRUST COMPUTED F EQUALS MDOT VE MINUS V0"]
    stepJ --> stepL
    stepK --> stepL
    stepD --> stepM["NOZZLE EXITS WITH PARTIAL THRUST LOSS"]
    stepG --> stepM
    stepL --> stepN["EXIT JET PRODUCES PROPULSIVE THRUST BY NEWTON THIRD LAW"]
    stepM --> stepN
```

---

### 4.2 Functional Block Diagram — Variable Geometry Nozzle Control

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    compA["FLIGHT COMPUTER RECEIVES MACH ALTITUDE DATA"] --> compB["CALCULATES REQUIRED EXIT AREA AE FOR PE EQUALS PA"]
    compB --> compC["DRIVES HYDRAULIC ACTUATORS ON NOZZLE PETALS"]
    compC --> compD["VARIABLE AREA CONVERGENT DIVERGENT NOZZLE RESHAPED"]
    compD --> compE["PE EQUALS PA MAINTAINED ACROSS FLIGHT ENVELOPE"]
    compE --> compF["THRUST EFFICIENCY MAXIMIZED"]
```

---

### 4.3 Schematic Description (Verbal Walkthrough) — Convergent-Divergent Nozzle

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Convergent-Divergent (De Laval) Nozzle — Sectional Description:**

- **Section 1 — Inlet (Subsonic):** Receives hot gases from turbine exit. Cross-sectional area is large. Pressure is high (P₀), velocity is moderate.
- **Section 2 — Convergent Duct:** Area decreases smoothly toward the throat. Velocity accelerates subsonically, pressure and temperature drop.
- **Section 3 — Throat (Critical Section):** Minimum cross-sectional area. Mach number reaches **M = 1**. Pressure drops to P* = 0.5404 P₀ (for γ = 1.33). This is the **choked** condition.
- **Section 4 — Divergent Duct:** Area expands downstream of throat. In subsonic regime this would *decelerate* flow, but because flow is now sonic at the throat, it instead **accelerates to supersonic** in this section.
- **Section 5 — Exit Plane:** Exit at supersonic velocity V_e and exit pressure P_e. For maximum efficiency, P_e is matched to ambient pressure P_a.

```
   TURBINE EXIT
        |
        v
   ____________
   \           \
    \           \
     \           \  <-- Convergent Section
      \           \
       \___________\  <-- Throat (M=1)
       /           /
      /           /   <-- Divergent Section
     /           /
    /           /
   /___________/  <-- Exit (M>1, Supersonic)
        |
        v
    EXIT JET
```

---

### 4.4 Operating Regimes — Flow State Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    state1["NOZZLE INLET CONDITIONS P0 T0 ESTABLISHED"] --> state2["THROAT MACH NUMBER COMPARED"]
    state2 -- M LESS THAN ONE --> state3["FLOW UNCHOKED VELOCITY BELOW SONIC AT EXIT"]
    state2 -- M EQUALS ONE --> state4["FLOW CHOKED MAX MASS FLOW ACHIEVED"]
    state4 --> state5{"IS THERE A DIVERGENT SECTION"}
    state5 -- NO --> state6["EXIT MACH EQUALS ONE CONVERGENT NOZZLE LIMIT"]
    state5 -- YES --> state7{"EXIT PRESSURE PE COMPARED TO AMBIENT PA"}
    state7 -- PE GREATER THAN PA --> state8["UNDEREXPANDED EXTERNAL EXPANSION"]
    state7 -- PE EQUALS PA --> state9["IDEALLY EXPANDED PEAK EFFICIENCY"]
    state7 -- PE LESS THAN PA --> state10["OVEREXPANDED INTERNAL SHOCKS RISK"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 | KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**Question 1 (3 Marks):**
**Define critical pressure ratio in an exhaust nozzle. State its value for air (γ = 1.4) and combustion gases (γ = 1.33).**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> The **critical pressure ratio** is the ratio of throat static pressure to upstream stagnation pressure at which the Mach number reaches unity (M = 1) at the throat, i.e., the flow becomes choked.

$$\dfrac{P^*}{P_0} = \left(\dfrac{2}{\gamma+1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma}{\gamma-1}}$$

- For **air (γ = 1.4):** P*/P₀ ≈ **0.5283**
- For **combustion gases (γ = 1.33):** P*/P₀ ≈ **0.5404**

---

**Question 2 (3 Marks):**
**What is a convergent-divergent (De Laval) nozzle? Why is it required in jet engines?**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> A **convergent-divergent (C-D) nozzle** consists of a converging section followed by a diverging section, with a throat at the minimum cross-sectional area.
>
> **Why required:** A simple convergent nozzle can only accelerate gases up to Mach 1 (choked). To achieve **supersonic exhaust velocities** (required for high propulsive thrust in jet aircraft), the divergent section is essential — it accelerates the already-sonic flow at the throat to supersonic speeds (M > 1).

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions — 14 Marks with Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Forgetting the pressure thrust term $(P_e - P_a) A_e$ when computing net thrust. Always include it; only omit if explicitly told P_e = P_a.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Using γ = 1.4 for combustion gases — the standard KTU assumption for hot products of combustion is **γ = 1.33**.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Confusing area ratios — A_e/A* (exit to throat) vs A*/A_e. Memorize: A* is the throat (smaller) area.
> - **Common Mistake 4:** Unit inconsistency — C_p must be in J/(kg·K) when V_e is in m/s, **not** kJ/(kg·K).
> - **Common Mistake 5:** Not converting stagnation quantities — the formula $V_e = \sqrt{2C_p T_0 [1-(P_e/P_0)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}]}$ uses T₀ and P₀ (stagnation), **not** static inlet values.

---

### 5.3 Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - June 2024]

**A jet engine operates with the following nozzle parameters:**

- Stagnation pressure at nozzle inlet: P₀ = 400 kPa
- Stagnation temperature at nozzle inlet: T₀ = 1000 K
- Exit static pressure: P_e = 110 kPa
- Ambient pressure: P_a = 101.325 kPa
- Flight velocity: V₀ = 250 m/s
- Specific heat ratio: γ = 1.33
- Specific heat at constant pressure: C_p = 1.148 kJ/(kg·K)
- Gas constant: R = 0.287 kJ/(kg·K)
- Nozzle exit area: A_e = 0.20 m²

**Solve:**

**(a)** Calculate the exit velocity V_e of the exhaust gases. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Calculate the mass flow rate ṁ if the throat area A* = 0.10 m². Also compute the net thrust F produced. (7 Marks)

---

#### Model Solution for Question A:

**Part (a) — Exit Velocity:**

Using the standard isentropic expansion formula:

$$V_e = \sqrt{2 \, C_p \, T_0 \left[1 - \left(\dfrac{P_e}{P_0}\right)^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}}\right]}$$

Compute the exponent:
$$\dfrac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma} = \dfrac{1.33 - 1}{1.33} = \dfrac{0.33}{1.33} = 0.2481$$

Compute the pressure ratio:
$$\dfrac{P_e}{P_0} = \dfrac{110}{400} = 0.275$$

Compute the bracketed term:
$$(0.275)^{0.2481}$$
Take log: $0.2481 \times \log(0.275) = 0.2481 \times (-0.5607) = -0.1391$
Antilog: $10^{-0.1391} = 0.7257$

Therefore:
$$V_e = \sqrt{2 \times 1148 \times 1000 \times (1 - 0.7257)}$$
$$= \sqrt{2 \times 1148 \times 1000 \times 0.2743}$$
$$= \sqrt{629{,}642.4}$$
$$V_e \approx 793.5 \text{ m/s}$$

**Part (b) — Mass Flow Rate and Thrust:**

First, compute the choked (max) mass flow rate:

$$\dot{m} = \dfrac{P_0 \, A^*}{\sqrt{T_0}} \sqrt{\dfrac{\gamma}{R}} \left(\dfrac{2}{\gamma+1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma+1}{2(\gamma-1)}}$$

Compute each factor:
- $\sqrt{T_0} = \sqrt{1000} = 31.623$
- $\sqrt{\dfrac{\gamma}{R}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1.33}{287}} = \sqrt{0.004634} = 0.06807$
- $\dfrac{2}{\gamma+1} = \dfrac{2}{2.33} = 0.8584$
- $\dfrac{\gamma+1}{2(\gamma-1)} = \dfrac{2.33}{0.66} = 3.5303$
- $(0.8584)^{3.5303}$: log = $3.5303 \times \log(0.8584) = 3.5303 \times (-0.0663) = -0.2341$
- Antilog: $0.5830$

Therefore:
$$\dot{m} = \dfrac{400{,}000 \times 0.10}{31.623} \times 0.06807 \times 0.5830$$

$$\dot{m} = 1264.9 \times 0.06807 \times 0.5830$$

$$\dot{m} = 1264.9 \times 0.03969 \approx 50.2 \text{ kg/s}$$

Now compute net thrust using the **general thrust equation**:

$$F = \dot{m}(V_e - V_0) + (P_e - P_a) A_e$$

$$F = 50.2 \times (793.5 - 250) + (110{,}000 - 101{,}325) \times 0.20$$

$$F = 50.2 \times 543.5 + 8675$$

$$F = 27{,}283.7 + 8{,}675$$

$$F \approx 35{,}958.7 \text{ N} \approx 36.0 \text{ kN}$$

---

### 5.4 Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]

**A turbojet engine exhaust nozzle receives gases at the following conditions:**

- Stagnation pressure: P₀ = 500 kPa
- Stagnation temperature: T₀ = 850 K
- Ambient (back) pressure: P_a = 50 kPa (high altitude flight)
- Specific heat ratio: γ = 1.4
- Specific heat at constant pressure: C_p = 1.005 kJ/(kg·K)
- Gas constant: R = 0.287 kJ/(kg·K)
- Flight velocity: V₀ = 200 m/s

**Solve:**

**(a)** Determine the **critical (choked) exit pressure P*** and the **maximum possible exit velocity** if the flow were to expand to ambient pressure P_a. (7 Marks)

**(b)** If the actual exit pressure is P_e = 200 kPa, determine whether the nozzle is **underexpanded, ideally expanded, or overexpanded**. Also compute the **exit velocity** for this actual case. (7 Marks)

---

#### Model Solution for Question B:

**Part (a) — Critical Pressure and Maximum Velocity:**

Critical pressure ratio for γ = 1.4:
$$\dfrac{P^*}{P_0} = \left(\dfrac{2}{2.4}\right)^{3.5} = (0.8333)^{3.5}$$

Compute: $\log(0.8333) = -0.0792$
$3.5 \times (-0.0792) = -0.2772$
Antilog: $10^{-0.2772} = 0.5283$

Therefore:
$$P^* = 0.5283 \times 500 = 264.15 \text{ kPa}$$

**Maximum exit velocity** (if expanding down to P_a = 50 kPa isentropically):

$$V_{e,max} = \sqrt{2 \times 1005 \times 850 \times \left[1 - \left(\dfrac{50}{500}\right)^{(1.4-1)/1.4}\right]}$$

Exponent: $\dfrac{0.4}{1.4} = 0.2857$
Pressure ratio: $\dfrac{50}{500} = 0.1$
$(0.1)^{0.2857}$: log = $0.2857 \times (-1) = -0.2857$
Antilog: $10^{-0.2857} = 0.5179$

$$V_{e,max} = \sqrt{2 \times 1005 \times 850 \times (1 - 0.5179)}$$
$$= \sqrt{1{,}708{,}500 \times 0.4821}$$
$$= \sqrt{823{,}571.85}$$
$$V_{e,max} \approx 907.5 \text{ m/s}$$

**Part (b) — Expansion State and Actual Exit Velocity:**

Since P_e = 200 kPa **< P_a = 50 kPa** is FALSE — actually P_e = 200 kPa **> P_a = 50 kPa**, so the nozzle is **underexpanded** (P_e > P_a). The exhaust gases continue expanding outside the nozzle to ambient pressure.

(Wait — let me re-read: P_e = 200 kPa and P_a = 50 kPa. Yes, P_e > P_a → **Underexpanded**.)

**Actual exit velocity** (at P_e = 200 kPa):

$$V_e = \sqrt{2 \times 1005 \times 850 \times \left[1 - \left(\dfrac{200}{500}\right)^{0.2857}\right]}$$

Pressure ratio: $\dfrac{200}{500} = 0.4$
$(0.4)^{0.2857}$: log = $0.2857 \times \log(0.4) = 0.2857 \times (-0.3979) = -0.1137$
Antilog: $10^{-0.1137} = 0.7696$

$$V_e = \sqrt{2 \times 1005 \times 850 \times (1 - 0.7696)}$$
$$= \sqrt{1{,}708{,}500 \times 0.2304}$$
$$= \sqrt{393{,}614.4}$$
$$V_e \approx 627.4 \text{ m/s}$$

**Net thrust** (for reference, not asked but useful):
$$F = \dot{m}(V_e - V_0) + (P_e - P_a) A_e$$

Note the **positive pressure thrust** $(P_e - P_a) A_e$ due to underexpansion.

---

### 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Exhaust Nozzles (Module 3)**

- ✅ The **primary function** of an exhaust nozzle is to **convert enthalpy into kinetic energy** and produce **thrust** by expelling a high-velocity jet.
- ✅ A **convergent nozzle** can accelerate gases only up to **M = 1** (sonic) at the exit.
- ✅ A **convergent-divergent (De Laval) nozzle** can achieve **supersonic** exit velocities by expanding through the divergent section.
- ✅ **Choking** occurs at the throat when M = 1; beyond this, mass flow rate becomes independent of downstream pressure.
- ✅ **Critical pressure ratio** for air (γ = 1.4): **P*/P₀ ≈ 0.5283**; for combustion gases (γ = 1.33): **P*/P₀ ≈ 0.5404**.
- ✅ **Exit velocity formula** (must memorize):
  $$V_e = \sqrt{2 \, C_p \, T_0 \left[1 - \left(\dfrac{P_e}{P_0}\right)^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}}\right]}$$
- ✅ **General thrust equation** (must memorize):
  $$F = \dot{m}(V_e - V_0) + (P_e - P_a) A_e$$
- ✅ **Three expansion regimes:** Underexpanded (P_e > P_a), Ideally Expanded (P_e = P_a), Overexpanded (P_e < P_a).
- ✅ **Maximum propulsive efficiency** is achieved when the nozzle is **ideally expanded** (P_e = P_a).
- ✅ **Variable area nozzles** maintain ideal expansion across a wide flight envelope — used in supersonic military aircraft.
- ✅ **Nozzle efficiency** η_N = ratio of actual KE to isentropic KE; typical values 0.95 to 0.99.
- ✅ **Thrust augmentation** via afterburning (reheat) can boost thrust by **40–70%**.
- ✅ **Area ratio equation** for C-D nozzle design:
  $$\dfrac{A_e}{A^*} = \dfrac{1}{M_e}\left[\dfrac{2}{\gamma+1}\left(1+\dfrac{\gamma-1}{2}M_e^2\right)\right]^{\frac{\gamma+1}{2(\gamma-1)}}$$
- ✅ **Always use stagnation quantities** (P₀, T₀) at the nozzle inlet — never static values — in the standard exit velocity formula.
- ✅ For combustion products in KTU board problems, **default γ = 1.33** and **C_p = 1.148 kJ/(kg·K)** unless stated otherwise.
- ✅ **Thrust reversers** (clamshell / cascade type) deflect the exhaust forward during landing to assist braking.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Turbine Blades: Blade Materials
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMET741 - GAS TURBINE AND JET PROPULSION > - **Module:** Module Module 3 > - **Topic:** Turbine Blades: Blade Materials # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Turbine Blade Materials** are highly specialized, high-performance superalloys (and advanced composite systems) engineered to survive the extreme **thermomechanical, oxidative, and corrosive** environment of a gas turbine's hot section. The First Stage Turbine Blade (FSTB) in modern aero-engines routinely operates at inlet gas temperatures of **1500 °C – 1700 °C**, well above the **melting point of the base nickel alloy (≈ 1300 °C)**, while sustaining centrifugal stresses of **150 MPa – 300 MPa** at rotational speeds of **10,000 – 15,000 rpm**. The selection of a blade material is therefore a **multi-constrained optimization problem** balancing: 1. **High-temperature tensile strength & creep resistance** 2. **Thermal fatigue & low-cycle fatigue (LCF) life** 3. **Oxidation & hot corrosion resistance** 4. **Density (to minimize disk-loading and bearing loads)** 5. **Manufacturability (castability, weldability, machinability)** ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Firefighter's Suit" Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Imagine a firefighter's bunker suit.** The firefighter is a turbine blade, and the fire is the **1700 °C combustion gas**. The outer layer of the suit is a **Thermal Barrier Coating (TBC)** — a thin ceramic skin that reflects heat. Beneath it lies a **moisture barrier with cooling channels (internal cooling holes)** — analogous to the serpentine passages in a modern blade that bleed cool air from the compressor. The structural fabric of the suit is a **superalloy matrix** — strong but heavy. And finally, the suit's design (woven fibers all going in one direction) is the **directional solidification** that prevents weak grain boundaries from cracking under stress. If **even one** of these "layers" or "design features" fails, the blade creeps, cracks, oxidizes, or shears off — a classic **uncontained failure** that destroys the engine and aircraft. ## 1.3 Why this topic is a **High-Yield KTU Module 3 Topic** - Direct 7–14 mark questions are asked on **superalloy metallurgy, TBC, single-crystal blades, and creep-rupture**. - The topic tests the **Course Outcome (CO3)**: *"Apply the principles of turbine cooling and materials to evaluate engine performance."* ## 1.4 Key Operating-Environment Metrics (Memorize these for KTU) | Parameter | Typical Range | Why it matters | |---|---|---| | Inlet Gas Temperature (TIT/TET) | 1500 °C – 1700 °C | Drives the choice between superalloy + TBC vs. ceramic matrix | | Blade Centrifugal Stress | 150 – 300 MPa | Controls creep-rupture life | | Rotational Speed | 10,000 – 15,000 rpm | Sets LCF (vibratory) stress amplitudes | | Blade Tip Speed | 350 – 450 m/s | Drives aerodynamic + thermal loading | | Required Creep Life | 10,000 – 30,000 hours | Aviation vs. industrial rating | # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Hierarchy of Turbine Blade Materials Turbine blade material evolution is a **direct response to increasing Turbine Entry Temperatures (TET)**. The progression is: ### Step 1 — Conventional Cast / Wrought Alloys (TET < 900 °C) - **Examples:** Nimonic 75, Nimonic 80A, Inconel 600 - **Metallurgy:** Polycrystalline, equiaxed grains. **All grain boundaries are weak links at high temperature.** - **Limitation:** Rapid creep rupture above 900 °C because grain boundaries slide. ### Step 2 — Vacuum-Cast Equiaxed Superalloys (TET 900–1050 °C) - **Examples:** Inconel 718, Udimet 500, Waspaloy - **Improvement:** Vacuum melting removes oxide inclusions; **γ′ (gamma-prime) precipitates** (Ni₃Al, Ni₃Ti) provide precipitation hardening. - **Limitation:** Still has transverse grain boundaries → weak in centrifugal stress direction. ### Step 3 — Directionally Solidified (DS) Alloys (TET 1050–1200 °C) - **Examples:** PWA 1422, GTD-111, Mar-M 002 - **Innovation:** Blade is cast in a **thermal gradient furnace** so grains grow **parallel to the principal stress axis (longitudinal)**. **No transverse grain boundaries** → creep life improved by ~3×. - **Mechanism:** HRS (High Rate Solidification) and LMC (Liquid Metal Cooling) processes. ### Step 4 — Single Crystal (SX) Alloys (TET > 1200 °C) - **Examples:** PWA 1480, CMSX-4, René N5, SRR 99 - **Innovation:** **Zero grain boundaries** (one continuous crystal). Eliminates the weakest link entirely. - **Trade-off:** Extremely expensive casting; all grain-boundary strengtheners (C, B, Zr, Hf) are removed, so **other strengthening mechanisms (Re, Mo, W, Ta, Ru)** must be added to the γ-matrix. - **Performance Gain:** Creep-rupture strength ~6× better than equiaxed; +200 °C temperature capability. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Hot Tip:** When asked to compare materials, always quote the **temperature capability** in °C and **creep-rupture strength** in MPa-hours. These two numbers decide the answer. ### Step 5 — Thermal Barrier Coatings (TBC) & Bond Coats - **Top Coat:** 6–8 wt% Y₂O₃-stabilized ZrO₂ (YSZ) — 250–400 µm thick. **Low thermal conductivity (≈ 2 W/m·K)** → drops metal surface temperature by **100–150 °C**. - **Bond Coat:** MCrAlY (M = Ni, Co) — 75–125 µm. Provides oxidation resistance and a rough surface for the YSZ to adhere to (via **Thermally Grown Oxide, TGO**). - **Substrate:** The superalloy underneath (DS or SX). ## 2.2 Material Property Requirements (The "TRISCO" Mnemonic) > [!NOTE] > **T** – Tensile strength at 950 °C ≥ 600 MPa > **R** – Resistance to creep (rupture strength ≥ 150 MPa at 1000 h) > **I** – Impact toughness at room temperature > **S** – Stress-rupture & fatigue (LCF + HCF) endurance > **C** – Corrosion (sulfidation) and oxidation resistance > **O** – Oxidation stability (chromia / alumina scale formers) ## 2.3 The γ-γ′ Microstructure (Most KTU-Favoured Topic) Modern superalloys are **two-phase alloys**: - **γ matrix** (FCC Nickel) – the continuous, ductile phase. - **γ′ precipitates** (Ni₃(Al,Ti), ordered L1₂ cubic) – cuboidal particles, **70% volume fraction** in advanced alloys. **Why is γ′ so special?** 1. It is **coherent** with the γ matrix (lattice mismatch < 0.5%) → low interfacial energy. 2. It is **ordered**, so dislocations must travel in **pairs** to preserve order → high anti-phase boundary (APB) energy → strengthening. 3.
