Its **strength increases with temperature** up to ~800 °C (anomalous yielding) — unlike most metals. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Equations you must memorize for KTU PEMET741 Module 3.** | # | Formula | Meaning | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $\sigma_c = \rho \, \omega^2 \, r \, L$ | Centrifugal stress in rotating blade | Pa | | 2 | $\varepsilon_{cr} = A \, \sigma^n \, \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{Q_c}{RT}\right)$ | Norton's creep law | s⁻¹ | | 3 | $t_r = B \, \sigma^{-m} \, \exp\!\left(\dfrac{Q_r}{RT}\right)$ | Creep-rupture life (Larson-Miller form) | h | | 4 | $\sigma_{th} = E \, \alpha \, \Delta T$ | Thermal stress (constrained) | Pa | | 5 | $\Delta T_{TBC} = \dfrac{q \, t}{k}$ | Temperature drop across TBC layer | °C | | 6 | $N_f = K \, \Delta \varepsilon^{-n}$ | Coffin-Manson LCF life | cycles | | 7 | $\sigma_a = \sigma_f' \, (2N_f)^{b}$ | Basquin's HCF relation | Pa | | 8 | $LMP = T\!\left[\log_{10} t_r + C\right]$ | Larson-Miller Parameter | K·h (C ≈ 20) | | 9 | $\dfrac{\Delta T_{eff}}{T_{gas}-T_{metal}} = \dfrac{h_g}{h_g + k/t_{TBC}}$ | TBC effectiveness | dimensionless | | 10 | $\dot{m}_{cool} = \rho A v$ | Coolant mass flow rate (continuity) | kg/s | **Where (key symbols):** - $\rho$ = density, $\omega$ = angular velocity (rad/s), $r$ = mid-span radius, $L$ = blade length - $Q_c, Q_r$ = activation energies (J/mol), $R$ = 8.314 J/mol·K - $E$ = Young's modulus, $\alpha$ = CTE (Coefficient of Thermal Expansion), $\Delta T$ = temperature difference - $q$ = heat flux (W/m²), $t$ = TBC thickness, $k$ = thermal conductivity - $h_g$ = gas-side heat transfer coefficient ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility | Field | Why this topic matters | |---|---| | **Aero-engine design (GE, Rolls-Royce, Pratt & Whitney)** | Every 10 °C rise in TET ≈ 0.5% specific thrust gain — material capability limits the engine. | | **Industrial gas turbines (Siemens, MHI)** | Power plants need 50,000 h creep life → favours DS over SX for cost. | | **Rocket turbopumps (SpaceX Raptor)** | Single-crystal blades handle LOX/methane combustion at 3300 °C. | | **Material science research** | Coatings, Re/Ru alloying, additive manufacturing of SX blades are active R&D areas. | # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Centrifugal Stress in a Rotating Blade **Assumptions (standard for KTU):** Tapered blade approximated as a uniform rotating beam of length $L$ at mid-radius $r_m$, with mass per unit length $m$. **Step 1 — Free Body of an Element** Consider a differential element of the blade at radius $r$, length $dr$. The mass is $dm = \rho A \, dr$ where $A$ is the cross-sectional area. **Step 2 — Centrifugal Force on the Element** The element experiences an outward centrifugal force as it rotates with the disk: $$dF = dm \cdot \omega^2 r = \rho A \, \omega^2 r \, dr$$ **Step 3 — Total Tensile Force at Section r** The total tensile force at any section of radius $r$ must support the centrifugal force of all the blade material lying **outside** that section (from $r$ to $r+L$). The axial position from the root is $x = r - r_{root}$, so integration runs from $r$ to $r_{root}+L$: $$F(r) = \int_{r}^{r_{root}+L} \rho A \, \omega^2 \, r' \, dr'$$ **Step 4 — Evaluate the Integral** For a first estimate, assume $A$ is constant and $\rho$ is constant: $$F(r) = \rho A \omega^2 \int_{r}^{r_{root}+L} r' \, dr' = \rho A \omega^2 \left[ \dfrac{(r_{root}+L)^2 - r^2}{2} \right]$$ **Step 5 — Maximum Force at the Root (r = r_root)** At the root, $r = r_{root}$, so the term becomes: $$F_{max} = \rho A \omega^2 \left[ \dfrac{(r_{root}+L)^2 - r_{root}^2}{2} \right] = \rho A \omega^2 \left[ r_{root} L + \dfrac{L^2}{2} \right]$$ **Step 6 — Centrifugal Stress** Dividing the maximum force by the root cross-sectional area $A$: $$\sigma_c = \dfrac{F_{max}}{A} = \rho \omega^2 \left( r_{root} L + \dfrac{L^2}{2} \right)$$ **Step 7 — Engineering Shortcut (KTU Preferred Form)** Because $r_{root} \gg L/2$ in real blades, the dominant term is $r_{root} L$. Engineers often write the **simplified form**: $$\boxed{\sigma_c \approx \rho \, \omega^2 \, r_m \, L}$$ where $r_m = r_{root} + L/2$ is the mean radius. This is the form listed in the KTU Formula Sheet. > [!NOTE] > **Examiner's Note:** The full integral is not required in KTU unless specifically asked. Quote the **boxed form** unless the question says *"derive"*. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Temperature Drop Across a Thermal Barrier Coating **Scenario:** Hot gas at $T_g$ impinges on a TBC layer of thickness $t$ and thermal conductivity $k$. The bond-coat and substrate interface is at $T_m$ (metal temperature). The gas-side heat transfer coefficient is $h_g$. **Step 1 — Steady-State 1-D Heat Conduction (Fourier's Law)** Heat flux $q$ through the TBC is: $$q = \dfrac{k}{t}\left(T_{s} - T_{m}\right)$$ where $T_s$ is the **gas-side surface temperature of the TBC**. **Step 2 — Convective Heat Transfer from Gas to TBC Surface** $$q = h_g \left(T_g - T_s\right)$$ **Step 3 — Eliminate q to Solve for T_s** Equating both expressions for $q$: $$h_g \left(T_g - T_s\right) = \dfrac{k}{t}\left(T_s - T_m\right)$$ $$h_g T_g - h_g T_s = \dfrac{k}{t} T_s - \dfrac{k}{t} T_m$$ $$h_g T_g + \dfrac{k}{t} T_m = T_s \!\left( h_g + \dfrac{k}{t} \right)$$ $$T_s = \dfrac{h_g T_g + \dfrac{k}{t} T_m}{h_g + \dfrac{k}{t}}$$ **Step 4 — Temperature Drop Across the TBC** $$\Delta T_{TBC} = T_s - T_m = \dfrac{h_g (T_g - T_m)}{h_g + \dfrac{k}{t}}$$ **Step 5 — Rearrange to "Effectiveness" Form** $$\boxed{\dfrac{\Delta T_{TBC}}{T_g - T_m} = \dfrac{h_g}{h_g + \dfrac{k}{t}} = \dfrac{1}{1 + \dfrac{k}{h_g \, t}}}$$ **Step 6 — Engineering Insight** - When $k/t \ll h_g$ (thick TBC, low conductivity) → effectiveness → 1 → almost **all** the gas-to-metal temperature difference is dropped across the TBC. - For YSZ ($k \approx 2$ W/m·K) with $t = 300$ µm and $h_g = 1500$ W/m²·K: $$\dfrac{k}{h_g t} = \dfrac{2}{1500 \times 300 \times 10^{-6}} = \dfrac{2}{0.45} \approx 4.4$$ $$\text{Effectiveness} = \dfrac{1}{1 + 4.4} \approx 0.185$$ So only ~18.5% of the gas-to-metal $\Delta T$ is dropped in this TBC alone — but combined with **internal convection cooling**, the **total cooling effectiveness can exceed 0.6**, allowing TET > 1600 °C. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Creep-Rupture Life (Larson-Miller Parameter) **Step 1 — Assume an Arrhenius-type Rupture Process** Time to rupture $t_r$ depends on stress $\sigma$ through an Arrhenius relation: $$t_r = C_1 \sigma^{-m} \exp\!\left(\dfrac{Q_r}{RT}\right)$$ **Step 2 — Take the Logarithm (Base 10)** $$\log_{10} t_r = \log_{10} C_1 - m \log_{10} \sigma + \dfrac{Q_r}{2.303 \, R \, T}$$ **Step 3 — Group All Temperature-Dependent Terms into a Single Parameter** $$\log_{10} t_r + \dfrac{Q_r}{2.303 \, R \, T} = \log_{10} C_1 - m \log_{10} \sigma$$ The left-hand side, multiplied by $T$, is the **Larson-Miller Parameter (LMP)**: $$\boxed{LMP = T \left[ \log_{10} t_r + C \right] \quad \text{where } C \approx 20 \text{ (empirical)}}$$ **Step 4 — Practical Use (Master Curve)** For a given alloy, plot $LMP$ vs. $\log_{10} \sigma$. All experimental data collapses onto **a single straight master curve** — extremely useful for extrapolating short-lab-test data to long-engine-life predictions. --- ## 3.4 Quick Numerical Worked Example (For KTU Practice) > A first-stage turbine blade is rotating at $\omega = 1500$ rad/s. The mid-span radius is $r_m = 0.25$ m, the blade length is $L = 0.08$ m, and the alloy density is $\rho = 8200$ kg/m³. Estimate the centrifugal stress. **Solution:** $$\sigma_c = \rho \omega^2 r_m L = 8200 \times (1500)^2 \times 0.25 \times 0.08$$ $$\sigma_c = 8200 \times 2.25 \times 10^{6} \times 0.02$$ $$\sigma_c = 8200 \times 45{,}000 = 3.69 \times 10^{8} \text{ Pa} = \mathbf{369 \text{ MPa}}$$ This is realistic for an aero-engine first-stage blade — well within the capability of SX superalloys (≈ 600 MPa at 1000 °C). # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Evolution of Blade Materials vs. TET Capability ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Conventional Wrought Alloys
TET less than 900 C] --> B[Vacuum Cast Equiaxed Superalloys
Inconel 718 Waspaloy
TET 900 to 1050 C] B --> C[Directionally Solidified DS Blades
PWA 1422 GTD 111
TET 1050 to 1200 C] C --> D[Single Crystal SX Blades
CMSX 4 Rene N5
TET greater than 1200 C] D --> E[Thermal Barrier Coatings TBC
YSZ plus MCrAlY Bond Coat
TET 1500 to 1700 C] E --> F[Ceramic Matrix Composites CMC
Future TET greater than 1700 C] A --> G[Limitation: Grain Boundary Sliding] B --> H[Limitation: Transverse Grain Boundaries] C --> I[Gain: No Transverse Grain Boundaries] D --> J[Gain: Zero Grain Boundaries] E --> K[Gain: 100 to 150 C Drop in Metal Temp] ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Cross-Section of a Modern Cooled Blade with TBC ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph HOT_GAS[Combustion Gas 1600 C] G1[Hot Gas Stream] end G1 -->|Convective Heat| COAT subgraph BLADE_SECTION[Turbine Blade Wall Cross Section] COAT[Top Coat YSZ 300 micron] COAT -->|Heat Conduction| BOND BOND[Bond Coat MCrAlY 100 micron] BOND -->|Heat Conduction| SUB SUB[Superalloy Substrate SX 1 to 2 mm] SUB --> INTC[Internal Cooling Channels] INTC -->|Cool Air from Compressor| COOLIN COOLIN[Cold Air 600 C] end INTC -->|Convective Cooling| SUB ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Material Selection Decision Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD START[New Turbine Blade Design] --> Q1{TET Required?} Q1 -->|less than 900 C| S1[Use Wrought Nimonic 80A] Q1 -->|900 to 1050 C| S2[Use Equiaxed Inconel 718] Q1 -->|1050 to 1200 C| S3[Use DS Alloy PWA 1422] Q1 -->|1200 to 1400 C| S4[Use SX CMSX 4] Q1 -->|greater than 1400 C| S5[Use SX plus TBC plus Internal Cooling] S1 --> VAL[Validate Creep and LCF] S2 --> VAL S3 --> VAL S4 --> VAL S5 --> VAL VAL --> END[Component Ready for Engine Test] ``` ## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4 — Microstructure of γ-γ′ Superalloy ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph MATRIX[Gamma Matrix FCC Nickel] M1[Ductile Continuous Phase] end subgraph PRECIP[Gamma Prime Ni3Al Cuboidal] P1[Ordered L1 Cubic] P2[70 percent Volume Fraction] P3[Strengthens with Temperature] end MATRIX --- PRECIP PRECIP -->|Dislocations Cut in Pairs| APB[Anti Phase Boundary Energy High] APB -->|Result| STR[High Temperature Strength] ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]** **Q1. What are the essential requirements of a material used for gas turbine blades? List any four.** **Model Answer:** A gas turbine blade material must simultaneously satisfy the following **TRISCO** requirements: 1. **High tensile & creep strength** at operating temperature (≥ 600 MPa at 950 °C for first-stage blades) to resist centrifugal loads. 2. **Excellent creep-rupture resistance** (≥ 150 MPa at 1000 h) to ensure 10,000–30,000 h service life. 3. **High thermal fatigue & LCF life** to survive repeated start-stop thermal cycling. 4. **Oxidation & hot-corrosion resistance** — must form a protective chromia or alumina scale in combustion gases containing S, Na, V contaminants. 5. **Low density** (Ni-base ≈ 8.2 g/cm³ preferred over Co-base ≈ 8.9) to reduce disk-loading. 6. **Manufacturability** — castable into complex cooled geometries (DS/SX casting capability). 7. **Phase stability** — no formation of embrittling TCP (topologically close-packed) phases during long-term exposure. *(Any 4 points × 1 mark + 1 mark for structure = 3 marks)* --- > **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question]** **Q2. Distinguish between Directionally Solidified (DS) and Single Crystal (SX) turbine blades.** **Model Answer:** | Feature | DS Blades | SX Blades | |---|---|---| | Grain structure | Columnar grains, all aligned along blade axis | One single crystal — no grain boundaries at all | | Grain boundaries present | Longitudinal only | **None** | | Casting method | HRS / LMC in thermal gradient furnace | Seed crystal + helical selector | | Creep-rupture life | ~3× better than equiaxed | ~6× better than equiaxed | | TET capability | 1050 – 1200 °C | 1200 – 1400 °C | | Cost | Moderate | Very high | | Grain-boundary strengtheners (C, B, Zr, Hf) | Present | Removed (deliberately) | | Alloying additions | Standard | Heavy elements — Re, W, Ta, Mo, Ru | | Example alloys | GTD-111, PWA 1422 | CMSX-4, PWA 1480, René N5 | --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) > **[KTU ESE Model Question — Module 3, KTU 2024 Scheme]** ### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) **(a)** Explain the **γ-γ′ microstructure** of nickel-based superalloys. How does the γ′ precipitate contribute to high-temperature strength? **(7 marks)** **(b)** A first-stage high-pressure turbine blade is rotating at **12,000 rpm** with a mean radius of **0.22 m** and an axial length of **0.075 m**. The blade material has a density of **8,200 kg/m³**. **(i)** Calculate the centrifugal stress. **(ii)** If the blade is upgraded to a hollow, internally cooled geometry that reduces the effective density contribution by **30%**, what is the new stress? **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution — Part (a) **1. Definition of γ and γ′ phases:** - The **γ matrix** is a continuous, disordered FCC nickel solid solution containing Co, Cr, Mo, W (solid-solution strengtheners). - The **γ′ precipitate** is an ordered intermetallic compound with the formula Ni₃(Al,Ti) having an L1₂ (Cu₃Au-type) crystal structure. It is **coherent** with the γ matrix. **2. Strengthening mechanisms of γ′:** - **Coherency strain strengthening:** Lattice mismatch between γ and γ′ (≈ 0.1–0.5%) generates elastic strain fields that impede dislocation motion. - **Antiphase boundary (APB) strengthening:** Because γ′ is *ordered*, a passing dislocation creates an APB. To restore order, dislocations must travel in **pairs** — one creates the APB, the other annihilates it. The APB energy (≈ 0.2 J/m²) acts as a drag force. - **Anomalous yielding:** The flow stress of γ′ *increases* with temperature up to ~800 °C, which is **opposite** to most metals. This is due to the **Kear-Wilsdorf lock** mechanism where screw dislocations cross-slip from {111} to {100}, becoming sessile. - **High volume fraction:** Modern alloys have **γ′ volume fraction up to 70%**, providing a dense forest of obstacles. **3. Why this matters for blades:** At 950 °C, the γ′ phase retains ~80% of its room-temperature strength, while the matrix is severely weakened. The γ′ therefore carries most of the load in the operating temperature range — making the blade material a "**precipitation-hardened composite at the microscale**". *(Expected: 1 mark for definition, 4 marks for mechanisms, 2 marks for engineering relevance = 7 marks)* #### Model Solution — Part (b) **(i) Centrifugal Stress Calculation** Convert rotational speed to angular velocity: $$\omega = 2\pi N / 60 = 2\pi \times 12000 / 60 = 2\pi \times 200 = 1256.64 \text{ rad/s}$$ Apply the KTU blade stress formula: $$\sigma_c = \rho \, \omega^2 \, r_m \, L$$ Substitute values: $$\sigma_c = 8200 \times (1256.64)^2 \times 0.22 \times 0.075$$ Compute step-by-step: $$\omega^2 = (1256.64)^2 = 1.5791 \times 10^{6} \text{ rad}^2/\text{s}^2$$ $$r_m \times L = 0.22 \times 0.075 = 0.0165 \text{ m}^2$$ $$\sigma_c = 8200 \times 1.5791 \times 10^{6} \times 0.0165$$ $$\sigma_c = 8200 \times 26{,}055$$ $$\sigma_c = 2.137 \times 10^{8} \text{ Pa} = \boxed{213.7 \text{ MPa}}$$ **(ii) Stress with 30% Mass Reduction (Hollow Cooled Blade)** The new effective mass is 70% of the original. Since $\sigma_c \propto \rho$: $$\sigma_{c,\text{new}} = 0.70 \times 213.7 = \boxed{149.6 \text{ MPa}}$$ This is well within the **creep-rupture strength** of CMSX-4 at 1000 °C (~250 MPa at 1000 h), so the design is **safe** with a safety factor of ~1.67. *(Expected: 2 marks for formula, 2 marks for substitution, 1 mark for ω² calculation, 1 mark for numerical answer, 1 mark for safety comment = 7 marks)* --- ### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice* **(a)** Describe the **Thermal Barrier Coating (TBC) system** used on modern turbine blades. Explain the role of each layer and derive the expression for the temperature drop across the TBC. **(7 marks)** **(b)** With the help of a **Larson-Miller Parameter (LMP)** curve, explain how creep-rupture data is used to predict long-term blade life from short-term lab tests. An alloy has a creep-rupture life of **5000 h at 900 °C under 200 MPa**. Using the LMP master curve, estimate its expected life at **1000 °C under the same stress** (assume $C = 20$). **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution — Part (a) **1. The TBC System (Top-to-Bottom):** | Layer | Material | Thickness | Function | |---|---|---|---| | **Top Coat** | 6–8 wt% Y₂O₃-stabilized ZrO₂ (YSZ) | 250–400 µm | Thermal insulation (low k ≈ 2 W/m·K), erosion resistance | | **Thermally Grown Oxide (TGO)** | α-Al₂O₃ | 0.5–5 µm | Forms at the bond coat–top coat interface; oxygen barrier | | **Bond Coat** | MCrAlY (M = Ni, Co) | 75–125 µm | Adherence, oxidation resistance, accommodates CTE mismatch | | **Substrate** | DS or SX Superalloy | 1–3 mm | Load-bearing | **2. Why YSZ?** - Polymorphism problem: pure ZrO₂ undergoes a tetragonal → monoclinic phase change at ~1170 °C with a 3–5% volume change → spallation. Adding **6–8% Y₂O₃** stabilizes the **tetragonal t′ phase**, suppressing the destructive transformation. - Has **low thermal conductivity** (2 W/m·K vs. 25 W/m·K for superalloy) due to phonon scattering by oxygen vacancies. **3. Derivation of Temperature Drop:** (Use the derivation from Section 3.2 above. The final boxed result is:) $$\boxed{\dfrac{\Delta T_{TBC}}{T_g - T_m} = \dfrac{1}{1 + \dfrac{k}{h_g \, t}}}$$ *(4 marks for layer description, 3 marks for derivation = 7 marks)* #### Model Solution — Part (b) **1. Concept of LMP:** The **Larson-Miller Parameter** correlates temperature, stress, and rupture time into a single number for a given alloy: $$LMP = T \left[\log_{10} t_r + 20\right] \quad \text{(with } T \text{ in Kelvin)}$$ **Master curve:** For a given alloy, plotting $LMP$ vs. $\log_{10} \sigma$ yields a single curve. From a short lab test, you read off the $LMP$ value at that stress, and then **predict the rupture time at any other temperature** by re-arranging: $$t_r = 10^{\,LMP/T - 20}$$ **2. Calculation:** **Given:** $t_{r1} = 5000$ h, $T_1 = 900\,°C = 1173$ K, $\sigma = 200$ MPa. **Step 1 — Compute $LMP_1$:** $$LMP_1 = 1173 \times \left[\log_{10}(5000) + 20\right]$$ $$\log_{10}(5000) = 3.699$$ $$LMP_1 = 1173 \times (3.699 + 20) = 1173 \times 23.699$$ $$\boxed{LMP_1 = 27{,}799 \text{ K·h (approx.)}}$$ **Step 2 — At new temperature $T_2 = 1000\,°C = 1273$ K, same stress, $LMP$ is constant (same curve point):** $$1273 \times \left[\log_{10} t_{r2} + 20\right] = 27{,}799$$ $$\log_{10} t_{r2} + 20 = \dfrac{27{,}799}{1273} = 21.838$$ $$\log_{10} t_{r2} = 1.838$$ $$t_{r2} = 10^{1.838} = \boxed{68.9 \text{ h}}$$ **3. Engineering Interpretation:** - A **100 °C increase in temperature** reduces life from 5000 h to **~69 h** — a **~72× reduction**. - This is the **"temperature-knife" effect** that drives the need for TBCs and internal cooling in modern engines. - In reality, a 100 °C jump would also change the LMP value at the same stress (the master curve is not perfectly horizontal), but this first-order estimate is what KTU expects. *(2 marks for concept, 3 marks for LMP₁, 2 marks for t_{r2} and interpretation = 7 marks)* --- > [!WARNING] > **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks:** > 1. **Unit mistakes:** Reporting $LMP$ in °C·h instead of **K·h** — costs 1 mark. > 2. **Forgetting to convert** rpm → rad/s (multiplication by $2\pi/60$) in centrifugal stress — costs 1 mark. > 3. **Confusing γ and γ′** in the microstructure answer. **γ = matrix, γ′ = precipitate** — clear writing is rewarded. > 4. **Omitting the TGO layer** in the TBC diagram — this is the most-skipped layer and costs 1 mark. > 5. **Quoting the "100–150 °C TBC drop" without the formula** — KTU wants the **expression** and a **numerical sample calculation**. > 6. **Forgetting the constant $C = 20$** in the LMP formula — many students use $C = 0$ and get a wrong answer by a factor of $10^{20}$! > 7. **Not stating engineering assumptions** (e.g., "1-D steady state, constant k") in derivations — KTU awards marks for clearly listing assumptions. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Print this section and stick it in your notebook the night before the exam.** - [ ] **Turbine blade materials must survive 1500–1700 °C gas at 150–300 MPa stress** while spinning at 10,000–15,000 rpm. - [ ] **Evolution sequence:** Wrought → Equiaxed → DS → SX → SX+TBC → CMC (future). - [ ] **γ-γ′ superalloy microstructure** is the backbone — γ matrix (FCC Ni) + γ′ precipitates (Ni₃Al, ordered L1₂, ~70% volume fraction). - [ ] **γ′ strengthening mechanisms:** Coherency strain, APB, anomalous yielding (Kear-Wilsdorf locks), high volume fraction. - [ ] **DS blades** eliminate transverse grain boundaries; **SX blades** eliminate *all* grain boundaries. - [ ] **Single-crystal alloys** use heavy elements (Re, W, Ta, Mo, Ru) because grain-boundary strengtheners (C, B, Zr, Hf) are removed. - [ ] **TBC** = YSZ top coat (6–8% Y₂O₃–ZrO₂, low k ≈ 2 W/m·K) + MCrAlY bond coat + TGO (α-Al₂O₃) + superalloy substrate. - [ ] **TBC effectiveness formula:** $\dfrac{\Delta T}{T_g - T_m} = \dfrac{1}{1 + k/(h_g t)}$. - [ ] **Centrifugal stress:** $\sigma_c = \rho \, \omega^2 \, r_m \, L$ (use $\omega$ in **rad/s**). - [ ] **Norton's creep law:** $\dot{\varepsilon}_{cr} = A \sigma^n \exp(-Q_c/RT)$. - [ ] **Larson-Miller Parameter:** $LMP = T[\log_{10} t_r + 20]$, with **T in Kelvin**, used to extrapolate long-term rupture life from short tests. - [ ] **Material requirements (TRISCO):** Tensile, Rupture, Impact, Stress/fatigue, Corrosion, Oxidation. - [ ] **Typical densities:** Ni-base ≈ 8.2 g/cm³, Co-base ≈ 8.9 g/cm³ — Ni preferred for low disk-loading. - [ ] **DS vs. SX life gain:** DS ≈ 3× equiaxed creep life; SX ≈ 6× equiaxed creep life. - [ ] **TBC temperature benefit:** 100–150 °C drop in metal surface temperature. - [ ] **Forces to remember:** $F = \rho A \omega^2 r \, dr$; integrate from $r$ to $r_{root}+L$ for full distribution. - [ ] **Always list assumptions** (1-D, steady state, constant properties) before any derivation on the exam. - [ ] **KTU loves** questions that ask: *"Compare DS and SX"* or *"Explain γ-γ′ microstructure with a neat sketch"* — practice these! - [ ] **Heavy alloying elements in SX:** Re (rhenium) and Ru (ruthenium) are the most expensive — Re costs ~USD 1500/oz, hence SX blades are costly. - [ ] **Emerging trend:** Additive manufacturing (laser powder-bed fusion) of SX-equivalent blades is an active research area but is **not yet flight-certified** for SX-grade.

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET741 - GAS TURBINE AND JET PROPULSION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Manufacturing Techniques

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# MANUFACTURING TECHNIQUES FOR GAS TURBINE COMPONENTS

## 1.1 Core Definition and Scope

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> Manufacturing Techniques in the context of Gas Turbine Engineering refer to the specialized **materials processing**, **forming**, **joining**, and **surface treatment methodologies** employed to fabricate critical hot-section and cold-section components — such as turbine blades, nozzle guide vanes (NGVs), combustion chamber liners, discs, shafts, and casings — using high-performance **nickel-based superalloys**, **titanium alloys**, **ceramic matrix composites (CMCs)**, and **thermal barrier coatings (TBCs)**, so that the components can sustain extreme in-service conditions including temperatures exceeding **1500 K**, centrifugal stresses above **300 MPa**, and corrosive combustion environments.

### Conceptual Analogy — Intuitive Understanding

Imagine you are tasked with building a high-performance Formula 1 racing engine. You cannot simply use regular cast iron or standard welding techniques because the engine operates at extreme temperatures and spins at 15,000+ RPM. Gas turbine components face similar — even harsher — conditions. A single turbine blade must survive:
- Inlet gas temperatures of **1700–2000 K**
- Tensile centrifugal stresses up to **500 MPa**
- Thermal cycling from cold start to full power
- Oxidation, hot corrosion, and creep

Therefore, the **manufacturing route** itself becomes a *design variable*. The way a blade is cast, forged, machined, or coated directly determines its in-service life. This is why gas turbine manufacturing is a sophisticated, multi-stage discipline blending **metallurgy**, **thermal engineering**, and **precision process control**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:**
> According to the KTU 2024 PEMET741 syllabus, Module 3 emphasizes the link between *manufacturing process selection* and *service performance*. Students are expected to justify process choice on the basis of **stress-temperature-corrosion** tri-axial requirements, not just geometry.

### 1.2 Classification of Gas Turbine Manufacturing Techniques

The manufacturing techniques for gas turbine components are broadly classified into the following families:

| S. No. | Process Family | Typical Components Manufactured |
| :---: | :--- | :--- |
| **1** | **Casting Techniques** | Turbine blades, NGVs, combustor swirlers |
| **2** | **Forming Techniques** | Discs, shaft forgings, ring-rolled casings |
| **3** | **Powder Metallurgy** | Discs, complex superalloy preforms |
| **4** | **Joining Techniques** | Combustor liners, transition pieces, honeycomb seals |
| **5** | **Machining & EDM** | Cooling holes, fir-tree roots, airfoil profiles |
| **6** | **Surface Engineering / Coatings** | TBCs, aluminides, MCrAlY bond coats |
| **7** | **Additive Manufacturing** | Prototype burners, repaired blades, complex cooling passages |

> [!TIP]
> **Key Insight for KTU Exams:**
> When asked to *select* a manufacturing process, always tie your answer to **three checkpoints**: (a) operating temperature, (b) stress state, and (c) required creep-fatigue life. This is exactly how KTU examiners grade Part B answers.

# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Investment Casting of Turbine Blades

Investment casting (also called **lost-wax casting**) is the *workhorse* process for manufacturing single-crystal and directionally-solidified superalloy blades. The process unfolds in these structured logic steps:

1. **Wax Pattern Injection:** A disposable wax replica of the blade is injection-molded with allowances for ceramic shell shrinkage and metal solidification shrinkage.
2. **Pattern Assembly:** Multiple wax patterns are mounted on a wax tree (gating system).
3. **Ceramic Shell Building:** The tree is repeatedly dipped in a refractory slurry (e.g., **colloidal silica + fused silica flour**) and stuccoed with alumina/silica grains. Typically **6–10 layers** are built.
4. **Dewaxing (Autoclaving):** The wax is melted out at **~150–200 °C** in an autoclave, leaving a green ceramic mold.
5. **Shell Firing:** The ceramic shell is fired at **~1000 °C** to remove residual wax and sinter the refractory.
6. **Metal Pouring:** Molten superalloy (e.g., **Inconel 738, Mar-M247, CMSX-4**) is poured at **~1500 °C** under vacuum.
7. **Directional / Single-Crystal Solidification:** A thermal gradient (typically **G = 50–200 K/cm**) is applied through a chill plate to control grain morphology.
8. **Shell Removal & Finishing:** The shell is knocked off, gates are cut, and the blade is inspected, machined, and coated.

### The Solidification Concept — Why It Matters

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why directional solidification?**
> In conventionally cast (equiaxed) blades, grain boundaries oriented transverse to the centrifugal stress axis act as **creep crack nucleation sites**. By aligning all grains parallel to the stress axis (columnar grains), or by eliminating grain boundaries altogether (single crystal), the creep life improves by **3× to 8×** at the same temperature.

The **thermal gradient (G)** and **solidification velocity (V)** govern the grain structure via the classical solidification parameter:

$$G / V \; \geq \; \text{Critical Value for Columnar Growth}$$

A high G/V ratio promotes columnar grains; a low G/V ratio promotes equiaxed (transverse) grains. The **cooling rate (Ġ = G · V)** controls the dendrite arm spacing:

$$\lambda_1 = A \cdot \dot{G}^{-n}$$

where $\lambda_1$ = primary dendrite arm spacing (in µm), $A$ and $n$ are alloy-dependent constants (typically $A \approx 100$, $n \approx 0.5$ for nickel superalloys).

### 2.2 Superplastic Forming (SPF) and Diffusion Bonding (DB)

For **titanium alloy** components such as compressor discs, fan blades (e.g., **Ti-6Al-4V**), and hollow wide-chord fans, superplastic forming is widely used.

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** Superplasticity is the ability of a fine-grained material to undergo **uniform tensile elongations exceeding 200%** without necking, when deformed at a temperature above ~0.5 × T_m and at low strain rates (typically $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-3} \text{ s}^{-1}$).

The governing constitutive equation is:

$$\sigma = K \cdot \dot{\varepsilon}^{m}$$

where $\sigma$ is flow stress, $\dot{\varepsilon}$ is strain rate, $K$ is the strength coefficient, and $m$ is the **strain-rate sensitivity index** (typically **0.3–0.7** for superplastic materials; $m \geq 0.3$ is the threshold).

**Diffusion Bonding (DB)** often accompanies SPF to produce hollow, integrally stiffened structures (e.g., hollow titanium fan blades with internal spar webs).

### 2.3 Thermal Barrier Coatings (TBC) Manufacturing

TBCs are applied via **Electron Beam Physical Vapor Deposition (EB-PVD)** or **Atmospheric Plasma Spraying (APS)**. The architecture consists of four layers:

1. **Substrate** — Ni-based superalloy
2. **Bond Coat** — MCrAlY (M = Ni, Co) or Pt-aluminide, ~100 µm
3. **Thermally Grown Oxide (TGO)** — α-Al₂O₃, ~1–2 µm (formed during service)
4. **Top Coat** — Yttria-Stabilized Zirconia (YSZ), ~100–500 µm

The temperature drop across a TBC is governed by **Fourier's law** for steady-state 1-D conduction:

$$\Delta T_{TBC} = \frac{q \cdot t_{TBC}}{k_{TBC}}$$

where $q$ is the heat flux (W/m²), $t_{TBC}$ is the top-coat thickness, and $k_{TBC}$ is the effective thermal conductivity of YSZ (~**0.8–2.0 W/m·K**, much lower than the substrate's ~**25 W/m·K**).

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — these are the high-yield formulas KTU examiners test in Module 3.**

| # | Formula / Equation | Symbol Legend | Application / Where Used |
| :-: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $G/V \geq G/V_{critical}$ | G = thermal gradient, V = solidification rate | Solidification morphology control in cast blades |
| 2 | $\lambda_1 = A \cdot \dot{G}^{-n}$ | $\lambda_1$ = dendrite spacing, $\dot{G}$ = cooling rate | Microstructural refinement in cast superalloys |
| 3 | $\sigma = K \cdot \dot{\varepsilon}^{m}$ | $\sigma$ = flow stress, $m$ = strain-rate sensitivity | Superplastic forming (SPF) of Ti alloys |
| 4 | $\Delta T_{TBC} = \frac{q \cdot t}{k_{TBC}}$ | $q$ = heat flux, $t$ = coat thickness, $k$ = conductivity | Thermal Barrier Coating design |
| 5 | $\dot{Q}_{rad} = \sigma \cdot \varepsilon \cdot (T_s^4 - T_{gas}^4)$ | $\sigma$ = Stefan-Boltzmann const., $\varepsilon$ = emissivity | Combustor liner cooling analysis |
| 6 | $\Delta R = \alpha_{th} \cdot \Delta T \cdot L_0$ | $\alpha_{th}$ = thermal expansion coeff., $L_0$ = length | Thermal growth allowance in cast components |
| 7 | $t_{creep} \propto \sigma^{-n} \cdot \exp(Q/RT)$ | $\sigma$ = stress, Q = activation energy, R = gas const. | Creep life of cast superalloy blades |
| 8 | $R_a = f(\text{machining}, \text{polishing}, \text{grit})$ | $R_a$ = arithmetic mean roughness | Surface finish required for coating adhesion |

> Real-world utility: Every modern turbofan engine (e.g., GE9X, Rolls-Royce Trent XWB, Pratt & Whitney GTF) employs a combination of at least **five** of these manufacturing technologies. A jet engine is essentially a "**manufacturing technology assembly**."

# 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS AND ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: Thermal Gradient and Cooling Rate Relationship

A frequently asked KTU derivation is the link between **thermal gradient (G)**, **solidification velocity (V)**, and **cooling rate (Ġ)**. The cooling rate is defined as the rate of temperature change with time at a fixed point in the solidifying metal:

$$\dot{G} = \frac{dT}{dt}$$

The thermal gradient is defined as the spatial rate of temperature change across the solid-liquid interface at a fixed instant:

$$G = \frac{dT}{dx}$$

The solidification velocity is the rate at which the solid-liquid interface advances along the casting axis:

$$V = \frac{dx}{dt}$$

Now applying the **chain rule of calculus** for composite derivatives:

$$\frac{dT}{dt} = \frac{dT}{dx} \cdot \frac{dx}{dt}$$

Substituting the definitions:

$$\dot{G} = G \cdot V$$

> This identity is foundational. KTU examiners often test whether students understand that **Ġ is the product, not the sum**, of G and V.

**Typical industrial values for gas turbine blade casting:**

| Parameter | Equiaxed Casting | DS Casting | SC Casting |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| G (K/cm) | 5–20 | 30–80 | 50–200 |
| V (cm/h) | 20–60 | 10–30 | 1–5 |
| Ġ (K/h) | 100–1200 | 300–2400 | 50–1000 |

## 3.2 Derivation: Temperature Drop Across a TBC Layer

Consider a 1-D steady-state heat conduction through a multi-layer wall: substrate → bond coat → TGO → ceramic top coat → hot gas. Assuming planar walls, the overall temperature drop is given by Fourier's law:

$$q = \frac{\Delta T_{total}}{R_{total}} = \frac{\Delta T_{total}}{\sum \frac{t_i}{k_i}}$$

For a simplified two-layer TBC system (top coat + bond coat):

$$\Delta T_{TBC} = q \cdot \left( \frac{t_{top}}{k_{top}} + \frac{t_{bond}}{k_{bond}} \right)$$

**Step 1 — Identify the thermal resistance of each layer:**

$$R_{top} = \frac{t_{top}}{k_{top}}, \quad R_{bond} = \frac{t_{bond}}{k_{bond}}$$

**Step 2 — Sum the resistances (series thermal circuit):**

$$R_{total} = R_{top} + R_{bond} = \frac{t_{top}}{k_{top}} + \frac{t_{bond}}{k_{bond}}$$

**Step 3 — Apply Fourier's law in differential form:**

$$q = \frac{\Delta T_{TBC}}{R_{total}}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $\Delta T_{TBC}$:**

$$\Delta T_{TBC} = q \cdot R_{total} = q \cdot \left( \frac{t_{top}}{k_{top}} + \frac{t_{bond}}{k_{bond}} \right)$$

**Worked numerical example (typical aero-engine HP blade):**

Given:
- $q = 1.0 \times 10^{6} \; \text{W/m}^2$
- $t_{top} = 250 \; \mu m = 2.5 \times 10^{-4} \; \text{m}$
- $k_{top} = 1.5 \; \text{W/m·K}$ (for YSZ)
- $t_{bond} = 100 \; \mu m = 1.0 \times 10^{-4} \; \text{m}$
- $k_{bond} = 17 \; \text{W/m·K}$ (for NiCoCrAlY)

Compute thermal resistances:

$$R_{top} = \frac{2.5 \times 10^{-4}}{1.5} = 1.667 \times 10^{-4} \; \text{m}^2 \text{K/W}$$

$$R_{bond} = \frac{1.0 \times 10^{-4}}{17} = 5.882 \times 10^{-6} \; \text{m}^2 \text{K/W}$$

Sum:

$$R_{total} = 1.667 \times 10^{-4} + 5.882 \times 10^{-6} = 1.726 \times 10^{-4} \; \text{m}^2 \text{K/W}$$

Temperature drop:

$$\Delta T_{TBC} = 1.0 \times 10^{6} \times 1.726 \times 10^{-4} = 172.6 \; \text{K}$$

> **Engineering Interpretation:** A TBC layer of total thickness 350 µm produces a **~170 K temperature drop**, allowing the superalloy substrate to operate ~170 K cooler than the gas. This is the *single most important reason* gas turbines can push turbine inlet temperatures (TIT) beyond the melting point of the blade alloy.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Process Selection for Common Gas Turbine Components

| Component | Material | Peak Service Temp. (K) | Dominant Failure Mode | Recommended Manufacturing Route | Justification |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| HP Turbine Blade | CMSX-4 (SC Ni-SX) | 1500–1700 | Creep, TGO spallation | Investment casting (SC) + EB-PVD TBC | Grain boundaries eliminated → creep life ↑ 5–8× |
| HP NGV | Inconel 738 LC | 1400–1600 | Thermal fatigue | Directional solidification casting + APS TBC | Columnar grains + rough TBC for turbulent cooling |
| Combustor Liner | Haynes 230 / Nimonic 263 | 1200–1400 | Oxidation, thermal fatigue | Sheet forming (roll forming) + welding + aluminide coating | Thin-gauge sheet for film cooling; weld for can-annular assembly |
| HP Compressor Disc | Ti-6Al-4V / IN718 | 600–900 | LCF, dwell-fatigue | Closed-die forging (β forging) | Refined grain flow → LCF life ↑ |
| Combustion Chamber Swirler | Inconel 625 | 900–1100 | Hot corrosion, oxidation | Investment casting | Complex geometry with internal swirl vanes |
| Fan Blade | Ti-6Al-4V | 350–400 | Foreign object damage, fatigue | SPF/DB hollow blade or forged | Hollow geometry → weight ↓ 30% |
| Exhaust Casing | Inconel 718 / SS 321 | 800–1000 | Oxidation, sheet bulging | Ring-rolling + welding | Large thin-walled structure |

# 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS AND SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Investment Casting Process Flow

The following Mermaid flowchart captures the end-to-end process flow for **directionally solidified (DS) and single-crystal (SC) blade casting**, which is the single most important manufacturing process in the hot section of a modern gas turbine.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[StepA: Wax Pattern Injection] --> B[StepB: Pattern Tree Assembly]
    B --> C[StepC: Ceramic Slurry Dipping Layer 1]
    C --> D[StepD: Stuccoing with Refractory Grains]
    D --> E[StepE: Repeat Dipping for 6 to 10 Layers]
    E --> F[StepF: Dewaxing in Autoclave 150 to 200 C]
    F --> G[StepG: Ceramic Shell Firing at 1000 C]
    G --> H[StepH: Vacuum Pouring of Molten Superalloy 1500 C]
    H --> I[StepI: Directional Solidification via Chiller]
    I --> J[StepJ: Single Crystal Grain Selector]
    J --> K[StepK: Shell Removal by Vibratory Knockout]
    K --> L[StepL: Gate Cutting and Heat Treatment]
    L --> M[StepM: Precision Machining of Root and Tips]
    M --> N[StepN: Surface Cleaning and Grit Blasting]
    N --> O[StepO: EB-PVD or APS Thermal Barrier Coating]
    O --> P[StepP: Dimensional and Crystallographic Inspection]
    P --> Q[StepQ: Final Approved Blade]

    H --> R[StepR: Air Cooled Vacuum]
    I --> S[StepS: High Thermal Gradient G greater than 50 K per cm]
    S --> T[StepT: Controlled Withdrawal Rate V]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Thermal Barrier Coating (TBC) Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph COMPONENT_LAYERS
        A1[Layer1: Ni Superalloy Substrate IN738 or CMSX4] --> A2[Layer2: Bond Coat MCrAlY or PtAluminide 100 microns]
        A2 --> A3[Layer3: Thermally Grown Oxide Al2O3 1 to 2 microns]
        A3 --> A4[Layer4: Yttria Stabilized Zirconia Top Coat 100 to 500 microns]
    end

    A4 --> B1[Heat Flux q enters from hot gas 1500 K plus]
    A1 --> B2[Substrate interface temperature reduced to 1100 K]
    B1 --> B3[Temperature drop DeltaT of 170 K across TBC]
    B2 --> B3
    B3 --> B4[Effective thermal conductivity k 0.8 to 2.0 W per mK]
```

## 4.3 Comparative Block Diagram — Conventional vs. Directional vs. Single-Crystal Casting

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Equiaxed Casting] --> A1[Random grain orientation]
    A1 --> A2[Transverse grain boundaries]
    A2 --> A3[Creep crack nucleation sites]
    A3 --> A4[Lowest creep life baseline]

    B[Directionally Solidified DS] --> B1[Columnar grains parallel to stress axis]
    B1 --> B2[No transverse grain boundaries]
    B2 --> B3[Creep life improved 3 to 5 times]
    B3 --> B4[Used for NGVs and early HP blades]

    C[Single Crystal SC] --> C1[No grain boundaries at all]
    C1 --> C2[Alloys with refractory additions Re W Ta Mo]
    C2 --> C3[Creep life improved 5 to 8 times over equiaxed]
    C3 --> C4[Used for modern HP turbine blades]
```

## 4.4 Process Block Flow — Superplastic Forming and Diffusion Bonding (SPF/DB)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step1: Ti-6Al-4V Sheet Preparation] --> B[Step2: Surface Cleaning and Etching]
    B --> C[Step3: Stop-off Material Pattern Application]
    C --> D[Step4: Sheet Stacking for DB]
    D --> E[Step5: Vacuum Hot Pressing 925 C and 4 MPa]
    E --> F[Step6: Diffusion Bonding at Interface T greater than 0.7 Tm]
    F --> G[Step7: Inert Gas Pressurization for SPF]
    G --> H[Step8: Superplastic Blow Forming at 925 C Strain Rate 10 to power minus 4]
    H --> I[Step9: Hollow Blade Cavity Formed]
    I --> J[Step10: Trimming and Final Machining]
    J --> K[Step11: Hollow Lightweight Fan Blade Ready]
```
