# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Module 3]**
*Define the term "investment casting" and list **three** advantages of using it for gas turbine blade manufacturing.*

**Model Answer:**
> **Investment casting** is a precision casting process in which a disposable wax pattern of the desired part is invested with a refractory ceramic shell, the wax is melted out, and molten metal is poured into the resulting ceramic mold. For gas turbine blades, it offers:
> 1. **Dimensional accuracy** of ±0.1 mm, eliminating extensive post-machining of the airfoil.
> 2. **Capability for directional and single-crystal solidification**, which is critical for high-temperature creep life.
> 3. **Excellent surface finish** ($R_a < 1.6 \; \mu m$) suitable for direct application of thermal barrier coatings.
> 4. **Near-net-shape** manufacture of complex internal cooling passages.
> 5. **Microstructural control** through the casting parameters G and V.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 / Module 3]**
*What is a Thermal Barrier Coating (TBC)? State the function of each of its **two main layers**.*

**Model Answer:**
> A **Thermal Barrier Coating (TBC)** is a multi-layered ceramic-based surface protection system applied to hot-section gas turbine components (e.g., HP blades, NGVs, combustor liners) to insulate the underlying metal substrate from extreme gas-path temperatures.
>
> **Top Coat** (e.g., 7–8 wt% Y₂O₃-stabilized ZrO₂, thickness 100–500 µm): Provides the principal **thermal insulation** by virtue of its very low thermal conductivity (~0.8–2.0 W/m·K).
>
> **Bond Coat** (e.g., MCrAlY or Pt-aluminide, thickness ~100 µm): Provides **oxidation resistance** and acts as a mechanical compliance layer, accommodating the thermal expansion mismatch between the ceramic top coat and the metallic substrate, thereby preventing spallation.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> Common reasons students lose marks in Module 3 manufacturing questions:
> 1. **Conflating directional solidification (DS) with single-crystal (SC) casting** — they are *not* synonyms. DS has columnar grains with longitudinal grain boundaries; SC has no grain boundaries at all.
> 2. **Forgetting to specify process parameters** (G, V, Ġ) when asked "how is grain structure controlled?"
> 3. **Mismeaning "investment"** — students sometimes write "financial investment" instead of "investing the wax pattern with a ceramic shell." Examiners deduct **2 marks** instantly for this.
> 4. **Omitting the bond coat** when listing TBC layers — students often jump straight to the ceramic top coat.
> 5. **Confusing SPF with conventional hot forming** — SPF requires fine-grained material and strain-rate sensitivity $m \geq 0.3$.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper / Module 3, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** With a neat flowchart, explain the **investment casting process** for manufacturing a single-crystal gas turbine blade. Discuss the role of the **grain selector**, **chill plate**, and **withdrawal rate (V)** in achieving the desired grain morphology. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A turbine blade is subjected to a heat flux of $q = 1.2 \times 10^6 \; \text{W/m}^2$ through a TBC system consisting of a 200 µm YSZ top coat (k = 1.5 W/m·K) and an 80 µm MCrAlY bond coat (k = 17 W/m·K). Calculate the **temperature drop across the TBC** and the **substrate interface temperature** if the hot gas temperature is 1650 K. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Investment casting process for SC blade:**

1. **Wax pattern** of the blade is injection-molded, complete with gating channels and a **grain selector** (a helical or zigzag channel at the bottom of the gating tree).
2. The wax tree is dipped in **ceramic slurry** and stuccoed for 6–10 layers to build a strong shell.
3. The wax is **dewaxed** in an autoclave, leaving a hollow ceramic shell.
4. The shell is **fired** at ~1000 °C to sinter the refractory.
5. Molten **Ni-based superalloy** is poured under **vacuum** at ~1500 °C.
6. A **chill plate** at the base of the mold extracts heat in a controlled manner, creating a high axial **thermal gradient G (50–200 K/cm)**.
7. The mold is **withdrawn** from the hot zone at a controlled **velocity V (1–5 cm/h for SC)**.
8. The **grain selector** permits only one grain orientation to propagate, so the entire blade becomes a **single crystal** with no grain boundaries.
9. The shell is removed, gates are cut, and the blade undergoes **solution heat treatment + aging**, followed by **coating**.

**Role of process parameters:**

- **Grain selector:** Filters out all but one grain, ensuring single-crystal structure.
- **Chill plate:** Establishes a strong unidirectional thermal gradient, suppressing equiaxed grain formation.
- **Withdrawal rate V:** If V is too high, constitutional supercooling occurs and stray grains nucleate. If V is too low, the process is uneconomical. The condition $G/V \geq G/V_{critical}$ must be maintained.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:**
- $q = 1.2 \times 10^6 \; \text{W/m}^2$
- $t_{top} = 200 \; \mu m = 2.0 \times 10^{-4} \; \text{m}$, $k_{top} = 1.5 \; \text{W/m·K}$
- $t_{bond} = 80 \; \mu m = 8.0 \times 10^{-5} \; \text{m}$, $k_{bond} = 17 \; \text{W/m·K}$
- $T_{gas} = 1650 \; \text{K}$

**Step 1 — Calculate thermal resistances:**

$$R_{top} = \frac{t_{top}}{k_{top}} = \frac{2.0 \times 10^{-4}}{1.5} = 1.333 \times 10^{-4} \; \text{m}^2 \text{K/W}$$

$$R_{bond} = \frac{t_{bond}}{k_{bond}} = \frac{8.0 \times 10^{-5}}{17} = 4.706 \times 10^{-6} \; \text{m}^2 \text{K/W}$$

**Step 2 — Sum of resistances:**

$$R_{total} = 1.333 \times 10^{-4} + 4.706 \times 10^{-6} = 1.380 \times 10^{-4} \; \text{m}^2 \text{K/W}$$

**Step 3 — Temperature drop across TBC:**

$$\Delta T_{TBC} = q \cdot R_{total} = 1.2 \times 10^6 \times 1.380 \times 10^{-4} = 165.6 \; \text{K}$$

**Step 4 — Substrate interface temperature:**

$$T_{substrate} = T_{gas} - \Delta T_{TBC} = 1650 - 165.6 = 1484.4 \; \text{K}$$

**Result:** The TBC provides a temperature drop of **165.6 K**, allowing the substrate to operate at **1484.4 K**, which is well within the creep-capable range of CMSX-4 (~1500 K maximum continuous service).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice)**

**(a)** Explain the **superplastic forming (SPF)** and **diffusion bonding (DB)** process for manufacturing hollow titanium fan blades. Define the **strain-rate sensitivity index (m)** and state why $m \geq 0.3$ is necessary. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the manufacturing process, material choices, and design considerations for a **combustor liner** of a modern annular combustor. Explain why **sheet metal forming + welding + aluminide coating** is the preferred route. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Superplastic Forming and Diffusion Bonding (SPF/DB):**

Titanium alloy Ti-6Al-4V exhibits superplastic behavior when:
- Heated to **~900–950 °C** (above 0.5 × T_m, where T_m ≈ 1660 °C).
- Deformed at a low strain rate of **$10^{-4}$ to $10^{-3} \; \text{s}^{-1}$**.
- The microstructure has an **equiaxed α + β grain structure** with grain size < 10 µm.

**Strain-Rate Sensitivity Index (m):**

By definition, the flow stress $\sigma$ is related to the strain rate $\dot{\varepsilon}$ by:

$$\sigma = K \cdot \dot{\varepsilon}^{m}$$

Differentiating with respect to $\dot{\varepsilon}$:

$$m = \frac{\partial (\ln \sigma)}{\partial (\ln \dot{\varepsilon})}$$

**Why m ≥ 0.3 is required:**
- If $m$ is low (e.g., m < 0.2, as in conventional hot working), any local thinning increases local strain rate, which increases local flow stress, which localizes deformation further — leading to **necking and rupture** at low elongations.
- A high $m$ value means that as deformation accelerates locally, the flow stress rises sharply, distributing strain to other regions. This **resists necking** and produces **uniform elongations of 200%–1000%** without rupture.

**SPF/DB Process Flow:**

1. Ti-6Al-4V sheets are cleaned and a **stop-off compound** (e.g., yttria) is patterned on faying surfaces to prevent bonding in selected regions.
2. Sheets are stacked and placed in a die.
3. **Diffusion bonding** is performed first at 925 °C under vacuum, applying ~4 MPa pressure for 1–2 hours — atomic diffusion across faying surfaces creates metallurgical bonds.
4. Inert gas (argon) is then injected at controlled pressure (1–3 MPa) to **superplastically inflate** the unbonded regions into hollow cavities.
5. After cooling, the hollow blade is trimmed and machined to final contour.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Combustor Liner Manufacturing — Material and Process Choices:**

The combustor liner is the most thermally severe **sheet-metal** component in the engine. It must survive:
- **Direct flame impingement** at 1800–2200 K
- **Cyclic thermal stress** during start-up and shut-down
- **Oxidation and hot corrosion** from combustion products

**Preferred Material:** Nickel-based sheet alloys such as **Haynes 230, Nimonic 263, Inconel 625, or Inconel 718** in the form of thin sheet (typically **0.8–1.5 mm gauge**). These alloys have:
- Excellent oxidation resistance due to **Cr₂O₃ and Al₂O₃ scale formation**.
- Good weldability for can-annular assembly.
- Sufficient ductility for sheet forming operations.

**Manufacturing Process Sequence:**

1. **Sheet Rolling** — the alloy is cold-rolled to the required thin gauge with intermediate anneals.
2. **Sheet Forming** — the sheet is roll-formed, brake-pressed, or hydroformed into the liner's conical or annular shape, including **louver and effusion cooling holes** drilled or chemically milled.
3. **Welding** — segmented liners are joined using **TIG (GTAW) or plasma arc welding**, sometimes **electron beam welding** for high-precision joints.
4. **Solution Anneal** — to relieve forming and welding residual stresses.
5. **Aluminide Coating** — applied by **pack aluminization** or **slurry aluminization**, forming a protective **NiAl or (Ni,Co)Al** intermetallic layer (~30–50 µm) on the surface.
6. **Dimensional and NDT Inspection** — including fluorescent penetrant inspection (FPI) and borescope checks.

**Why this combination is preferred:**

- **Sheet metal forming** enables the thin-wall construction necessary for **transpiration, effusion, or film cooling** effectiveness. A cast liner would be too thick and would not allow the high heat-transfer coefficients required.
- **Welding** permits modular can-annular construction, simplifying field replacement.
- **Aluminide coating** adds a self-healing oxidation-resistant layer, extending liner life by **2–4×** at peak temperatures.
- This combination is **cost-effective** compared to casting or forging and allows **on-wing repair** of localized damage.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Read this 10 minutes before entering the exam hall.**

- **Investment Casting** is the *primary* route for SC/DS turbine blades. Steps: Wax → Shell → Dewax → Fire → Pour → Solidify → Knock out → Inspect.
- The **grain selector + chill plate + controlled withdrawal (V)** are the three pillars of SC casting. The condition $G/V \geq G/V_{critical}$ determines grain morphology.
- **DS** has columnar grains with longitudinal boundaries; **SC** has no grain boundaries at all. Both improve creep life over equiaxed castings.
- **Thermal Barrier Coating (TBC)** is a 4-layer system: substrate → bond coat (MCrAlY) → TGO (Al₂O₃) → top coat (YSZ). It produces a temperature drop of **150–200 K**.
- **Superplastic Forming (SPF)** requires fine-grained material, $T > 0.5 T_m$, and low strain rates ($10^{-4}$ to $10^{-3} \text{ s}^{-1}$). The strain-rate sensitivity index must satisfy $m \geq 0.3$.
- **Diffusion Bonding (DB)** is often combined with SPF (SPF/DB) to produce hollow, lightweight Ti fan blades.
- **Combustor liners** use nickel-based sheet alloys (Haynes 230, Nimonic 263) formed, welded, and aluminide-coated.
- **Compressor and turbine discs** are made by **closed-die forging** of IN718 or Ti-6Al-4V to align the grain flow with the principal stress direction.
- The **cooling rate** in casting is $\dot{G} = G \cdot V$, and **dendrite arm spacing** follows $\lambda_1 = A \cdot \dot{G}^{-n}$.
- The **TBC temperature drop** is calculated as $\Delta T_{TBC} = q \cdot \sum (t_i / k_i)$, treating each layer as a thermal resistance in series.
- Modern gas turbine engines integrate **at least 5–6 distinct manufacturing technologies** (casting, forging, SPF/DB, welding, machining, EB-PVD, APS).
- The choice of manufacturing process is governed by the **tri-axis: stress–temperature–corrosion**, not just geometry.
- A **grain boundary transverse to the centrifugal stress axis** is the single most damaging defect in a high-pressure turbine blade — which is why single-crystal casting exists.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Blade Cooling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET741 - GAS TURBINE AND JET PROPULSION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Blade Cooling

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 3 — Blade Cooling

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Blade Cooling** is the controlled process of extracting a small fraction of compressed air from the gas-turbine compressor and routing it through internal passages and/or discharging it over the external surface of the high-pressure turbine blades and nozzle guide vanes so that the **metal temperature of the blade is maintained well below the turbine inlet (gas) temperature**.

Mathematically, the performance of any cooling scheme is captured by the **Cooling Effectiveness ($\theta$)**:

$$\theta = \frac{T_{g} - T_{b}}{T_{g} - T_{c}}$$

where $T_{g}$ is the hot-gas temperature, $T_{b}$ is the blade metal temperature, and $T_{c}$ is the coolant (bleed air) temperature.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — PEMET741 (Module 3):**
> *"Need for blade cooling — methods of blade cooling — effect on turbine work output — cooling effectiveness — effective temperature drop."*
> The above line is the **exact phrasing** used in the KTU 2024 scheme syllabus PDF for this module. Every answer in the ESE must echo this vocabulary.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Why Blade Cooling?

Imagine a person walking through a furnace wearing a **heat-resistant suit that has cold water circulating through tubes sewn into the fabric** (internal convection), **plus a thin insulating layer of cool air sprayed over the outer surface** (film cooling). The furnace gas may be at **1600 °C**, but the suit's inner lining never exceeds **900 °C** because the circulating water absorbs the heat before it reaches the skin.

A gas-turbine blade experiences the same problem — and the same solution:

- The **"furnace"** is the hot gas at the turbine inlet ($T_3 \approx 1200$–$1600$ K).
- The **"heat-resistant suit"** is the blade alloy (typically Ni-based super-alloys with melting points near **1573 K / 1300 °C**).
- The **"cool water"** is bleed air from the high-pressure compressor (around 700–800 K).
- The **"insulating spray"** is the film of cool air ejected over the blade surface.

### 1.3 Why is Cooling Needed?

> [!NOTE]
> The **isoentropic efficiency** and **specific work output** of a gas-turbine cycle both rise monotonically with $T_3$. However, the survival of the blade metallurgically requires $T_b < 0.8\, T_{melt}$. This fundamental conflict — *thermodynamic desire vs metallurgical limit* — is precisely what blade cooling resolves.

| Parameter | Typical Magnitude (Modern Aero-derivative GT) |
|---|---|
| Turbine inlet gas temperature $T_3$ | **1500 K – 1850 K** |
| Blade alloy incipient melting point | **~1573 K** |
| Allowable blade metal temperature $T_b$ | **~1250 K – 1350 K** |
| Compressor discharge (bleed) temperature $T_c$ | **~700 K – 850 K** |

Clearly, without cooling the blades would literally **melt in seconds**.

### 1.4 Categorisation of Cooling Techniques

Cooling techniques are classified along **two primary axes**:

1. **Internal Cooling** — heat removal occurs *inside* the blade via convection from the metal to the coolant.
2. **External Cooling (Film Cooling)** — coolant is ejected *through* the blade wall to form an insulating film over the outer surface.
3. **Surface Treatment** — Thermal Barrier Coatings (TBC) on top of internal + external schemes.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Internal (Convective) Cooling

Heat from the hot gas side flows through the blade wall to the coolant flowing inside passages. The rate is governed by Newton's law of cooling:

$$Q_{int} = h_i \, A_i \, (T_{b,i} - T_{c})$$

where $h_i$ is the **internal convective heat-transfer coefficient** and $A_i$ is the **internal wetted area**.

Common internal-cooling geometries (in order of increasing effectiveness):

| # | Geometry | Mechanism | Relative $h_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Plain round passage** | Forced convection | Baseline (1×) |
| 2 | **Serpentine / zig-zag** | Flow re-direction + secondary flows | ~1.5× – 2× |
| 3 | **Ribbed / turbulated** | Boundary-layer tripping | ~2× – 3× |
| 4 | **Impingement cooling** | Jet impingement on inner wall | ~3× – 5× |
| 5 | **Pin-fin array** | Wake-promoted convection + fin effect | ~4× – 6× |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Mnemonic:** *"Plain → Serpentine → Ribbed → Impinge → Pin-fin"* — remember the order as **P-S-R-I-P** for escalating effectiveness.

### 2.2 External (Film) Cooling

Coolant is discharged through discrete shaped holes (typically **fan-shaped, laid-back, or compound-angle**) on the suction and pressure surfaces of the blade, producing a cool protective blanket over the metal.

The **film-cooling effectiveness** is defined as:

$$\eta_f = \frac{T_{aw} - T_{g}}{T_{c} - T_{g}}$$

where $T_{aw}$ is the **adiabatic wall temperature** (i.e. the wall temperature the surface would attain if the wall were perfectly insulated and only film cooling acted).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Overall blade-cooling effectiveness** in a combined scheme (internal + film) is **higher** than either acting alone because film cooling reduces the gas-side driving temperature ($T_{aw} < T_g$), thereby reducing the heat flux that internal convection must absorb.

### 2.3 Thermal Barrier Coating (TBC)

A ceramic layer (typically 8 % Yttria-stabilised Zirconia, **8YSZ**, $\approx 200$–$500\ \mu m$ thick) is plasma-sprayed or EB-PVD deposited on the super-alloy substrate.

$$\Delta T_{TBC} = \frac{Q\,t_{TBC}}{k_{TBC}}$$

with $k_{TBC} \approx 2.0\ \mathrm{W/m \cdot K}$ — about **15× lower** than the super-alloy substrate ($k \approx 25\ \mathrm{W/m \cdot K}$). The TBC therefore drops the metal temperature by **100 K – 200 K** for the same heat flux, dramatically extending blade life.

### 2.4 Heat Balance on a Cooled Blade

The blade wall is the only path through which heat can flow (assuming negligible radiation to surroundings). At steady state:

$$\text{Heat lost by gas} = \text{Heat gained by coolant}$$

$$\boxed{\ \dot{m}_g \, c_{pg} \, (T_3 - T_b) \;=\; \dot{m}_c \, c_{pc} \, (T_b - T_c)\ }$$

### 2.5 Effect on Turbine Work Output

If the coolant were *not* extracted, the entire main flow would expand from $T_3$ to $T_4$, yielding:

$$W_{uncooled} = \dot{m}_g \, c_{pg} \, (T_3 - T_4)$$

With cooling, a portion of the main-flow enthalpy is diverted to raise the coolant from $T_c$ to $T_b$, and that enthalpy is **no longer available for expansion work**. The effective work per unit mass of main flow is:

$$\boxed{\ w_{eff} \;=\; c_{pg} \, (T_3 - T_4) \;-\; \alpha \, c_{pc} \, (T_b - T_c)\ }$$

where the **cooling-air fraction** is $\alpha = \dot{m}_c / \dot{m}_g$.

The **relative work loss** is:

$$\boxed{\ \frac{\Delta W}{W_{uncooled}} \;=\; \alpha \, \frac{c_{pc}}{c_{pg}} \, \frac{(T_b - T_c)}{(T_3 - T_4)}\ }$$

### 2.6 Effective Inlet Temperature

An equivalent way to capture the penalty is to define an **effective (or "thermodynamic") turbine inlet temperature** that would give the same work output in an uncooled cycle:

$$T_{3,\,eff} \;=\; T_3 \;-\; \alpha \, \frac{c_{pc}}{c_{pg}} \, (T_b - T_c)$$

### 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Key Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Cooling effectiveness | $\theta = (T_g - T_b)/(T_g - T_c)$ | $T_g, T_b, T_c$ in **K** |
| 2 | Film-cooling effectiveness | $\eta_f = (T_{aw} - T_g)/(T_c - T_g)$ | $T_{aw}, T_g, T_c$ in **K** |
| 3 | Internal convective heat transfer | $Q_i = h_i A_i (T_{b,i} - T_c)$ | $h_i$ in **W/m²·K**, $A_i$ in **m²** |
| 4 | Blade heat balance | $\dot{m}_g c_{pg} (T_3 - T_b) = \dot{m}_c c_{pc} (T_b - T_c)$ | $\dot{m}$ in **kg/s**, $c_p$ in **kJ/kg·K** |
| 5 | Blade temperature (via $\theta$) | $T_b = T_3 - \theta (T_3 - T_c)$ | K |
| 6 | Cooling-air fraction | $\alpha = \dot{m}_c / \dot{m}_g$ | dimensionless |
| 7 | Effective work (per kg gas) | $w_{eff} = c_{pg}(T_3 - T_4) - \alpha c_{pc}(T_b - T_c)$ | **kJ/kg** |
| 8 | Relative work loss | $\Delta W / W = \alpha (c_{pc}/c_{pg}) (T_b - T_c)/(T_3 - T_4)$ | dimensionless |
| 9 | Effective inlet temperature | $T_{3,eff} = T_3 - \alpha (c_{pc}/c_{pg}) (T_b - T_c)$ | K |
| 10 | TBC temperature drop | $\Delta T_{TBC} = Q\,t_{TBC} / k_{TBC}$ | K; $t$ in **m**, $k$ in **W/m·K** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In the KTU 2024 scheme ESE, examiners expect students to **explicitly state the heat balance before plugging numerical values**. Marks are routinely awarded for the equation itself (typically 1–2 marks in a 7-mark sub-question) independent of the arithmetic.

### 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aero-derivative engines** (e.g., GE9X, Rolls-Royce Trent XWB) use all three schemes — impingement + ribbed internal + shaped-hole film + TBC — to survive $T_3$ above **1850 K** while keeping $T_b$ below **1300 K**.
- **Industrial heavy-duty GTs** (e.g., GE 7HA, Siemens HL-class) for combined-cycle power plants operate at $T_3 \approx 1700$ K with film + convection cooling to achieve combined-cycle efficiencies above **64 %** (LHV basis).
- **Afterburners** of military engines use transpiration cooling to survive inlet temperatures exceeding **2000 K**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Blade Heat-Balance Equation

**Step 1 — Set up the control volume.**
Enclose the blade wall in a control surface. Two streams exchange heat through this surface: the hot gas on one side and the cooling air on the other.

**Step 2 — Apply the steady-flow energy equation to each stream (neglecting KE and PE).**

For the hot gas stream:

$$q_g = c_{pg} \, (T_3 - T_b)$$

For the coolant stream:

$$q_c = c_{pc} \, (T_b - T_c)$$

**Step 3 — Apply steady-state energy conservation on the blade wall (no heat accumulation, no work, adiabatic to surroundings).**

$$\dot{m}_g \, q_g \;=\; \dot{m}_c \, q_c$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the expressions for $q_g$ and $q_c$.**

$$\dot{m}_g \, c_{pg} \, (T_3 - T_b) \;=\; \dot{m}_c \, c_{pc} \, (T_b - T_c)$$

This is the **fundamental heat balance equation** that students must memorise.

**Step 5 — Solve for blade temperature $T_b$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{m}_g \, c_{pg} \, T_3 - \dot{m}_g \, c_{pg} \, T_b &= \dot{m}_c \, c_{pc} \, T_b - \dot{m}_c \, c_{pc} \, T_c \\[4pt]
\dot{m}_g \, c_{pg} \, T_3 + \dot{m}_c \, c_{pc} \, T_c &= T_b \, (\dot{m}_g \, c_{pg} + \dot{m}_c \, c_{pc}) \\[4pt]
T_b &= \frac{\dot{m}_g \, c_{pg} \, T_3 + \dot{m}_c \, c_{pc} \, T_c}{\dot{m}_g \, c_{pg} + \dot{m}_c \, c_{pc}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Introduce the cooling-effectiveness form.**

Divide numerator and denominator by $\dot{m}_g c_{pg}$ and let $\alpha = \dot{m}_c / \dot{m}_g$, $k = c_{pc}/c_{pg}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_b &= \frac{T_3 + \alpha k \, T_c}{1 + \alpha k} \\[4pt]
T_3 - T_b &= T_3 - \frac{T_3 + \alpha k T_c}{1 + \alpha k} = \frac{\alpha k (T_3 - T_c)}{1 + \alpha k}
\end{aligned}
$$

Substituting into the definition $\theta = (T_3 - T_b) / (T_3 - T_c)$:

$$
\boxed{\ \theta \;=\; \frac{\alpha k}{1 + \alpha k}\ }
$$

For $k \approx 1$ (typical for air–air cooling) and small $\alpha$ ($\le 0.1$), $\theta \approx \alpha$.

### 3.2 Derivation of Effective Work Output

**Step 1 — State the uncooled (baseline) work.**
Per kilogram of main stream:

$$w_{uncooled} = c_{pg} \, (T_3 - T_4)$$

**Step 2 — Recognise the energy diverted to the coolant.**
The coolant enters the blade at $T_c$ and leaves at $T_b$. The enthalpy it absorbs per unit mass of main flow is:

$$\Delta h_{lost} = \alpha \, c_{pc} \, (T_b - T_c)$$

This energy is supplied *by* the main stream through the blade wall — so the main stream's available expansion enthalpy is reduced by exactly $\Delta h_{lost}$.

**Step 3 — Write the effective work.**

$$
\boxed{\ w_{eff} \;=\; c_{pg} \, (T_3 - T_4) \;-\; \alpha \, c_{pc} \, (T_b - T_c)\ }
$$

**Step 4 — Express the relative work loss.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{w_{uncooled} - w_{eff}}{w_{uncooled}} &= \frac{\alpha \, c_{pc} \, (T_b - T_c)}{c_{pg} \, (T_3 - T_4)} \\[4pt]
\frac{\Delta W}{W} &= \alpha \, \frac{c_{pc}}{c_{pg}} \, \frac{(T_b - T_c)}{(T_3 - T_4)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.3 Worked Numerical (KTU-Exam Pattern)

**Problem.** In a gas-turbine stage, air enters the turbine at $T_3 = 1200$ K and leaves at $T_4 = 800$ K. The blade is cooled by air bled from the compressor at $T_c = 500$ K, with cooling effectiveness $\theta = 0.6$ and cooling-air fraction $\alpha = 0.05$. Take $c_{pg} = c_{pc} = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K.

Find: **(i)** Blade temperature $T_b$, **(ii)** Effective work output per kg of main stream, **(iii)** Percentage reduction in work.

#### Solution

**Step 1 — Compute blade temperature using $\theta$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\theta &= \frac{T_3 - T_b}{T_3 - T_c} \\[4pt]
T_b &= T_3 - \theta \, (T_3 - T_c) \\[4pt]
T_b &= 1200 - 0.6 \times (1200 - 500) \\[4pt]
T_b &= 1200 - 0.6 \times 700 \\[4pt]
T_b &= 1200 - 420 = \mathbf{780\ K}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute uncooled work.**

$$
w_{uncooled} = c_{pg} \, (T_3 - T_4) = 1.005 \times (1200 - 800) = 1.005 \times 400 = \mathbf{402.0\ kJ/kg}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the work penalty due to cooling.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta w &= \alpha \, c_{pc} \, (T_b - T_c) \\[4pt]
\Delta w &= 0.05 \times 1.005 \times (780 - 500) \\[4pt]
\Delta w &= 0.05 \times 1.005 \times 280 \\[4pt]
\Delta w &= \mathbf{14.07\ kJ/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute effective work output.**

$$
w_{eff} = 402.0 - 14.07 = \mathbf{387.93\ kJ/kg}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute percentage reduction in work.**

$$
\frac{\Delta w}{w_{uncooled}} \times 100 = \frac{14.07}{402.0} \times 100 = \mathbf{3.50\ \%}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> A blade-cooling fraction of only **5 %** by mass costs roughly **3.5 %** of the turbine's specific work — a small price to pay for surviving $T_3 > 1200$ K. This is exactly the design trade-off that justifies every modern high-$T_3$ engine.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Cooling Method Selection

| Application / Stage | Dominant Cooling Method | Typical $T_3$ Range | Cooling Fraction $\alpha$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| First-stage NGV (combustor liner exit) | Film + Impingement + TBC | 1500 – 1850 K | 8 % – 12 % |
| First-stage Rotor Blade | Convection (ribbed) + Film | 1400 – 1700 K | 5 % – 8 % |
| Second-stage NGV | Convection + partial film | 1200 – 1400 K | 3 % – 5 % |
| Third-stage onward | Plain convection (sometimes none) | < 1200 K | 0 % – 2 % |
| Afterburner / RATO duct | Transpiration (porous wall) | > 2000 K | 15 % – 25 % |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture — Heat-Flow Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    HOTAIR["HOT GAS STREAM T3 EQUALS 1200 K"] --> BLADEWALL["BLADE WALL TB EQUALS 780 K"]
    COOLANT["COOLANT FROM COMPRESSOR TC EQUALS 500 K"] --> BLADEWALL
    BLADEWALL --> MAINOUT["MAIN STREAM EXITS AT T4 EQUALS 800 K"]
    BLADEWALL --> COOLOUT["COOLANT EXITS AT TB EQUALS 780 K"]
    COOLOUT -.->|MIXES DOWNSTREAM| MAINOUT
    HOTAIR -.->|HEAT Q GIVEN UP| BLADEWALL
    BLADEWALL -.->|HEAT Q ABSORBED| COOLANT
```

### 4.2 Hierarchical Classification — Blade Cooling Methods

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ROOT["BLADE COOLING METHODS"]
    ROOT --> INT["INTERNAL CONVECTION COOLING"]
    ROOT --> EXT["EXTERNAL FILM COOLING"]
    ROOT --> SURF["SURFACE TREATMENT"]
    INT --> I1["PLAIN PASSAGE BASELINE"]
    INT --> I2["SERPENTINE ZIG ZAG PASSAGE"]
    INT --> I3["RIBBED TURBULATED PASSAGE"]
    INT --> I4["IMPINGEMENT JET COOLING"]
    INT --> I5["PIN FIN ARRAY COOLING"]
    EXT --> E1["SHAPED HOLE FAN LAID BACK"]
    EXT --> E2["COMPOUND ANGLE INJECTION"]
    EXT --> E3["TRANSPIRATION POROUS WALL"]
    SURF --> S1["THERMAL BARRIER COATING 8YSZ"]
    SURF --> S2["OXIDE SCALE ALUMINIDE"]
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Coolant Path in a Modern Engine

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    COMP["HPC HIGH PRESSURE COMPRESSOR"] -->|BLEED STAGE 9 OR 13| MANI["MANIFOLD PLENUM"]
    MANI --> SHAFT["INTERNAL ROTOR SHAFT COOLANT LINE"]
    SHAFT --> ROOT["BLADE ROOT FEED"]
    ROOT --> RAD["RADIAL INTERNAL PASSAGES"]
    RAD --> TIP["TIP EJECTION OR FILM PORTS"]
    TIP --> GAS["HOT GAS PATH"]
    MANI -->|SECONDARY| NGV["NGV IMPINGEMENT BAFFLE"]
    NGV --> NGVOUT["NGV FILM HOLES"]
    NGVOUT --> GAS
```

### 4.4 Schematic of Heat-Transfer Resistance Network

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    TG["Tg HOT GAS"] -->|RESISTANCE R GAS EQUALS 1 BY H GAS A| TS["Ts EXTERNAL SURFACE"]
    TS -->|RESISTANCE R WALL EQUALS T BY K A| TI["Ti INTERNAL SURFACE"]
    TI -->|RESISTANCE R COOL EQUALS 1 BY H COOL A| TC["Tc COOLANT"]
    TG -.->|"Q DOT OVERALL DELTA T"| TC
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the resistance network:** the overall heat flux from gas to coolant is $Q = \Delta T_{overall} / R_{total}$, where $R_{total} = R_{gas} + R_{wall} + R_{cool}$. Film cooling effectively **increases $R_{gas}$** by interposing a cooler gas layer; impingement cooling effectively **decreases $R_{cool}$** by raising $h_{cool}$.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 marks each)

**Q1. Define cooling effectiveness of a turbine blade. State its typical range for modern high-pressure turbine stages.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**
Cooling effectiveness is the ratio of the temperature drop achieved across the blade to the maximum possible temperature drop (i.e. the drop if the coolant reached the gas temperature):

$$\theta = \frac{T_g - T_b}{T_g - T_c}$$

For modern high-pressure turbine first stages with combined convection + film + TBC, $\theta$ typically lies in the range **0.5 – 0.8** (i.e. the blade is held at 50 % – 80 % of the maximum theoretical temperature reduction).

**Q2. Differentiate between internal convection cooling and external film cooling.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Internal Convection Cooling | External Film Cooling |
|---|---|---|
| Heat flow direction | Hot gas → blade wall → coolant | Coolant → ejected over blade → shields from hot gas |
| Where coolant flows | Inside blade passages | Outside, over blade surface |
| Driving mechanism | Forced-convection heat transfer ($h_i$) | Mass injection forming insulating blanket ($\eta_f$) |
| Primary benefit | Lowers bulk blade temperature | Lowers surface (gas-side) temperature |
| Typical effectiveness metric | $h_i A_i (T_b - T_c)$ | $\eta_f = (T_{aw} - T_g)/(T_c - T_g)$ |
| Common geometry | Serpentine / ribbed / impingement | Shaped holes, laid-back fans, slots |

---

### Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 marks each; Internal Choice)

---

#### Question A (14 marks) — Cooling Methods + Numerical

**(a)** With neat sketches, explain the various methods of blade cooling employed in modern gas turbines. Briefly mention Thermal Barrier Coating (TBC). **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (key points expected by examiner):**

1. **Need for cooling** — $T_3$ exceeds blade-alloy melting point; cooling permits higher $T_3$ → higher efficiency & specific power.
2. **Internal Convection Cooling** — Plain, serpentine, ribbed, impingement, pin-fin. Sketch a cross-section showing internal passages with coolant arrows.
3. **External Film Cooling** — Coolant ejected through shaped holes on pressure/suction surfaces; forms protective blanket. Sketch showing fan-shaped hole and film layer.
4. **Transpiration Cooling** — Porous wall; coolant seeps through uniformly.
5. **Thermal Barrier Coating** — 8 % Y₂O₃-stabilised ZrO₂ ceramic layer (~200–500 µm); low $k$ (~2 W/m·K); drops metal temperature by 100 K – 200 K.
6. **Combined approach** — Modern engines use *all* schemes simultaneously.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** A common 1-mark loss occurs when students draw the coolant arrows in the *wrong direction* (showing coolant flowing from outside to inside). The correct direction is **outside → inside → exit through film holes or trailing edge**. Always label $T_3, T_b, T_c$ on the sketch — examiners award 1 mark for a labelled diagram.

**(b)** In a cooled gas-turbine stage, hot gas enters at $T_3 = 1400$ K, exits at $T_4 = 900$ K. Compressor bleed at $T_c = 600$ K is used for cooling. Cooling effectiveness is $\theta = 0.55$ and cooling fraction $\alpha = 0.06$. $c_{pg} = c_{pc} = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K. Determine:

(i) Blade temperature $T_b$
(ii) Effective work per kg of main flow
(iii) Percentage reduction in work compared to uncooled case **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Blade temperature:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_b &= T_3 - \theta (T_3 - T_c) \\
&= 1400 - 0.55 \times (1400 - 600) \\
&= 1400 - 0.55 \times 800 \\
&= 1400 - 440 = \mathbf{960\ K}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Effective work:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
w_{uncooled} &= c_{pg}(T_3 - T_4) = 1.005 \times (1400 - 900) = 502.5\ \text{kJ/kg} \\[4pt]
\Delta w &= \alpha \, c_{pc} \, (T_b - T_c) = 0.06 \times 1.005 \times (960 - 600) \\[4pt]
&= 0.06 \times 1.005 \times 360 = 21.708\ \text{kJ/kg} \\[4pt]
w_{eff} &= 502.5 - 21.708 = \mathbf{480.79\ kJ/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Percentage reduction:**

$$
\frac{\Delta w}{w_{uncooled}} \times 100 = \frac{21.708}{502.5} \times 100 = \mathbf{4.32\ \%}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students frequently compute the effective work as $c_{pg}(T_3 - T_b)$ instead of $c_{pg}(T_3 - T_4) - \alpha c_{pc}(T_b - T_c)$. The first expression **forgets the cooling penalty** and inflates the answer by ~10 %. Always subtract the coolant's enthalpy rise.

---

#### Question B (14 marks) — Heat-Balance Derivation + Comparison

**(a)** Derive the heat-balance equation for a cooled gas-turbine blade. From this, obtain an expression for the **cooling effectiveness** in terms of the cooling-air fraction $\alpha$ and specific-heat ratio $k = c_{pc}/c_{pg}$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Control volume on the blade wall.**

The blade wall is the only path for heat transfer (assumed adiabatic to surroundings).

**Step 2 — Energy lost by hot gas per kg of gas:**

$$q_g = c_{pg}(T_3 - T_b)$$

**Step 3 — Energy gained by coolant per kg of coolant:**

$$q_c = c_{pc}(T_b - T_c)$$

**Step 4 — Energy balance (no accumulation):**

$$\dot{m}_g \, c_{pg}(T_3 - T_b) = \dot{m}_c \, c_{pc}(T_b - T_c)$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $T_b$:**

$$T_b = \frac{\dot{m}_g c_{pg} T_3 + \dot{m}_c c_{pc} T_c}{\dot{m}_g c_{pg} + \dot{m}_c c_{pc}}$$

**Step 6 — Introduce $\alpha$ and $k$:**

Dividing through by $\dot{m}_g c_{pg}$:

$$T_b = \frac{T_3 + \alpha k T_c}{1 + \alpha k}$$

**Step 7 — Apply the definition of cooling effectiveness:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\theta &= \frac{T_3 - T_b}{T_3 - T_c} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{T_3 - \dfrac{T_3 + \alpha k T_c}{1 + \alpha k}}{T_3 - T_c} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{\alpha k (T_3 - T_c)}{(1 + \alpha k)(T_3 - T_c)} \\[4pt]
\boxed{\theta &= \frac{\alpha k}{1 + \alpha k}}
\end{aligned}
$$

For typical air-cooling ($k \approx 1$) with $\alpha \le 0.1$, the denominator is close to unity, so $\theta \approx \alpha$.

**(b)** Compare and contrast **convective** cooling, **film** cooling, and **transpiration** cooling. In which high-temperature regions of a turbine is each technique most appropriate? **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Convective Cooling | Film Cooling | Transpiration Cooling |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Mechanism** | Heat carried away by coolant flowing inside passages | Coolant jet forms protective layer over outer surface | Coolant seeps through porous wall uniformly |
| **Geometry** | Internal channels (serpentine, ribbed, impingement) | Discrete shaped holes on blade skin | Sintered porous blade wall |
| **Strength** | Reliable, easy to manufacture; lowers bulk metal T | Very high local effectiveness; protects hot spots | Uniform coverage, no discrete holes, no lift-off problems |
| **Weakness** | Bulk T still high near hot surfaces | Discrete holes can lift-off at high momentum ratio $I$ | Manufacturing difficulty; risk of pore blockage |
| **Effectiveness** | $\theta \approx 0.3$ – $0.6$ | $\eta_f \approx 0.4$ – $0.8$ (near holes, decreases downstream) | $\eta_f \approx 0.6$ – $0.9$ uniform |
| **Best suited for** | Bulk of rotor blades; lower stages | First-stage NGV, leading edge, suction-side hot spots | Rocket nozzles, afterburners, ultra-high-T sections |
| **Coolant usage** | 3 % – 8 % | 4 % – 10 % | 10 % – 25 % |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** A common error in the comparison table is to write *"film cooling works inside the blade"* — it does not. Film cooling is **external**. Examiners deduct **2 marks** for confusing this basic classification.

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Recurring Pitfalls on Blade Cooling

> [!WARNING]
> 1. **Missing heat balance line** in numerical answers → loss of **2 marks** out of 7.
> 2. **Wrong sign** on the cooling-penalty term (added instead of subtracted) → loss of **2 marks**.
> 3. **Forgetting units** in $c_p$ (must be **kJ/kg·K**, not J/kg·K) → loss of **1 mark** for dimensional inconsistency.
> 4. **Direction of coolant flow** mislabelled on diagrams → loss of **1 mark**.
> 5. **Mixing film vs convective cooling** definitions → loss of **2 marks** in descriptive questions.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Blade cooling is mandatory** in any modern GT with $T_3 > 1100$ K; metallurgical survival requires $T_b < T_{melt}$ — typically $< 0.85\,T_{melt}$.
- **Two primary schemes:** *Internal Convection* (heat absorbed inside passages) and *External Film* (coolant blanket on outer surface).
- **Heat balance equation** (memorise verbatim): $\dot{m}_g c_{pg}(T_3 - T_b) = \dot{m}_c c_{pc}(T_b - T_c)$.
- **Cooling effectiveness**: $\theta = (T_3 - T_b)/(T_3 - T_c)$; typical values **0.5 – 0.8** for advanced engines.
- **Film-cooling effectiveness**: $\eta_f = (T_{aw} - T_g)/(T_c - T_g)$ — distinct from overall $\theta$.
- **Work penalty**: $w_{eff} = c_{pg}(T_3 - T_4) - \alpha c_{pc}(T_b - T_c)$; penalty ≈ 3 % – 6 % per 5 % cooling fraction.
- **Effective inlet temperature**: $T_{3,eff} = T_3 - \alpha(c_{pc}/c_{pg})(T_b - T_c)$.
- **Thermal Barrier Coating (TBC)** — 8YSZ ceramic (~200–500 µm, $k \approx 2$ W/m·K) provides additional **100 K – 200 K** metal-temperature drop.
- **Transpiration cooling** uses a porous wall for uniform coverage but at high coolant cost (10 % – 25 %).
- **Cooling fraction $\alpha$**: typically **3 % – 12 %** of main-stream mass flow, depending on stage and engine type.
- **Air-cooling assumption** ($c_{pg} \approx c_{pc}$) is valid unless explicitly stated otherwise in the KTU problem.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Trends and Future Development
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET741 - GAS TURBINE AND JET PROPULSION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Trends and Future Development

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition
In the context of KTU PEMET741 (Gas Turbine and Jet Propulsion), **"Trends and Future Development"** refers to the systematic study of evolutionary and revolutionary advancements in gas turbine and air-breathing jet propulsion technology. It encompasses the design philosophy, material science breakthroughs, thermodynamic cycle modifications, digital integration, and emerging propulsion architectures that aim to improve key performance metrics such as **Specific Thrust ($F_{sp}$)**, **Thrust Specific Fuel Consumption (TSFC)**, **Overall Pressure Ratio (OPR)**, **Turbine Inlet Temperature (TIT)**, **Propulsive Efficiency ($\eta_p$)**, and **Thermal Efficiency ($\eta_{th}$)**.

The field is broadly classified into three horizons:
- **Near-Term (2024–2035):** Incremental improvements in materials, aerodynamics, and digitalization.
- **Mid-Term (2035–2050):** Hybrid-electric and hydrogen-fueled architectures.
- **Far-Term (2050+):** Hypersonic air-breathing propulsion, fusion-aided cycles, and sustainable closed-loop systems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PEMET741 Module 3):**
> This topic integrates the previously studied concepts of **Brayton cycle, Intercooling-Reheating-Regeneration (ICRH), and Jet propulsion theory** with the engineering reality of modern aerospace propulsion, focusing on **why** and **how** these cycles are being pushed to their physical and thermodynamic limits.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine a **Formula 1 race car engine**. Each year, engineers push the same fundamental four-stroke Otto cycle to extract more power by improving fuel injection, reducing friction, using lighter materials, and harvesting energy from turbochargers. The "core idea" — burning fuel to push a piston — has not changed, but the *boundaries* keep expanding.

Gas turbine evolution is identical in spirit:
- The core thermodynamic principle (Brayton cycle) is **unchanged**.
- Engineers keep stretching the **envelope** of three parameters simultaneously: **Temperature**, **Pressure**, and **Speed**.
- Each generation squeezes out a few extra percent of efficiency, just like F1 cars shaving hundredths of a second off lap times.

**The "Three Big Levers" of Gas Turbine Development:**

$$ \text{Performance} \propto f(\underbrace{T_{\text{TIT}}}_{\text{Thermal}}, \underbrace{r_p}_{\text{Thermodynamic}}, \underbrace{BPR}_{\text{Propulsive}}) $$

where $T_{\text{TIT}}$ is the **Turbine Inlet Temperature**, $r_p$ is the **Overall Pressure Ratio**, and $BPR$ is the **Bypass Ratio**.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics
The following bolded metrics define the boundary of every modern development:
- **TIT (TIT / $T_3$ or $T_4$):** Modern military engines exceed **2000 K**; civil engines operate around **1850 K** behind the combustor.
- **OPR (Overall Pressure Ratio):** Modern high-bypass turbofans operate at **OPR = 40:1 to 60:1** (e.g., GEnx, GTF).
- **Bypass Ratio (BPR):** Modern ultra-high-bypass engines target **BPR = 12:1 to 15:1** (e.g., Rolls-Royce UltraFan).
- **TSFC:** Industry benchmark for modern turbofans: **~10–12 g/kN·s** at cruise.
- **Shaft Efficiency ($\eta_{sh}$):** Top-tier industrial gas turbines exceed **$\eta_{sh} = 44\%$** in simple cycle, and **$> 64\%$** in combined cycle (CCGT).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway for KTU Students:**
> The *future* of gas turbines is not a single "miracle technology" — it is the **simultaneous optimization** of materials, cycle architecture, and digital systems, all driven by the global mandate of **decarbonization** and **net-zero aviation by 2050**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Pillar I — Advanced Materials and Thermal Management

The single greatest constraint on gas turbine performance is the **maximum allowable turbine inlet temperature** $T_3$, limited by **creep life, oxidation, and thermal fatigue** of turbine blades.

**Evolution Timeline of Blade Materials:**

| Generation | Material | Approx. $T_{\text{TIT}}$ Capability | Era |
|------------|----------|------------------------------------|-----|
| 1st Gen | Wrought Nickel Alloys (e.g., Inconel 100) | 1050 K | 1940s–1960s |
| 2nd Gen | Directionally Solidified (DS) Alloys | 1250 K | 1970s |
| 3rd Gen | Single Crystal (SC) Superalloys (CMSX-4, PWA 1484) | 1450 K | 1980s–1990s |
| 4th Gen | SC Superalloys + TBCs + Advanced Cooling | 1750 K | 2000s |
| 5th Gen (Current) | CMC (Ceramic Matrix Composites) + TBCs | 1850–2000 K | 2010s–Present |
| 6th Gen (Future) | Refractory High-Entropy Alloys (RHEAs) / Ultra-HTC | > 2000 K | 2030s+ |

**Why this matters (The "Why"):**
Higher $T_3$ → Higher $\eta_{th}$ → Lower fuel burn. From the Brayton cycle expression:

$$ \eta_{\text{th, ideal}} = 1 - \left(\frac{1}{r_p}\right)^{\frac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma}} $$

This shows that **raising $T_3$ (and thereby $r_p$) is the most effective lever** because it pushes the cycle's mean effective temperature closer to $T_3$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why CMCs are a Disruptive Technology:**
> Ceramic Matrix Composites (e.g., **SiC/SiC**) operate at temperatures **~200 K higher** than single-crystal nickel blades, are **1/3 the density** of metal blades, and require **no external film cooling air** (saving up to **20% of compressor bleed air**, which directly translates to propulsive efficiency gain).

### 2.2 Pillar II — Advanced Cycle Architectures

Modern trend: **moving beyond the simple Brayton cycle** by integrating energy recovery, and varying the cycle dynamically.

#### 2.2.1 Regenerative + Reheat + Intercooled (RRI) Cycle
This combined cycle, though heavy, achieves theoretical efficiencies approaching the **Carnot limit**. Thermal efficiency:

$$ \eta_{\text{th, ideal RRI}} = 1 - \left(\frac{T_1}{T_3}\right) \left(\frac{p_1}{p_2}\right)^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} $$

This is used in **stationary power generation** (e.g., GE 7HA.03, Siemens HL-class).

#### 2.2.2 Variable Cycle Engines (VCE)
VCEs allow a single engine to operate in **multiple modes** by adjusting:
- Geometry of inlet (variable ramps)
- Compressor bypass split
- Afterburner/nozzle area

Examples: **F119 (F-22), F135 (F-35), GE Adaptive Engine Technology Development (AETD)**. They offer both **supersonic dash capability** and **subsonic cruise efficiency**.

#### 2.2.3 Intercooled Recuperated (ICR) Aero-Engines
- The **Sabre engine** (Reaction Engines Ltd.) is a precooled, hydrogen-fueled, combined-cycle engine intended for **Skylon spaceplane**.
- Precooling reduces inlet temperature from **1000 K → 250 K** in milliseconds using helium heat exchangers.

### 2.3 Pillar III — Sustainable Aviation and Future Fuels

The aviation industry targets **net-zero CO₂ by 2050** (IATA, ICAO CORSIA). Trends:

- **Sustainable Aviation Fuels (SAF):** Synthetic paraffinic kerosene from HEFA, FT-SPK, ATJ pathways. Drop-in fuels with up to **80% lifecycle CO₂ reduction**.
- **Liquid Hydrogen (LH₂):** Zero-carbon fuel, but **3× the volume of Jet-A** and cryogenic storage at **20 K**.
- **Ammonia (NH₃):** Hydrogen carrier with easier storage; combustion challenges due to low flame speed.
- **Hybrid-Electric & Turbo-Electric:** Battery-powered or fuel-cell-assisted propulsion for regional aircraft (< 1000 km).

### 2.4 Pillar IV — Digital Transformation and AI

- **Digital Twins:** Real-time virtual replicas of engines used for predictive maintenance.
- **AI-based Health Monitoring:** Reduces unscheduled removals by up to **30%**.
- **Additive Manufacturing (3D Printing):** Enables complex internal cooling passages, lightweight lattices, and part-count reduction (GE Catalyst: **855 parts → 12 parts**).

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Notes |
|---|---------|-------------|---------------|
| 1 | $\eta_{th} = \dfrac{W_{net}}{Q_{in}} = 1 - \dfrac{Q_{out}}{Q_{in}}$ | Thermal efficiency of gas turbine | Dimensionless |
| 2 | $\eta_{th} = 1 - \left(\dfrac{T_1}{T_3}\right) \left(\dfrac{p_1}{p_2}\right)^{\dfrac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}}$ | Brayton ideal efficiency (with $T_1, p_1$ at inlet) | Used in KTU problems |
| 3 | $\eta_{p} = \dfrac{2}{1 + \dfrac{V_j}{V_a}}$ | Propulsive efficiency | $V_j$: jet velocity, $V_a$: aircraft velocity |
| 4 | $\eta_{o} = \eta_{th} \times \eta_{p} \times \eta_{g}$ | Overall efficiency (product of efficiencies) | $\eta_g$: gearbox/mechanical efficiency |
| 5 | $F_{sp} = \dfrac{F}{\dot{m}}$ | Specific thrust | N/(kg/s) |
| 6 | $TSFC = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{f}}{F}$ | Thrust specific fuel consumption | kg/(N·s) or g/(kN·s) |
| 7 | $BPR = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{bypass}}{\dot{m}_{core}}$ | Bypass ratio (turbofan metric) | Dimensionless |
| 8 | $\dfrac{T_3}{T_1} = \left(\dfrac{p_2}{p_1}\right)^{\dfrac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}}$ | Isentropic compression relation | K, dimensionless ratio |
| 9 | $r_{p,\text{opt}} = \left(\dfrac{T_3}{T_1} \cdot \eta_c \cdot \eta_t\right)^{\dfrac{\gamma}{2(\gamma-1)}}$ | Optimum pressure ratio for max work | Dimensionless |
| 10 | $\Delta \eta_{th} \approx \dfrac{(\gamma-1)}{\gamma} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta T_{TIT}}{T_{TIT}}$ | Sensitivity of $\eta_{th}$ to $T_{TIT}$ | First-order approximation |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** The last formula (#10) is extremely high-yield for KTU numerical problems — it shows that **each 1% increase in TIT yields approximately 0.4% rise in thermal efficiency** (for $\gamma = 1.33$), which is precisely why material development is the industry's primary R&D focus.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Section Policy:** This section presents the derivations most frequently required in KTU ESE questions for the "Trends" topic. Every algebraic step is shown explicitly; no shortcuts or hidden transitions are permitted.

### 3.1 Derivation: How Does $T_3$ Improvement Translate to $\eta_{th}$ Gain?

**Given:**
- Brayton ideal cycle with $T_1$ = 300 K (inlet)
- $T_3$ = 1500 K (TIT)
- $r_p$ = 10, $\gamma = 1.4$

**Step 1: Write the ideal Brayton efficiency equation.**

$$ \eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{1}{r_p^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}}} $$

**Step 2: Calculate the exponent.**

$$ \frac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma} = \frac{1.4 - 1}{1.4} = \frac{0.4}{1.4} = 0.2857 $$

**Step 3: Evaluate the pressure ratio term.**

$$ r_p^{0.2857} = 10^{0.2857} $$

To compute: $\log_{10}(10^{0.2857}) = 0.2857$, so $10^{0.2857} \approx 1.93$.

**Step 4: Compute the efficiency.**

$$ \eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{1}{1.93} = 1 - 0.518 = 0.482 \approx 48.2\% $$

**Step 5: Now, increase $T_3$ from 1500 K to 1700 K (a 200 K rise).**

Using the more general form:

$$ \eta_{th} = 1 - \left(\frac{T_1}{T_3}\right) \cdot \frac{1}{r_p^{0.2857}} $$

$$ \eta_{th,\text{new}} = 1 - \left(\frac{300}{1700}\right) \cdot \frac{1}{1.93} $$

$$ \eta_{th,\text{new}} = 1 - (0.1765) \cdot (0.518) $$

$$ \eta_{th,\text{new}} = 1 - 0.0914 = 0.9086 \approx 90.86\% $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this unrealistic value?** Because the **simple Brayton efficiency formula does not include $T_3$ explicitly** — it depends only on $r_p$. The more general derivation including temperature explicitly is shown below.

**Step 6: Use the full thermal efficiency expression for a closed cycle with fixed $T_1, T_3$.**

$$ \eta_{th} = \frac{c_p(T_3 - T_4) - c_p(T_2 - T_1)}{c_p(T_3 - T_2)} $$

For ideal cycle: $T_4 = T_3 / r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$ and $T_2 = T_1 \cdot r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$.

**Step 7: Substituting $T_1 = 300$ K, $T_3 = 1500$ K, $r_p = 10$:**

$$ T_2 = 300 \times 1.93 = 579 \text{ K} $$

$$ T_4 = \frac{1500}{1.93} = 777.2 \text{ K} $$

**Step 8: Compute $W_{\text{compressor}}$ and $W_{\text{turbine}}$ in terms of $c_p$:**

$$ W_C = c_p(T_2 - T_1) = c_p(579 - 300) = 279 c_p $$

$$ W_T = c_p(T_3 - T_4) = c_p(1500 - 777.2) = 722.8 c_p $$

$$ Q_{in} = c_p(T_3 - T_2) = c_p(1500 - 579) = 921 c_p $$

**Step 9: Net work and efficiency:**

$$ W_{net} = W_T - W_C = 722.8 c_p - 279 c_p = 443.8 c_p $$

$$ \eta_{th} = \frac{443.8}{921} = 0.482 = 48.2\% $$

**Step 10: Repeat for $T_3 = 1700$ K (all else constant):**

$$ T_4 = \frac{1700}{1.93} = 881 \text{ K} $$

$$ W_T = c_p(1700 - 881) = 819 c_p $$

$$ Q_{in} = c_p(1700 - 579) = 1121 c_p $$

$$ \eta_{th} = \frac{819 - 279}{1121} = \frac{540}{1121} = 0.482 $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight:** This is a counter-intuitive result. For an **ideal, reversible** Brayton cycle with fixed $T_1$ and fixed $r_p$, the efficiency is **independent of $T_3$**! However, in **real engines**, raising $T_3$ does improve efficiency because the **component efficiencies ($\eta_c, \eta_t$) and cycle irreversibilities** are functions of temperature — and the energy loss in real components is a smaller fraction of the larger $T_3$ work output.

### 3.2 Derivation: Bypass Ratio vs Propulsive Efficiency

**Given:** Aircraft velocity $V_a = 250$ m/s, Jet velocity $V_j$ varies.

**Step 1: Recall the propulsive efficiency equation.**

$$ \eta_p = \frac{2(V_j / V_a)}{1 + (V_j / V_a)^2} $$

This is a function only of the **velocity ratio** $\lambda = V_j / V_a$. It is maximized when $\lambda = 1$, giving $\eta_p = 1$ (100%).

**Step 2: Express $V_j$ in terms of bypass ratio.**

By momentum balance on a turbofan:

$$ F = \dot{m}_{core} (V_j - V_a) + \dot{m}_{bypass} (V_{fan} - V_a) $$

For the *idealized* case where the bypass stream is accelerated just enough:

$$ V_j \approx V_a \cdot \left(1 + \frac{1}{\sqrt{BPR}}\right) \quad \text{(approximation)} $$

**Step 3: Compute $\eta_p$ for BPR = 1, 5, 10:**

For BPR = 1: $V_j / V_a \approx 2.0$, $\eta_p = 2(2)/(1+4) = 0.80 = 80\%$
For BPR = 5: $V_j / V_a \approx 1.45$, $\eta_p = 2(1.45)/(1+2.10) = 0.94 = 94\%$
For BPR = 10: $V_j / V_a \approx 1.32$, $\eta_p = 2(1.32)/(1+1.74) = 0.96 = 96\%$

> [!NOTE]
> **Future Trend Validation:** This derivation mathematically justifies the **industry-wide push toward ultra-high bypass ratios (BPR = 12–15)**, since propulsive efficiency asymptotically approaches unity as BPR $\to \infty$. It is the central engineering reason that modern turbofans look "wider" with each generation.

### 3.3 KTU Analytical Matrix — Trend → Driver → Limitation

| Future Trend | Underlying Engineering Driver | Current Limitation | Expected Impact (2030+) |
|--------------|------------------------------|--------------------|--------------------------|
| Higher TIT (>2000 K) | Higher $\eta_{th}$ via Carnot drift | Blade creep / oxidation | +5% $\eta_{th}$, -10% SFC |
| Higher OPR (60:1) | Higher mean effective pressure | Compressor stall, weight | +3% $\eta_{th}$ |
| Ultra-High BPR (15:1) | Higher $\eta_p$ | Fan diameter, ground clearance | -15% TSFC |
| CMC Blades | Hotter operation w/o cooling | Manufacturing cost | +20% component life |
| Hydrogen Fuel | Zero CO₂ emissions | Volumetric energy density | Net-zero aviation |
| Hybrid-Electric | Bypass thermodynamic limits | Battery energy density | Regional aircraft (<1000 km) |
| Variable Cycle | Multi-mission flexibility | Mechanical complexity | Supersonic + subsonic efficiency |
| Rotating Detonation | Constant-volume combustion | Unsteady flow, control | Theoretical +10% $\eta_{th}$ |
| Scramjets | Hypersonic air-breathing | Net thrust at low Mach | Mach 5+ access-to-space |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> The following diagrams map the conceptual architecture of future gas turbine and jet propulsion trends. Mermaid syntax is used with strictly alphanumeric node IDs and clean uppercase labels (no markdown tags inside double-quoted labels).

### 4.1 Master Roadmap of Future Propulsion Trends

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeStart([GAS TURBINE AND JET PROPULSION FUTURE TRENDS]) --> nodeA[AIRCRAFT ENGINE TRENDS]
    nodeStart --> nodeB[INDUSTRIAL GAS TURBINE TRENDS]
    nodeStart --> nodeC[ROCKET COMBINED CYCLE TRENDS]

    nodeA --> nodeA1[ULTRA HIGH BYPASS TURBOFAN]
    nodeA --> nodeA2[VARIABLE CYCLE ENGINES]
    nodeA --> nodeA3[HYDROGEN FUELED ENGINES]
    nodeA --> nodeA4[HYBRID ELECTRIC PROPULSION]
    nodeA --> nodeA5[OPEN ROTOR ENGINES]
    nodeA --> nodeA6[ROTATING DETONATION ENGINES]

    nodeA1 --> nodeA1A[GTF GEARED TURBOFAN]
    nodeA1 --> nodeA1B[PROPFAN OPEN ROTOR]
    nodeA1 --> nodeA1C[ULTRAFAN RR]

    nodeA2 --> nodeA2A[ADAPTIVE CYCLE F135 AETD]
    nodeA2 --> nodeA2B[THREE STREAM ADAPTIVE]

    nodeA3 --> nodeA3A[LIQUID H2 CRYOGENIC]
    nodeA3 --> nodeA3B[AMMONIA NH3]
    nodeA3 --> nodeA3C[SAF SYNTHETIC KEROSENE]

    nodeA4 --> nodeA4A[BATTERY ASSISTED TURBOFAN]
    nodeA4 --> nodeA4B[FUEL CELL HYBRID]

    nodeB --> nodeB1[CCGT COMBINED CYCLE GT]
    nodeB --> nodeB2[IGCC INTEGRATED GASIFICATION]
    nodeB --> nodeB3[ADVANCED COOLING CMC]
    nodeB --> nodeB4[ADDITIVE MANUFACTURING]

    nodeC --> nodeC1[SABRE PRECOOLED ENGINE]
    nodeC --> nodeC2[SCRAMJET HYPERSONIC]
    nodeC --> nodeC3[PDE PULSE DETONATION]
    nodeC --> nodeC4[ROCKET BASED COMBINED CYCLE RBCC]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Variable Cycle Engine (VCE) Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeIN[INLET VARIABLE GEOMETRY] --> nodeFAN[FAN MODULE]
    nodeFAN --> nodeBPCS[BYPASS DUCT COLD STREAM]
    nodeFAN --> nodeCORE[CORE ENGINE STREAM]
    nodeBPCS --> nodeMIX[BYPASS MIXER NOZZLE]
    nodeCORE --> nodeHPC[HIGH PRESSURE COMPRESSOR]
    nodeHPC --> nodeCC[COMBUSTOR]
    nodeCC --> nodeHPT[HIGH PRESSURE TURBINE]
    nodeHPT --> nodeLPT[LOW PRESSURE TURBINE]
    nodeLPT --> nodeA/B[AFTERBURNER OPTIONAL]
    nodeA/B --> nodeEXH[VARIABLE AREA NOZZLE]
    nodeEXH --> nodeMIX
    nodeMIX --> nodeTHRUST([NET THRUST])
```

### 4.3 Functional Block Diagram: Hydrogen-Fueled Propulsion System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeLH2[LIQUID H2 TANK 20 K CRYOGENIC] --> nodePUMP[CRYOGENIC PUMP]
    nodePUMP --> nodeHEATEX[FUEL HEAT EXCHANGER PREHEAT]
    nodeHEATEX --> nodeFUELMETER[FLOW METER AND CONTROL]
    nodeFUELMETER --> nodeCOMB[MODIFIED COMBUSTOR H2 SAFE]
    nodeCOMB --> nodeT3[HOT GAS T3 1850 K]
    nodeT3 --> nodeTURBINE[TURBINE SECTION]
    nodeTURBINE --> nodeGEN[POWER GENERATION OR THRUST]
    nodeGEN --> nodeEXHAUST[WATER VAPOR EXHAUST ZERO CO2]
    nodeOXID[RAM AIR OR LIQUID OXIDIZER] --> nodeCOMB
```

### 4.4 Classification Tree: Hypersonic Air-Breathing Engines

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeHYP[HYPERSONIC AIR BREATHING ENGINES MACH 5 PLUS] --> nodeRAMJ[RAMJET MACH 3 TO 6]
    nodeHYP --> nodeSCRAM[SCRAMJET MACH 6 TO 15]
    nodeHYP --> nodeDUAL[DUAL MODE RAMJET SCRAMJET]
    nodeHYP --> nodePDE[PULSE DETONATION ENGINE]
    nodeHYP --> nodeRDE[ROTATING DETONATION ENGINE]
    nodeHYP --> nodeTBCC[TURBORAMJET TBCC]
    nodeHYP --> nodeSABRE[SABRE PRECOOLED]

    nodeRAMJ --> nodeRAMJ1[NO MOVING PARTS COMPRESSION BY RAM]
    nodeSCRAM --> nodeSCRAM1[SUPERSONIC COMBUSTION RAM]
    nodePDE --> nodePDE1[CONSTANT VOLUME CYCLE]
    nodeRDE --> nodeRDE1[CONTINUOUS ROTATING DETONATION]
    nodeTBCC --> nodeTBCC1[TURBOJET + RAMJET + SCRAMJET]
    nodeSABRE --> nodeSABRE1[HELIUM PRECOOLER + ROCKET MODE]
```
