## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]**
**Define "Bypass Ratio" (BPR) in a turbofan engine and explain why the modern trend is toward *ultra-high* bypass ratios (BPR > 12).**

**Model Answer:**

The **Bypass Ratio (BPR)** is defined as the ratio of the mass flow rate of air that bypasses the core (through the fan duct) to the mass flow rate of air passing through the core (compressor–combustor–turbine):

$$ BPR = \frac{\dot{m}_{bypass}}{\dot{m}_{core}} $$

**Why the trend toward ultra-high BPR:**
1. The propulsive efficiency of a turbofan is given by $\eta_p = \dfrac{2(V_j/V_a)}{1 + (V_j/V_a)^2}$, and it approaches 100% as $V_j \to V_a$ (i.e., the jet velocity approaches the aircraft velocity).
2. A higher BPR means a larger portion of thrust is generated by the slower, cooler bypass stream, which accelerates the air by a smaller amount — making $V_j$ closer to $V_a$.
3. This results in a significant reduction in **TSFC** (typically 15–25% lower than low-BPR engines).
4. Example: The Rolls-Royce UltraFan targets **BPR = 15:1**, achieving up to **25% fuel-burn reduction** versus first-generation turbofans.

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Tip:** A 3-mark answer must include (1) the formula, (2) the physical meaning, and (3) at least one quantitative justification. Diagrams are not mandatory but earn bonus marks.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**
**List and briefly explain *any three* emerging trends in gas turbine materials for modern aero-engines.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Single-Crystal (SC) Superalloys (e.g., CMSX-4):** These are nickel-based superalloys cast in a single-crystal form, eliminating grain boundaries that act as creep failure sites. They allow operation at $T_3 \approx 1450\text{ K}$ with superior creep life compared to polycrystalline blades.

2. **Ceramic Matrix Composites (CMCs), e.g., SiC/SiC:** These combine ceramic fibers (high-temperature strength) with a ceramic matrix (environmental resistance). They weigh **~1/3 of nickel superalloys**, operate at $T_3$ up to **1850 K**, and require **no external film-cooling air** — saving ~20% of compressor bleed for thrust.

3. **Thermal Barrier Coatings (TBCs):** Applied as a thin layer (~100–500 μm) of yttria-stabilized zirconia (YSZ) on blade surfaces, TBCs reduce the metal substrate temperature by **100–150 K**, effectively enabling the engine to run hotter than the blade's bulk melting point.

4. *(Optional bonus)* **Refractory High-Entropy Alloys (RHEAs):** Future-generation materials being researched for $T_3 > 2000\text{ K}$ applications, combining multiple principal elements for exceptional high-temperature strength.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **As per KTU ESE 2024 Scheme:** Module 3 carries internal choice. You must answer EITHER **Question A** OR **Question B** (full 14 marks). Each sub-part is 7 marks.

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 3 Choice-1]*

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the major trends in modern aircraft engine development with respect to **Turbine Inlet Temperature (TIT)**, **Overall Pressure Ratio (OPR)**, and **Bypass Ratio (BPR)**. Support your answer with at least two real-world engine examples.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With the aid of a neat schematic, describe the working of a **Variable Cycle Engine (VCE)**. How does it differ from a conventional turbofan, and what operational advantages does it offer?

---

**Model Solution (a) — TIT, OPR, and BPR Trends:**

The three primary performance levers in modern aircraft engine development are TIT, OPR, and BPR. Each influences the cycle efficiency in a distinct way.

**1. Turbine Inlet Temperature (TIT):**
TIT has steadily increased from ~1050 K in the 1960s to **~1850 K in modern civil engines** and **>2000 K in military engines**. This is enabled by:
- Single-crystal superalloy blades
- TBCs (thermal barrier coatings)
- Advanced film cooling
- CMCs in high-pressure turbine sections

**Example:** The **GE9X** (Boeing 777X) operates at $T_3 \approx 1625\text{ K}$ with TBC-coated TBC + CMC shrouds.

**2. Overall Pressure Ratio (OPR):**
Modern high-bypass turbofans operate at OPRs of **40:1 to 60:1**, while military engines (which favor thrust-to-weight over fuel economy) are at **25:1 to 30:1**. Higher OPR increases the **mean effective pressure** of the cycle, improving $\eta_{th}$.

**Example:** The **Rolls-Royce Trent XWB** operates at **OPR = 52:1**, the highest of any in-service engine.

**3. Bypass Ratio (BPR):**
BPR has increased from ~1:1 in early turbofans to **10:1 (GEnx, GTF)** to **15:1 (future UltraFan)**. The driver is the asymptotic approach of $\eta_p$ to unity as BPR increases.

**Example:** The **Pratt & Whitney PW1000G GTF** has **BPR = 12:1** with a reduction gear, achieving **15–20% lower fuel burn** than previous-generation engines.

**Why these trends work together (Saha cycle integration):**

The modern design point seeks to simultaneously maximize:
$$ \eta_o = \eta_{th} \cdot \eta_p \cdot \eta_m \cdot \eta_g $$

Where each lever contributes:
- Higher TIT → Higher $\eta_{th}$
- Higher OPR → Higher $\eta_{th}$ (with diminishing returns beyond OPR ≈ 40)
- Higher BPR → Higher $\eta_p$

**Conclusion:** Future engines will continue to push all three levers, but the physical limits (creep, compressor stability, fan diameter) mean that **incremental gains will be smaller**, demanding cycle innovations like VCE, ICR, and hydrogen combustion.

---

**Model Solution (b) — Variable Cycle Engine:**

A **Variable Cycle Engine (VCE)** is a propulsion system capable of operating in **multiple distinct thermodynamic cycles** during a single mission by varying geometry and flow splits.

**Schematic (ASCII representation since diagrams may not reproduce):**

```
   [VARIABLE INLET] → [FAN] ─┬─→ [BYPASS DUCT (COLD)]      ─┐
                              │                              ├─→ [MIXER] → [VARIABLE NOZZLE] → THRUST
                              └─→ [CORE: HPC → COMB → HPT] ─┘
                                       ↑
                              [LPT drives FAN]
```

**Key VCE features:**
1. **Variable Inlet Geometry:** Adjusts the capture area for supersonic vs. subsonic flight.
2. **Modulating Bypass Flow:** A splitter valve regulates how much air goes through the core.
3. **Variable Area Nozzle (VAN):** Optimizes $V_j$ for thrust or efficiency.
4. **Multi-Mode Operation:** Subsonic cruise (high BPR mode) and supersonic dash (low BPR, high thrust mode).

**Differences from Conventional Turbofan:**

| Feature | Conventional Turbofan | VCE |
|---------|----------------------|-----|
| Bypass ratio | Fixed | Variable |
| Inlet geometry | Fixed | Variable |
| Mission profile | Single optimum | Multi-mode |
| Thrust-to-weight | High at design point | Compromise but adaptive |
| Complexity | Lower | Higher |

**Operational Advantages:**
- **Supersonic + Subsonic:** Capable of efficient cruise at both regimes.
- **Thrust Augmentation:** Higher thrust during combat (vs. efficient cruise).
- **Thermal Management:** Variable bypass flow allows turbine cooling during high-power settings.
- **Stealth:** Lower IR signature at subsonic cruise.

**Examples:** **F119 (F-22 Raptor)**, **F135 (F-35 Lightning II)**, **GE AETD (Adaptive Engine Technology Development)**.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 3 Choice-2]*

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the concept of **Sustainable Aviation Fuels (SAF)** and **hydrogen as a future aviation fuel**. Compare their advantages and limitations.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With relevant equations, explain why the **propulsive efficiency** of a turbofan approaches unity as the **bypass ratio increases**. Calculate the propulsive efficiency for BPR = 8 if the jet velocity is 280 m/s and the aircraft velocity is 240 m/s.

---

**Model Solution (a) — SAF and Hydrogen:**

**1. Sustainable Aviation Fuels (SAF):**
SAF refers to **synthetic, non-fossil-derived jet fuels** that are chemically similar to Jet-A/Jet-A1 but produced from renewable feedstocks.

**Production Pathways:**
- **HEFA** (Hydroprocessed Esters and Fatty Acids): From used cooking oil, animal fats, algae.
- **FT-SPK** (Fischer–Tropsch Synthetic Paraffinic Kerosene): From biomass gasification.
- **ATJ** (Alcohol-to-Jet): From fermentation of sugars → isobutanol → jet fuel.
- **PtL** (Power-to-Liquid): Green H₂ + captured CO₂ → synthetic kerosene.

**Advantages:** Drop-in compatible, up to **80% lifecycle CO₂ reduction**, no aircraft modification.
**Limitations:** Currently **2–5× the cost** of Jet-A, limited supply (< 0.1% of global jet fuel demand in 2023), and only 50% blend certified (ASTM D7566).

**2. Hydrogen as a Future Aviation Fuel:**
- Zero CO₂ emissions; exhaust is essentially water vapor.
- Available as **liquid H₂ (LH₂)** at 20 K, requiring cryogenic tanks.

**Advantages:** True zero-carbon, high energy density by mass (**~120 MJ/kg** vs. Jet-A's 43 MJ/kg).
**Limitations:**
- Volume **3× larger** than Jet-A for the same energy (low density: 71 kg/m³).
- Tank insulation and boil-off management.
- Airport infrastructure overhaul.
- Combustion challenges: high flame speed, flashback in conventional combustors.
- NOx emissions may rise (due to higher flame temperatures).

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | Jet-A | SAF | Liquid H₂ |
|-----------|-------|-----|-----------|
| Energy density (MJ/kg) | 43 | 43 | 120 |
| Energy density (MJ/L) | 35 | 35 | 8.5 |
| CO₂ emissions | 100% (baseline) | 20% | 0% |
| Cost (relative) | 1× | 2–5× | 4–8× |
| Aircraft modification | None | None | Major (cryogenic tanks) |

---

**Model Solution (b) — Propulsive Efficiency and BPR:**

**Theoretical Background:**
The propulsive efficiency of a jet engine is given by:

$$ \eta_p = \frac{2(V_j / V_a)}{1 + (V_j / V_a)^2} = \frac{2\lambda}{1 + \lambda^2} $$

where $\lambda = V_j / V_a$ is the velocity ratio.

**Maximizing $\eta_p$:** Differentiating with respect to $\lambda$:

$$ \frac{d\eta_p}{d\lambda} = \frac{2(1 + \lambda^2) - 2\lambda(2\lambda)}{(1 + \lambda^2)^2} = \frac{2(1 - \lambda^2)}{(1 + \lambda^2)^2} $$

Setting the derivative to zero gives $\lambda = 1$, i.e., $V_j = V_a$, yielding $\eta_p = 1$ (100%).

**Connection to BPR:**
The jet velocity $V_j$ in a turbofan relates to the bypass ratio through the **Betz momentum equation**. For a mixed-stream turbofan (idealized):

$$ V_j = V_a \left( 1 + \frac{\sqrt{2 BPR}}{1 + BPR} \right) \quad \text{(qualitative trend)} $$

As $BPR \to \infty$, $V_j \to V_a$, hence $\eta_p \to 1$.

**Numerical Calculation:**

**Given:** BPR = 8 (irrelevant for direct $\eta_p$ computation; we use $V_j$ and $V_a$).
- $V_j = 280$ m/s
- $V_a = 240$ m/s

**Step 1: Calculate $\lambda$.**

$$ \lambda = \frac{V_j}{V_a} = \frac{280}{240} = 1.1667 $$

**Step 2: Substitute into the $\eta_p$ formula.**

$$ \eta_p = \frac{2 \times 1.1667}{1 + (1.1667)^2} $$

$$ \eta_p = \frac{2.3333}{1 + 1.3611} = \frac{2.3333}{2.3611} $$

**Step 3: Compute the result.**

$$ \eta_p = 0.9882 = 98.82\% $$

**Step 4: Interpretation.**
A propulsive efficiency of ~98.8% is achievable because the jet velocity (280 m/s) is close to the aircraft velocity (240 m/s). This reflects the **modern high-BPR philosophy**: accelerate a large mass of air by a small amount, rather than a small mass by a large amount.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfall Alert:**
> 1. **Do not confuse $\eta_p$ with $\eta_{th}$.** Propulsive efficiency is about converting **kinetic energy of the jet into useful work (thrust × velocity)**. Thermal efficiency is about converting **chemical energy of fuel into work output**. Many students mix these up — read the question carefully.
> 2. **The $\eta_p$ formula $2\lambda/(1+\lambda^2)$** is for a **single-stream engine (turbojet)**. For a turbofan with a separate bypass stream, the more accurate expression is $\eta_p = (2 / (1 + \lambda_{core}^2)) \times (\text{mixing factor})$. For KTU problems at the Module-3 level, the simpler form is acceptable.
> 3. **BPR is not directly substituted into the $\eta_p$ formula.** BPR affects $V_j$ indirectly. In a 7-mark question, you will lose marks if you do not show this connection explicitly.
> 4. **Always state units** for $V_j$, $V_a$ (m/s) and dimensionless for $\eta_p$ and BPR.
> 5. **Do not forget to use the "increased $T_3$" example in Trends questions** — at least one numerical example with TIT/OPR variation is expected by KTU examiners.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Summary for Last-Minute KTU Revision**

#### **Core Definitions**
- **BPR** = $\dot{m}_{bypass} / \dot{m}_{core}$ → drives $\eta_p$ toward 1.
- **OPR** = $p_2 / p_1$ → drives $\eta_{th}$ higher (with diminishing returns).
- **TIT ($T_3$)** → strongest material-science driver; raises $\eta_{th}$ in real (lossy) cycles.
- **TSFC** = $\dot{m}_f / F$ → lower is better; modern turbofans: 10–12 g/(kN·s).

#### **Future Trends (Memorize 5 Pillars)**
1. **Higher TIT** (via CMC + TBC + single-crystal blades).
2. **Higher OPR** (up to 60:1 in civil engines).
3. **Ultra-High BPR** (12:1 → 15:1).
4. **Sustainable Fuels** (SAF + LH₂ + ammonia).
5. **Digital & Manufacturing** (AI, digital twins, additive manufacturing).

#### **Advanced Engine Concepts**
- **VCE** (Variable Cycle Engine): F-22, F-35, AETD.
- **GTF** (Geared Turbofan): PW1000G, BPR = 12:1.
- **SABRE**: Precooled hydrogen engine for Skylon spaceplane.
- **Scramjet**: Mach 6+ air-breathing, X-43A, X-51A.
- **RDE**: Continuous rotating detonation (next-gen concept).
- **PDE**: Pulse detonation engine (constant-volume cycle).

#### **Critical Numerical Formulas**
- $\eta_{th} = 1 - 1 / r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$ (ideal Brayton)
- $\eta_p = 2\lambda / (1 + \lambda^2)$ with $\lambda = V_j / V_a$
- $F_{sp} = F / \dot{m}$ → use it to compare engines of different sizes
- $\Delta \eta_{th} \approx 0.4 \times \Delta T_{TIT} / T_{TIT}$ (sensitivity, %)

#### **High-Yield Facts to Remember**
- GE9X TIT ≈ 1625 K; **Trent XWB OPR = 52:1** (highest in-service).
- GEnx BPR = 9.6; **UltraFan target BPR = 15:1**.
- **CCGT efficiency** can exceed 64% (HL-class, 7HA.03).
- **HEFA, FT-SPK, ATJ** are the three main SAF pathways.
- **IATA net-zero target**: 2050.

#### **Common Exam Traps to Avoid**
- Confusing $\eta_{th}$ and $\eta_p$.
- Forgetting that **ideal Brayton $\eta_{th}$ is independent of $T_3$** (but real engines are not).
- Stating BPR = 10:1 for the GEnx when it is actually 9.6:1.
- Calling CMC a "metal alloy" — it is a **ceramic matrix** composite.
- Forgetting the **velocity ratio $\lambda$** in $\eta_p$ problems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Examination Tip:**
> For Module 3's "Trends and Future Development" question, structure your answer in **three layers**:
> 1. *What is the trend?* (definition + metric)
> 2. *Why does the trend exist?* (driver, e.g., efficiency equation)
> 3. *How is it achieved?* (technology enabler, e.g., CMC, GTF gear)
> This layered approach matches KTU's OBE marking rubric and earns full marks consistently.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Micro Gas Turbines - Smart Energy System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET741 - GAS TURBINE AND JET PROPULSION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Micro Gas Turbines - Smart Energy System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Micro Gas Turbine (MGT)** is a small-scale turbomachine-based prime mover that operates on the **Brayton thermodynamic cycle**, delivering a usable shaft power output typically in the range of **30 kW to 500 kW** (with some industrial units extending up to **1 MW**). When integrated with digital controls, bidirectional power electronics, storage, and renewable sources, the MGT evolves into the core of a **Smart Energy System (SES)** — an intelligent, distributed, demand-responsive energy hub capable of delivering electrical power, thermal energy (heating/cooling), and grid ancillary services in real time.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition Highlight**
> Micro Gas Turbines, as per Module 3, are studied as **Smart Energy Systems** — emphasizing their role in distributed generation (DG), microgrids, combined heat and power (CHP), combined cooling heating and power (CCHP / trigeneration), and hybrid renewable integration.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Small But Smart Power Plant"

Imagine a **full-size jet engine**, but instead of pushing an aircraft at 900 km/h, it is bolted to the floor of a factory, a hospital, or a remote village, spinning a tiny electric generator at **50,000 to 100,000 rpm**. To make it smart, we add a "brain" — a digital controller that:

1. Talks to the **electrical grid**,
2. Listens to the **building's heating demand**, and
3. Adjusts fuel input within **milliseconds** to balance supply and demand.

Think of it as the **"Swiss Army Knife"** of small-scale power:
- 🔥 Produces electricity like a generator,
- ♨️ Produces hot water like a boiler,
- ❄️ Produces chilled water (via absorption chiller) like an AC plant,
- ⚡ Stabilizes the grid like a battery — all from a single box the size of a refrigerator.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Electrical efficiency (ηₑ):** **25% to 35%** (simple cycle), **up to 40%** (with recuperator)
- **Overall (total) efficiency (CHP mode):** **75% to 90%**
- **Pressure ratio (rp):** **3:1 to 4.5:1**
- **Turbine inlet temperature (TIT):** **950 °C to 1100 °C**
- **Shaft speed:** **50,000 to 100,000 rpm**
- **Exhaust temperature:** **250 °C to 300 °C** (ideal for CHP)
- **Specific power density:** **~2 to 5 kW/kg**
- **NOx emissions:** **< 9 ppm** (with lean premixed combustion)

> [!NOTE]
> The low pressure ratio (compared to large utility turbines where $r_p$ can exceed 30) is what makes the micro turbine truly "micro" — small blades, low cost, and modest efficiency are balanced by **fuel flexibility** and **modular scalability**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operating Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

The micro gas turbine operates on the **open Brayton cycle** but, unlike large utility turbines, almost always incorporates a **recuperator (regenerator)** to recover waste heat from the exhaust and preheat the compressed air before combustion.

**Sequential Logic:**

1. **Intake & Filtration** — Atmospheric air enters through a silenced plenum and high-efficiency particulate filter (HEPA grade for clean combustion).
2. **Radial (Centrifugal) Compression** — A single centrifugal compressor raises the air pressure from ambient (≈ 101.325 kPa) to typically **3–4.5 bar**. Because the compressor is radial (not axial), it tolerates **high pressure ratios per stage** — ideal for compact design.
3. **Recuperation** — Hot turbine exhaust passes through one side of a compact **primary surface recuperator** (typically a stainless-steel plate-fin heat exchanger). The compressed air leaving the compressor passes through the other side, gaining heat before entering the combustor. Effectiveness ε = **0.80 to 0.92**.
4. **Combustion** — Preheated air mixes with fuel (natural gas, biogas, diesel, kerosene, or hydrogen blends) in a **lean premixed annular combustor**. Lean premixing keeps the flame temperature low enough to suppress thermal NOx.
5. **Radial Turbine Expansion** — Hot gases expand through a **single-stage radial inflow turbine**, producing shaft work. Because the turbine and compressor are mounted on a **common shaft**, the turbine drives both the compressor and the generator.
6. **Power Conversion** — A **permanent magnet synchronous generator (PMSG)** produces high-frequency AC (typically 1500–3000 Hz). A solid-state **power electronic converter (rectifier → DC link → inverter)** converts this to grid-quality 50/60 Hz AC.
7. **Exhaust Heat Recovery (CHP layer)** — The still-hot exhaust (~ 280 °C) feeds a **secondary heat exchanger** to produce hot water, steam, or to drive an **absorption chiller** for cooling.
8. **Smart Controller Layer** — A PLC/DSP-based controller monitors grid frequency, building load, fuel cost, and emissions, and modulates fuel flow for optimum part-load efficiency.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description / Application |
|---|---------|---------------------------|
| 1 | $W_c = c_{p_a}(T_{02} - T_{01})$ | Compressor work per kg of air |
| 2 | $W_t = c_{p_g}(T_{03} - T_{04})$ | Turbine work per kg of gas |
| 3 | $W_{net} = W_t - W_c$ | Net specific work output (kJ/kg) |
| 4 | $\eta_{th} = \dfrac{W_{net}}{Q_{in}} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}}$ | Ideal Brayton thermal efficiency |
| 5 | $Q_{in} = c_{p_g}(T_{03} - T_{05})$ | Heat added in combustor (with recuperator) |
| 6 | $T_{05} = T_{01} + \varepsilon (T_{04} - T_{01})$ | Recuperator effectiveness relation |
| 7 | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{T_{05} - T_{01}}{T_{04} - T_{01}}$ | Recuperator effectiveness definition |
| 8 | $\eta_{recup} = \dfrac{T_{05} - T_{02}}{T_{03} - T_{02}}$ | Energy saved by recuperation |
| 9 | $\eta_{el} = \dfrac{P_{electrical}}{\dot{m}_f \cdot CV}$ | Electrical efficiency (real cycle) |
| 10 | $\eta_{total} = \dfrac{P_{el} + Q_{useful}}{\dot{m}_f \cdot CV}$ | Total (CHP) efficiency |
| 11 | $P_{el} = \eta_{gen} \cdot \eta_{mech} \cdot \dot{m}_a \cdot W_{net}$ | Electrical power output |
| 12 | $F = \dfrac{\dot{m}_a}{\dot{m}_f}$ | Air–fuel ratio (typically 45–65 for lean burn) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Units Convention (KTU Board Standard):**
> - Temperatures: **Kelvin (K)** for calculations, **°C** for engineering statements.
> - Specific heats: $c_{p_a} = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K (air), $c_{p_g} = 1.148$ kJ/kg·K (combustion products, using $\gamma_g = 1.33$).
> - Pressure ratio is **dimensionless**.
> - Mass flow: **kg/s**.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Micro gas turbines power:
- **Distributed generation** in commercial buildings, hospitals, data centers, and hotels.
- **Hybrid microgrids** combined with solar PV, wind, and battery energy storage (BESS).
- **Range-extender units** in series-hybrid electric vehicles and military unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs).
- **Remote-area power** in oil & gas platforms, islands, and disaster zones.
- **Trigeneration plants** delivering electricity, heating, and absorption-cooling.

They qualify as **Smart Energy Systems** because of:
- Bidirectional grid interaction (load following, peak shaving),
- Real-time **demand response** capability,
- **Frequency and voltage support** to weak grids,
- Fuel-flexible operation (natural gas, biogas, syngas, H₂ blends),
- Modular scalability (N+1 redundancy).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Brayton Cycle Efficiency Without Recuperator

We begin with the **ideal Brayton cycle** consisting of two isentropic processes (1→2 compression, 3→4 expansion) and two isobaric processes (2→3 heat addition, 4→1 heat rejection).

**Step 1:** Apply the isentropic relation for compression:

$$
\frac{T_{02}}{T_{01}} = \left(\frac{p_2}{p_1}\right)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} = r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}
$$

**Step 2:** Solve for the compressor outlet temperature:

$$
T_{02} = T_{01} \cdot r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}
$$

**Step 3:** Apply the same isentropic relation to the turbine (inlet T₃, outlet T₄):

$$
T_{04} = \frac{T_{03}}{r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}}
$$

**Step 4:** Compute the net specific work:

$$
W_{net} = W_t - W_c = c_p(T_{03} - T_{04}) - c_p(T_{02} - T_{01})
$$

**Step 5:** Substitute the expressions for $T_{02}$ and $T_{04}$:

$$
W_{net} = c_p T_{03}\!\left[1 - r_p^{-(\gamma-1)/\gamma}\right] - c_p T_{01}\!\left[r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - 1\right]
$$

**Step 6:** Compute the heat added in the combustor (no recuperation):

$$
Q_{in} = c_p(T_{03} - T_{02}) = c_p\!\left[T_{03} - T_{01}\,r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}\right]
$$

**Step 7:** Form the thermal efficiency ratio:

$$
\eta_{th} = \frac{W_{net}}{Q_{in}} = \frac{(T_{03}/T_{01})\!\left[1 - r_p^{-(\gamma-1)/\gamma}\right] - \left[r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - 1\right]}{(T_{03}/T_{01}) - r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}}
$$

**Step 8:** Take the limit as the **firing temperature $T_{03} \to \infty$**, which corresponds to the practical upper bound for any gas-turbine cycle:

$$
\lim_{T_{03}/T_{01} \to \infty} \eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{1}{r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This result is the **famous KTU board result**: the Brayton efficiency depends *only* on the pressure ratio, not on TIT — provided TIT is "high enough". In micro turbines, however, TIT is limited to **~1100 °C**, so this limit is **not fully achieved**, which is why we recover waste heat using a **recuperator**.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Effect of Recuperator on Thermal Efficiency

When a recuperator of effectiveness $\varepsilon$ is added, the air entering the combustor is preheated from $T_{02}$ to $T_{05}$:

**Step 1:** Write the recuperator outlet temperature for the cold stream (compressed air):

$$
T_{05} = T_{02} + \varepsilon(T_{04} - T_{02})
$$

**Step 2:** The new heat input is therefore reduced:

$$
Q_{in,\,recup} = c_p(T_{03} - T_{05}) = c_p\!\left[T_{03} - T_{02} - \varepsilon(T_{04} - T_{02})\right]
$$

**Step 3:** The net work output remains **unchanged** (recuperator is external to the gas path):

$$
W_{net,\,recup} = W_{net}
$$

**Step 4:** Hence the new thermal efficiency is:

$$
\eta_{th,\,recup} = \frac{W_{net}}{Q_{in,\,recup}} = \frac{W_{net}}{c_p\!\left[T_{03} - T_{02} - \varepsilon(T_{04} - T_{02})\right]}
$$

**Step 5:** Express the result in terms of the original (non-recuperated) efficiency:

$$
\eta_{th,\,recup} = \frac{\eta_{th,\,simple}}{1 - \dfrac{\varepsilon\, c_p(T_{04} - T_{02})}{c_p(T_{03} - T_{02})}} = \frac{\eta_{th,\,simple}}{1 - \varepsilon \cdot \dfrac{T_{04} - T_{02}}{T_{03} - T_{02}}}
$$

**Step 6:** Substitute numerical micro-turbine values: $\varepsilon = 0.85$, $T_{04} = 873$ K, $T_{02} = 440$ K, $T_{03} = 1373$ K:

$$
\eta_{th,\,recup} = \frac{\eta_{th,\,simple}}{1 - 0.85 \cdot \dfrac{873 - 440}{1373 - 440}} = \frac{\eta_{th,\,simple}}{1 - 0.85 \times 0.465} \approx \frac{\eta_{th,\,simple}}{0.605}
$$

**Step 7:** If $\eta_{th,\,simple} = 0.18$, then:

$$
\eta_{th,\,recup} \approx \frac{0.18}{0.605} \approx 0.298 \;\;(\approx 29.8\%)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This calculation shows that a **recuperator nearly doubles the electrical efficiency** in micro turbines — a vital reason why almost every commercial MGT (e.g., Capstone C30/C65/C200, Ingersoll-Rand MT250) is recuperated.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Net Electrical Power Output

The electrical power delivered to the grid is:

**Step 1:** Mechanical power from the turbine-compressor shaft:

$$
\dot{W}_{mech} = \dot{m}_a \cdot W_{net}
$$

**Step 2:** Multiply by generator and mechanical efficiencies:

$$
P_{el} = \eta_{gen} \cdot \eta_{mech} \cdot \dot{m}_a \cdot W_{net}
$$

**Step 3:** Typical micro-turbine values: $\dot{m}_a = 0.4$ kg/s, $W_{net} = 110$ kJ/kg, $\eta_{gen} = 0.96$, $\eta_{mech} = 0.98$:

$$
P_{el} = 0.96 \times 0.98 \times 0.4 \times 110 = 41.4 \;\; \text{kW}
$$

This matches the rating of a typical **30–65 kW class Capstone micro gas turbine**.

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Micro Turbine vs. Other Small Power Sources

| Parameter | Micro Gas Turbine | Diesel Genset | Fuel Cell (PEM) | Small Steam Turbine |
|-----------|------------------|---------------|-----------------|--------------------|
| Electrical efficiency | **25–35 %** | 30–40 % | 35–55 % | 15–25 % |
| NOx emissions | **< 9 ppm** | 50–500 ppm | Negligible | Low |
| Maintenance interval | **8,000 h** | 500–2,000 h | 4,000 h | 5,000 h |
| Noise level | **65 dB(A)** @ 10 m | 85 dB(A) | 55 dB(A) | 75 dB(A) |
| Fuel flexibility | **High** (NG, biogas, H₂) | Low (diesel) | Medium (H₂) | Medium |
| Smart-grid compatibility | **Excellent** | Moderate | Excellent | Moderate |
| Capital cost (per kW) | $1,500–3,000 | $400–800 | $4,000–8,000 | $2,500–5,000 |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Micro Gas Turbine as Smart Energy System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Atmospheric Air Intake] --> B[Silencer and HEPA Filter]
    B --> C[Radial Centrifugal Compressor]
    C --> D[Recuperator Cold Side]
    D --> E[Annular Lean Premixed Combustor]
    F[Natural Gas or Biogas Supply] --> E
    E --> G[Radial Inflow Turbine]
    G --> C
    G --> H[Permanent Magnet Synchronous Generator]
    H --> I[AC to DC Rectifier]
    I --> J[DC Link Capacitor Bank]
    J --> K[DC to AC Inverter]
    K --> L[Smart Grid Coupling Point PCC]
    L --> M[Building Load]
    L --> N[Microgrid Bus]
    G --> O[Recuperator Hot Side Exhaust]
    O --> P[Exhaust Heat Exchanger]
    P --> Q[Hot Water for Heating]
    P --> R[Absorption Chiller]
    R --> S[Chilled Water for Cooling]

```


### 4.3 T-s Diagram (Schematic — Descriptive)

Although a true T-s diagram cannot be rendered in Mermaid, the cycle on a T-s plot has the following landmarks:

| Point | State | Typical Temperature (K) | Typical Pressure (kPa) |
|-------|-------|------------------------|------------------------|
| 1 | Ambient air | 300 | 101 |
| 2 | After compressor | 440 | 405 (rp = 4) |
| 3 | After combustor (TIT) | 1373 | 405 |
| 4 | After turbine | 873 | 101 |
| 5 | After recuperator (cold side) | 800 | 101 |

The cycle traces **1 → 2** (vertical compression), **2 → 5 → 3** (heat addition via recuperator + combustor), **3 → 4** (vertical expansion), and rejects heat **4 → 1** through the recuperator hot side.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define a micro gas turbine. State any four of its distinguishing features that qualify it as a "smart energy system".*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A micro gas turbine (MGT) is a small-scale prime mover in the **30–500 kW** range, operating on the **Brayton cycle**, typically with a **radial compressor**, **radial inflow turbine**, **recuperator**, and **permanent-magnet generator**.

Four smart-energy features:
1. **Bidirectional grid interaction** through solid-state power electronics (load following, peak shaving).
2. **Real-time demand response** via PLC/DSP-based controller.
3. **Modular scalability** — units can be paralleled for N+1 redundancy.
4. **Fuel flexibility** — operates on natural gas, biogas, syngas, or H₂ blends with emissions control.
5. **CHP/CCHP capability** — high-grade exhaust heat for heating and absorption cooling.

*(Award 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for four features.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Explain the function of a recuperator in a micro gas turbine. Why is it almost always mandatory in MGTs but optional in large utility turbines?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A recuperator is a **compact plate-fin heat exchanger** that transfers heat from the turbine exhaust to the compressor discharge air **before combustion**, reducing the fuel needed to reach TIT. Its effectiveness is typically **0.80–0.92**.

In micro turbines, the **pressure ratio is low (3–4.5)** and **TIT is constrained by blade-cooling limitations**, so the ideal Brayton efficiency is only ~18 %. The recuperator nearly **doubles** the electrical efficiency to ~30 %, making the unit commercially viable.

In large utility turbines, the pressure ratio is already high (15–30) and TIT exceeds 1500 °C, so the cycle efficiency is intrinsically high (~35–40 %) and the additional pressure loss across a recuperator would outweigh its benefits.

*(Award 1 mark for function, 2 marks for comparison.)*

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

> **KTU ESE Module Internal Choice:** Answer **ONE** of the following.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]*

**(a)** Derive the expression for the thermal efficiency of an ideal Brayton cycle. Show that in the limit of high firing temperature, the efficiency depends only on pressure ratio.

**(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Starting from first principles:

1. Apply isentropic relation to compressor:

$$
T_{02} = T_{01} \cdot r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}
$$

2. Apply isentropic relation to turbine:

$$
T_{04} = T_{03} / r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}
$$

3. Net specific work:

$$
W_{net} = c_p\!\left[(T_{03} - T_{04}) - (T_{02} - T_{01})\right]
$$

4. Heat added:

$$
Q_{in} = c_p(T_{03} - T_{02})
$$

5. Thermal efficiency:

$$
\eta_{th} = \frac{W_{net}}{Q_{in}} = \frac{(T_{03} - T_{04}) - (T_{02} - T_{01})}{T_{03} - T_{02}}
$$

6. Divide numerator and denominator by $T_{01}$ and substitute $T_{02}/T_{01} = r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$:

$$
\eta_{th} = \frac{(T_{03}/T_{01})[1 - r_p^{-(\gamma-1)/\gamma}] - [r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - 1]}{(T_{03}/T_{01}) - r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}}
$$

7. Taking the limit as $T_{03}/T_{01} \to \infty$:

$$
\boxed{\eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{1}{r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}}}
$$

This proves that the cycle efficiency becomes independent of TIT and is governed solely by the **pressure ratio**.

*(Award 1 mark per logical step; full 7 marks for complete derivation.)*

**(b)** A micro gas turbine operates with pressure ratio $r_p = 4$, compressor inlet at 300 K, TIT at 1300 K, and a recuperator of effectiveness $\varepsilon = 0.85$. Assume $\gamma = 1.4$, $c_p = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K. Determine: (i) Compressor outlet temperature, (ii) Turbine outlet temperature, (iii) Recuperator cold-side outlet temperature, and (iv) Net specific work output.

**(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compressor outlet (isentropic):**

$$
T_{02} = T_{01} \cdot r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} = 300 \times 4^{0.2857}
$$

Since $4^{0.2857} = 1.486$:

$$
T_{02} = 300 \times 1.486 = 445.8 \;\; \text{K}
$$

**Step 2 — Turbine outlet (isentropic):**

$$
T_{04} = T_{03} / r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} = 1300 / 1.486 = 874.8 \;\; \text{K}
$$

**Step 3 — Recuperator cold-side outlet:**

$$
T_{05} = T_{02} + \varepsilon(T_{04} - T_{02}) = 445.8 + 0.85 \times (874.8 - 445.8)
$$

$$
T_{05} = 445.8 + 0.85 \times 429.0 = 445.8 + 364.7 = 810.5 \;\; \text{K}
$$

**Step 4 — Net specific work:**

$$
W_{net} = c_p\!\left[(T_{03} - T_{04}) - (T_{02} - T_{01})\right]
$$

$$
W_{net} = 1.005 \times \left[(1300 - 874.8) - (445.8 - 300)\right]
$$

$$
W_{net} = 1.005 \times [425.2 - 145.8] = 1.005 \times 279.4 = 280.8 \;\; \text{kJ/kg}
$$

*(Award 1.5 marks per sub-part with units; final rounding to 1 decimal place.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – May 2023]*

**(a)** Explain with a neat block diagram how a micro gas turbine is integrated into a smart energy system. Discuss its role in (i) combined heat and power (CHP), and (ii) microgrid operation with renewable sources.

**(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

A micro gas turbine integrates into a smart energy system through a **layered architecture** comprising:

1. **Prime-mover layer:** Compressor → Recuperator → Combustor → Turbine → PMSG.
2. **Power-electronics layer:** Rectifier → DC link → Inverter → Point of Common Coupling (PCC).
3. **Thermal-recovery layer:** Exhaust → Heat Exchanger → Hot water + Absorption chiller.
4. **Intelligence layer:** EMS/SCADA controller with sensors on grid frequency, building load, fuel cost.

**Role in CHP:**
The turbine exhaust at **~280 °C** is fed to a heat-recovery boiler producing **90 °C hot water** for space heating or domestic use. Total efficiency (electricity + heat) reaches **75–90 %**. In winter, the heat is directly used; in summer, the same heat drives an **absorption chiller** (LiBr–H₂O) producing chilled water at 7 °C for air-conditioning (CCHP / trigeneration).

**Role in Microgrid Operation:**
The MGT acts as a **dispatchable, firm, dispatchable generator** that complements intermittent renewables:

| Source | Role in Microgrid |
|--------|-------------------|
| Solar PV | Daytime generation, intermittent |
| Wind | Variable generation |
| **Micro Gas Turbine** | **Firm, dispatchable backup; load-follows; provides spinning reserve** |
| Battery BESS | Fast-response storage (seconds to minutes) |
| MGT + Battery | MGT handles slow load changes, BESS handles fast transients |

The smart controller dispatches the MGT to **smoothen net load**, **island** the microgrid during outages, and **sell ancillary services** (frequency regulation, voltage support) to the main grid.

*(Award 2 marks for block diagram, 2 marks for CHP, 3 marks for microgrid.)*

**(b)** A micro gas turbine delivers 65 kW electrical power at an electrical efficiency of 28 %. The exhaust heat is recovered in a CHP exchanger producing 110 kW of useful thermal energy. Calculate: (i) Fuel input power (based on calorific value), (ii) Total (CHP) efficiency, and (iii) Fuel energy saving compared with separate production of 65 kW electric (grid at 35 % efficiency) and 110 kW heat (boiler at 80 % efficiency).

**(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Fuel input power:**

$$
\eta_{el} = \frac{P_{el}}{\dot{Q}_{fuel}} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \dot{Q}_{fuel} = \frac{P_{el}}{\eta_{el}} = \frac{65}{0.28} = 232.1 \;\; \text{kW}
$$

**Step 2 — Total CHP efficiency:**

$$
\eta_{total} = \frac{P_{el} + Q_{thermal}}{\dot{Q}_{fuel}} = \frac{65 + 110}{232.1} = \frac{175}{232.1} = 0.754 \;\;(\approx 75.4\%)
$$

**Step 3 — Separate production reference:**

Fuel for grid electricity alone:

$$
\dot{Q}_{grid} = \frac{65}{0.35} = 185.7 \;\; \text{kW}
$$

Fuel for boiler alone:

$$
\dot{Q}_{boiler} = \frac{110}{0.80} = 137.5 \;\; \text{kW}
$$

Total separate fuel:

$$
\dot{Q}_{separate} = 185.7 + 137.5 = 323.2 \;\; \text{kW}
$$

**Step 4 — Fuel saving:**

$$
\text{Saving} = \frac{323.2 - 232.1}{323.2} \times 100\% = \frac{91.1}{323.2} \times 100\% \approx 28.2\%
$$

**Conclusion:** The micro-turbine CHP system saves approximately **28 %** of the fuel energy that would otherwise be consumed in separate generation.

*(Award 2 marks for (i), 2 marks for (ii), 3 marks for (iii).)*

---

### 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert °C → K** before substitution into the Brayton efficiency formula. KTU examiners deduct **1 full mark** per error.
> 2. **Using $\gamma = 1.4$ for combustion gases** (correct: $\gamma_g = 1.33$, $c_p = 1.148$ kJ/kg·K). Mixing these is a **favourite trap**.
> 3. **Confusing net work $W_{net}$ with turbine work $W_t$** — they are not equal. Many students write $W_{net} = W_t$ and lose **2 marks**.
> 4. **Misapplying recuperator effectiveness**: writing $T_{05} = T_{02} + \varepsilon(T_{04} - T_{01})$ instead of the correct $T_{05} = T_{02} + \varepsilon(T_{04} - T_{02})$. Both look similar but yield very different answers.
> 5. **Skipping units in the final answer.** Always state **kW** or **kJ/kg** explicitly.
> 6. **Omitting part (a) when answering part (b)** — even a labeled diagram in (a) earns **partial marks** when (b) is incomplete.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Micro Gas Turbine (MGT):** 30–500 kW, Brayton cycle, low pressure ratio (3–4.5), high shaft speed (50,000–100,000 rpm).
- ✅ **Always recuperated** in commercial designs; recuperator nearly **doubles** electrical efficiency.
- ✅ **Smart Energy System** features: bidirectional grid tie, demand response, CHP/CCHP, fuel flexibility, modular N+1 redundancy.
- ✅ **Key temperatures:** TIT ≈ 1100 °C; exhaust ≈ 280 °C (ideal for absorption chilling).
- ✅ **Key efficiencies:** $\eta_{el} = 25$–$35$ %; $\eta_{total} = 75$–$90$ %.
- ✅ **Brayton efficiency limit:** $\eta_{th} = 1 - 1/r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$ — depends only on $r_p$ at high TIT.
- ✅ **Recuperator effectiveness relation:** $T_{05} = T_{02} + \varepsilon(T_{04} - T_{02})$.
- ✅ **Net work:** $W_{net} = c_p[(T_{03} - T_{04}) - (T_{02} - T_{01})]$.
- ✅ **CHP advantage:** ~25–30 % fuel saving vs. separate generation + boiler.
- ✅ **Power electronics chain:** PMSG → Rectifier → DC link → Inverter → Grid.
- ✅ **Microgrid role:** Firm, dispatchable generator complementing solar PV, wind, and BESS.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Jet Propulsion - Cycles and Analysis - Gas Turbine Engines
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMET741 - GAS TURBINE AND JET PROPULSION > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Jet Propulsion - Cycles and Analysis - Gas Turbine Engines --- # 🚀 Jet Propulsion — Cycles and Analysis of Gas Turbine Engines > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor (PEMET741 / Module 4):** This module unifies the thermodynamic Brayton cycle with real propulsion systems. The KTU examiner expects students to confidently derive **Thrust (F), Specific Thrust, Air Specific Fuel Consumption (ASFC), Thermal Efficiency, Propulsive Efficiency, and Overall Efficiency** for turbojet, turboprop, turbofan, ramjet, and pulse-jet configurations, along with cycle modifications (reheat, intercooling, regeneration). --- ## 1.1 Formal Definition — Jet Propulsion & Gas Turbine Engines > [!IMPORTANT] > **Jet Propulsion** is a method of producing thrust by accelerating a mass of fluid (the working medium — usually atmospheric air) in the direction opposite to that of the desired motion, in accordance with **Newton's Second and Third Laws of Motion**. A **Gas Turbine Engine** is an internal combustion heat engine that operates on the **Brayton (Joule) cycle**, in which atmospheric air is compressed, heated at (nearly) constant pressure by burning fuel, and then expanded through a turbine. Part of the turbine work drives the compressor, and the remaining work is used either to produce a high-velocity jet (thrust) or to drive a propeller. The general energy balance for any propulsion system is: $$\text{Chemical Energy of Fuel} = \text{Kinetic Energy at Nozzle Exit} - \text{Kinetic Energy at Inlet} + \text{Heat Lost} + \text{Drives Losses}$$ --- ## 1.2 Classification of Jet Propulsion Engines (KTU Board Perspective) | Family | Sub-type | Source of Thrust | Speed Regime | |---|---|---|---| | **Air-breathing (Atmospheric)** | Turbojet, Turbofan, Turboprop, Turboshaft, Ramjet, Pulse-jet, Scramjet | Reaction of accelerated exhaust gases (Turbofan/Turboprop also use propeller) | Subsonic → Hypersonic | | **Non-air-breathing (Rocket)** | Liquid, Solid, Hybrid Propellant | Reaction of self-contained exhaust gases | Space / very high altitude | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Board Tip:** Module 4 of PEMET741 focuses heavily on **air-breathing** engines. Rockets appear only for conceptual comparison — do **not** spend board-time memorising rocket nozzle equations. --- ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Straw and Balloon" Engine Imagine an inflated balloon with the mouth open and taped to a straw. When you release it: - Air rushes out the back of the balloon (high-velocity exhaust). - The balloon shoots forward in the opposite direction — **this is thrust**. A **jet engine** does the same thing continuously: 1. It **sucks in** air (the diffuser/ram effect). 2. It **compresses** that air (compressor). 3. It **heats** the air enormously by burning fuel (combustion chamber). 4. It **shoots** the hot air out the back at huge velocity through a nozzle. The reaction force of that exiting jet **pushes the aircraft forward**. The faster the jet is ejected, the more thrust you get — but you also burn more fuel. **Module 4 is fundamentally about managing this trade-off.** --- ## 1.4 The Ideal Brayton Cycle — Foundation of All Gas Turbine Analysis > [!IMPORTANT] > Every gas turbine cycle in Module 4 is a modification of the **Ideal Brayton (Joule) Cycle**, consisting of four reversible processes: > > 1 → 2: **Isentropic compression** in the diffuser + compressor (pressure rises from $p_1$ to $p_2$). > 2 → 3: **Constant-pressure heat addition** in the combustion chamber. > 3 → 4: **Isentropic expansion** in the turbine + nozzle. > 4 → 1: **Constant-pressure heat rejection** to the atmosphere. The cycle is fully defined by two parameters: - **Pressure ratio**, $r_p = p_2/p_1$ - **Turbine inlet (max) temperature**, $T_3$ These two parameters dictate *every* performance metric — thrust, efficiency, fuel consumption — and are the levers the KTU examiner loves to manipulate in numerical problems. --- --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Four Engine Performance Pillars Every gas turbine propulsion engine is evaluated on four inter-locking metrics. KTU questions almost always require you to compute **all four** for a given engine. ### 2.1.1 Thrust (F) The net axial force pushing the aircraft forward. For a turbojet accelerating air from inlet velocity $V_a$ to exit velocity $V_j$: $$F = \dot{m}_a (V_j - V_a)$$ where: - $\dot{m}_a$ = mass flow rate of air (kg/s) - $V_a$ = aircraft (flight) velocity (m/s) - $V_j$ = jet (exit) velocity (m/s) If fuel mass $\dot{m}_f$ is added: $$F = (\dot{m}_a + \dot{m}_f) V_j - \dot{m}_a V_a$$ ### 2.1.2 Specific Thrust Thrust per unit mass flow of air — a *normalised* measure of engine "muscle": $$F_s = \frac{F}{\dot{m}_a} = (1 + f) V_j - V_a \approx V_j - V_a$$ where $f = \dot{m}_f / \dot{m}_a$ is the **fuel–air ratio** (small for turbojet, typically 0.01–0.02). ### 2.1.3 Air-Specific Fuel Consumption (ASFC) The mass of fuel burned per unit thrust per unit time — *lower is better*: $$\text{ASFC} = \frac{\dot{m}_f}{F} = \frac{f}{F_s} \quad \text{[kg/(N·s)]}$$ The reciprocal $\frac{1}{\text{ASFC}}$ is called **Specific Impulse, $I_s$** — common in rocket literature but the KTU examiner uses ASFC. ### 2.1.4 The Three Efficiencies (MUST memorise the *triangle*) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Propulsive Efficiency (η_p):** How effectively the jet's kinetic energy becomes useful propulsive work. > > **Thermal Efficiency (η_th):** How well fuel's chemical energy converts to jet kinetic energy. > > **Overall Efficiency (η_o):** How well fuel's chemical energy becomes useful propulsive work. > > **Master relation (often the first line of any 14-mark KTU answer):** > $$\eta_o = \eta_{th} \times \eta_p$$ --- ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet — Master Reference Table > [!IMPORTANT] > **Notation used:** $\gamma = c_p/c_v$, $c_p$ = specific heat of air (≈ **1004 J/kg·K**), $V_a$ = aircraft velocity, $V_j$ = jet velocity, $V_{rel} = V_j/V_a$, $r_p$ = compressor pressure ratio, $T_{01}$, $T_{02}$, $T_{03}$, $T_{04}$ = stagnation (total) temperatures at stations 1, 2, 3, 4. | # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Compressor work (ideal) | $w_c = c_p (T_{02} - T_{01})$ | J/kg of air | | 2 | Turbine work (ideal) | $w_T = c_p (T_{03} - T_{04})$ | J/kg of air | | 3 | Net specific work (turboprop) | $w_{net} = w_T - w_c$ | J/kg — drives propeller | | 4 | Specific thrust (turbojet) | $F_s = V_j - V_a$ | N/(kg/s) | | 5 | Jet velocity (ideal) | $V_j = \sqrt{2 c_p (T_{04} - T_{05})}$ | m/s | | 6 | Fuel–air ratio | $f = \dfrac{c_p (T_{03} - T_{02})}{η_b \cdot \text{CV}}$ | CV ≈ 43,100 kJ/kg (jet-A) | | 7 | Thermal efficiency | $\eta_{th} = \dfrac{V_j^{2} - V_a^{2}}{2 f \cdot \text{CV}}$ | Dimensionless | | 8 | Propulsive efficiency | $\eta_p = \dfrac{2 V_a}{V_j + V_a} = \dfrac{2}{1 + V_{rel}}$ | Maximum when $V_j = V_a$ | | 9 | Overall efficiency | $\eta_o = \eta_{th} \cdot \eta_p = \dfrac{V_a (V_j - V_a)}{f \cdot \text{CV}}$ | Maximum at $V_j = 2 V_a$ | | 10 | ASFC | $\text{ASFC} = \dfrac{f}{F_s}$ | kg/(N·s) | | 11 | Stagnation temperature rise in compressor | $T_{02}/T_{01} = r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$ | Ideal isentropic | | 12 | Optimum pressure ratio for turbojet η_o | $r_{p,opt} = \left( \dfrac{\gamma-1}{\gamma} \cdot \dfrac{T_{03}}{T_{01}} \cdot \dfrac{V_a^{2}}{2 c_p T_{01}} \right)^{-\gamma/(2(\gamma-1))}$ | Maximises η_o for fixed $T_{03}$ | | 13 | Ram effect temperature rise | $T_{01} = T_a + \dfrac{V_a^{2}}{2 c_p}$ | Flight-altitude ram | | 14 | Thrust of turbofan (bypass) | $F = \dot{m}_{core} V_{j,core} + \dot{m}_{bypass} V_{j,bypass} - (\dot{m}_{core}+\dot{m}_{bypass}) V_a$ | Bypass ratio μ = $\dot{m}_{bypass}/\dot{m}_{core}$ | > [!WARNING] > **Critical pitfall — Bypass ratio confusion:** KTU answers often mix $\dot{m}_a$ (total inlet air) with $\dot{m}_{core}$ (only the air entering the core). Always declare which one you are using at the top of your answer. --- ## 2.3 Engine-by-Engine Theoretical Highlights ### 2.3.1 Turbojet (The "Pure Jet") - All air goes through the core: **compressor → combustor → turbine → nozzle**. - Turbine extracts *just enough* work to drive the compressor; the **remainder is converted into jet kinetic energy** in the exhaust nozzle. - Highest specific thrust among turbo-engines; lowest propulsive efficiency at low $V_a$. ### 2.3.2 Turbofan (Bypass Engine — Modern Civil Aviation) - Splits the inlet air into **core flow** (through gas generator) and **bypass flow** (around the engine, accelerated by a separate fan). - **Bypass ratio**, $\mu = \dot{m}_b / \dot{m}_c$ — modern high-bypass turbofans: $\mu = 8$–$11$. - **Lower jet velocity** $V_j$ → higher propulsive efficiency → quieter, more fuel-efficient at subsonic cruise. ### 2.3.3 Turboprop (Propeller + Gas Generator) - Turbine extracts *more* work than needed for the compressor — excess work **drives a propeller**. - Best efficiency at **low speeds (300–600 km/h)**; poor above ~800 km/h (propeller tips reach transonic). - Propulsive efficiency is much higher than pure turbojet because the propeller accelerates a large mass of air by a small amount. ### 2.3.4 Ramjet - **No moving parts**: no compressor, no turbine. Forward motion itself compresses the air (ram compression). - Cannot produce thrust at zero velocity — **must be launched at Mach ≈ 0.5** by another aircraft. - Best suited for **Mach 2–4** sustained flight (missiles, reconnaissance). ### 2.3.5 Pulse-Jet - Has no compressor/turbine but uses **shock waves and intake valves** for intermittent compression. - Famous from the **V-1 "Buzz Bomb"** of WWII; very noisy; low efficiency. --- ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility > [!NOTE] > The Brayton cycle with reheat/intercooling/regeneration isn't just academic. Real engines like the **GE9X (turbofan)** use 10-stage compressors, TET = 1627 K, and pressure ratios of 27:1. The **Pratt & Whitney PW1000G** geared turbofan uses a reduction gearbox to let the fan and LP turbine spin at *different* speeds — a direct practical embodiment of the "turboprop principle inside a turbofan." > > **Bypass ratio** is the single most important civil-engine design choice: doubling μ cuts specific fuel consumption by ~25% at cruise but reduces specific thrust, requiring a larger fan. This is the central engineering trade-off KTU expects you to recognise. --- --- # 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations ## 3.1 Derivation — Specific Thrust of an Ideal Turbojet **Starting Point:** Control volume around the entire engine, mass and momentum balance. **Step 1 — Mass Conservation.** In steady flow, mass entering = mass leaving + fuel added: $$\dot{m}_a + \dot{m}_f = (\dot{m}_a + \dot{m}_f)$$ The fuel is a small perturbation: $\dot{m}_f \ll \dot{m}_a$. **Step 2 — Momentum Equation (Newton's 2nd Law).** For steady flow along the flight axis: $$F = \dot{m}_{out} V_{out} - \dot{m}_{in} V_{in} = (\dot{m}_a + \dot{m}_f) V_j - \dot{m}_a V_a$$ **Step 3 — Divide by $\dot{m}_a$ (to get specific form):** $$F_s = \frac{F}{\dot{m}_a} = (1 + f) V_j - V_a$$ **Step 4 — Assume $f \ll 1$ (typical for turbojet, f ≈ 0.018):** $$\boxed{F_s \approx V_j - V_a}$$ > **Why this matters:** KTU questions ask "specific thrust" — you must show this derivation. Don't skip the **approximation step** (1+f)→1; the examiner awards partial credit for recognising and justifying it. --- ## 3.2 Derivation — Propulsive Efficiency (η_p) **Definition:** Propulsive efficiency is the ratio of *useful propulsive power* (thrust × aircraft velocity) to the *total kinetic power added to the air stream* by the engine. $$\eta_p = \frac{\text{Useful Power Output}}{\text{Power Input to Air Stream}} = \frac{F \cdot V_a}{\tfrac{1}{2}\dot{m}_a(V_j^2 - V_a^2)}$$ **Substitute** $F = \dot{m}_a(V_j - V_a)$: $$\eta_p = \frac{\dot{m}_a (V_j - V_a) V_a}{\tfrac{1}{2}\dot{m}_a (V_j - V_a)(V_j + V_a)}$$ Cancel $\dot{m}_a(V_j - V_a)$: $$\eta_p = \frac{2 V_a}{V_j + V_a}$$ **Introduce velocity ratio** $V_{rel} = V_j/V_a$: $$\boxed{\eta_p = \frac{2}{1 + V_{rel}} = \frac{2}{1 + (V_j/V_a)}}$$ **Physical interpretation:** - If $V_j \to V_a$: $\eta_p \to 1$ (all kinetic energy goes to propulsion — ideal). - If $V_j \gg V_a$: $\eta_p \to 0$ (most energy is wasted in the jet wake). > **KTU Examiner's Marker:** Many students write $\eta_p = V_a/V_j$. **That is wrong.** The correct form contains the factor of 2 in the numerator (it comes from the kinetic-energy definition $\tfrac{1}{2}mv^2$, not $mv^2$). Always start from $F \cdot V_a$ in the numerator. --- ## 3.3 Derivation — Thermal Efficiency (η_th) **Definition:** Fraction of fuel energy that appears as *kinetic energy of the jet*: $$\eta_{th} = \frac{\text{Kinetic Energy of Jet per kg Air} - \text{Kinetic Energy of Inlet Air per kg Air}}{\text{Heat Released per kg Air}}$$ $$\eta_{th} = \frac{\tfrac{1}{2}(V_j^2 - V_a^2)}{f \cdot \text{CV}}$$ Using $c_p(T_{04} - T_{01}) = \tfrac{1}{2}(V_j^2 - V_a^2)$ (from energy conservation across the engine): $$\boxed{\eta_{th} = \frac{c_p(T_{04} - T_{01})}{f \cdot \text{CV}} = \frac{V_j^2 - V_a^2}{2 f \cdot \text{CV}}}$$ --- ## 3.4 Derivation — Overall Efficiency (η_o) and the *Turbojet Miracle* **Definition:** Fraction of fuel energy that becomes *useful propulsive power*: $$\eta_o = \frac{F \cdot V_a}{\dot{m}_f \cdot \text{CV}} = \frac{\dot{m}_a (V_j - V_a) V_a}{\dot{m}_f \cdot \text{CV}} = \frac{(V_j - V_a) V_a}{f \cdot \text{CV}}$$ **Verify the master identity:** $$\eta_o = \eta_{th} \times \eta_p = \frac{V_j^2 - V_a^2}{2 f \cdot \text{CV}} \cdot \frac{2 V_a}{V_j + V_a}$$ $$= \frac{(V_j - V_a)(V_j + V_a)}{f \cdot \text{CV}} \cdot \frac{V_a}{V_j + V_a} = \frac{(V_j - V_a) V_a}{f \cdot \text{CV}}$$ $$\boxed{\therefore\;\eta_o = \eta_{th}\cdot \eta_p = \frac{(V_j - V_a) V_a}{f \cdot \text{CV}}}$$ --- ## 3.5 Derivation — Optimum Jet Velocity for Maximum Overall Efficiency **Step 1.** Express $\eta_o$ purely in terms of $V_j$ (treating $V_a$ and $f$ CV as constants): $$\eta_o = \frac{(V_j - V_a) V_a}{f \cdot \text{CV}}$$ **Step 2.** Maximum requires $\dfrac{d\eta_o}{dV_j} = 0$: $$\frac{d}{dV_j}\left[\frac{V_j V_a - V_a^2}{f \cdot \text{CV}}\right] = \frac{V_a}{f \cdot \text{CV}} = 0\;\; \text{(impossible at fixed } V_a)$$ > **Important Correction:** When $V_j$ is varied, the fuel-air ratio $f$ is **not** independent — $f$ depends on the thermodynamic state at turbine inlet, which depends on $V_j$ through the expansion process. **Step 3.** With $V_j$ coupled to cycle temperatures, set $\partial \eta_o / \partial r_p = 0$ (Rayleigh/Hill approach) to obtain the **optimum pressure ratio**: $$\boxed{r_{p,opt} = \left[\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}\cdot\frac{T_{03}}{T_{01}}\cdot\frac{V_a^{2}}{2 c_p T_{01}}\right]^{\gamma/(2(\gamma-1))}}$$ Equivalently, maximum $\eta_o$ occurs when the jet velocity satisfies: $$V_j^{opt} = 2 V_a$$ > **Physical meaning — the "Turbojet Miracle":** An ideal turbojet achieves *maximum* overall efficiency when its exhaust velocity equals **twice** the flight velocity. This is why aircraft cruise at speeds where $V_j \approx 2 V_a$. Below this ratio, the engine wastes too much energy as heat; above it, the jet blows past the aircraft uselessly. --- ## 3.6 Derivation — Turboprop Overall Efficiency For a turboprop, the *useful work output* is the **shaft work to the propeller** *plus* the small direct jet thrust. $$\eta_{o,\,turboprop} = \frac{w_{net}\cdot \eta_{propeller} + F_s V_a \cdot (1-\text{fraction})}{f \cdot \text{CV}}$$ where $\eta_{propeller}$ is the propeller's mechanical efficiency (~0.85). The defining feature is that **propulsive efficiency is high** because the propeller accelerates a large mass by a small amount (high $\dot{m}_a$, low $\Delta V$). --- ## 3.7 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style 14-Mark Format) > [!NOTE] > **Problem:** A turbojet aircraft flies at $V_a = 200$ m/s. Compressor pressure ratio $r_p = 6$, turbine inlet temperature $T_{03} = 1200$ K. Ambient $T_{01} = 300$ K, $\gamma = 1.4$, $c_p = 1004$ J/kg·K, CV = 43,100 kJ/kg. Combustion efficiency = 100%. Find: (a) compressor work, turbine work, jet velocity, fuel-air ratio, and (b) thrust, ASFC, $\eta_p$, $\eta_{th}$, $\eta_o$. Comment on the optimum. **Part (a):** **Step 1.** Compressor exit stagnation temperature: $$T_{02} = T_{01}\cdot r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} = 300 \times 6^{0.4/1.4}$$ $$= 300 \times 6^{0.2857} = 300 \times 1.6685 = 500.55\;\text{K}$$ **Step 2.** Turbine work must equal compressor work (single-spool ideal): $$w_T = w_c = c_p (T_{02} - T_{01}) = 1004 \times (500.55 - 300) = 1004 \times 200.55 = 201{,}352\;\text{J/kg}$$ **Step 3.** Turbine exit temperature: $$T_{04} = T_{03} - \frac{w_T}{c_p} = 1200 - \frac{201{,}352}{1004} = 1200 - 200.55 = 999.45\;\text{K}$$ **Step 4.** Heat added (fuel-air ratio × calorific value): $$f \cdot \text{CV} = c_p(T_{03} - T_{02}) = 1004 \times (1200 - 500.55) = 1004 \times 699.45 = 702{,}288\;\text{J/kg}$$ $$f = \frac{702{,}288}{43{,}100{,}000} = 0.01629$$ **Step 5.** Jet velocity (expand from $T_{04}$ to ambient $T_1$): $$V_j = \sqrt{2 c_p (T_{04} - T_1)} = \sqrt{2 \times 1004 \times (999.45 - 300)}$$ $$= \sqrt{2 \times 1004 \times 699.45} = \sqrt{1{,}404{,}575.6} = 1185.2\;\text{m/s}$$ **Part (b):** **Step 6.** Specific thrust: $$F_s = V_j - V_a = 1185.2 - 200 = 985.2\;\text{N per kg/s}$$ **Step 7.** ASFC: $$\text{ASFC} = \frac{f}{F_s} = \frac{0.01629}{985.2} = 1.654 \times 10^{-5}\;\text{kg/(N·s)}$$ Or equivalently **59.5 mg/(N·s)** — typical of real turbojets. **Step 8.** Propulsive efficiency: $$\eta_p = \frac{2 V_a}{V_j + V_a} = \frac{2 \times 200}{1185.2 + 200} = \frac{400}{1385.2} = 0.2888 \;\; (28.88\%)$$ **Step 9.** Thermal efficiency: $$\eta_{th} = \frac{V_j^2 - V_a^2}{2 f \cdot \text{CV}} = \frac{1185.2^2 - 200^2}{2 \times 702{,}288}$$ $$= \frac{1{,}404{,}691 - 40{,}000}{1{,}404{,}576} = \frac{1{,}364{,}691}{1{,}404{,}576} = 0.9715 \;\; (97.15\%)$$ **Step 10.** Overall efficiency: $$\eta_o = \eta_{th}\cdot \eta_p = 0.9715 \times 0.2888 = 0.2806\;\; (28.06\%)$$ Or directly: $$\eta_o = \frac{F_s \cdot V_a}{f \cdot \text{CV}} = \frac{985.2 \times 200}{702{,}288} = 0.2806$$ **Comment on optimum:** $$V_j^{opt} = 2 V_a = 400\;\text{m/s}$$ Our computed $V_j = 1185$ m/s is **far above** optimum → engine is over-expanding, losing efficiency in the wake. **Conclusion:** This pressure ratio is far too high for the chosen cruise speed; reducing $r_p$ to ~2.5 would yield near-maximum $\eta_o$. --- --- # 4.
