KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, May 2024]` > **Differentiate between propulsive efficiency and thermal efficiency of a turbojet engine. Show that the overall efficiency is the product of these two.** **Model Answer:** Propulsive efficiency $\eta_p$ measures how effectively the kinetic energy of the exhaust jet is converted into useful propulsive work: $$\eta_p = \frac{2 V_a}{V_j + V_a}$$ Thermal efficiency $\eta_{th}$ measures how well the chemical energy of the fuel is converted into kinetic energy of the jet stream: $$\eta_{th} = \frac{V_j^{2} - V_a^{2}}{2\, f\, \text{CV}}$$ The overall efficiency $\eta_o$ represents the fraction of the fuel's chemical energy that becomes useful thrust work: $$\eta_o = \frac{F\, V_a}{\dot{m}_f\, \text{CV}} = \frac{(V_j - V_a) V_a}{f\, \text{CV}}$$ Multiplying the two expressions: $$\eta_{th}\cdot \eta_p = \frac{V_j^{2} - V_a^{2}}{2 f\,\text{CV}}\cdot \frac{2 V_a}{V_j + V_a} = \frac{(V_j - V_a) V_a}{f\,\text{CV}} = \eta_o$$ $$\boxed{\eta_o = \eta_{th} \cdot \eta_p}$$ --- ### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` > **Explain why the optimum jet velocity for maximum overall efficiency of a turbojet equals twice the aircraft velocity.** **Model Answer:** The overall efficiency of a turbojet is: $$\eta_o = \frac{(V_j - V_a) V_a}{f\, \text{CV}}$$ As $V_j$ increases, the **fuel–air ratio** $f$ also increases (because more energy must be supplied to accelerate a larger amount of exhaust to a higher velocity). Substituting the energy balance $f \cdot \text{CV} = \tfrac{1}{2}(V_j^{2} - V_a^{2})$ yields: $$\eta_o = \frac{2 V_a (V_j - V_a)}{V_j^{2} - V_a^{2}} = \frac{2 V_a}{V_j + V_a}$$ Differentiating with respect to $V_j$ and setting $\dfrac{d\eta_o}{dV_j}=0$: $$\frac{d\eta_o}{dV_j} = \frac{-2 V_a}{(V_j + V_a)^{2}} = 0 \quad \text{(gives a trivial minimum)}$$ The correct optimisation requires a coupled variation of $V_j$ with cycle parameters. For a fixed turbine inlet temperature, the optimum pressure ratio yields $V_j^{opt} = 2 V_a$. Physically, this means **the jet should exit at twice the aircraft's forward speed** — fast enough to produce useful thrust, yet slow enough to minimise wake energy losses. **(3 Marks)** --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer (14 Marks) — **Choice A** ### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]` > **(a)** Derive an expression for the **propulsive efficiency** of a turbojet engine in terms of jet velocity $V_j$ and aircraft velocity $V_a$. Plot its variation with $V_j/V_a$ and comment on the result. **(7 Marks)** > > **(b)** An aircraft is powered by a turbojet engine flying at a velocity of $240$ m/s. The jet velocity at the nozzle exit is $480$ m/s and the fuel-air ratio is $0.018$. Calorific value of fuel = 43,100 kJ/kg. Calculate: **(i)** Specific thrust, **(ii)** Propulsive efficiency, **(iii)** Thermal efficiency, **(iv)** Overall efficiency, and **(v)** Air-specific fuel consumption. **(7 Marks)** --- ### **Solution to Question A (a):** **Step 1.** Propulsive power (useful output): $P_{useful} = F \cdot V_a$ **Step 2.** Thrust from Newton's 2nd law for control volume: $F = \dot{m}_a (V_j - V_a)$ **Step 3.** Total kinetic power added to the airstream: $P_{jet} = \tfrac{1}{2}\dot{m}_a(V_j^{2} - V_a^{2})$ **Step 4.** Ratio gives propulsive efficiency: $$\eta_p = \frac{\dot{m}_a(V_j - V_a) V_a}{\tfrac{1}{2}\dot{m}_a (V_j^{2} - V_a^{2})} = \frac{2 V_a}{V_j + V_a}$$ **Step 5.** In terms of velocity ratio $\lambda = V_j/V_a$: $$\boxed{\eta_p = \frac{2}{1 + \lambda}}$$ **Plot description (verbal, for KTU answer sheets):** - At $V_j = V_a$ (i.e., $\lambda = 1$): $\eta_p = 1$ (100%) — no wake losses, but thrust is zero. - At $V_j = 2 V_a$ ($\lambda = 2$): $\eta_p = 2/3$ (66.7%). - At $V_j = 3 V_a$ ($\lambda = 3$): $\eta_p = 1/2$ (50%). - As $V_j \to \infty$: $\eta_p \to 0$. **Conclusion:** Propulsive efficiency **decreases monotonically** as jet velocity exceeds flight velocity. To balance thrust and efficiency, jet engines are designed so that $V_j \approx 2 V_a$ in cruise. --- ### **Solution to Question A (b):** Given: $V_a = 240$ m/s, $V_j = 480$ m/s, $f = 0.018$, CV = 43,100 kJ/kg = 43.1 MJ/kg. **(i) Specific thrust:** $$F_s = V_j - V_a = 480 - 240 = \boxed{240\;\text{N per kg/s of air}}$$ **(ii) Propulsive efficiency:** $$\eta_p = \frac{2 V_a}{V_j + V_a} = \frac{2 \times 240}{480 + 240} = \frac{480}{720} = \boxed{0.6667\;(66.67\%)}$$ **(iii) Thermal efficiency:** $$\eta_{th} = \frac{V_j^{2} - V_a^{2}}{2 f \cdot \text{CV}} = \frac{480^{2} - 240^{2}}{2 \times 0.018 \times 43{,}100{,}000}$$ $$= \frac{230{,}400 - 57{,}600}{1{,}551{,}600} = \frac{172{,}800}{1{,}551{,}600} = \boxed{0.1114\;(11.14\%)}$$ **(iv) Overall efficiency:** $$\eta_o = \eta_{th}\cdot \eta_p = 0.1114 \times 0.6667 = \boxed{0.0742\;(7.42\%)}$$ Cross-check: $\eta_o = \dfrac{(V_j - V_a) V_a}{f \cdot \text{CV}} = \dfrac{240 \times 240}{0.018 \times 43{,}100{,}000} = \dfrac{57{,}600}{775{,}800} = 0.0742$ ✓ **(v) Air-specific fuel consumption:** $$\text{ASFC} = \frac{f}{F_s} = \frac{0.018}{240} = 7.5 \times 10^{-5}\;\text{kg/(N·s)}$$ Or: $\text{ASFC} = 7.5 \times 10^{-5} \times 3600 = 0.27\;\text{kg/(N·h)}$ $$\boxed{\text{ASFC} = 0.27\;\text{kg/(N·h)}}$$ > [!WARNING] > **Examiner Pitfall — Unit Confusion:** KTU students frequently write ASFC in `kg/s` instead of `kg/(N·s)`. The "N" is essential — without it, you cannot compare engines. Always include units in your final answer. --- ## 5.3 Part B — Long Answer (14 Marks) — **Choice B** ### **Question B** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, July 2023]` > **(a)** With the aid of a **block diagram** and **T–s diagram**, describe the working principle of a turbojet engine. Explain how thrust is produced in such an engine. **(7 Marks)** > > **(b)** A turbojet engine operates with a compressor pressure ratio of 8 and turbine inlet temperature of 1400 K. The ambient temperature is 290 K. Assuming ideal Brayton cycle with $\gamma = 1.4$ and $c_p = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K, find: **(i)** Compressor exit temperature, **(ii)** Turbine exit temperature, **(iii)** Jet velocity (assuming expansion to ambient pressure), **(iv)** Thermal efficiency, **(v)** Overall efficiency at flight velocity 250 m/s. **(7 Marks)** --- ### **Solution to Question B (a):** **Block Diagram (describe verbally for answer sheet):** > Intake → Compressor → Combustion Chamber → Turbine → Exhaust Nozzle → Atmosphere. **Working Principle:** 1. **Intake / Diffuser (1→2):** Ram air slows down from flight velocity to near-stagnation; static pressure rises from $p_1$ to $p_2$ (isentropic diffusion). 2. **Compressor (2→2'):** Axial or centrifugal compressor raises the pressure further to $p_3 = r_p \cdot p_2$ using shaft work from the turbine. 3. **Combustion Chamber (3→4):** Fuel is sprayed and burned at (nearly) constant pressure, raising temperature from $T_3$ to the maximum cycle temperature $T_4$ (the **Turbine Inlet Temperature, TIT**). 4. **Turbine (4→5):** Hot gases expand through the turbine, which extracts just enough work to drive the compressor. The remaining energy stays in the gas as enthalpy. 5. **Exhaust Nozzle (5→6):** The remaining enthalpy is converted into kinetic energy in a converging (or converging–diverging) nozzle, producing the high-velocity jet. **Thrust Production (Newton's 3rd Law):** The reaction to the rearward ejection of mass at velocity $V_j$ is an equal and opposite forward force on the engine and aircraft: $$F = \dot{m}_a (V_j - V_a)$$ The greater the **rate of change of momentum**, the greater the thrust. --- ### **Solution to Question B (b):** Given: $r_p = 8$, $T_{03} = 1400$ K, $T_{01} = 290$ K, $\gamma = 1.4$, $c_p = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K, $V_a = 250$ m/s. **(i) Compressor exit temperature:** $$T_{02} = T_{01} \cdot r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} = 290 \times 8^{0.2857} = 290 \times 1.8114 = 525.3\;\text{K}$$ **(ii) Turbine exit temperature:** Energy balance for ideal cycle (turbine work = compressor work): $$c_p (T_{03} - T_{04}) = c_p (T_{02} - T_{01})$$ $$T_{04} = T_{03} - (T_{02} - T_{01}) = 1400 - (525.3 - 290) = 1400 - 235.3 = 1164.7\;\text{K}$$ **(iii) Jet velocity:** $$V_j = \sqrt{2 c_p (T_{04} - T_{01})} = \sqrt{2 \times 1005 \times (1164.7 - 290)}$$ $$= \sqrt{2 \times 1005 \times 874.7} = \sqrt{1{,}758{,}147} = \boxed{1325.9\;\text{m/s}}$$ **(iv) Thermal efficiency:** For ideal Brayton: $$\eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{1}{r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}} = 1 - \frac{1}{8^{0.2857}} = 1 - \frac{1}{1.8114} = 1 - 0.5521 = 0.4479\;(44.79\%)$$ **(v) Overall efficiency:** $$\eta_p = \frac{2 V_a}{V_j + V_a} = \frac{2 \times 250}{1325.9 + 250} = \frac{500}{1575.9} = 0.3173\;(31.73\%)$$ $$\boxed{\eta_o = \eta_{th}\cdot \eta_p = 0.4479 \times 0.3173 = 0.1421\;(14.21\%)}$$ **Comment:** The high pressure ratio (8) gives good thermal efficiency but a very high $V_j = 1326$ m/s, leading to poor propulsive efficiency (31.7%). For $V_a = 250$ m/s, a lower pressure ratio (~2–3) would yield a higher $\eta_o$. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Module 4 Common Pitfalls** > > 1. **Mixing up static and stagnation temperatures.** In a moving aircraft, $T_{01} = T_a + V_a^2/(2 c_p)$. If the question says "ambient temperature = 290 K" without specifying whether it's static or stagnation, assume static (use the stagnation temperature only if explicitly stated or if ram effect is asked). > > 2. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in propulsive efficiency.** Writing $\eta_p = V_a/V_j$ instead of $2V_a/(V_j+V_a)$ is the single most common error — costs 2–3 marks per question. > > 3. **Not stating assumptions.** Always write "assuming ideal Brayton cycle, perfect combustion (η_b = 1), adiabatic compressor and turbine, and expansion to ambient pressure in the nozzle" at the start of any numerical answer. > > 4. **Unit confusion in ASFC.** Always include "N" in the denominator. > > 5. **Confusing turbojet, turbofan, turboprop formulas.** Each engine has a different formula for overall efficiency. The KTU examiner will deduct 2 marks if you use a turboprop formula for a turbojet problem. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **🚀 Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4 (Jet Propulsion Cycles & Analysis)** ### A. Core Equations (must be at your fingertips) - ☐ $F_s = V_j - V_a$ (specific thrust, turbojet) - ☐ $\eta_p = \dfrac{2 V_a}{V_j + V_a}$ - ☐ $\eta_{th} = \dfrac{V_j^{2} - V_a^{2}}{2 f \cdot \text{CV}}$ - ☐ $\eta_o = \eta_{th}\cdot \eta_p = \dfrac{(V_j - V_a) V_a}{f \cdot \text{CV}}$ - ☐ $\text{ASFC} = f / F_s$ - ☐ $V_j^{opt} = 2 V_a$ (max overall efficiency condition) ### B. Cycle-State Relations - ☐ $T_{02}/T_{01} = r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$ - ☐ $w_c = w_T = c_p(T_{02} - T_{01})$ — ideal Brayton - ☐ $f\cdot\text{CV} = c_p(T_{03} - T_{02})$ - ☐ $T_{04} = T_{03} - (T_{02} - T_{01})$ (ideal single-spool) ### C. Engine Family Recognition - ☐ **Turbojet:** all air through core; high $V_j$; used at supersonic. - ☐ **Turbofan:** split flow with bypass ratio μ; high $\eta_p$ at subsonic cruise. - ☐ **Turboprop:** gas generator drives propeller; best at low speed (<800 km/h). - ☐ **Ramjet:** no compressor; needs M ≥ 0.5 launch. - ☐ **Pulse-jet:** intermittent shock compression; very noisy. ### D. Numerical Problem-Solving Steps (always follow this order) 1. **List given data** with units. 2. **State assumptions** (ideal cycle, perfect combustion, etc.). 3. **Find compressor work** → $T_{02}$. 4. **Find turbine work** → $T_{04}$. 5. **Find fuel–air ratio** $f$. 6. **Find jet velocity** $V_j$. 7. **Compute** $F_s$, $\eta_p$, $\eta_{th}$, $\eta_o$, ASFC. 8. **Comment** on optimum $V_j = 2 V_a$. ### E. Common Traps - Don't forget **ram effect** when $V_a > 100$ m/s. - For turbofan, distinguish **core mass flow** vs **total mass flow**. - Afterburner (reheat) raises $V_j$ → more thrust but lower $\eta_{th}$. - Regeneration raises $\eta_{th}$ but adds weight and complexity. ---

topics.title: Turboprop
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMET741 - GAS TURBINE AND JET PROPULSION > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Turboprop # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Turboprop (Turbo-Propeller) Engine** — A gas turbine propulsion system in which a significant portion (typically **> 70 %**) of the turbine's gross output power is extracted as **shaft power** to drive an air-screw (propeller) through a reduction gear-box, while a comparatively small fraction of the residual energy is expelled as a high-velocity jet to produce auxiliary thrust. In KTU 2024-scheme nomenclature, a turboprop is classified as a **shaft-power turbo-engine** — sitting in the design spectrum between the pure **turbojet** (all thrust from exhaust jet) and the **turboshaft** (almost all power delivered as shaft work, negligible jet thrust, e.g., helicopter engines). The defining KTU highlight is the **dual-energy-extraction** philosophy: $$ P_{\text{turbine}} \;=\; P_{\text{shaft}} \;+\; P_{\text{jet exhaust}} $$ where the **reduction gear-box** (gear ratio typically **15 : 1** to **20 : 1**) steps down the high-speed turbine rotation (~**30 000 rpm**) to a propeller-shaft speed of ~**1 500 – 2 000 rpm** suitable for efficient propeller operation. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain-English Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Analogy — The Pedalled-Boat with a Small Water-Jet Boost:** > Imagine a pedalled boat (the propeller driven by your legs via bicycle chains = the **reduction gearbox**). Most of your effort turns the paddle-wheel to push the boat forward (this is **shaft power**). But you also fix a small hosepipe at the back that squirts water rearward — the recoil from this jet gives a tiny extra push. The boat moves mostly because of the paddle-wheel, with a small "jet-rocket" assist. That is exactly a **turboprop**: a gas turbine whose combustion gases drive a propeller (the dominant thrust source) and the leftover exhaust gases give a small extra push (the jet thrust). A real-world engineering analogy: a turboprop is to an aircraft what a **hybrid car** is to road transport — the dominant propulsion comes from one mode (propeller/screw), supplemented by another (residual jet). Cruise speeds: typically **400 – 800 km/h**, altitudes **≤ 9 000 m** — making turboprops the workhorses of regional aviation (ATR-72, Dash 8, C-130 Hercules, Il-76, Saab 340). ## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics | Metric | Typical Range / Value | |---|---| | Bypass ratio (propeller) | **15 : 1 to 40 : 1** (mass-flow through propeller vs. core) | | Reduction gear ratio | **15 : 1 – 20 : 1** | | Power split (shaft : jet) | **~ 90 : 10** (at cruise) | | Propeller tip-speed | **< 0.9 Mach** (to avoid compressibility loss) | | Propulsive efficiency | **0.80 – 0.85** (at design point) | | Overall efficiency | **0.30 – 0.40** | | Specific fuel consumption (SFC) | **0.30 – 0.45 kg/kWh** | > [!TIP] > **KTU 2024 Take-Away:** The propeller's contribution makes a turboprop the **most fuel-efficient turbo-engine** in the 400–800 km/h regime, because propeller propulsive efficiency is much higher than that of a pure jet at low flight speeds. # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Working Principle — Step-by-Step Logic The turboprop thermodynamic cycle is fundamentally a **Brayton (gas-turbine) cycle**, identical in core architecture to a turbojet, but with the **turbine deliberately over-sized** to extract additional work for the propeller. - **Step 1 — Air Induction:** Ambient air at station **0** enters the diffuser/inlet, decelerated and slightly pressurised to station **2**. For a subsonic turboprop, ram pressure rise is small. - **Step 2 — Compression:** The **axial-flow compressor** raises pressure from $p_2$ to $p_3$ (pressure ratio $\pi_c = p_3/p_2$, typically **8 – 15**). Compressor work is $W_C = \dot{m} \, c_{p,c}(T_3 - T_2)$. - **Step 3 — Combustion:** In the **combustion chamber** (station 3 → 4), fuel is injected at $\dot{m}_f$ with calorific value $CV$. Heat added: $Q_{in} = \dot{m}_f \, CV$. Combustion efficiency $\eta_b \approx 0.98$–$0.99$. - **Step 4 — Turbine Expansion (Dual Extraction):** Hot gases expand through the turbine in **two stages** (in some designs) or in a single multi-stage turbine: - **First-stage turbine** drives the **compressor** (work $W_C$). - **Second-stage / Free turbine** drives the **propeller** via the **reduction gear-box** (work $W_{PT}$ — propeller-turbine work). - **Step 5 — Propeller Thrust:** The reduction gear-box converts high-speed low-torque turbine output to low-speed high-torque propeller rotation. Propeller converts **shaft power** to **thrust** with efficiency $\eta_{pr}$. - **Step 6 — Residual Jet Exhaust:** The exhaust gas leaving the turbine (station 5) is still at moderate pressure and elevated temperature, accelerating through the **jet pipe / propelling nozzle** to velocity $V_j$ (≈ 350 – 500 m/s), producing residual jet thrust $F_j = \dot{m}_e (V_j - V_0)$. > **Engineering Insight — "Why the free turbine?"** > A two-spool (free-turbine) arrangement is preferred in modern turboprops because the propeller and compressor can then run at **independent speeds**, allowing the propeller to be feathered/optimised for varying flight conditions without disturbing compressor stability. ## 2.2 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Regional airline transport** (40–80 seats, 500–1500 km routes): ATR 72, Bombardier Dash 8 Q400. - **Military transport aircraft**: Lockheed C-130 Hercules, Transall C-160. - **Maritime patrol / surveillance**: Beechcraft King Air, P-3 Orion. - **Agribusiness / utility**: Thrush 510, Air Tractor (crop-dusting). The **high propulsive efficiency at low speed** is the singular engineering reason turboprops continue to dominate this niche — a turbojet of equivalent power would burn ~**30–40 % more fuel** at 600 km/h cruise. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield Equation Set — Memorise the symbols and the **physical meaning**, not just the algebra.** | # | Equation | Physical Meaning | Symbol Glossary | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $W_C = \dot{m} \, c_{p,c}(T_3 - T_2)$ | Compressor work per second | $\dot{m}$ = core mass-flow, $c_{p,c}$ = $c_{p,air}$ | | 2 | $Q_{in} = \dot{m}_f \, CV$ | Heat added from fuel | $CV \approx 43 \text{ MJ/kg}$ for jet-A | | 3 | $W_{PT} = \dot{m} \, c_{p,g}(T_4 - T_5)$ | Shaft work extracted by free turbine | $c_{p,g}$ = $c_{p,gas}$ ≈ 1.148 kJ/kg·K | | 4 | $W_C = \dot{m} \, c_{p,g}(T_4' - T_5')$ | Work to drive compressor (first turbine stage) | $T_4' = T_4$ if single-spool | | 5 | $F_{pr} = \dfrac{\eta_{pr} \, \dot{W}_{PT}}{V_0}$ | Propeller thrust from shaft power | $V_0$ = flight speed | | 6 | $F_j = \dot{m}_e (V_j - V_0)$ | Residual jet thrust | $\dot{m}_e = \dot{m} + \dot{m}_f$ | | 7 | $F_T = F_{pr} + F_j$ | **Total usable thrust** | | | 8 | $P_{eq} = \dot{W}_{PT} + F_j \, V_0$ | **Equivalent shaft power** | Sum of mechanical + jet power | | 9 | $\eta_{pr} = \dfrac{F_{pr} V_0}{\dot{W}_{PT}}$ | **Propeller efficiency** | | | 10 | $\eta_{th} = \dfrac{\dot{W}_{PT} + F_j V_0}{\dot{m}_f \, CV}$ | **Thermal efficiency** | Useful energy / fuel energy | | 11 | $\eta_{p} = \dfrac{F_T \, V_0}{\dot{W}_{PT} + F_j V_0} = \dfrac{F_T V_0}{P_{eq}}$ | **Propulsive efficiency** | Thrust power / total useful power | | 12 | $\eta_o = \eta_{th} \cdot \eta_p$ | **Overall efficiency** | Thrust power / fuel energy | | 13 | $SFC = \dfrac{\dot{m}_f}{P_{eq}} \; [\text{kg/kWs}]$ or $[\text{kg/kWh}]$ | **Specific fuel consumption** | Lower is better | | 14 | $\eta_{pr,\text{ideal}} = \dfrac{2}{1 + V_{\text{tip}}/V_0}$ | Ideal propeller efficiency (actuator disc) | $V_{\text{tip}}$ = propeller tip speed | > [!NOTE] > **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** The vertical bar in expressions like $\dot{m}_f$ (mass-flow of fuel) is the **subscript underscore**, not a table-pipe. In all rendered markdown tables, every absolute-value sign has been replaced with the LaTeX macro `\vert` to keep table syntax intact. ## 2.4 The "Why" Behind the Configuration - The propeller is essentially a **transmission device for momentum**: it imparts a small velocity increment to a *very large* mass of air (mass-flow = $\rho A V_0$). The kinetic-energy increase per kg is small, but the *integrated* thrust is large. - This contrasts with a turbojet, which imparts a *huge* velocity increment to a *small* mass of air. - The **momentum theory** (Rankine-Froude actuator disc) shows that the ideal propeller is twice as efficient as a pure jet at low $V_0$ — hence the turboprop's supremacy in this regime. # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation I — Propeller Thrust from Shaft Power **Statement:** Express the propeller thrust $F_{pr}$ in terms of shaft power $\dot{W}_{PT}$, propeller efficiency $\eta_{pr}$, and flight velocity $V_0$. **Step 1 — Definition of propeller efficiency.** By convention, propeller efficiency is the ratio of useful thrust power to the mechanical power delivered to the propeller shaft: $$ \eta_{pr} \;=\; \frac{\text{Thrust power}}{\text{Shaft power}} \;=\; \frac{F_{pr} \cdot V_0}{\dot{W}_{PT}} $$ **Step 2 — Cross-multiply to isolate $F_{pr}$.** $$ \eta_{pr} \cdot \dot{W}_{PT} \;=\; F_{pr} \cdot V_0 $$ **Step 3 — Solve for $F_{pr}$.** $$ F_{pr} \;=\; \frac{\eta_{pr} \, \dot{W}_{PT}}{V_0} $$ **Physical interpretation:** For a fixed shaft power, propeller thrust varies *inversely* with flight speed. This is why turboprops are inefficient at high $V_0$ (e.g., > 800 km/h) — thrust drops off, and residual jet thrust must be increased (degrading propulsive efficiency). --- ## 3.2 Derivation II — Total Thrust and Equivalent Shaft Power **Step 1 — Identify the two thrust sources.** Propeller thrust (from shaft) + residual jet thrust (from exhaust): $$ F_T \;=\; F_{pr} \;+\; F_j $$ **Step 2 — Substitute $F_{pr}$ from Derivation I and $F_j$ from Newton's law.** $$ F_T \;=\; \frac{\eta_{pr} \, \dot{W}_{PT}}{V_0} \;+\; \dot{m}_e (V_j - V_0) $$ **Step 3 — Define equivalent shaft power.** Multiplying the entire thrust equation by $V_0$ gives the **propulsive (thrust) power**: $$ F_T \cdot V_0 \;=\; \eta_{pr} \, \dot{W}_{PT} \;+\; \dot{m}_e (V_j - V_0) V_0 $$ The first term on the right, $\eta_{pr} \dot{W}_{PT}$, is the propeller's thrust power; the second, $\dot{m}_e (V_j - V_0) V_0 = F_j V_0$, is the jet's thrust power. Their sum is the **total useful power output**, called the **equivalent shaft power**: $$ \boxed{\,P_{eq} \;=\; \eta_{pr} \, \dot{W}_{PT} \;+\; F_j \, V_0 \;=\; \dot{W}_{PT,\text{ideal}} + F_j V_0\,} $$ In some KTU texts the equivalent shaft power is simply defined (without the $\eta_{pr}$ factor) as $P_{eq} = \dot{W}_{PT} + F_j V_0$, on the assumption that the gearbox-shaft power is fully converted (the propeller loss is then absorbed into the *propulsive* efficiency, not the *thermal* efficiency). **Both conventions appear in the KTU 2024 scheme — check the model-answer key in your module.** --- ## 3.3 Derivation III — Propulsive Efficiency, Thermal Efficiency, and Overall Efficiency **Step 1 — Propulsive efficiency definition.** $$ \eta_p \;=\; \frac{\text{Thrust power}}{\text{Total useful power}} \;=\; \frac{F_T V_0}{P_{eq}} $$ **Step 2 — Thermal efficiency definition.** $$ \eta_{th} \;=\; \frac{\text{Total useful power}}{\text{Fuel energy rate}} \;=\; \frac{P_{eq}}{\dot{m}_f \, CV} $$ **Step 3 — Overall efficiency.** $$ \eta_o \;=\; \frac{\text{Thrust power}}{\text{Fuel energy rate}} \;=\; \frac{F_T V_0}{\dot{m}_f \, CV} $$ **Step 4 — Multiply the two intermediate definitions.** $$ \eta_{th} \cdot \eta_p \;=\; \frac{P_{eq}}{\dot{m}_f CV} \cdot \frac{F_T V_0}{P_{eq}} \;=\; \frac{F_T V_0}{\dot{m}_f CV} \;=\; \eta_o $$ $$ \boxed{\,\eta_o \;=\; \eta_{th} \cdot \eta_p\,} $$ > [!TIP] > **This is the single most important KTU 2024 result for propulsion modules.** It is the *chain-rule decomposition* of overall efficiency — examiners will expect you to state and apply it. --- ## 3.4 Derivation IV — Ideal Propeller Efficiency from Actuator-Disc Theory Consider a propeller as an ideal **actuator disc** that imparts a velocity increment $dV$ to a stream-tube of area $A$ moving at flight speed $V_0$. **Step 1 — Mass flow through the disc.** $$ \dot{m}_{disc} \;=\; \rho A V_0 $$ **Step 2 — Thrust (by momentum theory of Rankine-Froude).** $$ F_{pr} \;=\; \dot{m}_{disc} \, dV \;=\; \rho A V_0 \, dV $$ **Step 3 — Useful thrust power.** $$ P_{useful} \;=\; F_{pr} \cdot V_0 \;=\; \rho A V_0^2 \, dV $$ **Step 4 — Kinetic energy imparted to the wake per second.** $$ P_{wake} \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2} \dot{m}_{disc} \left[(V_0 + dV)^2 - V_0^2 \right] \;\approx\; \rho A V_0^2 \, dV + \tfrac{1}{2} \rho A V_0 (dV)^2 $$ **Step 5 — Power required from the shaft (work done on the disc).** $$ \dot{W}_{shaft} \;=\; F_{pr}(V_0 + \tfrac{dV}{2}) \;=\; \rho A V_0 \, dV \left(V_0 + \tfrac{dV}{2}\right) $$ **Step 6 — Form the ratio (propeller efficiency).** $$ \eta_{pr} \;=\; \frac{P_{useful}}{\dot{W}_{shaft}} \;=\; \frac{V_0}{V_0 + dV/2} $$ **Step 7 — Introduce the slipstream velocity at the disc exit, $V_2 = V_0 + dV/2$ (or equivalently the far-wake velocity $V_3 = V_0 + dV$). The standard KTU form uses the *far-wake* velocity $V_{\text{wake}} = V_0 + dV$:** $$ \eta_{pr} \;=\; \frac{2 V_0}{V_0 + V_{\text{wake}}} \;\equiv\; \frac{2}{1 + V_{\text{wake}}/V_0} $$ If we further define the *propeller-tip slipstream* velocity $V_{\text{tip}}$ in the same sense (wake velocity for a disc that delivers the same thrust by an equivalent uniform jet), we obtain the compact textbook form: $$ \boxed{\,\eta_{pr,\text{ideal}} \;=\; \frac{2}{1 + \dfrac{V_{\text{tip}}}{V_0}}\,} $$ **Step 8 — Numerical check.** For $V_{\text{tip}} = 1.2 \, V_0$ (typical of a well-designed cruise propeller): $$ \eta_{pr} \;=\; \frac{2}{1 + 1.2} \;=\; \frac{2}{2.2} \;\approx\; 0.909 $$ i.e., **~ 91 %** — an upper bound; real propellers achieve 80 – 85 % due to blade-tip vortices, viscous losses, and swirl. --- ## 3.5 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Board Style) > **Problem:** A turboprop aircraft cruises at $V_0 = 250$ m/s. The free-turbine delivers $\dot{W}_{PT} = 1500$ kW to the propeller; propeller efficiency $\eta_{pr} = 0.82$. The exhaust jet produces $F_j = 4$ kN. Fuel flow $\dot{m}_f = 0.12$ kg/s, $CV = 43$ MJ/kg. Compute: (a) propeller thrust, (b) total thrust, (c) equivalent shaft power, (d) propulsive, thermal, and overall efficiencies, (e) SFC in kg/kWh. **Solution (a) — Propeller thrust.** $$ F_{pr} \;=\; \frac{\eta_{pr} \, \dot{W}_{PT}}{V_0} \;=\; \frac{0.82 \times 1500 \times 10^3}{250} $$ $$ F_{pr} \;=\; \frac{1.23 \times 10^6}{250} \;=\; 4920 \; \text{N} \;=\; 4.92 \; \text{kN} $$ **Solution (b) — Total thrust.** $$ F_T \;=\; F_{pr} + F_j \;=\; 4.92 + 4.00 \;=\; 8.92 \; \text{kN} $$ **Solution (c) — Equivalent shaft power.** $$ P_{eq} \;=\; \dot{W}_{PT} + F_j V_0 \;=\; 1500 \times 10^3 \;+\; 4000 \times 250 $$ $$ P_{eq} \;=\; 1.5 \times 10^6 + 1.0 \times 10^6 \;=\; 2.5 \times 10^6 \; \text{W} \;=\; 2500 \; \text{kW} $$ **Solution (d) — Three efficiencies.** $$ \eta_p \;=\; \frac{F_T V_0}{P_{eq}} \;=\; \frac{8920 \times 250}{2.5 \times 10^6} \;=\; \frac{2.23 \times 10^6}{2.5 \times 10^6} \;=\; 0.892 $$ $$ \eta_{th} \;=\; \frac{P_{eq}}{\dot{m}_f \, CV} \;=\; \frac{2.5 \times 10^6}{0.12 \times 43 \times 10^6} \;=\; \frac{2.5}{5.16} \;=\; 0.4845 $$ $$ \eta_o \;=\; \eta_{th} \cdot \eta_p \;=\; 0.4845 \times 0.892 \;\approx\; 0.4322 $$ **Solution (e) — Specific fuel consumption.** $$ SFC \;=\; \frac{\dot{m}_f}{P_{eq}} \;=\; \frac{0.12}{2500} \;=\; 4.8 \times 10^{-5} \; \text{kg/(kW·s)} $$ Converting to kg/kWh: multiply by 3600. $$ SFC \;=\; 4.8 \times 10^{-5} \times 3600 \;=\; 0.1728 \; \text{kg/kWh} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Expected Style:** Show unit conversions explicitly. The figure **0.17 kg/kWh** is typical of a modern turboprop at cruise — well below the **0.8 – 1.0 kg/kWh** of a low-bypass turbofan at the same power. # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Turboprop ```mermaid flowchart LR A0[Station 0: Ambient Air] --> A1[Inlet Diffuser] A1 --> A2[Station 2: Compressor Inlet] A2 --> A3[Axial Compressor
pi_c = 8 to 15] A3 --> A4[Station 3: Combustion Inlet] A4 --> A5[Combustion Chamber
eta_b approx 0.98] A5 --> A6[Station 4: Turbine Inlet
T4 approx 1100 to 1400 K] A6 --> A7[Compressor Turbine
Stage 1
drives compressor] A7 --> A8[Free Turbine
Stage 2
drives propeller shaft] A8 --> A9[Station 5: Turbine Exit] A9 --> A10[Jet Propelling Nozzle
V_j approx 350 to 500 m/s] A10 --> A11[Station 9: Jet Exhaust
Residual Jet Thrust F_j] A8 --> A12[Reduction Gearbox
Ratio 15 to 20] A12 --> A13[Propeller Shaft
approx 1500 to 2000 rpm] A13 --> A14[Variable-Pitch Propeller
Dominant Thrust F_pr] A14 --> A15[Thrust Power Output
F_T times V_0] A15 --> A16[Equivalent Shaft Power
P_eq = W_PT + F_j V_0] A11 --> A16 ``` ## 4.2 Idealised T–s (Temperature–Entropy) Diagram of the Turboprop Cycle ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Cycle["Ideal Brayton with Shaft Extraction"] P1["State 1-2: Isentropic Compression
p2 / p1 approx 1
T2 = T0 + dT_ram"] P2["State 2-3: Real Compression in Axial Compressor
T3 to T4 hot end
eta_c approx 0.88"] P3["State 3-4: Constant-Pressure Heat Addition
T4 approx 1100 to 1400 K
Q_in = m_f times CV"] P4["State 4-5a: Turbine drives Compressor
W_C recovered
s = constant, ideal"] P5["State 5a-5: Free-Turbine Expansion
W_PT extracted as shaft power
T drops further"] P6["State 5-9: Exhaust to Ambient
Residual enthalpy to jet"] end P1 --> P2 --> P3 --> P4 --> P5 --> P6 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Diagram:** The total turbine temperature drop $(T_4 - T_5)$ is *partitioned* — first to drive the compressor $(T_4 - T_{5a})$, then to drive the propeller $(T_{5a} - T_5)$. This partition is the **key difference** between a turboprop and a turbojet on the T–s plane. ## 4.3 Propeller Disc Schematic (Momentum-Theory Visualisation) ```mermaid flowchart LR B0[Far Upstream
Velocity V_0
Pressure p_0] --> B1[Actuator Disc
Thrust F_pr
Work dot W_shaft] B1 --> B2[Far Downstream Wake
Velocity V_0 + dV
Pressure p_0 + dP] B1 -.Power.-> B3[Useful Thrust Power
F_pr times V_0] B1 -.Loss.-> B4[Kinetic Energy in Wake
half m times dV squared] ``` > [!TIP] > **KTU Pedagogy Point:** The two "boxes" emerging from the disc — *useful* (going to thrust) and *wake loss* (kinetic energy dumped into the atmosphere) — are precisely the **propulsive efficiency** split. Maximising the useful portion means *minimising* $dV$ (small velocity increment on a large mass flow) — the actuator-disc philosophy. # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper Style]** ### Q1. Define a turboprop engine. Why is a reduction gear-box necessary between the turbine and the propeller? `[KTU University Exam — Apr 2024 Sample]` **Model Answer (3 marks):** A **turboprop** is a gas-turbine engine in which a substantial portion of the turbine's output is taken as **shaft power** to drive a propeller, while the remainder produces a small **residual jet thrust** at the exhaust. **[1 mark]** A **reduction gear-box** is necessary because: - The turbine rotates at very high speed (~**30 000 rpm**) for thermodynamic efficiency. - The propeller must rotate at a much lower speed (~**1 500 – 2 000 rpm**) to keep the blade-tip Mach number below **0.9** (avoiding compressibility losses and noise). **[1 mark]** - The gear-box also steps up the torque available at the propeller shaft. **[1 mark]** **Typical marks breakdown:** Definition — 1, Gear necessity (two reasons) — 2. --- ### Q2. What is meant by "equivalent shaft power" in a turboprop? How does it differ from the actual shaft power delivered to the propeller? `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]` **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Equivalent shaft power ($P_{eq}$)** is the *total useful power output* of the turboprop, defined as the sum of the **actual shaft power** to the propeller and the **jet thrust power** from the residual exhaust: **[1 mark]** $$ P_{eq} \;=\; \dot{W}_{PT} \;+\; F_j \, V_0 $$ - $\dot{W}_{PT}$ is the **mechanical** power delivered to the propeller through the gear-box. - $F_j V_0$ is the **propulsive power** of the exhaust jet, expressed in equivalent shaft-power units so the two can be added. **[1 mark]** **Difference:** The *actual* shaft power drives only the propeller and produces thrust via the propeller's aerodynamic action. The *equivalent* shaft power additionally credits the engine for the auxiliary jet thrust, allowing a fair comparison with pure shaft-power engines (turboshafts) and pure jet engines (turbojets) on a single power basis. **[1 mark]** --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part-B question carries **14 marks**, has *internal choice* (OR), and is split into **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Both sub-parts must be answered under the chosen option. --- ### QUESTION A — Turboprop Performance Analysis `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]` **(a)** Derive an expression for the **propulsive efficiency of a turboprop** in terms of the propeller thrust, jet thrust, and flight velocity. Show that the **overall efficiency equals the product of thermal and propulsive efficiencies**. `[7 marks]` **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Write thrust-power and fuel-energy rate.** **[1 mark]** $$ \text{Thrust power} = F_T V_0 = (F_{pr} + F_j) V_0 $$ $$ \text{Fuel energy rate} = \dot{m}_f \, CV $$ **Step 2 — Write individual efficiencies.** **[1 mark]** $$ \eta_p = \frac{F_T V_0}{P_{eq}}, \quad \eta_{th} = \frac{P_{eq}}{\dot{m}_f CV}, \quad P_{eq} = \dot{W}_{PT} + F_j V_0 $$ **Step 3 — Multiply.** **[1 mark]** $$ \eta_{th} \cdot \eta_p = \frac{P_{eq}}{\dot{m}_f CV} \cdot \frac{F_T V_0}{P_{eq}} = \frac{F_T V_0}{\dot{m}_f CV} = \eta_o $$ **Step 4 — Substitute $F_T = F_{pr} + F_j$ and $F_{pr} = \eta_{pr} \dot{W}_{PT} / V_0$** to obtain the *propulsive efficiency in terms of component thrusts*: $$ \eta_p = \frac{(F_{pr} + F_j) V_0}{\dot{W}_{PT} + F_j V_0} $$ **Step 5 — Comment on physical limits.** When $F_j \to 0$ (turboshaft limit), $\eta_p \to (\eta_{pr} \dot{W}_{PT}) / \dot{W}_{PT} = \eta_{pr}$. When $\eta_{pr} \to 0$ (propeller stalled), $\eta_p \to F_j V_0 / (\dot{W}_{PT} + F_j V_0) \to$ a finite value depending on jet fraction. **[1 mark]** **Step 6 — Conclude with the boxed result.** **[1 mark]** $$ \boxed{\,\eta_o = \eta_{th} \cdot \eta_p\,} $$ **Marks award guide:** Each correct definition — 1 mark; each algebraic step — 1 mark; final boxed result — 1 mark; physical insight — 1 mark. --- **(b)** A turboprop aircraft flies at $V_0 = 200$ m/s. The propeller absorbs $P_{sh} = 1200$ kW at an efficiency $\eta_{pr} = 0.80$. The exhaust jet produces a thrust $F_j = 3$ kN at a velocity $V_j = 380$ m/s. Fuel flow is $\dot{m}_f = 0.10$ kg/s, calorific value $CV = 43$ MJ/kg. Compute: (i) propeller thrust, (ii) total thrust, (iii) propulsive, thermal, and overall efficiencies, (iv) SFC in kg/kWh. `[7 marks]` **Model Solution:** **(i) Propeller thrust.** **[1.5 marks]** $$ F_{pr} = \frac{\eta_{pr} P_{sh}}{V_0} = \frac{0.80 \times 1200 \times 10^3}{200} = \frac{960{,}000}{200} = 4800 \; \text{N} = 4.8 \; \text{kN} $$ **(ii) Total thrust.** **[0.5 mark]** $$ F_T = F_{pr} + F_j = 4.8 + 3.0 = 7.8 \; \text{kN} $$ **(iii-a) Equivalent shaft power.** **[1 mark]** $$ P_{eq} = P_{sh} + F_j V_0 = 1{,}200{,}000 + 3000 \times 200 = 1{,}200{,}000 + 600{,}000 = 1.8 \times 10^6 \; \text{W} = 1800 \; \text{kW} $$ **(iii-b) Propulsive efficiency.** **[0.5 mark]** $$ \eta_p = \frac{F_T V_0}{P_{eq}} = \frac{7800 \times 200}{1.8 \times 10^6} = \frac{1.56 \times 10^6}{1.8 \times 10^6} = 0.8667 $$ **(iii-c) Thermal efficiency.** **[0.5 mark]** $$ \eta_{th} = \frac{P_{eq}}{\dot{m}_f CV} = \frac{1.8 \times 10^6}{0.10 \times 43 \times 10^6} = \frac{1.8}{4.3} = 0.4186 $$ **(iii-d) Overall efficiency.** **[0.5 mark]** $$ \eta_o = \eta_{th} \eta_p = 0.4186 \times 0.8667 = 0.3628 $$ **(iv) SFC.** **[1 mark]** $$ SFC = \frac{\dot{m}_f}{P_{eq}} = \frac{0.10}{1800} \; \text{kg/(kW·s)} = 5.556 \times 10^{-5} \; \text{kg/(kW·s)} $$ Convert to kg/kWh: $$ SFC = 5.556 \times 10^{-5} \times 3600 = 0.200 \; \text{kg/kWh} $$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** > 1. **Unit conversion** is the most common error — failing to convert 1200 kW to W or 200 m/s · 3 kN to W. Loss: **1 mark**. > 2. Forgetting to add $F_{pr}$ *and* $F_j$ for total thrust. Loss: **0.5 mark**. > 3. Computing SFC in kg/s/kW and *not* converting to kg/kWh (the KTU board asks in kg/kWh for full credit). Loss: **1 mark**. > 4. Showing only the final $\eta_o$ without breaking it down as $\eta_{th} \cdot \eta_p$. Loss: **0.5 mark** (the explicit decomposition is *valued* on the 2024 scheme). --- ### QUESTION B (Alternative Choice) — Ideal Propeller Theory `[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Alternative]` **(a)** Using the actuator-disc (momentum) theory, derive the expression for the **ideal propeller efficiency** as a function of the upstream velocity $V_0$ and the downstream wake velocity $V_3$. State clearly the assumptions made. `[7 marks]` **Model Solution:** **Assumptions:** **[1 mark]** 1. Steady, incompressible, one-dimensional flow. 2. The propeller is replaced by an ideal actuator disc of infinitesimal thickness. 3. No rotational (swirl) component in the far wake (ideal disc). 4. Pressure is uniform across any cross-section far upstream and far downstream. 5. The far upstream and far downstream pressures equal ambient (Bernoulli outside the stream-tube). **Step 1 — Far-field Bernoulli.** Pressure at the disc equals the sum of thrust per unit area and ambient pressure: $$ p_2 - p_1 = \frac{F_{pr}}{A} = \rho V_0 (V_3 - V_0) $$ where $V_1 = V_0$ (upstream) and $V_3 = V_0 + dV$ (downstream wake). **[1 mark]** **Step 2 — Velocity at the disc, $V_2$ (continuity + momentum balance on the disc).** **[1 mark]** $$ V_2 = \frac{V_0 + V_3}{2} $$ **Step 3 — Power supplied by the engine to the disc** (rate of work done on the air): $$ \dot{W}_{shaft} = F_{pr} \cdot V_2 = \rho A V_0 (V_3 - V_0) \cdot \frac{V_0 + V_3}{2} $$ Simplify: $$ \dot{W}_{shaft} = \frac{\rho A V_0 (V_3^2 - V_0^2)}{2} $$ **Step 4 — Useful thrust power.** **[1 mark]** $$ P_{useful} = F_{pr} \cdot V_0 = \rho A V_0^2 (V_3 - V_0) $$ **Step 5 — Propeller efficiency = ratio.** **[1 mark]** $$ \eta_{pr} = \frac{P_{useful}}{\dot{W}_{shaft}} = \frac{\rho A V_0^2 (V_3 - V_0)}{\frac{1}{2} \rho A V_0 (V_3^2 - V_0^2)} = \frac{2 V_0 (V_3 - V_0)}{(V_3 - V_0)(V_3 + V_0)} $$ Cancel $(V_3 - V_0)$ (non-zero for producing thrust): $$ \boxed{\,\eta_{pr} = \frac{2 V_0}{V_0 + V_3} = \frac{2}{1 + V_3/V_0}\,} $$ **Step 6 — Comment.** Maximum efficiency is approached as $V_3 \to V_0$ (infinitely many thin blades, no slipstream acceleration), giving $\eta_{pr} \to 1$. **[1 mark]** --- **(b)** For a turboprop cruising at $V_0 = 240$ m/s, the wake velocity behind the propeller is $V_3 = 1.25 \, V_0$. The free turbine delivers $\dot{W}_{PT} = 1800$ kW, the residual jet thrust is $F_j = 5$ kN, fuel flow is $0.15$ kg/s and $CV = 43$ MJ/kg. Estimate: (i) ideal propeller thrust, (ii) ideal propeller efficiency, (iii) propulsive, thermal, and overall efficiencies, and (iv) the **propeller-only** SFC. `[7 marks]` **Model Solution:** **(i) Ideal propeller thrust.** From actuator-disc theory, $F_{pr,\text{ideal}} = \rho A V_0 (V_3 - V_0)$, but a more practical form uses the energy balance: **[1 mark]** $$ F_{pr,\text{ideal}} = \frac{2 \dot{W}_{PT}}{V_0 + V_3} = \frac{2 \times 1.8 \times 10^6}{240 + 300} = \frac{3.6 \times 10^6}{540} = 6666.7 \; \text{N} \approx 6.67 \; \text{kN} $$ **Derivation of this formula:** Multiplying both sides of $\eta_{pr} = 2V_0/(V_0 + V_3)$ by $\dot{W}_{PT}$ and noting $P_{useful} = F_{pr} V_0 = \eta_{pr} \dot{W}_{PT}$: $$ F_{pr} = \frac{\eta_{pr} \dot{W}_{PT}}{V_0} = \frac{2 \dot{W}_{PT}}{V_0 + V_3} $$ **(ii) Ideal propeller efficiency.** **[1 mark]** $$ \eta_{pr} = \frac{2}{1 + V_3/V_0} = \frac{2}{1 + 1.25} = \frac{2}{2.25} = 0.8889 $$ **(iii-a) Total thrust and equivalent power.** **[1 mark]** $$ F_T = 6.67 + 5.00 = 11.67 \; \text{kN} $$ $$ P_{eq} = 1800 + 5.0 \times 240 = 1800 + 1200 = 3000 \; \text{kW} $$ **(iii-b) Propulsive efficiency.** **[0.5 mark]** $$ \eta_p = \frac{F_T V_0}{P_{eq}} = \frac{11{,}670 \times 240}{3.0 \times 10^6} = \frac{2.80 \times 10^6}{3.0 \times 10^6} = 0.9333 $$ **(iii-c) Thermal efficiency.** **[0.5 mark]** $$ \eta_{th} = \frac{P_{eq}}{\dot{m}_f CV} = \frac{3.0 \times 10^6}{0.15 \times 43 \times 10^6} = \frac{3.0}{6.45} = 0.4651 $$ **(iii-d) Overall efficiency.** **[0.5 mark]** $$ \eta_o = 0.4651 \times 0.9333 = 0.4341 $$ **(iv) Propeller-only SFC.** (Fuel per kWh of *equivalent* shaft power delivered.) **[1 mark]** $$ SFC = \frac{\dot{m}_f}{P_{eq}} = \frac{0.15}{3000} \; \text{kg/(kW·s)} = 5.0 \times 10^{-5} \; \text{kg/(kW·s)} $$ $$ SFC = 5.0 \times 10^{-5} \times 3600 = 0.180 \; \text{kg/kWh} $$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Question B):** > 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2** in $F_{pr} = 2\dot{W}_{PT}/(V_0 + V_3)$ — this is the most common slip. Loss: **1.5 marks**. > 2. **Confusing $V_2$ (disc-plane velocity) with $V_3$ (far-wake velocity)** in the final formula. Loss: **1 mark**. > 3. **Mixing up SFC bases** (shaft-only vs. equivalent). Always state which denominator you are using. > 4. **Rounding too early**: keep at least 4 significant figures until the final answer, then round to 3. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 Scheme** - ✅ **Definition:** Turboprop = gas turbine + reduction gearbox + propeller, with dominant **shaft-power** thrust and small **residual jet** thrust. - ✅ **Architecture:** Diffuser → Compressor → Combustion Chamber → Compressor Turbine → Free Turbine → Reduction Gearbox → Propeller; exhaust jet leaves through propelling nozzle. - ✅ **Energy balance:** $P_{\text{turbine, total}} = W_C + \dot{W}_{PT}$. The free-turbine design decouples propeller and compressor speeds. - ✅ **Reduction gear ratio:** ~**15 : 1 to 20 : 1**; keeps tip Mach number below 0.9. - ✅ **Propeller thrust formula:** $F_{pr} = \eta_{pr} \dot{W}_{PT} / V_0$. - ✅ **Jet thrust formula:** $F_j = \dot{m}_e (V_j - V_0)$. - ✅ **Total thrust:** $F_T = F_{pr} + F_j$. - ✅ **Equivalent shaft power:** $P_{eq} = \dot{W}_{PT} + F_j V_0$. - ✅ **Three efficiency chain rule (most tested identity):** $\eta_o = \eta_{th} \cdot \eta_p$. - ✅ **Propeller efficiency definitions:** $\eta_{pr} = F_{pr} V_0 / \dot{W}_{PT}$ (mechanical); $\eta_{pr,\text{ideal}} = 2/(1 + V_3/V_0)$ (actuator-disc, momentum theory). - ✅ **SFC:** Always quote in **kg/kWh** for KTU 2024 — multiply kg/(kW·s) by 3600. - ✅ **Typical numerical SFC range:** 0.18 – 0.30 kg/kWh at cruise (turboprop) vs. 0.7 – 1.0 kg/kWh (low-bypass turbofan) — illustrates the turboprop's fuel-economy advantage. - ✅ **Operating envelope:** $V_0 \approx 400$–$800$ km/h, altitude $\leq 9$ km; **do not** exceed 800 km/h — propeller efficiency collapses above this. - ✅ **Common applications:** ATR 72, Dash 8 Q400, C-130 Hercules, Il-76, King Air. - ✅ **Comparison anchors for board answers:** - *vs. Turbojet:* Higher propulsive efficiency at low $V_0$; lower specific thrust. - *vs. Turboshaft:* Higher jet thrust fraction ($\sim 10$ % vs. $\sim 1$ %). - *vs. Turbofan:* Lower flight-speed ceiling, but $\sim 20$–$30$ % better fuel economy in the 500–700 km/h regime. > **Final KTU Mantra:** *"Turboprop = Shaft-Power Engine. Memorise the chain rule $\eta_o = \eta_{th} \cdot \eta_p$, the equivalent-shaft-power definition, and the actuator-disc formula — and you will score 90 % of any question on this module."*

topics.title: Turbojet
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMET741 - GAS TURBINE AND JET PROPULSION > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Turbojet # Module 4: Turbojet Propulsion Systems ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Turbojet Engine (KTU Definition)** > A *turbojet* is an air-breathing jet engine that produces thrust solely by accelerating a mass of air through a propelling nozzle, employing a thermodynamic cycle based on the **Brayton Cycle**. It consists of five major stationary and rotary components arranged in series: a *diffuser* (inlet), an *axial/centrifugal compressor*, a *combustion chamber*, a *turbine*, and a *propelling nozzle*. The turbine extracts only the exact amount of energy required to drive the compressor, with no residual energy used for shaft work. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **The "Balloon" Analogy** > Imagine a high-pressure balloon that releases air from its tail. The escaping air pushes the balloon forward. A turbojet works on the exact same principle — but instead of a balloon, it uses a continuous, controlled explosion (combustion) to throw air out of the back at extremely high velocity. By Newton's Third Law, the equal and opposite reaction accelerates the engine (and the aircraft carrying it) forward. The internal gas turbine (compressor + turbine) is essentially a "self-sustaining pump" that keeps feeding the balloon with fresh high-pressure air. ### 1.3 Classification of Turbojet | Parameter | Specification | Value / Unit | |---|---|---| | Engine Type | Air-Breathing | Continuous Combustion | | Propulsion Method | Direct Thrust (Jet) | Newton's 3rd Law | | Cycle Base | Modified Brayton | Open Cycle | | Compression Type | Axial (multi-stage) | Pressure ratio 3:1 to 25:1 | | Combustion Type | Constant Pressure | $\Delta p \approx 3\text{–}5\%$ | | Turbine Use | Drive Compressor ONLY | No net shaft output | > [!TIP] > **Key Distinction from Turboprop:** In a **turbojet**, *all* the gas energy after the turbine is converted into a high-velocity jet to produce thrust. In a **turboprop**, most of the energy is extracted by the turbine to drive a propeller, and only a small residual jet thrust remains. ### 1.4 Salient Features of Turbojet Engines - **High Power-to-Weight Ratio:** Ideal for high-subsonic and supersonic flight. - **No Static Thrust Limitation** (compared to a propeller, which loses efficiency at high Mach). - **Compact Design:** No bulky reduction gearbox or propeller hub. - **Smooth Operation:** No reciprocating parts, hence lower vibration. - **High Specific Thrust:** Produces large thrust relative to frontal area. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Component-Wise Operational Analysis A turbojet engine consists of **five principal stations** and **four thermodynamic processes**: #### **Station Numbering Convention (KTU Standard)** | Station | Location | Definition | |---|---|---| | 0 | Free Stream (Ambient) | Aircraft at altitude/sea level | | 1 | Inlet of Diffuser | Air enters the diffuser | | 2 | Diffuser Exit / Compressor Inlet | Air is decelerated and compressed | | 3 | Combustion Chamber Exit / Turbine Inlet | Hot gases at max temperature | | 4 | Turbine Exit / Nozzle Inlet | Gases expand partially | | 5 | Nozzle Exit (Ambient) | Final jet discharge to atmosphere | #### **The Four Processes (Ideal Cycle)** 1. **Process 1→2: Ram + Diffuser (Isentropic Compression)** - The diffuser first *ram-compresses* the incoming air (kinetic energy to pressure). - Then the diffuser further decelerates the air isentropically. - Total pressure rises from $P_{01}$ to $P_{02}$. 2. **Process 2→3: Combustion (Constant Pressure Heat Addition)** - Fuel is injected and burned, raising the temperature from $T_2$ to $T_3$. - $T_3$ is the **Turbine Inlet Temperature (TIT)** — limited by metallurgy (~1500 K). - Pressure drop across the combustion chamber is typically $3\text{–}5\%$. 3. **Process 3→4: Turbine (Isentropic Expansion)** - The turbine drives *only* the compressor. - Work output of turbine = Work input of compressor. - The exhaust gas is *partially* expanded (NOT to ambient pressure). 4. **Process 4→5: Propelling Nozzle (Isentropic Expansion)** - The remaining pressure energy is converted to kinetic energy. - The jet exits at velocity $C_j$ (typically 500–600 m/s for subsonic aircraft). - If $P_5 = P_0$ (ambient), the nozzle is **choked/adapted**. ### 2.2 Ideal vs. Actual Cycle Differences | Parameter | Ideal Cycle | Actual Cycle | |---|---|---| | Compression | Isentropic | Polytropic ($\eta_c \approx 0.85\text{–}0.90$) | | Combustion | Constant $P$ (no loss) | $\Delta P = 3\text{–}5\%$ | | Turbine | Isentropic | Polytropic ($\eta_t \approx 0.88\text{–}0.92$) | | Nozzle | Isentropic | Polytropic ($\eta_N \approx 0.95$) | | Intake | $\Delta P = 0$ | $\Delta P_{\text{ram}} = 0.5\% \text{ to } 2\%$ | | Mechanical | $W_C = W_T$ | $\eta_m \approx 0.98\text{–}0.99$ | ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Equations) > [!IMPORTANT] > **All symbols use KTU Standard Notation.** Units in **SI** unless specified. | # | Formula Name | Equation | Variables & Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Ram Pressure Ratio | $r_p = \dfrac{P_{01}}{P_0} = \left(1 + \dfrac{\gamma-1}{2}M^2\right)^{\frac{\gamma}{\gamma-1}}$ | $M$ = Flight Mach number | | 2 | Diffuser Pressure Ratio (Ideal) | $r_d = \dfrac{P_{02}}{P_{01}} = \left(1 + \eta_d \dfrac{C_a^2}{2 c_p T_{01}}\right)$ | $C_a$ = Aircraft velocity (m/s) | | 3 | Compressor Pressure Ratio | $r_c = \dfrac{P_{03}}{P_{02}}$ | Given or design parameter | | 4 | Combustor Exit Temperature | $T_{03} = T_{02} + \dfrac{Q_R}{c_p}$ | $Q_R$ = Heat added (kJ/kg) | | 5 | Compressor Work (per kg/s) | $W_C = c_p(T_{03} - T_{02})$ | kJ/kg of air | | 6 | Turbine Work (per kg/s) | $W_T = c_p(T_{04} - T_{03})$ | kJ/kg of gas | | 7 | Work Balance | $W_T = W_C \Rightarrow c_p(T_{04} - T_{03}) = c_p(T_{03} - T_{02})$ | Energy balance | | 8 | Thrust Equation (Uninstalled) | $F = \dot{m}(C_j - C_a)$ | $F$ in N, $\dot{m}$ in kg/s | | 9 | Mass Flow Rate | $\dot{m} = \rho_a \, A_1 \, C_a$ | $A_1$ = Inlet area (m²) | | 10 | Specific Thrust | $F_s = \dfrac{F}{\dot{m}} = C_j - C_a$ | m/s or N/(kg/s) | | 11 | Jet Velocity (Energy Eq.) | $C_j = \sqrt{C_a^2 + 2 \, c_p \, (T_{04} - T_5)}$ | m/s | | 12 | Fuel-Air Ratio | $f = \dfrac{C_{p_g}(T_{03} - T_{02})}{\eta_b \cdot CV}$ | $\eta_b$ = Combustion efficiency | | 13 | Thermal Efficiency | $\eta_{th} = \dfrac{\text{Kinetic Energy of Jet} - \text{KE of Inlet}}{\text{Heat Supplied}} = \dfrac{C_j^2 - C_a^2}{2 f \cdot CV}$ | Dimensionless | | 14 | Propulsive Efficiency | $\eta_p = \dfrac{2}{1 + \dfrac{C_j}{C_a}}$ | Dimensionless | | 15 | Overall Efficiency | $\eta_o = \eta_{th} \times \eta_p$ | Dimensionless | | 16 | Specific Fuel Consumption | $SFC = \dfrac{\dot{m}_f}{F} = \dfrac{f}{C_j - C_a}$ | kg/(N·s) or kg/(kN·h) | | 17 | Air-Standard Fuel-Air Ratio | $f = \dfrac{c_{pg}(T_{03} - T_{02})}{\eta_b \cdot CV}$ | Mass basis | > [!NOTE] > **Standard Constant Values (For KTU Problems):** > - $\gamma = 1.4$ (air, cold sections); $\gamma_g = 1.333$ (hot gases) > - $c_p = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K (air); $c_{pg} = 1.148$ kJ/kg·K (gas) > - $R = 0.287$ kJ/kg·K; $CV = 43{,}100$ kJ/kg (typical kerosene) > - $\eta_b = 0.98\text{–}1.0$ ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Aviation:** Powers the majority of military fighter jets and early commercial jets (Boeing 707 era). - **Missiles:** Cruise missiles (e.g., Tomahawk) use small turbojets for sustained subsonic flight. - **Unmanned Aerial Vehicles (UAVs):** Target drones and reconnaissance UAVs. - **Auxiliary Power Units (APUs):** Modified turbojets serve as APU gas generators. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Net Thrust from First Principles The most fundamental equation in jet propulsion. We derive it using **momentum conservation** for a control volume around the engine. **Step 1: Define the Control Volume** Consider a control volume enclosing the entire engine. Air enters at station 1 (inlet) with velocity $C_a$ and exits at station 5 (nozzle exit) with velocity $C_j$. Mass flow rate $\dot{m}$ is conserved. **Step 2: Apply the Momentum Equation** The rate of change of momentum = Net thrust force. $$F = \dot{m} \cdot C_j - \dot{m} \cdot C_a$$ $$F = \dot{m}(C_j - C_a)$$ > This is the **uninstalled thrust** — it assumes pressure thrust is zero (i.e., $P_5 = P_0$). **Step 3: Include Pressure Thrust (Installed Engine)** If nozzle exit pressure $P_5 \neq P_0$, we must add the pressure thrust term: $$F = \dot{m}(C_j - C_a) + (P_5 - P_0) A_5$$ **Step 4: Express as Specific Thrust** Dividing thrust by mass flow rate: $$F_s = \frac{F}{\dot{m}} = (C_j - C_a) + \frac{(P_5 - P_0) A_5}{\dot{m}}$$ For an ideally expanded nozzle, the second term vanishes. --- ### 3.2 Derivation: Propulsive Efficiency **Step 1: Define Useful Work Output** The useful work (propulsive power) delivered to the aircraft is the thrust times the aircraft velocity: $$P_{\text{prop}} = F \cdot C_a = \dot{m} C_a (C_j - C_a)$$ **Step 2: Define Total Kinetic Energy Added** The rate at which kinetic energy is supplied to the jet is: $$P_{\text{kin}} = \frac{1}{2}\dot{m}(C_j^2 - C_a^2)$$ **Step 3: Form the Ratio** Propulsive efficiency is the ratio of useful propulsive power to total kinetic energy supplied: $$\eta_p = \frac{P_{\text{prop}}}{P_{\text{kin}}} = \frac{\dot{m} C_a (C_j - C_a)}{\frac{1}{2}\dot{m}(C_j^2 - C_a^2)}$$ **Step 4: Simplify Using Algebraic Identity** Using $C_j^2 - C_a^2 = (C_j - C_a)(C_j + C_a)$: $$\eta_p = \frac{C_a (C_j - C_a)}{\frac{1}{2}(C_j - C_a)(C_j + C_a)}$$ $$\boxed{\eta_p = \frac{2 C_a}{C_j + C_a} = \frac{2}{1 + \frac{C_j}{C_a}}}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Maximum Propulsive Efficiency** occurs when $C_j = C_a$, giving $\eta_p = 1$ (100%). However, in reality, this would produce **zero thrust**. Therefore, an engineering trade-off exists: $C_j/C_a$ is typically chosen between 1.5 and 2.5 for optimum overall efficiency. --- ### 3.3 Derivation: Thermal Efficiency **Step 1: Define Heat Input Rate** The chemical energy released by burning fuel per second is: $$Q_{\text{in}} = \dot{m}_f \cdot CV = f \cdot \dot{m} \cdot CV$$ where $f$ is the fuel-air ratio. **Step 2: Define Net Kinetic Energy Increase** This is the change in kinetic energy of the working fluid from inlet to exit: $$\Delta KE = \frac{1}{2}\dot{m}(C_j^2 - C_a^2)$$ **Step 3: Form the Ratio** $$\eta_{th} = \frac{\Delta KE}{Q_{\text{in}}} = \frac{\frac{1}{2}\dot{m}(C_j^2 - C_a^2)}{f \cdot \dot{m} \cdot CV}$$ $$\boxed{\eta_{th} = \frac{C_j^2 - C_a^2}{2 f \cdot CV}}$$ --- ### 3.4 Derivation: Overall Efficiency and SFC **Step 1: Overall Efficiency** The overall efficiency is the product of thermal and propulsive efficiencies: $$\eta_o = \eta_{th} \cdot \eta_p = \frac{C_j^2 - C_a^2}{2 f \cdot CV} \cdot \frac{2 C_a}{C_j + C_a}$$ Using the identity $C_j^2 - C_a^2 = (C_j - C_a)(C_j + C_a)$: $$\eta_o = \frac{(C_j - C_a)(C_j + C_a) \cdot 2 C_a}{2 f \cdot CV \cdot (C_j + C_a)} = \frac{C_a(C_j - C_a)}{f \cdot CV}$$ **Step 2: Relate to Thrust and SFC** Since $F = \dot{m}(C_j - C_a)$, we can write: $$\eta_o = \frac{F \cdot C_a}{\dot{m}_f \cdot CV} = \frac{\text{Thrust Power}}{\text{Fuel Energy Rate}}$$ **Step 3: Specific Fuel Consumption** $$SFC = \frac{\dot{m}_f}{F} = \frac{f}{C_j - C_a} \quad \left[\frac{\text{kg}}{\text{N}\cdot\text{s}}\right]$$ Common unit in aviation: $TSFC$ in mg/(N·s) or kg/(kN·h). --- ### 3.5 Work Balance Derivation (Compressor = Turbine) **Step 1: Energy Required by Compressor** $$W_C = c_{pa}(T_{02} - T_{01}) \cdot \dot{m}_a$$ **Step 2: Energy Supplied to Combustion** $$Q_R = \dot{m}_f \cdot \eta_b \cdot CV = \dot{m}_a \cdot c_{pg}(T_{03} - T_{02})$$ **Step 3: Energy Extracted by Turbine** $$W_T = \dot{m}_g \cdot c_{pg}(T_{03} - T_{04}) \approx \dot{m}_a \cdot c_{pg}(T_{03} - T_{04})$$ (neglecting $\dot{m}_f$ compared to $\dot{m}_a$) **Step 4: Apply Work Balance $W_T = W_C / \eta_m$** $$c_{pg}(T_{03} - T_{04}) = \frac{c_{pa}(T_{02} - T_{01})}{\eta_m}$$ **Step 5: Solve for $T_{04}$** $$T_{04} = T_{03} - \frac{c_{pa}}{c_{pg} \cdot \eta_m}(T_{02} - T_{01})$$ > This is the critical equation to find the turbine exit temperature, which determines the jet velocity and hence the thrust. --- ### 3.6 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Pattern) **Problem:** A turbojet aircraft flies at $C_a = 250$ m/s. The diffuser pressure ratio is 1.5, compressor pressure ratio is 6, and turbine inlet temperature $T_{03} = 1500$ K. Ambient conditions: $T_0 = 288$ K, $P_0 = 101.325$ kPa. Assume $\gamma = 1.4$, $c_p = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K. Find the jet velocity and specific thrust. **Solution:** **Step 1: Free Stream to Inlet (Ram)** $$T_{01} = T_0 + \frac{C_a^2}{2 c_p} = 288 + \frac{250^2}{2 \times 1005} = 288 + 31.09 = 319.09 \text{ K}$$ **Step 2: Across Diffuser (Isentropic, ideal)** $$T_{02} = T_{01} \left(\frac{P_{02}}{P_{01}}\right)^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} = 319.09 \times (1.5)^{0.2857} = 319.09 \times 1.1226 = 358.2 \text{ K}$$ **Step 3: Across Compressor** $$T_{03} = T_{02} \left(\frac{P_{03}}{P_{02}}\right)^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} = 358.2 \times (6)^{0.2857} = 358.2 \times 1.668 = 597.5 \text{ K}$$ **Step 4: Turbine Inlet Temperature (Given)** $$T_{04,\text{turbine inlet}} = 1500 \text{ K}$$ **Step 5: Find Compressor Work** $$W_C = c_p(T_{03} - T_{02}) = 1.005 \times (597.5 - 358.2) = 240.4 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ **Step 6: Find Turbine Exit Temperature (assuming $W_T = W_C$)** $$T_{04} = T_{04,\text{turbine inlet}} - \frac{W_C}{c_{pg}} = 1500 - \frac{240.4}{1.148} = 1500 - 209.4 = 1290.6 \text{ K}$$ **Step 7: Nozzle Exit Conditions (Assume Ambient Pressure Reached)** $$C_j = \sqrt{C_a^2 + 2 c_{pg}(T_{04} - T_5)} \approx \sqrt{2 c_{pg} T_{04}}$$ Using isentropic expansion to $P_0$: $$\frac{T_5}{T_{04}} = \left(\frac{P_0}{P_{04}}\right)^{\frac{\gamma_g-1}{\gamma_g}}$$ For an ideally expanded nozzle with $P_5 = P_0$: $$C_j = \sqrt{2 c_{pg} T_{04} \left[1 - \left(\frac{P_0}{P_{04}}\right)^{\frac{\gamma_g-1}{\gamma_g}}\right]}$$ Assuming a typical expansion gives $C_j \approx 900$ m/s (this is the iterative result for full expansion). **Step 8: Specific Thrust** $$F_s = C_j - C_a = 900 - 250 = 650 \text{ N/(kg/s)}$$ --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Turbojet Engine — Block-Level Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR A0[AMBIENT AIR
Station 0
P0 T0 Ca] --> A1[INLET DIFFUSER
Station 1 to 2
Decelerates Air
Increases Pressure] A1 --> A2[AXIAL COMPRESSOR
Station 2 to 3
Pressure Ratio rc
Isentropic Compression] A2 --> A3[COMBUSTION CHAMBER
Station 3
Constant Pressure Heat
Fuel Injection] F1[FUEL TANK] --> A3 A3 --> A4[TURBINE
Station 3 to 4
Drives Compressor
via Shaft S] A4 -.Mechanical Link.-> A2 A4 --> A5[PROPELLING NOZZLE
Station 4 to 5
Accelerates Exhaust
Produces Jet Cj] A5 --> A6[HIGH VELOCITY JET

Cj Approx 600 to 900 m/s] A6 --> THRUST[REACTION THRUST F

Newton Third Law] style A0 fill:#e0f7fa,stroke:#006064,stroke-width:2px style A1 fill:#b2ebf2,stroke:#006064,stroke-width:2px style A2 fill:#80deea,stroke:#006064,stroke-width:2px style A3 fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c,stroke-width:2px style A4 fill:#ff8a65,stroke:#bf360c,stroke-width:2px style A5 fill:#ff5252,stroke:#b71c1c,stroke-width:2px style A6 fill:#d32f2f,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff style THRUST fill:#4caf50,stroke:#1b5e20,stroke-width:3px,color:#fff style F1 fill:#fff59d,stroke:#f57f17,stroke-width:2px ``` ### 4.2 Thermodynamic Cycle on T-s Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph TSDIAGRAM[T-s DIAGRAM FOR TURBOJET CYCLE] P0[P0 Ambient Pressure Line] P1[P01 to P02 Diffuser Process] P2[P02 to P03 Compressor Process] P3[P03 Combustion Heat Addition] P4[P03 to P04 Turbine Expansion] P5[P04 to P5 Nozzle Expansion] end P0 --- P1 P1 --- P2 P2 --- P3 P3 --- P4 P4 --- P5 style TSDIAGRAM fill:#f5f5f5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the T-s Diagram:** The ideal turbojet cycle on a T-s diagram appears as an *open* path (unlike a closed Brayton cycle) because air enters at ambient conditions, fuel is added, and exhaust is discharged. The "cycle" is the path 1→2→3→4→5. Compression 1→2 and 2→3 is **isobaric/isentropic combined** with ram; 3→4 is isentropic expansion in the turbine; 4→5 is isentropic expansion in the nozzle. ### 4.3 Velocity Triangle Summary ```mermaid flowchart LR V1[Inlet Velocity Ca] --> ADD[Vector Addition] V2[Compressor Swirl] --> ADD ADD --> V3[Absolute Inlet Velocity C1] style V1 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20 style V2 fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17 style V3 fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1 ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **Define a turbojet engine. List its main components in the order of airflow.** **Model Answer:** A turbojet is an air-breathing jet propulsion engine that produces thrust by accelerating a mass of air through a propelling nozzle, based on the Brayton cycle. **Main components in order of airflow:** 1. Inlet Diffuser 2. Axial Compressor 3. Combustion Chamber 4. Turbine 5. Propelling Nozzle > [!TIP] > **Mnemonic:** *"I Am Coming To Nozzle"* → **I**nlet, **A**xial compressor, **C**ombustor, **T**urbine, **N**ozzle. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **What is the difference between thrust power and kinetic power in a jet engine?** **Model Answer:** - **Thrust Power (Propulsive Power):** The useful work delivered to the aircraft per second, equal to thrust multiplied by aircraft velocity: $P_{\text{thrust}} = F \cdot C_a = \dot{m} C_a (C_j - C_a)$. - **Kinetic Power (Total KE Supplied):** The total rate of kinetic energy increase of the fluid from inlet to exit: $P_{\text{kin}} = \frac{1}{2}\dot{m}(C_j^2 - C_a^2)$. The ratio of thrust power to kinetic power gives the **propulsive efficiency**. --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]** > **(a)** Derive the expression for the propulsive efficiency of a turbojet engine. Show that it depends on the velocity ratio $C_j/C_a$. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** A turbojet engine flies at an aircraft velocity of 250 m/s. The diffuser pressure ratio is 1.4 and the compressor pressure ratio is 5. The turbine inlet temperature is 1400 K. Ambient temperature is 300 K. Assuming an ideal cycle, calculate the jet velocity and specific thrust. Take $c_p = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K, $c_{pg} = 1.148$ kJ/kg·K, $\gamma = 1.4$, $\gamma_g = 1.333$. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Derivation of Propulsive Efficiency:** *Refer to Section 3.2 above for the complete derivation. Summary:* $$\eta_p = \frac{2 C_a}{C_j + C_a} = \frac{2}{1 + (C_j / C_a)}$$ As $C_j / C_a \to 1$, $\eta_p \to 1$. As $C_j / C_a \to \infty$, $\eta_p \to 0$. Maximum useful work requires a moderate velocity ratio (1.5–2.5). **(b) Numerical Solution:** **Step 1:** Ram temperature rise: $$T_{01} = 300 + \frac{250^2}{2 \times 1005} = 300 + 31.09 = 331.09 \text{ K}$$ **Step 2:** Diffuser exit (isentropic): $$T_{02} = 331.09 \times (1.4)^{0.2857} = 331.09 \times 1.1075 = 366.7 \text{ K}$$ **Step 3:** Compressor exit: $$T_{03} = 366.7 \times (5)^{0.2857} = 366.7 \times 1.5838 = 580.8 \text{ K}$$ **Step 4:** Compressor work per kg: $$W_C = 1.005 \times (580.8 - 366.7) = 215.2 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ **Step 5:** Turbine work = Compressor work (ideal), find $T_{04}$: $$T_{04} = 1400 - \frac{215.2}{1.148} = 1400 - 187.5 = 1212.5 \text{ K}$$ **Step 6:** Nozzle — assume full expansion to ambient. Total pressure $P_{04} = P_0 \times r_p \times r_d \times r_c$. Assuming $P_0 = 101.325$ kPa and ideal expansion, the jet velocity (ignoring residual pressure thrust) is: $$C_j = \sqrt{2 c_{pg} T_{04}} = \sqrt{2 \times 1148 \times 1212.5} = \sqrt{2{,}783{,}250} \approx 1668 \text{ m/s}$$ This is supersonic — for a subsonic cruise, the expansion would not reach $P_0$. A more realistic $C_j \approx 700\text{–}800$ m/s. **Step 7:** Specific thrust: $$F_s = C_j - C_a = 750 - 250 = 500 \text{ N/(kg/s)}$$ --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **[KTU University Exam – May 2023]** > **(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the working principle of a turbojet engine. Show the various processes on a T-s diagram. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** A turbojet engine has the following data at a flight speed of 200 m/s at an altitude where $T_0 = 250$ K and $P_0 = 50$ kPa: Compressor pressure ratio = 8, Turbine inlet temperature = 1500 K, Combustion efficiency = 0.98, Calorific value of fuel = 43,000 kJ/kg. Calculate: (i) Fuel-air ratio, (ii) Thermal efficiency, (iii) Propulsive efficiency. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Sketch and Working:** Refer to Section 4.1 (Mermaid diagram) and Section 2.1 for the four processes described in detail. **(b) Numerical Solution:** **Step 1: Total/Ram Temperature** $$T_{01} = 250 + \frac{200^2}{2 \times 1005} = 250 + 19.9 = 269.9 \text{ K}$$ **Step 2: Compressor Exit (assuming no diffuser pressure rise modeled separately)** $$T_{03} = 269.9 \times (8)^{0.2857} = 269.9 \times 1.8114 = 488.9 \text{ K}$$ **Step 3: Fuel-Air Ratio** $$f = \frac{c_{pg}(T_{03,\text{turbine}} - T_{03})}{\eta_b \cdot CV} = \frac{1.148 \times (1500 - 488.9)}{0.98 \times 43{,}000}$$ $$f = \frac{1.148 \times 1011.1}{42{,}140} = \frac{1160.7}{42{,}140} = 0.02755$$ So **f ≈ 0.0276** (a typical value for turbojets is 0.02–0.03). **Step 4: Turbine Exit Temperature** Compressor work: $W_C = 1.005 \times (488.9 - 269.9) = 220.0$ kJ/kg $$T_{04} = 1500 - \frac{220.0}{1.148} = 1500 - 191.6 = 1308.4 \text{ K}$$ **Step 5: Jet Velocity (assume ideal expansion, ambient pressure)** $$C_j = \sqrt{2 \times 1148 \times 1308.4} = \sqrt{3{,}004{,}090} \approx 1733 \text{ m/s}$$ Using moderate expansion realistic value: $C_j \approx 800$ m/s (a more reasonable result considering non-idealities). **Step 6: Thermal Efficiency** $$\eta_{th} = \frac{C_j^2 - C_a^2}{2 f \cdot CV} = \frac{800^2 - 200^2}{2 \times 0.0276 \times 43{,}000} = \frac{600{,}000}{2{,}373{,}600} = 0.2527$$ **So $\eta_{th} \approx 25.27\%$** **Step 7: Propulsive Efficiency** $$\eta_p = \frac{2 C_a}{C_j + C_a} = \frac{2 \times 200}{800 + 200} = \frac{400}{1000} = 0.40$$ **So $\eta_p = 40\%$** **Step 8: Overall Efficiency** $$\eta_o = 0.2527 \times 0.40 = 0.1011 = 10.11\%$$ --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Turbojet Problems:** > 1. **Confusing $C_a$ with $C_j$:** Aircraft velocity $C_a$ is the flight speed; jet velocity $C_j$ is the exhaust speed. Mixing them is the #1 cause of incorrect answers. > 2. **Forgetting Ram Effect:** Students often skip the kinetic energy conversion $T_{01} = T_0 + C_a^2/(2c_p)$ and use $T_0$ directly. **Always account for ram.** > 3. **Mixing $c_p$ and $c_{pg}$:** Use $c_p = 1.005$ for compressor (cold air) and $c_{pg} = 1.148$ for turbine/nozzle (hot gas). Using $c_p$ everywhere yields wrong answers. > 4. **Work Balance Direction:** $W_T = W_C$, so the temperature DROP in the turbine equals the temperature RISE in the compressor (scaled by $c_p/c_{pg}$). Don't invert this. > 5. **Units of SFC:** $TSFC$ in kg/(kN·h) requires multiplication by $3.6 \times 10^6$ from SI units. Many students lose marks on unit conversion. > 6. **Neglecting Pressure Thrust:** When nozzle exit pressure $\neq$ ambient, the pressure thrust term $(P_5 - P_0)A_5$ must be added. For perfectly expanded nozzles, this is zero. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Turbojet** ✅ **Definition:** Air-breathing engine producing thrust by accelerating a mass of air (Newton's 3rd Law), based on the Brayton cycle. ✅ **Five Components (in order):** Diffuser → Compressor → Combustion Chamber → Turbine → Nozzle. ✅ **Four Processes:** Isentropic Compression → Constant-Pressure Heat Addition → Isentropic Expansion (Turbine) → Isentropic Expansion (Nozzle). ✅ **Five Station Numbers:** 0 (ambient), 1 (inlet), 2 (compressor inlet), 3 (compressor/turbine inlet), 4 (turbine exit/nozzle inlet), 5 (nozzle exit). ✅ **Key Equation — Thrust:** $F = \dot{m}(C_j - C_a) + (P_5 - P_0)A_5$ ✅ **Key Equation — Propulsive Efficiency:** $\eta_p = \dfrac{2 C_a}{C_j + C_a} = \dfrac{2}{1 + C_j/C_a}$ ✅ **Key Equation — Thermal Efficiency:** $\eta_{th} = \dfrac{C_j^2 - C_a^2}{2 f \cdot CV}$ ✅ **Key Equation — Overall Efficiency:** $\eta_o = \eta_{th} \cdot \eta_p = \dfrac{F \cdot C_a}{\dot{m}_f \cdot CV}$ ✅ **Work Balance:** $c_{pg}(T_{03} - T_{04}) = c_{pa}(T_{02} - T_{01}) / \eta_m$ ✅ **Ram Effect:** $T_{01} = T_0 + C_a^2/(2 c_p)$ — always include for flying engines. ✅ **Constants:** $c_{pa} = 1.005$, $c_{pg} = 1.148$, $\gamma = 1.4$, $\gamma_g = 1.333$, $CV \approx 43{,}100$ kJ/kg. ✅ **Optimum Velocity Ratio:** $C_j / C_a \approx 1.5 \text{ to } 2.5$ balances $\eta_{th}$ and $\eta_p$. ✅ **Difference from Turboprop:** Turbojet → all energy → jet thrust. Turboprop → most energy → propeller; small jet thrust. ✅ **Difference from Rocket:** Turbojet → air-breathing (uses atmospheric $O_2$). Rocket → carries its own oxidizer. ✅ **Limitation:** No static thrust production comparable to a propeller at low speeds; best at high subsonic/supersonic speeds.

topics.title: Ramjet
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET741 - GAS TURBINE AND JET PROPULSION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Ramjet

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

A **Ramjet** (also called a *flying stovepipe* or *athodyd* — *Aero-Thermo-Duct*) is the simplest form of **air-breathing jet propulsion engine** in which the air required for combustion is compressed entirely by the **ram effect** generated by the forward motion of the engine itself. Unlike a turbojet or turbofan, the ramjet contains **no rotating compressor or turbine**, and therefore has **no mechanical rotating components** in its main flow path. The forward velocity of the vehicle converts ambient kinetic energy of the air into a useful pressure rise across an **inlet diffuser**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Must Memorize):**
> *"A ramjet is a ducted, air-breathing, non-rotating, supersonic propulsion device in which compression of the inlet air is achieved solely by the conversion of the free-stream kinetic energy into static pressure through a divergent duct (diffuser)."*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Plain-English Intuition

Imagine you are riding a bicycle fast and a fly suddenly hits your open mouth. The fly is compressed hard inside your mouth — that compression came from your **motion**, not from any "blower" inside you. The ramjet works on the exact same principle:

| Everyday Analogy | Ramjet Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Squeezing air by blowing into a narrow tube | Diffuser (ram compression) |
| Adding perfume/spray to compressed air | Combustion chamber (heat addition at constant pressure) |
| Releasing the spray out — air rushes out fast | Propelling nozzle (expansion → thrust) |
| The bicycle/bottle moves forward | Thrust produced |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in KTU:** A common 3-mark question asks: *"Why does a ramjet not work at zero flight speed?"* The answer lies in this analogy — **no forward motion = no ram = no compression**. The minimum operating speed corresponds to **M ≈ 0.5 to 0.8**.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- Flight Mach range for **efficient operation**: **M = 2.0 to 5.0** (practical sweet spot)
- Theoretical lower limit: **M ≈ 0.5** (subsonic — compression too weak)
- Theoretical upper limit: **M ≈ 6 to 7** (above this, scramjet regime begins)
- Stagnation temperature ratio at diffuser exit: governed by **γ = 1.4** for air
- Typical combustion temperature: **T₀₄ ≈ 2200 K – 2800 K**
- Stagnation pressure recovery in diffuser: **η_D = 0.90 – 0.96** for supersonic flight
- Fuel used: **kerosene, hydrogen, or methane**

## 1.4 Why the Ramjet is Important — KTU 2024 Perspective

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 4 Spotlight:**
> Ramjets sit at the intersection of *high-speed aerodynamics* and *thermodynamics of jet propulsion*. They form the **theoretical baseline** against which all other air-breathing engines (turbojets, turbofans, scramjets) are compared. Most KTU numerical problems in Module 4 are framed as "treat the engine as an ideal ramjet and find thrust/efficiency."

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Working Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

A ramjet converts **fuel energy** into **propulsive kinetic energy** through the following thermodynamically distinct stages:

1. **Ramming (Station 1 → 2):** Free-stream air at velocity $V_0$ enters the diffuser. Because the duct diverges, $V$ decreases while $P$ rises. The kinetic energy of incoming air is partially converted into pressure energy.
2. **Subsonic diffusion & combustion (Station 2 → 3 → 4):** Air is decelerated further in a subsonic diffuser. Fuel is injected and burned at **approximately constant pressure** (ideally). Heat is added at $P \approx \text{const}$.
3. **Expansion & ejection (Station 4 → 9):** Hot, high-pressure gases accelerate through a convergent–divergent nozzle, producing an exhaust velocity $V_9 > V_0$. The momentum difference between the inlet and exit mass flows creates **thrust**.
4. **Cycle:** It is essentially the **Brayton (Joule) cycle** executed in open-flow form, without any rotating shaft work.

## 2.2 Station Numbering Convention (KTU Standard)

| Station | Location | Typical Property |
|:---:|---|:---|
| **0** | Ambient / far upstream | $T_0,\ P_0,\ V_\infty$ |
| **1** | Inlet face / cowl lip | Captures the free-stream |
| **2** | Diffuser exit / combustor inlet | Highest $P$ in the engine |
| **3** | Combustor exit (pre-turbine station; unused in pure ramjet) | — |
| **4** | Nozzle inlet / combustor exit (ramjet convention) | Highest $T$ |
| **9** (or 7) | Nozzle exit plane | $P_9 \approx P_0$ ideally |

> [!NOTE]
> In pure ramjet literature, the notation $0\to2\to4\to9$ is common because there is no turbine station. This is the convention KTU adopts in numericals.

## 2.3 High-Yield Formula Sheet (KTU Board Ready)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All variables below are assumed steady-state, 1-D, with air as calorically perfect unless stated.**

$$
\boxed{T_{0a} = T_0\!\left(1 + \frac{\gamma-1}{2}M^2\right)}
$$

$$
\boxed{P_{0a} = P_0\!\left(1 + \frac{\gamma-1}{2}M^2\right)^{\!\gamma/(\gamma-1)}}
$$

$$
\boxed{\rho_{0a} = \rho_0\!\left(1 + \frac{\gamma-1}{2}M^2\right)^{\!1/(\gamma-1)}}
$$

### Component Efficiencies

$$
\boxed{\eta_{D} = \frac{P_{02}-P_1}{P_{0a}-P_1}}
\qquad\text{(Diffuser / ram efficiency)}
$$

$$
\boxed{\eta_{b} = \frac{T_{04}-T_{02}}{T_{04s}-T_{02}}}
\qquad\text{(Combustion efficiency)}
$$

$$
\boxed{\eta_{N} = \dfrac{V_9^2-V_5^2}{V_{9s}^2-V_5^2}}
\qquad\text{(Nozzle efficiency)}
$$

### Thrust & Performance Relations

$$
\boxed{F = \dot{m}_a\!\left[(1+f)\,V_9 - V_0\right]}
$$

$$
\boxed{C_F \equiv \frac{F}{\dot{m}_a} = (1+f)V_9 - V_0}
\qquad\text{(Specific thrust, m/s)}
$$

$$
\boxed{F_{gross} - F_{ram\;drag} = \dot{m}_a\!\left[(1+f)V_9 - V_0\right] - \dot{m}_a\!\left[V_0 - V_1\right]}
$$

For a perfect inlet ($V_1 = V_0$): **ram drag is zero**, and only $F = \dot{m}_a[(1+f)V_9 - V_0]$ remains.

### Energy / Fuel Equation

$$
\boxed{f = \frac{C_p\,(T_{04}-T_{02})}{\eta_{b}\cdot \text{HCV}}}
$$

### Efficiencies

$$
\boxed{\eta_{\text{th}} = \dfrac{F\cdot V_0}{\dot{m}_a\,f\cdot \text{HCV}}}
\qquad\text{(Thermal efficiency)}
$$

$$
\boxed{\eta_{p} = \dfrac{2\,V_0}{V_9+V_0}}
\qquad\text{(Propulsive efficiency, ideal)}
$$

$$
\boxed{\eta_{o} = \eta_{\text{th}}\cdot\eta_{p}}
\qquad\text{(Overall efficiency)}
$$

### Compressor-Work Equivalent (Work Done by Ram)

$$
\boxed{W_{\text{ram}} = C_p\,(T_{02}-T_1)}
\qquad\text{(per unit mass of air)}
$$

### Stagnation Temperature Across Cycle

$$
\boxed{T_{0a} > T_{02} > T_{04}\;(\text{in some references } T_{04}>T_{02})}
$$

The chain is: $T_{0a}$ (free-stream stagnation) $\to$ $T_{02}$ (after diffuser loss) $\to$ $T_{04}$ (after heat addition).

## 2.4 Critical Compressibility Notes

- **Compressible flow relation** is mandatory when $M \geq 0.3$.
- The **ram compression ratio** $P_{02}/P_1 = (1 + \tfrac{\gamma-1}{2}M^2)^{\gamma/(\gamma-1)}$ is what replaces the mechanical compressor ratio of a turbojet.
- The **equivalent compression ratio** of a ramjet is therefore a function of flight Mach number alone — **not an independent design parameter**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:**
> Ramjets are used in **surface-to-air missiles** (e.g., the Russian 9M96E missile), **target drones**, **supersonic cruise research**, and the **integral ramjet** stage of **ramjet–rocket combined cycles (RBCC)** for access-to-space vehicles like Lockheed Martin's SR-72 concept. Understanding ramjet performance is therefore directly relevant to **modern hypersonic weapons systems** and **reusable launch architectures**.

---
