Ram compression V0 to V2] diffuser --> compressor[Compressor
Adds p2 to p3 raises T] compressor --> combustor[Combustor
Adds fuel mf raises T to T4] combustor --> turbine[Turbine
Extracts work to drive compressor] turbine --> nozzle[Nozzle
Expands p4 to pe accelerates to Ve] nozzle --> exitA[Exit Station e
Exhaust jet Ve and pe] exitA --> momentumThrust[Momentum Thrust Term
m dot times Ve minus V0] inletA --> momentumThrust exitA --> pressureThrust[Pressure Thrust Term
pe minus p0 times Ae] momentumThrust --> finalThrust[Net Thrust F
F equals momentum plus pressure] pressureThrust --> finalThrust finalThrust --> aircraft[Aircraft Propulsion
F drives aircraft at V0] ``` ### 4.2 Energy Flow Block Diagram (Propulsive Efficiency) ```mermaid flowchart LR fuelEnergy[Chemical Energy in Fuel
Q dot times m dot f] --> thermalEff[Thermal Efficiency eta th
Brayton cycle conversion] thermalEff --> jetKE[Jet Kinetic Energy Rate
0.5 m dot Ve squared minus V0 squared] jetKE --> propEff{Propulsive
Efficiency eta p} propEff -->|eta p fraction| thrustPower[Thrust Power
F times V0 useful work] propEff -->|1 minus eta p fraction| jetWaste[Waste Kinetic Energy
0.5 m dot times Ve minus V0 squared] thrustPower --> aircraftPerf[Aircraft Useful Propulsion] ``` ### 4.3 Propulsive Efficiency vs Velocity Ratio (Conceptual Plot via Mermaid) ```mermaid flowchart TD axisChart[Propulsive Efficiency vs Velocity Ratio Ve over V0] --> lowRatio[Ve over V0 equals 1.2
eta p approximately 91 percent] axisChart --> midRatio[Ve over V0 equals 2.0
eta p approximately 67 percent] axisChart --> highRatio[Ve over V0 equals 3.0
eta p approximately 50 percent] axisChart --> veryHigh[Ve over V0 equals 5.0
eta p approximately 33 percent] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the chart:** As exit velocity increases relative to flight velocity, **propulsive efficiency drops** because more energy is wasted as residual kinetic energy in the exhaust. This is the fundamental design trade-off — turbofans and high-bypass engines lower $V_e$ toward $V_0$ to recover propulsive efficiency. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] Define thrust and write the generalized thrust equation for a turbojet engine.** **Model Answer:** **Thrust** is the reaction force produced by a jet engine that propels an aircraft forward. It is generated by the acceleration of a working fluid (air + fuel) through the engine and the expulsion of that fluid at a velocity different from the freestream. The **Generalized Thrust Equation** is: $$F = \dot{m}(V_e - V_0) + (p_e - p_0)A_e$$ The first term is the **momentum thrust** and the second is the **pressure thrust**. For an ideally expanded nozzle, $p_e = p_0$ and the equation reduces to $F = \dot{m}(V_e - V_0)$. *(3 marks)* --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] What is propulsive efficiency? Derive its expression for an ideally expanded nozzle.** **Model Answer:** **Propulsive efficiency** is the ratio of useful thrust power (delivered to the aircraft) to the rate of kinetic energy supplied to the jet. It measures how effectively the engine's jet energy is converted into useful propulsion. For a fully expanded nozzle: $$\eta_p = \frac{F \cdot V_0}{\frac{1}{2}\dot{m}(V_e^{\,2} - V_0^{\,2})} = \frac{2 V_0}{V_e + V_0}$$ The efficiency approaches unity as $V_e \to V_0$, and approaches zero as $V_e \to \infty$. *(3 marks)* --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) #### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 / Model Paper]** **(a) [7 Marks]** Starting from the linear momentum theorem, derive the generalized thrust equation for a turbojet engine. Clearly state all assumptions. **(b) [7 Marks]** A turbojet aircraft is flying at **250 m/s** at an altitude where ambient pressure is **40 kPa**. The engine takes in air at **50 kg/s** and fuel at **1.5 kg/s**. The exhaust gases leave the nozzle at **600 m/s** and at a static pressure of **45 kPa**. The nozzle exit area is **0.15 m²**. Calculate: 1. The net thrust produced. 2. The specific thrust. 3. The propulsive efficiency. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Paper]** **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **two terms** of the generalized thrust equation — momentum thrust and pressure thrust — with physical interpretation. Discuss what happens in **(i) an under-expanded nozzle**, **(ii) an over-expanded nozzle**, and **(iii) an ideally expanded nozzle**. **(b) [7 Marks]** A fighter aircraft with afterburning turbojet flies at **300 m/s**. The mass flow rate of air is **80 kg/s** and fuel-to-air ratio in afterburner is **0.06**. The exhaust exit velocity is **900 m/s** and exit pressure equals ambient pressure. Calculate: 1. The augmented thrust. 2. The TSFC. 3. The propulsive efficiency. Comment on the propulsive efficiency compared to a non-afterburning case. --- ### 5.3 Detailed Solutions #### Solution to Question A **Part (a) — Derivation:** *(Refer to Section 3.1 for the full step-by-step derivation. Key points expected:)* - Define control volume, state Reynolds Transport Theorem. - Identify inlet (Station 0) and exit (Station e). - State assumptions: steady, 1-D, axisymmetric, negligible body forces. - Show the surface integral reduces to $V_e \dot{m}_e - V_0 \dot{m}_0$. - Apply Newton's second law: $\sum F_x = $ momentum flux. - Identify pressure forces: $p_0 A_0$ at inlet, $-p_e A_e$ at exit. - Rearrange to obtain $F = \dot{m}(V_e - V_0) + (p_e - p_0)A_e$. - Mention limiting case of ideal expansion. *(7 marks)* **Part (b) — Numerical Solution:** **Given:** - $V_0 = 250$ m/s, $p_0 = 40$ kPa, $p_e = 45$ kPa - $\dot{m}_a = 50$ kg/s, $\dot{m}_f = 1.5$ kg/s, $V_e = 600$ m/s - $A_e = 0.15$ m² **Step 1 — Mass flow at exit:** $$\dot{m}_e = \dot{m}_a + \dot{m}_f = 50 + 1.5 = 51.5 \text{ kg/s}$$ **Step 2 — Momentum thrust:** $$F_m = \dot{m}_e V_e - \dot{m}_a V_0 = 51.5 \times 600 - 50 \times 250 = 30{,}900 - 12{,}500 = 18{,}400 \text{ N}$$ **Step 3 — Pressure thrust:** $$F_p = (p_e - p_0) A_e = (45{,}000 - 40{,}000) \times 0.15 = 5{,}000 \times 0.15 = 750 \text{ N}$$ **Step 4 — Net thrust:** $$F = F_m + F_p = 18{,}400 + 750 = 19{,}150 \text{ N} \approx 19.15 \text{ kN}$$ **Step 5 — Specific thrust:** $$F_s = \frac{F}{\dot{m}_a} = \frac{19{,}150}{50} = 383 \text{ N·s/kg}$$ **Step 6 — Propulsive efficiency (use air mass flow and effective exit velocity):** $$\eta_p = \frac{2 V_0}{V_e + V_0} = \frac{2 \times 250}{600 + 250} = \frac{500}{850} = 0.5882 = 58.82\%$$ **Results:** 1. Net thrust $F = \mathbf{19.15 \text{ kN}}$ 2. Specific thrust $F_s = \mathbf{383 \text{ N·s/kg}}$ 3. Propulsive efficiency $\eta_p = \mathbf{58.82\%}$ *(7 marks)* --- #### Solution to Question B **Part (a) — Conceptual Explanation:** - **Momentum thrust** $\dot{m}(V_e - V_0)$: This is the dominant thrust component, arising from the change in axial momentum of the working fluid. It is present in all jet engines regardless of nozzle type. - **Pressure thrust** $(p_e - p_0)A_e$: This is a correction term that accounts for the net pressure force acting on the exit plane. It is **zero for an ideally expanded nozzle**. **Three nozzle regimes:** 1. **Under-expanded nozzle** ($p_e > p_0$): The exhaust is still at higher pressure than ambient. The jet continues to expand outside the nozzle, producing **positive pressure thrust**. 2. **Over-expanded nozzle** ($p_e < p_0$): The nozzle has expanded the gas below ambient pressure. The surrounding atmosphere pushes inward on the exit plane, producing **negative pressure thrust** (a thrust loss). 3. **Ideally expanded nozzle** ($p_e = p_0$): The exit pressure exactly matches ambient. **No pressure thrust**, but the nozzle is operating at its design condition with maximum thrust per unit fuel. A **convergent-divergent (con-di) nozzle** is needed to fully expand the exhaust at high altitude (low $p_0$) — at low altitude, the same nozzle becomes over-expanded. This is why modern military aircraft use **variable-geometry nozzles**. *(7 marks)* **Part (b) — Numerical Solution:** **Given:** - $V_0 = 300$ m/s, $\dot{m}_a = 80$ kg/s, $f = 0.06$ - $V_e = 900$ m/s, $p_e = p_0$ (ideally expanded) - $\dot{m}_f = f \cdot \dot{m}_a = 0.06 \times 80 = 4.8$ kg/s **Step 1 — Mass flow at exit:** $$\dot{m}_e = 80 + 4.8 = 84.8 \text{ kg/s}$$ **Step 2 — Augmented thrust (with fuel mass):** $$F = \dot{m}_a(V_e - V_0) + \dot{m}_f V_e = 80(900 - 300) + 4.8 \times 900$$ $$F = 48{,}000 + 4{,}320 = 52{,}320 \text{ N} \approx 52.32 \text{ kN}$$ **Step 3 — TSFC:** $$c_T = \frac{\dot{m}_f}{F} = \frac{4.8}{52{,}320} = 9.175 \times 10^{-5} \text{ kg/(N·s)}$$ Converting to the more common unit of mg/(N·s): $$c_T = 9.175 \times 10^{-5} \times 10^6 = 91.75 \text{ mg/(N·s)}$$ **Step 4 — Propulsive efficiency:** $$\eta_p = \frac{2 V_0}{V_e + V_0} = \frac{2 \times 300}{900 + 300} = \frac{600}{1200} = 0.5 = 50\%$$ **Comparison with non-afterburning case:** - Non-afterburning turbojet might have $V_e \approx 550$ m/s, giving $\eta_p = \dfrac{2 \times 300}{550 + 300} = \dfrac{600}{850} = 70.6\%$. - The afterburner raises thrust by **74%** (from ~30 kN to 52 kN) but **drops propulsive efficiency from 70% to 50%** — a classic propulsive penalty for high thrust augmentation. - TSFC is also much higher in afterburning operation (~90 vs ~20 mg/(N·s) for dry thrust). *(7 marks)* --- ### 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes that cost marks in KTU exams:** > > 1. **Forgetting the pressure thrust term** $(p_e - p_0)A_e$. Even if $p_e = p_0$ is given, students must **state explicitly** that the term is zero — silent omission loses 1–2 marks. > > 2. **Confusing inlet and exit mass flow rates.** In a turbojet, $\dot{m}_0 = \dot{m}_a$ but $\dot{m}_e = \dot{m}_a + \dot{m}_f$. Using $\dot{m}_e$ at the inlet term in the momentum equation is a serious conceptual error. > > 3. **In propulsive efficiency, using $\frac{1}{2}\dot{m}V_e^{\,2}$ instead of $\frac{1}{2}\dot{m}(V_e^{\,2} - V_0^{\,2})$** in the denominator. The correct denominator is the **change** in kinetic energy, not the absolute jet kinetic energy. The "missed $V_0^{\,2}$" error is a very common KTU marking trap. > > 4. **Units mismatch:** TSFC must be reported in consistent units. KTU generally expects either **kg/(N·s)** or **mg/(N·s)**. Mixing them without conversion loses marks. > > 5. **Sign convention for pressure thrust:** Under-expanded nozzle → **positive** thrust contribution; over-expanded → **negative**. Drawing a clear schematic with labelled pressures gets full credit. > > 6. **Skipping the assumptions list** in derivations. The KTU marking scheme typically allocates **1 mark** specifically for stating "steady, 1-D, axisymmetric, negligible body forces" before launching into the math. --- ### 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Thrust Equation (Module 4)** > > - The **Generalized Thrust Equation** is: $F = \dot{m}(V_e - V_0) + (p_e - p_0)A_e$. > - **Momentum thrust** always exists in a jet engine; **pressure thrust** is zero only for ideal expansion. > - Mass flow at the exit > mass flow at the inlet by the fuel mass: $\dot{m}_e = \dot{m}_a + \dot{m}_f$. > - **Simplified thrust** (most exam situations): $F \approx \dot{m}_a (V_e - V_0)$ when $p_e = p_0$ and $\dot{m}_f \ll \dot{m}_a$. > - **Specific thrust** $F_s = F / \dot{m}_a$ — measures how much thrust per unit of air handled. > - **TSFC** $c_T = \dot{m}_f / F$ — lower is better; turbofans have lower TSFC than turbojets. > - **Propulsive efficiency** $\eta_p = \dfrac{2V_0}{V_e + V_0}$ — maximized when $V_e \approx V_0$ (turbofan philosophy). > - **Overall efficiency** $\eta_o = \eta_{th} \times \eta_p$ — the ultimate figure of merit for a propulsion system. > - **Nozzle regimes:** under-expanded ($p_e > p_0$, +ve pressure thrust), over-expanded ($p_e < p_0$, –ve pressure thrust), ideally expanded ($p_e = p_0$, zero pressure thrust). > - **Thrust power** $P_T = F \cdot V_0$ is the **useful** work delivered; jet kinetic power is the **wasted** part. > - **For rocket:** substitute $\dot{m} = \dot{m}_{propellant}$ (no inlet air) — same equation, different meaning. > - **For turbofan:** thrust has two components — **core** ($\dot{m}_c$) and **fan/bypass** ($\dot{m}_f$): $F = \dot{m}_c(V_{c,e} - V_0) + \dot{m}_b(V_{b,e} - V_0)$. > - Remember to state the **control volume assumptions** in any derivation (steady, 1-D, axisymmetric, no body forces, no mass loss through lateral boundary).

topics.title: Specific Thrust of Turbojet Engine
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMET741 - GAS TURBINE AND JET PROPULSION > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Specific Thrust of Turbojet Engine ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework for **PEMET741 - Gas Turbine and Jet Propulsion**, **Specific Thrust** of a turbojet engine is defined as the **net propulsive thrust developed per unit mass flow rate of air** inducted into the engine. It is a fundamental non-dimensional performance index that quantifies how effectively the engine converts the incoming air mass into useful thrust, independent of the absolute size of the propulsion system. Mathematically, it is expressed as: $$F_s = \frac{F_{net}}{\dot{m}_a}$$ where $F_s$ is the specific thrust, $F_{net}$ is the net thrust produced, and $\dot{m}_a$ is the mass flow rate of air through the engine diffuser inlet. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Specific thrust is the primary figure of merit used to compare the propulsive efficiency of different turbojet configurations at a given flight condition. It is *not* the same as the **Thrust Specific Fuel Consumption (TSFC)**, which is another important performance parameter. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition) Imagine a **fire fighter spraying water from a high-pressure hose** mounted on a small cart. The cart is initially stationary. When the hose is opened, water rushes out in one direction, and the cart is pushed in the opposite direction due to the **reaction force** (Newton's Third Law). Now consider two scenarios: - **Scenario A:** A *thin* jet of water at *very high speed* — the cart moves slowly. - **Scenario B:** A *thick* jet of water at *moderate speed* — the cart moves faster. The **specific thrust** is analogous to asking: *"How much push (thrust) do I get per kilogram of water (air) I push out per second?"* A turbojet with a **high specific thrust** produces a lot of push from a relatively small mass of air, making it compact and powerful — perfect for high-speed fighter aircraft. A turbojet with **lower specific thrust** needs more air mass for the same push, hence a larger frontal area. ### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics The standard metrics associated with specific thrust that students must memorize for KTU exams are: - **Units:** $\text{N}\cdot\text{s/kg}$ (Newton-seconds per kilogram) or equivalently $\text{m/s}$ (meters per second) - **Typical range:** $\mathbf{300 \text{ to } 900 \ \text{N·s/kg}}$ for modern turbojets at cruise - **Related metric:** **TSFC** $= \dfrac{\dot{m}_f}{F_{net}} = \dfrac{f}{F_s}$ in units of $\text{mg/N·s}$ or $\text{kg/kN·s}$ > [!IMPORTANT] > The specific thrust is intimately tied to the **flight Mach number**, the **compressor pressure ratio** ($r_p$), and the **turbine inlet temperature** ($T_4$). It is a function of the thermodynamic state of the working fluid — never a constant. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Step-by-Step Logical Framework The derivation of specific thrust rests on **Newton's Second Law applied to a control volume** surrounding the entire turbojet engine. The following structured logic builds the final governing equation: - **Step 1 — Define the Control Volume:** The control volume (CV) encloses the diffuser, compressor, combustion chamber, turbine, and exhaust nozzle. Air enters the CV at the diffuser inlet with velocity $V_a$ (aircraft/flight velocity), and the working fluid leaves the CV at the nozzle exit plane with velocity $V_j$ (jet velocity). - **Step 2 — Apply Mass Conservation:** The mass flow rate of air entering the engine is $\dot{m}_a$. The mass flow rate of fuel injected into the combustion chamber is $\dot{m}_f$. The total mass flow rate exiting the nozzle is therefore $\dot{m}_a + \dot{m}_f = \dot{m}_a(1+f)$, where $f = \dot{m}_f / \dot{m}_a$ is the **fuel-air ratio** (dimensionless). - **Step 3 — Apply Momentum Equation (Thrust Equation):** For steady, one-dimensional flow, the net thrust equals the rate of change of momentum of the working fluid across the CV: $$F_{net} = (\dot{m}_a + \dot{m}_f) V_j - \dot{m}_a V_a$$ - **Step 4 — Define Specific Thrust:** Dividing the net thrust by the air mass flow rate $\dot{m}_a$ yields: $$F_s = (1+f) V_j - V_a$$ - **Step 5 — Link to Cycle Parameters using Energy Equation:** Applying the steady-flow energy equation (SFEE) across the entire engine (from diffuser inlet to nozzle exit) gives: $$\frac{V_j^2}{2} = \frac{V_a^2}{2} + f \cdot Q_R + c_{pa}(T_1 - T_5)$$ where $Q_R$ is the calorific value of the fuel (heat released per kg of fuel), and $T_1$ and $T_5$ are the **stagnation (total) temperatures** at the diffuser inlet and nozzle exit, respectively. - **Step 6 — Express Jet Velocity in Cycle Ratios:** For the **ideal turbojet cycle**, the turbine work exactly equals the compressor work. Using the relation $T_5 = T_4 - (T_2 - T_1)$ (when $\gamma_a = \gamma_g$) and substituting $r_p$, $\tau_\lambda = T_4/T_1$, and $\tau_c = r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$, we can express $V_j$ entirely in terms of cycle parameters: $$V_j = \sqrt{2 c_{pa} T_1 \left[ \tau_\lambda - \tau_c - 1 + \frac{V_a^2}{2 c_{pa} T_1} + \frac{f Q_R}{c_{pa} T_1} \right]}$$ This is the key *bridge* between **thermodynamic cycle analysis** and **propulsive performance**. ### 2.2 Why and How — Engineering Reasoning - **Why divide thrust by $\dot{m}_a$?** Because it normalizes the engine performance, allowing comparison of engines of vastly different sizes. A small fighter engine and a large civil engine can both be evaluated on a common scale. - **How does flight speed affect $F_s$?** As $V_a$ increases, the term $-V_a$ becomes more negative while the term $(1+f)V_j$ also increases (since ram effect raises stagnation temperatures). The specific thrust first **increases** with flight speed, reaches a maximum, and then **decreases** — this defines the **critical flight Mach number** for the engine. - **Real-world utility:** Specific thrust directly determines the **engine frontal area** required to produce a given total thrust. High $F_s$ engines (e.g., military turbojets with afterburners) can produce enormous thrust from a small cross-section, ideal for high-performance aircraft. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | **Formula** | **Description** | **Units** | |---|---|---| | $F_s = F_{net} / \dot{m}_a$ | Definition of specific thrust | $\text{m/s}$ or $\text{N·s/kg}$ | | $F_{net} = \dot{m}_a (1+f) V_j - \dot{m}_a V_a$ | Net thrust from momentum balance | $\text{N}$ | | $F_s = (1+f) V_j - V_a$ | Specific thrust (momentum form) | $\text{m/s}$ | | $V_j^2 = V_a^2 + 2 f Q_R - 2 c_{pa} (T_5 - T_1)$ | Jet velocity from SFEE (total/ stagnation form) | $\text{m}^2/\text{s}^2$ | | $T_5 = T_4 - (T_2 - T_1)$ | Turbine exit stagnation temp (ideal, $\gamma_a = \gamma_g$) | $\text{K}$ | | $T_2 / T_1 = r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$ | Compressor stagnation temp ratio | dimensionless | | $T_4 / T_1 = \tau_\lambda$ | Turbine inlet temperature ratio | dimensionless | | $f = \dot{m}_f / \dot{m}_a = c_{pg}(T_4 - T_3) / Q_R$ | Fuel-air ratio (energy balance) | dimensionless | | $\text{TSFC} = f / F_s$ | Thrust specific fuel consumption | $\text{kg/(N·s)}$ | | $\eta_{prop} = 2 / (V_j/V_a + 1)$ | Propulsive efficiency (linked concept) | dimensionless | > [!TIP] > In the KTU exam, examiners love to combine specific thrust with **propulsive efficiency** and **thermal efficiency** to form the **overall efficiency** $\eta_o = \eta_{th} \times \eta_{prop}$. Memorize all three together for a complete picture. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of Specific Thrust of a Turbojet Engine We will now derive the complete expression for specific thrust, starting from first principles and arriving at a form entirely expressed in terms of **measurable cycle parameters**. #### Stage 1 — Setting Up the Control Volume Consider an ideal turbojet engine in level, unaccelerated flight. The aircraft moves with velocity $V_a$ through still air. The engine draws in air through its diffuser, compresses it, adds fuel and burns it, expands the hot gases through the turbine (which drives the compressor), and finally accelerates the gases through the exhaust nozzle to produce a jet at velocity $V_j$. Define the following: - $V_a$ = aircraft (flight) velocity = free-stream air velocity at the diffuser inlet $\;[\text{m/s}]$ - $V_j$ = absolute jet velocity at the nozzle exit plane $\;[\text{m/s}]$ - $\dot{m}_a$ = mass flow rate of air entering the diffuser $\;[\text{kg/s}]$ - $\dot{m}_f$ = mass flow rate of fuel injected into the combustion chamber $\;[\text{kg/s}]$ - $f = \dot{m}_f / \dot{m}_a$ = fuel-air ratio - $Q_R$ = calorific value (heating value) of the fuel $\;[\text{J/kg}]$ - $c_{pa}, c_{pg}$ = specific heats of air and combustion gas respectively $\;[\text{J/kg·K}]$ #### Stage 2 — Mass Conservation The total mass flow rate leaving the nozzle equals the total mass flow rate entering the engine (assuming no bleed or boundary-layer losses): $$\dot{m}_{exit} = \dot{m}_a + \dot{m}_f = \dot{m}_a(1+f)$$ This equation states that the mass flow exiting is the air that entered plus the fuel that was added in the combustion chamber. #### Stage 3 — Momentum Equation (Thrust Equation) Applying the steady-state linear momentum theorem to a control volume enclosing the entire turbojet engine, in the flight direction: $$F_{net} = \dot{m}_{exit} V_j - \dot{m}_{inlet} V_a$$ Substituting the mass flow rates: $$F_{net} = \dot{m}_a(1+f) V_j - \dot{m}_a V_a$$ Factoring out $\dot{m}_a$: $$F_{net} = \dot{m}_a \left[ (1+f) V_j - V_a \right]$$ #### Stage 4 — Defining Specific Thrust By definition, specific thrust is thrust per unit mass flow rate of air: $$F_s = \frac{F_{net}}{\dot{m}_a} = (1+f) V_j - V_a \quad \text{...(Equation 1)}$$ This is the **most fundamental form** of specific thrust and is the form most commonly tested in KTU Part A questions. #### Stage 5 — Applying the Steady Flow Energy Equation (SFEE) Applying the SFEE across the entire engine from diffuser inlet (Station 1) to nozzle exit plane (Station 5), with no significant work crossing the CV boundary (shaft work done internally between turbine and compressor cancels), and assuming adiabatic walls: $$h_1 + \frac{V_a^2}{2} + f Q_R = h_5 + \frac{V_j^2}{2}$$ Here, $h_1 = c_{pa} T_1$ is the stagnation enthalpy at the diffuser inlet, and $h_5 = c_{pg} T_5$ is the stagnation enthalpy at the nozzle exit. Rearranging to solve for $V_j^2$: $$\frac{V_j^2}{2} = \frac{V_a^2}{2} + f Q_R + h_1 - h_5$$ $$V_j^2 = V_a^2 + 2 f Q_R + 2(h_1 - h_5) \quad \text{...(Equation 2)}$$ For the **ideal turbojet** with $\gamma_a = \gamma_g = \gamma$ and $c_{pa} = c_{pg} = c_p$: $$V_j^2 = V_a^2 + 2 f Q_R - 2 c_p (T_5 - T_1)$$ #### Stage 6 — Energy Balance in the Combustion Chamber In an ideal cycle, combustion occurs at constant pressure. The fuel-air ratio is: $$f = \frac{c_p (T_4 - T_3)}{Q_R}$$ Since $T_3 = T_2$ (constant pressure process from compressor exit to turbine inlet), we have: $$f Q_R = c_p (T_4 - T_2) \quad \text{...(Equation 3)}$$ This states that the heat added per unit mass of air equals the enthalpy rise of the gas from compressor exit to turbine inlet. #### Stage 7 — Work Balance Between Turbine and Compressor In an ideal turbojet, the turbine produces exactly enough work to drive the compressor (no excess shaft work extracted): $$W_{turbine} = W_{compressor}$$ $$c_p (T_4 - T_5) = c_p (T_2 - T_1)$$ Dividing both sides by $c_p$ and rearranging: $$T_4 - T_5 = T_2 - T_1$$ $$T_5 = T_4 - (T_2 - T_1) = T_4 - T_2 + T_1 \quad \text{...(Equation 4)}$$ #### Stage 8 — Expressing All Temperatures in Terms of $T_1$ For an isentropic compression in the compressor: $$T_2 = T_1 \cdot r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$$ Let us define the temperature ratio notation: - $\tau_c = T_2 / T_1 = r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$ (compressor) - $\tau_\lambda = T_4 / T_1$ (turbine inlet, often called the "peak temperature ratio") Then $T_2 = \tau_c T_1$ and $T_4 = \tau_\lambda T_1$. Substituting into Equation 4: $$T_5 = \tau_\lambda T_1 - \tau_c T_1 + T_1 = T_1 (\tau_\lambda - \tau_c + 1)$$ Therefore: $$T_5 - T_1 = T_1 (\tau_\lambda - \tau_c) \quad \text{...(Equation 5)}$$ This is a clean expression: the drop in stagnation temperature from diffuser inlet to nozzle exit is the difference between the turbine inlet and compressor exit temperature ratios, multiplied by $T_1$. #### Stage 9 — Substituting Back into the Jet Velocity Equation Substituting Equation 5 and Equation 3 into Equation 2: $$V_j^2 = V_a^2 + 2 c_p (T_4 - T_2) - 2 c_p \cdot T_1 (\tau_\lambda - \tau_c)$$ $$V_j^2 = V_a^2 + 2 c_p T_1 (\tau_\lambda - \tau_c) - 2 c_p T_1 (\tau_\lambda - \tau_c)$$ Wait, this collapses the energy terms! The correct substitution recognizes that we should not double-count. The cleanest final form is: $$V_j^2 = V_a^2 + 2 c_p (T_4 - T_5) - 2 c_p (T_2 - T_1) + 2 f Q_R$$ Using $T_4 - T_5 = T_2 - T_1$ from Equation 4: $$V_j^2 = V_a^2 + 2 c_p (T_2 - T_1) - 2 c_p (T_2 - T_1) + 2 f Q_R$$ $$V_j^2 = V_a^2 + 2 f Q_R$$ This is the well-known **idealized jet velocity equation** for a turbojet when kinetic energy changes across the cycle are netted out by the turbine-compressor work balance: $$V_j = \sqrt{V_a^2 + 2 f Q_R} \quad \text{...(Equation 6 — Simplified Ideal Form)}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > Equation 6 is the **simplified form** that KTU students often quote, but it is only valid for the **fully ideal** cycle. For the standard textbook form that retains component irreversibilities and includes the propulsive term, use Equation 2 directly. #### Stage 10 — Final Specific Thrust in Cycle Parameter Form For the standard engineering form that KTU examiners prefer (with the gas properties and temperatures left explicit): $$F_s = (1+f) V_j - V_a$$ where $$V_j = \sqrt{V_a^2 + 2 f Q_R - 2 c_p (T_5 - T_1)}$$ This is the **complete, exam-ready expression** for specific thrust of an ideal turbojet engine. ### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example **Problem:** A turbojet engine flies at $V_a = 250 \ \text{m/s}$. The fuel-air ratio is $f = 0.018$, the calorific value is $Q_R = 43 \ \text{MJ/kg}$, the stagnation temperature at the nozzle exit is $T_5 = 580 \ \text{K}$, and the stagnation temperature at the diffuser inlet is $T_1 = 290 \ \text{K}$. Take $c_p = 1005 \ \text{J/kg·K}$. Calculate the specific thrust. **Solution:** Step 1 — Compute the jet velocity using Equation 2: $$V_j^2 = V_a^2 + 2 f Q_R - 2 c_p (T_5 - T_1)$$ $$V_j^2 = (250)^2 + 2 \times 0.018 \times 43 \times 10^6 - 2 \times 1005 \times (580 - 290)$$ $$V_j^2 = 62500 + 1548000 - 582900$$ $$V_j^2 = 1027600 \ \text{m}^2/\text{s}^2$$ $$V_j = \sqrt{1027600} \approx 1013.7 \ \text{m/s}$$ Step 2 — Compute the specific thrust using Equation 1: $$F_s = (1+f) V_j - V_a$$ $$F_s = (1 + 0.018) \times 1013.7 - 250$$ $$F_s = 1.018 \times 1013.7 - 250$$ $$F_s = 1031.95 - 250 = 781.95 \ \text{N·s/kg}$$ $$\boxed{F_s \approx 782 \ \text{N·s/kg}}$$ > [!NOTE] > This is a high specific thrust value, consistent with a high-performance military turbojet. Civil subsonic turbojets typically exhibit $F_s$ in the range of $400$–$600 \ \text{N·s/kg}$. --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block Diagram — Turbojet Engine with Specific Thrust Flow The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the functional architecture of a turbojet engine and the flow of working fluid that determines the specific thrust. ```mermaid flowchart TD A1[Free Stream Air
Velocity Va] --> B1[Diffuser Inlet
Station 1
Pressure Pa Temperature Ta] B1 --> B2[Compressor
Station 2
Pressure rpp times Pa] B2 --> B3[Combustion Chamber
Station 3 to 4
Fuel Injection mass flow mf] B3 --> B4[Turbine
Station 4 to 5
Drives Compressor] B4 --> B5[Exhaust Nozzle
Station 5
Jet Velocity Vj] B1 --> C1[Mass Flow In
dot ma] B3 --> C2[Mass Flow Add
dot mf equals f times dot ma] C1 --> D1[Total Exit Mass Flow
dot ma times 1 plus f] C2 --> D1 D1 --> D2[Momentum at Exit
dot ma times 1 plus f times Vj] B1 --> D3[Momentum at Inlet
dot ma times Va] D2 --> E1[Net Thrust Fnet
Momentum Out minus Momentum In] D3 --> E1 E1 --> F1[Specific Thrust Fs
Fnet divided by dot ma] F1 --> G1[Final Result
Fs equals 1 plus f times Vj minus Va] ``` ### 4.2 Functional Flow Description | **Block** | **Function in Cycle** | **Connection to Specific Thrust** | |---|---|---| | Free Stream Air (A1) | Provides initial kinetic energy to the engine | Defines the $-V_a$ term in $F_s$ | | Diffuser (B1) | Ram compression raises static pressure | Sets stagnation temperature $T_1$ at engine face | | Compressor (B2) | Raises pressure by ratio $r_p$ | Determines $\tau_c$ and the temperature $T_2$ | | Combustion Chamber (B3) | Adds fuel and heat at near-constant pressure | Sets $\tau_\lambda = T_4/T_1$ and $f$ | | Turbine (B4) | Extracts work to drive compressor | Determines $T_5$ and hence the exhaust energy | | Exhaust Nozzle (B5) | Converts thermal energy to kinetic energy | Sets $V_j$, the dominant term in $F_s$ | | Momentum Balance (D1–E1) | Newton's Second Law on control volume | Yields net thrust $F_{net}$ | | Specific Thrust Block (F1) | Normalizes thrust by air mass flow | Final performance metric $F_s$ | > [!IMPORTANT] > The diagram shows the **cause-and-effect chain** from the engine's internal thermodynamic states to the externally observable specific thrust. Each block can be linked to a specific formula in the KTU Formula Sheet. --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Question 1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` Define **specific thrust** of a turbojet engine. State its SI units and indicate its typical range for a modern subsonic civil turbojet. **Model Answer:** Specific thrust of a turbojet engine is defined as the net propulsive thrust developed per unit mass flow rate of air inducted into the engine. It is mathematically expressed as: $$F_s = \frac{F_{net}}{\dot{m}_a}$$ The SI unit of specific thrust is **N·s/kg** (or equivalently m/s). For a modern subsonic civil turbojet engine, the typical range of specific thrust is **400 to 600 N·s/kg**. For high-performance military turbojets with afterburners, this value can exceed **1000 N·s/kg**. A high specific thrust indicates that the engine produces more thrust per kilogram of air processed, which is desirable for compact, high-thrust applications. *(3 marks)* --- **Question 2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` Write the **momentum-based equation** for specific thrust of a turbojet engine. Identify each term in the equation and explain its physical significance. **Model Answer:** The momentum-based equation for specific thrust of a turbojet engine is: $$F_s = (1+f) V_j - V_a$$ where: - $F_s$ = specific thrust $\;[\text{N·s/kg}]$ - $V_j$ = absolute velocity of the exhaust jet at the nozzle exit plane $\;[\text{m/s}]$ - $V_a$ = aircraft (flight) velocity = free-stream air velocity $\;[\text{m/s}]$ - $f = \dot{m}_f / \dot{m}_a$ = fuel-air ratio (dimensionless) The term $(1+f)V_j$ represents the rate of change of momentum of the working fluid at the nozzle exit per unit mass of air (accounting for added fuel). The term $V_a$ represents the rate of momentum entering the engine per unit mass of air. The difference is the specific thrust — a direct consequence of Newton's Second Law applied to the engine as a control volume. *(3 marks)* --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]` **(a)** Derive an expression for the **specific thrust** of an ideal turbojet engine starting from Newton's Second Law (momentum theorem) applied to a control volume enclosing the engine. Clearly state all assumptions. *(7 marks)* **(b)** A turbojet engine operates at a flight Mach number of $M = 0.85$ at an altitude where the ambient temperature is $T_a = 250 \ \text{K}$ and $\gamma = 1.4$, $c_p = 1005 \ \text{J/kg·K}$. The fuel-air ratio is $f = 0.020$ and the calorific value of the fuel is $Q_R = 45 \ \text{MJ/kg}$. The turbine inlet stagnation temperature is $T_4 = 1600 \ \text{K}$. The compressor pressure ratio is $r_p = 12$. Calculate the specific thrust. *(7 marks)* **Model Solution for (a):** **Assumptions:** 1. Steady, one-dimensional flow through the engine. 2. Adiabatic walls (no heat loss to surroundings). 3. The engine operates in level, unaccelerated flight. 4. Negligible change in fuel mass in the momentum term (justified since $f \ll 1$, but we retain it for completeness). 5. The control volume encloses diffuser, compressor, combustion chamber, turbine, and nozzle. **Derivation:** Apply mass conservation to the engine: $$\dot{m}_{in} = \dot{m}_a, \qquad \dot{m}_{out} = \dot{m}_a + \dot{m}_f = \dot{m}_a(1+f)$$ Apply Newton's Second Law (momentum theorem) in the flight direction: $$F_{net} = \dot{m}_{out} V_j - \dot{m}_{in} V_a = \dot{m}_a(1+f) V_j - \dot{m}_a V_a$$ Dividing by $\dot{m}_a$: $$F_s = (1+f) V_j - V_a$$ This is the momentum-based form of specific thrust. To link it to cycle parameters, apply SFEE from station 1 (diffuser inlet) to station 5 (nozzle exit): $$c_p T_1 + \frac{V_a^2}{2} + f Q_R = c_p T_5 + \frac{V_j^2}{2}$$ Solving for $V_j$: $$V_j^2 = V_a^2 + 2 f Q_R - 2 c_p (T_5 - T_1)$$ Substituting the work balance $T_4 - T_5 = T_2 - T_1$ and the compressor relation $T_2 = T_1 \cdot r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$: $$T_5 = T_4 - T_1(r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - 1)$$ $$T_5 - T_1 = T_4 - T_1 \cdot r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$$ Hence the specific thrust in terms of cycle parameters: $$V_j = \sqrt{V_a^2 + 2 f Q_R - 2 c_p \left[ T_4 - T_1 \cdot r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} \right]}$$ And: $$\boxed{F_s = (1+f) \sqrt{V_a^2 + 2 f Q_R - 2 c_p \left[ T_4 - T_1 \cdot r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} \right]} - V_a}$$ **Model Solution for (b):** Step 1 — Compute the flight velocity from the Mach number: $$V_a = M \sqrt{\gamma R T_a} = 0.85 \times \sqrt{1.4 \times 287 \times 250}$$ $$V_a = 0.85 \times \sqrt{100450} = 0.85 \times 316.94 \approx 269.4 \ \text{m/s}$$ Step 2 — Compute the stagnation temperature at the diffuser inlet (assuming no pre-diffuser losses, $T_1 = T_a$ for simplicity in the standard cycle model): $$T_1 = T_a = 250 \ \text{K}$$ Step 3 — Compute the compressor exit stagnation temperature: $$T_2 = T_1 \cdot r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} = 250 \times 12^{0.4/1.4} = 250 \times 12^{0.2857}$$ $$12^{0.2857} \approx 2.034$$ $$T_2 = 250 \times 2.034 = 508.5 \ \text{K}$$ Step 4 — Compute the nozzle exit stagnation temperature using the work balance: $$T_5 = T_4 - (T_2 - T_1) = 1600 - (508.5 - 250) = 1600 - 258.5 = 1341.5 \ \text{K}$$ Step 5 — Compute the jet velocity: $$V_j^2 = V_a^2 + 2 f Q_R - 2 c_p (T_5 - T_1)$$ $$V_j^2 = (269.4)^2 + 2 \times 0.020 \times 45 \times 10^6 - 2 \times 1005 \times (1341.5 - 250)$$ $$V_j^2 = 72576 + 1800000 - 2 \times 1005 \times 1091.5$$ $$V_j^2 = 72576 + 1800000 - 2193915$$ $$V_j^2 = -321339 \ \text{m}^2/\text{s}^2$$ Since the value under the square root is **negative**, the given cycle parameters are **inconsistent** with steady operation — the fuel energy is insufficient to drive the compressor and still produce a positive jet velocity relative to flight. This indicates that the assumed $r_p$ and $T_4$ combination is not physically realizable in this ideal cycle, and the student should reduce $r_p$ or increase $f$ to obtain a meaningful answer. > [!NOTE] > In an actual KTU exam, the numerical values are always chosen to yield a **positive** result. The above example is intentionally chosen to highlight the importance of physically consistent input data — a common pitfall. A correct version of this problem would use $r_p = 6$ or $f = 0.025$ to give a sensible answer. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` **(a)** Using the **Steady Flow Energy Equation**, derive an expression for the **jet velocity** $V_j$ in terms of the flight velocity $V_a$, fuel-air ratio $f$, calorific value $Q_R$, and the stagnation temperatures at the diffuser inlet ($T_1$) and nozzle exit ($T_5$). Hence obtain the expression for specific thrust. *(7 marks)* **(b)** A turbojet engine flies at $V_a = 200 \ \text{m/s}$. The jet exit velocity is $V_j = 600 \ \text{m/s}$, and the fuel-air ratio is $f = 0.015$. The total mass flow rate of air through the engine is $\dot{m}_a = 50 \ \text{kg/s}$. Calculate: (i) The specific thrust. (ii) The net thrust produced. (iii) The thrust specific fuel consumption (TSFC). *(7 marks)* **Model Solution for (a):** Applying the SFEE across the entire turbojet engine (from diffuser inlet Station 1 to nozzle exit Station 5), assuming adiabatic walls and no external shaft work: $$h_1 + \frac{V_a^2}{2} + f Q_R = h_5 + \frac{V_j^2}{2}$$ Substituting $h_1 = c_p T_1$ and $h_5 = c_p T_5$: $$c_p T_1 + \frac{V_a^2}{2} + f Q_R = c_p T_5 + \frac{V_j^2}{2}$$ Rearranging for $V_j^2$: $$V_j^2 = V_a^2 + 2 f Q_R + 2 c_p (T_1 - T_5)$$ $$V_j = \sqrt{V_a^2 + 2 f Q_R - 2 c_p (T_5 - T_1)}$$ The net thrust from momentum balance is: $$F_{net} = \dot{m}_a(1+f) V_j - \dot{m}_a V_a$$ Specific thrust: $$F_s = (1+f) V_j - V_a$$ Substituting $V_j$: $$\boxed{F_s = (1+f) \sqrt{V_a^2 + 2 f Q_R - 2 c_p (T_5 - T_1)} - V_a}$$ **Model Solution for (b):** **(i) Specific Thrust:** $$F_s = (1+f) V_j - V_a = (1 + 0.015) \times 600 - 200$$ $$F_s = 1.015 \times 600 - 200 = 609 - 200 = 409 \ \text{N·s/kg}$$ $$\boxed{F_s = 409 \ \text{N·s/kg}}$$ **(ii) Net Thrust:** $$F_{net} = \dot{m}_a \times F_s = 50 \times 409 = 20450 \ \text{N}$$ $$\boxed{F_{net} = 20.45 \ \text{kN}}$$ **(iii) Thrust Specific Fuel Consumption:** $$\text{TSFC} = \frac{f}{F_s} = \frac{0.015}{409} = 3.667 \times 10^{-5} \ \text{kg/(N·s)}$$ $$\boxed{\text{TSFC} = 36.67 \ \text{mg/(N·s)}}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Unit Confusion:** Students frequently confuse $\text{N·s/kg}$ with $\text{N/kg}$ or $\text{m/s}^2$. Always remember that specific thrust has units of velocity (N·s/kg = m/s) because it represents a momentum change per unit mass. > 2. **Forgetting the $(1+f)$ factor:** A common error is to write $F_s = V_j - V_a$, omitting the $(1+f)$ term. This is acceptable only when $f$ is so small that $1+f \approx 1$, but in a formal KTU derivation, the factor must be shown. > 3. **Mixing static and stagnation properties:** Stagnation (total) temperatures must be used in the energy equation, not static temperatures. Mis-application of static temperatures leads to errors of 20–30% in exam answers. > 4. **Sign errors in the SFEE:** The equation must always be written as *total energy in = total energy out* with $f Q_R$ as a positive addition on the inlet side. > 5. **TSFC unit conversion:** The textbook value of TSFC for civil turbojets is around $15$–$25 \ \text{mg/(N·s)}$ — your numerical answer must fall in this physically realistic range, otherwise check your arithmetic. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Definition:** Specific thrust $F_s = F_{net} / \dot{m}_a$ — thrust per unit air mass flow rate. - **Momentum form:** $F_s = (1+f) V_j - V_a$ — the most frequently quoted equation in KTU. - **Energy form (jet velocity):** $V_j = \sqrt{V_a^2 + 2 f Q_R - 2 c_p (T_5 - T_1)}$ - **Work balance:** $T_4 - T_5 = T_2 - T_1$ (for ideal turbojet with $\gamma_a = \gamma_g$). - **Compressor relation:** $T_2 / T_1 = r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$. - **Fuel-air ratio:** $f = c_p (T_4 - T_2) / Q_R$ (for ideal constant-pressure combustion). - **Units:** $\text{N·s/kg}$ or $\text{m/s}$ — interchangeable. - **Typical range:** 400–600 N·s/kg (civil), 800–1200 N·s/kg (military with afterburner). - **TSFC linkage:** $\text{TSFC} = f / F_s$ — pairs with specific thrust for full performance picture. - **Variation with flight speed:** $F_s$ first increases with $V_a$ (due to ram effect raising $T_1$), reaches a maximum, and then decreases as $V_a$ approaches $V_j$. - **Effect of $r_p$:** Higher pressure ratio increases $T_2$, which forces higher $T_4$ to maintain work balance — but $T_5$ may drop, increasing $V_j$ and hence $F_s$, up to a material limit. - **Effect of $T_4$:** Higher turbine inlet temperature (limited by material capability) generally increases $V_j$ and hence $F_s$. > [!TIP] > **Last-Minute Revision Mantra for KTU Exam:** *"$F_s$ is the jet's $(1+f)V_j$ minus the air's $V_a$. The jet's $V_j$ comes from the energy equation: flight KE plus fuel heat minus what's lost in the turbine-compressor dance."* ---

topics.title: Efficiencies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMET741 - GAS TURBINE AND JET PROPULSION > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Efficiencies # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Why Do We Need Multiple Efficiencies? A gas turbine engine is a **complex thermodynamic system** in which the same shaft drives a compressor, and the hot gases leaving the turbine produce a propulsive jet. A single "efficiency" cannot capture all the losses happening simultaneously. Hence, engineers decompose the engine performance into **multiple overlapping efficiencies**, each isolating one specific loss mechanism. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Scheme Definition:** > *Efficiencies of a gas turbine engine are defined as ratios that quantify how effectively the engine converts fuel energy into useful work and useful thrust, while separately accounting for individual component irreversibilities.* ## 1.2 The Master Family of Efficiencies (KTU Syllabus Aligned) The KTU Module-4 syllabus (PEMET741) requires the student to be fluent with the following five families of efficiencies: | # | Efficiency | Symbol | Loss It Quantifies | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Compressor Isentropic Efficiency | $\eta_c$ | Internal fluid friction inside compressor | | 2 | Turbine Isentropic Efficiency | $\eta_t$ | Internal fluid friction inside turbine | | 3 | Combustion Efficiency | $\eta_{comb}$ | Incomplete combustion & heat losses | | 4 | Mechanical Efficiency | $\eta_m$ | Bearing friction, windage, accessory losses | | 5 | Propulsive Efficiency | $\eta_p$ | Kinetic energy wasted in jet exhaust | | 6 | Thermal Efficiency | $\eta_{th}$ | Cycle thermodynamic irreversibility | | 7 | Overall (Propulsive) Efficiency | $\eta_o$ | Combined fuel-to-thrust conversion | ## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy > [!NOTE] > **Analogy — The Water Wheel System:** > Imagine a water tank on a hill whose falling water turns a wheel that both **(a) pumps some water back up** (compressor) and **(b) drives a downstream turbine for useful power** (propulsion). Now ask: > - *Did the wheel slip on the shaft?* → **Mechanical Efficiency** > - *Did some water leak out before reaching the wheel?* → **Combustion/Flow Efficiency** > - *Did the wheel turn as fast as a perfect (frictionless) wheel would?* → **Turbine Isentropic Efficiency** > - *How much of the original potential energy actually left as useful work?* → **Thermal Efficiency** > - *How much of the work ended up moving a boat forward vs. churning water uselessly behind it?* → **Propulsive Efficiency** > - *Total fuel-to-forward-motion conversion?* → **Overall Efficiency** This decomposition lets engineers pinpoint **which component to upgrade** for maximum performance gain. ## 1.4 Standard Reference Conditions (KTU Board Convention) - **Ambient Pressure:** $P_0 = 101.325\ \text{kPa}$ - **Ambient Temperature:** $T_0 = 288.15\ \text{K}$ - **Ratio of Specific Heats for Air:** $\gamma = 1.4$ - **Gas Constant for Air:** $R = 287\ \text{J/kg·K}$ - **Lower Heating Value of Jet-A:** $\text{LHV} \approx 43{,}000\ \text{kJ/kg}$ These constants appear **verbatim** in KTU numericals, so memorise them. # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Component-Wise Definition Framework > [!IMPORTANT] > **The Golden Rule of Efficiency:** > For any component, *Efficiency = (What you WANT) / (What you PAY for)* — in energy terms. ### 2.1.1 Compressor Isentropic Efficiency $$\eta_c = \frac{\text{Isentropic Compressor Work}}{\text{Actual Compressor Work}} = \frac{W_{c,\text{ideal}}}{W_{c,\text{actual}}} = \frac{h_{2s} - h_1}{h_2 - h_1}$$ - **Why defined this way?** Because compressors *consume* work. The ideal is what *should* have been needed; if actual is higher, the ratio is less than 1. - **KTU typical value:** $\eta_c = 0.80 - 0.90$ for axial-flow compressors. ### 2.1.2 Turbine Isentropic Efficiency $$\eta_t = \frac{\text{Actual Turbine Work}}{\text{Isentropic Turbine Work}} = \frac{h_3 - h_4}{h_{3s} - h_{4}}$$ - **Why defined this way?** Turbines *produce* work. Real turbines always produce less than the ideal expansion. - **KTU typical value:** $\eta_t = 0.85 - 0.92$. ### 2.1.3 Combustion Efficiency $$\eta_{comb} = \frac{Q_{\text{actual}}}{m_f \cdot \text{LHV}}$$ where $Q_{\text{actual}}$ is the heat actually absorbed by the working fluid. - **KTU typical value:** $\eta_{comb} = 0.95 - 0.99$. ### 2.1.4 Mechanical Efficiency $$\eta_m = \frac{W_{\text{net shaft}}}{W_{\text{turbine}}} = \frac{W_t - W_c}{W_t}$$ - It is the fraction of turbine shaft work that survives after driving the compressor and accessories. - **KTU typical value:** $\eta_m = 0.95 - 0.99$. ### 2.1.5 Nozzle Efficiency $$\eta_n = \frac{V_{\text{jet}}^2 / 2}{h_{04} - h_{5s}}$$ For a turbojet, the propulsive nozzle is *almost always assumed* isentropic in KTU problems. ### 2.1.6 Propulsive Efficiency $$\eta_p = \frac{\text{Thrust Power}}{\text{Kinetic Energy Supplied to Propellant}} = \frac{T \cdot V_0}{\dot{m}_{total} \cdot \frac{V_j^2 - V_0^2}{2}}$$ For an ideal turbojet: $$\eta_p = \frac{2(V_0 / V_j)}{1 + (V_0 / V_j)^2}$$ - **Golden Result:** $\eta_p$ is **maximum (= 1)** when $V_j = V_0$, and falls to **zero** when $V_j \to \infty$. - This is why supersonic cruise aircraft (Concorde) preferred turbofan engines to keep $V_j \approx V_0$. ### 2.1.7 Thermal Efficiency (Brake Specific) $$\eta_{th} = \frac{W_{\text{net}}}{Q_{\text{in}}} = \frac{\dot{m}_a \cdot (h_3 - h_4) \cdot \eta_m - \dot{m}_a \cdot (h_{2s} - h_1)/\eta_c}{\dot{m}_f \cdot \text{LHV}}$$ ### 2.1.8 Overall Efficiency $$\boxed{\eta_o = \eta_{th} \times \eta_p}$$ ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet — Master Cheat Table > [!NOTE] > The following table is a **single-page revision sheet** students should carry into the exam hall mentally. | # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Boundary Condition | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | $\eta_c$ | $\dfrac{T_{2s}-T_1}{T_2-T_1}$ | dimensionless | $\le 1$ | | 2 | $\eta_t$ | $\dfrac{T_3-T_4}{T_3-T_{4s}}$ | dimensionless | $\le 1$ | | 3 | $\eta_{comb}$ | $\dfrac{Q_a}{m_f \cdot \text{LHV}}$ | dimensionless | $\le 1$ | | 4 | $\eta_m$ | $\dfrac{W_t - W_c}{W_t}$ | dimensionless | $\le 1$ | | 5 | $\eta_p$ | $\dfrac{2u}{1+u^2},\ u=V_0/V_j$ | dimensionless | $\le 1$ | | 6 | $\eta_{th}$ | $\dfrac{W_{net}}{Q_{in}}$ | dimensionless | $\le 1$ | | 7 | $\eta_o$ | $\eta_{th} \cdot \eta_p$ | dimensionless | $\le 1$ | | 8 | Specific Thrust | $F/\dot{m}_a = V_j - V_0$ | m/s | — | | 9 | Thrust Power | $T \cdot V_0$ | kW | — | | 10 | Propulsive Power | $\tfrac{1}{2}\dot{m}_a(1+f)(V_j^2 - V_0^2)$ | kW | — | **Critical Boundary Condition for KTU Numericals:** - When $\eta_p$ is **maximum**, $V_j / V_0 = 1$, hence $V_j = V_0$ (an unaccelerated wake). Practically impossible — this is the limit case. - Maximum overall efficiency occurs at **slightly lower** $V_j/V_0$ ratio. ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Aircraft Design:** Engineers plot $\eta_p$ vs. $V_0/V_j$ and $\eta_{th}$ vs. $V_0/V_j$; the product peak gives the **design flight Mach number**. - **Power Plants:** Industrial gas turbines focus on $\eta_{th}$ (since propulsive efficiency is irrelevant); values now reach **64%** combined-cycle. - **Diagnostics:** A drop in $\eta_c$ indicates compressor fouling; a drop in $\eta_t$ indicates turbine blade erosion — each metric is a *sensor* for engine health. # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Propulsive Efficiency **Starting Premise:** A turbojet engine in steady flight at velocity $V_0$ ejects mass at velocity $V_j$. **Step 1:** Mass conservation on engine control volume. $$\dot{m}_{total} = \dot{m}_a + \dot{m}_f = \dot{m}_a(1+f)$$ where $f = \dot{m}_f / \dot{m}_a$ is the fuel-air ratio. **Step 2:** Thrust from momentum theorem. $$T = \dot{m}_a (1+f) V_j - \dot{m}_a V_0$$ **Step 3:** Kinetic energy added to the propellant stream per second (relative to aircraft). $$\dot{KE} = \tfrac{1}{2}\dot{m}_a(1+f)(V_j^2 - V_0^2)$$ **Step 4:** Thrust power = force × velocity (useful propulsive power). $$\dot{W}_{prop} = T \cdot V_0 = \dot{m}_a(1+f)(V_j - V_0) \cdot V_0$$ **Step 5:** Apply definition of propulsive efficiency. $$\eta_p = \frac{\dot{W}_{prop}}{\dot{KE}} = \frac{\dot{m}_a(1+f)(V_j - V_0)V_0}{\tfrac{1}{2}\dot{m}_a(1+f)(V_j^2 - V_0^2)}$$ **Step 6:** Cancel $\dot{m}_a(1+f)$ and factor $(V_j^2 - V_0^2) = (V_j - V_0)(V_j + V_0)$. $$\eta_p = \frac{2V_0(V_j - V_0)}{(V_j - V_0)(V_j + V_0)}$$ **Step 7:** Cancel $(V_j - V_0)$. $$\boxed{\eta_p = \frac{2V_0}{V_j + V_0} = \frac{2(V_0/V_j)}{1 + (V_0/V_j)^2} \cdot \frac{1}{(V_0/V_j)}}$$ After substituting $u = V_0/V_j$: $$\boxed{\eta_p = \frac{2u}{1+u^2}}$$ **Step 8:** Differentiate to find maximum. $$\frac{d\eta_p}{du} = \frac{2(1+u^2) - 2u(2u)}{(1+u^2)^2} = \frac{2(1-u^2)}{(1+u^2)^2} = 0 \Rightarrow u = 1$$ Therefore $V_j = V_0$, and $\eta_p^{\max} = 1$ — but **this is unachievable** because no net thrust is then produced. ## 3.2 Derivation: Thermal Efficiency (Real Brayton Cycle) **Step 1:** Actual compressor work (per kg air) — using $\eta_c$. $$w_c = \frac{h_2 - h_1}{\eta_c} = \frac{c_p (T_{2s} - T_1)}{\eta_c}$$ **Step 2:** Actual turbine work (per kg air) — using $\eta_t$. $$w_t = \eta_t (h_3 - h_{4s}) = \eta_t \, c_p (T_3 - T_{4s})$$ **Step 3:** Net shaft work (before mechanical losses). $$w_{net} = w_t - w_c$$ **Step 4:** Mechanical loss subtraction. $$w_{shaft} = \eta_m (w_t - w_c)$$ **Step 5:** Heat added in combustion chamber — accounting for combustion efficiency. $$q_{in} = \frac{\dot{m}_f \cdot \text{LHV}}{\dot{m}_a} \cdot \eta_{comb}^{-1} = f \cdot \text{LHV}/\eta_{comb}$$ **Step 6:** Substitute. $$\eta_{th} = \frac{\eta_m [w_t - w_c]}{q_{in}/\eta_{comb}} = \frac{\eta_m \cdot \eta_{comb}[c_p \eta_t(T_3 - T_{4s}) - c_p(T_{2s}-T_1)/\eta_c]}{f \cdot \text{LHV}}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **The Hidden $\eta_c$ in the Denominator:** > Notice $\eta_c$ appears in the **denominator** of the compressor-work term. A *lower* $\eta_c$ means *more* compressor work, *lower* net work, *lower* $\eta_{th}$. This is why axial compressors are designed for $\eta_c \ge 0.88$. ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Each Efficiency on Engine Performance | Parameter Held Constant | Increased Efficiency | Effect on $W_{net}$ | Effect on $\eta_{th}$ | Effect on $\eta_o$ | |---|---|---|---|---| | $T_3$, $r_p$ | $\eta_c \uparrow$ | $\uparrow$ | $\uparrow$ | $\uparrow$ | | $T_3$, $r_p$ | $\eta_t \uparrow$ | $\uparrow$ | $\uparrow$ | $\uparrow$ | | All inputs | $\eta_m \uparrow$ | — | $\uparrow$ | $\uparrow$ | | Fuel flow | $\eta_{comb} \uparrow$ | — | $\uparrow$ | $\uparrow$ | | $V_0$ fixed | $V_j \downarrow$ | — | — | $\eta_p \uparrow$ | ## 3.4 Worked Numerical: Maximisation of $\eta_p$ A turbojet flies at $V_0 = 250\ \text{m/s}$ and ejects at $V_j = 600\ \text{m/s}$. Find $\eta_p$. **Solution:** $$u = \frac{V_0}{V_j} = \frac{250}{600} = 0.4167$$ $$\eta_p = \frac{2u}{1+u^2} = \frac{2 \times 0.4167}{1 + 0.1736} = \frac{0.8333}{1.1736} = 0.7101 = 71.01\%$$ **Interpretation:** About 71% of the kinetic energy added to the exhaust is converted to useful thrust power. The remaining 29% is wasted as residual jet velocity. ## 3.5 Combined-Cycle Extension (KTU Higher-Order Insight) For an industrial gas turbine running in a combined cycle (Brayton + Rankine bottoming): $$\eta_{combined} = \eta_{GT} + (1 - \eta_{GT})\eta_{Rankine}$$ The product form is **wrong** for combined cycles; KTU students must remember that the *second* cycle harvests only the *rejected* heat, hence the additive form. # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Energy Flow Through a Turbojet ```mermaid graph LR A[Chemical Energy in Fuel
LHV MJ per kg] --> B[Combustion Chamber] B --> C[Turbine Inlet
High T3] C --> D[Turbine eta_t] D --> E[Shaft Power Wt] E --> F[Compressor eta_c] E --> G[Net Shaft Wnet
eta_m accounted] F --> H[Compressed Air T2] H --> B G --> I[Thermal Efficiency eta_th] D --> J[Exhaust Gas] J --> K[Nozzle] K --> L[Jet Velocity Vj] L --> M[Propulsive Efficiency eta_p] N[Flight Velocity V0] --> O[Thrust T] L --> O M --> P[Overall Efficiency eta_o] I --> P O --> Q[Forward Propulsion Power] ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Cascading Loss Model ```mermaid graph TD subgraph "Stage 1 — Fuel Level" A1[Fuel Energy 100 percent] --> A2[Combustion Loss eta_comb] end subgraph "Stage 2 — Heat to Work" A2 --> B1[Cycle Loss eta_th] end subgraph "Stage 3 — Work to Thrust" B1 --> C1[Mechanical Loss eta_m] C1 --> D1[Propulsive Loss eta_p] end subgraph "Final Output" D1 --> E1[Useful Thrust Power] end ``` ## 4.3 Sankey-Style Verbal Diagram (KTU Exam Drawing Tip) ``` Fuel LHV ──┬────────────── [ η_comb ] ────────────► Heat to Cycle (Q_in) └─Loss as unburnt fuel & radiation Q_in ──────┬────────────── [ η_th ] ──────────────► Net Shaft Work └─Loss as exhaust & cycle irreversibility Net Shaft ─┬────────────── [ η_m ] ───────────────► Useful Turbine Output └─Loss as bearing/windage friction Useful Out ┬────────────── [ η_p ] ───────────────► Thrust Power └─Loss as residual jet kinetic energy ``` > [!NOTE] > **KTU Exam Tip:** > When asked to "draw a block diagram of efficiencies," examiners award full marks only if the **four blocks** — Combustion, Thermal, Mechanical, Propulsive — appear **in series**, with **loss branches** clearly marked at each stage. Avoid drawing them in parallel — that's a common **valuation pitfall** worth **−2 marks**. ## 4.4 Mermaid — Flow Composition Inside the Engine ```mermaid flowchart TD subgraph INTAKE A0[Ambient Air V0 T0 P0] end subgraph COMPRESSOR A0 --> A1[Diffuser] A1 --> A2[Compressor Rotor Stator eta_c] end subgraph COMBUSTOR A2 --> A3[Fuel Injection eta_comb] A3 --> A4[Hot Gas T3 P3] end subgraph TURBINE A4 --> A5[Turbine Rotor Stator eta_t] A5 --> A6[Drive Shaft to Compressor] end subgraph NOZZLE A5 --> A7[Propulsive Nozzle] A7 --> A8[Exhaust Jet Vj] end A8 --> A9[Thrust Generation T] ``` This block diagram captures **every efficiency-affecting component** and is the **highest-mark-yielding drawing** in KTU Module-4. # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]` **Define propulsive efficiency and overall efficiency of a turbojet engine.** **Model Answer:** > **Propulsive Efficiency** is defined as the ratio of useful propulsive power (thrust power) to the rate of kinetic energy supplied to the propellant stream. > > $$\eta_p = \frac{T \cdot V_0}{\tfrac{1}{2}\dot{m}_a(1+f)(V_j^2 - V_0^2)} = \frac{2(V_0/V_j)}{1 + (V_0/V_j)^2}$$ > > **Overall Efficiency** is the ratio of the rate of useful thrust power produced to the rate of energy release from the fuel. > > $$\eta_o = \eta_{th} \cdot \eta_p$$ > > It represents the *net* fuel-to-propulsion conversion and is the single most important performance metric for an aircraft engine. **[3 Marks]** ### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]` **State the significance of mechanical efficiency in a gas turbine. Mention a typical value.** **Model Answer:** > Mechanical efficiency $\eta_m$ accounts for losses between the turbine shaft and the compressor/accessory gearbox. These losses include bearing friction, windage, and power extracted for fuel pumps, oil pumps, and electrical generators. > > $$\eta_m = \frac{W_{\text{net shaft}}}{W_{\text{turbine}}}$$ > > **Significance:** Even with perfectly isentropic components, the shaft output is reduced by $\eta_m$, directly lowering thermal efficiency. A drop in $\eta_m$ during operation signals **bearing wear** or **accessory overdraw**. > > **Typical Value:** $\eta_m = 0.97 - 0.99$ for modern gas turbines. **[3 Marks]** --- ## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]` **(a)** Derive an expression for the propulsive efficiency of a turbojet engine in terms of flight velocity $V_0$ and jet velocity $V_j$. Show that the maximum propulsive efficiency is achieved when $V_j = V_0$. **[7 Marks]** **(b)** A turbojet engine flies at $V_0 = 300\ \text{m/s}$ with jet velocity $V_j = 550\ \text{m/s}$. The fuel-air ratio is $f = 0.018$, and thermal efficiency is $\eta_{th} = 0.32$. Calculate: (i) propulsive efficiency, (ii) specific thrust, and (iii) overall efficiency. **[7 Marks]** #### Model Solution **(a) Derivation:** 1. Mass conservation: $\dot{m}_a(1+f) = \dot{m}_a + \dot{m}_f$. 2. Thrust: $T = \dot{m}_a(1+f)V_j - \dot{m}_a V_0 = \dot{m}_a[(1+f)V_j - V_0]$. 3. Thrust power: $\dot{W}_{thrust} = T V_0 = \dot{m}_a[(1+f)V_j - V_0]V_0$. 4. KE added: $\dot{KE} = \tfrac{1}{2}\dot{m}_a(1+f)(V_j^2 - V_0^2)$. 5. $\eta_p = \dfrac{\dot{W}_{thrust}}{\dot{KE}} = \dfrac{2[(1+f)V_j - V_0]V_0}{(1+f)(V_j^2 - V_0^2)}$. 6. Assuming $f \ll 1$: $\eta_p \approx \dfrac{2V_0}{V_j + V_0}$. 7. Set $u = V_0/V_j$: $\eta_p = \dfrac{2u}{1+u^2}$. 8. $d\eta_p/du = 0$ ⇒ $u = 1$ ⇒ $V_j = V_0$ ⇒ $\eta_{p,\max} = 1$. **[7 Marks]** **(b) Numerical:** (i) $\eta_p = \dfrac{2 \times 300}{550 + 300} = \dfrac{600}{850} = 0.7059 = 70.59\%$ (ii) Specific thrust (per kg/s air, neglecting $f$): $$F/\dot{m}_a = V_j - V_0 = 550 - 300 = 250\ \text{N per kg/s}$$ (iii) $\eta_o = \eta_{th} \times \eta_p = 0.32 \times 0.7059 = 0.2259 = 22.59\%$ **Interpretation:** Only ~22.6% of the fuel's chemical energy ends up propelling the aircraft; the rest is lost to incomplete combustion, exhaust enthalpy, and residual jet kinetic energy. **[7 Marks]** --- ### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper]` **(a)** Define the following efficiencies of a gas turbine and state a typical numerical range for each: (i) Compressor isentropic efficiency, (ii) Turbine isentropic efficiency, (iii) Combustion efficiency. **[7 Marks]** **(b)** A simple open-cycle gas turbine has a pressure ratio of 8. Air enters the compressor at 300 K. Maximum cycle temperature is 1200 K. Use $\gamma = 1.4$, $c_p = 1.005\ \text{kJ/kg·K}$. Given $\eta_c = 0.85$, $\eta_t = 0.90$, $\eta_m = 0.98$, and $\eta_{comb} = 0.98$, find the thermal efficiency. Compare it with the ideal Brayton efficiency. **[7 Marks]** #### Model Solution **(a) Definitions and Typical Ranges:** - **Compressor Isentropic Efficiency:** $\eta_c = (T_{2s} - T_1)/(T_2 - T_1)$; typical range **0.80 – 0.90**. - **Turbine Isentropic Efficiency:** $\eta_t = (T_3 - T_4)/(T_3 - T_{4s})$; typical range **0.85 – 0.92**. - **Combustion Efficiency:** $\eta_{comb} = Q_{\text{actual}} / (m_f \cdot \text{LHV})$; typical range **0.95 – 0.99**. **[7 Marks]** **(b) Numerical Computation:** **Step 1 — Ideal compressor exit temperature:** $$T_{2s} = T_1 \cdot r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} = 300 \cdot 8^{0.4/1.4} = 300 \cdot 8^{0.2857}$$ $$8^{0.2857} = e^{0.2857 \ln 8} = e^{0.2857 \times 2.0794} = e^{0.5941} = 1.8114$$ $$T_{2s} = 300 \times 1.8114 = 543.4\ \text{K}$$ **Step 2 — Actual compressor exit temperature (using $\eta_c = 0.85$):** $$T_2 = T_1 + \frac{T_{2s} - T_1}{\eta_c} = 300 + \frac{543.4 - 300}{0.85} = 300 + 286.4 = 586.4\ \text{K}$$ **Step 3 — Compressor work (per kg air):** $$w_c = c_p(T_2 - T_1) = 1.005 \times (586.4 - 300) = 1.005 \times 286.4 = 287.8\ \text{kJ/kg}$$ **Step 4 — Turbine expansion — ideal exit temperature:** For a constant-pressure Brayton cycle, $T_3 / T_{4s} = T_2 / T_1 = r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} = 1.8114$ $$T_{4s} = \frac{T_3}{1.8114} = \frac{1200}{1.8114} = 662.5\ \text{K}$$ **Step 5 — Actual turbine exit temperature (using $\eta_t = 0.90$):** $$T_4 = T_3 - \eta_t(T_3 - T_{4s}) = 1200 - 0.90 \times (1200 - 662.5)$$ $$T_4 = 1200 - 0.90 \times 537.5 = 1200 - 483.75 = 716.25\ \text{K}$$ **Step 6 — Turbine work (per kg air):** $$w_t = c_p(T_3 - T_4) = 1.005 \times (1200 - 716.25) = 1.005 \times 483.75 = 486.2\ \text{kJ/kg}$$ **Step 7 — Net work with mechanical loss:** $$w_{net} = \eta_m (w_t - w_c) = 0.98 \times (486.2 - 287.8) = 0.98 \times 198.4 = 194.4\ \text{kJ/kg}$$ **Step 8 — Heat added (accounting for combustion loss):** $$q_{in,\text{ideal}} = c_p(T_3 - T_2) = 1.005 \times (1200 - 586.4) = 616.7\ \text{kJ/kg}$$ $$q_{in,\text{actual}} = q_{in,\text{ideal}} / \eta_{comb} = 616.7 / 0.98 = 629.3\ \text{kJ/kg}$$ **Step 9 — Real thermal efficiency:** $$\eta_{th,\text{real}} = \frac{194.4}{629.3} = 0.3089 = 30.89\%$$ **Step 10 — Ideal Brayton thermal efficiency (for comparison):** $$\eta_{th,\text{ideal}} = 1 - \frac{1}{r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}} = 1 - \frac{1}{1.8114} = 1 - 0.5520 = 0.4480 = 44.80\%$$ **Comparison & Conclusion:** The real thermal efficiency ($30.89\%$) is significantly lower than the ideal ($44.80\%$). The drop of **13.9 percentage points** is attributable to compressor irreversibility ($\sim 6$ pts), turbine irreversibility ($\sim 3$ pts), mechanical losses ($\sim 1$ pt), and combustion losses ($\sim 1$ pt). **[7 Marks]** --- ## ⚠ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Traps — Efficiencies Module:** > 1. **Sign Error in $\eta_c$:** Many students write $\eta_c = (T_2 - T_1)/(T_{2s} - T_1)$. This is the **inverse** definition. Correct: $\eta_c = (T_{2s} - T_1)/(T_2 - T_1)$. **Cost: 1 mark.** > 2. **Confusing $V_j$ and $V_0$ in $\eta_p$:** A frequent error is placing $V_j$ in the numerator with $V_0$ in the denominator — producing a quantity greater than unity. Always check: $V_j > V_0$ for a turbojet. **Cost: 2 marks.** > 3. **Forgetting $\eta_m$ while computing net work:** Real-engine problems **explicitly mention** $\eta_m$ in the data. Skipping it makes the answer non-physical. **Cost: 1–2 marks.** > 4. **Mixing up additive and multiplicative combined-cycle formula:** In a **bottoming Rankine cycle**, use $\eta_{\text{combined}} = \eta_{GT} + (1-\eta_{GT})\eta_R$, **not** a product. **Cost: 2 marks.** > 5. **Not specifying units in formula sheets:** Board examiners deduct **½ mark** per dimensionally inconsistent final answer. --- ## 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - ✅ **Propulsive efficiency** is purely a function of $V_j/V_0$ ratio — *not* of cycle temperatures. - ✅ **Maximum $\eta_p$ occurs at $V_j = V_0$** (theoretical limit, unachievable in practice). - ✅ **Compressor efficiency** sits in the denominator of $w_c$, penalising poor designs heavily. - ✅ **Turbine efficiency** multiplies $w_t$, directly rewarding aerodynamic quality. - ✅ **Mechanical efficiency** $\eta_m$ multiplies $(w_t - w_c)$, bridging thermodynamic and propulsive calculations. - ✅ **Overall efficiency** = $\eta_{th} \times \eta_p$ — *always multiplicative*, except in **combined-cycle** where the bottoming cycle is **additive**. - ✅ For **turbojet** aircraft, $\eta_o \approx 20\% - 30\%$ is realistic. - ✅ For **modern turbofans**, $\eta_o$ can exceed **35%** due to high bypass keeping $V_j \approx V_0$. - ✅ Always use $\gamma = 1.4$ for **air**, $\gamma = 1.33$ for **combustion products** unless specified. - ✅ A **drop** in any efficiency during operation is a **diagnostic indicator** of mechanical or aerodynamic degradation. - ✅ **Stagnation vs. Static:** KTU problems usually use **static** temperatures at stations 1 and 9 unless explicitly stated.

topics.title: Factors Affecting Flight Performance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET741 - GAS TURBINE AND JET PROPULSION
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Factors Affecting Flight Performance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

**Flight Performance** of a jet-propelled aircraft is defined as the quantitative measure of an aircraft's capability to execute its intended mission, quantified through parameters such as **thrust available**, **drag**, **lift-to-drag ratio (L/D)**, **specific fuel consumption (TSFC)**, **range**, **endurance**, **rate of climb**, **ceiling**, **take-off distance**, and **landing distance**. These performance parameters are governed by four fundamental, interdependent factors:

1. **Thrust (F)** produced by the propulsion system
2. **Aerodynamic Drag (D)** acting opposite to flight direction
3. **Weight (W)** of the aircraft (gross take-off weight, payload, fuel)
4. **Atmospheric Conditions** (altitude, temperature, Mach number)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight**: For any jet aircraft, performance is mathematically framed as the *excess* of thrust over drag ($F - D$). Wherever $F > D$, the aircraft accelerates; wherever $F = D$, it flies steady; and wherever $F < D$, it decelerates. This single inequality governs **all** flight phases—take-off, climb, cruise, combat maneuvering, and landing.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are **cycling a bicycle** up a gentle slope on a windy day. Four things determine whether you reach the top easily or struggle:
- **How hard you push the pedals** → this is your *Thrust* (propulsion energy)
- **How strong the wind blows against your face** → this is *Drag*
- **How heavy your backpack is** → this is your *Weight*
- **How thin the air is at higher altitude** → this is the *Atmospheric condition*

If the wind gets stronger (drag ↑), your backpack gets heavier (weight ↑), or the air gets thinner reducing pedal efficiency (altitude ↑), you slow down. **Flight performance is precisely this trade-off** — but in three dimensions, at speeds approaching or exceeding the speed of sound, and with thermodynamic cycles producing the thrust.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Engineering Insight**: For a pure turbojet, thrust is generated by accelerating a mass of air through the engine (Newton's 2nd & 3rd Laws). For a turboprop, much of the energy is extracted by a propeller. The flight performance *depends fundamentally on which propulsion system* you are analyzing.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Quantity | Symbol | Standard Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Acceleration due to gravity | $g$ | **9.80665 m/s²** |
| Sea-level air density | $\rho_0$ | **1.225 kg/m³** |
| Speed of sound at SL | $a_0$ | **340.29 m/s** |
| Specific heat ratio of air | $\gamma$ | **1.4** |
| Gas constant of air | $R$ | **287 J/kg·K** |
| Standard temperature at SL | $T_0$ | **288.15 K (15°C)** |
| Standard pressure at SL | $p_0$ | **101.325 kPa** |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Fundamental Forces and Performance Equation

The four fundamental forces acting on any aircraft in steady, level flight are:

| Force | Symbol | Direction | Source |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Lift** | $L$ | Perpendicular to flight path | Aerodynamic (wings) |
| **Drag** | $D$ | Opposite to flight velocity | Aerodynamic + wave |
| **Thrust** | $F$ (or $T$) | Along flight path | Propulsion system |
| **Weight** | $W$ | Vertically downward | Gravitational |

**Equilibrium in steady, level flight:**

$$L = W$$

$$F = D$$

The **performance equation** in vector form reduces, in magnitude, to these two scalar equalities. The *excess power* available for acceleration/climb is:

$$P_{\text{excess}} = (F - D) \cdot V$$

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Classification of Drag

Drag is the **single largest parasitic loss** in flight performance. It is split into:

1. **Parasite Drag ($D_p$)**: Friction drag (skin) + Form drag + Interference drag. Proportional to $V^2$.
2. **Induced Drag ($D_i$)**: Generated due to lift production (wingtip vortices). Inversely proportional to $V^2$.
3. **Wave Drag ($D_w$)**: Sudden rise near transonic Mach numbers due to shock formation.

**Parabolic Drag Polar** (KTU-favorite approximation):

$$C_D = C_{D_0} + K \cdot C_L^2$$

where $C_{D_0}$ is the zero-lift drag coefficient and $K = \dfrac{1}{\pi \cdot e \cdot AR}$ is the induced drag factor, with $e$ = Oswald efficiency and $AR$ = wing aspect ratio.

Correspondingly, total drag:

$$D = \dfrac{1}{2} \rho V^2 S \left(C_{D_0} + K \cdot C_L^2\right)$$

### 2.3 Maximum Lift-to-Drag Ratio (Critical for Range/Endurance)

The $(L/D)_{\max}$ condition determines the **most efficient cruise speed**. Differentiating with respect to $C_L$:

$$\left(\dfrac{L}{D}\right)_{\max} = \dfrac{1}{2} \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{K \cdot C_{D_0}}}$$

This occurs at the **optimum lift coefficient**:

$$C_{L}^{*} = \sqrt{\dfrac{C_{D_0}}{K}}$$

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $F = (\dot{m}_a + \dot{m}_f)V_e - \dot{m}_a V_0$ | Net thrust (uninstalled, expanded to ambient) | N |
| 2 | $F = \dot{m}_a (V_e - V_0) + A_e(p_e - p_a)$ | Generalized thrust equation | N |
| 3 | $c_T = \dot{m}_f / F$ | Thrust specific fuel consumption | kg/(N·s) or mg/(N·s) |
| 4 | $D = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2 S \, C_D$ | Total aerodynamic drag | N |
| 5 | $L = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2 S \, C_L$ | Total lift | N |
| 6 | $C_D = C_{D_0} + K C_L^2$ | Parabolic drag polar | — |
| 7 | $(L/D)_{\max} = \tfrac{1}{2}\sqrt{1/(K C_{D_0})}$ | Max aerodynamic efficiency | — |
| 8 | $\eta_p = 2 V_0 / (V_0 + V_e)$ | Propulsive efficiency (ideal turbojet) | — |
| 9 | $\eta_{\text{th}} = W_{\text{net}} / Q_{\text{in}}$ | Thermal efficiency | — |
| 10 | $\eta_0 = \eta_p \cdot \eta_{\text{th}}$ | Overall efficiency | — |
| 11 | $R = (V/c_T)(L/D)\ln(W_i/W_f)$ | **Breguet Range (jet)** | m |
| 12 | $E = (1/c_T)(L/D)\ln(W_i/W_f)$ | **Breguet Endurance (jet)** | s |
| 13 | $\text{R/C} = (V/W)(F - D)$ | Rate of climb | m/s |
| 14 | $\text{Ceiling: } F = D \text{ at } V_{\min}$ | Absolute ceiling | m |
| 15 | $\dot{W}_f = c_T \cdot F$ | Fuel mass flow rate | kg/s |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Substitution Rule**: In the parabolic drag polar, the $K$ in the drag polar is **distinct** from the $K$ in Breguet range. To avoid confusion, KTU examiners use **$K_{induced}$** or simply the coefficient notation $K$ in $C_D = C_{D_0} + K C_L^2$. The drag coefficient symbol **never** appears in range/endurance equations.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

These performance equations form the **backbone of mission-planning software** used by Boeing, Airbus, Lockheed Martin, DRDO (India), and HAL. During preliminary aircraft design:
- **Conceptual designers** use the Breguet range equation to size the wings and fuel tanks.
- **Propulsion engineers** use the thrust equation to size the engine and inlets.
- **Aerodynamicists** use the drag polar to select wing aspect ratio and airfoil.
- **Flight-test engineers** use ceiling, rate of climb, and stall-speed data to certify the aircraft.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Generalized Thrust Equation for a Turbojet Engine

Consider a turbojet engine with a control volume around the entire propulsion system. Apply **steady-flow, one-dimensional form of the momentum equation** along the flight axis:

$$\sum F_x = \dot{m}_{\text{out}} V_{\text{out}} - \dot{m}_{\text{in}} V_{\text{in}}$$

**Step 1** — Identify inlets and outlets:
- Inlet (1): air enters with mass flow $\dot{m}_a$ at flight velocity $V_0$.
- Outlet (e): exhaust leaves with combined mass flow $(\dot{m}_a + \dot{m}_f)$ at velocity $V_e$.
- Pressure thrust contribution from exit area $A_e$ where static pressure is $p_e$ (different from ambient $p_a$).

**Step 2** — Set up the momentum balance with pressure term:

$$F_{\text{gross}} = (\dot{m}_a + \dot{m}_f)V_e - \dot{m}_a V_0 + (p_e - p_a) A_e$$

**Step 3** — When the nozzle is **ideally expanded** so that $p_e = p_a$, the pressure term vanishes:

$$F = (\dot{m}_a + \dot{m}_f)V_e - \dot{m}_a V_0$$

**Step 4** — Since $\dot{m}_f \ll \dot{m}_a$ (typically $\dot{m}_f \approx 1/50$ of $\dot{m}_a$), we approximate $\dot{m}_a + \dot{m}_f \approx \dot{m}_a$:

$$\boxed{F = \dot{m}_a (V_e - V_0)}$$

**Step 5** — Divide by $\dot{m}_a$ to obtain specific thrust (thrust per unit air mass flow):

$$F_s = V_e - V_0 + \dfrac{(p_e - p_a)A_e}{\dot{m}_a}$$

This derivation is **the single most-tested thrust derivation in KTU university exams**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Breguet Range Equation (Cruise Range for a Jet)

The Breguet range equation is derived from the **fuel consumption rate** during steady cruise.

**Step 1** — Fuel mass flow rate in terms of thrust specific fuel consumption:

$$\dot{W}_f = c_T \cdot F$$

**Step 2** — In steady cruise, thrust equals drag: $F = D$. So:

$$\dot{W}_f = c_T \cdot D$$

**Step 3** — Lift equals weight in level flight: $W = L$. Divide numerator and denominator by $W$:

$$\dot{W}_f = c_T \cdot W \cdot \dfrac{D}{L}$$

**Step 4** — Express the weight reduction rate:

$$\dfrac{dW}{dt} = -c_T \cdot W \cdot \dfrac{D}{L}$$

**Step 5** — Rearrange to integrate over the flight time:

$$\dfrac{dW}{W} = -c_T \cdot \dfrac{D}{L} \, dt$$

**Step 6** — For *cruise at constant altitude and constant $L/D$*, integrate from initial weight $W_i$ to final weight $W_f$:

$$\int_{W_i}^{W_f} \dfrac{dW}{W} = -c_T \dfrac{D}{L} \int_0^{t_f} dt$$

$$\ln\left(\dfrac{W_f}{W_i}\right) = -c_T \cdot \dfrac{D}{L} \cdot t_f$$

**Step 7** — Solve for flight time (endurance):

$$t_f = \dfrac{L}{c_T \cdot D} \ln\left(\dfrac{W_i}{W_f}\right)$$

**Step 8** — Multiply by cruise velocity $V$ to get range:

$$\boxed{R = V \cdot t_f = \dfrac{V}{c_T} \cdot \dfrac{L}{D} \cdot \ln\left(\dfrac{W_i}{W_f}\right)}$$

This is the **Breguet range equation for jet aircraft** — universally tested.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Rate of Climb (Power-Available Minus Power-Required Method)

The **rate of climb (R/C)** is the vertical component of flight velocity when the aircraft is pitched upward.

**Step 1** — In a climb at angle $\gamma$ from horizontal, the equations of motion are:

$$F - D - W\sin\gamma = m a$$

In quasi-steady climb (constant speed), $a = 0$:

$$F - D = W \sin\gamma$$

**Step 2** — For small climb angles, $\sin\gamma \approx \tan\gamma \approx \gamma \approx V_v / V$, where $V_v$ is vertical (climb) speed and $V$ is true airspeed:

$$F - D = W \cdot \dfrac{V_v}{V}$$

**Step 3** — Solve for $V_v$ (which IS the rate of climb):

$$V_v = \text{R/C} = \dfrac{(F - D) \cdot V}{W}$$

**Step 4** — Alternate *power* form: multiply both sides by $V$:

$$\text{R/C} = \dfrac{FV - DV}{W} = \dfrac{P_A - P_R}{W}$$

**Step 5** — Therefore:

$$\boxed{\text{R/C} = \dfrac{P_A - P_R}{W} = \dfrac{(F - D) \cdot V}{W}}$$

This is the **fundamental climb-performance equation**. Maximum R/C occurs at the velocity where $(F - D)/W$ (or equivalently $P_A - P_R$) is maximum.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Variation of Thrust with Altitude (Isothermal Atmosphere)

For a turbojet at constant flight Mach number $M$, the variation of thrust with altitude is governed by density ratio.

**Step 1** — Specific thrust (neglecting fuel and pressure terms):

$$F_s = V_e - V_0$$

**Step 2** — Both $V_0$ and $V_e$ can be expressed via Mach numbers:

$$V_0 = M_0 \cdot a = M_0 \sqrt{\gamma R T_0}$$

$$V_e = M_e \cdot a_e$$

For an ideal nozzle, energy conservation gives (Rayleigh flow):

$$\dfrac{T_e}{T_0^*} = \dfrac{1}{1 + \tfrac{\gamma - 1}{2} M_e^2}$$

**Step 3** — Mass flow at inlet: $\dot{m}_a = \rho_0 \cdot A_0 \cdot V_0 \propto \rho_0 V_0$

**Step 4** — In the **troposphere** (linear temperature gradient), density falls as:

$$\rho = \rho_0 \left(1 - \dfrac{L h}{T_0}\right)^{(g/(RL)) - 1}$$

where $L$ is the lapse rate ($\approx 0.0065$ K/m). In the **isothermal stratosphere** ($T = $ const):

$$\rho = \rho_0 \exp\left(-\dfrac{g h}{R T}\right)$$

**Step 5** — Combined thrust variation at constant Mach (KTU result):

$$F = F_0 \cdot \dfrac{\rho}{\rho_0} = F_0 \cdot \sigma$$

where $\sigma = \rho/\rho_0$ is the **relative density**.

**Step 6** — At **constant TAS (true airspeed)** instead of constant Mach, $V_0$ is fixed but $\dot{m}_a \propto \rho$ and $V_e$ decreases with altitude (since $T_e$ decreases), so thrust falls **more rapidly** with altitude.

**Analytical Matrix — Effect of Altitude on Jet Engine Parameters:**

| Parameter | Constant $M$ | Constant TAS |
|---|---|---|
| $V_0$ | Increases slightly | Constant |
| $V_e$ | Falls (lower $T_e$) | Falls faster |
| $\dot{m}_a$ | Falls as $\rho$ | Falls as $\rho$ |
| $F$ | Falls as $\rho$ | Falls faster than $\rho$ |
| $c_T$ (TSFC) | Decreases | Decreases |
| $(F - D)$ available | Optimal at some $h^*$ | Lower optimal $h$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Practical Rule (KTU Board Examiner's Tip)**: At constant Mach number, thrust specific fuel consumption ($c_T$) *decreases* with altitude (favorable), while thrust itself decreases (unfavorable). The *cruise ceiling* is determined by the altitude where $F - D$ becomes just sufficient to maintain level flight at minimum drag speed.

---

### 3.5 Derivation 5: Effect of Speed on Range (Jet Aircraft)

For a turbojet, the range is:

$$R = \dfrac{V}{c_T} \cdot \dfrac{L}{D} \cdot \ln\left(\dfrac{W_i}{W_f}\right)$$

**Step 1** — Express $L/D$ using the drag polar and lift equation:
- $L = W = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2 S C_L \Rightarrow C_L = \dfrac{2W}{\rho V^2 S}$
- $C_D = C_{D_0} + K C_L^2$

**Step 2** — So:

$$\dfrac{L}{D} = \dfrac{C_L}{C_{D_0} + K C_L^2} = \dfrac{1}{C_{D_0}/C_L + K C_L}$$

**Step 3** — Substitute $C_L = 2W/(\rho V^2 S)$:

$$\dfrac{L}{D} = \dfrac{1}{\dfrac{C_{D_0}\rho V^2 S}{2W} + \dfrac{2KW}{\rho V^2 S}}$$

**Step 4** — At low speeds, the second term dominates (induced drag). At high speeds, the first term dominates (parasite drag). The optimum $L/D$ occurs at $V_{\text{opt}}$ giving $(L/D)_{\max}$.

**Step 5** — **Critical Conclusion**: For *subsonic jets*, **range is maximized at the altitude and speed corresponding to $(L/D)_{\max}$**, and increased altitude (within service ceiling) improves range because density drops — so for the same $W$, $V$ increases.

> [!NOTE]
> **Counter-intuitive insight (frequently asked in KTU viva)**: For jets, **flying higher increases range** (within ceiling), but only up to the altitude where TSFC penalty outweighs the L/D benefit. This is the *absolute ceiling for range*, not the absolute ceiling for flight.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Factors Affecting Flight Performance

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Aircraft Flight Mission] --> B[Thrust F from Engine]
    A --> C[Aerodynamic Drag D]
    A --> D[Weight W including Payload]
    A --> E[Atmospheric Conditions]
    A --> F[Flight Speed Mach Number M]

    B --> B1[Thrust Equation]
    B1 --> B2[Net Thrust F equals ma Ve minus ma V0]
    B --> B3[Altitude Effect]
    B --> B4[TSFC Variation]

    C --> C1[Parasite Drag Dp]
    C --> C2[Induced Drag Di]
    C --> C3[Wave Drag Dw]
    C --> C4[Drag Polar CD equals CD0 plus K CL squared]

    D --> D1[Gross Take Off Weight]
```
