```mermaid
    D --> D2[Fuel Weight Wf]
    D --> D3[Payload Weight Wpl]

    E --> E1[Density rho from Altitude]
    E --> E2[Temperature T]
    E --> E3[Pressure p]

    F --> F1[Subsonic Regime M less than 0.8]
    F --> F2[Transonic Regime 0.8 to 1.2]
    F --> F3[Supersonic Regime M greater than 1.2]

    B2 --> P[Performance Metrics]
    B3 --> P
    B4 --> P
    C4 --> P
    D1 --> P
    E1 --> P
    F1 --> P
    F2 --> P
    F3 --> P

    P --> P1[Range R]
    P --> P2[Endurance E]
    P --> P3[Rate of Climb RC]
    P --> P4[Absolute Ceiling hA]
    P --> P5[Take Off Distance]
    P --> P6[Landing Distance]
```

### 4.2 Functional Diagram — Steady Level Flight Force Balance

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Aircraft[Aircraft in Steady Level Flight]
        L[LIFT L upward] --> W[WEIGHT W downward]
        F[THRUST F forward] --> DR[DRAG D backward]
    end

    W --> EQ1[L equals W]
    DR --> EQ2[F equals D]
    EQ1 --> PERF[Flight Performance]
    EQ2 --> PERF
    PERF --> OUT[Excess F minus D drives Acceleration or Climb]
```

### 4.3 Functional Diagram — Drag Polar and Velocity Variation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    V[Flight Velocity V] --> CD1[Parasite Drag Dominates at High V]
    V --> CD2[Induced Drag Dominates at Low V]
    CD1 --> TOTAL[Total Drag D]
    CD2 --> TOTAL
    TOTAL --> LD[L over D Ratio]
    LD --> MAXLD[Maximum L over D at V optimum]
    MAXLD --> BREGUET[Maximum Breguet Range]
    MAXLD --> ENDURE[Maximum Endurance]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The parabolic drag polar $C_D = C_{D_0} + K C_L^2$ is the **most heavily relied-upon model** in KTU board examinations for performance analysis. It is valid only for **low subsonic flight** (Mach < 0.7) without significant wave drag.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2023]**
*Define thrust specific fuel consumption (TSFC) of a jet engine and explain its variation with altitude and flight speed.*

**Model Answer:**
TSFC is defined as the **mass flow rate of fuel per unit thrust produced** by the engine:

$$c_T = \dfrac{\dot{m}_f}{F}$$

It is measured in **mg/(N·s)** or **kg/(kN·s)** or **1/hr** (using $c_T \cdot 3600$).

**Variation with altitude (at constant Mach):**
- Ambient pressure and temperature decrease → combustor inlet conditions shift → higher cycle temperatures and pressures can be achieved → thermal efficiency improves.
- $c_T$ **decreases** with altitude.

**Variation with speed (at constant altitude):**
- At low speeds, TSFC is high because thrust is low while fuel flow is significant.
- TSFC reaches a **minimum** at a design Mach number.
- Beyond design Mach, ram drag grows faster than useful thrust → $c_T$ rises again.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]**
*List any six factors that affect the flight performance of a jet-propelled aircraft.*

**Model Answer:**
1. **Thrust available (F)** from the propulsion system
2. **Aerodynamic drag (D)** — parasite, induced, wave
3. **Aircraft weight (W)** including payload and fuel
4. **Altitude (h)** affecting density, temperature, pressure
5. **Flight speed (V) / Mach number (M)**
6. **Thrust-specific fuel consumption (TSFC)**
7. **Wing loading (W/S) and aspect ratio (AR)**
8. **Engine cycle (turbojet, turbofan, turboshaft)**
9. **Atmospheric conditions (ISA, hot day, cold day)**
10. **Flight phase (take-off, climb, cruise, descent, landing)**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

#### Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam - June 2024]**

> **(a)** *Derive the generalized thrust equation for a turbojet engine. Explain how thrust varies with (i) altitude, and (ii) flight Mach number, for an ideally expanded nozzle.* **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** *A turbojet aircraft cruises at $V_0 = 250$ m/s at an altitude where the ambient air density is $\rho = 0.5$ kg/m³. The wing area is $S = 30$ m², zero-lift drag coefficient $C_{D_0} = 0.018$, Oswald efficiency factor $e = 0.85$, and wing aspect ratio $AR = 7$. The aircraft weight during cruise is $W = 60{,}000$ N. Determine:*
>
> *(i) The optimum lift coefficient $C_L^*$ for maximum $L/D$.*
> *(ii) The maximum lift-to-drag ratio $(L/D)_{\max}$.*
> *(iii) The minimum drag at this condition.* **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A:

**Part (a) — Thrust Equation Derivation:**

Consider a control volume around the turbojet engine, bounded by the freestream at the inlet (section 1) and the nozzle exit plane (section e). Apply the **steady-flow, one-dimensional momentum equation** in the flight direction:

$$\sum F_x = \dot{m}_{\text{out}} V_{\text{out}} - \dot{m}_{\text{in}} V_{\text{in}}$$

The forces in the $x$-direction are:
- Thrust force $F$ acting on the engine from the air (reaction)
- Pressure thrust from inlet area: $p_a A_1$ (forward)
- Pressure thrust from exit area: $-p_e A_e$ (rearward, hence negative)
- External pressure $p_a$ acting on the rest of the engine surface (radially inward, no $x$-component by symmetry)

Net force on the control volume:

$$F = \dot{m}_e V_e - \dot{m}_1 V_1 + (p_e - p_a) A_e$$

With $\dot{m}_e = \dot{m}_a + \dot{m}_f$, $V_1 = V_0$, and $\dot{m}_f \ll \dot{m}_a$:

$$\boxed{F = \dot{m}_a (V_e - V_0) + (p_e - p_a) A_e}$$

For **ideal expansion** ($p_e = p_a$):

$$F = \dot{m}_a (V_e - V_0)$$

**(i) Variation of thrust with altitude (constant Mach):**

At constant Mach, $V_0 \propto \sqrt{T_0}$ which changes slowly with altitude. The dominant effect is:

$$\dot{m}_a = \rho_0 A_0 V_0 \propto \rho_0$$

For an ideal nozzle, $V_e$ depends only on turbine inlet conditions (which improve with altitude due to lower ambient temperature). For a typical turbojet, the net effect on **thrust** is:

$$F \approx F_0 \cdot \sigma = F_0 \cdot \dfrac{\rho}{\rho_0}$$

i.e., thrust decreases roughly **proportionally to air density** with altitude. **TSFC decreases** with altitude (favorable).

**(ii) Variation of thrust with Mach (constant altitude):**

As $M_0$ increases:
- $V_0$ increases → first term in thrust equation $V_e - V_0$ becomes smaller or negative (ram drag).
- $\dot{m}_a = \rho_0 A_0 V_0$ increases linearly with $V_0$.
- $V_e$ increases only marginally (choked nozzle).

Net thrust **rises initially** then **peaks around $M \approx 0.6$–$0.8$**, then **decreases** as $V_0$ approaches $V_e$. This is why **supersonic aircraft require afterburners or different cycle designs (ramjets, scramjets)**.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Problem Solution:**

Given:
$V_0 = 250$ m/s, $\rho = 0.5$ kg/m³, $S = 30$ m², $C_{D_0} = 0.018$, $e = 0.85$, $AR = 7$, $W = 60{,}000$ N.

**Step 1** — Compute the induced drag factor $K$:

$$K = \dfrac{1}{\pi \cdot e \cdot AR} = \dfrac{1}{\pi \cdot 0.85 \cdot 7} = \dfrac{1}{18.69} = 0.0535$$

**Step 2** — Compute $(L/D)_{\max}$:

$$\left(\dfrac{L}{D}\right)_{\max} = \dfrac{1}{2} \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{K \cdot C_{D_0}}} = \dfrac{1}{2} \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{0.0535 \cdot 0.018}}$$

$$= \dfrac{1}{2} \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{0.000963}} = \dfrac{1}{2} \sqrt{1038.4} = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot 32.22$$

$$\boxed{(L/D)_{\max} = 16.11}$$

**Step 3** — Compute the **dynamic pressure**:

$$q = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_0^2 = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot 0.5 \cdot 250^2 = 0.25 \cdot 62500 = 15{,}625 \text{ Pa}$$

**Step 4** — Optimum lift coefficient $C_L^*$:

$$C_L^* = \sqrt{\dfrac{C_{D_0}}{K}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{0.018}{0.0535}} = \sqrt{0.3364} = 0.580$$

**Step 5** — Lift at this condition must equal weight (level flight):

$$L = q \cdot S \cdot C_L^* = 15{,}625 \cdot 30 \cdot 0.580 = 271{,}875 \text{ N}$$

Since $L \neq W = 60{,}000$ N, the aircraft cannot fly at $(L/D)_{\max}$ at this altitude & speed. We need to **find the velocity that gives $(L/D)_{\max}$ for the actual $W$**.

Reformulating: at $(L/D)_{\max}$, $C_L = C_L^* = 0.580$:

$$V_{\text{opt}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2W}{\rho S C_L^*}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \cdot 60{,}000}{0.5 \cdot 30 \cdot 0.580}}$$

$$V_{\text{opt}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{120{,}000}{8.7}} = \sqrt{13{,}793.1} = 117.44 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 6** — Drag at $(L/D)_{\max}$:

$$D_{\min} = \dfrac{L}{(L/D)_{\max}} = \dfrac{60{,}000}{16.11} = 3724.4 \text{ N}$$

Or, equivalently:

$$D_{\min} = q \cdot S \cdot 2 C_{D_0} = 15{,}625 \cdot 30 \cdot 2 \cdot 0.018 \cdot \dfrac{C_L^*}{C_L^*}$$

$$D_{\min} = q \cdot S \cdot 2 \sqrt{C_{D_0} \cdot K}$$

$$D_{\min} = 15{,}625 \cdot 30 \cdot 2 \cdot \sqrt{0.018 \cdot 0.0535} = 937{,}500 \cdot 2 \cdot 0.03103 = 937{,}500 \cdot 0.06206$$

$$D_{\min} \approx 58{,}183 \text{ N}$$ (using $V_{\text{opt}}$ dynamic pressure, not $V = 250$ m/s)

**Final Answers (Q-A Part b):**
- (i) $C_L^* = 0.580$
- (ii) $(L/D)_{\max} = 16.11$
- (iii) $D_{\min} \approx 3{,}724.4$ N (when $L = W = 60{,}000$ N)

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **[KTU University Exam - June 2024]**

> **(a)** *Explain in detail the various components of drag acting on an aircraft. Derive the parabolic drag polar equation and obtain the condition for maximum lift-to-drag ratio.* **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** *Using the Breguet range equation for a jet aircraft, calculate the range of an aircraft flying at the optimum $(L/D)_{\max} = 18$. Given: $V = 240$ m/s, $c_T = 0.000025$ kg/(N·s), initial weight $W_i = 90{,}000$ N, final weight $W_f = 60{,}000$ N.* **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B:

**Part (a) — Drag Components and Parabolic Drag Polar:**

**Drag Components:**

1. **Parasite Drag ($D_p$)**: Independent of lift; arises from friction and form.
   - Skin friction drag: $C_{f} = 0.455 / (\log_{10} Re)^2.58$ (Turbulent flat plate, ITTC)
   - Form drag: depends on shape (streamlined vs bluff)
   - Interference drag: at junctions (wing-fuselage, tail-fuselage)
   - Profile drag = skin friction + form + interference

2. **Induced Drag ($D_i$)**: Consequence of lift generation.
   - Caused by wingtip vortices that produce a downwash.
   - Downwash reduces the effective angle of attack, altering lift direction backward → drag component.
   - $D_i = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2 S \cdot K C_L^2$ with $K = 1/(\pi e AR)$.

3. **Wave Drag ($D_w$)**: Becomes significant near Mach 1.
   - Caused by formation of shock waves on wings and fuselage.
   - Rises rapidly in the transonic regime (0.85 < M < 1.2).
   - Reduced by supercritical airfoils, area ruling (Whitcomb).

**Parabolic Drag Polar Derivation:**

From thin-airfoil theory, the change in lift coefficient with angle of attack is linear: $C_L = a \alpha$. For small angles, the induced drag arises purely from the trailing vortices:

$$C_{D_i} = \dfrac{C_L^2}{\pi e AR} = K C_L^2$$

Adding parasite drag (independent of lift):

$$C_D = C_{D_0} + K C_L^2$$

**Condition for $(L/D)_{\max}$:**

$$\dfrac{L}{D} = \dfrac{C_L}{C_{D_0} + K C_L^2}$$

Differentiate with respect to $C_L$ and set to zero:

$$\dfrac{d(L/D)}{dC_L} = \dfrac{(C_{D_0} + K C_L^2) - C_L(2K C_L)}{(C_{D_0} + K C_L^2)^2} = 0$$

Numerator = 0:

$$C_{D_0} + K C_L^2 - 2K C_L^2 = 0 \Rightarrow C_{D_0} = K C_L^2$$

$$C_L^* = \sqrt{\dfrac{C_{D_0}}{K}}$$

Substituting back:

$$\left(\dfrac{L}{D}\right)_{\max} = \dfrac{C_L^*}{C_{D_0} + K (C_L^*)^2} = \dfrac{C_L^*}{2 C_{D_0}} = \dfrac{\sqrt{C_{D_0}/K}}{2 C_{D_0}}$$

$$\boxed{\left(\dfrac{L}{D}\right)_{\max} = \dfrac{1}{2\sqrt{K C_{D_0}}}}$$

---

**Part (b) — Range Calculation using Breguet Equation:**

Given: $(L/D)_{\max} = 18$, $V = 240$ m/s, $c_T = 2.5 \times 10^{-5}$ kg/(N·s), $W_i = 90{,}000$ N, $W_f = 60{,}000$ N.

**Breguet Range Equation:**

$$R = \dfrac{V}{c_T} \cdot \dfrac{L}{D} \cdot \ln\left(\dfrac{W_i}{W_f}\right)$$

**Step 1** — Compute the logarithmic weight ratio:

$$\ln\left(\dfrac{W_i}{W_f}\right) = \ln\left(\dfrac{90{,}000}{60{,}000}\right) = \ln(1.5) = 0.4055$$

**Step 2** — Substitute all values:

$$R = \dfrac{240}{2.5 \times 10^{-5}} \cdot 18 \cdot 0.4055$$

$$R = (9.6 \times 10^6) \cdot 18 \cdot 0.4055$$

$$R = 9.6 \times 10^6 \cdot 7.299 = 70.07 \times 10^6 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3** — Convert to kilometers:

$$\boxed{R \approx 70{,}070 \text{ km} \approx 70{,}000 \text{ km}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Sign convention confusion**: The Breguet equation uses $W_i/W_f$ (NOT $W_f/W_i$). The logarithm of a number greater than 1 is positive — getting this backwards gives a negative range, which is **wrong**.
> 2. **Units of $c_T$**: KTU frequently uses mg/(N·s) or kg/(kN·hr). Always convert to **kg/(N·s)** before substituting in the SI-based Breguet equation.
> 3. **Mixing up range and endurance**: Endurance $E = (1/c_T)(L/D)\ln(W_i/W_f)$ — note the **absence of $V$**. Students who multiply endurance by velocity get endurance × V (which is *not* range).
> 4. **Drag polar constant confusion**: $K$ in the drag polar is **not** the same as $K$ in the Breguet equation (one is induced drag factor, the other is just a placeholder). Always re-derive before substituting.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Module 4: Factors Affecting Flight Performance**

- [ ] **Force equilibrium** in level flight: $L = W$ and $F = D$. Performance = excess thrust over drag.
- [ ] **Generalized thrust equation**: $F = \dot{m}_a(V_e - V_0) + (p_e - p_a)A_e$. For ideal expansion: $F = \dot{m}_a(V_e - V_0)$.
- [ ] **Thrust falls ≈ proportionally to density** with altitude at constant Mach number.
- [ ] **TSFC decreases** with altitude (better thermal efficiency at lower $T_a$).
- [ ] **Drag components**: Parasite (skin + form + interference), Induced ($K C_L^2$), Wave (transonic).
- [ ] **Parabolic drag polar**: $C_D = C_{D_0} + K C_L^2$ where $K = 1/(\pi e AR)$.
- [ ] **Maximum L/D**: $(L/D)_{\max} = \tfrac{1}{2\sqrt{K C_{D_0}}}$, occurs at $C_L^* = \sqrt{C_{D_0}/K}$.
- [ ] **Breguet range (jet)**: $R = (V/c_T)(L/D)\ln(W_i/W_f)$.
- [ ] **Breguet endurance (jet)**: $E = (1/c_T)(L/D)\ln(W_i/W_f)$.
- [ ] **Rate of climb**: $\text{R/C} = (FV - DV)/W = (P_A - P_R)/W$.
- [ ] **Absolute ceiling**: altitude where $F = D$ at $V_{R/C_{\max}}$ — but $P_A - P_R \to 0$.
- [ ] **Range optimization**: Jet aircraft fly at $(L/D)_{\max}$ AND at the **highest possible altitude** (up to service ceiling) to maximize range.
- [ ] **Speed for max range vs max endurance**: Max range is at $(L/D)_{\max}$; max endurance is also at $(L/D)_{\max}$ for jets (different for propeller aircraft).
- [ ] **Mach number effects**: Thrust peaks around $M = 0.6$–$0.8$ for subsonic turbojets without afterburner.
- [ ] **Hot-day derating**: Higher $T_a$ reduces $\rho$ and hence $\dot{m}_a$, reducing thrust — critical for take-off performance.
- [ ] **Wave drag onset**: Typically at $M_{crit} = 0.7$–$0.85$ — aircraft is constrained to cruise below this for efficient flight.
- [ ] **Propulsive vs Thermal vs Overall efficiency**: $\eta_0 = \eta_p \cdot \eta_{\text{th}}$. Maximizing one does not necessarily maximize the other.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Thrust Augmentation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET741 - GAS TURBINE AND JET PROPULSION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Thrust Augmentation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Thrust Augmentation** is the process of temporarily increasing the net thrust output of a gas turbine engine (turbojet/turbofan) **beyond its rated (un-augmented) design value**, without altering the basic thermodynamic cycle of the engine core. The augmentation is achieved by injecting additional mass into the gas stream or by adding heat to the exhaust gases **after the turbine**, but **before the propelling nozzle**.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus, thrust augmentation is studied under the umbrella of engine performance enhancement and is restricted to the following standard, board-approved techniques:

1. **Water (or Water–Methanol) Injection** — Into the combustion chamber or the compressor diffuser.
2. **Reheat (Afterburning / Tail-Pipe Burning)** — Secondary combustion chamber fitted between the turbine exit and the propelling nozzle.
3. **Ejector / Hot-Gas Thrust Augmenter** — A secondary nozzle-ram arrangement that entrains extra ambient air, used typically on turboprop-class engines.

The **Thrust Augmentation Ratio (TAR)** is the standard KTU metric used to quantify the effectiveness of any augmentation scheme:

$$
\text{TAR} = \frac{\text{Augmented Thrust } (F_{\text{aug}})}{\text{Un-Augmented Thrust } (F)}
$$

A typical military afterburning turbojet yields a TAR in the range of **1.4 to 1.6** at maximum afterburner power, while water injection typically yields a TAR of **1.2 to 1.3** for short-duration bursts.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Squeezing Garden Hose"**
> Imagine holding a garden hose with a thumb partially covering the nozzle. By tightening your thumb, you don't add any more water (mass) to the hose, but you make the water come out **faster** because the same mass must squeeze through a smaller opening. The reaction force pulling the hose backward is stronger — the thrust is higher. This is *velocity-based* thrust. Now imagine you have a small blowtorch at the hose tip that **reheats the water just before it exits** — the water expands and shoots out even faster, giving you much more thrust. This reheat is exactly what an **afterburner** does to the hot exhaust gases of a turbojet. Alternatively, imagine a fireman briefly **spraying extra water into the hose upstream** — more mass, more recoil. That is **water injection augmentation**.

The key insight is that thrust has two levers:
- **Mass flow rate** $\dot{m}$ (kg/s) — you can boost it by injecting water or by an ejector.
- **Exit velocity** $V_j$ (m/s) — you can boost it by adding heat (reheat) downstream of the turbine.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Range |
|---|---|---|
| Afterburner exit temperature | $T_{je}$ | **1800 K – 2100 K** |
| Thrust augmentation ratio (afterburner) | TAR | **1.4 – 1.6** |
| Thrust augmentation ratio (water injection) | TAR | **1.2 – 1.3** |
| Reheat fuel–air ratio | $f_{RH}$ | **0.03 – 0.06** |
| Specific thrust increase | $\Delta F_{sp}$ | up to **60 %** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In KTU board problems, the values given in the question are to be used **as-is**. Do not assume a default TAR value; always derive it from the cycle data supplied.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two-Lever Model of Thrust

The general thrust equation of a turbojet is:

$$
F = \dot{m}_a (V_j - V_a) + (\dot{m}_f - \dot{m}_{ab}) V_j
$$

For an air-breathing jet engine without bleed and neglecting fuel mass addition, the simpler form used in KTU problems is:

$$
F = \dot{m} (V_j - V_a)
$$

where:
- $\dot{m}$ = total mass flow rate through the engine (kg/s)
- $V_j$ = absolute jet exit velocity (m/s)
- $V_a$ = aircraft (flight) velocity (m/s)

**Thrust augmentation therefore targets two parameters:**
- $\dot{m}$ — increased by **water injection** or **ejector entrainment**.
- $V_j$ — increased by **reheat/afterburning**.

## 2.2 Method 1 — Water Injection (Mass Augmentation)

In water injection, water (often mixed with methanol as an anti-freeze) is sprayed into the **combustion chamber** or the **compressor diffuser**. The benefits are:

1. **Combustion chamber cooling** — The latent heat of vaporisation absorbs heat, allowing more fuel to be burned without exceeding turbine inlet temperature limits.
2. **Mass flow boost** — The water vaporises and adds mass to the gas stream, increasing $\dot{m}$ at the nozzle.
3. **Compressor stall recovery** — Cooler air in the diffuser increases mass density, preventing surge during rapid throttle-up.

**Disadvantages:** Limited duration (water tank size), corrosion, ice-formation risk, and increased fuel consumption due to richer combustion.

> [!NOTE]
> KTU Syllabus Highlight: Water injection is a **short-duration** boost technique (typically < 2 minutes), used in WWII-era engines like the Junker Jumo 004, the de Havilland Ghost, and the Pratt & Whitney J57 (take-off only).

## 2.3 Method 2 — Reheat / Afterburning (Velocity Augmentation)

The **afterburner (or reheat unit)** is a secondary combustion chamber installed **between the turbine exit and the propelling nozzle**. Hot turbine exhaust gases (typically at 800 K – 900 K) still contain a large fraction of unburnt oxygen because combustion in the main chamber is limited by the turbine blade metallurgy. Additional fuel is injected and burned in the afterburner, raising the gas temperature to 1800 K – 2100 K just before the nozzle.

**Key relationships in a reheat cycle (KTU-favoured derivation form):**

Let
- $T_{j}$ = turbine exit / primary nozzle inlet temperature (without reheat) — typically 800 K
- $T_{je}$ = reheat (afterburner exit) temperature — typically 1800 K
- $c_p$ = specific heat of exhaust gas (~ **1148 J/kg·K** at high T)

From the **steady-flow energy equation** across the afterburner (neglecting kinetic energy change and pressure drop):

$$
c_p (T_{je} - T_j) = f_{RH} \cdot Q_{RH}
$$

where
- $f_{RH}$ = reheat fuel–air ratio (mass of reheat fuel / mass of primary air)
- $Q_{RH}$ = calorific value of the reheat fuel (~ **43 MJ/kg** for kerosene)

Solving for $f_{RH}$:

$$
f_{RH} = \frac{c_p (T_{je} - T_j)}{Q_{RH}}
$$

The **augmented thrust** then becomes:

$$
F_{\text{aug}} = \dot{m}_a (V_{je} - V_a) + \dot{m}_{f,RH} (V_{je} - V_a)
$$

Substituting $\dot{m}_{f,RH} = f_{RH} \cdot \dot{m}_a$:

$$
F_{\text{aug}} = \dot{m}_a (1 + f_{RH}) (V_{je} - V_a)
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In KTU board problems, $f_{RH}$ is **neglected** in the mass-flow term $(1 + f_{RH}) \approx 1$ if the question explicitly states "neglect fuel mass" or if the percentage error in the result is < 5 %. Always read the question's assumptions section carefully.

**Disadvantages of reheat:** Increased fuel consumption (a fully-augmented engine can consume **3× to 4×** the dry thrust fuel), poor propulsive efficiency (high $V_j$ relative to $V_a$), and a luminous, radar-visible exhaust signature.

## 2.4 Method 3 — Ejector / Hot-Gas Thrust Augmenter

Used in some turboprop derivatives. A secondary nozzle at the tail-pipe exit entrains surrounding air, which is mixed and accelerated. The combined jet is then expelled through a final nozzle. This is a **mass-augmentation** technique and forms a small but syllabus-relevant subsection.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Typical KTU Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $F = \dot{m} (V_j - V_a)$ | Un-augmented thrust | Baseline calculation |
| 2 | $F_{\text{aug}} = \dot{m}_a (1 + f_{RH}) (V_{je} - V_a)$ | Reheat-augmented thrust | Afterburner problems |
| 3 | $f_{RH} = \dfrac{c_p (T_{je} - T_j)}{Q_{RH}}$ | Reheat fuel–air ratio | Energy balance across reheat |
| 4 | $TAR = F_{\text{aug}} / F$ | Augmentation effectiveness | Final answer metric |
| 5 | $V_j = \sqrt{2 c_p (T_{j} - T_a) + V_a^2}$ | Ideal nozzle expansion velocity | Inlet conditions given |
| 6 | $V_{je} = \sqrt{2 c_p (T_{je} - T_a) + V_a^2}$ | Reheat exit velocity | Afterburner exit |
| 7 | $F_{sp} = \dfrac{F}{\dot{m}_a}$ | Specific thrust | Normalised comparison |
| 8 | $\Delta F = F_{\text{aug}} - F$ | Absolute thrust increase | Question asks for "increase" |
| 9 | $f_{aug} = \dfrac{m_{aug,\text{fuel}}}{m_a}$ | Augmentation fuel–air ratio | Energy balance problems |
| 10 | $V_{je} = V_j \sqrt{T_{je}/T_j}$ | Isentropic velocity ratio | Subsonic expansion only |

> [!NOTE]
> Real-World Engineering Utility
> Afterburners are standard on every modern military fighter (F-16, Su-30MKI, Rafale, Eurofighter Typhoon, F-22). The **concorde** used water-methanol injection on take-off to boost thrust beyond its rated dry thrust. Reheat is what enables **supersonic dash capability** and **combat manoeuvres** in military aircraft.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Reheat Fuel–Air Ratio from Energy Balance

**Statement:** Derive the expression for the reheat fuel–air ratio $f_{RH}$ required to raise the gas temperature from $T_j$ (turbine exit) to $T_{je}$ (reheat exit) using a fuel of calorific value $Q_{RH}$.

### Step 1 — Define the Control Volume

Take a control volume enclosing the reheat (afterburner) chamber, with:
- **Inlet (Station 3′):** Hot gas at $T_j$, mass flow $\dot{m}_a$
- **Outlet (Station 7):** Heated gas at $T_{je}$, mass flow $\dot{m}_a + \dot{m}_{f,RH}$
- **Fuel addition:** $\dot{m}_{f,RH} = f_{RH} \cdot \dot{m}_a$

### Step 2 — Apply the Steady-Flow Energy Equation (SFEE)

Neglecting kinetic-energy change and shaft work, and assuming no pressure drop:

$$
\dot{m}_a h_j + \dot{m}_{f,RH} Q_{RH} = (\dot{m}_a + \dot{m}_{f,RH}) h_{je}
$$

### Step 3 — Expand and Rearrange

$$
\dot{m}_a h_j + \dot{m}_{f,RH} Q_{RH} = \dot{m}_a h_{je} + \dot{m}_{f,RH} h_{je}
$$

$$
\dot{m}_{f,RH} (Q_{RH} - h_{je}) = \dot{m}_a (h_{je} - h_j)
$$

### Step 4 — Introduce Fuel–Air Ratio

Divide throughout by $\dot{m}_a$ and use $f_{RH} = \dot{m}_{f,RH} / \dot{m}_a$:

$$
f_{RH} (Q_{RH} - h_{je}) = (h_{je} - h_j)
$$

### Step 5 — Use Sensible Heat Form

For gases, $h = c_p T$. Substituting:

$$
f_{RH} (Q_{RH} - c_p T_{je}) = c_p (T_{je} - T_j)
$$

### Step 6 — Solve for $f_{RH}$

$$
f_{RH} Q_{RH} - f_{RH} c_p T_{je} = c_p T_{je} - c_p T_j
$$

$$
f_{RH} Q_{RH} = c_p T_{je} - c_p T_j + f_{RH} c_p T_{je}
$$

$$
f_{RH} (Q_{RH} - c_p T_{je}) = c_p (T_{je} - T_j)
$$

Since $c_p T_{je} \ll Q_{RH}$ (1148 × 2000 ≈ 2.3 MJ/kg vs. 43 MJ/kg), the $c_p T_{je}$ term is often retained in the rigorous KTU form, but **dropped in the standard textbook shortcut**, giving:

$$
\boxed{f_{RH} = \frac{c_p (T_{je} - T_j)}{Q_{RH}}}
$$

**Numerical Example (KTU-style):**
Given: $T_j = 850 \text{ K}$, $T_{je} = 1800 \text{ K}$, $c_p = 1148 \text{ J/kg·K}$, $Q_{RH} = 43 \times 10^6 \text{ J/kg}$

$$
f_{RH} = \frac{1148 \times (1800 - 850)}{43 \times 10^6}
$$

$$
f_{RH} = \frac{1148 \times 950}{43 \times 10^6} = \frac{1,090,600}{43,000,000}
$$

$$
\boxed{f_{RH} \approx 0.02536}
$$

So roughly **2.54 % of reheat fuel** is needed per kg of primary air.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Thrust Augmentation Ratio (TAR)

**Statement:** Derive the TAR for a turbojet operating with reheat at static (zero flight) conditions.

### Step 1 — Write Un-Augmented Thrust at $V_a = 0$

$$
F = \dot{m}_a \cdot V_j
$$

where $V_j = \sqrt{2 c_p (T_j - T_a)}$ from isentropic nozzle expansion.

### Step 2 — Write Augmented Thrust at $V_a = 0$

$$
F_{\text{aug}} = \dot{m}_a (1 + f_{RH}) \cdot V_{je}
$$

where $V_{je} = \sqrt{2 c_p (T_{je} - T_a)}$.

### Step 3 — Form the Ratio

$$
TAR = \frac{F_{\text{aug}}}{F} = \frac{\dot{m}_a (1 + f_{RH}) V_{je}}{\dot{m}_a V_j} = (1 + f_{RH}) \frac{V_{je}}{V_j}
$$

### Step 4 — Substitute Isentropic Nozzle Velocities

$$
\frac{V_{je}}{V_j} = \frac{\sqrt{2 c_p (T_{je} - T_a)}}{\sqrt{2 c_p (T_j - T_a)}} = \sqrt{\frac{T_{je} - T_a}{T_j - T_a}}
$$

### Step 5 — Final Compact Form

$$
\boxed{TAR = (1 + f_{RH}) \sqrt{\frac{T_{je} - T_a}{T_j - T_a}}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> At static conditions, the inlet temperature $T_a$ is the ambient temperature (288 K standard). In flight, $T_a$ is replaced by the **ram temperature** $T_{t2} = T_a \left(1 + \frac{\gamma - 1}{2} M^2\right)$, where $M$ is the flight Mach number. KTU questions often specify either static or flight conditions explicitly.

**Numerical Example (KTU-style):**
Given: $T_j = 850$ K, $T_{je} = 1800$ K, $T_a = 288$ K, $f_{RH} = 0.025$

$$
TAR = (1 + 0.025) \sqrt{\frac{1800 - 288}{850 - 288}} = 1.025 \times \sqrt{\frac{1512}{562}}
$$

$$
TAR = 1.025 \times \sqrt{2.690} = 1.025 \times 1.640 = 1.681
$$

So the augmented thrust is **~68.1 % higher** than dry thrust.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Augmented Propulsive Efficiency

**Statement:** Show that reheat **decreases** propulsive efficiency at high $V_j / V_a$ ratios, providing the classic trade-off.

The propulsive efficiency is:

$$
\eta_p = \frac{2}{1 + V_j / V_a}
$$

For augmented operation, replace $V_j$ with $V_{je} > V_j$:

$$
\eta_{p,\text{aug}} = \frac{2}{1 + V_{je} / V_a}
$$

Since $V_{je} > V_j$ and $V_a$ is unchanged, the denominator increases, so $\eta_{p,\text{aug}} < \eta_p$. This is the **KTU board-favoured pitfall** — students often assume reheat always improves performance; in reality, it improves **thrust** but reduces **propulsive efficiency** (and dramatically increases **specific fuel consumption**).

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Augmentation Methods

| Parameter | Water Injection | Reheat / Afterburning | Ejector Augmenter |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Mechanism** | Mass addition | Energy addition | Entrainment mass addition |
| **Augments** | $\dot{m}$ | $V_j$ | $\dot{m}$ |
| **Typical TAR** | 1.20 – 1.30 | 1.40 – 1.70 | 1.10 – 1.25 |
| **Duration** | Short (~minutes) | Medium (~minutes) | Continuous |
| **Fuel penalty** | Moderate | **Very High (3× – 4×)** | Moderate |
| **Effect on $\eta_p$** | Slight increase | **Significant decrease** | Slight increase |
| **KTU exam weight** | High (Part B) | **Highest (Part B)** | Low (Part A) |
| **Real-world example** | Concorde, J57 | F-16, Su-30MKI | T-58 turboshaft variants |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Turbojet Engine with Reheat (Afterburner) — Functional Block Diagram

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the functional architecture of a turbojet fitted with an afterburner. Note: node IDs are alphanumeric; labels are clean alphanumeric text (no markdown formatting inside quotes).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[INLET DIFFUSER] --> A2[COMPRESSOR]
    A2 --> A3[MAIN COMBUSTION CHAMBER]
    A3 --> A4[TURBINE]
    A4 --> A5[REHEAT AFTERBURNER]
    A5 --> A6[PROPELLING NOZZLE]
    A6 --> A7[EXIT JET TO ATMOSPHERE]

    P1[FUEL TANK 1 PRIMARY] --> A3
    P2[FUEL TANK 2 REHEAT] --> A5

    subgraph AIRPATH[AIR INTAKE PATH]
        A1
        A2
    end

    subgraph COMBUSTIONPATH[HIGH TEMPERATURE PATH]
        A3
        A4
        A5
    end

    subgraph EXHAUSTPATH[EXHAUST PATH]
        A5
        A6
        A7
    end

    style A5 fill:#ffcc66,stroke:#cc6600,stroke-width:3px
    style P2 fill:#ff9966,stroke:#993300
    style A7 fill:#ccffff,stroke:#006666
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Reading the diagram left-to-right traces the gas path. The **reheat afterburner (A5)** is the only component physically inserted between the turbine and the propelling nozzle. Fuel from the **secondary fuel tank (P2)** is injected into A5. The highlighted (yellow) block represents the augmentation device under study.

## 4.2 Thrust Augmentation Method Selection Tree — Functional Logic

The following diagram shows the **decision logic** a propulsion engineer follows when selecting an augmentation method, mapped to KTU exam scenarios.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    B1[NEED MORE THRUST] --> B2{DURATION REQUIRED}
    B2 -->|SHORT BURST| B3{WEIGHT BUDGET}
    B2 -->|CONTINUOUS| B4[EJECTOR AUGMENTER]

    B3 -->|TOLERABLE| B5[WATER METHANOL INJECTION]
    B3 -->|NOT TOLERABLE| B6{OK WITH HIGH FUEL BURN}

    B6 -->|YES| B7[REHEAT AFTERBURNER]
    B6 -->|NO| B8[NO AUGMENTATION - REDESIGN CORE]

    B5 --> B9[TAR 1.2 TO 1.3]
    B7 --> B10[TAR 1.4 TO 1.7]
    B4 --> B11[TAR 1.1 TO 1.25]

    style B7 fill:#ff9966,stroke:#cc3300,stroke-width:2px
    style B5 fill:#99ccff,stroke:#003366
    style B4 fill:#99ff99,stroke:#006600
```

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU board examiner frequently asks: *"Compare reheat and water injection in terms of thrust augmentation ratio, propulsive efficiency, and fuel consumption."* The selection tree above gives the structure to answer such a question in 14 marks.

## 4.3 Energy Flow Block — With vs Without Reheat

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    C1[CHEMICAL ENERGY IN FUEL] --> C2{CYCLE POSITION}
    C2 -->|MAIN COMBUSTOR| C3[KINETIC ENERGY AT PRIMARY NOZZLE EXIT VJ]
    C2 -->|AFTERBURNER| C4[ADDITIONAL KINETIC ENERGY DELTA VJ]

    C3 --> C5[THRUST WITHOUT REHEAT F]
    C4 --> C6[THRUST WITH REHEAT FAUG]

    C5 --> C7[PROPULSIVE EFFICIENCY ETA P HIGH]
    C6 --> C8[PROPULSIVE EFFICIENCY ETA PAUG LOWER]

    style C4 fill:#ffcc66,stroke:#cc6600
    style C6 fill:#ff9966,stroke:#cc3300
    style C8 fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#990000
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This block diagram makes the **fundamental trade-off** of thrust augmentation explicit: more energy from fuel → more thrust → but lower propulsive efficiency. This trade-off is the most-tested concept in KTU 14-mark questions on this topic.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 (3 Marks)
> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
> *"Define thrust augmentation. List any two methods used to augment thrust in a gas turbine engine."*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Thrust augmentation is the technique of temporarily increasing the net thrust output of a gas turbine engine beyond its rated (un-augmented) design value, without altering the basic thermodynamic cycle of the engine core.

**Two methods (1.5 marks — 0.75 each):**
1. **Water (or water-methanol) injection** into the combustion chamber — mass augmentation.
2. **Reheat (Afterburning / Tail-pipe burning)** — secondary combustion between turbine exit and nozzle — velocity augmentation.

*(Acceptable alternative: Ejector / hot-gas thrust augmenter.)*

---

### Question A2 (3 Marks)
> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
> *"Explain the term 'Thrust Augmentation Ratio' (TAR). State its typical range for an afterburning turbojet."*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** Thrust Augmentation Ratio (TAR) is defined as the ratio of augmented thrust ($F_{\text{aug}}$) to the un-augmented (dry) thrust ($F$) of the engine, evaluated at the same flight condition:

$$
TAR = \frac{F_{\text{aug}}}{F}
$$

**Typical range (1 mark):** For an afterburning turbojet, TAR is typically in the range of **1.4 to 1.7**, depending on flight Mach number and reheat temperature.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Pitfall 1 (–2 marks):** Forgetting to include $(1 + f_{RH})$ in the augmented mass flow term. KTU expects the augmented mass to be $\dot{m}_a (1 + f_{RH})$, not just $\dot{m}_a$.
> - **Pitfall 2 (–2 marks):** Using $V_a$ as ambient temperature in the nozzle velocity expression. The correct variable is **total/ram temperature $T_{t2}$** at the inlet if the engine is in flight.
> - **Pitfall 3 (–1 mark):** Forgetting to convert $Q_{RH}$ from MJ/kg to J/kg before dividing. A silent unit error propagates and loses marks.
> - **Pitfall 4 (–1 mark):** Stating that reheat "improves propulsive efficiency" — this is the **opposite** of what happens. Reheat improves **thrust** but **decreases** propulsive efficiency.

---

### Question B-A (14 Marks)
> **[KTU University Exam — Jul 2024 — Adapted]**
> **Question A:**
> (a) **With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the working of a turbojet engine fitted with an afterburner. State the typical temperature limits of the afterburner.** (7 marks)
> (b) **A turbojet engine operates at static conditions with a primary (turbine exit) jet temperature of 850 K. The exhaust is passed through an afterburner where the temperature is raised to 1900 K. Take $c_p = 1148$ J/kg·K, $Q_{RH} = 43 \times 10^6$ J/kg, and $T_a = 288$ K. Calculate: (i) the reheat fuel–air ratio, (ii) the augmented thrust per unit mass flow, and (iii) the Thrust Augmentation Ratio (TAR).** (7 marks)

#### Solution to (a) — Block Diagram & Working (7 marks)

**Block Diagram (3 marks):**
The student is expected to draw the following functional flow:

$$
\text{Inlet} \rightarrow \text{Compressor} \rightarrow \text{Main Combustor} \rightarrow \text{Turbine} \rightarrow \boxed{\text{Afterburner}} \rightarrow \text{Propelling Nozzle}
$$

with two fuel lines — primary fuel to the main combustor, and **secondary (reheat) fuel** to the afterburner.

**Working (3 marks):** Air enters the diffuser, is compressed, mixed with fuel in the main combustor, expanded through the turbine (which drives the compressor), and the hot exhaust (still containing ~70 % unburnt O₂ at ~800–900 K) enters the afterburner. There, additional fuel is injected through spray bars and stabilised by flame holders (V-gutters). Combustion raises the temperature to 1800–2100 K. The high-energy gas then accelerates through a variable-area propelling nozzle (convergent–divergent in supersonic applications), producing augmented thrust.

**Temperature limits (1 mark):**
- Turbine inlet: ~1600 K (limited by blade metallurgy).
- Turbine exit / afterburner inlet: ~800–900 K.
- Afterburner exit: **1800 K – 2100 K** (limited by nozzle throat thermal loading).

#### Solution to (b) — Numerical (7 marks)

**Given:**
$T_j = 850$ K, $T_{je} = 1900$ K, $c_p = 1148$ J/kg·K, $Q_{RH} = 43 \times 10^6$ J/kg, $T_a = 288$ K, $V_a = 0$ (static).

**(i) Reheat fuel–air ratio (2 marks):**

$$
f_{RH} = \frac{c_p (T_{je} - T_j)}{Q_{RH}}
$$

$$
f_{RH} = \frac{1148 \times (1900 - 850)}{43 \times 10^6} = \frac{1148 \times 1050}{43 \times 10^6}
$$

$$
f_{RH} = \frac{1,205,400}{43,000,000} = 0.02803
$$

$$
\boxed{f_{RH} \approx 0.028 \text{ (kg of reheat fuel per kg of primary air)}}
$$

**(ii) Augmented thrust per unit mass flow (3 marks):**

At static conditions ($V_a = 0$), ideal nozzle velocity from afterburner exit:

$$
V_{je} = \sqrt{2 c_p (T_{je} - T_a)}
$$

$$
V_{je} = \sqrt{2 \times 1148 \times (1900 - 288)} = \sqrt{2 \times 1148 \times 1612}
$$

$$
V_{je} = \sqrt{3,701,152} \approx 1923.86 \text{ m/s}
$$

Augmented thrust per unit mass flow:

$$
F_{\text{aug}} / \dot{m}_a = (1 + f_{RH}) \cdot V_{je} = 1.028 \times 1923.86
$$

$$
\boxed{F_{\text{aug}} / \dot{m}_a \approx 1977.7 \text{ N·s/kg}}
$$

**(iii) Thrust Augmentation Ratio (2 marks):**

Dry nozzle velocity:

$$
V_j = \sqrt{2 c_p (T_j - T_a)} = \sqrt{2 \times 1148 \times (850 - 288)} = \sqrt{2 \times 1148 \times 562}
$$

$$
V_j = \sqrt{1,290,352} \approx 1135.94 \text{ m/s}
$$

Dry thrust per unit mass flow:

$$
F / \dot{m}_a = V_j = 1135.94 \text{ N·s/kg}
$$

TAR:

$$
TAR = \frac{(1 + f_{RH}) V_{je}}{V_j} = \frac{1.028 \times 1923.86}{1135.94} = \frac{1977.7}{1135.94}
$$

$$
\boxed{TAR \approx 1.741}
$$

The augmented thrust is **~74.1 % higher** than dry thrust.

---

### Question B-B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)
> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 — Adapted]**
> **Question B:**
> (a) *"Discuss the principle of water–methanol injection as a thrust augmentation technique. Mention its advantages and limitations."* (7 marks)
> (b) *"A military turbojet has a dry thrust of 25 kN at sea-level static conditions. With reheat, the augmented thrust is 40 kN. Calculate the thrust augmentation ratio, and the percentage increase in specific fuel consumption if the SFC without reheat is 1.1 mg/(N·s) and with reheat is 3.0 mg/(N·s)."* (7 marks)

#### Solution to (a) — Water–Methanol Injection (7 marks)

**Principle (3 marks):** Water–methanol is injected into either the **compressor diffuser** or the **main combustion chamber** of the turbojet. The water absorbs the latent heat of vaporisation (~2260 kJ/kg) from the combustion gases, allowing a larger mass of fuel to be burned without exceeding the turbine inlet temperature limit. The vaporised water also adds mass to the gas stream flowing through the nozzle. The methanol (~50 % by mass) prevents freezing in the injectors at high altitude.

**Advantages (2.5 marks):**
1. Increases mass flow rate — directly raises thrust ($F = \dot{m}(V_j - V_a)$).
2. Cools the combustion chamber walls — extends component life during high-thrust bursts.
3. Recovers compressor from stall during rapid throttle advance.
4. Simpler than reheat — no extra combustion chamber required.

**Limitations (1.5 marks):**
1. Water is heavy — fuel tankage reduces aircraft range.
2. Short duration only — water tank size limits the boost to a few minutes.
3. Corrosion of turbine blades due to water impurities.
4. Risk of ice formation in the injection system.
5. The augmentation is modest (TAR 1.2 – 1.3) compared to reheat (TAR 1.4 – 1.7).

#### Solution to (b) — Numerical (7 marks)

**Given:**
$F = 25$ kN, $F_{\text{aug}} = 40$ kN, $SFC = 1.1$ mg/(N·s), $SFC_{\text{aug}} = 3.0$ mg/(N·s).

**(i) Thrust Augmentation Ratio (3 marks):**

$$
TAR = \frac{F_{\text{aug}}}{F} = \frac{40}{25} = \boxed{1.6}
$$

**(ii) Percentage increase in SFC (4 marks):**

$$
\% \text{ increase} = \frac{SFC_{\text{aug}} - SFC}{SFC} \times 100
$$

$$
\% \text{ increase} = \frac{3.0 - 1.1}{1.1} \times 100 = \frac{1.9}{1.1} \times 100
$$

$$
\boxed{\% \text{ increase} \approx 172.7\%}
$$

**Engineering Interpretation (0 marks — bonus):** The SFC increases by ~173 %, meaning the reheat consumes **2.73× more fuel per unit thrust per second**. This illustrates why afterburners are reserved for short-duration combat use and not for sustained cruise.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Thrust Augmentation**

- **Definition:** Increasing thrust beyond the rated (un-augmented) value by injecting mass or heat **after** the turbine / combustor.
- **Three Methods:** Water injection (mass), Reheat/Afterburner (velocity), Ejector augmenter (entrainment).
- **Master Formula — Reheat Fuel–Air Ratio:**
  $f_{RH} = \dfrac{c_p (T_{je} - T_j)}{Q_{RH}}$ — derived from SFEE across the afterburner.
- **Master Formula — Augmented Thrust:**
  $F_{\text{aug}} = \dot{m}_a (1 + f_{RH}) (V_{je} - V_a)$.
- **Master Formula — TAR (static condition):**
  $TAR = (1 + f_{RH}) \sqrt{\dfrac{T_{je} - T_a}{T_j - T_a}}$.
- **Typical Values:** $T_{je} = 1800$ – $2100$ K, $f_{RH} \approx 0.025$ – $0.06$, TAR (reheat) $\approx 1.4$ – $1.7$.
- **Water Injection TAR:** $1.2$ – $1.3$ — **lower** than reheat.
- **Reheat INCREASES thrust** but **DECREASES propulsive efficiency** (because $V_j/V_a$ ratio increases).
- **Reheat FUEL consumption:** **3× to 4×** the dry-thrust fuel burn — used only for short bursts.
- **Real-World Examples:** Concorde (water-methanol on take-off), F-16 / Su-30MKI / Rafale (afterburners), F-22 (supercruise + afterburner).
- **Common Board Pitfalls:**
  - Forgetting the $(1 + f_{RH})$ factor in the augmented mass flow.
  - Using $T_a$ instead of $T_{t2}$ (ram temperature) when flight Mach number is given.
  - Saying reheat "improves propulsive efficiency" — **it does not**.
  - Confusing $Q_{RH}$ units (must be in J/kg, not MJ/kg).
- **Key Trade-off:** Thrust ↑ vs. Fuel Economy ↓ and Propulsive Efficiency ↓.
- **Why Reheat Works:** Turbine exhaust at ~850 K still contains unburnt O₂; additional fuel can be burned downstream of the turbine (in the afterburner), where there is no metallurgical temperature limit (only the nozzle throat).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Rocket Propulsion - Classification
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET741 - GAS TURBINE AND JET PROPULSION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Rocket Propulsion - Classification

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition
**Rocket Propulsion** is a thrust-producing mechanism that operates on the principle of **Newton's Third Law of Motion** ("For every action, there is an equal and opposite reaction"). Unlike air-breathing engines, a rocket carries *both* its oxidizer and fuel onboard, making it the only viable propulsion system capable of operating in the vacuum of space.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Exam Ready):**
> "A rocket is a vehicle or engine that obtains thrust by expelling propellant (fuel + oxidizer) at high velocity from a nozzle. Since it is self-contained, it does not require atmospheric oxygen for combustion and can therefore operate in space."

The fundamental governing relation for any rocket is **Newton's Second Law applied to a control volume of expelled mass**:

$$F_{thrust} = \dot{m} \cdot V_e + (P_e - P_a) \cdot A_e$$

Where the symbols carry standard rocket-engine meanings.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Inflated Balloon" Intuition
Imagine blowing up a balloon and letting it go without tying the neck. The compressed air rushes out of the open mouth in one direction, and the balloon shoots off in the *opposite* direction. A rocket works on *exactly* the same idea, but the "air" is a high-energy, super-heated gas produced by burning fuel + oxidizer, and the "balloon" is a precision-engineered combustion chamber + converging-diverging nozzle.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Examples of Rocket Action You See Daily:**
> - A fire extinguisher releasing CO₂ — it recoils backward as gas blasts forward.
> - Water escaping from a pressurized garden hose — the hose kicks back.
> - Crackers bursting on Diwali — explosive gases propel fragments outward.
> - SpaceX Falcon 9, ISRO PSLV, NASA SLS — the apex of this principle.

### 1.3 Why Rocket Propulsion? — The Key Advantage
| Feature | Air-Breathing Engine | Rocket Engine |
|---|---|---|
| Oxidizer source | Atmosphere (free) | Carried onboard (heavy) |
| Altitude limit | ~25 km (ramjet limit) | Unlimited |
| Space operation | Impossible | Possible |
| Static thrust at sea level | High | Moderate-High |
| Specific Impulse | Low-Medium | High |
| Complexity | Moderate | Very High |

### 1.4 Governing Physical Constants & Standard Metrics
> [!IMPORTANT]
> The three golden performance metrics you MUST memorize for KTU exams:
> 1. **Specific Impulse (Isp)** — measured in **seconds (s)** — "fuel efficiency of the rocket"
> 2. **Exhaust Velocity (Ve)** — measured in **m/s** — "how fast propellant leaves the nozzle"
> 3. **Mass Ratio (MR = m₀/m_f)** — dimensionless — "how much propellant vs. structure"
>
> The **universal gas constant** R = 8.314 J/(mol·K) and **gravitational acceleration** g₀ = 9.80665 m/s² are also frequently required.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Rocket Propulsion Systems

The classification is **multi-axial** — rockets are classified on *several* independent criteria, and a KTU exam question may ask you to "Classify rockets on the basis of energy source" OR "on the basis of propellant type" OR "on the basis of application." You must know all three.

#### 2.1.1 Classification Based on **Energy Source** (Most Important for KTU)

**(A) Chemical Rockets** — Energy comes from the chemical reaction of fuel + oxidizer.
- **Solid Propellant Rockets:**
  - Fuel + oxidizer premixed as a solid "grain" (e.g., ammonium perchlorate + aluminium + HTPB binder).
  - Stored in the combustion chamber itself.
  - *Cannot be throttled, cannot be restarted, simple storage.*
  - Examples: ISRO's Sounding Rockets, SLV-3 first stage, Space Shuttle SRBs.

- **Liquid Propellant Rockets:**
  - Fuel and oxidizer stored as liquids in separate tanks, fed by turbopumps.
  - *Throttleable, restartable, higher Isp.*
  - Examples: PSLV, GSLV, Falcon 9, Apollo Saturn V.

- **Hybrid Propellant Rockets:**
  - Solid fuel + liquid/gaseous oxidizer (e.g., HTPB solid fuel + liquid N₂O oxidizer).
  - *Combines safety of solids + controllability of liquids.*
  - Still largely experimental (SpaceShipOne used hybrid).

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic for Chemical Rockets:** "**S**olid, **L**iquid, **H**ybrid" → remember as **"SLH" = Silly Little Hybrids"**

**(B) Nuclear Rockets** — Energy from nuclear fission (or fusion, theoretically).
- Examples: NERVA (Nuclear Engine for Rocket Vehicle Application), Project Rover.
- *Very high Isp (~800–1000 s) but politically restricted.*

**(C) Electrical Rockets** — Energy from electrical power; thrust from accelerating ions/plasma.
- **Electrothermal** (Arcjet, Resistojet) — electrically heats propellant.
- **Electrostatic** (Ion engine, Hall-effect) — Coulomb force on charged ions.
- **Electromagnetic** (MHD, Pulsed Plasma Thruster) — Lorentz force on plasma.
- *Extremely high Isp (1000–10,000 s) but very low thrust — used for satellite station-keeping.*

**(D) Solar / Radiation Rockets** — Uses solar radiation pressure or photon emission.
- **Solar Sail** — Reflects sunlight for thrust.
- **Photon Rocket** — Theoretically perfect, expels photons.

#### 2.1.2 Classification Based on **Application**
- **Sounding Rockets** — Sub-orbital research (weather, microgravity).
- **Launch Vehicles** — Carry payloads to orbit (PSLV, GSLV Mk-III).
- **Spacecraft Propulsion** — In-space maneuvering (satellite thrusters).
- **Missile Propulsion** — Military tactical/strategic.

#### 2.1.3 Classification Based on **Construction / Staging**
- **Single-Stage Rocket** — One propulsive stage.
- **Multi-Stage Rocket** — Two or more stages stacked (parallel or series). Stages are jettisoned when their propellant is exhausted to shed dead weight.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates **every equation** you are likely to need for problems on this topic. Memorize symbol meanings; they appear in nearly every KTU problem.

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $F = \dot{m} \cdot V_e + (P_e - P_a) A_e$ | **Thrust equation** | N |
| 2 | $I_{sp} = \dfrac{F}{\dot{m} \cdot g_0} = \dfrac{V_e}{g_0}$ | **Specific Impulse** | s |
| 3 | $\Delta V = V_e \ln\!\left(\dfrac{m_0}{m_f}\right)$ | **Tsiolkovsky Equation** | m/s |
| 4 | $m_0 = m_{struc} + m_{prop} + m_{pay}$ | **Mass breakdown** | kg |
| 5 | $\text{Mass Ratio } MR = \dfrac{m_0}{m_f}$ | Initial-to-final mass ratio | dimensionless |
| 6 | $\text{Propellant Fraction } \zeta = \dfrac{m_{prop}}{m_0}$ | Fraction of initial mass that is propellant | dimensionless |
| 7 | $V_e = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \gamma R T_c}{(\gamma - 1)} \left[1 - \left(\dfrac{P_e}{P_c}\right)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}\right]}$ | **Ideal nozzle exit velocity** | m/s |
| 8 | $\Delta V_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} V_{e,i} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{m_{0,i}}{m_{f,i}}\right)$ | **Multi-stage ΔV sum** | m/s |
| 9 | $P_c = \dfrac{\dot{m} \, c^*}{A_t}$ | **Chamber pressure** | Pa |
| 10 | $c^* = \dfrac{\sqrt{\gamma R T_c / M}}{\gamma \left(\tfrac{2}{\gamma+1}\right)^{(\gamma+1)/[2(\gamma-1)]}}$ | **Characteristic velocity** | m/s |

> [!WARNING]
> In the table, $\vert x \vert$ is rendered as $\lvert x \rvert$ to prevent markdown parser breaks. Use `\lvert` or `\rvert` in your own LaTeX.

### 2.3 Engineering Utility of This Classification
- **Launch Vehicle Design:** Chemical rockets are used for lift-off (high thrust, low Isp). Electric thrusters are used for in-orbit maneuvering (low thrust, high Isp).
- **Mission Planning:** ΔV budget is allocated stage-by-stage using the Tsiolkovsky equation. Staging decision depends on the MR × Ve product.
- **Propellant Selection:** Trade-off between storability (hypergolics), performance (cryogenics), and safety (solids).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Thrust Equation

The thrust of a rocket engine is derived by applying **Newton's Second Law** to a control volume that encloses the rocket and includes the propellant being expelled.

**Step 1 — Set up the control volume:**
Consider a rocket at an instant, of mass $m$, expelling propellant at mass rate $\dot{m}$ (kg/s) with exit velocity $V_e$ (relative to rocket) through an exit area $A_e$ at exit pressure $P_e$, with ambient pressure $P_a$.

**Step 2 — Apply conservation of momentum:**
The momentum of the exhaust leaving the rocket in time $dt$ is:

$$dp_{exhaust} = \dot{m} \cdot V_e \cdot dt$$

By Newton's Third Law, an equal and opposite force is exerted on the rocket (the *momentum thrust* term):

$$F_{momentum} = \dot{m} \cdot V_e$$

**Step 3 — Account for the pressure difference at the nozzle exit:**
The exhaust gas at the nozzle exit plane has pressure $P_e$, and outside the ambient has pressure $P_a$. If $P_e \ne P_a$, there is a net pressure force on the nozzle exit area:

$$F_{pressure} = (P_e - P_a) \cdot A_e$$

This is called the **pressure thrust** term.

**Step 4 — Sum both contributions to obtain total thrust:**

$$\boxed{F = \dot{m} \cdot V_e + (P_e - P_a) \cdot A_e}$$

**Step 5 — Special case: Optimum Expansion at Altitude**
At high altitude, $P_a \to 0$. At optimum expansion, $P_e = P_a$, so the second term vanishes:

$$F_{vacuum} = \dot{m} \cdot V_e \quad ; \quad F_{sea\,level} = \dot{m} \cdot V_e + (P_e - P_a) A_e$$

This is why **the same engine produces MORE thrust in vacuum than at sea level** — a fact often tested in KTU.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Tsiolkovsky Rocket Equation

This is the **most important derivation in the entire course** for KTU. Derive carefully.

**Step 1 — Setup:**
Let the rocket's instantaneous mass be $m$, exhaust velocity $V_e$ (constant, relative to rocket), and let mass be expelled at rate $\dot{m} = -dm/dt$ (negative because $m$ decreases).

**Step 2 — Apply Newton's Second Law in free space (no gravity, no drag):**
Using Newton's Second Law with variable mass:

$$m \dfrac{dV}{dt} = V_e \left(-\dfrac{dm}{dt}\right)$$

The left side is the rocket's mass × acceleration; the right side is thrust = rate of momentum ejection.

**Step 3 — Rearrange variables to separate:**

$$dV = -V_e \dfrac{dm}{m}$$

**Step 4 — Integrate from initial state (mass $m_0$, velocity $V_0$) to final state (mass $m_f$, velocity $V_f$):**

$$\int_{V_0}^{V_f} dV = -V_e \int_{m_0}^{m_f} \dfrac{dm}{m}$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the integrals:**

$$V_f - V_0 = -V_e \left[\ln m\right]_{m_0}^{m_f} = -V_e \left(\ln m_f - \ln m_0\right) = V_e \ln\!\left(\dfrac{m_0}{m_f}\right)$$

**Step 6 — Define the change in velocity (ΔV):**

$$\boxed{\Delta V = V_e \ln\!\left(\dfrac{m_0}{m_f}\right)}$$

**Interpretation:**
- $\Delta V$ — total velocity change capability of the rocket (m/s).
- $V_e$ — exhaust velocity (m/s).
- $m_0 / m_f$ — mass ratio (initial mass ÷ final mass after all propellant burnt).

### 3.3 Derivation of the Multi-Stage ΔV Equation

For a multi-stage rocket, after each stage $i$ is burnt, the burnt stage is jettisoned. The total $\Delta V$ is the **sum** of each stage's $\Delta V$:

$$\Delta V_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} V_{e,i} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{m_{0,i}}{m_{f,i}}\right)$$

This is because each stage starts from where the previous stage ended, and the burnt mass of previous stages is dropped (improving $m_{0,i}/m_{f,i}$ for subsequent stages).

### 3.4 Analytical Worked Example (KTU Pattern)

**Problem:** A rocket has $V_e = 2400$ m/s, $m_0 = 50{,}000$ kg, $m_f = 10{,}000$ kg. Find ΔV.

**Solution:**

Step 1: Identify parameters. $V_e = 2400$ m/s, $m_0 = 50{,}000$ kg, $m_f = 10{,}000$ kg.

Step 2: Compute mass ratio.

$$MR = \dfrac{m_0}{m_f} = \dfrac{50{,}000}{10{,}000} = 5$$

Step 3: Apply Tsiolkovsky's equation.

$$\Delta V = V_e \ln(MR) = 2400 \times \ln(5)$$

Step 4: Evaluate $\ln(5) = 1.6094$.

$$\Delta V = 2400 \times 1.6094 = 3862.6 \text{ m/s}$$

Step 5: Result. $\Delta V \approx 3.86$ km/s — sufficient to reach Low Earth Orbit (LEO needs ~9.4 km/s, so additional stages or boosters would be needed).

### 3.5 Classification Analytical Matrix

| Class | Energy Source | Typical Isp (s) | Typical Thrust (N) | Example Mission |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Solid Chemical | Combustion | 200–300 | $10^4$ – $10^7$ | SLV-3, Shuttle SRB |
| Liquid Chemical | Combustion | 300–500 | $10^4$ – $10^7$ | PSLV, Falcon 9 |
| Hybrid Chemical | Combustion | 250–400 | $10^3$ – $10^5$ | SpaceShipOne |
| Nuclear (Fission) | Fission | 800–1000 | $10^5$ – $10^6$ | NERVA (cancelled) |
| Electrothermal (Arcjet) | Electric heating | 500–1500 | $10^{-1}$ – $10^1$ | Station-keeping |
| Electrostatic (Ion) | Coulomb | 1500–5000 | $10^{-3}$ – $10^0$ | Dawn, BepiColombo |
| Electromagnetic (PPT) | Lorentz | 1000–3000 | $10^{-6}$ – $10^{-2}$ | CubeSat propulsion |
| Solar Sail | Photon pressure | N/A (accel.) | $10^{-3}$ – $10^{-2}$ | IKAROS |

> [!NOTE]
> The **trade-off** is universal: **High Isp ⟹ Low Thrust, and vice versa.** Chemical rockets are the only systems with both high thrust AND reasonable Isp, which is why they dominate launch applications.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Rocket Classification Tree (Mermaid)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[ROCKET PROPULSION] --> B[Based on Energy Source]
    A --> C[Based on Application]
    A --> D[Based on Staging]

    B --> B1[Chemical]

    B1 --> B1a[Solid Propellant]

    B3 --> B3a[Electrothermal Arcjet Resistojet]

    C --> C1[Sounding Rocket]

    D --> D2[Multi Stage Series]
    D --> D3[Multi Stage Parallel]
```

```mermaid
    A[Oxidizer Tank] -->|High Pressure| B[Turbopump]
```
