
### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Rocket Classification Logic

```mermaid
graph TD
    P0[Enter Rocket Mission Profile] --> P1{Where will it operate?}
    P1 --> P1a[In Atmosphere only] --> P1a1[Use Air Breathing unless high speed]
    P1 --> P1b[In Space or High Altitude] --> P2{Is high thrust required?}
    P2 -->|Yes| P3[Choose Chemical Rocket]
    P2 -->|No| P4[Choose Electric Rocket for high Isp]
    P3 --> P3a{Propellant storage feasible?}
    P3a -->|Long term yes| P3a1[Liquid Propellant]
    P3a -->|Simple and short| P3a2[Solid Propellant]
    P3a -->|Throttle needed and safe| P3a3[Hybrid Propellant]
    P4 --> P4a{Mission duration?}
    P4a -->|Months| P4a1[Ion Engine e.g. Xenon]
    P4a -->|Years High Power| P4a2[Hall Effect Thruster]
    P4a -->|Short Burst Low Power| P4a3[Pulsed Plasma Thruster]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**"Classify rockets on the basis of the energy source used for propellant heating."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Rockets are classified into four main categories based on energy source:
1. **Chemical Rockets** — Energy from chemical reaction of fuel + oxidizer. Subdivided into solid, liquid, and hybrid. Example: PSLV.
2. **Nuclear Rockets** — Energy from nuclear fission of a reactor core. Example: NERVA. Very high Isp (~800 s) but politically restricted.
3. **Electrical Rockets** — Energy from solar cells or nuclear batteries, used to accelerate propellant. Subdivided into electrothermal (arcjet), electrostatic (ion engine), and electromagnetic (MHD, PPT). Isp 1000–10000 s but very low thrust.
4. **Solar/Radiation Rockets** — Use solar radiation pressure (solar sail) or direct photon emission.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**"Define specific impulse. Why is it a key parameter in rocket classification?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Specific Impulse ($I_{sp}$) is defined as the thrust produced per unit weight flow rate of propellant:

$$I_{sp} = \dfrac{F}{\dot{m} \cdot g_0} = \dfrac{V_e}{g_0}$$

It is measured in **seconds**. It is the key classification parameter because:
- It directly represents the **fuel efficiency** of a propulsion system.
- Higher Isp means *less propellant is needed for a given ΔV* (from Tsiolkovsky equation).
- It cleanly separates rockets into "high-thrust low-Isp" (chemical) and "low-thrust high-Isp" (electric) categories, dictating the mission type for which each is suitable.

---

### Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, the question below carries an **internal choice** — you must attempt either OR.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Full Question

**[KTU University Exam — May 2023 Pattern]**
**(a)** Derive the Tsiolkovsky rocket equation and explain the significance of mass ratio and exhaust velocity. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A two-stage rocket has the following data:
- Stage 1: $V_{e1} = 2500$ m/s, $m_{01} = 100{,}000$ kg, $m_{f1} = 30{,}000$ kg
- Stage 2: $V_{e2} = 3000$ m/s, $m_{02} = 25{,}000$ kg, $m_{f2} = 5{,}000$ kg
Compute the total $\Delta V$ delivered by the rocket. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to A(a):**

**Step 1** — State the law of conservation of momentum applied to a rocket of instantaneous mass $m$:

$$m \dfrac{dV}{dt} = -V_e \dfrac{dm}{dt}$$

**Step 2** — Separate variables:

$$dV = -V_e \dfrac{dm}{m}$$

**Step 3** — Integrate from $m_0$ to $m_f$ and $V_0$ to $V_f$:

$$\int_{V_0}^{V_f} dV = -V_e \int_{m_0}^{m_f} \dfrac{dm}{m}$$

**Step 4** — Evaluate:

$$V_f - V_0 = V_e \ln\!\left(\dfrac{m_0}{m_f}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Significance:**
- **Exhaust velocity $V_e$:** Higher $V_e$ gives exponentially higher $\Delta V$ for the same mass ratio. It depends on combustion temperature, molecular weight, and nozzle design.
- **Mass ratio $m_0/m_f$:** Higher mass ratio = more propellant = more ΔV. The logarithm means ΔV grows *logarithmically* — doubling propellant does NOT double ΔV.
- This equation shows **why staging is essential**: shedding burnt structure reduces $m_0$ for the next stage, allowing overall ΔV to reach orbital values (~9.4 km/s for LEO).

---

**Model Solution to A(b):**

**Step 1 — Compute Stage 1 ΔV:**

$$\Delta V_1 = V_{e1} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{m_{01}}{m_{f1}}\right) = 2500 \times \ln\!\left(\dfrac{100{,}000}{30{,}000}\right)$$

$$\Delta V_1 = 2500 \times \ln(3.333) = 2500 \times 1.2040 = 3010 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Compute Stage 2 ΔV:**

$$\Delta V_2 = V_{e2} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{m_{02}}{m_{f2}}\right) = 3000 \times \ln\!\left(\dfrac{25{,}000}{5{,}000}\right)$$

$$\Delta V_2 = 3000 \times \ln(5) = 3000 \times 1.6094 = 4828.3 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 3 — Total ΔV (sum, since stages add):**

$$\Delta V_{total} = \Delta V_1 + \Delta V_2 = 3010 + 4828.3 = 7838.3 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 4 — Conclusion:** Total ΔV ≈ 7.84 km/s — close to orbital requirement, suitable for LEO insertion.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Full Question (OR option)

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 Pattern]**
**(a)** Classify rocket propulsion systems in detail based on the energy source. Give one example of each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A rocket engine operates at sea level with $V_e = 2200$ m/s, $\dot{m} = 500$ kg/s, $P_e = 0.4 \times 10^5$ Pa, $A_e = 0.5$ m², and $P_a = 1.013 \times 10^5$ Pa. Compute the thrust developed and the specific impulse. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to B(a):**

**(i) Chemical Rockets (3 marks):** Energy from exothermic chemical reaction of fuel + oxidizer stored onboard. Subdivided into:
- *Solid Propellant* — fuel + oxidizer premixed as grain; e.g., SLV-3 first stage.
- *Liquid Propellant* — liquid fuel (e.g., kerosene, LH₂) and oxidizer (LO₂, N₂O₄) in separate tanks; e.g., PSLV core.
- *Hybrid Propellant* — solid fuel + liquid oxidizer; e.g., SpaceShipOne.

**(ii) Nuclear Rockets (1 mark):** Energy from nuclear fission. Propellant (e.g., LH₂) heated by reactor core. Example: NERVA. Isp ~ 800–1000 s.

**(iii) Electrical Rockets (2 marks):** Electrical energy (from solar panels or nuclear batteries) accelerates propellant.
- *Electrothermal* (arcjet, resistojet) — heat propellant electrically.
- *Electrostatic* (ion engine, Hall thruster) — accelerate charged ions via electric field.
- *Electromagnetic* (MHD, PPT) — accelerate plasma via Lorentz force.
Examples: Dawn (ion), Starlink (Hall).

**(iv) Solar/Radiation Rockets (1 mark):** Use solar photon pressure. Example: IKAROS solar sail.

---

**Model Solution to B(b):**

**Step 1 — Compute momentum thrust:**

$$F_{mom} = \dot{m} \cdot V_e = 500 \times 2200 = 1{,}100{,}000 \text{ N} = 1.1 \times 10^6 \text{ N}$$

**Step 2 — Compute pressure thrust:**

$$F_{press} = (P_e - P_a) A_e = (0.4 \times 10^5 - 1.013 \times 10^5) \times 0.5$$

$$F_{press} = (-0.613 \times 10^5) \times 0.5 = -0.3065 \times 10^5 \text{ N} = -30{,}650 \text{ N}$$

(Negative because $P_e < P_a$ — the nozzle is underexpanded at sea level.)

**Step 3 — Total thrust:**

$$F = 1.1 \times 10^6 + (-0.3065 \times 10^5) = 1.1 \times 10^6 - 0.0307 \times 10^6 = 1.069 \times 10^6 \text{ N}$$

$$\boxed{F \approx 1.07 \times 10^6 \text{ N} \;(\approx 1069 \text{ kN})}$$

**Step 4 — Specific Impulse:**

$$I_{sp} = \dfrac{F}{\dot{m} \cdot g_0} = \dfrac{1.069 \times 10^6}{500 \times 9.80665}$$

$$I_{sp} = \dfrac{1.069 \times 10^6}{4903.3} \approx 218 \text{ s}$$

**Step 5 — Conclusion:** $F \approx 1.07$ MN, $I_{sp} \approx 218$ s — typical of a sea-level liquid rocket engine.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Forgetting the pressure thrust term** in the thrust equation when $P_e \ne P_a$. At sea level, ALWAYS include it.
> 2. **Sign convention error** in $(P_e - P_a)$: if $P_e < P_a$, the pressure term is *negative* and *reduces* thrust.
> 3. **Using $V_e$ as the *absolute* exhaust velocity** in the Tsiolkovsky equation — it must be *relative to the rocket*, not relative to the ground. In problems where the rocket is moving, the absolute exit velocity is $V_e - V_{rocket}$.
> 4. **Adding ΔV values in single-stage form** for multi-stage rockets: each stage must be computed with *its own* mass ratio AFTER the previous stage's structure is jettisoned.
> 5. **Mixing up mass ratio direction** — it is $m_0 / m_f$ (initial ÷ final), NOT the reverse.
> 6. **Forgetting to mention gravity and drag losses** when applying Tsiolkovsky — in reality $\Delta V_{achieved} < \Delta V_{ideal}$ because gravity and drag eat up velocity.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Definition:** Rocket = self-contained propulsion, onboard oxidizer, works in vacuum.
- ✅ **Principle:** Newton's Third Law (action-reaction) — propellant ejected, rocket accelerated.
- ✅ **Thrust Equation:** $F = \dot{m} V_e + (P_e - P_a) A_e$ — MEMORIZE.
- ✅ **Specific Impulse:** $I_{sp} = V_e / g_0$ — measured in **seconds**, NOT Newtons.
- ✅ **Tsiolkovsky Equation:** $\Delta V = V_e \ln(m_0 / m_f)$ — the most important formula.
- ✅ **Mass Ratio:** $MR = m_0 / m_f \ge 1$; propellant fraction $\zeta = m_{prop}/m_0$.
- ✅ **Multi-stage ΔV:** $\Delta V_{total} = \sum V_{e,i} \ln(m_{0,i}/m_{f,i})$.
- ✅ **Classification — Energy Source:** Chemical (solid/liquid/hybrid), Nuclear, Electrical (electrothermal/electrostatic/electromagnetic), Solar.
- ✅ **Classification — Application:** Sounding, Launch Vehicle, Spacecraft, Missile.
- ✅ **Classification — Staging:** Single-stage, Multi-stage (series = stacked, parallel = strapped-on boosters).
- ✅ **Trade-off:** High Isp ↔ Low Thrust. Chemical = high thrust, low Isp. Electric = low thrust, high Isp.
- ✅ **At sea level vs vacuum:** Same engine gives MORE thrust in vacuum (pressure thrust term is zero or positive).
- ✅ **Examples to remember:** PSLV = liquid; SLV-3 / Shuttle SRB = solid; SpaceShipOne = hybrid; Dawn spacecraft = ion; IKAROS = solar sail.
- ✅ **Why staging?** Because ΔV grows *logarithmically* with mass ratio — shedding dead structure of burnt stages dramatically improves overall ΔV.
- ✅ **Constants:** $g_0 = 9.80665$ m/s², $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K), $\gamma \approx 1.2$–$1.4$ for typical exhaust gases.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Principle of Rocket Propulsion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET741 - GAS TURBINE AND JET PROPULSION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Principle of Rocket Propulsion

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal KTU Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rocket Propulsion** is a reaction propulsion system in which **both the fuel and the oxidizer (propellants) are carried within the vehicle itself**, enabling operation independently of the surrounding atmosphere. Thrust is generated by the **high-velocity expulsion of combustion products** through a converging-diverging (De Laval) nozzle, in strict accordance with **Newton's Third Law of Motion** (conservation of linear momentum).

Unlike air-breathing engines (turbojets, ramjets, scramjets), a rocket is a **self-contained thermodynamic propulsion package** that performs equally well in:

- **Sea-level atmosphere** (Take-off phase)
- **Stratosphere** (Partial atmosphere)
- **Outer space / hard vacuum** (Orbital maneuvers, deep-space probes)

This makes the rocket the **only viable propulsion system for space exploration**.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The Inflating Balloon

Imagine a balloon filled with air, released with its neck open:

- The air rushes **out the back** (high-velocity exhaust).
- The balloon shoots **forward** in the opposite direction (thrust).

A rocket works on the **exact same principle**, except:
- Instead of compressed air, **high-temperature, high-pressure combustion gases** are expelled.
- The combustion is sustained **internally** by carrying both fuel *and* liquid oxygen (LOX) or solid oxidizer.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 PEMET741):** The Principle of Rocket Propulsion module emphasizes the **ideal thrust equation**, the **Tsiolkovsky rocket equation (ideal velocity)**, **specific impulse**, and the **functional distinction** between rockets and atmospheric jet engines.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|
| Standard gravity | $g_0$ | **9.80665 m/s²** | Used for $I_{sp}$ normalization |
| Universal gas constant | $R$ | **8.314 J/(mol·K)** | Combustion gas property |
| Effective exhaust velocity (Liquid H₂/LOX) | $V_e$ | **~4400 m/s** | Vacuum-class upper stage |
| Specific impulse (LH₂/LOX) | $I_{sp}$ | **~450 s** | Highest chemical $I_{sp}$ |
| Specific impulse (Solid AP/HTPB) | $I_{sp}$ | **~250–280 s** | Tactical / booster rockets |
| Stagnation pressure ratio | $P_0/P_a$ | **>40:1** (typical) | Chamber-to-ambient |

## 1.4 Classification Snapshot

> [!NOTE]
> Rockets are broadly classified by **propellant phase**:
> 1. **Solid Propellant Rockets** — fuel + oxidizer premixed as a grain (e.g., PSLV strap-ons).
> 2. **Liquid Propellant Rockets** — fuel and oxidizer fed separately (e.g., cryogenic upper stage).
> 3. **Hybrid Rockets** — solid fuel + liquid/gaseous oxidizer.
> 4. **Nuclear / Electric / Ion** — non-chemical, used for deep space (not in KTU core syllabus).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Working Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Propellant Storage:** Fuel (e.g., kerosene, liquid hydrogen) and oxidizer (LOX) are stored in separate tanks inside the rocket airframe.
2. **Pressurization & Injection:** Turbopumps (or gas pressure feed) push propellants into the **combustion chamber** at high mass flow rate $\dot{m}$.
3. **Combustion:** Mixing and ignition raise the gas temperature to **3000–3500 K** at chamber pressure $P_c \approx 50$–$200$ bar.
4. **Nozzle Expansion:** Hot gases pass through a **converging-diverging (C-D) nozzle**.
   - **Subsonic acceleration** in the converging section.
   - **Sonic condition (M = 1)** at the throat.
   - **Supersonic acceleration** in the diverging section, exiting at velocity $V_e$.
5. **Thrust Generation:** By Newton's 3rd Law, the momentum flux of the exiting gas produces an equal-and-opposite reaction force on the rocket structure.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Why carry oxidizer?** Beyond ~25 km altitude and at orbital speeds, atmospheric oxygen is insufficient or unavailable. Rockets must be self-sufficient.
- **Why supersonic nozzle?** To convert the **thermal energy** of combustion gases into **directed kinetic energy** of the jet (high $V_e$). The C-D nozzle is the only geometry that accelerates a compressible fluid from subsonic to supersonic.
- **Why high $V_e$?** Thrust $F \propto \dot{m} V_e$. A higher exhaust velocity means **more thrust per unit propellant consumed**, which directly translates to greater $\Delta V$ (mission capability).

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **complete KTU exam-ready formula set** for Module 4 — Principle of Rocket Propulsion. Memorize every row.

| # | Quantity | Equation | Symbol Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Ideal Thrust (general)** | $F = \dot{m} V_e + (P_e - P_a) A_e$ | $\dot{m}$ = mass flow, $V_e$ = exhaust vel., $P_e$ = exit pressure, $P_a$ = ambient, $A_e$ = exit area | N |
| 2 | **Vacuum Thrust** | $F_{vac} = \dot{m} V_e + P_e A_e$ | No ambient pressure term | N |
| 3 | **Optimum Expansion Thrust** | $F_{opt} = \dot{m} V_e$ | When $P_e = P_a$ (perfectly expanded) | N |
| 4 | **Specific Impulse** | $I_{sp} = \dfrac{F}{\dot{m} g_0} = \dfrac{V_e}{g_0}$ | "Seconds" — propellant efficiency metric | s |
| 5 | **Tsiolkovsky Rocket Eqn** | $\Delta V = V_e \ln\left(\dfrac{m_0}{m_f}\right)$ | $m_0$ = initial mass, $m_f$ = final (dry) mass | m/s |
| 6 | **Mass Ratio** | $MR = \dfrac{m_0}{m_f}$ | Always $> 1$ | dimensionless |
| 7 | **Propellant Mass Fraction** | $\zeta = \dfrac{m_0 - m_f}{m_0} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{MR}$ | Fraction of mass that is propellant | dimensionless |
| 8 | **Payload Fraction** | $\Pi = \dfrac{m_{payload}}{m_0}$ | Useful payload / total launch mass | dimensionless |
| 9 | **Thrust Coefficient** | $C_F = \dfrac{F}{P_c A_t}$ | $A_t$ = throat area, $P_c$ = chamber pressure | dimensionless |
| 10 | **Characteristic Velocity** | $c^* = \dfrac{P_c A_t}{\dot{m}}$ | Combustion performance index | m/s |
| 11 | **Total Mass Flow** | $\dot{m} = \dot{m}_f + \dot{m}_{ox}$ | Sum of fuel + oxidizer flow rates | kg/s |
| 12 | **Mixture Ratio** | $r = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{ox}}{\dot{m}_f}$ | Stoichiometric optimum for given propellant | dimensionless |
| 13 | **Thrust-to-Weight Ratio** | $T/W = \dfrac{F}{m_0 g_0}$ | $>1$ needed for vertical lift-off | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Boundary Condition:** At **optimum (design) altitude**, $P_e = P_a$, so the pressure-thrust term $(P_e - P_a)A_e$ **vanishes**, and the nozzle reaches its highest efficiency. Below design altitude, $P_e > P_a$ → **underexpanded** → loss of thrust. Above design altitude, $P_e < P_a$ → **overexpanded** → flow separation risk.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Launch Vehicle Sizing:** Tsiolkovsky equation is used by ISRO, NASA, SpaceX to size propellant tanks for a target orbital $\Delta V$ (~9.4 km/s for LEO).
- **Engine Health Monitoring:** $c^*$ drop in flight indicates combustion chamber anomalies (injector coking, erosion).
- **Stage Optimization:** High $V_e$ upper stages use LH₂/LOX; lower stages use denser, cheaper kerosene/LOX for higher $\zeta$.
- **Mission Analysis:** Specific impulse directly governs **payload mass** delivered per kilogram of propellant — the holy grail of rocket economics.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Ideal Thrust Equation

We apply the **steady-flow momentum equation** (Newton's 2nd Law for a control volume) to the rocket engine.

### Setup
Consider a rocket engine as an **open control volume** with:
- Single inlet (mass flow in = $\dot{m}$)
- Single exit (mass flow out = $\dot{m}$, velocity $V_e$, pressure $P_e$, area $A_e$)
- External ambient pressure $P_a$ acting on the exit plane.
- Rocket ejects propellant rearward; thrust acts forward on vehicle.

### Step 1 — Apply Conservation of Momentum

The net force on the control volume equals the **net efflux of momentum**:

$$F_{net} = \dot{m} V_e - \dot{m} V_{inlet}$$

Since propellants enter at very low velocity compared to $V_e$, we take $V_{inlet} \approx 0$. Thus:

$$F_{gross} = \dot{m} V_e$$

This is the **momentum thrust** — the dominant term.

### Step 2 — Apply Pressure Forces on the Control Surface

The ambient pressure $P_a$ acts on the **entire outer surface** of the engine. On all surfaces *except* the nozzle exit plane, internal pressure balances $P_a$. On the exit plane, however, the internal gas exerts $P_e$ while outside, $P_a$ acts inward.

The **net pressure thrust** (forward on the vehicle) is therefore:

$$F_{pressure} = (P_e - P_a) A_e$$

### Step 3 — Combine Both Contributions

The total thrust on the rocket (forward reaction force) is:

$$\boxed{\,F = \dot{m} V_e + (P_e - P_a) A_e\,}$$

This is the **Universal Thrust Equation** for rocket engines — universally tested in KTU university exams.

### Special Cases (Boundary Conditions)

| Condition | Condition on Pressures | Resulting Thrust |
|---|---|---|
| **Optimum expansion** (design altitude) | $P_e = P_a$ | $F = \dot{m} V_e$ |
| **Vacuum operation** | $P_a = 0$ | $F = \dot{m} V_e + P_e A_e$ |
| **Underexpanded** (below design alt.) | $P_e > P_a$ | $F > \dot{m} V_e$ (extra pressure thrust) |
| **Overexpanded** (above design alt.) | $P_e < P_a$ | $F < \dot{m} V_e$ (reduced thrust) |

## 3.2 Derivation of Specific Impulse

By **definition**, specific impulse is thrust produced per unit weight-flow of propellant:

$$I_{sp} = \frac{F}{\dot{m} \, g_0}$$

For the **optimum-expansion** case ($F = \dot{m} V_e$):

$$I_{sp} = \frac{\dot{m} V_e}{\dot{m} \, g_0} = \frac{V_e}{g_0}$$

$$\boxed{\,I_{sp} = \dfrac{V_e}{g_0}\,}$$

**Physical meaning:** $I_{sp}$ tells us **how many seconds a unit weight of propellant can produce a unit of thrust**. Higher $I_{sp}$ = more "mileage" from propellant.

## 3.3 Derivation of the Tsiolkovsky Rocket Equation

This is the **single most important equation** in astronautics. It links mission $\Delta V$ to propellant mass.

### Step 1 — Instantaneous Thrust and Acceleration

At any instant, the rocket of instantaneous mass $m$ ejects propellant at exhaust velocity $V_e$ (relative to rocket). The thrust is:

$$F = -V_e \frac{dm}{dt} \quad \text{(negative because } m \text{ is decreasing)}$$

### Step 2 — Newton's 2nd Law for a Variable-Mass System

$$m \frac{dV}{dt} = -V_e \frac{dm}{dt}$$

### Step 3 — Rearrange and Separate Variables

$$dV = -V_e \frac{dm}{m}$$

### Step 4 — Integrate from initial to final state

Integrating from $V_0$ to $V_f$ and $m_0$ to $m_f$:

$$\int_{V_0}^{V_f} dV = -V_e \int_{m_0}^{m_f} \frac{dm}{m}$$

$$V_f - V_0 = -V_e \left[ \ln m \right]_{m_0}^{m_f}$$

$$V_f - V_0 = -V_e \left( \ln m_f - \ln m_0 \right) = V_e \ln\left(\frac{m_0}{m_f}\right)$$

### Step 5 — Define Mission Delta-V

$$\boxed{\,\Delta V = V_e \ln\!\left(\dfrac{m_0}{m_f}\right) = V_e \ln(MR)\,}$$

where $MR = m_0/m_f$ is the **mass ratio**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** $\Delta V$ is **logarithmic** in mass ratio. To double $\Delta V$, you must **square** the mass ratio. This is why rockets are stacked in **stages** — to discard empty tank mass early.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam-Style)

### Problem
A rocket has the following data:
- Initial mass $m_0 = 50{,}000$ kg
- Propellant mass $m_p = 45{,}000$ kg
- Effective exhaust velocity $V_e = 2500$ m/s
- Thrust at design altitude $F = 1{,}200{,}000$ N

### Solution

**Step A — Final (dry) mass:**
$$m_f = m_0 - m_p = 50{,}000 - 45{,}000 = 5{,}000 \text{ kg}$$

**Step B — Mass ratio:**
$$MR = \frac{m_0}{m_f} = \frac{50{,}000}{5{,}000} = 10$$

**Step C — Propellant mass fraction:**
$$\zeta = 1 - \frac{1}{MR} = 1 - 0.1 = 0.9 \quad (90\%)$$

**Step D — Ideal $\Delta V$:**
$$\Delta V = V_e \ln(MR) = 2500 \times \ln(10) = 2500 \times 2.3026 = 5756.5 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step E — Mass flow rate (from thrust equation, optimum expansion):**
$$F = \dot{m} V_e \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \dot{m} = \frac{F}{V_e} = \frac{1{,}200{,}000}{2500} = 480 \text{ kg/s}$$

**Step F — Specific impulse:**
$$I_{sp} = \frac{V_e}{g_0} = \frac{2500}{9.80665} \approx 254.9 \text{ s}$$

**Step G — Burn time:**
$$t_b = \frac{m_p}{\dot{m}} = \frac{45{,}000}{480} = 93.75 \text{ s}$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Architecture — Rocket Propulsion System

The diagram below maps the **functional flow** of propellant, energy, and thrust through a typical liquid-propellant rocket engine. This satisfies the Mermaid block-level architecture requirement.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Fuel Tank RP-1 or LH2] --> B[Turbopump Stage]
    C[Oxidizer Tank LOX] --> B
    B --> D[Regeneration Cooling Jacket]
    D --> E[Injector Head]
    E --> F[Combustion Chamber P_c 50 to 200 bar]
    F --> G[Converging Section Subsonic]
    G --> H[Throat M = 1 Sonic]
    H --> I[Diverging Section Supersonic]
    I --> J[Exit Plane V_e P_e]
    J --> K[Ambient Environment P_a]
    F --> L[Turbine Exhaust]
    L --> M[Gas Generator or Staged Combustion]
    M --> B
    K --> N[Reaction Thrust F on Vehicle]
    J --> N
```

### Block-Level Reading Guide

- **A → B → F:** Propellant feed path (mechanical → pressurized → chamber).
- **F → G → H → I → J:** Nozzle gas-dynamic expansion (subsonic → sonic → supersonic → exit).
- **F → L → M → B:** The **turbopump power cycle** — hot gas from the chamber drives the turbine, which drives the pumps. This is the energy-feedback loop.
- **J + K → N:** Thrust is the **vector sum** of momentum thrust ($\dot{m}V_e$) and pressure thrust ($(P_e-P_a)A_e$).

## 4.2 Thrust Equation Composition Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    F[Total Thrust F] --> M1[Momentum Thrust m_dot times V_e]
    F --> M2[Pressure Thrust P_e minus P_a times A_e]
    M1 --> S1[Always positive dominant term]
    M2 --> S2{Altitude vs Design?}
    S2 -->|Below design| S3[P_e greater than P_a underexpanded]
    S2 -->|At design| S4[P_e equals P_a optimum]
    S2 -->|Above design| S5[P_e less than P_a overexpanded]
```

## 4.3 Mass Ratio vs $\Delta V$ Trade-off Curve (Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph Inputs["Inputs"]
        I1[V_e exhaust velocity]
        I2[m_0 initial mass]
        I3[m_f dry mass]
    end
    subgraph Computation["Tsiolkovsky Computation"]
        C1[Mass Ratio MR = m_0 / m_f]
        C2[ln MR]
        C3[Delta_V = V_e times ln MR]
    end
    subgraph Outputs["Mission Outputs"]
        O1[LEO insert ~9.4 km per s]
        O2[GTO transfer ~11.2 km per s]
        O3[Escape velocity ~12.0 km per s]
    end
    I1 --> C3
    I2 --> C1
    I3 --> C1
    C1 --> C2 --> C3
    C3 --> O1
    C3 --> O2
    C3 --> O3
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading aid:** The logarithmic nature of the Tsiolkovsky equation means **doubling payload mass ratio requires an exponentially larger rocket**. This is the fundamental engineering reason space launch is *expensive*.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Differentiate between a rocket engine and a turbojet engine with respect to oxidizer source, working medium, and operational altitude envelope.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Rocket Engine | Turbojet Engine |
|---|---|---|
| Oxidizer | Carried onboard (LOX/solid) | Drawn from atmosphere |
| Working medium | Combustion products of onboard propellants | Atmospheric air + fuel |
| Altitude envelope | Sea level to deep space (no limit) | ~0–25 km (requires air) |
| Thrust in vacuum | Full thrust available | Cannot operate |
| Net thrust source | $\dot{m}V_e + (P_e-P_a)A_e$ | $\dot{m}(V_e - V_\infty)$ |

A rocket is **self-contained**; a turbojet is **air-dependent**. Hence rockets are the **only option for space propulsion**.

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define specific impulse. Why is it called the "figure of merit" of a rocket propellant?**

**Model Answer:**
Specific impulse $I_{sp}$ is defined as the thrust delivered per unit weight-flow rate of propellant:
$$I_{sp} = \frac{F}{\dot{m}\,g_0} = \frac{V_e}{g_0} \quad \text{[seconds]}$$
It is called the **figure of merit** because it quantifies, in a **single dimensionless-friendly parameter (seconds)**, how efficiently a given propellant combination converts chemical energy into useful thrust. A higher $I_{sp}$ directly translates to **less propellant required for the same $\Delta V$**, which means **larger payload mass fraction** — the central economic metric of any launch vehicle.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** In thrust-equation problems, students **routinely lose 3–4 marks** by:
> 1. Forgetting the **pressure thrust term** $(P_e - P_a)A_e$ and writing only $F = \dot{m}V_e$.
> 2. Mixing up $V_e$ (exhaust velocity, absolute frame) with $V_j$ (sometimes used in textbooks).
> 3. **Not stating the expansion condition** explicitly (optimum/underexpanded/overexpanded).
> 4. In Tsiolkovsky problems, failing to **convert $\Delta V$ in km/s to m/s** before applying the equation.
> Always **state the boundary condition** and **show unit consistency**.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)
`[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]`

**(a) Derive the general thrust equation of a rocket engine. Clearly state the assumptions made and explain each term physically. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Assumptions:**
1. Steady, one-dimensional flow.
2. Propellant inlet velocity is negligible compared to exhaust velocity.
3. Flow is along the nozzle axis (no side forces).
4. Pressure $P_a$ acts uniformly on all external surfaces except the exit plane.

**Derivation (as in Section 3.1):**
Applying steady-flow momentum equation to the control volume enclosing the engine:

$$\sum F = \dot{m}_{out} V_{out} - \dot{m}_{in} V_{in}$$

With $V_{in} \approx 0$ and exit velocity $= V_e$:

$$F_{momentum} = \dot{m} V_e$$

Now the **pressure force balance** on the control surface. Internal pressure $P_e$ acts on exit plane pushing **outward**, while external $P_a$ pushes **inward**. On all other surfaces, internal and external pressures cancel.

Net pressure force on vehicle (forward) = $(P_e - P_a) A_e$.

Adding both contributions:

$$\boxed{F = \dot{m} V_e + (P_e - P_a) A_e}$$

**Physical meaning of each term:**
- $\dot{m} V_e$: **Momentum thrust** — kinetic energy flux of expelled gases.
- $(P_e - P_a) A_e$: **Pressure thrust** — net pressure imbalance at exit plane. Zero at optimum altitude.

**(b) A rocket operates at sea level with the following data: mass flow rate $\dot{m} = 400$ kg/s, $V_e = 2200$ m/s, $P_e = 0.45$ bar, $P_a = 1.013$ bar, $A_e = 0.6$ m². Compute (i) the total thrust, (ii) the thrust loss due to non-optimum expansion, and (iii) the specific impulse. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $\dot{m} = 400$ kg/s
- $V_e = 2200$ m/s
- $P_e = 0.45$ bar $= 45{,}000$ Pa
- $P_a = 1.013$ bar $= 101{,}300$ Pa
- $A_e = 0.6$ m²

**(i) Total Thrust:**

$$F = \dot{m} V_e + (P_e - P_a) A_e$$

$$F = (400)(2200) + (45{,}000 - 101{,}300)(0.6)$$

$$F = 880{,}000 + (-56{,}300)(0.6)$$

$$F = 880{,}000 - 33{,}780 = 846{,}220 \text{ N}$$

**(ii) Thrust Loss (compared to optimum expansion where $P_e = P_a$):**

Optimum thrust $F_{opt} = \dot{m} V_e = 880{,}000$ N

Loss $= F_{opt} - F_{actual} = 880{,}000 - 846{,}220 = 33{,}780$ N

The loss is purely the **negative pressure thrust** because $P_e < P_a$ — the nozzle is **overexpanded** at sea level (this is expected, since sea level is below the design altitude).

**(iii) Specific Impulse:**

$$I_{sp} = \frac{V_e}{g_0} = \frac{2200}{9.80665} = 224.34 \text{ s}$$

Note: $I_{sp}$ is a **propellant property** (depends only on $V_e$), independent of altitude or $P_a$.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — INTERNAL CHOICE
`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a) Derive the Tsiolkovsky rocket equation. Discuss why staging is essential for orbital launch vehicles. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Derivation (as in Section 3.3):**
For a rocket of instantaneous mass $m$ ejecting mass at $V_e$:

$$m \frac{dV}{dt} = -V_e \frac{dm}{dt}$$

Separating variables:

$$\int_{V_0}^{V_f} dV = -V_e \int_{m_0}^{m_f} \frac{dm}{m}$$

$$\Delta V = V_e \ln\!\left(\frac{m_0}{m_f}\right)$$

**Why Staging is Essential:**

The Tsiolkovsky equation is **logarithmic** in mass ratio. To achieve orbital $\Delta V \approx 9.4$ km/s using a single stage with $V_e = 3$ km/s (typical hydrocarbon):

$$MR = e^{\Delta V / V_e} = e^{9400/3000} = e^{3.133} \approx 22.9$$

This means $m_f / m_0 = 1/22.9$, so the **propellant fraction must exceed 95%** — practically impossible with structural tanks, engines, and payload.

By **staging**, each stage operates over a smaller $\Delta V$ with a manageable mass ratio. Empty lower stages are **jettisoned** once their propellant is exhausted, removing their structural dead weight from the mass equation. This effectively **raises the achievable $\Delta V$** for the same total launch mass.

**Example:** A two-stage rocket with each stage having $V_e = 3$ km/s and $MR = 5$ per stage achieves:
$$\Delta V = 2 \times 3000 \times \ln(5) = 2 \times 3000 \times 1.609 = 9654 \text{ m/s}$$
which exceeds orbital requirement — using $MR = 5$ (80% propellant fraction) **per stage**, structurally feasible.

**(b) A three-stage rocket is to deliver 1000 kg payload to LEO. The stages have effective exhaust velocities $V_{e1} = 2800$ m/s, $V_{e2} = 3200$ m/s, $V_{e3} = 4500$ m/s. Required mission $\Delta V = 9400$ m/s (losses ignored). If each stage achieves equal $\Delta V$ contribution, determine the mass ratio required for each stage. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Equal $\Delta V$ contribution: $\Delta V_i = 9400/3 = 3133.33$ m/s per stage.

**Stage 1 (booster):**
$$MR_1 = \exp(\Delta V_1 / V_{e1}) = \exp(3133.33 / 2800) = \exp(1.119) = 3.062$$

**Stage 2:**
$$MR_2 = \exp(3133.33 / 3200) = \exp(0.9792) = 2.663$$

**Stage 3 (upper):**
$$MR_3 = \exp(3133.33 / 4500) = \exp(0.6963) = 2.006$$

**Interpretation:**
- Stage 1 needs the highest mass ratio (3.062) because it has the lowest $V_e$.
- Stage 3 needs only 2.006 because cryogenic LH₂/LOX provides much higher $V_e$.
- Total launch mass ratio $MR_{total} = 3.062 \times 2.663 \times 2.006 \approx 16.36$ — this is feasible with modern staging.

> [!NOTE]
> In practice, **losses** (gravity drag ~1500 m/s, aerodynamic drag ~200 m/s, steering losses ~100 m/s) add ~1800 m/s to the ideal $\Delta V$, so the *actual* required $\Delta V$ is ~11.2 km/s, requiring somewhat higher mass ratios.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — High-Density Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Fire Memory Bank for Last-Minute KTU Revision**

- ✅ Rocket = **self-contained** propulsion; carries oxidizer onboard.
- ✅ Operating principle = **Newton's 3rd Law** (momentum conservation).
- ✅ **Universal Thrust Equation:** $F = \dot{m} V_e + (P_e - P_a) A_e$.
- ✅ **Optimum expansion** condition: $P_e = P_a$ → only momentum thrust acts.
- ✅ **Vacuum thrust:** $F_{vac} = \dot{m} V_e + P_e A_e$ (since $P_a = 0$).
- ✅ **Underexpanded** at low altitude (loss of pressure thrust, $P_e > P_a$).
- ✅ **Overexpanded** at high altitude (negative pressure thrust, $P_e < P_a$).
- ✅ **Specific impulse** $I_{sp} = V_e / g_0$ — measured in **seconds**.
- ✅ **Tsiolkovsky equation:** $\Delta V = V_e \ln(m_0/m_f)$ — logarithmic in mass ratio.
- ✅ **Mass ratio** $MR = m_0/m_f > 1$.
- ✅ **Propellant mass fraction** $\zeta = 1 - 1/MR$.
- ✅ **Thrust coefficient** $C_F = F / (P_c A_t)$.
- ✅ **Characteristic velocity** $c^* = P_c A_t / \dot{m}$ — measures combustion efficiency.
- ✅ **Thrust-to-weight ratio** $T/W > 1$ required for vertical liftoff.
- ✅ **Staging** is essential because $\Delta V$ demand for orbit exceeds single-stage feasibility.
- ✅ Liquid H₂ + LOX gives **highest chemical $I_{sp}$** (~450 s); solid AP/HTPB gives ~250–280 s.
- ✅ The **C-D nozzle** (De Laval) is mandatory for supersonic exhaust acceleration.
- ✅ Always **state expansion condition** and **show units** in KTU derivations.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Optimum Expansion Ratio
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Optimum Expansion Ratio — Core Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> **Optimum Expansion Ratio** in a jet-propulsion nozzle is defined as the *ratio of nozzle exit area to throat area* (or equivalently the *ratio of chamber pressure to exit pressure*) at which the propelling nozzle is **ideally expanded**, meaning the static pressure at the nozzle exit plane equals the ambient (static) pressure of the surrounding atmosphere.

For a convergent–divergent (C-D) propelling nozzle, the expansion ratio is expressed as:

$$\varepsilon_{opt} \;=\; \frac{A_e}{A_t} \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad \frac{p_7}{p_e}$$

where:
- $A_e$ = nozzle exit area
- $A_t$ = nozzle throat area
- $p_7$ = combustion chamber (nozzle inlet) total/stagnation pressure
- $p_e$ = nozzle exit static pressure
- $\gamma$ = ratio of specific heats for combustion gases (≈ **1.33** for jet engines)
- $R$ = specific gas constant for combustion products (**287 J/kg·K** nominal)

When $\varepsilon$ is chosen such that $p_e = p_a$ (ambient pressure), the nozzle is said to be in its **ideally expanded** or **optimum** condition for that particular flight altitude.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Balloon Inside a Box" Model

Imagine you are holding a fully inflated balloon inside a closed cardboard box:
- If you pop the balloon with the lid **screwed shut** (small exit hole), the air rushes out at high velocity but at a pressure greater than the box interior — much of the thrust "pushes" uselessly against the closed lid. This is **underexpansion** ($p_e > p_a$).
- If you **cut a perfectly-sized hole** in the box that lets the air out smoothly at exactly the box's internal pressure, you get the loudest, cleanest "whoosh." This is **optimum (ideal) expansion** ($p_e = p_a$).
- If you **remove the entire box front** (huge opening), the balloon just sighs out — the air exits at a pressure lower than ambient and the surrounding air even pushes *back* on the exit plane, reducing net thrust. This is **overexpansion** ($p_e < p_a$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU 2024 Module-4 outcomes require the student to identify the *condition* under which a propelling nozzle delivers **maximum specific thrust** and **maximum propulsive efficiency**, and to derive the corresponding area-ratio expression.

---

## 1.3 The Three Nozzle Expansion Regimes

| Regime | Condition | Net Pressure Thrust Term | Thrust vs. Altitude |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Underexpanded** | $p_e > p_a$ | Positive, but exit kinetic energy is reduced | Below design altitude |
| **Ideally Expanded (Optimum)** | $p_e = p_a$ | Zero — maximum exit kinetic energy achieved | At design altitude |
| **Overexpanded** | $p_e < p_a$ | Negative (drag penalty) | Above design altitude |

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical constant for exam:** $\gamma_{gas} \approx 1.33$ (typical for jet-A1 combustion products in the nozzle). For pure cold-air calcs use **$\gamma = 1.40$**.

---

## 1.4 Why "Optimum" Matters in Real Engineering

- A supersonic fighter (e.g., MiG-21) cruising at Mach 2 at 12 km altitude needs a *different* optimum area ratio than a civil turbofan at Mach 0.85 at 10 km — because $p_a$ and $T_a$ change with altitude.
- Variable-geometry (convergent–divergent) nozzles on military jets mechanically re-position the after-nozzle petals to maintain $p_e \approx p_a$ across the entire flight envelope — this is the *practical* pursuit of the **optimum expansion ratio**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Conceptual Decomposition — The "Why" Behind Optimum Expansion

The general thrust equation for a jet engine operating in still ambient air is:

$$F \;=\; \dot{m}\,u_e \;+\; A_e\,(p_e - p_a)$$

where $\dot{m}$ is the propellant mass flow rate and $u_e$ is the absolute exit gas velocity.

**Decomposition of thrust contributions:**
1. **Momentum thrust:** $\dot{m}\,u_e$ — created by accelerating the combustion gases rearward.
2. **Pressure thrust:** $A_e(p_e - p_a)$ — the net pressure force acting on the closed control-volume surface at the exit plane.

**Logical sequence for the optimum condition:**
- For a *fixed* combustion-chamber condition ($p_7, T_7$), as we progressively increase the nozzle expansion ratio $\varepsilon = A_e/A_t$:
  - $p_e$ falls monotonically, so $u_e$ **increases** (favourable).
  - But simultaneously the pressure-thrust term $A_e(p_e-p_a)$ **decreases** and eventually turns negative.
- The **scalar thrust $F$ is maximised** when the *gain* in momentum thrust exactly matches the *loss* in pressure thrust — i.e., when the derivative $\dfrac{dF}{d\varepsilon} = 0$.
- Algebraically, this derivative vanishes precisely when $p_e = p_a$.

---

## 2.2 Isentropic Relations Governing the Nozzle

For an isentropic expansion from chamber conditions $(p_7, T_7)$ to exit $(p_e, T_e)$:

$$\frac{T_e}{T_7} \;=\; \left(\frac{p_e}{p_7}\right)^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}}$$

$$\frac{\rho_e}{\rho_7} \;=\; \left(\frac{p_e}{p_7}\right)^{\frac{1}{\gamma}}$$

Exit velocity from the steady-flow energy equation:

$$u_e \;=\; \sqrt{2\,c_p\,T_7\!\left[\,1 - \left(\frac{p_e}{p_7}\right)^{\!\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}}\,\right]}$$

Exit density:

$$\rho_e \;=\; \frac{p_7}{R\,T_7}\left(\frac{p_e}{p_7}\right)^{\!\frac{1}{\gamma}}$$

Required exit area for a given mass flow:

$$A_e \;=\; \frac{\dot{m}}{\rho_e\,u_e}$$

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Governing Equation | Notes / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Specific heat ratio (comb. gas) | $\gamma \approx 1.33$ | **dimensionless** |
| 2 | Specific heat at const. $p$ | $c_p = \dfrac{\gamma R}{\gamma - 1}$ | **J/kg·K** |
| 3 | Critical (choking) pressure ratio | $\dfrac{p_7}{p_c} = \left(\dfrac{\gamma+1}{2}\right)^{\!\frac{\gamma}{\gamma-1}}$ | $p_c$ = throat-static pressure |
| 4 | Exit velocity (isentropic) | $u_e = \sqrt{2c_p T_7\!\left[1 - (p_e/p_7)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}\right]}$ | **m/s** |
| 5 | Exit area for $\dot m$ | $A_e = \dfrac{\dot m}{\rho_e\,u_e}$ | **m²** |
| 6 | Expansion ratio (area form) | $\dfrac{A_e}{A_t} = \dfrac{1}{M_e}\!\left[\dfrac{2}{\gamma+1}\!\left(1+\tfrac{\gamma-1}{2}M_e^2\right)\right]^{\!\frac{\gamma+1}{2(\gamma-1)}}$ | Choked C-D nozzle |
| 7 | General thrust equation | $F = \dot m u_e + A_e(p_e - p_a)$ | **N** |
| 8 | Specific impulse | $I_{sp} = F/\dot m\,g$ | **seconds** |
| 9 | Propulsive efficiency | $\eta_p = \dfrac{2 u_a / u_e}{1 + (u_a/u_e)^2}$ (ideal, $p_e = p_a$) | **dimensionless** |
| 10 | Thermal efficiency | $\eta_{th} = \dfrac{u_e^2/2}{\dot Q_R}$ | **dimensionless** |
| 11 | Overall efficiency | $\eta_0 = \eta_{th}\,\eta_p$ | **dimensionless** |
| 12 | **Optimum Expansion Condition** | $\boxed{p_e = p_a}$ | For max thrust at given $p_7, T_7$ |
| 13 | Optimum pressure ratio across nozzle | $\boxed{p_7/p_e = p_7/p_a}$ | At the design altitude |
| 14 | Optimum expansion area ratio | $\varepsilon_{opt} = A_e/A_t$ evaluated at $p_e = p_a$ | **dimensionless** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In KTU board valuation, examiners **deduct marks** if you write the thrust equation as $F = \dot m u_e$ *without* the pressure-thrust term. Always include $A_e(p_e - p_a)$ explicitly.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Concorde** at Mach 2 cruise used a *variable-geometry* nozzle that continuously adjusted $A_e/A_t$ to track the optimum as ambient pressure dropped with altitude.
- **Space-Shuttle Main Engines (SSME)** operate in a deep *overexpanded* regime at sea level but transition to ideally-expanded at altitude — informing optimum expansion ratio sizing.
- **Civil turbofans** (e.g., GE90, Trent 900) use a *fixed* optimum expansion ratio tuned for the cruise Mach–altitude block of the airliner.

# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Optimum Expansion Ratio for Maximum Specific Thrust

### Given
- Combustion-chamber stagnation conditions: $p_7, T_7$ (held fixed)
- Ambient static pressure: $p_a$ (given by altitude)
- Nozzle is choked at the throat (always true for a designed C-D nozzle in jet propulsion)

### Step 1 — Write thrust per unit mass flow (specific thrust)

Define specific thrust $F_s = F/\dot m$:

$$F_s \;=\; u_e \;+\; \frac{A_e}{\dot m}\,(p_e - p_a)$$

### Step 2 — Substitute $\dfrac{A_e}{\dot m} = \dfrac{1}{\rho_e u_e}$

$$F_s \;=\; u_e \;+\; \frac{p_e - p_a}{\rho_e\,u_e}$$

### Step 3 — Express $u_e$ and $\rho_e$ in terms of $\beta \equiv p_e/p_7$

Let $\beta \equiv p_e/p_7$ (so $0 < \beta \le 1$).

From the isentropic relations:

$$u_e \;=\; \sqrt{2c_p T_7\!\left[1 - \beta^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}\right]}$$

$$\rho_e \;=\; \frac{p_7}{R T_7}\,\beta^{1/\gamma}$$

### Step 4 — Substitute into $F_s$ and differentiate

$$F_s(\beta) \;=\; \sqrt{2c_p T_7\!\left[1 - \beta^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}\right]} \;+\; \frac{R T_7}{p_7\,u_e}\!\left(\beta p_7 - p_a\right)$$

$$F_s(\beta) \;=\; u_e(\beta) \;+\; \frac{R T_7}{u_e(\beta)}\!\left(\beta - \frac{p_a}{p_7}\right)$$

Setting $\dfrac{dF_s}{d\beta} = 0$ and applying product-rule calculus on the two terms (both depend on $\beta$ through $u_e$ and the linear $\beta$ term) yields the first-order optimality condition:

$$\boxed{\;\beta_{opt} \;=\; \frac{p_e}{p_7} \;=\; \frac{p_a}{p_7}\;}$$

### Step 5 — Physical interpretation

$$\boxed{\;p_e \;=\; p_a\;}$$

**Conclusion:** The optimum expansion ratio for maximum specific thrust is obtained when the **exit static pressure equals the ambient static pressure**. The corresponding optimum *area* ratio is then computed by substituting $\beta_{opt} = p_a/p_7$ into the C-D isentropic area-Mach relation.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Optimum Area Ratio $\varepsilon_{opt}$ for the C-D Nozzle

### Step 1 — Critical condition at the throat

For a choked throat ($M_t = 1$):

$$\frac{p_t}{p_7} \;=\; \left(\frac{2}{\gamma+1}\right)^{\!\frac{\gamma}{\gamma-1}}$$

### Step 2 — Isentropic area-Mach relation

$$\frac{A_e}{A_t} \;=\; \frac{1}{M_e}\!\left[\frac{2}{\gamma+1}\!\left(1+\tfrac{\gamma-1}{2}M_e^2\right)\right]^{\!\frac{\gamma+1}{2(\gamma-1)}}$$

### Step 3 — Express exit Mach number in terms of $\beta = p_e/p_7$

From the energy equation:

$$\frac{T_7}{T_e} \;=\; 1 + \frac{\gamma - 1}{2}M_e^2 \quad\text{and}\quad \frac{T_7}{T_e} = \beta^{-(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$$

Equating:

$$1 + \frac{\gamma - 1}{2}M_e^2 \;=\; \beta^{-(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$$

$$M_e \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{2}{\gamma-1}\!\left[\beta^{-(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - 1\right]}$$

### Step 4 — Optimum area ratio

Plug $M_e$ evaluated at $\beta_{opt} = p_a/p_7$ into the area ratio:

$$\boxed{\;\varepsilon_{opt} \;=\; \frac{A_e}{A_t}\bigg|_{\,p_e\,=\,p_a} \;=\; \frac{1}{M_{e,opt}}\!\left[\frac{2}{\gamma+1}\!\left(1+\tfrac{\gamma-1}{2}M_{e,opt}^2\right)\right]^{\!\frac{\gamma+1}{2(\gamma-1)}}\;}$$

with

$$M_{e,opt} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{2}{\gamma-1}\!\left[\left(\frac{p_7}{p_a}\right)^{\!\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} - 1\right]}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Optimum Expansion Ratio for Maximum Propulsive Efficiency

### Step 1 — Ideal-cycle propulsive efficiency (no pressure thrust loss, $p_e = p_a$)

$$\eta_p \;=\; \frac{F\,u_a}{\tfrac{1}{2}\dot m\,u_e^2} \;=\; \frac{(\dot m\,u_e) u_a}{\tfrac{1}{2}\dot m\,u_e^2} \;=\; \frac{2 u_a}{u_e}$$

This *ideal* expression assumes pure momentum thrust.

### Step 2 — Real-cycle propulsive efficiency (including pressure thrust)

$$\eta_p \;=\; \frac{F\,u_a}{\tfrac{1}{2}\dot m\left[u_e^2 + \tfrac{2\,A_e}{\dot m}(p_e - p_a)\,u_e\right]} \;=\; \frac{2\,u_a}{u_e + \dfrac{2A_e(p_e - p_a)}{\dot m}}$$

### Step 3 — For an *overexpanded* nozzle with $p_e < p_a$, the pressure term is negative

$$\eta_p \;=\; \frac{2\,u_a}{u_e - \dfrac{2A_e(p_a - p_e)}{\dot m}}$$

This shows propulsive efficiency *increases* as the negative pressure thrust term grows — i.e., higher expansion ratios at altitude *boost* $\eta_p$ even though specific thrust drops slightly.

### Step 4 — The practical compromise

Define the **equivalent exhaust velocity** $c_e$:

$$c_e \;=\; u_e + \frac{A_e(p_e - p_a)}{\dot m}$$

Then $\eta_p = \dfrac{2 u_a/c_e}{1 + (u_a/c_e)^2}$ which is maximised when $c_e = u_a$ — but this is impractical. In real design, $\varepsilon_{opt}$ is chosen so that $c_e$ falls in the range **1.6 $u_a$ to 2.2 $u_a$**, depending on mission.

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** The board examiners at KTU frequently ask the student to *derive the optimum condition* and then *comment on whether $\eta_p$ and $F$ can be simultaneously maximised*. The standard answer is: **No.** A single $\varepsilon$ cannot simultaneously maximise thrust and efficiency — designers choose based on mission (fighter ⇒ max thrust; airliner ⇒ max $\eta_p$).

---

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix

| Criterion | Optimum Exit Condition | Altitude Behaviour | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| Max specific thrust | $p_e = p_a$ | Strictly altitude-specific | Military fighter |
| Max propulsive efficiency | $c_e \to u_a$ (impractical) | Favours very low $p_a$ | High-altitude cruise |
| Overall mission optimum | $p_e = p_a$ at cruise altitude | Designed for one altitude | Civil turbofan |
| Specific impulse $I_{sp}$ max | $p_e = p_a$ | Same as thrust optimum | Rockets at design altitude |

# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Nozzle Expansion Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Chamber Conditions p7 T7] --> B{Choked at Throat?}
    B -- No --> C[Subsonic Nozzle Only]
    B -- Yes --> D[Exit Mach Me via Isentropic Relation]
    D --> E[Compute Required Area Ratio Ae/At]
    E --> F{Compare pe with pa}
    F -- pe greater than pa --> G[Underexpanded: Exit Pressure Thrust Positive]
    F -- pe equals pa --> H[IDEALLY EXPANDED: Optimum for Max Thrust]
    F -- pe less than pa --> I[Overexpanded: Pressure Thrust Negative]
    G --> J[Increase Area Ratio: Variable Geometry Nozzle]
    I --> K[Decrease Area Ratio or Accept Loss]
    H --> L[Maximum Specific Thrust Achieved]
    J --> M[New Iteration: Recompute pe]
    K --> M
    M --> F
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading guide:** The diagram maps the closed-loop decision a variable-geometry nozzle controller follows every flight-second. The dashed-cycle back-edge `(M → F)` shows the iterative re-evaluation as flight Mach and altitude change.

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Jet-Propulsion Cycle Highlighting the Nozzle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A0[Atmosphere Inlet Diffuser] --> A1[Compressor]
    A1 --> A2[Combustion Chamber: p7 T7]
    A2 --> A3[Turbine]
    A3 --> A4[Propelling Nozzle]
    A4 -- pe, ue, Ae --> A5[Exhaust Jet to Atmosphere pa]
    A5 -.pressure mismatch.-> A6{(pe vs pa) Decision}
    A6 -- pe greater pa --> A7[Underexpansion Loss]
    A6 -- pe equals pa --> A8[Optimum Expansion Ratio Achieved]
    A6 -- pe less pa --> A9[Overexpansion Loss]
```

---

## 4.3 Schematic Block — Three Expansion Regimes Compared

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R1[Regime 1: Underexpansion] --> R1A[pe greater than pa]
    R1A --> R1B[Exit jet diverges outward as it leaves nozzle]
    R1B --> R1C[Some pressure energy wasted: not fully converted to kinetic energy]

    R2[Regime 2: Ideal or Optimum] --> R2A[pe equals pa]
    R2A --> R2B[Exit jet leaves smoothly parallel to axis]
    R2B --> R2C[Maximum specific thrust for given p7 T7]

    R3[Regime 3: Overexpansion] --> R3A[pe less than pa]
    R3A --> R3B[Exit jet contracts inward just outside nozzle]
    R3B --> R3C[Ambient air pushes back on exit plane: negative pressure thrust]
```

---

## 4.4 Conceptual Block — How Optimum Expansion Ratio Varies with Flight Regime

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    FL1[Takeoff: pa high, ua near zero] --> FLV1[Small optimum area ratio required]
    FL2[Subsonic Cruise: pa moderate, ua moderate] --> FLV2[Medium optimum area ratio]
    FL3[Supersonic Dash: pa low, ua high] --> FLV3[Large optimum area ratio]
    FLV1 --> NOZ[Variable Geometry Propelling Nozzle]
    FLV2 --> NOZ
    FLV3 --> NOZ
    NOZ --> OUT[Continuously Tracks pe equals pa]
```
