# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]`
**Define the term "optimum expansion ratio" of a jet-engine propelling nozzle and state the condition under which it occurs.**

**Model Answer (≈ 3 marks):**
The *optimum expansion ratio* of a jet-engine propelling nozzle is the ratio of exit area to throat area (or equivalently the chamber-to-exit pressure ratio) at which the nozzle is **ideally expanded** — i.e., the static pressure of the exhaust gases at the nozzle exit plane exactly equals the static pressure of the surrounding ambient atmosphere. The condition is $p_e = p_a$. At this condition the propelling nozzle delivers **maximum specific thrust** for the given combustion-chamber conditions $(p_7, T_7)$.

*(1 mark — definition, 1 mark — area-ratio statement, 1 mark — optimum condition)*

---

### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary Exam]`
**Distinguish between underexpansion and overexpansion of a propelling nozzle, with the help of the general thrust equation.**

**Model Answer (≈ 3 marks):**
The general thrust equation is $F = \dot m u_e + A_e(p_e - p_a)$.
- **Underexpansion:** $p_e > p_a$. The pressure-thrust term $A_e(p_e - p_a)$ is positive but the exit velocity $u_e$ is lower than its isentropic maximum; not all available pressure energy has been converted to kinetic energy. Occurs at altitudes *below* the design altitude.
- **Overexpansion:** $p_e < p_a$. The pressure-thrust term is **negative**, acting as a drag on the engine. Occurs at altitudes *above* the design altitude.
The optimum is the boundary between the two: $p_e = p_a$.

*(1 mark — equation, 1 mark — underexpansion, 1 mark — overexpansion)*

---

## Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]`

**(a)** *Starting from the general thrust equation of a turbojet engine, derive the condition for maximum specific thrust and show that this condition corresponds to the nozzle being ideally expanded. State any assumptions.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *A turbojet engine operates at a flight altitude of 10 km where the ambient pressure is 26.5 kPa. The combustion-chamber (nozzle inlet) stagnation conditions are $p_7 = 120$ kPa and $T_7 = 1200$ K. Assuming $\gamma = 1.33$, $R = 287$ J/kg·K and $c_p = 1148$ J/kg·K, calculate:* **(7 marks)**
  - (i) The optimum pressure ratio across the nozzle.
  - (ii) The exit Mach number at optimum conditions.
  - (iii) The optimum expansion area ratio $A_e/A_t$.

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Assumptions:**
- Combustion-chamber conditions $p_7, T_7$ are fixed.
- Nozzle is choked at the throat ($M_t = 1$).
- Flow through the nozzle is isentropic.
- Neglect fuel addition in the nozzle (handled in the chamber).

**Step 1:** Write the specific thrust:

$$F_s \;=\; u_e \;+\; \frac{p_e - p_a}{\rho_e u_e}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute $\beta = p_e/p_7$ and the isentropic relations for $u_e$ and $\rho_e$:

$$F_s(\beta) \;=\; \sqrt{2c_p T_7\!\left[1 - \beta^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}\right]} \;+\; \frac{R T_7}{u_e(\beta)}\!\left(\beta - \frac{p_a}{p_7}\right)$$

**Step 3:** Differentiate with respect to $\beta$ and set to zero:

$$\frac{dF_s}{d\beta} \;=\; 0 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; p_e \;=\; p_a$$

**Step 4:** Since $p_e = p_a$ is the very definition of an ideally-expanded nozzle, the optimum specific thrust coincides with ideal expansion.

*(Mark split: 2 marks — assumptions & equation; 3 marks — derivation; 2 marks — conclusion & interpretation)*

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**(i) Optimum pressure ratio across the nozzle:**

$$\boxed{\;\frac{p_7}{p_e}\bigg|_{opt} \;=\; \frac{p_7}{p_a} \;=\; \frac{120}{26.5} \;=\; 4.528\;}$$

**(ii) Exit Mach number at optimum conditions:**

From the isentropic relation:

$$M_e \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{2}{\gamma-1}\!\left[\left(\frac{p_7}{p_a}\right)^{\!\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} - 1\right]}$$

Compute the exponent and base:

$$\left(\frac{p_7}{p_a}\right)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} \;=\; (4.528)^{0.33/1.33} \;=\; (4.528)^{0.2481}$$

$$\ln(4.528) = 1.5105 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 0.2481 \times 1.5105 = 0.3747 \quad \Rightarrow \quad e^{0.3747} = 1.4545$$

Therefore:

$$M_e \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{2}{0.33}\,(1.4545 - 1)} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{2}{0.33}\times 0.4545} \;=\; \sqrt{2.7545} \;=\; 1.660$$

$$\boxed{\;M_{e,opt} \approx 1.66\;}$$

**(iii) Optimum expansion area ratio $A_e/A_t$:**

Use the isentropic area-Mach relation:

$$\frac{A_e}{A_t} \;=\; \frac{1}{M_e}\!\left[\frac{2}{\gamma+1}\!\left(1+\tfrac{\gamma-1}{2}M_e^2\right)\right]^{\!\frac{\gamma+1}{2(\gamma-1)}}$$

Plug in $\gamma = 1.33$, $M_e = 1.66$:

$$\frac{\gamma-1}{2}M_e^2 \;=\; \frac{0.33}{2}\times (1.66)^2 \;=\; 0.165 \times 2.7556 \;=\; 0.4547$$

$$1 + 0.4547 \;=\; 1.4547$$

$$\frac{2}{\gamma+1} \;=\; \frac{2}{2.33} \;=\; 0.8584$$

$$0.8584 \times 1.4547 \;=\; 1.2487$$

$$\frac{\gamma+1}{2(\gamma-1)} \;=\; \frac{2.33}{0.66} \;=\; 3.5303$$

$$(1.2487)^{3.5303} \;=\; e^{3.5303 \times \ln(1.2487)} \;=\; e^{3.5303 \times 0.2223} \;=\; e^{0.7848} \;=\; 2.192$$

$$\frac{A_e}{A_t} \;=\; \frac{2.192}{1.66} \;=\; 1.321$$

$$\boxed{\;\varepsilon_{opt} \;=\; \frac{A_e}{A_t} \;\approx\; 1.32\;}$$

*(Mark split for part (b): (i) 2 marks, (ii) 2 marks, (iii) 3 marks)*

---

### Question B — `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**(a)** *With the help of neat sketches, describe the three regimes of nozzle expansion in a turbojet engine and indicate the corresponding altitude behaviour.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *Derive an expression for the optimum expansion ratio of a propelling nozzle that maximises propulsive efficiency. Discuss why this optimum differs from the optimum for maximum thrust.* **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Three regimes of nozzle expansion:**

**1. Underexpansion ($p_e > p_a$):**
The static pressure at the nozzle exit is greater than the surrounding ambient pressure. Outside the nozzle, the exhaust jet **diverges** (expansion fans emanate from the lip). Some of the available pressure energy remains unconverted to kinetic energy, and the engine delivers less thrust than theoretically possible.

**Altitude behaviour:** Occurs at altitudes **lower than** the design altitude (or equivalently at $p_a$ higher than the design value). For a fixed-area nozzle, take-off and low-altitude cruise are typical underexpanded scenarios.

**2. Ideally Expanded / Optimum ($p_e = p_a$):**
The static pressure at the exit plane equals ambient. The exhaust jet leaves the nozzle **parallel** to the engine axis. Pressure thrust is zero, but all the pressure energy has been converted to kinetic energy. Maximum specific thrust for the chamber conditions.

**Altitude behaviour:** Occurs **at the design altitude** of the nozzle — the single altitude for which the fixed-area nozzle was sized.

**3. Overexpansion ($p_e < p_a$):**
The exit pressure is below ambient. Outside the nozzle the jet **contracts** (oblique shocks form in the exhaust plume). The negative $A_e(p_e - p_a)$ term acts as a thrust-reducing drag.

**Altitude behaviour:** Occurs at altitudes **higher than** design — typical for high-altitude supersonic cruise.

*(Mark split: 2 marks — sketch description, 1 mark — equation linkage, 1 mark — altitude mapping, 2 marks — thrust implications, 1 mark — engineering example)*

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1 — Define propulsive efficiency:**

$$\eta_p \;=\; \frac{\text{Thrust Power}}{\text{Kinetic Energy Added to Flow}} \;=\; \frac{F u_a}{\tfrac{1}{2}\dot m\left[u_e^2 + \tfrac{2A_e(p_e - p_a)}{\dot m} u_e\right]}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $F = \dot m u_e + A_e(p_e - p_a)$:**

$$\eta_p \;=\; \frac{2 u_a\!\left[u_e + \tfrac{A_e(p_e - p_a)}{\dot m}\right]}{u_e^2 + \tfrac{2A_e(p_e - p_a)}{\dot m}\,u_e} \;=\; \frac{2 u_a\,c_e}{c_e^2 + u_a^2}$$

where the equivalent exhaust velocity is:

$$c_e \;=\; u_e + \frac{A_e(p_e - p_a)}{\dot m}$$

**Step 3 — Differentiate $\eta_p$ with respect to $c_e$ and set to zero:**

$$\frac{d\eta_p}{dc_e} \;=\; \frac{2 u_a (c_e^2 + u_a^2) - 2 u_a c_e (2 c_e)}{(c_e^2 + u_a^2)^2} \;=\; 0$$

$$c_e^2 + u_a^2 - 2 c_e^2 \;=\; 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad c_e \;=\; u_a$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation:**

The propulsive efficiency is maximised when the *equivalent* exhaust velocity equals the flight velocity: $c_e = u_a$.

**Discussion on why this optimum differs from maximum thrust:**
- Maximum **thrust** is obtained when $p_e = p_a$ (no pressure thrust loss).
- Maximum **propulsive efficiency** requires $c_e \approx u_a$, which in practice requires a *high* expansion ratio at high altitude (where $p_a$ is low and $u_a$ is large).
- A single fixed area ratio cannot satisfy both conditions across the flight envelope, hence the use of **variable-geometry nozzles** on military engines and the cruise-tuned fixed nozzles on civil engines.

*(Mark split: 3 marks — derivation of $\eta_p$, 2 marks — differentiation, 2 marks — discussion)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Most Commonly Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Omitting the pressure-thrust term** $A_e(p_e - p_a)$ in the thrust equation. KTU examiners *consistently* deduct 2 marks for this in Module-4 questions.
> 2. **Confusing $p_7$ (chamber stagnation) with $p_c$ (throat static).** Use $p_7/p_c = \left(\frac{\gamma+1}{2}\right)^{\gamma/(\gamma-1)}$ correctly.
> 3. **Wrong value of $\gamma$:** Use $\gamma = 1.33$ for combustion gases, NOT $1.40$ (which is for cold air). Civil Module-4 questions *often* specify $\gamma = 1.4$ explicitly — read carefully.
> 4. **Mis-stating units** of specific impulse as "m/s" instead of "seconds" ($I_{sp} = F/\dot m g$).
> 5. **Skipping the altitude behaviour discussion** in 7-mark sketches — examiners allocate 1–2 marks specifically for altitude correlation.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Optimum expansion ratio** = exit-area/throat-area setting at which **$p_e = p_a$**.
- **Maximum specific thrust condition:** $p_e = p_a$. No pressure-thrust contribution, but all pressure energy is converted to kinetic energy.
- **Maximum $\eta_p$ condition:** Equivalent exhaust velocity $c_e \approx u_a$. (Impractical in real engines — only achievable at infinitely high expansion ratios.)
- **General thrust:** $F = \dot m u_e + A_e(p_e - p_a)$. **Never** write $F = \dot m u_e$ alone.
- **Three regimes:**
  - Underexpansion: $p_e > p_a$ → altitudes *below* design.
  - Ideal: $p_e = p_a$ → at *design* altitude.
  - Overexpansion: $p_e < p_a$ → altitudes *above* design.
- **Isentropic area-Mach relation** is the bridge between area-ratio and pressure-ratio forms.
- **Choking condition:** Throat reaches $M_t = 1$ when $p_7/p_a \ge \left(\dfrac{\gamma+1}{2}\right)^{\gamma/(\gamma-1)} \approx 1.85$ for $\gamma = 1.4$.
- **Variable-geometry nozzles** on military engines continuously re-trim $\varepsilon$ to track $p_e = p_a$ across the flight envelope.
- **Design compromise:** Military engines bias toward max thrust (lower altitude optimum); civil turbofans bias toward max $\eta_p$ (higher altitude optimum).
- **Key exam constants to memorise:** $\gamma_{gas} \approx 1.33$, $R = 287$ J/kg·K, $c_p = 1148$ J/kg·K.
- **Critical formula to reproduce from memory:** $M_{e,opt} = \sqrt{\frac{2}{\gamma-1}\!\left[(p_7/p_a)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - 1\right]}$.
- **KTU-favourite trick question:** "Can a single expansion ratio simultaneously maximise thrust and propulsive efficiency?" **Answer:** No — they are conflicting objectives.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Solid Propellant Rocket and Liquid Propellant Rocket
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET741 - GAS TURBINE AND JET PROPULSION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Solid Propellant Rocket and Liquid Propellant Rocket

# 🚀 SOLID PROPELLANT ROCKET AND LIQUID PROPELLANT ROCKET

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 ROCKET PROPULSION — FUNDAMENTAL CONCEPT

A **rocket** is a self-contained propulsion system that carries **both the fuel and the oxidizer onboard**, allowing it to operate in the vacuum of space — unlike air-breathing engines (jet engines, gas turbines) which require atmospheric oxygen. The rocket ejects high-velocity exhaust gases rearward to generate thrust, in strict accordance with **Newton's Third Law** (every action has an equal and opposite reaction).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:**
> A rocket is a reaction engine in which the propellant (fuel + oxidizer) is carried entirely within the vehicle. The high-pressure, high-temperature gases produced in the combustion chamber are accelerated through a convergent-divergent (de Laval) nozzle to produce thrust.

**Rocket Thrust Equation (Ideal):**

$$F = \dot{m} V_e + (P_e - P_a) A_e$$

Where:
- $F$ = Thrust (N)
- $\dot{m}$ = Mass flow rate of propellant (kg/s)
- $V_e$ = Effective exhaust velocity (m/s)
- $P_e$ = Nozzle exit pressure (Pa)
- $P_a$ = Ambient pressure (Pa)
- $A_e$ = Nozzle exit area (m²)

### 1.2 INTUITIVE OVERVIEW — THE "BALLOON ANALOGY"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy (Balloon Rocket):**
> Imagine you inflate a balloon and release it without tying the opening. Air rushes out backwards (action), and the balloon shoots forwards (reaction). A rocket works on the same principle — except instead of air leaking out, we deliberately burn propellant at extremely high temperatures (3000+ K) and accelerate the gas through a precision-shaped nozzle to exhaust velocities of **2000–4500 m/s**.

The two principal classifications of chemical rockets based on the **physical state of the propellant** are:
1. **Solid Propellant Rockets (SPR)** — propellant is a solid "grain"
2. **Liquid Propellant Rockets (LPR)** — fuel and oxidizer are stored as liquids in separate tanks

---

### 1.3 SOLID PROPELLANT ROCKET (SPR) — DEFINITION

A **solid propellant rocket** (also called a *solid rocket motor*, SRM) is a rocket engine that uses a **solid mixture of fuel and oxidizer** (called the *propellant grain*) packed inside the combustion chamber. Once ignited, the grain burns from the inside out (or end-to-end depending on geometry) until fully consumed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Insight:**
> The propellant in an SPR serves a **dual purpose**: it is BOTH the energy source (fuel) AND the structural mass of the combustion chamber itself (until it burns away). This is a defining feature that distinguishes it from liquid rockets.

**Key Characteristics of Solid Propellants:**

| Property | Value/Range |
|---|---|
| Density | **1500–1800 kg/m³** |
| Specific Impulse ($I_{sp}$) | **200–300 s** |
| Flame Temperature | **2500–3800 K** |
| Exhaust Velocity | **2000–2800 m/s** |
| Storage Life | **Years to decades** |
| Throttleability | **Not possible** (burn once, fixed profile) |
| Restarts | **Not possible** |

**Two Main Classes of Solid Propellants:**

> **1. Homogeneous (Double-Base) Propellants:**
> - Nitrocellulose + Nitroglycerin dissolved together
> - Examples: Ballistite, Cordite
> - Used in small tactical missiles

> **2. Heterogeneous (Composite) Propellants:**
> - Solid oxidizer (e.g., Ammonium Perchlorate, AP) + Fuel binder (e.g., HTPB - Hydroxyl-Terminated Polybutadiene) + powdered metal (Aluminum)
> - Used in large boosters (Space Shuttle SRBs, PSLV, GSLV S9 strap-ons)

**Components of a Solid Propellant Rocket Motor:**

> [!NOTE]
> 1. **Motor Casing** — Steel/composite vessel that holds the grain and contains combustion pressure
> 2. **Propellant Grain** — The actual solid fuel-oxidizer mixture, geometrically shaped for desired burn profile
> 3. **Igniter** — Pyrotechnic device to initiate combustion
> 4. **Nozzle** — Convergent-divergent de Laval nozzle for gas acceleration
> 5. **Insulation Liner** — Thermal protection between grain and casing
> 6. **Liner/Case Bond** — Adhesive layer bonding grain to casing

**Advantages of SPR:**
- ✅ Simple design (no moving parts)
- ✅ Storable for long durations (ready to launch instantly)
- ✅ High reliability (>99%)
- ✅ Lower cost
- ✅ Higher thrust-to-weight ratio

**Disadvantages of SPR:**
- ❌ Cannot be throttled
- ❌ Cannot be stopped or restarted once ignited
- ❌ Lower specific impulse than liquid engines
- ❌ Grain cracks may cause catastrophic pressure spikes

---

### 1.4 LIQUID PROPELLANT ROCKET (LPR) — DEFINITION

A **liquid propellant rocket** uses **liquid fuel and liquid oxidizer stored in separate tanks**, which are metered, mixed, and burned in the combustion chamber. The combustion products are then expanded through a nozzle to generate thrust.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Insight:**
> Unlike SPRs, in an LPR the propellant does NOT form the combustion chamber walls. The chamber is a separate pressure vessel, and propellants are fed in from external tanks — this allows **active control, throttling, and multiple restarts**.

**Two Main Classes of Liquid Propellant Systems:**

> **1. Monopropellant Rockets:**
> - A single fluid decomposes exothermically (e.g., Hydrazine → NH₃ + N₂ + H₂)
> - Simple, used for attitude control thrusters

> **2. Bipropellant Rockets (Most common):**
> - Two separate liquids — fuel + oxidizer
> - Examples: LOX/LH₂ (cryogenic), LOX/RP-1 (kerosene), N₂O₄/MMH (storable hypergolic)

**Major Components of a Liquid Propellant Rocket:**

> [!NOTE]
> 1. **Fuel Tank & Oxidizer Tank** — Cryogenic or pressure-stored
> 2. **Feed System** — Pressure-fed OR Pump-fed (turbopumps)
> 3. **Combustion Chamber** — High-pressure reaction zone
> 4. **Nozzle** — Convergent-divergent expansion
> 5. **Gas Generator / Preburner** — Drives turbopumps
> 6. **Throttle/Valve Controls** — Variable thrust and shutoff
> 7. **Pressurization System** — Helium bottles or autogenous

**Two Feed System Architectures:**

| Feed Type | Description | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Pressure-Fed** | High-pressure inert gas (He/N₂) pushes propellants into chamber | Small thrusters, upper stages |
| **Pump-Fed** | Turbopumps pressurize propellants before injection | Large launch vehicles (Saturn V, Falcon 9) |

**Key Characteristics of Liquid Propellants:**

| Property | Typical Value |
|---|---|
| Density | **800–1140 kg/m³** (LOX/RP-1 ≈ 1030 kg/m³) |
| Specific Impulse ($I_{sp}$) | **300–470 s** (vacuum) |
| Flame Temperature | **3500–3800 K** (LOX/LH₂) |
| Exhaust Velocity | **3200–4500 m/s** |
| Throttle Range | **40%–100%** |
| Restarts | **Multiple possible** |
| Storage | **Cryogenic (short)** or **Storable (long)** |

**Advantages of LPR:**
- ✅ Higher $I_{sp}$ (more efficient)
- ✅ Throttleable and restartable
- ✅ Precise mixture control
- ✅ Higher chamber pressures achievable (300+ bar)

**Disadvantages of LPR:**
- ❌ Complex plumbing, pumps, valves
- ❌ Cryogenic propellants boil off (boil-off loss)
- ❌ Lower thrust-to-weight ratio
- ❌ Higher development cost
- ❌ Longer countdown

---

### 1.5 SOLID vs LIQUID — COMPARATIVE OVERVIEW

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — "Candle vs Bunsen Burner":**
> Think of an SPR as a **candle** — once lit, it burns steadily at a fixed rate until the wax is gone. You cannot control the flame size or relight it. An LPR is like a **Bunsen burner** — you can adjust the gas flow (throttle), turn it off, and relight it whenever you want.

| Parameter | Solid Propellant | Liquid Propellant |
|---|---|---|
| State of Propellant | Solid grain | Liquid fuel + oxidizer |
| $I_{sp}$ | 200–300 s | 300–470 s |
| Throttle Control | ❌ No | ✅ Yes |
| Restart | ❌ No | ✅ Yes (multiple) |
| Storage Life | Years | Hours–Days (cryogenic) |
| Complexity | Simple | Complex |
| Reliability | Very High | High |
| Cost | Low | High |
| Examples | PSLV S9, Shuttle SRB, Agni | PSLV-XL upper, GSLV, Falcon 9 |
| Mass Fraction | Higher (no tanks/pumps) | Lower (tanks, pumps, plumbing) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 FUNDAMENTAL ROCKET EQUATIONS — STEP-BY-STEP LOGIC

#### **Step 1: Conservation of Momentum → Thrust Equation**

When propellant gases of mass $dm$ are expelled rearward with velocity $V_e$ in time $dt$, by Newton's Third Law, an equal forward momentum is imparted to the rocket. The rate of change of momentum gives thrust.

$$\boxed{F = \dot{m} V_e + (P_e - P_a) A_e}$$

- **Pressure Thrust Term:** $(P_e - P_a) A_e$ — due to expansion of gas below ambient pressure
- **Momentum Thrust Term:** $\dot{m} V_e$ — due to accelerated mass ejection

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Optimum Expansion Condition:** For maximum thrust at a given altitude, the nozzle should be designed such that $P_e = P_a$. This is called the **optimum expansion ratio**, $\epsilon_{opt}$.

#### **Step 2: Specific Impulse ($I_{sp}$)**

Specific impulse is the **total impulse (thrust × time) delivered per unit weight of propellant consumed**. It is the single most important figure of merit for rocket engines.

$$\boxed{I_{sp} = \frac{V_e}{g_0} = \frac{F}{\dot{m} \cdot g_0}}$$

Where $g_0 = 9.80665$ m/s² (standard gravity). Higher $I_{sp}$ = more efficient engine.

#### **Step 3: Effective Exhaust Velocity**

$$V_e = V_{exit} + \frac{(P_e - P_a) A_e}{\dot{m}}$$

#### **Step 4: Tsiolkovsky Rocket Equation (Δ-V Budget)**

This is the **fundamental equation governing rocket motion** — derived from Newton's laws for a variable-mass system:

$$\boxed{\Delta V = V_e \ln\left(\frac{m_0}{m_f}\right) = g_0 \cdot I_{sp} \cdot \ln\left(\frac{m_0}{m_f}\right)}$$

Where:
- $\Delta V$ = Velocity change achieved (m/s)
- $m_0$ = Initial (wet) mass of rocket including propellant (kg)
- $m_f$ = Final (dry) mass after all propellant burned (kg)
- Ratio $m_0/m_f$ is called the **mass ratio** (MR)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the logarithm?** This equation reveals that ΔV increases **linearly** with exhaust velocity $V_e$ but only **logarithmically** with mass ratio. Hence increasing propellant mass gives diminishing returns — for orbital velocity (~9.4 km/s for LEO), MR must be very large.

#### **Step 5: Propellant Mass Fraction**

$$\boxed{\zeta = \frac{m_{propellant}}{m_0} = \frac{m_0 - m_f}{m_0}}$$

Typical values:
- **Solid rockets:** $\zeta = 0.85 - 0.92$
- **Liquid rockets:** $\zeta = 0.80 - 0.91$
- **Ion/electric:** $\zeta = 0.20 - 0.40$ (but very high $I_{sp}$)

#### **Step 6: Characteristic Velocity ($C^*$)**

A measure of combustion chamber efficiency, independent of nozzle:

$$\boxed{C^* = \frac{P_c \cdot A_t}{\dot{m}}}$$

Where $P_c$ = chamber pressure, $A_t$ = nozzle throat area.

#### **Step 7: Thrust Coefficient ($C_F$)**

$$\boxed{C_F = \frac{F}{P_c \cdot A_t}}$$

- For SPR: $C_F \approx 1.4 - 1.6$
- For LPR: $C_F \approx 1.5 - 2.0$ (vacuum-optimized)

#### **Step 8: Mass Flow Rate Equation**

$$\dot{m} = \frac{P_c \cdot A_t}{C^*}$$

#### **Step 9: Total Impulse**

$$\boxed{I_t = \int_0^{t_b} F \, dt = F \cdot t_b}$$

For a SPR with constant thrust, $I_t = F \cdot t_b$. For an LPR, it may vary with throttle.

#### **Step 10: Solid Propellant Burn Rate (St. Robert's Law)**

$$\boxed{r = a \cdot P_c^n}$$

Where:
- $r$ = Linear burn rate (m/s) — typically 5–50 mm/s
- $a$ = Burn rate coefficient
- $n$ = Pressure exponent (**0.2 to 0.6** for stable combustion; >0.6 is dangerous)
- $P_c$ = Chamber pressure

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** For a stable rocket motor, $n < 1$. If $n \geq 1$, the motor becomes unstable — pressure oscillations can lead to catastrophic explosion.

---

### 2.2 SOLID PROPELLANT ROCKET — DEEP ANALYSIS

#### **2.2.1 Propellant Grain Geometries (Burn Patterns)**

The geometry of the grain controls the **burn surface area** vs **time** profile, which in turn controls the **thrust vs time** profile:

> **1. End-Burning Grain:**
> - Burns on one end face only → **neutral thrust** (nearly constant)
> - Low web fraction, long burn time, low thrust
> - Used in: Gas generators, escape systems

> **2. Internal (Cigarette) Burning:**
> - Burns along cylindrical inner surface → **progressive thrust** (increasing)
> - High thrust, shorter duration
> - Used in: Small tactical missiles

> **3. Star-Perforated Grain:**
> - Multi-pointed star cross-section → **progressive-neutral** (rises, then plateaus)
> - Very common in space booster SRBs

> **4. Multi-Perforated (Slotted) Grain:**
> - Multiple slots/circles → **regressive** (decreasing thrust)
> - Used in sounding rockets

> **5. 3-D / Finocyl Grain:**
> - Complex geometry for tailored thrust profiles

**Burn Surface Area vs Time:**

$$A_b(t) = \text{geometry}(r_0 + r \cdot t)$$

**Web Burn Time:**

$$t_b = \frac{\text{Web thickness}}{r}$$

Where web thickness is the maximum distance the burn surface must travel.

#### **2.2.2 Port-to-Throat Area Ratio (Knutson Criterion)**

For a stable SPR, the port area must always exceed the throat area:

$$\boxed{\frac{A_{port}}{A_{throat}} > 1 \quad \text{throughout burn}}$$

If $A_{port} < A_{throat}$, choking occurs in the grain → pressure spike → motor explosion.

#### **2.2.3 SPR Pressure-Time Profile**

Using St. Robert's law with grain geometry, $P_c$ vs $t$ is obtained. For a balanced star grain, $P_c$ remains roughly constant (neutral profile).

#### **2.2.4 SPR Nozzle Design**

- **Nozzle throat area:** Determines mass flow rate
- **Exit area:** Determines expansion ratio $\epsilon = A_e/A_t$
- **Erosion:** Nozzle throat erodes during burn → $A_t$ increases → $P_c$ decreases slightly → thrust profile changes
- Typically made of graphite, refractory metals, or ablative composites

---

### 2.3 LIQUID PROPELLANT ROCKET — DEEP ANALYSIS

#### **2.3.1 Bipropellant Cycle Types**

> **1. Gas Generator Cycle (Open Cycle):**
> - A small fraction of propellants burned in a gas generator → drives turbopumps → exhaust dumped overboard
> - Simplest, lower chamber pressure (~70 bar)
> - Examples: Merlin 1D, RD-180, PSLV second stage (Vikas)

> **2. Staged Combustion Cycle (Closed Cycle):**
> - Preburner runs fuel-rich or oxidizer-rich → drives turbopumps → exhaust fed into main chamber
> - Higher pressure (~250+ bar), higher $I_{sp}$
> - Examples: Space Shuttle SSME/RS-25, NK-33, Raptor

> **3. Expander Cycle:**
> - Fuel heated in nozzle cooling tubes → drives turbine → returns to chamber
> - Used in vacuum-optimized upper stages
> - Examples: RL-10, Vinci

> **4. Pressure-Fed Cycle:**
> - High-pressure gas (He) pushes propellants into chamber
> - No turbopumps, simplest
> - Used in: Small satellites, Apollo LMDE

#### **2.3.2 Injection System**

The propellants enter the combustion chamber through an **injector**, which atomizes and mixes them. Common types:
- **Impinging stream (like-on-like, unlike doublet)**
- **Spray injectors**
- **Coaxial injectors**

#### **2.3.3 Cooling**

Liquid rockets are **regeneratively cooled**: one of the propellants (usually fuel) circulates through cooling channels around the nozzle and chamber before entering the injector. This:
- Prevents the chamber from melting
- Pre-heats the fuel → improves combustion efficiency

#### **2.3.4 LPR — Chamber Pressure Equations**

For pump-fed systems, $P_c$ can be independently controlled. Typical values:
- Merlin 1D: **~97 bar**
- RD-180: **~260 bar**
- Raptor 2: **~300+ bar**
- F-1 (Saturn V): **~70 bar**

---

### 2.4 KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table for KTU 2024 ESE — these are the equations that appear in Part B 14-mark derivation problems.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $F = \dot{m} V_e + (P_e - P_a)A_e$ | Ideal thrust equation | N |
| 2 | $I_{sp} = V_e / g_0$ | Specific impulse | s |
| 3 | $\Delta V = V_e \ln(m_0/m_f)$ | Tsiolkovsky equation | m/s |
| 4 | $\zeta = m_{prop}/m_0$ | Propellant mass fraction | — |
| 5 | $m_0/m_f = e^{\Delta V / V_e}$ | Mass ratio | — |
| 6 | $C^* = P_c A_t / \dot{m}$ | Characteristic velocity | m/s |
| 7 | $C_F = F / (P_c A_t)$ | Thrust coefficient | — |
| 8 | $r = a P_c^n$ | St. Robert's burn rate law | m/s |
| 9 | $t_b = \text{web}/r$ | Burn time | s |
| 10 | $I_t = F \cdot t_b$ | Total impulse | N·s |
| 11 | $\rho_m = \rho_{ox} \cdot \phi + \rho_f$ | Mixture density (LOX/RP-1 φ=2.56) | kg/m³ |
| 12 | $\epsilon = A_e / A_t$ | Nozzle expansion ratio | — |
| 13 | $A_t / A_e = [(\gamma+1)/2]^{1/(\gamma-1)} \cdot (P_e/P_c)^{1/\gamma} \cdot \sqrt{[(\gamma-1)/(\gamma+1)]/[1-(P_e/P_c)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}]}$ | Nozzle area ratio (isothermal) | — |
| 14 | $\dot{m} = P_c A_t / \sqrt{T_c} \cdot \sqrt{\gamma/R} \cdot [(\gamma+1)/2]^{-(\gamma+1)/[2(\gamma-1)]}$ | Mass flow rate (choked flow) | kg/s |
| 15 | $V_{e,max} = \sqrt{2 \gamma R T_c / (\gamma-1) \cdot [1 - (P_e/P_c)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}]}$ | Isentropic exit velocity | m/s |

> [!NOTE]
> **Constants to Remember:**
> - $g_0 = 9.80665$ m/s² (standard gravity)
> - $\gamma$ for typical combustion gases: **1.20 – 1.30**
> - Universal gas constant $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K)
> - Chamber temperature $T_c$ for LOX/LH₂ ≈ **3500 K**

---

### 2.5 REAL-WORLD ENGINEERING UTILITY

> [!NOTE]
> - **Aerospace:** Launch vehicles (PSLV, GSLV Mk III, Falcon 9, Ariane 5)
> - **Defense:** Ballistic missiles (Agni, Prithvi, SLBM), tactical missiles
> - **Space Exploration:** Satellite insertion, lunar/planetary missions
> - **Military:** Air-launched, ship-launched missiles, decoys
> - **Civil:** Sounding rockets for upper atmosphere research
> - **Future:** Reusable rockets (SpaceX), Raptor full-flow staged combustion, methane-LOX

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 DERIVATION 1 — IDEAL ROCKET THRUST EQUATION

**Starting Principle:** Newton's Second Law applied to a control volume around the rocket.

Let the rocket expel a small mass $dm$ of gas at velocity $V_e$ relative to the rocket in time $dt$. The change in momentum of the rocket is:

$$d(mv) = -V_e \, dm$$

Since the rocket is losing mass, $dm < 0$ (mass ejected). For a rocket moving at velocity $v$ in inertial frame:

$$m \frac{dv}{dt} = -V_e \frac{dm}{dt}$$

But the **absolute force** (thrust) on the rocket, from control-volume analysis considering pressure imbalance at nozzle exit:

$$F = \dot{m} V_e + (P_e - P_a) A_e$$

**Step 1:** Define mass flow rate $\dot{m} = -dm/dt$ (positive quantity of ejected mass per second).

**Step 2:** Apply momentum theorem to control volume:
- Inlet: nothing enters axially (no incoming flow on the rocket)
- Outlet: propellant gas exits at $V_e$ relative to rocket

**Step 3:** The momentum efflux rate is $\dot{m} V_e$.

**Step 4:** The pressure at the nozzle exit plane ($P_e$) acts on area $A_e$ in the forward direction (since the exit pressure pushes on the rocket), while ambient pressure $P_a$ pushes backward on the nozzle exit annulus. Net pressure force:

$$F_{pressure} = (P_e - P_a) A_e$$

**Step 5:** Total thrust is the sum:

$$\boxed{F = \dot{m} V_e + (P_e - P_a) A_e}$$

**Units Check:**
- $\dot{m} V_e$ → (kg/s)(m/s) = N ✓
- $(P_e - P_a) A_e$ → (Pa)(m²) = N ✓

---

### 3.2 DERIVATION 2 — TSIOLKOVSKY ROCKET EQUATION

This derivation yields the **fundamental equation of rocket motion in vacuum**.

**Step 1:** From Newton's Second Law for a variable mass system (rocket):

$$m \frac{dv}{dt} = -V_e \frac{dm}{dt}$$

(The exhaust velocity $V_e$ is assumed constant and directed opposite to rocket motion.)

**Step 2:** Rearrange to separate variables:

$$m \, dv = -V_e \, dm$$

**Step 3:** Divide both sides by $m$:

$$dv = -V_e \frac{dm}{m}$$

**Step 4:** Integrate from initial state $(v_0, m_0)$ to final state $(v_f, m_f)$:

$$\int_{v_0}^{v_f} dv = -V_e \int_{m_0}^{m_f} \frac{dm}{m}$$

**Step 5:** Evaluate both integrals:

$$v_f - v_0 = -V_e [\ln m_f - \ln m_0] = -V_e \ln\left(\frac{m_f}{m_0}\right)$$

**Step 6:** Note that since $m_f < m_0$, $\ln(m_f/m_0) < 0$, so the negative sign yields a positive change. The velocity change (assuming $v_0 = 0$) is:

$$\boxed{\Delta v = V_e \ln\left(\frac{m_0}{m_f}\right)}$$

**Step 7:** In terms of specific impulse:

$$\Delta v = g_0 I_{sp} \ln\left(\frac{m_0}{m_f}\right)$$

**Numerical Example:**

Given: $V_e = 3000$ m/s, $m_0 = 500,000$ kg, $m_f = 100,000$ kg

$$\Delta v = 3000 \times \ln\left(\frac{500{,}000}{100{,}000}\right)$$

$$\Delta v = 3000 \times \ln(5) = 3000 \times 1.6094 = 4828.3 \text{ m/s}$$

---

### 3.3 DERIVATION 3 — SOLID PROPELLANT BURN RATE & CHAMBER PRESSURE

**Step 1:** From St. Robert's law (empirical):

$$r = a P_c^n$$

where $r$ is the linear regression rate of the burning surface (m/s), $a$ is a constant depending on propellant, $n$ is the pressure exponent.

**Step 2:** Mass conservation in the chamber:
Mass burned per second = mass ejected through throat per second:

$$\dot{m} = \rho_p \cdot A_b \cdot r = P_c A_t / C^*$$

Where $\rho_p$ is propellant density, $A_b$ is instantaneous burn surface area.

**Step 3:** Equating:

$$\rho_p A_b r = \frac{P_c A_t}{C^*}$$

**Step 4:** Substitute St. Robert's law:

$$\rho_p A_b \cdot a P_c^n = \frac{P_c A_t}{C^*}$$

**Step 5:** Solve for chamber pressure:

$$\boxed{P_c = \left(\frac{\rho_p A_b \cdot a \cdot C^*}{A_t}\right)^{\frac{1}{1-n}}}$$

This equation shows that chamber pressure depends on:
- **Burn area** $A_b$ (set by grain geometry)
- **Throat area** $A_t$ (set by nozzle design)
- **Propellant properties** ($a$, $n$, $\rho_p$, $C^*$)

**Step 6:** For neutral burning (constant $P_c$), the burn area must grow in a specific way as the web burns:

$$A_b \propto A_t^{\text{const}}$$

This is why **star-perforated grains** are designed — the inner star surface increases as the grain burns, compensating for throat erosion.

---

### 3.4 DERIVATION 4 — NOZZLE AREA RATIO (DE LAVAL)

For isentropic (reversible, adiabatic) flow through a converging-diverging nozzle with choked throat, applying conservation of mass, energy, and momentum:

**Step 1:** Mass flow rate through throat (choked):

$$\dot{m} = \frac{P_c A_t}{\sqrt{T_c}} \sqrt{\frac{\gamma}{R}} \left(\frac{2}{\gamma+1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma+1}{2(\gamma-1)}}$$

**Step 2:** For isentropic expansion from $P_c, T_c$ to exit pressure $P_e$:

$$\frac{T_e}{T_c} = \left(\frac{P_e}{P_c}\right)^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}}$$

**Step 3:** Exit velocity from energy conservation:

$$V_e = \sqrt{\frac{2 \gamma R T_c}{\gamma-1}\left[1 - \left(\frac{P_e}{P_c}\right)^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}}\right]}$$

**Step 4:** The nozzle area ratio for supersonic exit flow:

$$\boxed{\frac{A_e}{A_t} = \frac{1}{M_e}\left[\frac{2}{\gamma+1}\left(1 + \frac{\gamma-1}{2}M_e^2\right)\right]^{\frac{\gamma+1}{2(\gamma-1)}}}$$

Where $M_e$ is the exit Mach number, related to $P_e/P_c$ by:

$$\frac{P_e}{P_c} = \left(1 + \frac{\gamma-1}{2}M_e^2\right)^{-\gamma/(\gamma-1)}$$

---

### 3.5 ANALYTICAL MATRIX — KEY DESIGN TRADE-OFFS

| Design Choice | Effect on $I_{sp}$ | Effect on Thrust | Effect on Complexity | Effect on Cost |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Higher chamber pressure $P_c$ | ↑ (small) | ↑↑ (large) | ↑↑ | ↑↑ |
| Larger nozzle area ratio $\epsilon$ | ↑ (vacuum) | ↑ (vacuum) | ↑ | ↑ |
| Cryogenic propellants (LOX/LH₂) | ↑↑↑ | ↑ | ↑↑ | ↑↑↑ |
| Storable hypergolics (N₂O₄/MMH) | ↑ | ↑ | ↑ | ↑ |
| Solid (composite AP/HTPB) | ↓ | ↑↑ | ↓↓ | ↓ |
| Higher mixture ratio (O/F) | Depends on propellant | ↑ | — | — |
| Staged combustion cycle | ↑↑ | ↑↑ | ↑↑↑ | ↑↑↑ |
| Pressure-fed cycle | ↓ | ↓ | ↓ | ↓ |

**Key Trade-off Insight:** Engineers must balance **specific impulse** (fuel efficiency) vs **thrust** (acceleration) vs **complexity/cost**. No single choice optimizes all three.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 SOLID PROPELLANT ROCKET MOTOR — BLOCK ARCHITECTURE

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A1[Igniter Pyrotechnic Charge] --> A2[Propellant Grain Solid AP HTPB]
    A2 --> A3[Combustion Chamber Casing]
    A3 --> A4[Nozzle Throat Choked Flow]
    A4 --> A5[Nozzle Exit Divergent Section]
    A5 --> A6[Exhaust Plume to Atmosphere]
    A3 --> A7[Insulation Liner]
    A2 --> A8[Internal Bore Star Pattern]
    A8 --> A9[Burning Surface Area]
    A9 --> A10[Mass Flow Rate m_dot]
    A10 --> A11[Chamber Pressure Pc]
    A11 --> A12[Thrust F]
```

**Working Sequence (read top-to-bottom-left-to-right):**
1. Igniter fires → ignites propellant surface
2. Grain burns along pre-designed surface → produces hot gas at high pressure
3. Hot gas flows through nozzle throat (choked)
4. Gas accelerates in divergent nozzle
5. Thrust = momentum + pressure thrust

---

### 4.2 LIQUID PROPELLANT ROCKET — PUMP-FED BIPROPELLANT FLOW

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    B1[Oxidizer Tank LOX] --> B2[Oxidizer Turbopump]
    B3[Fuel Tank RP-1 or LH2] --> B4[Fuel Turbopump]
    B2 --> B5[Injector]
    B4 --> B5
    B5 --> B6[Combustion Chamber Pc 250 bar]
    B6 --> B7[Nozzle Throat]
    B7 --> B8[Nozzle Exit]
    B8 --> B9[Exhaust to Vacuum]
    B6 --> B10[Regenerative Cooling Jacket]
    B10 --> B4
    B11[Gas Generator Preburner] --> B12[Turbine]
    B12 --> B2
    B12 --> B4
    B11 --> B13[Exhaust Dump]
    B5 --> B14[Throttle Valve]
    B14 --> B15[Variable Thrust F]
```

**Cycle Description (Gas Generator Open Cycle):**
- Pressurized tanks feed propellants
- A small portion is burned in preburner → hot gas drives turbine → turbine powers both turbopumps
- Main propellant streams enter injector at high pressure
- Combustion produces high-T, high-P gas
- Nozzle expands gas to produce thrust
- Throttle valve varies flow rate for variable thrust

---

### 4.3 COMPARATIVE BLOCK FLOW — SPR vs LPR

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SPR[Solid Propellant Rocket]
        S1[Stored Solid Grain] --> S2[Single Combustion Zone]
        S2 --> S3[Fixed Thrust Curve]
    end
    subgraph LPR[Liquid Propellant Rocket]
        L1[Fuel Tank] --> L2[Injector]
        L3[Oxidizer Tank] --> L2
        L2 --> L4[Combustion Chamber]
        L4 --> L5[Variable Throttle]
    end
    S3 --> SX[Single Burn Profile]
    L5 --> LX[Multiple Restart Capability]
```

---

### 4.4 SOLID PROPELLANT GRAIN GEOMETRY — BURN EVOLUTION

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    G1[Initial Grain Star Cross Section] --> G2[Burn Surface Grows Regressive to Neutral]
    G2 --> G3[Burn Area Profile A_b vs Time]
    G3 --> G4[Thrust Profile F vs Time]
    G4 --> G5[Total Impulse Integral F dt]
```

**Key Observation:** The grain geometry **encodes** the desired thrust profile. A "neutral" profile gives constant thrust; "progressive" gives rising thrust.

---

### 4.5 TSOLKOVSKY EQUATION — CONCEPTUAL FLOW

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    T1[Initial Mass m0] --> T2[Propellant Burns dm]
    T2 --> T3[Mass Decreases m becomes mf]
    T3 --> T4[Velocity Gains dv]
    T4 --> T5[Final Velocity vf]
    T1 --> T6[Exhaust Velocity Ve]
    T6 --> T7[Delta V Calculation]
    T3 --> T7
    T7 --> T8[Delta V equals Ve times ln m0 divided by mf]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — SHORT ANSWER QUESTIONS (3 MARKS EACH)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**
**Differentiate between solid propellant and liquid propellant rockets based on (i) specific impulse, (ii) throttling capability, and (iii) storage life.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Solid Propellant Rocket | Liquid Propellant Rocket |
|---|---|---|
| (i) Specific Impulse | Lower, typically **200–300 s** | Higher, typically **300–470 s** |
| (ii) Throttling | **Not possible** — burn rate fixed by grain geometry | **Possible** — propellant flow rate controlled by valves/pumps |
| (iii) Storage Life | **Years to decades** (ready to launch) | **Hours to days** for cryogenic; longer for storable |

> [!NOTE]
> Solid rockets are simpler, storable, but less efficient and inflexible. Liquid rockets are complex, more efficient, and offer superior control.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
**Define specific impulse. Why is it considered the most important performance parameter of a rocket engine?**

**Model Answer:**

**Specific Impulse ($I_{sp}$)** is defined as the total impulse delivered per unit weight of propellant consumed:

$$I_{sp} = \frac{F}{\dot{m} \cdot g_0} = \frac{V_e}{g_0}$$

**Why it is the most important parameter:**
1. It represents the **fuel efficiency** of the engine — higher $I_{sp}$ means more thrust per kg of propellant.
2. It directly determines the **mass ratio** needed for a given mission $\Delta V$ via Tsiolkovsky's equation.
3. It allows **objective comparison** between different engine types (solid, liquid, hybrid, electric) regardless of size.
4. A 10% increase in $I_{sp}$ can reduce the required propellant mass by 20–40%, depending on mission.

For example, **LOX/LH₂ in vacuum** gives $I_{sp} \approx 470$ s, while **AP/HTPB composite solid** gives $I_{sp} \approx 270$ s.

---

### 5.2 PART B — 14 MARK QUESTIONS (CHOOSE ONE)

---

## **QUESTION A — 14 MARKS** [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]

### (a) Derive the Tsiolkovsky rocket equation starting from Newton's second law for a variable mass system. Explain its significance in mission design. **(7 marks)**

### (b) A rocket has a total mass of 2000 kg at launch, including 1600 kg of propellant. The effective exhaust velocity is 2500 m/s. Calculate: (i) mass ratio, (ii) propellant mass fraction, (iii) final velocity of the rocket in vacuum. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Starting from Newton's Second Law** for a variable-mass rocket in vacuum (no external forces):

The momentum of the rocket system is $p = mv$. In time $dt$, the rocket ejects mass $-dm$ (negative since mass decreases) at exhaust velocity $V_e$ relative to rocket (directed opposite to motion).

**Conservation of momentum:**

$$m v = (m + dm)(v + dv) + (-dm)(v - V_e)$$

**Step 1:** Expand the right-hand side:

$$m v = mv + m \, dv + v \, dm - V_e \, dm$$

**Step 2:** Cancel $mv$ from both sides:

$$0 = m \, dv + v \, dm - V_e \, dm$$

**Step 3:** Simplify:

$$m \, dv = -V_e \, dm$$

**Step 4:** Separate variables (note $V_e$ is constant for ideal rocket):

$$dv = -V_e \, \frac{dm}{m}$$

**Step 5:** Integrate from initial to final state:

$$\int_0^{v_f} dv = -V_e \int_{m_0}^{m_f} \frac{dm}{m}$$

**Step 6:** Evaluate:

$$v_f = -V_e [\ln m_f - \ln m_0] = V_e \ln\left(\frac{m_0}{m_f}\right)$$

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{\Delta v = v_f - v_0 = V_e \ln\left(\frac{m_0}{m_f}\right)}$$

**Significance in Mission Design:**

1. **Mission ΔV Budget:** The total ΔV required for a mission (e.g., LEO insertion ≈ 9.4 km/s, GTO ≈ 11 km/s, Mars transfer ≈ 12 km/s) must equal $V_e \ln(m_0/m_f)$. This determines the required propellant mass.
2. **Staging:** By dropping empty tanks and engines during flight, $m_f$ is reduced stage-by-stage, allowing lower mass ratios per stage.
3. **Engine Trade-off:** For a fixed mission, increasing $V_e$ (e.g., LOX/LH₂ vs solid) reduces the required propellant mass.
4. **Limit:** Because of the logarithm, no single-stage rocket can reach orbital velocity using only chemical propellants — staging is essential.

---

### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:**
- $m_0 = 2000$ kg (initial wet mass)
- $m_{prop} = 1600$ kg (propellant mass)
- $V_e = 2500$ m/s
- Initial velocity $v_0 = 0$ (launch from rest in vacuum)

**Step 1: Calculate final mass**

$$m_f = m_0 - m_{prop} = 2000 - 1600 = 400 \text{ kg}$$

**(i) Mass Ratio:**

$$\frac{m_0}{m_f} = \frac{2000}{400} = 5$$

**(ii) Propellant Mass Fraction:**

$$\zeta = \frac{m_{prop}}{m_0} = \frac{1600}{2000} = 0.8 = 80\%$$

**(iii) Final Velocity (Tsiolkovsky Equation):**

$$\Delta v = V_e \ln\left(\frac{m_0}{m_f}\right)$$

$$\Delta v = 2500 \times \ln(5)$$

$$\Delta v = 2500 \times 1.6094$$

$$\boxed{\Delta v = 4023.6 \text{ m/s} \approx 4.02 \text{ km/s}}$$

**Interpretation:** This single-stage rocket achieves 4.02 km/s — insufficient for orbit (requires ~9.4 km/s), confirming the need for multi-stage rockets or higher exhaust velocities.

---

## **QUESTION B — 14 MARKS** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]

### (a) With the help of a neat sketch, describe the construction and working principle of a solid propellant rocket motor. Explain the role of the propellant grain geometry in determining the thrust-time profile. **(7 marks)**

### (b) Define characteristic velocity ($C^*$) and thrust coefficient ($C_F$) for a rocket engine. A liquid propellant rocket has chamber pressure 80 bar, throat area 0.05 m², and exhaust velocity 2400 m/s. Calculate: (i) mass flow rate, (ii) thrust in vacuum (assume $P_e = 0$), (iii) thrust coefficient. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Construction of a Solid Propellant Rocket Motor:**

A typical SPR consists of the following major components (refer to schematic in Section 4.1):

1. **Motor Casing:** A high-strength steel or composite cylindrical vessel that contains the combustion pressure (often 50–100 bar). Must be lightweight yet strong.

2. **Propellant Grain:** The solid mixture of fuel and oxidizer (e.g., AP + HTPB + Aluminum), cast or pressed into the desired shape and bonded to the casing.

3. **Insulation Liner:** A layer of rubber-based or silica-filled insulation between the casing and grain to prevent heat transfer.

4. **Igniter:** A small pyrotechnic charge (typically boron-potassium nitrate pellets) electrically initiated.

5. **Nozzle:** A convergent-divergent (de Laval) nozzle, often made of graphite or carbon-carbon composite due to extreme thermal loads (exit velocities exceed Mach 3).

**Working Principle:**

- The igniter fires, producing hot gases that ignite the inner surface of the grain.
- The grain burns **regressively** (inward) at rate $r = aP_c^n$ (St. Robert's Law).
- Combustion gases at high pressure (~70 bar) and temperature (~3000 K) flow through the choked throat, then accelerate supersonically in the divergent section.
- Thrust is generated by momentum change of the exhaust gases.

**Role of Grain Geometry in Thrust-Time Profile:**

The **burn surface area $A_b(t)$** determines the mass flow rate, which determines chamber pressure, which determines thrust:

$$\dot{m} = \rho_p A_b r \implies P_c \propto A_b^{\frac{1}{1-n}} \implies F \propto A_b^{\frac{1}{1-n}}$$

Different grain geometries give different thrust profiles:

| Grain Type | $A_b$ vs Time | Thrust Profile |
|---|---|---|
| End-burning | Constant | Neutral (constant) |
| Cigarette (internal) | Increasing | Progressive |
| Star-perforated | Increasing then constant | Progressive-neutral |
| Multi-perforated (rod & tube) | Decreasing | Regressive |

**Example:** PSLV's S9 strap-on motors use a star-perforated composite propellant grain that gives an initial thrust spike followed by a sustained plateau — ideal for first-stage augmentation.

---

### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Definitions:**

**Characteristic Velocity ($C^*$):** A measure of the combustion chamber's performance, independent of the nozzle expansion. It depends only on the propellant thermodynamics and chamber conditions:

$$C^* = \frac{P_c A_t}{\dot{m}}$$

Units: m/s. Higher $C^*$ = more efficient combustion.

**Thrust Coefficient ($C_F$):** A measure of nozzle performance — how effectively chamber pressure and area are converted to thrust:

$$C_F = \frac{F}{P_c A_t}$$

For vacuum operation with $P_e = 0$:

$$C_F^{vac} = \frac{\dot{m} V_e}{P_c A_t} = \frac{V_e}{C^*}$$

---

**Given Data:**
- $P_c = 80$ bar $= 80 \times 10^5$ Pa $= 8 \times 10^6$ Pa
- $A_t = 0.05$ m²
- $V_e = 2400$ m/s
- $P_e = 0$ (vacuum condition)

**Step 1: Assume typical $C^*$ for LOX/RP-1 combustion ≈ 1700 m/s**

**(i) Mass Flow Rate:**

$$\dot{m} = \frac{P_c A_t}{C^*} = \frac{(8 \times 10^6)(0.05)}{1700}$$

$$\dot{m} = \frac{400{,}000}{1700} = 235.29 \text{ kg/s}$$

$$\boxed{\dot{m} \approx 235.3 \text{ kg/s}}$$

**(ii) Thrust in Vacuum ($P_e = 0$, $P_a = 0$):**

From the thrust equation:

$$F = \dot{m} V_e + (P_e - P_a)A_e$$

For vacuum, $P_a = 0$. For this simplified calculation assuming $P_e = 0$:

$$F = \dot{m} V_e = 235.29 \times 2400$$

$$\boxed{F = 564{,}706 \text{ N} \approx 564.7 \text{ kN}}$$

**(iii) Thrust Coefficient:**

$$C_F = \frac{F}{P_c A_t} = \frac{564{,}706}{(8 \times 10^6)(0.05)} = \frac{564{,}706}{400{,}000}$$

$$\boxed{C_F \approx 1.412}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Check using alternate formula:** $C_F = V_e / C^* = 2400 / 1700 = 1.412$ ✓ (Consistent)

**Interpretation:** A thrust coefficient of ~1.41 is typical for moderately expanded nozzles; higher $C_F$ values (1.7–2.0) are achieved with vacuum-optimized nozzles with larger area ratios.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🛑 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Units Mistake:** Forgetting to convert bar to Pa (1 bar = 10⁵ Pa). Marks deducted for inconsistent units.
> 2. **Tsiolkovsky Sign Error:** Writing $\Delta V = V_e \ln(m_f/m_0)$ (negative) instead of $\ln(m_0/m_f)$ — loses 2 marks.
> 3. **Confusing $I_{sp}$ and $V_e$:** $I_{sp} = V_e/g_0$, not $I_{sp} = V_e$. Use $g_0 = 9.80665$ m/s².
> 4. **Missing the "Vacuum" condition:** If problem says "in vacuum," set $P_a = 0$ from the start.
> 5. **Burn Rate vs Pressure:** St. Robert's law is $r = aP_c^n$, NOT $r = aP_c$ (linear). Note the **exponent n**.
> 6. **Staging Question Trap:** For multi-stage rockets, compute ΔV stage-wise and sum — do not apply Tsiolkovsky to the entire rocket as one.

---

### 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER 🚀

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Solid & Liquid Propellant Rockets:**

### **🔥 Core Definitions to Memorize:**

- ✅ **Rocket:** Self-contained reaction engine carrying its own oxidizer
- ✅ **Thrust Equation:** $F = \dot{m}V_e + (P_e - P_a)A_e$
- ✅ **Specific Impulse:** $I_{sp} = V_e / g_0$ (seconds)
- ✅ **Total Impulse:** $I_t = \int F \, dt = F \cdot t_b$ (for constant thrust)
- ✅ **Tsiolkovsky Equation:** $\Delta V = V_e \ln(m_0/m_f)$
- ✅ **Mass Fraction:** $\zeta = m_{prop}/m_0$
- ✅ **Characteristic Velocity:** $C^* = P_c A_t / \dot{m}$
- ✅ **Thrust Coefficient:** $C_F = F / (P_c A_t)$
- ✅ **Burn Rate (SPR):** $r = a P_c^n$ — St. Robert's Law

### **🎯 Key Numerical Values:**

- ✅ $g_0 = 9.80665$ m/s²
- ✅ $I_{sp}$: SPR = 200–300 s; LPR = 300–470 s
- ✅ $T_c$: SPR = 2500–3800 K; LPR (LOX/LH₂) = ~3500 K
- ✅ $P_c$: SPR = 50–100 bar; LPR = 70–300+ bar
- ✅ $\Delta V_{LEO} \approx 9.4$ km/s
- ✅ $V_e$: SPR = 2000–2800 m/s; LPR = 3200–4500 m/s

### **🏗️ SPR-Specific:**

- ✅ Grain types: Homogeneous (double-base) vs Heterogeneous (composite)
- ✅ Components: Casing, Grain, Igniter, Nozzle, Insulation
- ✅ Grain geometries: End-burning, Cigarette, Star, Multi-perforated
- ✅ Knutson Criterion: $A_{port}/A_{throat} > 1$ always
- ✅ Stable combustion: $n < 1$ in St. Robert's law
- ✅ Cannot throttle, cannot restart, very reliable, long storage

### **💧 LPR-Specific:**

- ✅ Components: Fuel tank, Oxidizer tank, Turbopump, Injector, CC, Nozzle
- ✅ Feed systems: Pressure-fed (simple) vs Pump-fed (powerful)
- ✅ Cycles: Gas Generator, Staged Combustion, Expander
- ✅ Can throttle, can restart, higher $I_{sp}$, more complex
- ✅ Cryogenic (LOX/LH₂, short storage) vs Storable (N₂O₄/MMH, long storage)
- ✅ Regenerative cooling by fuel circulates in jacket

### **📊 Comparative Facts:**

- ✅ SPR $I_{sp}$ ≈ 250 s (composite AP/HTPB) < LPR $I_{sp}$ ≈ 470 s (LOX/LH₂ vac)
- ✅ LPR: Higher chamber pressure = Higher thrust
- ✅ Larger expansion ratio $\epsilon$ = Better vacuum performance
- ✅ Tsiolkovsky: Linear in $V_e$, Logarithmic in mass ratio

### **🧠 KTU Exam Strategy Tips:**

- ✅ Always state assumptions (vacuum, ideal gas, constant $V_e$, $g_0 = 9.81$ m/s²)
- ✅ Show step-by-step derivation; don't skip algebra
- ✅ Convert all units to SI before substituting
- ✅ Include units in final answer
- ✅ In derivation questions, end with a "boxed" final result
- ✅ Memorize typical $I_{sp}$ values for SPR (250 s) and LPR (300–450 s)

### **🌍 Real-World Examples:**

- ✅ **SPR Examples:** PSLV S9/S9 strap-ons, GSLV Mk III S200, Space Shuttle SRB, Agni series
- ✅ **LPR Examples:** PSLV-XL upper stage (Vikas), GSLV Mk III (CE-20), Falcon 9 Merlin, Ariane Vulcain, Saturn V F-1
- ✅ **ISRO Special:** India's cryogenic engine CE-20 uses gas-generator cycle, LOX/LH₂, $I_{sp} \approx 440$ s (vacuum)

---

**🎓 END OF MODULE 4 NOTES — SOLID & LIQUID PROPELLANT ROCKETS**
